

<!-- block_017 master -->

# Mistake of Law

<!-- block_017 procedure -->

### Mistake of Law — Practitioner commentary (Southern Pross submissions)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Mistake of law overview and practitioner note] [CONCEPTS: mistake of law, ignorance of law, exculpation, Southern Pross, driving whilst disqualified] [CITATION: Mistake of Law - Practitioner Commentary]

Tactical coverage: Mistake of law overview and practitioner note (Southern Pross submissions on a driving whilst disqualified case are on file).

Commonly known as the defence of ignorance and is not a ground for exculpation. It involves a misconception that occurs when a person with complete knowledge of the facts reaches an erroneous conclusion as to their legal effect; an incorrect opinion or inference, arising from a flawed evaluation of the facts.

A mistake of law, as distinct from a mistake of fact, does not generally exculpate the defendant's criminal liability.

Determining what is a mistake of fact and what is a mistake of law is not always straightforward. The process is further complicated when the mistake involves a combination of factual and legal elements.

---

### Thomas v R (1937) 59 CLR 279 and Strathfield Municipal Council v Elvy (1992) NSWLR 745 — mixed mistakes analysis
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Thomas v R (1937) 59 CLR 279; Strathfield Municipal Council v Elvy (1992) NSWLR 745] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Mixed mistakes — classification as fact or law] [CONCEPTS: mixed mistakes, mistake of fact, mistake of law, Thomas v R, Strathfield Municipal Council] [CITATION: Thomas v R (1937) 59 CLR 279 - Mixed Mistakes Analysis]

Tactical coverage: Distinction between mixed mistakes as mistakes of fact or law.

In Thomas v R (1937) 59 CLR 279, Dixon J (Latham CJ and Rich J agreeing) drawing on English authorities it concluded that such mixed mistakes should be considered mistakes of fact. However more recently in Strathfield Municipal Council v Elvy (1992) NSWLR 745 at 751, Gleeson CJ held that mixed mistakes will not ordinarily constitute mistakes of fact.

---

### Minear v Rudrum [2001] WASCA 10 — provisional licence / automatic disqualification (case authority)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Minear v Rudrum [2001] WASCA 10] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Mistake of law — provisional licence and automatic disqualification case] [CONCEPTS: provisional licence, automatic disqualification, mistake of law, traffic infringement, Minear v Rudrum] [CITATION: Minear v Rudrum [2001] WASCA 10 - Provisional Licence Case]

Tactical coverage: Minear v Rudrum facts and appellate outcome.

In Minear v Rudrum [2001] WASCA 10 where the Court of Appeal dismissed an appeal because the mistake was legal not factual.

In that case the appellant was the holder of a provisional driver licence and issued with a traffic infringement notice resulting in automatic disqualification. The appellant's mother managed the infringement notice on behalf of her son and made enquiries with the traffic infringement section. Here erroneous advice was received to the effect that disqualification would not take effect until the licence was collected.

The appellant relied on such advice and was charged and convicted of driving offences.

On appeal it was held that the cancellation of the licence arose automatically by operation of the law and therefore any mistake he made was one of law. It was accepted that the appellant was advised that his licence remained valid and the honest and reasonable limbs of his claim were (presumably) made out, but dismissed.

The advice he received was advice as to the law and provision of the law remained applicable irrespective of such advice.

---

### Brooks v Roberts (unreported, SCt of WA, Library 970072, 17 February 1997) — case note
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Brooks v Roberts, unreported, SCt of WA, Library 970072, 17 February 1997] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Unreported WA case on licence validity and ignorance of law] [CONCEPTS: licence validity, ignorance of law, Brooks v Roberts, correction of sentence, ex parte] [CITATION: Brooks v Roberts (1997) - Licence Validity Case]

Tactical coverage: Brooks v Roberts summary and relation to Minear.

Brooks v Roberts, unreported, SCt of WA, Library 970072, 17 February 1997 was followed. This involved circumstances where the court failed to impose the minimum statutory period of disqualification and a correction of sentence application was made. The application was not served on the defendant and resulted in the sentence being corrected ex parte. The defendant then drove contrary to the disqualification and it was held his belief as to the validity of his licence could have been based only on his ignorance of the law.

---

### Wroblewski v Starling [1987] WAR 233 — distinguished case
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Wroblewski v Starling [1987] WAR 233] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Administrative action required to effect suspension — distinction] [CONCEPTS: administrative action, driver suspension, Wroblewski v Starling, distinction, licence suspension] [CITATION: Wroblewski v Starling [1987] WAR 233 - Distinction]

Tactical coverage: Distinguishing Wroblewski v Starling from Minear/Brooks.

A similar case of Wroblewski v Starling [1987] WAR 233 was distinguished as that involved particular administrative action that had to be taken to effect the period of driver suspension.

It is submitted that in the present circumstances there was no further action required to be taken to effect the court sanctioned disqualification period. It took effect immediately and any subsequent erroneous action taken by the Registrar of Motor Vehicle, or the advantage the defendant took of that mistake, does not impact upon the validity of the sentence.

---

### Ostrowski v Palmer [2004] HCA 30 — leading High Court authority on mistake of law
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Ostrowski v Palmer [2004] HCA 30] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Mistake induced by advice — legal not factual] [CONCEPTS: Ostrowski v Palmer, mistake of law, Fisheries WA, advice, mistake induced] [CITATION: Ostrowski v Palmer [2004] HCA 30 - Advice-Induced Mistake]

Tactical coverage: Ostrowski v Palmer High Court decision and key quotation.

A leading case which emphasises the distinction between mistake of law and mistake of fact is the High Court decision of Ostrowski v Palmer [2004] HCA 30.

The appellant was a fisherman who had sought advice from the Western Australia Fisheries Department as to regulations applicable to rock lobster fishing. He was provided with insufficient information of which he relied upon and was found in breach of an area restriction.

The High Court found the mistake to be one of law and not fact. In particular, Hugh J at paragraph 53 held "it is irrelevant that Mr Palmer's mistake was induced by the conduct of an employee of Fisheries WA. That conduct cannot convert a mistake as to the applicable law into a mistake of fact. If a defendant knows all the relevant facts that constitute the offence and acts on erroneous advice as to the legal effect of those facts, the defendant, like the adviser, has been mistaken as to the law, not the facts".

---

### Honest and reasonable mistake of fact — preliminary annotation and legislative context
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Honest and reasonable mistake of fact — policy context and Hindrum v Lane reference] [CONCEPTS: honest and reasonable, mistake of fact, Road Safety Act, Hindrum v Lane, policy of statute] [CITATION: Honest and Reasonable Mistake of Fact - Preliminary & Policy Context]

Tactical coverage: Preliminary note on honest and reasonable mistake of fact and statutory purpose context.

It is worth noting as a preliminary issue that in Hindrum v Lane [2014] Tennant J referred with approval to the comment of Rowland J in the Western Australian case of Pearce v Stanton [1984] WAR 358 (at 363) to the effect that a consideration of whether in the circumstances a belief is reasonable must also look at the policy behind the relevant statute. The section of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 which is the subject of the charge was introduced in 2005 by an amending Act. During the Second Reading Speech introducing the amending Bill, the Minister responsible noted 'the purpose of this Bill is to reform the Act to address the road safety issues associated with people who drive motor vehicles with illicit drugs in their blood, increasing the risk of road trauma on Tasmanian roads.' It is submitted this purpose should be kept in mind by a Court considering any question of honest and reasonable mistake in relation to the presence of amphetamine in a driver's system.

---

### Defence of honest and reasonable mistake — evidentiary/onus procedural note
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Defence of honest and reasonable mistake — evidentiary burden and test] [CONCEPTS: evidentiary burden, onus, honest belief, reasonable belief, prosecution's burden] [CITATION: Defence of Honest and Reasonable Mistake - Evidentiary Burden]

Tactical workflow for raising and responding to the defence of honest and reasonable mistake.

Tactical coverage: Defence mechanics, evidentiary burden and standard of proof.

Raised by the evidence adduced by the defendant The defendant has no special burden when raising the defence of honest and reasonable mistake, they need only raise the evidentiary burden to satisfy its foundation. . This can occur in the cross examination in prosecution witnesses, though.

Notwithstanding common usage it is strictly not a defence. Rather if held it means prosecution have been unable to establish all of the essential ingredients of the charge.

Once raised by the defendant, the onus is on the prosecuting authority to dispel any doubt that may have been raised by the evidence that the acts performed were a result of honest and reasonable mistake. The standard is beyond reasonable doubt.

The general test is did the defendant have an honest and reasonable mistaken belief in a certain state of things which, if that state of things existed, the defendant's actions would have been innocent.

The belief is based on both a subjective and objective test.

Court in Hindrum v Lane [2014] TASFC 5. The Full Court's consideration of the question of the evidentiary burden: All judgments in the Full Court made the point that the requirement that the belief be reasonable is a requirement that the belief honestly held by the accused person is a belief held on reasonable grounds, not that the belief itself must be objectively reasonable.

Honest (subjective)

Reasonable (mixed -- subjective attributes of specific person in subjective context against an objective standard)

When raised the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the applicant did not act pursuant to an honest belief, held on reasonable grounds, in a state of facts which, if true, would render him not guilty of the offence.

---

### Threshold to raise defence — procedural checklist and Mr King example
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Threshold for raising honest and reasonable mistake — factual sufficiency and examples] [CONCEPTS: threshold, evidentiary sufficiency, Mr King, Barnes v Fowler, admissible evidence] [CITATION: Threshold to Raise Defence - Mr King & Barnes v Fowler Example]

Tactical coverage: Assessing whether factual assertions suffice to raise defence.

The first question to be asked is whether Mr King's assertions are sufficient to raise the issue of honest and reasonable mistake. It is submitted this is arguable, given Mr King simply made statements which could not be regarded as evidence, in the sense of admissible evidence, and those statements were untested by cross examination and were unsupported by any other evidence. Furthermore, there was no opportunity for prosecution to test other aspects of Mr King's statements which might lead to questions about his credibility.

For example, at one stage in his statement, he referred to unknown persons at the birthday party calling Police. He also referred to his previous assistance to Police on an unidentified matter. Given there were no particulars given about either of those statements, prosecution has been unable to test their veracity.

As then Acting Chief Magistrate Daly said in the analogous case of Barnes v Fowler [2016] TASMC 8, referring to drinks being spiked, '...in order for the evidence on this issue to be sufficient there would need to be something more than the merest of possibilities of an opportunity for such an

occurrence' (at [30]).

In that case, Acting Chief Magistrate Daly, in considering the reasonableness of the defendant's belief that she had not taken methylamphetamine, concluded:

'there is nothing against which the Court can independently assess the objective reasonableness of the belief asserted by the defendant. The evidence to which the defendant points to show her evidential burden is discharged is vague and, ultimately, bedevilled by a real absence of any details or particulars. The evidence as to her belief remains solely within her own knowledge, untestable against any other material independent facts. In effect, it amounts to mere assertion' (at [32]).

---

### "Reasonable" — annotated exposition with Mei Ying Su and authority extracts
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Mei Ying Su; GJ Coles & Co Ltd v Goldsworthy [1985] WAR 183; O'Brien v Ostrowski [1999] WASCA 184; BRK v R [2001] WASCA 161; Aubertin v Western Australia (2006) 33 WAR 87; R v Mrzljak (2005) 1 Qd R 308; others cited] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Meaning of "reasonable" in honest and reasonable belief — mixed subjective/objective standard] [CONCEPTS: reasonable belief, subjective-objective standard, Mei Ying Su, s 9.2, case extracts] [CITATION: Reasonable - Mei Ying Su & Authority Extracts]

Tactical coverage: Detailed exposition of the meaning of "reasonable" in the mixed subjective/objective test with cited authorities.

My summary (but cited in Hindrum v Lane [2014] TASFC 5 (1 July 2014))

The word 'reasonable' in honest and reasonable

(a) does not involve the hypothetical ordinary or reasonable person test;

(b) requires that the belief be that of the accused;

(c) requires that the accused's belief be objectively reasonable i.e. rational, based on reason, or capable of sustaining belief; and

(d) requires the objective reasonableness of the accused's belief to be assessed by reference to the subjective circumstances in which the accused was placed, including the accused's personal attributes and the information available to him or her at the time."

"'In Mei Ying Su at [104], Reeves J said at [104]-[106]:'

'[104] In the Criminal Code jurisdictions of Queensland, Western Australia and Tasmania, the defence of acting under an honest and reasonable, but mistaken belief, has been said to reflect the common law with "complete accuracy": see Thomas at 305 -- 306 per Dixon J and CTM at [3] per Gleeson CJ and Gummow, Crennan and Kiefel JJ. Different judges of the Western Australian Court of Appeal (and the Full Court) have described the expression "honest and reasonable belief" (in s 24 of the West Australian Criminal Code) in the following terms:

(a) "...it must be reasonable, which is to say that it must be based on his appreciation of primary objective fact which is in reason capable of sustaining the belief ...", in GJ Coles & Co Ltd v Goldsworthy [1985] WAR 183 at 187 per Burt CJ, Brinsden and Smith JJ;

(b) "... a reasonable man or woman in the position of the defendant carrying out the same actions in the same circumstances may make the same mistake", in O'Brien v Ostrowski [1999] WASCA 184 at [108] per McKechnie J;

(c) "It is that belief which must be reasonable; ie, in all the circumstances it must have been reasonable for the accused person to hold the belief which, in this case, would render his conduct innocent"; and "... it is the actual belief of the accused with which the section is concerned, in judging reasonableness it will always be necessary to advert to the circumstances as they existed at the time in so far as they were known to the accused", in BRK v R [2001] WASCA 161 at [34] and [36] per Murray J.

(d) "For there to be an operative mistake under s 24, an accused must have acted under an actual belief in the existence of a state of things (subjective element) and the accused belief must be reasonable (mixed element) ... the mixed element is not wholly objective; reasonableness is not to be judged by the standard of the hypothetical ordinary or reasonable person. The mixed element is a combination of subjective and objective aspects. The requirement that the belief be reasonable imports an objective standard. The subjective aspect is that the reasonableness is to be judged by reference to the personal attributes and characteristics of the accused that are capable of affecting his or her appreciation or perception of the circumstances in which he or she found himself or herself", in Aubertin v Western Australia (2006) 33 WAR 87; [2006] WASCA 229 at [43] per McLure JA (with whom Roberts-Smith JA and Buss JA agreed).

[105] The Queensland Court of Appeal considered the equivalent expression (in s 24 of the Queensland Criminal Code 1899 (Qld)) in R v Mrzljak (2005) 1 Qd R 308; (2004) 152 A Crim R 315; [2004] QCA 420, and described it as requiring:

(a) "...a consideration of whether there were reasonable grounds for the belief, not what a reasonable person would have believed" per McMurdo P at [21];

(b) "...that the critical focus is on the offender rather than the theoretical reasonable person. It is the information available to the offender which must determine whether the belief was honest and also was reasonable ..." per Williams JA at [53];

(c) that "[t]he section directs attention to the actual belief of the accused; nothing in its language invites reference to the reasonable man's putative belief. What must be considered, in my view, is the reasonableness of an accused's belief based on the circumstances as he perceived them to be", per Holmes JA at [81].

[106] From these authorities it is apparent that the word "reasonable" in s 9.2:

(a) does not involve the hypothetical ordinary or reasonable person test;

(b) requires that the belief be that of the accused;

(c) requires that the accused's belief be objectively reasonable ie rational, based on reason, or capable of sustaining belief; and

(d) requires the objective reasonableness of the accused's belief to be assessed by reference to the subjective circumstances in which the accused was placed, including the accused's personal attributes and the information available to him or her at the time'."

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Smith v Marshall [2024] TASMC 12] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Research on internet chat groups not a reasonable source of legal advice] [CONCEPTS: Smith v Marshall, internet chat groups, reasonable belief, legal advice, TASMC 2024] [CITATION: Smith v Marshall [2024] TASMC 12 - Internet Chat Groups Not Reasonable]

### Counters to "reasonable" — checklist of prosecutorial responses
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Counters to claims of reasonableness] [CONCEPTS: prior restrictions, sentence clarity, representation, infer seeking clarification, unreasonable assertions, evidence] [CITATION: Counters to Reasonable - Checklist]

Tactical coverage: Practical counters to an accused's claim that belief was reasonable.

As to existing restrictions or sentences:

- Imposed in court where the defendant did appear. It was unequivocally made clear to the defendant

- The assertion that the defendant did not understand the sentence is unreasonable as he was represented by legal counsel and it could be inferred if there was any doubt in his mind he would have sought clarification.

An example:

Smith v Marshall [2024] TASMC 12

In this decision, court said research on internet chat groups was not reasonable as it was not a legal practitioner or other appropriate authority

### Hindrum v Lane [2014] TASFC 5 — fisherman lobster example (honest but not reasonable)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Hindrum v Lane [2014] TASFC 5] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Honest but not reasonable — fisherman measuring practice example] [CONCEPTS: Hindrum v Lane, honest belief, not reasonable, lobster measurement, visual inspection] [CITATION: Hindrum v Lane [2014] TASFC 5 - Fisherman Example]

Tactical coverage: Example where belief was honest but not objectively reasonable.

Fisherman. Pulled lobsters out one at a time. Many years experience. If in doubt on vidual inspection would measure with tape. Police found a lobster 2 mm under the min size. Held it was honest. Not reasonable.

The appellant did not measure every fish he took by the use of a measure. He had a practice of visually inspecting each fish. His experience of many years allowed him to make a fairly accurate assessment by that visual inspection as to whether a fish was legal size or not. If he was in any doubt he would measure. Evidence was given about fish flapping about when they were pulled from the pot to be assessed or actually measured. In the present case quite clearly this fish, if checked, would not have been assessed as obviously over the legal size. At best it had to have fallen into the doubtful category. In those circumstances, had it been measured with a measure, the length would have been less than 110 millimetres. The appellant was a fisherman of many years' experience. It must be inferred he had measured fish under both difficult and calm conditions. It must also have been inferred he knew the significant consequences of being found with an undersize fish.

The only conclusion open is that he did not measure this fish and that he either did not visually check it, or if he did so, he did it so casually as to not give him an accurate assessment. In those circumstances any belief he might have held cannot be said to have been objectively reasonable.

There are only two possible explanations for the mistake. The first is that the appellant did not measure the lobster at all. The second is that he measured the lobster incorrectly

 If the appellant's error was to rely on his estimation rather than measurement, then his belief cannot be said to be objectively reasonable, despite his experience. In this case, a belief based on an incorrect measurement could not be objectively reasonable either. The appellant had an accurate measuring device. He was experienced in measuring and responsible for his own measurement. He had ample opportunity to check his measurement, but said he only ever measured once.

### Otherwise lawful — annotation and Tasmania v Bell [2019] TASSC 34
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Tasmania v Bell [2019] TASSC 34] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Defence only if act would otherwise be lawful — Tasmania v Bell example] [CONCEPTS: otherwise lawful, mistake of fact, Tasmania v Bell, supply to minor, alternative offences, practitioner note] [CITATION: Otherwise Lawful - Tasmania v Bell [2019] TASSC 34]

Tactical coverage: Limitation of honest and reasonable mistake defence where the act remains unlawful.

Defence only available of the honest and reasonable mistake of fact would otherwise render the relevant act as LAWFUL. If the act was still unlawful for some other reason, then the defence would not stand (I'm a bit iffy about this...)

Tasmania v Bell [2019] TASSC 34

Def was charged with supplying to a minor. Tried to claim mistake of fact as to age. No alternative charge available to get conviction for supply generally, if pross failed to win supply to minor. BLOW CJ held that the defence was not open, because even if there was a mistake as to age, the act still breached the supply offence under another section. The act would not have otherwise have been lawful.

---

### Duress — Criminal Code Act 1924 s 20 (Tas) — elements and common law formulation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 20] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Duress — when compulsion a defence and common law elements] [CONCEPTS: duress, compulsion defence, immediate threat, exceptions, Hurley and Murray, Criminal Code s20] [CITATION: Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 20 - Duress Elements] [CASE: Hurley and Murray [1967] VR 129]

Tactical coverage: Duress elements under statute and common law checklist (Hurley and Murray).

Criminal Code Act 1924 -- Section 20.   When compulsion a defence

Tasmania

Essential Elements:

(***Hurley and Murray [1967] VR 129)***

1)  There must be a threat of immediate death or grievous bodily harm

- If there is opportunity to report the criminals and place themselves in the protection of authorities, the defence will not succeed.

2)  Accused must be subject TO the threats

- Need not be the subject of the threats -- may be a threat made by a person present at the commission of the offence to harm any person

3)  The person making the threat must be actually present at the commission of the offence.

4)  The accused must believe that the threat will be executed

5)  The offence committed by the accused must not be an 'excepted offence'

- Expressed in the section itself "treason, murder, piracy, offences deemed to be piracy, attempting to murder, rape, forcible abduction, aggravated armed robbery, armed robbery, aggravated robbery, robbery, causing grievous bodily harm, and arson."

6)  The defence will not be available to the accused if he is a party to any association or conspiracy the being a party to which rendered him subject to compulsion.

- Translated: Where defendant joins an association or conspiracy and actually foresaw that they might be the subsequently threated to commit an offence.

Common Law

Where there is evidence of duress the onus denying its presence or its influence should rest on the prosecution. That the conduct was not the product of duress then becomes one of the absence of duress in the appropriate sense and one of the "essential elements of criminal responsibility"

Smith J in ***Hurley and Murray [1967] VR 129***

I consider the following affirmative proposition may be stated. Where the accused has been required to do the act charged against him

1)  Under threat that death or grievous bodily harm will be inflicted unlawfully upon a human being if the accursed fails to do the act and

2)  The circumstances were such that a person of ordinary firmness would have been likely to yield to the threat n the way the accused did and

3)  The threat was present and continuing, imminent and impending and

4)  The accused reasonably apprehended that the threat would be carried out and

5)  He was induced thereby to commit the crime charged and

6)  That the crime was not murder nor any other crim so heinous so as to be excepted from the doctrine and

7)  The accused di not by fault on his part when free from the duress, expose himself to its application and

8)  He had no means with safety to himself, of preventing the execution of the threat..

> *then the accused, in such circumstances at least, has a defence of duress.*

# Duress — Express and implied threats

<!-- block_018 procedure -->

### Chunk 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: The King v Anna Rowan [2024] HCA 9 - Express and implied threats] [CASE: The King v Anna Rowan - A Pseudonym [2024] HCA 9 (13 March 2024)] [CATEGORY: Defence - Duress] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Express and implied threats in duress — paragraphs 37-40 of Rowan] [CONCEPTS: duress, threat, implied threat, The King v Anna Rowan, Dawson, Lorenz, Hurley, imminence, operative threat]

Covers: Express and implied threats in duress — The King v Anna Rowan paras 37-40.

37. The necessity for the accused to be subject to a threat of infliction of the relevant form of harm if the accused fails to commit the acts that constitute the offence charged has been a consistent feature of the consideration of the common law of duress by intermediate courts of appeal in the common law jurisdictions in this country.[**^\[31\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn31)

38. The nature of the requisite threat is illustrated by the facts of *R v Dawson*[**^\[32\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn32) and *R v Lorenz*.[**^\[33\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn33) In *Dawson*, the accused escaped from prison but led evidence that he did so because he feared for his life after receiving threats from other prisoners. The Full Court held that duress was not open because the threats were not made on the basis that, if he did not escape, he would be subject to violence.[**^\[34\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn34) In *Lorenz*, the accused robbed a supermarket employee with a knife after her de facto partner threatened to kill her if she did not obtain enough money to re-register his car. The accused was pregnant at the time.[**^\[35\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn35) After noting *Hurley* and *Dawson*, Crispin J held that the defence of duress was not available as the accused\'s de facto partner did not direct her to commit the robbery.[**^\[36\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn36)

39. However, neither *Dawson* nor *Lorenz *suggests that a threat to inflict the relevant form of harm if the accused fails to commit the acts constituting the charge cannot be implied. In *Hurley*, Smith J postulated the scenario of armed men taking possession of a house at gunpoint and sending the accused to commit a crime on their behalf while retaining a hostage such that "the threat to the hostage\'s life, *whether it is formulated in words or left unsaid*, may be held to be, during the execution of the commission, sufficiently present and continuing, imminent and impending to found the defence of duress" (emphasis added).[**^\[37\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn37)

40. The references in *Hurley* to the threat being "present and continuing, imminent and impending" do not require that all the conduct relied upon as giving rise to the threat be engaged in immediately prior to the crime being committed. Instead, those statements should be taken as conveying a requirement that the threat conform with the description of the equivalent aspect of duress noted in other cases, namely that the threat be "operative"[**^\[38\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn38) or "effective"[**^\[39\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn39) at the time the accused committed the acts that constitute the offence charged. The relevant threat does not have to immediately precede the accused\'s criminal act if there was no reasonable opportunity to avoid committing the offence in the meantime (that being the issue raised by element (viii) in *Hurley*).[**^\[40\]^**](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2024/9.html#fn40)

---

### Chunk 2
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Tangye (1997) - Duty to declare basis of alleged criminal liability] [CASE: R v Tangye, CCA, 10 April 1997 (1997) 92 A Crim R 545] [CATEGORY: Parties, Accessories and Principles] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: Duty of Crown prosecutor to outline conceptual nature of Crown case at opening] [CONCEPTS: Tangye, duty to declare, Crown opening, particulars, change of case, jury directions]

Covers: Duty to declare basis of alleged criminal liability (Tangye 1997)

*The obligation of the Crown Prosecutor in opening the Crown case is not merely to outline the facts which the Crown proposes to establish in evidence. It is also to indicate, in conceptual terms, the nature of the Crown case. This is to assist both the thai judge and counsel for the accused, more so than the jury. It is essential that any doubt about R v Tangye, CCA, 10 April 1997 (1997) (1997) 92 A Crim R 545, 1997 WL 33115907 © Thomson Reuters. 14 the nature of the Crown case, conceptually, be removed at that early stage. If it is not done at that stage, or if there had been some change in its nature since the case was opened, it is vital that it be identified with some precision, in the absence of the jury, before counsel commence their final addresses. It becomes very difficult for the judge sensibly to make alterations to directions already given once it is learnt that the issues are different to those which had been assumed to exist*

---

### Chunk 3
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code, 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: 3] [CITATION: Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 3 - Which parties to crimes to be deemed principals in the first degree] [CATEGORY: Parties, Accessories and Principles] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: s3 Who is deemed a party/principal in the first degree] [CONCEPTS: Criminal Code s3, principal, aiding, abetting, instigation, parties to crime, accessory, mens rea]

Covers: s 3 Which parties to crimes to be deemed principals in the first degree (Criminal Code 1924 s3).

3. Which parties to crimes to be deemed principals in the first degree

      (1) Where a crime is committed, each of the following persons is deemed to be a party to, and to be guilty of, the crime, and may be charged with actually committing it:

(a) every person who actually commits the crime;

(b) every person who does any act or makes any omission for the purpose of enabling or

aiding another person to commit the crime;

(c) every person who abets another person in committing the crime;

(d) every person who instigates any other person to commit the crime.

      (2) Any person who instigates another to do any act or make any omission of such a nature that, if he had himself done the act or made the omission, the act or omission would have constituted a crime on his part, is guilty of the same crime as if he had himself done the act or made the omission; and may be charged with himself committing that crime.

---

### Chunk 4
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Act, 1959 (Tas)] [SECTION: 73] [CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 73 - Accessories] [CATEGORY: Parties, Accessories and Principles] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: s73 Accessories for simple offences] [CONCEPTS: Justices Act s73, accessories, simple offences, aiding, abetting, instigation, complaint]

Covers: s73 Accessories (Justices Act 1959 s73).

73. Accessories

      (1) Subject to any contrary intention in the Act creating the offence, where a simple offence is committed, each of the following persons is deemed to be a party to, and to be guilty of, the offence, and may be charged with actually committing it, namely:

(a) a person who actually commits the offence;

(b) a person who does any act or makes any omission for the purpose of enabling or aiding another person to commit the offence;

(c) a person who abets another person in committing the offence;

(d) a person who instigates another person to commit the offence.

   

(2) A person who instigates another person to do an act or make an omission of such a nature that, if he had himself done the act or made the omission, the act or omission would have constituted a simple offence on his part, is guilty of the same offence as if he had himself done the act or made the omission, and may be charged with himself committing the offence.

      (3) A person who is alleged to have instigated, aided, or abetted the commission of a simple offence may be convicted upon a complaint charging him with having committed the offence, or upon a complaint charging him with having instigated, aided, or abetted, as the case may be, the commission thereof.

Covers: Code v Common Law - observation on willful blindness and knowledge.

CODE v COMMON LAW

There are few, if any, distinctions to be drawn between the Code approach and that of the Common Law. The only unsettled question in both jurisdictions appears to be the role and function of 'willful blindness' in regard to knowledge when helping an offender.

If unsure, can declare unsure at outset and that court can find guilty on either basis -- but must be declared at beginning. ### Chunk 7
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: AID OR ABET - definitions and requirements (Giorgianni; Gray)] [CASE: Giorgianni (1985) 156 CLR 47; Gray (1917) 12 Cr App R 244] [CATEGORY: Parties, Accessories and Principles] [TYPE: annotation / case authority] [TOPIC: Definitions of aid and abet and requirements for accessory liability] [CONCEPTS: aid, abet, definitions, Giorgianni, Gray, mens rea, requirements, presence, intention]

Covers: Aid or abet — definitions, key cases, and practitioner-listed requirements.

A person who contributes to the commission of a crime/offence may in certain circumstances be charged as a principal to that crime/offence - [if it can be shown that the contribution was done or made with knowledge, willingness and a desire to assist]{.underline}.

'Aid' is different to 'abet', but they tend to be used interchangeably in many conversations.

To 'aid' in the commission of a crime involves a conscious positive decision to act in a way which is designed, and which will in fact enable another to commit the crime or offence. Abet still requires knowledge of what is to transpire, but describes an equally conscious positive decision to be present and supportive in a manner that encourages the other to proceed with that which is proposed.

It is a well established fact that "...*merely standing by while a crime is committed will not, of itself, establish guilt*" - Gray (1917) 12 Cr App R 244 as cited by Nettlefold J at 4 in *R v Smith* unreported No 4/1979.

***Giorgianni* (1985) 156 CLR 47**

This case, which dealt with a common law situation of a supervisor directing a driver to drive a defective vehicle leading to a crash, makes clear that parties who are said to aid, abet or instigate an offence, must have full "mens rea" (knowledge of what constitutes the offence) and intend by that contribution, support or urging to enable or facilitate the commission of the offence by the principal.

Requirements:

(i) The commission of the offence by the principal offender;

(ii) That the defendant was present at the time when the offence was committed;

(iii) The defendant knew all the essential facts or circumstances that must be established by the prosecution in order to show the offence was committed by the principal offender;

(iv) That, with that knowledge, he intentionally assisted or encouraged the principle to commit that offence. His intention to assist must be based on that knowledge.

### Chunk 8
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Arnol [1980] Tas R 222 - aiding rape principles] [CASE: Arnol [1980] Tas R 222] [CATEGORY: Parties, Accessories and Principles] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: Arnol — jury must be satisfied of aiding elements; meaning of "for the purpose of"] [CONCEPTS: Arnol, aiding, rape, mens rea, intention, "for the purpose of", Everett J]

Covers: Arnol — requirements for conviction for aiding (case quote).

***Arnol* \[1980\] Tas R 222,**

Arnol and six other youths were charged with rape \[Code principles\] and aiding rape arising out of the pack rape of a sixteen-year-old girl. The aiding rape allegation was particularised as holding the victim while others raped her.

EVERETT J stated at 228:

1.  The Jury must be satisfied on evidence admissible against the accused charged with aiding that the crime of rape was committed.

2.  The jury must be satisfied that the particular accused did an act intending that it should aid the principal offender to commit the crime-in this case, rape.

EVERETT J Unreported Judgment No 64/1980 at p2) when he said:

"*The third element is that the jury must be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that an accused charged with \'aiding\' did the act alleged intending that it should aid the principal offender named in each count to commit the crime of rape. The words \'for the purpose of\' in s3(1)(b) of the Criminal Code mean \'intended\' or \'designed to\'. The word \'purpose\' means \'the action or fact of intending or meaning to do something\'. It is important that the jury should appreciate that the prosecution does not have to prove whether or not any act it is satisfied was done by a person who is charged under s3(1)(b) in fact aided another person to commit the crime. It is the intention of the alleged \'alder\' which is important. Of course, the jury must be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the crime in respect of which \'aiding\' is alleged was committed*."

### Chunk 9
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Truman v Buxton [2009] TASSC 96 - aiding and abetting analysis and authorities] [CASE: Truman v Buxton [2009] TASSC 96; Randall v R; Farmer v R [2004] TASSC 42; R v Coney & Ors (1882) 8 QBD 534] [CATEGORY: Parties, Accessories and Principles] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: Truman v Buxton — distinguishing mere presence from aiding/abetting; factual analysis] [CONCEPTS: Truman v Buxton, aiding, abetting, mere presence, Randall v R, R v Coney, intent to encourage, factual participation]

Covers: Truman v Buxton — aiding/abetting principles and factual application.

**Truman v Buxton \[2009\] TASSC 96**

The defendant was convicted of stealing as an aider by being in company with some males who attended two bottleshops whilst they stole some alcohol. The conviction was appealed by defence counsel.

At 12 Tennet J stated;

In *Randall v R; Farmer v R* \[2004\] TASSC 42 at par40, Cox CJ dealt with the concepts of aiding and of abetting. He said:

"40 The concepts of aiding and of abetting, as the learned trial judge acknowledged, can overlap. In respect of aiding, the *Criminal Code, s3,* requires that there be an act done or an omission made for the purpose of enabling or aiding the principal offender to commit the crime. Abetting requires not merely wilful encouragement by the abettor, but the communication of that encouragement to the offender with the consequence that the offender is in fact encouraged. In either case, some active step must be taken with intent to bring about the commission of the crime by the principal offender. In most cases of aiding, some physical act or deliberate omission can be readily identified and in many cases of abetting, the communication of encouragement may take an identifiable physical form such as gestures or spoken words.

In *R v Coney & Ors* (1882) 8 QBD 534, Hawkins J said at 557 - 558:

'In my opinion, to constitute an aider and abettor some active steps must be taken by word, or action, with the intent to instigate the principal, or principals. Encouragement does not of necessity amount to aiding and abetting, it may be intentional or unintentional, a man may unwittingly encourage another in fact by his presence, by misinterpreted words, or gestures, or by his silence, on non-interference, or he may encourage intentionally by expressions, gestures, or actions intended to signify approval. In the latter case he aids and abets, in the former he does not. It is no criminal offence to stand by, a mere passive spectator of a crime, even of a murder. Non-interference to prevent a crime is not itself a crime. But the fact that a person was voluntarily and purposely present witnessing the commission of a crime, and offered no opposition to it, though he might reasonably be expected to prevent and had the power so to do, or at least to express his dissent, might under some circumstances, afford cogent evidence upon which a jury would be justified in finding that he wilfully encouraged and so aided and abetted.'

Mere presence, even if it has the effect of encouraging the principal offender to commit the crime and is accompanied on the part of the accomplice by an intention to render assistance, if required, is not sufficient (*R v Jones & Mirrless* (1977) 65 Cr App R 250 at 252; *Roughley v R* (supra) at 12)."

At 18 Tennet J stated;

This was clearly, on the material before the learned magistrate, not a case of just mere presence of the applicant. It involved his travelling in a car for some distance with the other three males, and knowing they were going to go into bottle shops and steal alcohol. It involved the applicant actually going into the two bottle shops with the other three males, standing nearby in one case in full view while the youth or youths physically took the alcohol and concealed it, remaining with them at Burnie when one of the youths activated an alarm, and then, in each case, leaving the bottle shops with the males and getting into a car with them and the stolen alcohol. The finding by the learned magistrate that the applicant actively participated in these thefts, as opposed to his simply being incidentally present, was, in my view, open to him on the evidence.

Shaw v TSCCA 22 -- at para \[70\] *There is one situation in which a person who intentionally offers encouragement to another person to commit a crime might not be guilty as an abettor. If the person who actually commits the crime is so intent upon committing it that he or she derives no encouragement from the person offering encouragement, then the person offering encouragement will not be deemed guilty as an abettor. Even if the two individuals have reached an understanding that the crime is to be committed, and both are present when it is committed, the person who does nothing but offer unnecessary encouragement will not incur criminal liability pursuant to s 3(1)(c).*

*R v Mundy* A58/1966

This is a case where a male was convicted of setting fires as he was in company with another male who during the day drove about setting multiple fires. The defendant was found to be guilty of aiding in the committing of all the offences other than the first one, as he was not aware of what the primary offender was going to do, but after that he was aware and was found to have aided him.

<!-- block_019 master -->

# Joint criminal enterprise and party liability
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: joint criminal enterprise / common purpose liability] [CONCEPTS: joint criminal enterprise, common purpose, mutual embarkation, party liability, joint venture, accomplice liability, common intention, joint liability, shared unlawful purpose, objective probability test]

The common law principle of "joint criminal enterprise" holds that parties may be found guilty where the prosecution proves beyond reasonable doubt that the parties had reached an understanding to commit a crime and that the crime was committed in accordance with that understanding, in which case each party is responsible for what the others did. This doctrine imputes liability to participants who share a common purpose and either act together or have agreed that the crime be committed. At common law, liability can arise from active participation or, in some circumstances, from mere presence where presence is intended to encourage the principal offender. Tasmania's statutory scheme (notably Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 3 and 4) overlaps with, but also departs from, common law joint enterprise principles by defining statutory modes of participation and by prescribing an objective "probable consequence" test for crimes committed in prosecution of a common unlawful purpose.

### Chunk 2 — Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 - Definition of "abets" and s 3(1) discussion
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 (7 March 2022)] [CITATION: Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 - Abets and s 3(1)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: definition of "abets", s 3(1)(b)/(c) aiding and abetting] [CONCEPTS: abets, encourages, s 3(1)(c), aiding, mere presence, abettor liability, Hutt v The Queen, Giorgianni]

This chunk covers the definition of "abets" and the operation of s 3(1) as discussed in Shaw v Tasmania.

Definition of abets:

[18]  

*In our s 3(1)(c), "abets" means "encourages":  It is possible for a person to abet the commission of an offence merely by being present at the place where it is committed:  Encouraging the commission of an offence in that way has been regarded in Queensland as aiding the commission of the offence.*

A person who intentionally assists the offender who commits a crime would be guilty of that crime under s 3(1)(b) of the Code as a "person who does any act or makes any omission for the purpose of enabling or aiding another person to commit the crime". A person who intentionally encourages an offender to actually commit a crime can be guilty by virtue of s 3(1)(c) as a "person who abets another person in committing the crime"

69 A person who, at common law, would be regarded as a party to a joint criminal enterprise, but who does not actively take part in that enterprise, might incur criminal liability as an abettor pursuant to s 3(1)(c). A person who is merely present at the scene of a crime will incur criminal liability as an abettor if he or she intends, by mere presence, to encourage the offender and if the offender is in fact encouraged by that person's mere presence: *Hutt v The Queen* (above) at [51] per Underwood J (as he then was), with whom Cox and Crawford JJ (as they then were) agreed.

---

### Chunk 3 — Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 - s 3(1)(a), s 4 interpretation and joint criminal enterprise (probable consequence)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 (7 March 2022)] [CITATION: Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 - s4 probable consequence test] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: s 3(1)(a) scope; s 4 mutual embarkation and probable consequence test] [CONCEPTS: s 3(1)(a), s 4, probable consequence, common intention, mutual embarkation, joint criminal enterprise, jury directions]

This chunk covers the Court of Criminal Appeal's discussion of s 3(1)(a) scope and the interpretation of s 4 regarding mutual embarkation and probable consequence.

[76] In our view it is clear that the words "every person who actually commits the crime" in s 3(1)(a) must be given their ordinary literal meaning, and must not be interpreted as including every person present when a crime is committed who has reached an understanding or arrangement with a person who actually commits the crime that it will be committed. In other words, s 3(1)(a) is not to be interpreted as somehow restating the common law as to the criminal liability of parties to joint criminal enterprises. The Crown's contentions as to the scope of s 3(1)(a) must be rejected. The learned trial judge directed the jury on an erroneous basis.

Section 4:

Not so limited as to apply only to offence b when intent was to commit offence a. "a" can be said to be a probable conseuqnece of intending to commit "a"!

[86]T... The foundation of criminal responsibility under s 4 is the "mutual embarkation on a crime". The section establishes the ambit of criminal responsibility by participants while carrying out a common unlawful purpose. In contrast to the common law, the Code defines this ambit by requiring a connection of objective probability between the common purpose and the committed crime.

[83] *Interpreted in this way, criminal responsibility will arise when:*

*(a) two or more persons form a common intention to prosecute an unlawful purpose in conjunction with one another, and*

*(b) a crime is committed in the prosecution of such unlawful purpose, and*

*(c) the crime committed is of such a nature that its commission is a probable consequence of the prosecution of the unlawful purpose.*

*83 Provided the requisite connection is established between the unlawful purpose and the crime, there is nothing in the text of the provision which suggests that its operation should be limited by excluding crimes which are included within the intended scope of the unlawful purpose. Obviously, the provision extends the ambit of responsibility beyond this, by applying an objective test of probable consequence. However, this prescribes the outer reach of the provision. It does not expressly or impliedly exclude intended crimes. Self-evidently, a crime which is intended will meet the objective test of "probable consequence".*

[87] "...The criminal responsibility of the person should be determined by application of the test prescribed by s 4 according to its terms. Under that test, a person will be responsible for any crime committed in the prosecution of the common purpose, provided that the crime is of such a nature that its commission was a probable consequence of such prosecution, including those where the test is satisfied because the crime was expressly agreed or contemplated by the common purpose. The intention of the provision is to attribute criminal responsibility to all such crimes, on the basis of their connection to the prosecution of the unlawful purpose, irrespective of the contribution actually made by the person as defined in s 3. The section defines the limits of criminal responsibility arising from the prosecution of common purpose. There is no justification for excluding crimes which fall within that ambit from attribution of criminal responsibility."

100 As already noted, a consequence of the wider interpretation is the potential for considerable overlap between ss 3 and 4, because in cases in which the crime committed is that which is intended by the participants, criminal responsibility will almost certainly arise under both provisions. We see no difficulty either in principle or in practical application with this outcome. In fact, we would consider it to be the expected and appropriate effect of provisions which attribute criminal responsibility to those complicit in a crime on more than one basis. Certainly, under s 3 it is common for criminal responsibility to arise under more than one provision. We accept that there is a practical danger that relying on both provisions in every case to which both may apply on the facts, could lead to unnecessarily complicated directions, and thereby create confusion for juries. Any such problem is capable of amelioration by the exercise of appropriate discretion on the part of the prosecution. As a matter of practice, resort should only be had by prosecutors to s 4 in cases where, on the facts, there may realistically be a need to rely on the extended ambit of the provision in order to attribute criminal responsibility, or where there is clear evidence of a preformed common unlawful purpose, which is additional to inferences drawn from the conduct of the parties in the commission of the crime. In any event, practical issues concerning the potential for overlap of the provisions will not "deny their separate utility".

108 In relation to the arson charge, the Crown bore the burden of proving beyond reasonable doubt that the fire was deliberately lit, that it was lit with an intention to set fire to the building, and that the appellant was criminally responsible for the commission of the crime.

### Chunk 5 — Particularisation and R v Chong [2012] QCA 265 — particulars and duplicity example
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: R v Chong [2012] QCA 265 (28 September 2012)] [CITATION: R v Chong [2012] QCA 265 - particulars and duplicity] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: particulars, duplicity, election, assault particulars] [CONCEPTS: particulars, duplicity, election, assault, threat vs throw, trial judge error, Holmes JA, Chong]

This chunk covers the R v Chong decision and practitioner takeaways on particulars and how a single act may be particularised.

Can particulairse different ways a single act may amount to an assault:

The original charge as particularised by the Crown was: "the assault as one where the appellant "threw, or threatened to throw, a rock or piece of concrete" in the direction of the complainant police officer."

The trial judge said this was duplicitious and that they had to narrow the particulars or lay two discreet charges as there was pne more than one act involved; one for threaten and one for attempt but if only a single act were involved, it was incumbent on the Crown to elect whether it would proceed on the basis that the appellant threw the rock, or that she threatened to throw it, since either would constitute an assault.  As a result, the Crown provided amended particulars, alleging that the appellant "threw a rock or piece of concrete" in the direction of the complainant police officer.  The trial then proceeded on that basis.

Blah blah blah some other stuff, but in the end on appeal they held that the trial judge had made a mistake in forcing the crown to amend their particuylars.  

"When assessing whether a miscarriage of justice had occurred because the Crown was permitted to depart from the particulars of the charge it had given at the start of the trial, Holmes JA first observed it was not necessary for the Crown to have made the election it did. Her Honour noted the case was not one of latent ambiguity in which there was more than one occasion which might be embraced by the count and the particulars given of it. Rather, it involved a single course of conduct and the only conflict in the evidence was as to its extent: whether the appellant's conduct was simply running in the direction of the complainant with a rock held over her head, or whether it included the further step of actually throwing it at the complainant. Her Honour considered the first scenario could properly be characterised as a threat to apply force within the meaning of s [245](https://jade.io/article/529633/section/1064) of the [*Criminal Code*](https://jade.io/article/529633), and the second as an attempt to do so.[^[7]^](https://jade.io/#_ftn7)"

Liability -- without prejudice basis anticipate blade and kid as principles.

Not set in stone -preliminary assessment.

### Chunk 6 — B v Visser — "wilfully", aiding/abetting, and s 4 alternative basis
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: B v Visser] [CITATION: B v Visser - wilfully, aiding and abetting, s 4 alternative] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: wilfully, recklessness, aiding/abetting, s 4 common intention] [CONCEPTS: wilfully, recklessly, arson, aiding and abetting, knowledge of essential facts, Giorgianni, common intention, s 4]

This chunk covers B v Visser excerpts on "wilfully", aiding or abetting, and the possible reliance on s 4.

B v Visser

[2] "Wilfully" -- includes recklessly so that arson may be committed if the accused deliberately does a willed act, aware that setting fire to a particular building is a likely consequence of that act, and if he or she recklessly does that act, regardless of that risk: *R v Hodgson* [1985] Tas R 75; *R v Gardenal-Williams* [1989] Tas R 62.

[4] To find guilty by aiding or abetting: it is necessary for the prosecution to prove that he knew all the essential facts which made his companion's act a crime, and that he intentionally aided or abetted his companion's act of "posting" the burning paper: *Giorgianni v R* (1985) 156 CLR 473. It was thus necessary for the prosecution to prove that the applicant knew his companion was contemplating putting a burning paper into the letterbox, and either knew that his companion intended thereby to set the building on fire or knew that his companion was aware of the likelihood of that consequence and recklessly indifferent to it. Thus one of the facts the prosecution had to prove was that the applicant, at the time of lighting the paper, knew of the likelihood that putting the burning paper in the letterbox could result in the building catching fire. Little turns on the point that it was knowledge of the likelihood of that consequence, as distinct from foresight of the likelihood of that consequence, that needed to be proved in relation to the applicant.

[5] The Code, s4, may have provided another basis for the prosecution case, quite apart from aiding and abetting. There was a live issue as to whether the evidence was sufficient to establish that the applicant joined his companion in forming a common intention to prosecute an unlawful purpose by putting the burning paper in the letterbox, or whether he was indifferent as to whether his companion would prosecute that purpose, and thus did not share any common intention.

### Chunk 8 — Section 50. Domestic discipline (Lane v James principles)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: 50] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 50 - Domestic discipline principles] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine / annotation] [TOPIC: s 50 domestic discipline; reasonable force by parent] [CONCEPTS: domestic discipline, reasonable force, parent, Lane v James, proportionality, age and physique, means of punishment]

This chunk covers s 50 (Domestic discipline) and the three principles from Lane v James.

This chunk covers Section 50 Domestic discipline and principles from Lane v James.

Section 50. Domestic discipline

It is lawful for a parent or a person in the place of a parent to use, by way of correction, any force towards a child in his or her care that is reasonable in the circumstances.

Three principles (from Lane v James [2010] TASMC 19)

- Punishment must be moderate and reasonable

- Must have a proper relationship to the age, physique and mentality of the child

- The punishment must be carried out with a reasonable means or instrument

### Chunk 10 — Attempts: Acts Interpretation s 34; Criminal Code s 299; R v Hodgson and alternative convictions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Acts Interpretation Act 1931 (Tas)] [SECTION: 34] [CITATION: Acts Interpretation Act 1931 s 34 & Criminal Code Act 1924 s 299 - Attempts] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine / case authority] [TOPIC: attempts to commit offences; required intention; alternative convictions] [CONCEPTS: attempt, Acts Interpretation s34, Criminal Code s299, R v Hodgson, intention to cause consequence, alternative conviction, s74 Justices Act, s342 Criminal Code]

This chunk covers statutory provisions on attempts and related case-law.

This chunk covers statutory provisions and case law concerning attempts to commit offences.

**ACTS INTERPRETATION ACT 1931 - SECT 34**

**34. Attempt to commit any offence to be an offence**

Where by any Act an offence is constituted, any attempt to commit such offence shall be an offence and be punishable in the same manner as the offence constituted by such Act.

**CRIMINAL CODE ACT 1924 -- SECT 299**

**299.**   **Attempts to commit crimes**

Any person who attempts to commit a crime is guilty of a crime.

Charge:  Attempting to commit [*specify particular crime*].

*R v Hodgson* [1985] TASSC 40

91 Attempts to commit a crime require an actual intention to bring about the proscribed consequence (*R v Bell *[(1972) Tas SR 127](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281972%29%20Tas%20SR%20127?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=arson) and *Haas v The Queen* [(1964) Tas SR 1).](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281964%29%20Tas%20SR%201?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=arson) In the same way it seems to me that Parliament might consistently require that the doing of potentially injurious acts not in fact causing injury should be accompanied by a like intention. One imagines that these acts are prohibited if accompanied by such an intention because they may not be sufficient in law to constitute an attempt to commit some crime involving actual damage by virtue of their not having gone beyond the merely preparatory stage, and yet obviously it is highly threatening to society, that such acts, if done with an intention to cause injury, should be tolerated.

Alternative convictions for attempts where actual not proven

Section 74 Justices Act 1959 for dimple offences

Section 342 Criminal Code Act 1924 for indictable

### Chunk 14 — Bail Hearing flow chart (after-hours court procedural workflow)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Bail hearing flow chart - after-hours court procedure] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: bail hearing workflow; after-hours court introduction; opposing bail submissions] [CONCEPTS: bail hearing, after-hours court, read charges, IFVO, opposition basis, prosecution submissions, tend priors, closing submissions, custody remand]

Tactical workflow for conducting and opposing a bail hearing (after-hours court flow chart).

### **Introduction:**

If an after Hours Court, introduce yourself to the JP. State the date and time and introduce the defendant by name and DOB

Ask the defendant to stand -- and identify him by asking him if he is x

- Read the charges in full

- Make application for IFVO if there is a FVO apo

*"There is an application for a family violence order before you, and the Prosecution seek that on an interim basis until the resolution of these matters."*

### **State the basis of the opposition:**

**Concern that def will not turn up to court to answer the charge**

"The defendant has a demonstrable tendency to avoid/ disregard / and or frustrate the criminal justice process".

**Contrary to the public interest in that the defendant is likely to continue offending**

There is a strong public interest in preventing the defendant from committing further serious offending. The defendant was granted the benefit of bail

**Section 12 Family Violence Act**

*"The court cannot be satisfied that the defendant's release would not be adverse to the safety, wellbeing and interests of the person to be protected"*

**[Remember s 12 *FV Act* for FV matters, and s 35(2) *Justices Act* for RO matters, the onus is reversed.]{.mark}**

### **The "current" matter that sees them before the court today:**

Advise court of the incident which brings the def before the court mention the nature of the charges (if not done already, and read the facts for prosecutor

Tender photos or other evidence available

- Make submission as to strength of the case

- The seriousness of the matter and the likelihood of conviction

- Consider likely period of incarceration if convicted against likely period of remand

<!-- -->

- Given the above, what incentive does he have not appear on the next occasion???

### **Circumstances of arrest**

Be sure to highlight any aggravating circumstances surrounding the def's apprehension, such as trying to evade arrest

- Was he on the run?

- Did he cooperate with Police?

- Does the defendant appreciate the severity of the situation? Or make threats?

I.e. Defendant says in interview or in an afterhours court that they have done nothing wrong, or that their conduct shouldn't/ doesn't amount to a crime/offence -- anything to demonstrate the person does not appreciate the significance of the allegation, and their inability to see it as offending behavior

**[This is particularly important for Section 12 as "*Demeanor*" is a legislative consideration.]{.mark}**

**Consider child/ren at scene:**

- Was there a child present? Could they hear? Were they subject to the consequences of the offending (i.e. seeing the parents in a state of distress?)

*"Master, x was born on the 8^th^ of November 2011"*

- He is said to have been present at both family Violence incidents

- On both occasions witnesses have reported that he was screaming and crying

Under section 13 sub-s (a) the court may consider the defendants may consider the offences to be aggravated.

### **Pending matters:**

Advise the court of any other pending matters the def may be facing (see CRIMES)

Read the facts of those if FV matters

Conclude*: It is for these reasons, your honor, that I submit that the defendant should be remanded in custody.*

### **Tender Priors:**

If a defendant refuses to accept priors, seek to have them remanded until certificates of conviction can be obtained from relevant courts

- Identify those relevant for the opposition

- Identify similar parties, such as previous FV victims

- Identify any breaches which may arise if conviction follows (i.e. Sus-S or UT)

- Remember -- breaches of court disqualifications still amount to breaches of court orders!!!

### **Closing Submission examples:**

*The family violence creates a presumption against bail. The onus is on the respondent to displace the presumption*

*The history of his breaches should leave no confidence that he will comply with the conditions of bail he submits would protect the complainant,*

*There are no grounds upon which the defendant can assure you that the existence of a family violence order would have any effect*

- *He has a history of violence*

- *A history of non-compliance with authorities,*

- *He has displayed a disregard for the law and contempt for the force of court orders.*

- *He has not been deterred by sentences of actual and suspended imprisonment*

- *The history of his breaches gives me no confidence that he will comply with the conditions of bail he says will protect the complainant.*

- *the respondent's release on bail would pose a continuing risk to the complainant and her children.*

*And it is for these reasons, and those made, your honor, that I submit that the defendant should be remanded in custody pending determination.*

If I can be of no further assistance to the court, that concludes the Prosecution's submissions.

---

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas); Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act 1995 (Tas)] [SECTION: Justices Act ss 34, 34A, 35; Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act s 4] [CITATION: Police bail statutory provisions - Justices Act & CDIA 1995 s4] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine / annotation] [TOPIC: police bail statutory provisions; requirement to bring accused before justice] [CONCEPTS: police bail, Justices Act s34, s34A, s35, CLDIA 1995 s4, bring accused before a justice, after police denial]

Police Bail: 

*Justices Act* 1959, ss 34, 34A & 35

If denied by Police, s4 of the *Criminal Law (Detention and Interrogation) Act* 1995 applies and requires the police to bring the accused before a justice in a reasonable time.

### Chunk 16 — Relevant considerations when opposing bail (checklist)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Bail opposition considerations - checklist] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: factors to consider when opposing bail] [CONCEPTS: bail considerations, attendance likelihood, public interest, priors, employment, property, witness interference, strength of case, severity of penalty]

This chunk covers a checklist of considerations when opposing bail.

This chunk covers considerations for opposing bail and the principle question.

Relevant considerations when opposing bail 

Save for s. 12, principle question is: ***whether there is a reasonable likelihood that the accused will be present at the hearing***. Bail is not to restrict lifestyle pending hearing or to impose punishments, but serves the purpose of non-interference with witnesses and the ***securing of attendance at trial***.

- Public interest

- Family ties

- Employment

- Property

- Prior Criminal record

- History of turning up or not turning up to court

- The probability of conviction or strength of the evidence Strength of the prosecution case

- Severity of the penalty that may be imposed.

- Likelihood of D interfering with witness

- Whether remaining in custody will prejudice their case

---

<!-- block_020 master -->

# Bail considerations — Tasmanian criminal law
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: overview of bail considerations] [CONCEPTS: bail factors, public interest, risk of reoffending, conditions, surety, family violence bail]

Bail decision-making in Tasmania requires balancing multiple considerations including the public interest in preventing further offending, the safety and welfare of affected persons, the accused's likelihood of attending court, the strength of the case, and the need for conditions to manage identified risks. Family violence matters attract a statutory presumption against bail under the Family Violence Act s 12, and the court must treat each bail application on its own materials. Relevant practical considerations include prior breaches of bail, prior convictions, ties to the jurisdiction, accommodation, demeanour, and the potential for witness tampering. The court may impose conditions tailored to the specific risks identified; general or standard conditions cannot simply be applied without linking them to the discrete risk the condition addresses.

<!-- block_020 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Public interest (prevention of further offending; Burton; Appleby & McMahon)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Public interest - Burton & Appleby considerations] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Public interest in bail decisions; preventing further offending] [CONCEPTS: public interest, prevention, bail, re-offending, Burton, Appleby, serious offending, risk assessment, antecedents, safety]

This chunk covers: public interest and whether the accused is likely to commit other offences (Burton); how this informs bail decisions.

"Public interest" and whether the accused is likely to commit other offences (*Burton*)

Where def breach bail on serious matters by committing other serious matters. The public interest is in preventing the def's very high risk (need to demonstrate there is a basis for this) from committing further serious offending.

- *Burton* notes that this factor shouldn't be considered with too much weight.

- Whether the accused has committed offences while on bail before see *R v Appleby & McMahon* (1966) 1 NSWR 35.

The second consideration is the safety of the public and the security of its property "public interest". Subsidiary to this are the character and antecedents of the accused, including any record, particularly any record of offences while on bail. If the offence presently charged had been committed while on bail the strength of the case becomes a factor in this assessment.

### Chunk 2 — Offences on bail (questions checklist)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Offences on bail - checklist] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Questions to assess risk where offences committed on bail] [CONCEPTS: offences on bail, checklist, prior breaches, violent offences, home invasions, criminal history, risk, propensity, bail refusal]

This chunk covers: questions and observations to consider where the defendant committed offences while on bail.

- Was the defendant on bail at the time of offending making it suggestive that he will continue?

- Does he have convictions for similar offending?

- Are they violent or abhorrent offences?

- Offences included breaking into people's homes at night?

- Extent of criminal history

*\*

*Victorian R v Gray* \[1977\] VR 225 at 230

It has been stated that "the real point is that the commission of offences whilst on bail indicates contempt for or disregard of the system of law under which bail was granted to the offender suggesting the offender has small regard for the law and little intention of obeying its commands."

### Chunk 4 — Sowerby v State of Tasmania [2024] TASFC 7 (Full Court decision; paras 15-16 and observations)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Sowerby v State of Tasmania [2024] TASFC 7] [CITATION: Sowerby v State of Tasmania 2024 - Full Court bail decision and commentary] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Full Court guidance on bail in family violence context; presumption and fresh consideration] [CONCEPTS: Sowerby, family violence, bail, fresh consideration, presumption, paragraph 15, paragraph 16, surety, breach, judicial reasoning]

This chunk covers: the Full Court's treatment of bail in a family violence context and the principle that each s.12 application is a fresh consideration.

Magistrate fucked up, applying a made up test and determining: "On balance, I'm not satisfied that there are compelling circumstances or a carefully structured plan to secure the safety, wellbeing and interest of the affected person, so bail is refused."

But SC also held that bail was appropriate.

They did note, that the fact a defendant has previously been granted bail is not the point. It is a fresh consideration each time s. 12 arises.

[15] *The appellant intends to defend the indictable assault charge. He is entitled to the presumption of innocence although in assessing the risk he may pose to Ms Williams-Jacques it was proper to take into account the possibility that the allegation may be true. There is likely to be a very substantial delay before the charge is brought to trial. Every instance of violence committed within a relationship or former relationship is to be regarded seriously. However, on the limited information available to this Court, the objective gravity of the crime alleged against the appellant and the absence of a serious record for violent offending meant that there was a real risk of injustice if he were remanded in custody, even if found guilty. Assuming conviction, it is reasonably possible that without bail he would remain in custody awaiting trial for a period well beyond any sentence of imprisonment reasonably proportionate to his crimes. Sometimes that is not an answer to the matters which s 12 requires be addressed. The provision, and the presumption against bail it contains, must be given effect according to its terms. There are very frequently occasions where protection of the safety, wellbeing and interests of an affected person or affected child requires remand of an accused person in custody for a period, sometimes a very lengthy period, even at the risk of injustice. However, in our view, this was not such a case.*

*[16] We concluded that, with the imposition of appropriate bail conditions, release of the appellant on bail would not be likely to adversely affect the safety, wellbeing and interests of Ms Williams-Jacques. Whilst on bail between 27 March 2024 and 24 September 2024 the appellant did not breach the terms of his bail or the police family violence order. Nor had he done any other act which suggested a substantial risk to the complainant. The appellant spent six days in custody following his arrest on 21 March 2024 and has been in custody since his arrest on 24 September 2024. Having spent more than seven weeks in custody he must be acutely aware of the likely consequences of future breach and has a strong incentive to comply. Mr Churchill impressed us as a person who will, as a surety, act conscientiously. He has the means to meet the recognizance if called upon and will be in a good position to monitor and supervise the appellant. The appellant will have an additional incentive to comply by reason of his awareness that any breach may mean that Mr Churchill, through no fault of his own, may forfeit a substantial sum.*
