# Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Dismissal
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: 453; 452F] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Dismissal as youth sentence] [CONCEPTS: dismissal, youth sentencing, no conviction recorded, conditional discharge, Youth Justice Act]

Dismissal under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) is a sentencing outcome in which a young person is found guilty but the charge is dismissed and no further sentence is ordered. The source materials state that dismissal is the least severe sentencing option and that where dismissal is ordered a conviction cannot be recorded (references given as 453 and 452F in the source text). The practical effect is that the youth is formally found guilty but discharged without any additional orders; there is no recorded conviction on the youth's record under the legislative scheme referenced in the source. This remedy is designed to allow the court to acknowledge guilt while avoiding the consequences of a recorded conviction, consistent with youth-specific sentencing objectives such as rehabilitation and protection of future prospects.

# Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Reprimand
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: 455] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Reprimand as youth sentence] [CONCEPTS: reprimand, formal warning, dismissal, no conviction, youth sentencing]

A reprimand under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) is a sentencing disposition in which a youth is found guilty, the charge is dismissed, and the youth is formally reprimanded. The court issues a formal warning but, as with dismissal, a conviction cannot be recorded. The reprimand functions as a stronger censure than a plain dismissal while still avoiding recording a conviction; it is used to denounce the conduct and to warn the young person about future behaviour without imposing custodial or recorded sanctions.

# Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Dismissal with an undertaking to be of good behaviour
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Undertaking to be of good behaviour] [CONCEPTS: undertaking, good behaviour, conditional release, unsupervised release, youth sentencing]

A dismissal with an undertaking to be of good behaviour is a form of conditional, unsupervised release for a youth under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas). The rule is that the court dismisses the charge conditional upon the youth giving an undertaking to be of good behaviour for a stated period. The undertaking is an agreement by the youth to comply with standards of conduct; if breached, the court may recall the youth to impose a new order. This disposition seeks to balance leniency and community protection by placing a behavioural expectation on the youth without imposing custodial or supervised orders unless compliance fails.

# Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Release and adjournment with conditions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Adjournment with conditions] [CONCEPTS: adjournment, conditional release, 12 months, deferred sentencing, discharge on compliance]

Release and adjournment with conditions permits the court to release the youth and postpone sentencing for a nominated period (the source states no more than 12 months) on conditions set by the court. The first sentence of this chunk states the rule: the youth is released and sentencing is adjourned subject to specified conditions for the adjournment period. If, at the conclusion of the adjourned period, the youth has complied with the conditions, the youth is discharged from the order and no further sentence is imposed. If the youth fails to comply, the youth may be recalled to court and the court may impose a new order. As with other youth dispositions, the source notes that a conviction cannot be recorded where the adjournment results in discharge.

# Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Fine (youth sentencing)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Fine as youth sentence] [CONCEPTS: fine, monetary penalty, youth sentencing, non-custodial sanction, Youth Justice Act]

A fine is an available sentencing option for young offenders under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas). The source lists "Fine" as a distinct sanction option but provides no further elaboration in the uploaded part. The basic rule is that the court may impose a monetary penalty appropriate to the offending and the youth's circumstances; practical use in youth contexts is constrained by principles favouring rehabilitation, proportionality, and the youth's ability to pay. (Note: the source contains only the header "Fine" without statutory cross-reference or particulars in this part.)

# Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Probation order
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: 469F; 470F; 471; 472] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Probation orders for youth] [CONCEPTS: probation, reporting, special conditions, counselling, curfew, youth justice worker]

A probation order under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) requires the youth to report to youth justice workers and to comply with any special conditions the court attaches. The court can require attendance at school or educational programs, counselling or treatment, abstinence from alcohol or controlled substances, submitting to testing for substances or alcohol, residence at a specified address, and compliance with curfews. The source references sections or provisions numbered 469F, 470F, 471 and 472 in relation to reporting and conditions. Probation is a supervised, non-custodial order aimed principally at rehabilitation, with enforceable requirements to promote compliance and community protection.

# Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) — Community service order
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Community service for youth] [CONCEPTS: community service, non-custodial, reparation, youth sentencing, supervision]

A community service order is a sentencing option under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) that requires a youth to perform a specified number of hours of unpaid work for the community. The source lists "Community service order" as a discrete sanction but does not include statutory cross-references or elaboration in this part. The order is typically used as a restorative, non-custodial sanction intended to promote accountability, community reparation, and the rehabilitation of the young offender.

# Youth sentencing principle — Detention as a last resort and the role of deterrence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Detention as last resort; rehabilitation vs deterrence] [CONCEPTS: last resort, detention, rehabilitation, general deterrence, personal deterrence, serious offending]

Detention is regarded as a last resort under the youth sentencing framework contemplated by the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas). The central rule is that rehabilitation is usually the primary or dominant consideration when sentencing youthful offenders, but both general and personal deterrence — and denunciation and just punishment — can be relevant, particularly for serious offending. The source references a line of authority (including DPP v NOP and Director of Public Prosecutions v JSP) and notes judicial recognition of tensions between rehabilitation and deterrence; Estcourt J is quoted emphasising rehabilitation's primacy but acknowledging occasions when general deterrence and just punishment become at least equally important. The practical effect is that courts tailor sentences to emphasise welfare and rehabilitation while reserving detention where necessary to protect the community or to achieve deterrence in serious cases.

# Youth sentencing — Repeat offending submissions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: Repeat offending and character submissions] [CONCEPTS: repeat offending, pattern of behaviour, not out of character, aggravation, sentencing submissions]

A prosecution or submission in youth sentencing concerning repeat offending may assert that a youth cannot claim the offences were out of character or a youthful aberration where the offending represents a continuation of consistent, significant and similar offending over a period. The rule as stated in the source is that prior consistent offending in the preceding year may be relied upon to rebut claims of aberration and to inform the court's assessment of the appropriate sentence, including the need for more restrictive or deterrent measures.

# Family Violence — Strangulation (section 170B referenced)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 170B] [TYPE: offence] [TOPIC: Strangulation elements and mens rea] [CONCEPTS: strangulation, choking, suffocation, specific intent, unlawful act, neck pressure, ordinary meaning, family violence]

Strangulation (referenced as section 170B in the source text) is an offence requiring proof that the accused intentionally caused choking, suffocation or strangulation of another person. The core rule stated is that the crime is one of specific intent: the Crown must prove the accused intended to commit the act (the actus reus being choking/suffocating/strangling). The source notes that these acts are not expressly defined in the legislation, so the words carry their ordinary meanings, which evolve with community expectations (source cites second reading speech 10 March 2022). Strangulation covers external pressure to the neck from any source and requires intentional conduct; evidence of injury may be rare in such cases, so absence of visible injury does not preclude the offence.

# Family Violence — Disabling (section 168) and suffocation (comparative notes)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 168] [TYPE: offence / element of offence] [TOPIC: Disabling and suffocation in family violence context] [CONCEPTS: disabling, suffocation, incapacity to flee, blunt force not necessary, pressure to chest, laying on chest]

Section 168 (referenced in the source) concerning disabling conduct covers rendering someone unable to flee. Suffocation is conceptually distinct from strangulation and may have a lower threshold: it can result from pressure to the chest, laying on the chest, or other acts that impede breathing without necessarily applying pressure to the neck. Blunt force is not required for suffocation; relatively light but obstructive pressure may suffice. The source highlights that disabling/suffocation and strangulation are related but distinct offences or modes of harm within family violence jurisprudence.

# Family Violence — Evidentiary note: injury often absent in strangulation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: Lack of physical injury in strangulation prosecutions] [CONCEPTS: visible injury rare, forensic evidence, marks on neck, medical evidence, circumstantial evidence]

The rule to note is that evidence of physical injury is often rare in strangulation offences; prosecutions frequently must rely on other forms of evidence (complainant testimony, circumstantial indicators, contemporaneous statements, medical symptoms such as difficulty breathing, voice changes, or internal injuries). The absence of external marks does not preclude a finding of strangulation; courts must assess all available evidence including signs and symptoms described by the victim and any medical or expert evidence.

# Family Violence — Comparative seriousness: strangulation vs assault on pregnant woman (s184A)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 184A] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Comparative seriousness of offences] [CONCEPTS: assault on pregnant woman, strangulation, offence hierarchy, aggravation, conference observations]

The source contains a doctrinal observation from a conference/round-table that strangulation is a more serious crime than assault on a pregnant woman under section 184A, and that assault (generally) is an entry-level charge that can escalate to aggravated assault and beyond. This chunk records the comparative assessment made in the materials: strangulation attracts greater seriousness than certain other family-violence-related assaults, reflecting the higher risk of lethal or disabling harm.

# Special Witness Applications — Evidence support and materials
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Preparing special witness applications] [CONCEPTS: special witness, psychological reports, FVMS history, affidavits, supporting material, prosecutor checklist]

Special witness applications (under the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 and related rules) require adequate supporting material; the source emphasises that reliance on a single psychological report is insufficient and recommends including family-violence management system (FVMS) history, supporting affidavits, and other corroborative materials. A prosecution aide-memoir (Southern Prosecution website) is recommended as a model for the information required to persuade a court to order special measures such as remote audiovisual evidence or intermediaries.

# Rules of court — Justices Rules r5 and Criminal Rules 2006 r41A (preliminary hearings)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Court rules for matters not provided for & preliminary hearings] [CONCEPTS: Justices Rules r5, Criminal Rules r41A, practice of Supreme Court, preliminary hearing, prosecutor assurances, hearsay in prelims]

Justices Rules r5 provides that in any matter not otherwise provided for by those rules, the practice of the Supreme Court is to be followed. Criminal Rules 2006 r41A concerns applications under the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 and provides that, for the purposes of a preliminary hearing under section 9(2) of the Act, a judge may inform themselves in any way they think fit and may accept statements and assurances of the prosecutor without hearing evidence from the child or person who is to give evidence. This rule underpins preliminary applications and permits a flexible fact-finding process at the interlocutory stage.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Preliminary hearing practice (r41A quoted)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Rules 2006 (Tas)] [SECTION: r41A] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Judge may inform self and accept prosecutor assurances] [CONCEPTS: preliminary hearing, inform self, prosecutor assurances, no child evidence required, interlocutory applications]

The Criminal Rules 2006 r41A, quoted in the source, states the procedural rule that for a preliminary hearing under section 9(2) of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 a judge may inform himself or herself in any way they think fit and may accept statements and assurances of the prosecutor without hearing evidence from the child or person who is giving, or is to give, evidence. The practical import is that judges have wide discretion to consider documentary and written material and to make interlocutory decisions without calling vulnerable witnesses to give evidence at that stage.

# Authority - Tasmania v Dolega [2016] TASSC 65
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Dolega [2016] TASSC 65] [TOPIC: Special witness application refused for 16-year-old witness] [CONCEPTS: special witness, preliminary hearing, refusal, child evidence, TASSC authority]

Tasmania v Dolega [2016] TASSC 65 is cited in the source as an authority where the court refused a special witness application; the young person was 16 and wanted to give evidence. The legal principle illustrated by the case is that applications under the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 will be scrutinised carefully and that age alone does not guarantee that special measures will be granted — courts assess likelihood of inability to give evidence satisfactorily or risk of severe emotional trauma on the available material. (See source paragraphs noted: 1–7, 47 onwards.)

# Authority - A v Filz [2003] TASSC 32
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: A v Filz [2003] TASSC 32] [TOPIC: Purpose and interpretation of Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001] [CONCEPTS: purpose of Act, special measures, interpretation, child witness, Tasmanian authority]

A v Filz [2003] TASSC 32 is cited in the source for consideration of the purpose of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001. The legal point drawn is that the Act aims to protect vulnerable witnesses while balancing their protections against the accused's rights; courts consider statutory purpose and legislative intent when deciding whether to grant special measures.

# Authority - Gee v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Gee v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 1] [TOPIC: Special hearing and statutory intentions] [CONCEPTS: special hearing, Act intentions, appellate discussion, prescribed witness, TASCCA]

Gee v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 1 is identified in the source as a authorities concerning special hearings under the Act and includes discussion on the intentions of the legislation. The case is relevant for principles on the statutory scheme for special measures (for example, treatment of prior statements and the role of intermediaries) and provides appellate-level guidance on how the Act's protections should be applied.

# Special witness applications — Information police should establish (s8 grounds)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001] [SECTION: s8] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: s8 grounds for special measures] [CONCEPTS: intellectual disability, age, cultural background, intimidation, severe emotional trauma, balance of probabilities]

The statutory grounds required for special witness measures are set out in section 8 of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 and, as stated in the source, require establishment that: (a) by reason of intellectual, mental or physical disability the person is, or is likely to be, unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the ordinary manner; or (b) by reason of age, cultural background, relationship to any party, the nature of the subject matter or any other relevant factor, the person is likely either (i) to suffer severe emotional trauma, or (ii) to be so intimidated or distressed as to be unable to give evidence or to give evidence satisfactorily. The onus is on the prosecution, but the standard is the balance of probabilities appropriate to interlocutory proceedings.

# Hearsay and interlocutory proceedings — Evidence Act 2001 s75
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s75] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: Hearsay in interlocutory proceedings] [CONCEPTS: hearsay, interlocutory proceedings, preliminary hearing, admissibility, s75]

Hearsay may be called at interlocutory proceedings including preliminary hearings under the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act because section 75 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) provides for exceptions and contexts in which hearsay can be admissible. The practical rule in the source is that evidence of stress symptoms, prior complaints, or police statements (which might otherwise be hearsay at trial) can be adduced in support of special measures at the preliminary stage.

# Special witness evidence — Symptoms of stress and PTSD that can be tendered
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: Medical and behavioural indicators admissible at prelims] [CONCEPTS: crying, muscle tension, chest pain, sleep disturbance, flashbacks, intrusive memories, racing heart, sweating, gastrointestinal symptoms]

The source supplies a non-exhaustive list of stress and post-traumatic symptoms that can be adduced to support a special witness application: crying, muscle tension, chest pain, abdominal pain, muscle spasms, unexplained rashes, pounding/racing heart, sweaty palms, sweating when not active, butterflies in stomach, indigestion, diarrhea, sleep disturbance, shortness of breath, and holding breath. For post-traumatic stress: intrusive upsetting memories, flashbacks, nightmares, intense distress on reminders, and intense physical reactions (pounding heart, rapid breathing, nausea, muscle tension, sweating). These symptoms may be proved by hearsay or expert evidence at preliminary hearings to show likely inability to give evidence satisfactorily.

# Special witness evidence — Indicators of intimidation preventing satisfactory evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: Evidence of intimidation and reluctance] [CONCEPTS: fear of reprisal, refusal to cooperate, expression of fear, uncontrolled crying, non-disclosure, intimidation, inability to testify]

To establish that a witness is likely to be so intimidated or distressed as to be unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the presence of the defendant, prosecution material can include evidence (including hearsay) of refusal to make statements, refusal to cooperate, expressions of fear of the defendant, uncontrolled crying, and other behaviours showing fear or intimidation. The rule is the court may rely on such indicators at the balance of probabilities to justify special measures.

# Special witness applications — Medical practitioner report requirements (for reports requested by police)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Medical report content for special witness applications] [CONCEPTS: qualifications, subject matter, diagnosis, symptoms, treatment, capacity to give evidence, emotional trauma]

When police request a medical or psychological report to support a special witness application, the source instructs the report should address: the practitioner’s qualifications and experience; the subject matter of the witness’s evidence; any disability or disorder (including stress disorders) with detailed symptoms and diagnostic reasoning and treatment; and an opinion whether the witness would be able to (i) give evidence in the presence of the defendant without suffering severe emotional trauma, or (ii) give evidence coherently in the presence of the defendant without excessive emotional disruption. The report should be detailed to assist the court’s interlocutory assessment.

# Special witness applications — Prosecutor guidance: onus, hearsay and expert evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Prosecution approach to special witness applications] [CONCEPTS: onus, balance of probabilities, hearsay at prelims, recent complaint evidence, expert evidence, s79 Evidence Act]

The prosecution bears the onus to satisfy the court that special measures are likely necessary, but the standard at the preliminary stage is the balance of probabilities. Hearsay evidence (see s75 Evidence Act 2001) may be admitted in interlocutory proceedings; the prosecution should consider calling recent complaint evidence (such as police officers’ notes) to establish distress/intimidation. Where psychiatric or psychological conditions are alleged, expert evidence will likely be needed (see s79 Evidence Act 2001 on opinion evidence), and a general practitioner may be insufficient for specialised psychiatric opinions.

# Authority - R v Goldman [2004] VSC 165 and DPP v Weiss (2002) VSC 15
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Goldman [2004] VSC 165; DPP v Weiss (2002) VSC 15] [TOPIC: Prejudice to defence vs witness protections; viewing witness on screen] [CONCEPTS: confrontation, prejudice, close-up view, demeanour, television evidence, balancing rights]

R v Goldman [2004] VSC 165 (cited at para 34 in the source) and DPP v Weiss (2002) VSC 15 (cited at para 8) are authorities discussing how special measures may prejudice the accused and how such prejudice may be mitigated. The principle extracted is that remote audiovisual evidence and close-up screens can preserve the defence's capacity to observe witness demeanour (for example a large screen with close-up view), and that prejudice to the defence must be balanced against the benefits to the vulnerable witness. These decisions articulate how courts assess whether special measures unjustifiably impair accused's rights.

# Authority - Kerrison v Buxton (2000) 9 Tas R 276
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Kerrison v Buxton (2000) 9 Tas R 276] [TOPIC: Confrontation rights; application procedure] [CONCEPTS: confrontation, application procedure, failure to use appropriate legislation, Tasmanian authority]

Kerrison v Buxton (2000) 9 Tas R 276 is cited in the source as an example where the prosecutor did not make the appropriate application under the relevant statutory regime; the decision does not stand for an absolute right to confront an accuser but highlights procedural compliance requirements. The legal principle is that failure to follow the statutory process may result in a missed opportunity to obtain special measures, and the case should not be read as authority for an unimpeachable confrontation right.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Key definitions (affected child; affected person; prescribed proceedings; prescribed witness; specified proceedings)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory definition] [TOPIC: Key definitions under the Act] [CONCEPTS: affected child, affected person, prescribed proceedings, prescribed witness, specified proceedings, child witness]

Key statutory definitions from the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 as summarised in the source: an "affected child" is a person under 18 giving evidence in family-violence matters or child sexual offences or who is a victim under specified Police Offences Act provisions; an "affected person" may be an adult who was under 18 at time of offending or a prescribed witness under s7J; "prescribed proceedings/offences" include family-violence offences, applications concerning PFVO and FVOs, specified Police Offences Act provisions and child sexual offences; a "prescribed witness" is a witness declared under s7J; "specified proceedings" concerns specified offences (source references section 8(1A)(a) and 35(3) of the Police Offences Act 1935). These definitions determine eligibility for special measures and intermediary assistance.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 4: Approved support person
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s4] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: Approved support person in proceedings concerning a child] [CONCEPTS: approved support person, child, presence in proceeding, court approval, limitation (not another witness)]

Section 4 of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 permits an approved support person to be near a child giving evidence in any proceeding concerning that child, subject to court approval. The rule states that the support person may be present during the child's evidence but cannot be another witness in the proceeding. The provision facilitates emotional support while safeguarding the integrity of oral testimony and the fairness of the trial.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 5: Prior statements in prescribed/specified hearings
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s5] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: Prior statements as evidence-in-chief] [CONCEPTS: prior statement, affected child, prescribed witness, prescribed proceeding, disclosure, cross-examination]

Section 5 allows, in prescribed or specified hearings, the prior statement (written or recorded) of an affected child or prescribed witness to be tendered as part or all of their evidence-in-chief, provided the statement has been disclosed and is available for cross-examination. The rule facilitates the use of prior recorded statements to reduce the need for vulnerable witnesses to repeat traumatic accounts in open court, subject to procedural safeguards including availability for cross-examination.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 5A: Prior audio/visual police statements as EIC
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s5A] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: Audio/visual statements tendered as evidence] [CONCEPTS: prior audio/visual statement, police recording, evidence-in-chief, special witness, child evidence]

Section 5A permits a prior audio/visual statement taken by police of a child, a special witness, a victim of certain Police Offences Act provisions, or a family-violence victim, to be tendered as part or all of the witness's evidence-in-chief. The rule enables use of recorded police interviews as the witness’s evidence, reducing the need for vulnerable witnesses to repeat testimony in open court while allowing the defence opportunity to cross-examine subject to procedural safeguards.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 6B: Remote audio/visual link where no s7 order
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s6B] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: Remote evidence where no s7 order in place] [CONCEPTS: remote link, a/v link, affected child, prescribed witness, specified proceeding, absence of s7 order]

Section 6B provides that where there is no section 7 order in place, an affected child or prescribed witness in a prescribed or specified proceeding is to be given the opportunity to give evidence by remote audio/visual link. The rule ensures access to remote testimony as a protective alternative in the absence of other formal orders, facilitating participation while protecting witness welfare.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 7I: Assessment report for child or adult with communication need
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7I] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Assessment report requirement for specified proceedings] [CONCEPTS: assessment report, witness intermediary, child witness, communication need, eligibility, s7I(3) discretion]

Section 7I requires that an assessment report be prepared and provided to the judge by a witness intermediary in respect of a witness in a specified proceeding where the witness is a child or an adult with a communication need. The rule states that a judge is to order such a report (subject to limited discretions under s7I(3) where ordering would be unnecessary, inappropriate, contrary to interests of justice, or where the witness objects). The assessment assists the court in determining whether a witness intermediary is required and in planning communication needs.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 7J: Witness intermediary order and prescribed witness
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7J] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Witness intermediary orders and prescribed witness status] [CONCEPTS: witness intermediary, prescribed witness, s7J order, intermediary functions, prescribed status]

Section 7J allows the court to make an order for the use of a witness intermediary in specified proceedings; doing so effectively converts the relevant witness into a "prescribed witness" for the purposes of the Act. The rule enables trained intermediaries to assist communication between the court and vulnerable witnesses and triggers associated statutory protections, such as the possibility of prior statement admissibility.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 7K: Ground rules hearing and consideration of assessment report
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7K] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Ground rules hearing] [CONCEPTS: ground rules hearing, adoption of recommendations, directions, assessment report, trial management]

Section 7K provides for a ground rules hearing once an assessment report has been prepared in respect of a prescribed witness. The judge is to consider the assessment report's contents and may adopt its recommendations when making directions about how the witness will give evidence. The ground rules hearing is a procedural mechanism to set communication protocols and courtroom conduct tailored to the witness’s needs.

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 — Section 8A: Defendant cannot cross-examine in prescribed proceeding
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s8A] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: Limit on defendant cross-examination] [CONCEPTS: cross-examination prohibition, prescribed proceeding, special measures, accused rights, courtroom procedure]

Section 8A provides that in prescribed proceedings the defendant is not permitted to personally cross-examine the witness. The provision supports protections for vulnerable witnesses (for example, children or those with communication needs) by restricting direct confrontational questioning by the accused and ensuring that cross-examination, where allowed, is conducted through legal representatives or governed by ground rules.

# Procedure — Order of events for special witness measures (petitioning for assessment and ground rules hearing)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Procedural steps for obtaining assessment report and ground rules hearing] [CONCEPTS: petition court, assessment report, ground rules hearing, adoption of recommendations, s7I, s7K, prescribed witness]

The practical procedure recommended in the source is: at the earliest opportunity petition the court to seek an assessment report under section 7I and adjourn for the purpose of obtaining that report; once the assessment report is available, petition the court for a ground rules hearing under section 7K and for an order under section 7J that a witness intermediary be used (thereby making the relevant witness a prescribed witness). The prosecutor should ask the court to adopt the assessment report's recommendations and identify any statutory consequences that flow from the witness's prescribed status (for example, prior statement admissibility under section 5).

# DPP notes — Eligible witnesses and communication need (s7I, s7F)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7I; s7F] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Who is eligible for assessment/intermediary] [CONCEPTS: eligible witnesses, under 18, communication need, excluded defendants, quality of evidence, understanding and expressing information]

DPP guidance cited in the source summarises that eligible witnesses under s7I include all children (persons under 18) giving evidence in specified offences and adults with communication needs in specified offences; defendants are excluded. A witness has a "communication need" (s7F) if the quality or clarity of evidence may be significantly diminished by their ability to understand, process or express information. The guidance emphasises early identification and assessment to determine intermediary support.

# Assessment reports — Timing, content and judicial discretion (s7I(3); s7L)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7I(3); s7L] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Assessment report process and judicial powers] [CONCEPTS: timing, 4–6 weeks, assessment report content, judicial information, s7I discretion, interests of justice]

The rule is that assessment reports should be ordered as early as practicable before the witness may be required to give evidence — the source indicates practical lead times of around 4–6 weeks to obtain an assessment. Section 7I(3) confers discretion to decline ordering a report where unnecessary, inappropriate, the witness objects, or it would be contrary to the interests of justice. Section 7L permits a judge to inform themselves on any matter in any manner they think fit when determining whether to order a report.

# Ground rules hearing outcomes — Typical directions and communication arrangements
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Typical directions from a ground rules hearing] [CONCEPTS: questioning directions, lists of questions, use of aids, length of questioning, intermediary role, pre-trial directions]

Following an assessment report, a ground rules hearing typically produces directions governing how the witness will be questioned: how long they may be questioned, the types of questions permitted, whether lists of questions are to be provided to the intermediary in advance, the use of visual aids (plans, models, body maps), limitations on repeated questioning, and other measures to assist communication. The judge will adopt recommendations from the report subject to any necessary modification to ensure fairness to both parties.

# Relevant legislation and rules (cross-list)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: statutory list] [TOPIC: Relevant Acts and Rules for special witnesses and evidence] [CONCEPTS: Evidence Act 2001, Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001, Justices Rules, Criminal Rules 2006, s12, s18, s26, s41, s75, s79]

Key legislative instruments and provisions identified in the source: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) — s12 (competency), s18 (compellability of child/parent), s26 (court’s control over questioning), s41 (improper questions), s75 (hearsay), s79 (opinion evidence); Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (multiple sections including s4, s5, s5A, s6B, s7I, s7J, s7K, s8, s8A); Justices Rules r5; Criminal Rules 2006 r41A (preliminary hearing procedure). These provisions form the statutory framework for special witness measures and interlocutory practice.

# Family Violence — Strangulation (section 170B) [REVIEW]
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 170B] [TYPE: offence] [TOPIC: Strangulation (statutory reference incomplete)] [CONCEPTS: strangulation, choking, suffocation, specific intent, neck pressure, absence of injury, section 170B, statutory definition missing, family violence]

Strangulation is described in the source as an offence referenced by "170B" that requires proof the accused intentionally caused choking, suffocation or strangulation of another person; the source emphasises the crime is one of specific intent and that the relevant words are not defined in the source material and therefore carry their ordinary meanings. This chunk records the substantive rule as presented: proof of intentional unlawful application of external pressure to the neck (or conduct causing choking/suffocation) is required and evidence of visible injury is often absent. Important caveat: the source supplies only the section number "170B" without naming the Act or providing a full statutory citation; accordingly the statutory context (Act title and year) is not established in the source and this chunk is marked [REVIEW] for completion of the legislative citation. Courts may treat "strangulation" broadly (any external pressure to the neck from any source) and will accept ordinary-language meanings evolving with community expectations; prosecutions often rely on victim symptoms and circumstantial evidence where external marks are limited or absent.

# Family Violence — Disabling (section 168) [REVIEW]
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 168] [TYPE: offence / element of offence] [TOPIC: Disabling conduct and suffocation (statutory reference incomplete)] [CONCEPTS: disabling, rendering unable to flee, suffocation, pressure to chest, laying on chest, non-blunt-force suffocation, section 168, statutory context missing]

Disabling conduct is referenced in the source by the section number "168" and is characterised as conduct that renders a person unable to flee; the source notes that suffocation may be treated as a disabling act distinct from strangulation, with a lower threshold in some circumstances (for example pressure to the chest or laying on the chest can suffice). The rule recorded is that disabling need not involve blunt force — relatively modest pressure that impairs breathing or mobility can meet the actus reus of disabling/suffocation-type offences. Important caveat: the source provides only the section number "168" without the Act name or full legislative citation, so the statutory provenance and exact wording cannot be reproduced from this part alone; this chunk is therefore marked [REVIEW] pending confirmation of the Act and full citation to permit precise statutory cross-referencing and citation of elements.

# Youth sentencing — Incomplete case citations referenced in source (DPP v NOP; Director of Public Prosecutions v JSP; TAP v Tasmania) [REVIEW]
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: sentencing principle / case reference] [TOPIC: Incomplete case citations cited for balance of rehabilitation and deterrence] [CONCEPTS: DPP v NOP, Director of Public Prosecutions v JSP, TAP v Tasmania, rehabilitation primacy, general deterrence, sentencing of youthful offenders, incomplete citation, [REVIEW]]

The source material refers to several appellate or trial authorities (notably "DPP v NOP," "Director of Public Prosecutions v JSP," and "TAP v Tasmania") in support of the sentencing principle that rehabilitation is ordinarily the primary consideration in youth sentencing but that general and personal deterrence — and denunciation and just punishment — can be relevant in serious cases. The substantive doctrinal point extracted from the source is that courts recognise a tension between welfare/rehabilitation and deterrence; judges such as Evan J (in the source's paraphrase) have indicated rehabilitation's primacy while acknowledging occasions where deterrence and punishment carry equal weight. Important caveat: the source does not supply full case citations for DPP v NOP, Director of Public Prosecutions v JSP, or TAP v Tasmania (no year, court or citation string provided in this part). Because the source lacks the complete citations, this chunk flags those references as incomplete and is marked [REVIEW] — full case citations should be obtained before creating formal case-authority chunks or relying on these authorities for research or citation.

### Chunk 1 — Youth Sentencing: Dismissal
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: 453 / 452F] [CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) - Dismissal] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Dismissal as a youth sentencing option] [CONCEPTS: youth sentencing, dismissal, Youth Justice Act, no conviction, Tasmanian youth court]

The chunk covers Dismissal as a youth sentencing option.

Dismissal

Dismissal is the least severe sentencing option for young offenders sentenced under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas). Dismissal means that a youth is found guilty, but the charge is dismissed and no other sentence is ordered. 453 A conviction cannot be recorded. 452F

---

### Chunk 2 — Youth Sentencing: Reprimand
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: 455] [CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) - Reprimand] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Reprimand as a youth sentencing option] [CONCEPTS: reprimand, youth sentencing, Youth Justice Act, formal warning, no conviction]

The chunk covers Reprimand as a youth sentencing option.

Reprimand

A reprimand means that a youth is found guilty and the charge is dismissed and the youth is reprimanded. 455 This is a formal warning by the court. A conviction cannot be recorded.

Dismissal with undertaking to be of good behaviour

A dismissal with an undertaking to be of good behaviour is a form of conditional unsupervised release. An undertaking to be of good behaviour is an agreement by a youth

### Chunk 4 — Youth Sentencing: Release and adjournment with conditions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: (unspecified)] [CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) - Adjournment with conditions] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Release and adjournment with conditions for youth sentencing] [CONCEPTS: adjournment, conditions, youth sentencing, conditional discharge, Youth Justice Act]

The chunk covers Release and adjournment with conditions in youth sentencing.

Release and adjournment with conditions

Under this sanction, the youth is released and sentencing is postponed for a nominated period (no more than 12 months) on conditions set out by the court. be recorded. A conviction cannot. At the conclusion of the adjournment with conditions, if the youth does everything required by the conditions, the youth is discharged from the order. If the youth does not do everything required under the conditions, the youth may have to return to court and the court may impose a new order.

### Chunk 6 — Youth Sentencing: Probation order

The chunk covers Probation orders for young people.

Probation order

Young people on probation orders must report to youth justice 469F workers as required. 472 470F 471 The court can attach special conditions, such as attending school or other educational programs, counselling or treatment. The youth may also be required to abstain from drinking alcohol or using controlled substances, submitting to testing for controlled substances or alcohol, to live at a specified address, and/or comply with a curfew.

### Chunk 8 — Youth Sentencing: Detention as a LAST resort and sentencing aims
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: (unspecified)] [CITATION: Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) - Detention as last resort & sentencing aims] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Detention as last resort; deterrence vs rehabilitation; case commentary] [CONCEPTS: detention last resort, general deterrence, rehabilitation, youth sentencing principles, DPP v NOP, DPP v JSP]

The chunk covers Detention as a last resort and the tensions between rehabilitation and deterrence in youth sentencing.

Detention as a LAST resort.

General deterrence ok for serious offending.

The Supreme Court has recognised that there are tensions in the aims and purposes of sentencing under the Youth Justice Act 1997 (Tas) that can be difficult to reconcile, particularly in relation to serious offending. 390F 391 It has been consistently accepted that while rehabilitation is often a predominant factor, both general and personal deterrence can have a role to play, particularly in relation to serious offending. 392 For example, in DPP v NOP, 391F 393 392F a case involving a conviction for rape, Evan J wrote that 'whilst rehabilitation is ordinarily the primary consideration for youthful offenders, this does not mean that the Court must disregard the needs of general deterrence and denunciation'. 393F 3

Most recently, in Director of Public Prosecutions v JSP, of the Court of Criminal Appeal in TAP v Tasmania: 399 398F 398 Estcourt J quoted the comments 397F There is no need to set out those provisions \[in the Youth Justice Act\]. Those relevant to sentencing restate the relevant principles concerning the sentencing of juvenile offenders which have long been established by the courts. The principles are that rehabilitation of the offender is always an important, if not the dominant consideration, and that any sentence should be tailored with greater emphasis on the welfare of the youth; the emphasis on rehabilitation is consistent with the broader sentencing goal of community protection ... It is true that the justification for the principles concerning the sentencing of youthful offenders is that such offenders are not able to appreciate the nature and extent of their criminality. They are most likely to make ill-considered and immature decisions. ... the importance of rehabilitation of a youthful offender is usually far more important than general deterrence, but that there are cases in which just punishment and general deterrence become at least equally important. 400

---

Strangulation:

170B

Elements:

"The crime is also one of specific intent, meaning that the Crown must show that the accused intended to cause the act of choking, suffocation or strangulation. Those acts are not 92 Thursday 10 March 2022 defined in the legislation so that the words will have their ordinary meanings that change and evolve over time in line with community expectations." From second reading speech Mar 10 22

Intentionally -- unlawfully -- another person

Consider also - S 168 - Disabling -- rendering someone unable to flee

Suffocation: (not as high threshold as strang)

- Blunt force not necessary

- Can just be pressure to chest -- or laying on their chest

Strang -- any part of the body (any external pressure from any source to the neck)

Must be intentional --

Evidence of injury is rare.

Assault on pregnant woman is a "lesser" charge than strangulation according to the conference held in Launceston.

Assault being an entry level charge to the Crime -- increasing to agg assault and upwards.

Strangulation is a "more" serious CRIME than assault on a pregnant woman (s 184A). Again, according to the round table discussion.

In any matter not otherwise provided for by these rules of court, the practice of the Supreme Court is to be followed.

### Chunk 17 — Special Witness Applications: Information for Investigating Police
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s8 / s75 Evidence Act referenced] [CITATION: Special Witness Applications - Info for Investigating Police] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: What needs to be established and evidence for special witness applications] [CONCEPTS: special witness, investigating police, s8, s75, evidence, checklist, hearsay, balance of probabilities]

The chunk covers what needs to be established for special witness applications and what evidence to gather.

Special Witness Applications- Information for Investigating Police

What needs to be established?

\(a\) by reason of intellectual, mental or physical disability, the person is, or is likely to be, unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the ordinary manner; or

\(b\) by reason of age, cultural background, relationship to any party to the

proceeding, the nature of the subject matter of the evidence or any other factor the court considers relevant, the person is likely ---

\(i\) to suffer severe emotional trauma; or

\(ii\) to be so intimidated or distressed as to be unable to give evidence or to give evidence satisfactorily.

(See s8 Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2401)

The onus of proof is on us, but its only balance of probabilities because we only need to establish that its likely.

Hearsay Evidence

You can call hearsay evidence, see s75 Evidence Act (Tas); these are interlocutory proceedings.

### Chunk 19 — Stress symptoms and Post Traumatic Stress (checklist)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Evidence of stress symptoms and PTSD for special witness applications] [CONCEPTS: stress symptoms, PTSD, crying, physical symptoms, witness distress, checklist, hearsay]

The chunk covers lists of stress, physical and PTSD symptoms that can be used as evidence (including hearsay) in support of special witness applications.

What evidence is there of the following stress symptoms: (this can be hearsay):

• crying

• Muscle tension

• Pains in chest

• Stomach/abdominal pain

• Muscle spasms or nervous tics

• Unexplained rashes or skin

irritations

• Pounding\' or \'racing\' heart

• Sweaty palms

• Sweating when not physically

active

• \'Butterflies\' in stomach

• Indigestion and \'the gurgles\'

• Diarrhea

• Unable to sleep or excessive

sleep

• Shortness of breath

• Holding breath

Post Traumatic Stress:

• Intrusive, upsetting memories of

the event

• Flashbacks (acting or feeling like

the event is happening again)

• Nightmares (either of the event

or of other frightening things)

• Feelings of intense distress when

reminded of the trauma

• Intense physical reactions to

reminders of the event (e.g,

pounding heart, rapid breathing, nausea, muscle tension,

sweating)

The chunk covers indicators that a witness is too intimidated to give evidence in presence of defendant.

What evidence is there of there being too intimidated to give evidence satisfactorily in presence of defendant (this can be hearsay)? Including any of the following:

• Refusal to make statement, give

evidence, cooperate with police for fear of reprisal ect

• Expression of fear of defendant;

Uncontrolled crying;

### Chunk 21 — Special Witness Applications: Information for Medical Practitioners
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: (unspecified)] [CITATION: Special Witness Applications - Info for Medical Practitioners] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Instructions and questions for medical reports to support special witness applications] [CONCEPTS: medical report, special witness, assessment, questions for medic, psychological diagnosis, opinion]

The chunk covers what is required of medical practitioners when preparing reports for special witness applications.

Special Witness Applications: Information for Medical Practitioners

Normally when witnesses give evidence in court it is in the presence of the defendant. The witness must retell their evidence of the offence in the witness box, within 2 to 3 metres of the defendant and in their full view. The police can apply for the witness to give evidence by video from a remote room but we need to satisfy the court of the following matters;

\(a\) by reason of intellectual, mental or physical disability, the Person is, or is likely to be, unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the ordinary manner;

Or

\(b\) by reason of age, cultural background, relationship to any party to the proceeding, the nature of the subject matter of the evidence or any other factor the court considers

relevant, the person is likely

\(i\) to suffer severe emotional trauma; or

\(ii\) to be so intimidated or distressed as to be unable to give evidence or to give evidence satisfactorily.

To satisfy the court of these matters we have requested that you prepare a report. It is necessary for you to cover the following matters in your report:

1.) What are your qualifications and experience?

2.) What is the subject matter of the witness\' evidence?

3.) What if any disability/ disorder (including stress disorders) does the witness

suffer? Please detail all relevant symptoms and how you have reached this

diagnosis. Please detail any treatment.

4.) Based on the witness\'s disability/ disorder or current stresses and the evidence

that they will give, please give your opinion to whether they would be able to

i\) Give evidence in the presence of the defendant without suffering

severe emotional trauma.

OR

ii\) Give evidence in the presence of the defendant coherently without

crying, shaking, losing concentration and or becoming too upset to give evidence Satisfactorily.

Please give as much detail as possible to support your opinion.

---

### Chunk 22 — Special Witness Applications: Information for Prosecutors
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s8] [CITATION: Special Witness Applications - Info for Prosecutors] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: What prosecutors need to establish and evidence to call for special witness applications] [CONCEPTS: prosecutors, special witness, s8, burden, hearsay, expert evidence, recent complaint evidence]

The chunk covers what prosecutors need to establish and tactical suggestions for evidence gathering.

Special Witness Applications- Information for Prosecutors

What needs to be established?

\(a\) by reason of intellectual, mental or physical disability, the person is, or is likely to b; unable to give evidence satisfactorily in the ordinary manner; or

\(b\) by reason of age, cultural background, relationship to any party to the

proceeding, the nature of the subject matter of the evidence or any other factor the court considers relevant, the person is likely ---

\(i\) to suffer severe emotional trauma; or

\(ii\) to be so intimidated or distressed as to be unable to give evidence or to give evidence satisfactorily.

(See s8 Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 200.1)

The onus of proof is on prosecution, but its only balance of probabilities because we only need to establish that its likely.

Hearsay Evidence

You can call hearsay evidence, see s75 Evidence Act; these are! interlocutory proceedings.

You should consider calling recent complaint evidence from Police Officer\'s who took the victim\'s statements to establish distress and intimidation and difficulty retelling the story ect.

Expert Evidence

To establish intellectual, mental or physical disability and or that a person is likely to

suffer severe emotional trauma, you may need to call expert evidence, consider whether you need a psychologist or physiatrist, a general practitioner may be unable to give

evidence from specialised knowledge in the area if your alleging psychiatric disorder see s79 Evidence Act.

Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act is beneficial legislation which is intended to protect vulnerable witnesses and to override any common !awl right to confront

accuser ect (See A v Fitz \[2003\] TASSC 32 for a consideration of the purpose of the legislation). The rights of witnesses have always needed to be balanced against the rights of accused. See R v Goldman \[2004\] VSC 165.

See R v Goldman \[2004\] VSC 165 at pare 34 where J Redlich when assessing any

prejudice to defence outlines \"there is the full opportunity to observe the demeanour of the witness on a large screen with a \"close up\" view of the witness\' head and upper body\', See also DPP v Weiss (2002) VSC 15 at para 8.

Kerrison v Buxton (2000) 9 Tas R 276 is not authority for an unimpeachable right to confront accuser, the prosecutor in that case simply did not make the appropriate application pursuant to the appropriate legislation.

---

### Chunk 23 — Expert Evidence and case references
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Expert evidence considerations and cited cases] [CONCEPTS: expert evidence, s79, R v Goldman, DPP v Weiss, Kerrison v Buxton, confrontation]

The chunk covers considerations about expert evidence and cites authority.

Expert Evidence

To establish intellectual, mental or physical disability and or that a person is likely to

suffer severe emotional trauma, you may need to call expert evidence, consider whether you need a psychologist or physiatrist, a general practitioner may be unable to give

evidence from specialised knowledge in the area if your alleging psychiatric disorder see s79 Evidence Act.

Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act is beneficial legislation which is intended to protect vulnerable witnesses and to override any common !awl right to confront

accuser ect (See A v Fitz \[2003\] TASSC 32 for a consideration of the purpose of the legislation). The rights of witnesses have always needed to be balanced against the rights of accused. See R v Goldman \[2004\] VSC 165.

See R v Goldman \[2004\] VSC 165 at pare 34 where J Redlich when assessing any

prejudice to defence outlines \"there is the full opportunity to observe the demeanour of the witness on a large screen with a \"close up\" view of the witness\' head and upper body\', See also DPP v Weiss (2002) VSC 15 at para 8.

Kerrison v Buxton (2000) 9 Tas R 276 is not authority for an unimpeachable right to confront accuser, the prosecutor in that case simply did not make the appropriate application pursuant to the appropriate legislation.

### Chunk 24 — Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act: Key definitions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: definitions (affected child, affected person, prescribed witness, specified proceedings)] [CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 - Key definitions] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Key definitions under the Act] [CONCEPTS: affected child, affected person, prescribed witness, specified proceedings, defined terms]

The chunk covers key definitions under the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act.

*Evidence (Children and Special Witness) Act* notes:

### Key definitions 

***Affected child:***

- Under 18

- Giving evidence in FV matters or child sex offences

- Victim of 8(1) or 35(3) POA

***Affected person:***

- Is 18 but under 18 when victim of child sex offences.

- A prescribed witness under s 7J

***Prescribed proceedings/ offences:***

- FV offences

- Applications concerning PFVO and FVO's

- 8(1) or 35(3) POA

- Child sex offences

- Number of indictable matters under purview of the DPP

***Prescribed witness:***

- Witness declared as such under s 7J

***Specified proceedings - concerning specified offences.***

- Section 8(1A)(a) or 35(3) of the Police Offences Act 1935

***Witness intermediary order***

- Order made under s 7J

---

### Chunk 25 — Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act: Relevant Sections 4,5,5A,6B
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: 4 / 5 / 5A / 6B] [CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 - Sections 4,5,5A,6B] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Provisions on support person, prior statements and use of prior audio/visual statements] [CONCEPTS: s4 support person, s5 prior statement, s5A audio-visual statements, s6B remote evidence, special witness provisions]

The chunk covers specific sections and their effects.

Relevant Sections:

**Section 4:**

In **[ANY]{.underline}** proceeding concerning a child -- can have an approved support person near-by. Approved by court. Can't be another witness.

**Section 5:**

In ***prescribed*** or ***specified*** hearing the prior statement (written or recorded) of *affected child* or *prescribed witness* can go in as long as disclosed and available for CX

**Section 5A:**

A prior audio/ visual statement taken by police of a child (in ANY proceeding), a *special witness, a victim of 35(3)* or a victim of FV can be tendered as part or ALL of EIC.

**Section 6B:**

Where there is no section 7 order in place, an *affected child* or *prescribed witness* in a *prescribed proceeding* or a *specified proceeding* is to be given by remote a/v/ link.

---

### Chunk 26 — Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act: Intermediaries (7I,7J,7K,8A)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: 7I / 7J / 7K / 8A] [CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 - Intermediaries and related provisions] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Witness intermediary provisions and ground rules hearings] [CONCEPTS: witness intermediary, s7I, s7J, s7K, ground rules hearing, prescribed witness, specified proceedings]

The chunk covers intermediary provisions and related orders.

Intermediaries:

Only applies to ***Specified** **Proceedings***/ offences!

**Section 7I:**

Assessment report can be sought for a child witness and/or child victim OR an adult witness/ victim with suspected identified communication need in a in a *specified proceeding.*

**Section 7J:**

Seek an order under 7J -- for the use of a witness intermediary in these proceedings. This makes the relevant witness a "*prescribed*" witness.

**Section 7K:**

Ground rules hearing -- adopt recommendations of the report.

If an assessment report has been made in respect of the *prescribed witness*, in making a direction under this section the judge is to consider any matters mentioned in that report.

**Section 8A:**

Defendant can't CX in a prescribed proceeding.

---

### Chunk 27 — Order of events: assessment report and ground rules hearing (procedure)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7I / s7K / s7J] [CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 - Order of events & ground rules hearing] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Procedural steps for seeking assessment and ground rules hearing] [CONCEPTS: preliminary hearing, assessment report, ground rules hearing, prescribed witness, witness intermediary, s7I]

The chunk covers the recommended order of events and ground rules hearing steps.

Order of events:

**At earliest opportunity:**

Petition court to consider seeking an assessment report under Section 7I and adj for that purpose to a later "ground rules hearing" under Section 7K

***Ground Rules hearing:***

Once report obtained, Petition court to formally order that a witness intermediary be used in respect o a witness in a specified proceeding under Section 7J. The result of this is that the witness becomes a "*prescribed witness*" for the purposes of the *Act*.

Petition the court to adopt the recommendations of the report (subject to anything silly in it).

Identify and advise the court of any other statutory presumption that arises by virtue of the witness becoming a *prescribed witness* (i.e. Section 5 -- prior statement as EIC).

---

### Chunk 28 — DPP's notes: Eligible Witnesses and communication need (annotation)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7I / s7F] [CITATION: DPP notes - eligible witnesses and communication need] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: DPP guidance on eligible witnesses and definition of communication need] [CONCEPTS: DPP notes, eligible witnesses, s7I, s7F, communication need, children, adults with communication need]

The chunk covers DPP notes about eligibility and communication need.

DPP's notes:

- Eligible Witnesses, S7I

<!-- -->

- All children (a person under the age of 18 years) who are giving evidence in relation to the specified offence. Applies equally to child complainants and child witnesses

- Adults (a person who has attained the age of 18 years) with a communication need who are giving evidence in relation to a specified offence

- Does not apply to defendants

<!-- -->

- Communication need, s 7F

<!-- -->

- A witness who is to give evidence in a specified proceeding is taken to have a communication need if the *quality or clarity* of evidence given by the witness may be significantly diminished by the witness's ability to *understand, process or express information-* See s7F(2) and (3) for more info.

---

### Chunk 29 — Assessment Reports (s7I) — process and timing
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7I / s7L] [CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 - Assessment reports s7I] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Assessment report orders and judge's powers] [CONCEPTS: assessment report, s7I, judge may inform, timing, intermediary, CARU]

The chunk covers who must order assessment reports, timing and practicalities.

Assessment Reports

- S7I A Judge **is** to make an order than an assessment report be prepared and provided to the judge by a witness intermediary in respect of a witness in a specified proceeding if that witness is child witness/adult with communication need (see definition in previous slide)

- In determining whether to order a report the Judge may inform themselves on any matter in any manner that they think fit (7L)

- Order for assessment to be made as early as practicable before the witness may be required to give evidence. Can take 4-6 weeks for the assessment

- This requires an assessment report by intermediary which will need to be organised through the Child Abuse Royal Commission Response Unit.

<!-- -->

- Note under s7I(3) there is some discretion re ordering the assessment in instances where the Judge is satisfied that;

- \(i\) it is unnecessary or inappropriate to make the order, or

- \(ii\) the witness does not wish the order to be made

- \(iii\) the making of the order would be contrary to the interests of justice.

- Examples; 17 year old child, articulate, educated, has given evidence before, have met with them and no communication need is apparent.

---

### Chunk 30 — After the assessment: orders and ground rules hearing
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7J / s7K] [CITATION: Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 - Post-assessment orders & ground rules] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Steps taken after assessment report including ground rules hearing and directions] [CONCEPTS: ground rules hearing, intermediary order, prescribed witness, directions, assessment report recommendations]

The chunk covers steps the judge and parties take after the assessment report.

What happens after the assessment?

- A judge is to make an order that a witness intermediary will be used if, having considered the assessment report, they are satisfied the use of the intermediary will assist the proceeding.

- Once the order is made, the Judge is to order a "ground rules hearing" to discuss communication and other related needs identified in the assessment report. Prosecutor/defence lawyer and intermediary are to attend (witness need not attend). The judge will make directions that will govern how the proceedings will be conducted. Any directions will be guided by the assessment report and may include;

<!-- -->

- Directions about how the witness will be questioned/ how long the witness will be questioned/ the types of questions that can be asked/ order that prosecutor/defence provide a list of questions to the intermediary in advance of the hearing/ directions as to the use of plans, models, body maps or other aids to help communicate a question or answer

---

### Chunk 31 — Relevant Legislation & Criminal Rules r41A restatement
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas) / Criminal Rules 2006 (Tas) / Justices Rules (Tas)] [SECTION: Evidence Act s12, s18, s26, s41; Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act; Criminal Rules r41A; Justices Rules r5] [CITATION: Relevant legislation list and r41A text - preliminary hearing] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Legislation relevant to special witness procedures and text of r41A] [CONCEPTS: Evidence Act, Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act, Justices Rules, Criminal Rules, r41A, preliminary hearing]

The chunk covers a consolidated list of relevant legislation and repeats the key Criminal Rules provision.

Relevant Legislation

Evidence Act 2001-

s12 Competency; sworn and unsworn evidence

S18 Compellability of child/parent

s26 Court\'s control over questioning

s41 Improper Questions

Evidence (Child and Special Witnesses) Act 2001

Justices Rules r5

Criminal Rules 2006-

r41A

**Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001;**

S9(1); In any prescribed proceeding (or specified proceeding) in which the prosecutor who is to call the witness requires approval under s4 (support person), s6A (special hearing- child) or 8 (special witness), the prosecutor or party calling the witness is to apply for a preliminary hearing to deal with the matters before the hearing is commenced.

S9(2); A preliminary hearing in respect of those proceedings may be provided for by the Rules of Court in which that proceeding is being brought.

**Justices Rules**

Rule 5 provides that any matter not provided for by those rules, the practice of the Supreme Court is to be followed.

- **Criminal Rules 2006**

- Rule 41A of the Criminal Rules 2006 provides;

[41A. Applications under Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001]{.underline}

***For the purposes of a preliminary hearing under section 9(2) of the Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 , a judge may inform himself or herself in any way he or she thinks fit and may accept statements and assurances of the prosecutor without hearing evidence from the child or person who is giving, or is to give, evidence***

---

<!-- block_025 master -->

# Evidence (Children and Special Witnesses) Act 2001 (Tas)

No additional doctrinal units were listed as [UNPROCESSED] in the prior coverage report; therefore there are no further chunks to produce. If you intended specific items or case authorities to be formatted that were not included in the original submission, please list them and I will convert each into a formatted chunk.

<!-- block_025 procedure -->

### Heading: What to put before the Magistrate
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Pre-hearing matters and submissions to put before the Magistrate] [CONCEPTS: applications to magistrate, support person, admission of prior statement, s3 principles, video recorded statements, special hearing, witness instructions, child witnesses, Magistrates Court]

Tactical coverage: What to put before the Magistrate — practical checklist for pre-hearing applications and submissions.

- File an application seeking the particular support/s

- You can get instructions directly from the witness/parents and make representations to the Magistrate

- Support person; it can not be a witness in the proceeding. WAS officers are trained in what they can and cant do. WAS officers available for certain matters.

- Admission of prior statement; Submissions to the effect that; (e.g.)

    - The Principles in s3 of the Act relating to child witnesses are intended to reduce the child's distress/trauma when giving evidence

    - That video recorded statements are taken soon after the event and are made when they are fresh in the memory of the witness, they are therefore more likely to be an accurate and reliable account of what occurred.

    - It is an irrefutable record of what was said, how the questions were asked/answers elicited

    - It captures gestures when child attempts to explain an action that has occurred

    - Also enables the fact finder to observe their age and demeanour at the time that they are recounting what occurred. Especially important when there has been a delay in listing for hearing

- For a special hearing; this may be sought when there is likely to be a lengthy delay or where the whole of the hearing can not be heard at one time, however as matters are heard by Magistrates and not Judge and Jury it is unlikely to be useful it in the Magistrates Court jurisdiction as the special hearing has to be recorded. The magistrate is more likely to just proceed with that part of the hearing without the need to conduct a special hearing.

(End of checklist content — preserve exact practitioner phrasing and asides.)

---

Crotty Legal

174 Macquarie Street

Hobart Tasmania 7000

Tamila Smith

Crown Counsel
