### Heading: Power of arrest — Need to state reason (Criminal Code s301(2))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code (Tas)] [SECTION: s301(2)] [CITATION: Criminal Code s301(2) - Duty to give notice on arrest - Johnson v Reeves note] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Duty to inform arrested person of cause of arrest; "practicable" exception] [CONCEPTS: arrest notice, s301(2), practicable, Johnson v Reeves, duty of person arresting, arrest procedure]

Tactical coverage: Duty to inform arrested person of the cause/process of arrest under Criminal Code s301(2) and practical exception.

 The *Criminal Code, *s301(2), provides as follows:

"**(2)** It is the duty of a person arresting another, whether with or without warrant, to give notice, if practicable, of the process or warrant under which he is acting, or of the cause of the arrest."

if it is pointless (i.e. they would not be able to hear the cops) to inform or attempt to inform an arrested person of the cause of the arrest, it is not "practicable" to do so within the meaning of s301(2) **Johnson v Reeves [2004] TASSC 110 (24 September 2004)**

---

### Heading: "Found offending" — Kim Hibble v ... (1997) TASSC 52 excerpt
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Kim Hibble v Jody William Phegan; Kim Hibble v Dean Mark Jenkins; David John Brooks v Leigh Andrew Phegan; Kim Hibble v Kim Maree Ogden; David John Brooks v Andrew David Hulse Files [1997] TASSC 52 (20 May 1997)] [CITATION: Kim Hibble (1997) TASSC 52 - Found offending and arrest without warrant] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Meaning of "found offending" and police power to arrest without warrant] [CONCEPTS: found offending, arrest without warrant, s34B(3), reasonable grounds, Lynch v Hargrave, intention to arrest, execution of duty]

Tactical coverage: Excerpt from Kim Hibble (TASSC 1997) on the meaning of "found offending" and the lawful basis for arrest without warrant under s34B(3).

48. The Act, s34B(3) provides the authority for a police officer to arrest without warrant a person committing certain offences, including the offences of resisting and assaulting a police officer in the execution of his duty. Some suggestion was made during the course of argument that this power of arrest is governed by the provisions of the Act, s55. I do not accept that submission because s34B is not a section in respect of which it is "specially provided" that a police officer may arrest without warrant a person found offending against that provision. What is provided for by s34B(3) is that a police officer may arrest without warrant a person "committing" an offence.

49. In the course of argument, considerable reference was made to Lynch v Hargrave [1971 VicRp 11; 1971 VR 99.] Relevantly, that case concerned the expression "found offending", and, in particular, whether, before it could be said that a person was "found offending", it needed to be established objectively that that person in fact was offending or whether it was sufficient that the person finding the alleged offender believed on reasonable grounds that the person was offending. Relying on Trebeck v Croudace [1918 1 KB 158], McInerney J held at 109 - 110:

"Accordingly, I am of the view the power of arrest of a person 'found offending' is to be regarded as exercisable according to the facts as then known to the member of the police force. Whether or not the person arrested was in fact and in law offending in the relevant time is a matter for subsequent determination of the court."

50. There is no reason why the same principle should not be applied to the word "offending". I do apply it, so that the power conferred by s34B(3) is exercisable whenever a police officer believes, on reasonable grounds, that the relevant person is committing a relevant offence created by s34B. It follows that the lawfulness of the arrests of Leigh Phegan and Hulse is not determined by whether they actually assaulted Lee. The arrests may have been lawful and, accordingly, Rowlands may have been acting in the execution of his duty at relevant times even if the obstruction of Lee was not established.

51. Applying Lynch v Hargrave (supra) and the well-established meaning of "committing" (see Simmons v Millingen [1846 EngR 275]; (1846) 2 CB 524; Hanway v Boultbee [1830 EngR 888]; (1830) 1 M & Rob 15, 174 ER 6; R v Smith [(1876) 14 SCR (NSW) 419)] I construe s34B(3) as conferring a power of arrest in those cases:

1 where a person is taken flagrente delicto doing the prohibited act;

2 where the arresting officer believes on reasonable grounds that a person taken is taken flagrente delicto doing the prohibited act; and

3 where a person is taken immediately and in fresh pursuit after being found in either of those circumstances.

52. A police officer's power of arrest is to be exercised by him or her personally and on the facts known to him or her. The mere direction of a senior officer provides no lawful authority for the making of an arrest by the police officer to whom the direction is given. An arrest is a serious interference with personal liberty. That liberty may not be infringed except by lawful authority. The direction of a senior officer does not provide that lawful authority. Any officer who complied with the direction given by Lee, did so at his peril unless there was some other basis which authorised that officer to make the arrest.

---

### Heading: Family Violence (power of arrest) — Barnes v Crossin [2017] TASSC 61
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s7, s4, s11, s11(4), s11(5)] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 - Barnes v Crossin [2017] TASSC 61 - power of arrest under s11] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: Family Violence Act arrest powers; meaning of family violence and purpose of s11 arrests] [CONCEPTS: family violence arrest, s11, definition of family violence, s7, s4, s11(5), arrest purposes, PFVO, safety audit, detention for questioning]

Tactical coverage: Excerpt from Barnes v Crossin on the scope and purpose of arrest powers under the Family Violence Act 2004.

31. Under s 11, the suspicion which provides authority for the arrest is that the person being arrested "has committed family violence". "Family violence" is defined by s 7 of the Act as follows:

"family violence means ---

(a) any of the following types of conduct committed by a person, directly or indirectly, against that person's spouse or partner:

(i) assault, including sexual assault;

(ii) threats, coercion, intimidation or verbal abuse;

(iii) abduction;

(iv) stalking within the meaning of section 192 of the Criminal Code ;

(v) attempting or threatening to commit conduct referred to in subparagraph (i) , (ii) , (iii) or (iv) ; or

(b) any of the following:

(i) economic abuse;

(ii) emotional abuse or intimidation;

(iii) contravening an external family violence order, an interim FVO, an FVO or a PFVO; or

(c) any damage caused by a person, directly or indirectly, to any property ---

(i) jointly owned by that person and his or her spouse or partner; or

(ii) owned by that person's spouse or partner; or

(iii) owned by an affected child."

32. As is obvious from this provision, conduct which constitutes family violence may not necessarily involve the commission of an offence. This distinction is emphasised by s 4 of the Act which separately defines "family violence offence" and "family violence". By s 4, "family violence" has the meaning defined in s 7. However, "family violence offence means any offence the commission of which constitutes family violence".

33. Hence, an arrest may take place notwithstanding that the police officer does not suspect that an offence has been committed and does not intend to charge the person being arrested with such an offence. It is clear that the broad powers given to police authorise arrest for the purpose of facilitating proactive intervention by police in circumstances in which there is a reasonable suspicion that the person has committed family violence. Such intervention might include removing the person from the immediate environment, often the family home, to preserve the immediate safety of other family members and to facilitate a wide range of follow-up action. That action may include investigation as to whether an offence has been committed and, if so, charging the person with that offence. The broad ambit of the purpose of an arrest under this section is consistent with the legislative scheme contained within the Act. That scheme includes the following:

    - Section 11(5) which provides that in deciding whether to arrest a person under subs (1) "the police officer is to give priority to the safety, wellbeing and interest of any affected person or affected child".

    - Section 11(4) which provides that after arrest a person may be detained for a range of purposes defined as follows:

"(a) determine the charge or charges which should be laid in relation to the family violence;

(b) carry out a risk screening or safety audit;

(c) implement the measures identified by a safety audit where it is practical to do so;

(d) make and serve a PFVO or an application for an FVO."

    - Section 11(2) and (3) which provide for a person to be brought before justices as soon as practicable, and also apply the provisions of s 4 of the Criminal Law Detention and Interrogation Act 1995. This section authorises detention for a reasonable time "for the purposes of questioning the person, or carrying out investigations in which the person participates in order to determine his or her involvement, if any, in relation to an offence."

(End of excerpt — preserve statutory cross-references and practitioner commentary.)

---

### Heading: Definitions — "Approach" (Kerr v Brown [2013] TASMC 30)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Kerr v Brown [2013] TASMC 30] [CITATION: Kerr v Brown [2013] TASMC 30 - meaning of "approach"] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: Construction of "approach" in protection orders; element of intent and ordinary meaning] [CONCEPTS: approach meaning, PFVO conditions, intent element, ordinary common meaning, Gee J, Moio, interpretation of orders, initiating contact]

Tactical coverage: Judicial construction of the word "approach" in conditions of an order — excerpts and practical interpretation.

*Kerr v Brown* [2013] TASMC 30

6. Perhaps surprisingly, the only decided judicial authority I can find as to the meaning of approach in such circumstances, is a decision of the Family Court of Australia, In the marriage of Moio [1984 FLC 91-575.] In that case, Gee J considered the meaning of the word "approach" in respect of an allegation of breach of an undertaking given to the court that a husband would "not harass approach, molest, assault or otherwise interfere with the wife or her defacto husband". The husband was alleged to have breached that provision, in particular, by approaching the defacto husband by driving his car, for a short period, in close proximity to a car in which the defacto husband was travelling. His Honour concluded that the meaning of the word "approach" needed to be interpreted in context of other words in the undertaking. He also considered the dictionary meaning of that word, in particular, the meaning in the Shorter Oxford English Dictionary "coming near a person both in time and circumstance". He considered, in particular, that there was an element of intent involved in the use of that word. His Honour was not satisfied that in the circumstances of the case there had been a breach of the undertaking.

7. In my view, it is appropriate to construe the word as it appears in this order not only having regard to the context of the balance of the condition, but also bearing in mind its ordinary common meaning. The order is intended to create obligations on the part of the respondent, the breach of which constitutes an offence and can result in the application of criminal sanction. It would be unfair to interpret the provision in any way other than that by which it would be understood by persons in the position of the defendant i.e. without legal training and of average experience and education. It is the defendant who is expected to comply with the obligations imposed by the condition, in jeopardy of criminal sanction, and, accordingly, it is fair that those obligations be understood according to their ordinary common meaning. The word should not be given any extended meaning.

8. It seems to me that what is clearly conveyed by the provision is that a person is not to deliberately, and with intent, initiate action in order to bring himself into the presence of, or initiate a communication directly or indirectly with the protected person. An interpretation which limits the obligation to not initiating contact is consistent with the reference in the order to the various electronic means of communication. The word approach, as Gee J pointed out, involves an element of intent, and hence necessarily must involve the initiation of some action on the part of the defendant to give effect to that intent. On the other hand, if the obligation was intended to be wider than this, for example, that the respondent must not allow himself passively to come into the physical company or proximity of the person to be protected in any circumstances and if that occurs, then to remove himself, then it seems to me that the relevant order could easily be worded in a way which would clearly spell out that obligation to the respondent. This order was not so worded.

---

### Heading: Definitions — "Be within" (Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18 (3 August 2020)] [CITATION: Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18 - meaning of "be within" in PFVO conditions] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: Interpretation of "be within" requiring intent element; not strict liability] [CONCEPTS: be within meaning, PFVO breach, intent element, not strict liability, Sundram v Bearman, comparison with approach]

Tactical coverage: Brief judicial observation that "be within" in PFVO conditions carries an element of intent and is not a strict liability offence.

**Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18 (3 August 2020)**

[161] The words 'be within' as they are used within the condition of the PFVO which it is alleged Mr Bearman breached (analogously with 'approach' in *Kerr v Brown*) must be interpreted in the context of the provisions of the Family Violence Act 2004 and with the other words in that order. I consider that an element of intent is involved in the use of those words.

---

### Heading: Definitions — "Abuse" / "verbal abuse" (K V K [2012] TASMC 123)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: K V K [2012] TASMC 123] [CITATION: K V K [2012] TASMC 123 - construction of "verbal abuse"] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: annotation / case authority] [TOPIC: Construction of "verbal abuse" in family violence context; contextual analysis and disadvantage consideration] [CONCEPTS: verbal abuse definition, s7(a)(ii), context, disadvantage, threats, intimidation, K V K, heated argument vs abuse]

Tactical coverage: Guidance on construing "verbal abuse" in the Family Violence Act context; not every heated argument is verbal abuse.

- use (something) to bad effect or for a bad purpose; misuse.

- treat with cruelty or violence, especially regularly or repeatedly.

***K V K [2012] TASMC 123***

[29] The term "verbal abuse" should be construed in light of the other words in s7(a)(ii). A heated argument with raised voices does not, without more, constitute verbal abuse. It is difficult to characterise or define what words may amount to threats, intimidation or abuse. The same words may in some circumstances amount to a threat or abuse when in other circumstances they may not. Much depends on the background and the context. In some circumstances even the most seemingly innocent words may be highly intimidatory. The court should consider whether one of the parties is in a position of disadvantage, either physically, emotionally, intellectually, socially or economically.

**Example where not found: K & K para 32.** *She is obviously a competent and intelligent person. I do not accept that she would be in a position of disadvantage in any verbal argument or exchange with her husband or in any disagreement concerning property of the relationship.*

---

### Heading: Definitions — "Court premises" (Court Security Act definition)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Court Security Act (Tas)] [SECTION: definition of "court premises"] [CITATION: Court Security Act - definition of "court premises"] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory definition of "court premises" for FV and court orders context] [CONCEPTS: court premises definition, courtroom, forecourt, audio-visual appearances, places used in conjunction with court, Court Security Act, entry/exit areas]

Tactical coverage: Statutory definition of "court premises" as relevant to applications/orders and behavior in court-related spaces.

***court premises*** means the premises or place where a court is held and includes --

(a) a courtroom; and

(b) a forecourt, courtyard, yard, parking area, toilet facility, hall, corridor, or other area, used in conjunction with the premises or place; and

(c) a part of the premises or place used as an entrance to or exit from the premises or place; and

(d) a part of any premises, or any place, being used to enable a person, who is at a different location, to appear before the court by means of audio, or audio-visual, communication; and

(e) a place at which a view, hearing or the taking of evidence is being carried out in relation to proceedings before a court; and

(f) a part of any premises, or of any place, that are premises, or a place, used in relation to the operation or management of a court or court premises or that is referred to in another paragraph of this definition;

(End of statutory definition — preserve exact list formatting.)

---

### Heading: Firearms (practitioner note)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Practitioner note on absence of firearm/possession definitions in FV Act and Firearms Act application; registry error re FVO expiry] [CONCEPTS: firearms definition, FV Act, Firearms Act, prescription of definitions, FVO expiry error, ultra vires arrest, registry mistake]

Tactical coverage: Practitioner observation about legislative drafting and a factual note about administrative error leading to ultra vires arrest.

"Firearm"/ "possession" not defined in FV act and firearms Act doesn't have automatic extension to other acts like the criminal code. Need to argue that the absence of either definition in the firearms act makes it more likely it was drafted with the firearms act in mind.

**FVO expiry date incorrectly recorded by Court Registry. Defendant not in breach. Subsequent attempt to arrest was ultra vires.**

(End of practitioner note — preserve informal phrasing and advocacy suggestion.)

<!-- block_026 master -->

# Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)

<!-- block_026 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — "harassing" (practitioner commentary)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: practitioner comment on definition of "harassing" and mens rea] [CONCEPTS: "harassing", mens rea, Family Violence Order, Personal Protection Family Violence Order, Cannell v G]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers practitioner commentary on s 4 "harassing" definitions and the mens rea issue.

Section 4 covers PFVO and FVO definetions of harras-/harassing. It is an exhaustive definition **Cannell v G; G v Cannell \[2018\] TASSC 55 \[para52\]**

**The only mental element is that the def intend the ACT that by virtue of the definition in s 4 constitutes harassing, not the act had any particular effect on the person to be protected.**

60. "Harass" in its ordinary sense involves conduct committed with the purpose of causing an effect on the person harassed, eg worry, fear or mental anguish: see *Johnson v Collier*[(1997) 142 FLR 409.](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281997%29%20142%20FLR%20409) However, in my view, the provision of an exhaustive definition of "harassing", by reference to the actions of the alleged harasser, excludes the need to establish that the actions in question have had any particular effect on the person protected by the order, unless expressly so provided in the definition. It must logically follow that there is no requirement that the defendant intend or otherwise foresee the causation of such an effect. These conclusions are confirmed by the inclusion in some of the defined actions of an express requirement that the action occur in circumstances "where it is likely" that there will be some consequence relevant to the protected person. For example, in par (f), it is necessary that the offensive material be left "where it is likely to be found by, given to or brought to the attention of" the protected person. There are similar express requirements contained in pars (g) and (h). There would, of course, be no need to include these specific provisions, if an intention to cause or the actual causation of apprehension or fear was already implied in the definition.

61. This construction is consistent with the subject matter and social purpose of the relevant legislation. The provisions in question are concerned with the establishment of firm and viable processes to ensure the protection of persons, from persons who probably have committed, and will again commit, family violence [(s 16(1)](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s16.html) of the Act). Family violence is recognised as a significant social problem and the Act is directly and expressly directed at dealing with this problem. A family violence order is the primary protective mechanism employed by the Act. [Section 3](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s3.html) provides:

"In the administration of this Act, the safety, psychological wellbeing and interests of people affected by family violence are the paramount considerations."

62. The construction of the Act in respect of the mens rea required for the offence of contravention of an order, will be heavily influenced by this statutory context. However, while these considerations weigh against the presumption in favour of proof of intention to commit the offence, they do not completely displace it. Rather, they fashion and define the required mental element. Although the offence created by [s 35](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s35.html) is summary, the section prescribes heavy penalties. This is also a relevant consideration, one which weighs in favour of the application of the presumption. In my view, the proper balance leads to the conclusion that the requisite mental element is that the defendant must intend to do the act, which by virtue of the definition in [s 4](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s4.html), constitutes "harassing". In this case, the relevant act is that defined by par (a), following the complainant. If this mental element is present, then the legislation operates to deem that the person has harassed the person protected by the order and, if the condition is in place, deems that there has been a contravention of the order. A requirement of this nature is consistent with the statutory scheme, is appropriate having regard to the subject matter of the provision, and enables and supports the viable enforcement of a family violence order. It also provides the person subject to the order with a clear understanding of the conduct proscribed by it.

[CITATION: Cannell v G; G v Cannell [2018] TASSC 55 - practitioner commentary excerpt] [CASE: Cannell v G; G v Cannell [2018] TASSC 55]

---

### Chunk 2 — Cannell v G; G v Cannell [2018] TASSC 55 (paras 60–62)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Cannell on meaning of "harass" and mens rea required] [CONCEPTS: Cannell v G, mens rea, "harass" definition, s4, family violence order] [CASE: Cannell v G; G v Cannell [2018] TASSC 55] [CITATION: Cannell v G; G v Cannell [2018] TASSC 55 - paras 60-62]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers the quoted judicial reasoning in Cannell v G; G v Cannell [2018] TASSC 55 paragraphs 60–62 on the nature of "harass" and mens rea.

60. "Harass" in its ordinary sense involves conduct committed with the purpose of causing an effect on the person harassed, eg worry, fear or mental anguish: see *Johnson v Collier*[(1997) 142 FLR 409.](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281997%29%20142%20FLR%20409) However, in my view, the provision of an exhaustive definition of "harassing", by reference to the actions of the alleged harasser, excludes the need to establish that the actions in question have had any particular effect on the person protected by the order, unless expressly so provided in the definition. It must logically follow that there is no requirement that the defendant intend or otherwise foresee the causation of such an effect. These conclusions are confirmed by the inclusion in some of the defined actions of an express requirement that the action occur in circumstances "where it is likely" that there will be some consequence relevant to the protected person. For example, in par (f), it is necessary that the offensive material be left "where it is likely to be found by, given to or brought to the attention of" the protected person. There are similar express requirements contained in pars (g) and (h). There would, of course, be no need to include these specific provisions, if an intention to cause or the actual causation of apprehension or fear was already implied in the definition.

61. This construction is consistent with the subject matter and social purpose of the relevant legislation. The provisions in question are concerned with the establishment of firm and viable processes to ensure the protection of persons, from persons who probably have committed, and will again commit, family violence [(s 16(1)](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s16.html) of the Act). Family violence is recognised as a significant social problem and the Act is directly and expressly directed at dealing with this problem. A family violence order is the primary protective mechanism employed by the Act. [Section 3](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s3.html) provides:

"In the administration of this Act, the safety, psychological wellbeing and interests of people affected by family violence are the paramount considerations."

62. The construction of the Act in respect of the mens rea required for the offence of contravention of an order, will be heavily influenced by this statutory context. However, while these considerations weigh against the presumption in favour of proof of intention to commit the offence, they do not completely displace it. Rather, they fashion and define the required mental element. Although the offence created by [s 35](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s35.html) is summary, the section prescribes heavy penalties. This is also a relevant consideration, one which weighs in favour of the application of the presumption. In my view, the proper balance leads to the conclusion that the requisite mental element is that the defendant must intend to do the act, which by virtue of the definition in [s 4](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/aia1931230/s4.html), constitutes "harassing". In this case, the relevant act is that defined by par (a), following the complainant. If this mental element is present, then the legislation operates to deem that the person has harassed the person protected by the order and, if the condition is in place, deems that there has been a contravention of the order. A requirement of this nature is consistent with the statutory scheme, is appropriate having regard to the subject matter of the provision, and enables and supports the viable enforcement of a family violence order. It also provides the person subject to the order with a clear understanding of the conduct proscribed by it.

---

### Chunk 3 — Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 4 "harassing" definition
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 4] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 4 - "harassing" definition] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: statutory definition of "harassing"] [CONCEPTS: "harassing", Family Violence Act s4, definition list, following, surveillance, offensive material]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers the full statutory text of s 4 definition of "harassing".

In this Act, unless the contrary intention appears --

Section 4 "harassing" means doing any one or more of the following actions in respect of a particular person:

(a) following the person;

(b) keeping the person under surveillance;

(c) loitering outside the residence or workplace of the person;

(d) loitering outside a place that the person frequents;

(e) entering or interfering with the property of the person;

(f) sending offensive material to the person or leaving offensive material where it is likely to be found by, given to or brought to the attention of the person;

(g) publishing or transmitting offensive material by electronic or any other means in such a way that the offensive material is likely to be found by, or brought to the attention of, the person;

(h) using the internet or any other form of electronic communication in a way that could reasonably be expected to cause the person to be apprehensive or fearful;

(i) contacting the person by postal, telephonic, electronic or any other means of communication;

---

### Chunk 4 — Following / Keeping under surveillance (Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Sundram on meaning of "following" and "keeping under surveillance"] [CONCEPTS: "following", "keeping under surveillance", continuous surveillance, loitering, Family Violence Act] [CASE: Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18] [CITATION: Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18 - para 154]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers the excerpt from Sundram v Bearman about the ordinary meaning and requirements of "following" and "keeping under surveillance".

\[154\] Obviously the words* 'following' *or *'keeping under surveillance' *are not actually used in the particulars of count 4 (unlike counts 5 and 7 which specifically allege that Mr Bearman followed Ms Paul). Grammatically, at least, it is difficult to see how the particulars of count 4 could possibly satisfy the term '*following'. *Further, the term *'keeping the person under surveillance'* requires proof (at least) of close observation of the protected person by the defendant; and to be guilty of '*keeping*' another person under surveillance requires that the surveillance be conducted on a continued basis (as distinct from, for example, just looking at someone only once - and only for a short period). Such an interpretation gives meaning to the word *'keeping'* and promotes the purpose and objects of the [*Family Violence Act 2004*](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) generally. Further, it avoids giving the word *'harassing'* too broad a meaning in the broader context within which this particular penal provision of the *[Family Violence Act 2004](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) *sits.

---

### Chunk 5 — Single act (Howe v S [2013] TASMC 33)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: whether a single act can satisfy "harassing"] [CONCEPTS: single act, contacting, telecommunications, course of conduct, Howe v S] [CASE: Howe v S [2013] TASMC 33] [CITATION: Howe v S [2013] TASMC 33 - paras 13-14]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers Howe v S on whether a single act (e.g. a single contact) can be "harassing".

{para 13} Harassing means doing any one or more of the following actions in respect of a particular person.

*(i)* Contacting the person by post or telephonic, electronic or any other means of communication.

The reference in the definition to *"any one or more of the following actions"* suggests that a single act might be sufficient. Furthermore, it is appropriate, in my view, to interpret the term having regard to the context in which it is used in the order. The reference to *"threaten, harass, abuse or assault"* suggests that the order is to be understood as providing protection to a person from contact with the respondent which is unwelcome, and might be in various forms or have variable effect.

{para 14} This interpretation is consistent with case authority (see *Daly v Medwell* [(1986) 40 SASR 281](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281986%29%2040%20SASR%20281), where it was stated that the offence of using a telecommunications service for the purpose of harassing a person could be constituted by a single call or a course of conduct consisting of several calls, depending on the nature and intention of the relevant conduct, per White J at 284, King CJ at 296 and Prior J at 297; see also *Devlin v Cooper* [\[1991\] TASSC 115).](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1991/115.html)

### Chunk 8 — Amendment to Section 68T (Family Law Act 1975) — removal of 21 day limit
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)] [SECTION: s 68T] [CITATION: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 68T - amendment re interim family violence orders] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: amendment to s68T removing 21-day limit on revival/variation/suspension of family law orders] [CONCEPTS: s68T, interim FVO, revival/variation/suspension, 21 day limit removed, Family Law Amendment 2018]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers the amendment to s 68T regarding the time period for revival/variation/suspension of family law orders when an interim family violence order is made.

Section 68R of the *Family Law Act 1975* provides that a court of a State or Territory which holds jurisdiction under Part VII of the Family Law Act may, when making a family violence order, revive, vary, discharge or suspend a range of family law orders to the extent to which they provide for a child to spend time with a person, to ensure they are consistent with the State or Territory family violence order. Subsection 68R(4) restricts the court's power when making an *interim* family violence order to revival, variation or suspension of a family law order.

Section 68T previously provided that where the court, when making an *interim* family violence order or an *interim variation* of a family violence order, revives, varies or suspends an order, injunction or arrangement under section 68R, that revival, variation or suspension ceases to have effect at the earlier of:

(a) the time the interim order stops being in force; and

(b) the end of the period of 21 days starting when the interim order was made.

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has removed the 21 day time limit in paragraph (b) of section 68T and replaced it with:

(b) the time specified in the interim order as the time at which the revival, variation or suspension ceases to have effect; and

(c) the time the order, injunction or arrangement is affected by an order (however described) made by a court, under section 68R or otherwise, after the revival, variation or suspension.

The amendments require judicial officers to consider whether the revival, variation or suspension should expire on a specific date (and if so, when), or whether it should expire when the interim family violence order expires, or when a court makes an alternative order.

These new provisions strengthen the court's ability to protect children and other vulnerable family members by allowing judicial officers to set timeframes according to the particular circumstances of the case. Relevant considerations when making such a decision may include balancing the safety risks to the child arising from having contact with a parent with the maintenance of the child/parent relationship. These changes are intended to better protect children and other vulnerable family members from violence by reducing the risk that can arise from inconsistent orders applying to a child's contact with an allegedly violent parent.

Judicial officers may only change family law orders under section 68R when they have evidence that was not before the court that made the original order (subsection 68R(3)). Evidence must support a finding that changing the family law order is in the best interests of the child concerned including because the family law order is inconsistent with a family violence order in force, or a person, including the child, has been or is likely to be, exposed to family violence as a result of the operation of that order (subsection 68R(5)).

---

### Chunk 9 — Amendment to Section 68P (Family Law Act 1975) — explanations to protected persons (children)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)] [SECTION: s 68P] [CITATION: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 68P - amendments 68P(2A)-(2C)] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: obligations of courts when making orders inconsistent with family violence orders; new exceptions re children] [CONCEPTS: s68P, explanation to protected person, child, 68P(2A), 68P(2B), 68P(2C), best interests]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers the amendments to s 68P introducing exceptions allowing courts to withhold explanations from children where in their best interests.

Section 68P of the Family Law Act sets out the obligations of a court when making an order, or granting an injunction under the Family Law Act, that is *inconsistent with an existing family violence order*. Subparagraph 68P(2)(c)(iii) requires the court, to the extent to which the order or injunction provides for a child to spend time with a person, or expressly or impliedly requires or authorises a person to spend time with a child, to explain the order or injunction to the person protected by the family violence order (if that person is not the applicant or respondent). In some circumstances, the person protected by the family violence order may be a child. Paragraph 68P(2)(d) sets out the matters that the court must include in the explanation, and notes that the explanation must be in language the person is likely to readily understand.

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has inserted three new subsections:

- subsection 68P(2A) provides that the court is not required to provide the explanation mandated by subparagraph 68P(2)(c)(iii) to a child if the court is satisfied that it is in the child's best interests [not]{.underline} to receive an explanation of the order or injunction;

- subsection 68P(2B) provides that the court is not required to include a particular matter otherwise required to be explained by paragraph 68P(2)(d) if the court is satisfied that it is in the child's best interests for the matter not to be included in the explanation; and

- subsection 68P(2C) provides that when determining what is in a child's best interests for the purposes of new subsections 68P(2A) and 69P(2B), the court is required to consider the matters in existing subsection 60CC(2), but is not required (but may choose) to consider the matters listed in existing subsection 60CC(3), despite the requirement in subsection 60CC(1).

In most cases, it will be in a child's best interests to understand how court orders apply to them and their family. However, this may not always be the case. In particular, it may not be in the child's best interests to be exposed to the conflict between their parents to the extent necessary for courts to comply with this requirement. In some cases, such as where the child is an infant, it will not be practical to give an explanation.

The provision is designed to ensure that the information children receive from a court is appropriate and does not expose them to further details of family violence which may result in re-traumatisation.

---

### Chunk 10 — Insertion of new Subdivision G — Section 69ZL Short form reasons for interim parenting orders
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)] [SECTION: s 69ZL] [CITATION: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 69ZL - short form reasons] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: short form reasons for interim parenting orders] [CONCEPTS: 69ZL, short form reasons, interim parenting orders, family law jurisdiction]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers the new section 69ZL permitting short form reasons for interim parenting orders.

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has inserted a new section 69ZL - Short form reasons for decisions relating to *interim parenting orders* into the Family Law Act. Section 69ZL provides that a court may give reasons in short form for a decision it makes in relation to an interim parenting order. This provision does not otherwise affect the obligation of a court to give reasons for a decision it makes in relation to any matter arising under this Act.

This amendment implements recommendation 3 of the Family Law Council's 2015 *Interim Report on Families with Complex Needs and the Intersection of the Family Law and Child Protection Systems.* In its report, the Council identified that a practical barrier to state children's or magistrates' courts exercising family law jurisdiction is that the writing of detailed judgments is not the usual practice of these courts. The Council recognised this practical barrier applied similarly for federal family law courts.

Courts are already able to give reasons for any decision in short form, as long as the reasons are adequate. The new provision is not intended to, and should not be read, to limit the courts' existing powers in any way. For example, it should not be implied that courts cannot deliver short form judgments in final proceedings or in proceedings under other Parts of the Act.

---

### Chunk 11 — Insertion of new Section 45A — Summary decrees
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)] [SECTION: s 45A] [CITATION: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 45A - summary decrees] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: summary decrees and dismissal of unmeritorious proceedings] [CONCEPTS: 45A, summary decree, frivolous, vexatious, abuse of process, dismissal, costs]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers the new s 45A empowering courts to make summary decrees and dismiss unmeritorious proceedings.

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has inserted a new section 45A into the Family Law Act, which strengthens and codifies family law judges' power to dismiss unmeritorious cases and proceedings that are frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process. While this does not significantly change the existing powers of the courts exercising family law jurisdiction to dismiss proceedings if they have no reasonable prospect of success, the provision is intended to improve court efficiency by providing greater clarity about when applications can be dismissed by the court.

Subsections 45A(1) and (2) allow the court to make a summary decree in favour of one party, in relation to the whole or part of a proceeding, if satisfied that a party has no reasonable prospect of successfully:

- prosecuting the proceedings or part of the proceedings, or

- defending the proceedings or part of the proceedings.

Subsection 45A(3) provides that, for the purposes of the section, in determining whether a defence or proceeding has no reasonable prospect of success, proceedings need not be hopeless or bound to fail.

Subsection 45A(4) empowers the court to dismiss all or part of a proceeding if it is frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process.

Subsection 45A(5) provides that proceedings or a part of proceedings are not to be considered frivolous, vexatious or an abuse of process just because a related application is made and later withdrawn. This is intended to operate as a safeguard against the court misinterpreting the actions of victims of family violence who are litigants in person, who might make an application and then withdraw it for reasons other than the merits of their case, for example as a result of the power and control dynamics of family violence.

Subsections 45A(6)-(8) provide that:

- the court may make costs orders as it sees fit

- the court may take such action of its own volition, or on the application of a party to the proceedings, and

- the new section does not limit any powers that the court has apart from this section.

Section 118 of the Family Law Act, which deals with frivolous or vexatious proceedings, has been repealed.

---

### Chunk 12 — Other amendments (Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act 2018)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)] [CITATION: Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) - other amendments (post-2018)] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: summary of other amendments including jurisdiction prescription, property limits, repeal of s114(2)] [CONCEPTS: jurisdiction, children's courts, Part VII, property value limit, s114(2) repeal, prescription by regulation]

Tactical anchor: This chunk covers the remaining miscellaneous amendments made by the Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act 2018.

The Family Law Amendment (Family Violence and Other Measures) Act has also amended the Family Law Act such that:

- state and territory courts can be prescribed by regulations to have the same jurisdiction in family law parenting matters as that held by state and territory courts of summary jurisdiction under Part VII of the Family Law Act. The intent is to enable children's courts to make appropriate orders under the Family Law Act where there are existing proceedings before the court, particularly in child protection matters (see section 69GA).

  - The new provisions allow for the prescription of classes of proceedings in regulations, in which the prescribed court will have Part VII jurisdiction. It also allows for the prescription of rules of court in regulations, to apply when the prescribed court is exercising family jurisdiction.

  - It is a requirement that the Attorney-General consult with the relevant state or territory Minister before a court or any other matter is prescribed.

- courts of summary jurisdiction can hear contested family law property matters without both parties' consent, where the value of the property is up to a new limit to be prescribed in regulations. The new limit will exceed the current value of $20 000, and different limits can be prescribed for different states and territories (see section 46A).

  - It is a requirement that the Attorney-General consult with the relevant state or territory Minister before a property value is prescribed.

  - Unless or until a new value is prescribed, the current limit of $20 000 will continue to apply.

- subsection 114(2) of the Family Law Act is repealed. Consequently it is no longer open to the court to make an order relieving a party to a marriage from an obligation to perform marital services or render conjugal rights. The High Court held in *R v L (1991)* 174 CLR 379 that there is no such obligation under Australian law. Section 114(2) has therefore been effectively redundant since that time.

---

### Chunk 2
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Act 1959] [SECTION: s30] [CITATION: Justices Act 1959 s30 - Statement of offences] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statement sufficiency for complaints, summonses and related documents] [CONCEPTS: complaint drafting, statement of offences, reasonable information, particulars, Justices Act]

Covers: Statement requirements under Justices Act s 30.

**JUSTICES ACT 1959 - SECT 30**

**30. Statement of offences, &c.**

(1) Any complaint, summons, warrant, or other document that is laid, issued, or made for the purpose of, or in connection with, proceedings before justices shall be sufficient if it --

(a) describes the matter of complaint with which the defendant is charged or of which he is convicted in ordinary language, avoiding as far as possible the use of technical terms and without necessarily stating all the essential elements of the matter of complaint; and

(b) contains such particulars as will give reasonable information of the nature of the matter complained of.

(2) Where an enactment constituting a simple offence or breach of duty states --

(a) the offence or breach of duty to be doing or omitting to do --

(i) any one of any different acts in the alternative; or

(ii) any act in any one of different capacities or with any one of different intentions; or

(b) any part of the offence or breach of duty in the alternative --

the acts, omissions, capacities, intentions, or other matters stated in the alternative in the enactment may be stated in the alternative in a complaint for that simple offence or breach of duty.

### Chunk 4
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Howe v S [2013] TASMC 33] [CITATION: Howe v S [2013] TASMC 33 - Course of conduct (s9)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Course of conduct requirement under s9 Family Violence Act] [CONCEPTS: course of conduct, s9, single act vs multiple acts, stalking definition, limiting freedom of movement, Gunes v Pearson]

Covers: Course of conduct requirement and whether a single act can suffice under s9.

**Howe v S [2013] TASMC 33 (29 July 2013)**

20. Mr Doyle again submits that the difficulty with this charge is that a single conversation cannot amount to a breach of ![http://www8.austlii.edu.au/images/displeft.png](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}** [[s.9]{.underline}](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s9.html) **![http://www8.austlii.edu.au/images/dispright.png](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} [*[Family Violence Act]{.underline}*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) in that it is an element of the offence created by that section that the defendant has pursued a course of conduct, and that this necessarily involves more than one conversation.

21. I agree with the general proposition that a course of conduct would normally be interpreted as *"conduct which is protracted or conduct which is engaged in on more than one separate occasion"*. (See *Gunes v Pearson* [1996] 89A Crim R 297 at 306). I note also that the definition of "*family violence*" in [[s.7]{.underline}](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s7.html), [*[Family Violence Act]{.underline}*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) includes "*stalking*" within the meaning of s.192, *Criminal Code*. That section defines *stalking* as a course of conduct. S.192(2) provides that for the purposes of that definition:

    - (a) A person pursues a course of conduct if the conduct is sustained or the conduct occurs on more than one occasion.

22. On the other hand, s.9 itself defines *"a course of conduct"* to include ***"limiting the freedom of movement of a person, spouse or partner by means of threats or intimidation**"*. It seems to me that if a threat or act of intimidation had the effect of limiting the freedom of movement of a person, spouse or partner, that a single act of threat or intimidation could, in those circumstances, amount to *"a course of conduct"* and hence amount to an offence contrary to that section. Because of what follows, however, I do not intend to decide this question.

---

### Chunk 8

Covers: Arrest and detention rules and interaction with Justices Act s34.

Section 12 of the FV Act says that section 34 of the justices act does NOT apply where charged for a breach of PFVO or FVO. Meaning they must taken to court.

However, where there is NO breach of a PFVO or FVO -- defendant can be released unconditionally or under police bail.

Only when there has been a charge for a breach must they be taken to court.

This means, they can be arrested for an order -- or for carry out a RAST etc, but there is no obligation for the police to lay charges then and there. They may release without charge, and review later.

Can police bail or unconditionally release for new FV charges (FVA s 11(2)). Where a def has been arrested for a breach of a PFVO of FVO, we cannot police bail them. Must be taken to a court (FV. 12(3)).

Only can release unconditionally where

### Chunk 10
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18] [CITATION: Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18 - Intent and knowledge for s35 offences] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Mental element, knowledge and intent under Family Violence Act s35] [CONCEPTS: intent, knowledge, s35, harassing by following, prosecutorial burden, deliberate conduct]

Covers: Requirement of intent and knowledge for offences under s35 of the Family Violence Act.

**Sundram v Bearman [2020] TASMC 18 (3 August 2020)**

156 The [*Family Violence Act 2004*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/), ![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}** [s35](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/s35.html) **![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} does not create an offence of strict liability, regardless of fault. The evidence must prove that Mr Bearman "*knew all the facts constituting the ingredients necessary to make his physical acts criminal were involved in what he was doing.*"[fn123] In addition, consistently with the approach taken by Brett M (as His Honour then was) in different but somewhat analogous circumstances in *Kerr v Brown*[fn124], the prosecution must prove an element of intent. That is, in order to satisfy the element of harassing by following, the prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that Mr Bearman deliberately and with intent travelled behind a vehicle which he knew was being driven by Ms Paul. Mr Bearman bears no onus to provide an innocent account of his presence on the road on that day; the prosecution case must exclude, beyond reasonable doubt, the possibility that Mr Bearman's presence and movements on the road at that time were innocent. Before convicting Mr Bearman of this count, the prosecution case must satisfy the Court beyond reasonable doubt that he intentionally followed Ms Paul for the apparently short journey between the position of the road where he stopped - and looked at Ms Paul as she passed -- and the intersection where Mr Bearman and Ms Paul drove off in different directions.

161 The words 'be within' as they are used within the condition of the PFVO which it is alleged Mr Bearman breached (analogously with 'approach' in *Kerr v Brown*[fn126]) must be interpreted in the context of the provisions of the [*Family Violence Act 2004*](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/) and with the other words in that order. I consider that an element of intent is involved in the use of those words.

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Collateral challenge rule - s35 proceedings (McKenna; Acting DPP v JCN; Buxton; Cannell)] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Collateral challenge rule in s35 hearings; third-party contact exceptions] [CONCEPTS: collateral challenge, s35(3), McKenna v Smith, Acting DPP v JCN, Buxton v Brinckman, Cannell v G, contact agreements]

Covers: Collateral challenge rule and related authority; third-party contact exception.

In hearings for charges under s 35 of the FV act, defence cannot seek to challenge the validity of the underlying orders that contain the conditions.

- **McKenna v Smith [2014] TASMC 11 (27 March 2014)**

Magi Bret -- not the forum. That is for separate admin review of the matter. To do otherwise defeats the purpose and intent of the 35(3).

- Each separate phone call would amount to a breach of the order (per Slicer J in Acting director of public prosecutions v JCN [2015] TASFC 13 at para 10!

- ***Buxton v Brinckman [2011] TASMC 24 (21 July 2011)***

Not an excuse to breach order even if inconsistent with bail conditions -- FV act prevails over all others.

Be mindful of 3rd party (children or other intermediaries) being used to make "one off" agreements for "current contact agreements" reached by means of an exception allowing for contact for the purposes of discussing matters relating to the children. In the matter of **Cannell v G; G v Cannell [2018] TASSC 55** The court discussed a scenario where a child talked between parents as to an alternative drop off location, and it was reasoned that this would fall within to contact agreement exception for not being within 100mt.

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: R v Meek (1981) 1 NZLR 499; cited in Gary John Carter v R No. SCCRM 94/72 Judgment No. 4498 (1994) SASC] [CITATION: R v Meek - Threats and subjective recklessness] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Threats as raising fear; subjective recklessness] [CONCEPTS: threats, recklessness, subjective intent, arousal of fear, R v Meek, Carter v R]

Covers: The required mental element for threats — subjective recklessness and intent to be taken seriously.

### Threat

Subjective recklessness (recklessly indifferent) as to whether fear is aroused in victim

R v Meek ** (1981) 1 NZLR 499 **(cited in **GARY JOHN CARTER v R No. SCCRM 94/72 Judgment No. 4498 Number of pages - 9 Criminal law and procedure - particular offences - offences against the person [1994] SASC 4498 (20 April 1994) -- SASC)**

*21. ... inherent in the concept of the making of a threat that the words used were intended to be taken seriously*

*by the person to whom they were being addressed; and that, if it remained a reasonable possibility that, in speaking as he did, the appellant was doing no more than merely unburden his feelings in a counselling context, the offence was not made out.*

*22 ... It seems to me that, in accordance with the ordinary meaning of that word, the notion of a threat also includes a mere declaration of hostile intent, which is made and intended to be taken seriously and thus influences the mind of the recipient, by arousing in that person an apprehension that the threat is at least likely to be carried out.*

*23. I consider that the evidence in this case fell short of establishing the existence of a positive intent to arouse the relevant fear, but it is impossible to escape the conclusion (at which the jury obviously arrived) that*

*The appellant was at least recklessly indifferent as to whether that fear would be aroused.*

### Chunk 13
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Kerr v Brown [2013] TASMC 30] [CITATION: Kerr v Brown [2013] TASMC 30 - Construing condition meanings] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Construction of terms in Family Violence Orders; ordinary meaning] [CONCEPTS: construing orders, ordinary meaning, defendant understanding, Kerr v Brown, condition interpretation]

Covers: Principles for construing terms of a Family Violence Order.

The general principles for construing the terms of a Family Violence Order were considered by Magistrate Brett (as he then was) in *Kerr v Brown* [2013] TASMC 30. His honour said at paragraph [7]:

*"7. In my view, it is appropriate to construe the word as it appears in this order not only having regard to the context of the balance of the condition, but also bearing in mind its ordinary common meaning. The order is intended to create obligations on the part of the respondent, the breach of which constitutes an offence and can result in the application of criminal sanction. It would be unfair to interpret the provision in any way other than that by which it would be understood by persons in the position of the defendant i.e. without legal training and of average experience and education. It is the defendant who is expected to comply with the obligations imposed by the condition, in jeopardy of criminal sanction, and, accordingly, it is fair that those obligations be understood according to their ordinary common meaning. The word should not be given any extended meaning."*

---

### Chunk 14
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Police v Martin (unreported, Magistrates Court of Tasmania, 20 September 2021 Jago, M) - Matters arising out of relationship] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Communications as "discussions" vs one-way apologies; purpose of communication] [CONCEPTS: apology vs discussion, one-way communication, matters arising out of relationship, Police v Martin, relevance to FV orders]

Covers: Whether a communication is "for the purpose of discussing matters arising out of the relationship" — apology not a discussion.

*Police v Martin* unreported, Magistrates court of Tasmania, 20 September 2021 Jago, M Cited in **Police v H [2021] TASMC 13 (22 December 2021)**

Magistrate Jago said in *Police v Martin* at paragraphs [158] -- [160]:

*"Here the communication sent was clearly an apology. It was an apology for having behaved in an appalling manner towards Ms Martin some five days earlier. It did not in my view involve any discussion. A discussion is a two-way process. It involves a conversation, debate or argument about an issue. It is the process of talking about something in order to reach a decision. It involves the exchange of ideas and comment. The Defendant's apology was not a discussion. It did not seek a response from Ms Martin. Did not raise any questions that required an answer. It did not seek to her to offer an alternate opinion about a matter. It did not raise any issues that required her input or opinion. It was a one-way communication which the Defendant was expressing his regret and acknowledging his wrongdoing. The communication was not sent for the purpose of discussing matters arising out of the relationship. The communication was sent for the purpose of apologising. The communication was not seeking and did not require any response from Ms Martin which is again indicative that it was not sent for the purpose of discussing matters arising out of the relationship."*

Covers: Statutory provision that an attempt to commit any offence is itself an offence.

**ACTS INTERPRETATION ACT 1931 - SECT 34**

**34. Attempt to commit any offence to be an offence**

Where by any Act an offence is constituted, any attempt to commit such offence shall be an offence and be punishable in the same manner as the offence constituted by such Act.

**142A. Proof of exemption**

(1) It is not necessary in any complaint or indictment to specify, negative or prove any exemption, exception, proviso, condition, excuse or qualification.

### Chunk 18
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: State of Tasmania v Ashley Luke Haintz (Geason J) - Comments on Passing Sentence 4 Dec 2019] [CITATION: State of Tasmania v Haintz - Sentencing comments on breaches and consent] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Sentencing considerations for breaches and consent of complainant; general deterrence; protracted conduct] [CONCEPTS: sentencing, breaches, consent, general deterrence, protracted offending, choking]

Covers: Sentencing remarks regarding breaches of PFVO and FVO and approach to breaches said to have occurred with victim consent.

**STATE OF TASMANIA v ASHLEY LUKE HAINTZ                                     GEASON J**
**COMMENTS ON PASSING SENTENCE                                         4 DECEMBER 2019**

"I note that some of the offences involving breaches of the police family violence order occurred with the apparent consent of the complainant.  Because of the multitude of circumstances, including emotional vulnerabilities which typically attend relationships such as this one, I do not approach breaches said to have occurred with the consent of the victim as mitigated by reason of that fact.  They are breaches of a court order designed to protect the vulnerable.  Unless enforced through the imposition of penalties for their breach, the efficacy of the system of family violence orders is eroded. I have approached the fixing of penalty on that basis, notwithstanding the way I have ultimately framed that penalty.  The failure to observe a court order intended to protect the innocent victims of abuse involves a discrete element of criminality required to be reflected in penalty, subject of course to the principles of totality which are engaged in a case like this.  The nature of the offending to which you have pleaded guilty is insidious.  It is difficult to detect because it occurs in the home and the ease with which powerful, physically strong and dominant people can inflict serious physical and other harm upon their partners in private requires sentences which deter such behaviour and demonstrate that the courts will not tolerate it.  General deterrence therefore looms large in framing a sentence.  Your conduct occurs in the face of court orders intended to protect the complainant and as I have noted, that is aggravating. Your conduct was protracted and unprovoked. It involved choking.  The complainant was unable to defend herself."

### Destroy Property (co-owned by offender)

To allow one impunity for damaging property jointly owned would allow a person to destroy the entire house with impunity. Tenants in common -- the decision of one party to unilaterally destroy property owned in common destroys the propriatal right of the other owner.

Section 267(2) of the Code states as a preamble to the chapter on unlawful injury to property, that having a part interest in (partial ownership) is not a defence

**267.**   **Injuries to property**

(2)  No person is justified in causing any injury to property by the fact that he has a partial interest therein.

For summary matters refer to code s 273 and code princples as like offence.

Covers: Sentencing aims and prosecutorial suggestions for family violence matters.

Her Honour (Magi Edwards) noted that sentencing aims relevant to family violence offences will likely include general deterrence, specific deterrence (in the case of repeat offenders) and denunciation and vindication of the victims. She also suggested that prosecution look at making greater use of Victim Impact Statements and, where relevant, BWC footage

Covers: Judicial statements on the serious nature of family/domestic violence and sentencing aims.

**Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 (22 November 2021)**

25 The nature of family or domestic violence needs to be noted and steadfastly borne in mind. "Family violence must always be regarded as a serious matter": *Bonde v Ellery* [2016 TASSC 43] per Brett J at [27]. In *Director of Public Prosecutions v Karklins* [2018 TASCCA 6], [29 Tas R 373] at [92], Geason J (with whom Blow CJ and I agreed) said:

Domestic violence is properly regarded as a most serious form of offending, frequently hidden from view, and thus difficult to detect. The court has a symbolic function. Censure for domestic violence should be communicated through the sentences which are imposed. Community attitudes to it are changing: *R v Kilic* [(2016) HCA 48; 259 CLR 256]. Strong denunciation is called for, and general deterrence is a primary consideration. For crimes which are difficult to detect, the consequences of discovery must be severe enough to counter the perception that their commission is a risk worth taking."

(Practitioner heading preserved: "FV is serious as fuq")

# Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)

<!-- block_028 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Opinion of victim in FV sentencing (Brown v Jones and related authorities)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 - Victim Attitude in Sentencing] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: weight of complainant's forgiveness in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: victim forgiveness, sentencing mitigation, family violence sentencing, Brown v Jones, Director of Public Prosecutions v Chatters]

Covers: The treatment of the complainant's attitude of forgiveness in sentencing in Brown v Jones and related cases.

For mitigation it means nothing.

**Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 (22 November 2021)**

Porter AJ citing among others DPP(Acting) v JCN -- the attitude's of the complainant with respect to forgiveness carries little weight.

[31] As to the attitude of the complainant, to the extent it was suggested it was one of forgiveness, it carries little weight: Director of Public Prosecutions v Chatters [2011] TASCCA 8, [21 Tas R 26 at [60], Menichelli v Tasmania [2009] TASSC 111, [19 Tas R 299 at [16]. In both cases reference was made to R v Palu [2002] NSWCCA 381, [134 A Crim R 174 in which the New South Wales Court of Appeal said the attitude of the victim cannot be allowed to interfere with the proper exercise of the sentencing discretion. The Court went on to say that sentencing proceedings are not a private matter between the victim and the offender, and that a serious crime as a wrong committed against the community at large and the community itself is entitled to retribution. See also R v Burton [2008] NSWCCA 128 at [102]--[106].

32 That matter of established principle has particular application in cases of family violence. I would respectfully agree with the following comments of Pearce J (with whom Blow CJ and Tennent J agreed) in Director of Public Prosecutions (Acting) v JCN [2015] TASFC 13 at [20]:

"The response to family violence is often complex. Family violence offences are not uncommonly accompanied by support of a perpetrator by a victim and reluctance on the part of the victim to assist a criminal prosecution. That is so for a range of possible factors including fear and a wish to preserve relationships, even dysfunctional and violent ones, for the sake of loyalty, affection, companionship, economic and domestic support. Sometimes those motivations are misguided but persist nevertheless. As a result, victims sometimes act in a way that seems to an objective observer to be incongruence and difficult to understand."

### Chunk 2 — Section 29A: declaration requires FVO and Court Considerations (Magi Edwards notes)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 s29A - Court Considerations (Magi Edwards)] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure / annotation] [TOPIC: when s29A declaration made and factors court considers] [CONCEPTS: s29A, declaration, FVO requirement, Magistrate considerations, family violence declarations]

Covers: Court considerations for declarations under s29A and the matters Magistrate Magi Edwards said she would consider (non-exhaustive list and practice notes).

29A

Where declaration made, there must be an FVO, either new or extant.

### Court Considerations (as discussed by Magi Edwards). 

In relation to the declarations under s29A and the associated provisions, Her Honour noted that the statutory threshold is *not a high one*. Both sides should identify when this is triggered and be in a position to address the Court as to whether a declaration is warranted. Section 29A(3) identifies the matters to which the Court is to have regard, but gives a wide scope to that enquiry, noting that s29A(3)(d) allows the Court to look at any other matter which it considers to be relevant.

Her Honour provided a (non-exhaustive) list of matters that she will look at, noting that other Magistrates may consider different factors to be relevant, as follows:

- Gravity and seriousness of the matters on which the defendant is currently being sentenced.

- Risk factors (prior matters, any reports available relating to the defendant).

- Antecedents -- in particular, the gravity of any previous, like offending.

- Whether there is, or has been, more than a single complainant -- a propensity to behave violently in a relationship elevates the risk.

- Escalation in offending.

- Persistence in offending, especially if previous sentences have failed to deter the defendant.

- Personal circumstances.

- Any recognition or insight by the defendant into their behaviour.

- Any attempt to hide the offending conduct.

- A lack of remorse, e.g. if the matter proceeded to hearing.

Her Honour clarified that, from her perspective, she considers the threshold can be met by three (or more) offences being dealt with at court on the same day, even in the absence of prior matters (provided the offences themselves have occurred on different dates). Her Honour also indicated she would be receptive to submissions regarding the length of a declaration, or a further declaration by way of an effective extension (if an existing serial family violence perpetrator is being sentenced for new offending); also to deferring the question of whether to make the declaration or not, if defence are seeking the opportunity to demonstrate rehabilitation.

(Crafty submission to make when there are significant priors to support a 29A:

*"Sentences of imprisonment for offences of violence have not acted as a deterrent. As the sentencing judge observed, the appellant was not to be sentenced for his previous offences, but his record of violent offending demonstrated that the assaults under consideration were \"not aberrations committed out of character\". In addition, as previous sentences of imprisonment have not deterred the appellant from committing violent offences, personal deterrence was an important factor in sentencing."*)

### Chunk 3 — Miller v McCullogh [2024] TASSC 61 (referred by Magi Edwards)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Miller v McCullogh [2024] TASSC 61 (11 November 2024)] [CITATION: Miller v McCullogh [2024] TASSC 61 - Rehabilitation and FV sentencing] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: rehabilitation steps and appeal against term of imprisonment in FV context] [CONCEPTS: rehabilitation, family violence sentencing, appeal, Miller v McCullogh, prior convictions]

Covers: Miller v McCullogh decision on rehabilitation between sentencing and offending and the impact on appeal.

Relevant cases referred to by Magi Edwards:

**Miller v McCullogh [2024] TASSC 61 (11 November 2024)**

**Held:** Def had taken appreciable rehabilitation steps between sentencing and offending, but given severity of physical violence in a FV context, appeal against term of imprisonment was rejected.

**Facts:** Def charged with multiple assaults -- non strangulation holding of neck, and emotional abuse. Def had priors for assault against vic, assault generally and breaching orders. Had PG before pre-set hearing date and had vol engaged in counselling to address issues before sentencing.

\[29\] As highlighted by counsel for the respondent, the maximum penalty for the Family Violence Act offence is two years' imprisonment. The other three all attract a maximum of 12 months. The applicant had prior convictions for violence, the most recent in April 2020 which led to a suspended prison term. When considering his earlier offending, in particular the family violence offences in 2019, it might be thought that the applicant may have been motivated to seek assistance with his mental health and alcohol abuse issues before being charged in August 2023 with the present matters, and his relationship ending as a result of his conduct.

\[30\] In any event, the fact remains that he did seek assistance both in relation to his mental health and specifically his alcohol abuse. Those rehabilitation attempts were pursued until he was remanded in custody on 22 April 2024. That fact and the potential continued rehabilitation efforts were not matters that had no significance in the sentencing process. Additionally, the applicant was entitled to some, but in the circumstances no great, credit for his pleas of guilty. The magistrate accepted an expression of remorse but treated it as a "late development". No doubt that was prompted by the initial pleas of not guilty and the very late change. Remorse seems first to have been expressed to Dr Little and given the developing insight in the sessions with him, it is reasonable to accept that it was then genuine.
