# Justices Rules 2003 r 54T — Jurisdiction of justices (Family Violence Act matters)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] [SECTION: r 54T] [CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) r 54T] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: jurisdiction of justices in Family Violence Act matters] [CONCEPTS: justices jurisdiction, interim FVO, bail under Family Violence Act, revocation of FVO, magistrate presence, bench composition, Family Violence Act powers, procedural limits, court constitution]

The rule in Justices Rules 2003 r 54T limits the jurisdiction exercisable by a court constituted by one or more justices when one justice is not a magistrate in proceedings under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas). Specifically, when the court is constituted by one or more justices (one of whom is not a magistrate), the bench may exercise only: (a) the granting or refusal of bail under section 12 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas); (b) the making, variation, extension or revocation of an interim family violence order under section 23 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas); and (c) the revocation (but not the making, variation or extension) of a family violence order under section 20 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas). This rule confines the substantive powers available to non‑magistrate justices in family violence proceedings, and indicates that more intrusive or substantive orders under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) should be reserved for courts constituted by magistrates where required by law or practice directions.

# Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 30 — Forms of FVO, PFVO and applications
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 30] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 30] [TYPE: procedural rule / statutory definition] [TOPIC: prescribed forms for orders and applications] [CONCEPTS: approved form, Chief Magistrate, Family Violence Order form, Interim FVO form, Police FVO, application form, court forms, filing requirements]

Section 30 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) requires that an FVO, a PFVO or an application under the Act be in a form approved by the Chief Magistrate. The practical effect is that applicants and police must use the prescribed forms approved by the Chief Magistrate when applying for an Interim Family Violence Order, a Family Violence Order, a Police Family Violence Order, or when seeking variation, extension or revocation. Use of the approved forms ensures uniformity of procedure and compliance with registry practice directions and facilitates the clerk’s handling and filing of applications under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas).

# Magistrates Court Act 1987 (Tas) s 15AA — Chief Magistrate practice directions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Magistrates Court Act 1987 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 15AA] [CITATION: Magistrates Court Act 1987 (Tas) s 15AA] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: Chief Magistrate practice directions and filing rules] [CONCEPTS: practice directions, Chief Magistrate, lower courts practice, filing in registry, vary or revoke directions, compliance with practice directions]

Magistrates Court Act 1987 (Tas) s 15AA empowers the Chief Magistrate to issue, vary or revoke practice directions in relation to practice and procedure of the lower courts, to publish those directions, and to specify circumstances in which initiating process must be filed in a registry. The statute also mandates that the practice and procedure of the lower courts be in accordance with practice directions unless other statutory instruments or Acts provide otherwise. This provision provides the statutory foundation for the Chief Magistrate’s approval of Family Violence Act forms and for the Practice Direction No. 3 of 2005 approving specific family violence forms.

# Practice Direction No. 3 of 2005 — Approved forms for Family Violence matters
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: approval of Family Violence Act forms; oral applications] [CONCEPTS: approved forms, Chief Magistrate, Family Violence Order, Interim FVO, application for variation, extension, revocation, oral application implication, registry practice]

Practice Direction No. 3 of 2005, issued by the Chief Magistrate under Magistrates Court Act 1987 (Tas) s 15AA, approves the specific forms for Family Violence Order, Interim Family Violence Order, Application for Family Violence Order, Application for variation of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order, Application for extension of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order, and Application for revocation of a Police Family Violence Order or a Family Violence Order. The direction records that these are not oral forms and thereby implies that oral applications for an IFVO lead primarily to exercise of judicial discretion under Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 23 rather than functioning as a formal application on the approved form. The direction provides the basis for registry practice that emphasises written, signed forms for family violence proceedings.

# Justices Rules 2003 r 54L — Content requirements for applications under the Family Violence Act
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] [SECTION: r 54L] [CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) r 54L] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: application contents; statutory declaration and affidavits] [CONCEPTS: application requirements, specify order sought, statutory declaration, police applicant, affidavit, filing with clerk, evidentiary form, applicant statements]

Justices Rules 2003 r 54L prescribes the content of an application under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas). An application must specify the order sought; include either (i) for a police officer applicant, a statutory declaration that the information in the application is true to the best of the officer’s knowledge and belief, or (ii) for any other applicant, an affidavit to the same effect; and must be filed with the clerk. The rule therefore mandates documentary verification of factual assertions by police (statutory declaration) or private applicants (affidavit) and requires filing for registry processing.

# Justices Rules 2003 r 54N — Service of family violence applications
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] [SECTION: r 54N] [CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) r 54N] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: service of applications; endorsement and lodging] [CONCEPTS: personal service, sealed copy, parties, Commissioner of Police service, endorsement memorandum, lodging with clerk, police revocation exception, returnable district]

Justices Rules 2003 r 54N requires that, as soon as practicable after filing, the applicant must cause a sealed copy of the application to be personally served on: (a) all other parties in the case of an application for an FVO or for variation/extension/revocation of an FVO; or (b) all other parties and the Commissioner of Police in the case of an application to vary/extend/revoke a PFVO. The rule also requires the server to endorse the application with a memorandum stating day, place and mode of service, sign that memorandum, and lodge the endorsed copy with the clerk for the district in which the matter is returnable. Service is not required where a police officer is the applicant for revocation of an FVO or PFVO. These procedures ensure parties and, where appropriate, the Commissioner of Police receive notice and that the court record documents service.

# Justices Rules 2003 r 54S — Power of justices to dispense with Part 4 compliance
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] [SECTION: r 54S] [CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) r 54S] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: dispensing with procedural requirements] [CONCEPTS: dispense with Part 4, procedural waiver, terms and conditions, judicial discretion, Family Violence Act compliance, practice and procedure]

Justices Rules 2003 r 54S authorises justices to dispense, upon such terms and conditions as they think necessary, with the need for a party to proceedings under Part 4 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) to comply with any provision of that Part. This provides procedural flexibility to the court to relax strict compliance with Part 4 requirements where justice or practicality requires, subject to the court’s imposition of conditions to ensure fairness and protection of affected persons.

# Justices Rules 2003 r 54P — Hearing procedures: affidavits, attendance and cross‑examination
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] [SECTION: r 54P] [CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) r 54P] [TYPE: evidentiary rule / procedural rule] [TOPIC: affidavits and cross‑examination at FVO hearings] [CONCEPTS: affidavits admissible, attendance not required, notice to attend, cross‑examination, refusal to use affidavit, adjournment, 10 clear working days]

Justices Rules 2003 r 54P provides that at the hearing of an application under Part 9B (Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) procedures) evidence may be given by affidavit; the deponent is not required to attend unless a party or the justice so requires; a party may by written notice require the deponent to attend for cross‑examination; and if the deponent fails to attend the court may refuse to allow the affidavit to be used, allow it with conditions, or adjourn until the deponent attends. Notice to require attendance for cross‑examination must be given not less than 10 clear working days before the proceedings. These rules set the framework balancing efficiency by affidavit and the right to test evidence by cross‑examination.

# Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 r 55 — Form and signing of affidavits
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas)] [SECTION: r 55] [CITATION: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) r 55] [TYPE: evidentiary rule / procedural rule] [TOPIC: affidavit form and execution requirements] [CONCEPTS: affidavit form, first person, numbered paragraphs, annexures, signature on each page, officer attestation, title of action, identifying deponent]

Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 r 55 prescribes the formal requirements for affidavits used in court proceedings. An affidavit must specify the action title, be drawn up in the first person, state the deponent’s name, address and occupation (or description if none), be divided into consecutively numbered paragraphs, specify monetary figures in numerals, contain an attestation by the person before whom it was sworn (full name of deponent, day and place sworn), and be signed on each page by the deponent and the person before whom it was sworn. Annexures or exhibits must be identified by title of action and signed by the attesting officer. The rule also allows the court to receive an affidavit despite irregularity of form unless otherwise ordered.

# Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 r 56 — Contents of affidavits and hearsay in interlocutory matters
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas)] [SECTION: r 56] [CITATION: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) r 56] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: facts deposed personally; hearsay exceptions in interlocutory affidavits] [CONCEPTS: personal knowledge, hearsay in interlocutory applications, source statement, grounds for belief, affidavit content, interlocutory evidence]

Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 r 56 requires affidavits to be confined to facts within the deponent’s own knowledge. However, an affidavit filed for an interlocutory application may contain statements based on information received if the affidavit also states the sources of that information and the grounds for believing it true. This allows limited hearsay material to be used in interlocutory family violence proceedings (for example, police reports) provided proper foundation for the information and the deponent’s belief is supplied.

# Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 r 74 — Hearing interlocutory applications
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas)] [SECTION: r 74] [CITATION: Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 (Tas) r 74] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: interlocutory hearing procedure; remote attendance; adjournments] [CONCEPTS: open court, telephone appearance, video link, adjournment limits, affidavit proof, interlocutory procedure]

Magistrates Court (Civil Division) Rules 1998 r 74 provides interlocutory hearing procedures: ordinarily such applications are heard in open court; with leave a person may appear by telephone or video link; the hearing may be adjourned on no more than two occasions and only to a fixed date; and facts at an interlocutory hearing may be proved by affidavit. These rules govern procedural fairness and efficiency in interlocutory family violence hearings.

# Oaths Act 2001 (Tas) s 3 — Interpretation of "affidavit"
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Oaths Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 3] [CITATION: Oaths Act 2001 (Tas) s 3] [TYPE: interpretive principle / evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: definition of affidavit and related documents] [CONCEPTS: affidavit definition, statutory declaration, affirmation, deposition, complainant statement, evidentiary scope, statutory interpretation]

Oaths Act 2001 (Tas) s 3 defines "affidavit" to include any affirmation, statutory declaration or other declaration; any acknowledgment, examination or attestation; and, for the purpose of s 7, any deposition or statement made by an informant or complainant. The section clarifies that statutory declarations may fall within the procedural category of "affidavit" for purposes of court forms and evidentiary rules, but practitioners should comply with formal affidavit and statutory declaration requirements under the Magistrates Court Rules and the Oaths Act when lodging evidence in family violence proceedings.

# Standing of police and amendment via s 31(2A) — McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100 context
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine / case authority] [CASE: McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100] [TOPIC: police party status in family violence proceedings; legislative response] [CONCEPTS: standing, party status, police as party, leave to vary, statutory amendment, s 31(2A), private applications, McLean v Kemp]

The doctrinal rule arising from McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100 is that, historically, police were found not to be a party to certain family violence proceedings, prompting Parliament to amend the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) by introducing s 31(2A) to permit police participation as a party in specified circumstances. The Supreme Court decision in McLean v Kemp concerned leave to hear an application and concluded that police were not automatically a party; Parliament subsequently remedied that procedural gap. The case highlights that police standing in private applications depends on statutory text and that s 31(2A) must be considered when determining whether the Commissioner of Police or police officers are parties to proceedings.

# Ambiguity from Lacroix decisions — fresh application vs variation/extension
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lacroix v Lacroix [2015] TASSC 42; Lacroix v Lacroix [2018] TASFC 8] [TOPIC: fresh application vs variation/extension; effect on police party status] [CONCEPTS: fresh application, variation, extension, s 14(8), s 15, Part 3, s 31(2A), application classification, procedural ambiguity]

The Lacroix decisions (Lacroix v Lacroix [2015] TASSC 42 and Lacroix v Lacroix [2018] TASFC 8) illustrate an ambiguity in family violence practice: whether proceedings that are effectively a "fresh application" rather than a mere extension or variation should be treated as applications under Part 3 of the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas). That classification matters because, if characterised as Part 3 applications, s 31(2A) may engage the Commissioner of Police as a party. The Lacroix line of authority raises the practical question whether a proceeding that changes the legal character of an order triggers police standing and different procedural protections and thresholds.

# Interaction of s 14(8) PFVO revocation and subsequent applications (s 15, Part 3)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 14(8); s 15; Part 3] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(8), s 15] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: PFVO revocation and conversion to IFVO/FVO proceedings] [CONCEPTS: PFVO revocation, automatic revocation, IFVO, FVO, s 15 application, Part 3 proceedings, legal character, police involvement]

The interpretive issue is that Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(8) provides that a Police Family Violence Order (PFVO) is automatically revoked where an Interim Family Violence Order (IFVO) or Family Violence Order (FVO) is made in relation to both parties. Where, however, a party files an application under s 15, it may be necessary to construe whether that application should be treated as made pursuant to Part 3 (which governs family violence orders), thereby invoking procedural rules including possible engagement of the Commissioner of Police under s 31(2A). This interpretive approach can alter who is a party, the applicable thresholds for orders, and whether a PFVO remains operative while the court considers formal orders.

# Family Court orders and Family Violence Act 2004 — definition and inconsistency powers
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: definition of family court order; s 68R referenced] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 68R; Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) Part VII] [TYPE: statutory definition / interpretive principle] [TOPIC: family court orders, inconsistency powers, suspending parenting orders] [CONCEPTS: family court order definition, Part 7 Family Law Act, parenting orders, inconsistency, s 68R, suspension of parenting orders, federal-state interaction]

Under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas), a "family court order" is defined to mean an order made under Part VII (parenting and children) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth). The Family Violence Act limits the operation of inconsistency powers (for example from s 68 onwards) to parenting orders under Part VII; the Family Violence Act may suspend or modify a Family Court parenting order to the extent of inconsistency (per s 68R). Practitioners must check the source Part of a Family Court order before relying on the Family Violence Act’s inconsistency powers, as not all Family Court orders fall within the defined category.

# PFVO — authority to issue and evidentiary presumption under s 35(3)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 35(3)] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 35(3)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: presumption of existence and validity of an order] [CONCEPTS: s 35(3), rebuttable presumption, proving authority, in force, alleged order evidence, evidentiary burden, presumption displacement]

Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 35(3) creates a statutory evidentiary presumption that an allegation that an order was made is evidence that the order was made and was in force at the time of the alleged contravention. Practically, this means that in prosecutions or hearings concerning alleged contraventions of a PFVO or FVO the allegation that the order existed operates as evidence of its making and validity; the presumption is rebuttable but requires positive evidence to displace it (for example proof that the issuing officer lacked authority). The effect is to ease proof for the prosecution while preserving a defence if the respondent produces credible evidence that an order was not validly made.

# McKenna v Smith [2014] TASMC 11 — s 35(3) and acting rank inference
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: McKenna v Smith [2014] TASMC 11] [TOPIC: s 35(3) presumption and officer authority to issue PFVO] [CONCEPTS: McKenna v Smith, s 35(3), acting sergeant, presumption of validity, rebuttable presumption, authority to issue, Police Service Act s 34, evidentiary inference]

McKenna v Smith [2014] TASMC 11 applied Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 35(3) to conclude that the allegation that a PFVO was made served as evidence that it was made pursuant to s 14 (including by an officer of requisite rank). The court accepted that, absent specific evidence that an officer did not hold the necessary acting rank (for example acting sergeant), the presumption should stand and the prosecution need not prove authority to act in rank. The decision emphasises that to rebut s 35(3) a respondent must provide positive evidence showing lack of authority; mere absence of proof is insufficient.

# Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) — acting rank, delegation and evidentiary certificates
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Service Act 2003 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 34; s 8; s 95] [CITATION: Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) s 34; s 8; s 95] [TYPE: statutory definition / evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: acting appointments and evidence of delegation] [CONCEPTS: acting sergeant, temporary appointment, delegation power, certificate evidence, s 95 certificate, delegation document, authority to act]

The Police Service Act 2003 (Tas) contains provisions relevant to PFVO issuing authority: s 34 permits an officer to be appointed to act temporarily in a higher rank and vest that officer with the powers of that rank; s 8 enables the Commissioner of Police to delegate powers vested in the Commissioner; and s 95 allows tendering a certificate as evidence that such a delegation occurred. These statutory mechanisms support the operation of PFVO issuance by an officer described by rank (including acting rank) and provide documentary and certificate evidence to support authority to issue when challenged.

# Acts Interpretation Act (Tas) s 23AA — references to rank sufficient
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Acts Interpretation Act (Tas)] [SECTION: s 23AA] [CITATION: Acts Interpretation Act (Tas) s 23AA] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: statutory references to rank versus person] [CONCEPTS: Acts Interpretation, reference to rank, sufficient description, person identification, statutory construction, rank as identifier]

Acts Interpretation Act s 23AA (Tas) provides interpretive assistance by allowing a statutory reference to a rank to be sufficient without naming an individual. In the context of PFVOs, this means that an order said to be made by an officer of a specified rank can stand as valid in the absence of naming the individual, and combined with Police Service Act delegation and certificate provisions, supports the practical operation of PFVO issuance and the rebuttable presumption under Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 35(3).

# PFVO conditions — Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(3)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 14(3)] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(3)] [TYPE: statutory definition / offence element] [TOPIC: permitted PFVO conditions] [CONCEPTS: vacate premises, not enter premises, surrender firearm, refrain from harassment, distance restriction, no contact, permitted restrictions, PFVO scope]

Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(3) specifies the conditions that a Police Family Violence Order (PFVO) may impose. A PFVO may require the subject to: (a) vacate premises; (b) not enter specified premises or only enter on stated conditions; (c) surrender firearms or other weapons; (d) refrain from harassing, threatening, verbally abusing or assaulting the affected person, affected child or other named person; (e) keep a specified distance from an affected person, affected child, other named person or certain premises; and (f) refrain from contacting an affected person, affected child or other named person directly or indirectly except under specified conditions. These provisions define the permissible scope of immediate police orders to protect safety.

# PFVO variation/extension/revocation procedure — leave, court vs inspector roles
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 14(7); s 14(8); s 15] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14(7), s 14(8), s 15] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: PFVO variation, inspector authority, automatic revocation] [CONCEPTS: PFVO variation, inspector consent, court application, leave requirement, substantial change, automatic revocation, IFVO conversion, procedural threshold]

The procedural framework for PFVOs under the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) distinguishes between actions that can be taken by an inspector and those requiring judicial intervention. Section 14(7) allows an application to vary a PFVO to be made to an inspector but only with the consent of both parties; court applications to vary, extend or revoke a PFVO ordinarily do not require leave, but a "second attempt" to convert a PFVO into an FVO (for example where the matter is at large or previously determined) may require leave of the court demonstrating a substantial change in circumstances. Section 14(8) provides that a PFVO is automatically revoked where an IFVO or FVO is made in relation to both parties, affecting the PFVO’s ongoing operation.

# Revocation of PFVO — standard and considerations distinct from making FVO
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 14; s 23; general objects] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 14; s 23] [TYPE: interpretive principle / sentencing principle] [TOPIC: standard for revocation of PFVO vs FVO; paramount safety consideration] [CONCEPTS: revocation standard, FVO making threshold, safety paramount, psychological wellbeing, affected children, administrative objects, evidentiary burden, judicial reluctance to revoke]

The legal standard for revocation of a Police Family Violence Order (PFVO) differs from the tests applied when making a Family Violence Order (FVO). An FVO typically requires the court to be satisfied that family violence has occurred and is likely to occur again; revocation of a PFVO does not require the same indicia. Nevertheless, the Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) makes the safety, psychological wellbeing and interests of people affected by family violence the paramount considerations in administration. Courts will generally be reluctant to revoke a PFVO absent strong evidence (including psychological evidence where only recent physical violence exists) showing revocation is necessary to protect the protected party’s welfare. Where a court finds the order validly made, lack of positive evidence of invalidity will not suffice to displace the presumption of validity.

# McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100 — leave threshold and substantial change
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100] [TOPIC: leave to vary or set aside orders; substantial change test] [CONCEPTS: McLean v Kemp, leave to vary, substantial change, relevant circumstances, magistrate discretion, family violence proceedings, evidentiary threshold]

McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100 addressed leave to hear an application to change family violence orders and emphasised that a magistrate must be satisfied there has been a "substantial change in the relevant circumstances" since the original order was made before granting leave. The terms "substantial change" and "relevant circumstances" were held to be undefined in the Act and were given their ordinary meaning as a significant or obvious change in circumstances affecting the parties. The decision distinguished the threshold for granting leave from the threshold for changing orders substantively and demonstrates the higher test required to permit relitigation or a second application concerning family violence orders.

# Lacroix v Lacroix authorities — procedural ambiguity (fresh application v variation)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lacroix v Lacroix [2015] TASSC 42; Lacroix v Lacroix [2018] TASFC 8] [TOPIC: characterisation of applications; party status and procedural consequence] [CONCEPTS: Lacroix, fresh application, variation, extension, Part 3, s 31(2A), police party status, procedural classification, appellate guidance]

The Lacroix decisions (Lacroix v Lacroix [2015] TASSC 42; Lacroix v Lacroix [2018] TASFC 8) provide authorities bearing on whether a new application should be treated as a fresh application rather than as a variation or extension of an existing order. That characterisation affects whether Part 3 procedures apply and whether the Commissioner of Police is engaged as a party under s 31(2A). The cases illustrate appellate consideration of procedural classification and inform practitioners that the substance of the application (not merely its label) determines the applicable procedural regime and potential involvement of police.

# Practice implication — oral IFVO applications and magistrate discretion under s 23
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 23] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 23] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: oral applications for Interim Family Violence Orders] [CONCEPTS: oral application, IFVO, magistrate discretion, s 23, interim relief, forms not oral, Practice Direction No. 3, immediate protection]

An oral application for an Interim Family Violence Order (IFVO) functions primarily as an invitation for the magistrate to exercise judicial discretion under Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 23 to make an interim order rather than serving as a formal application on the approved form. Because Practice Direction No. 3 of 2005 prescribes non‑oral approved forms for IFVOs, an oral approach typically leads the magistrate to consider whether immediate interim relief is appropriate under s 23 and whether subsequent formal application on approved forms will be required for longer term orders.

<!-- block_029 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Restriction on JP's: Jurisdiction of justices (Justices Rules 2003 s54T)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] [SECTION: 54T] [CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 s54T - Jurisdiction of justices] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: jurisdiction limits for courts constituted by one or more justices under Family Violence Act] [CONCEPTS: JP jurisdiction, Family Violence Act, interim FVO, bail, revocation, Justices Rules, magistrate limitation, IFVO, PFVO, s54T]

This chunk covers the jurisdiction limits of courts constituted by one or more justices under the Family Violence Act.

[**Justices Rules 2003**](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/sr-2003-184)

**54T.**   **Jurisdiction of justices**

[*\[Rule 54T Substituted by S.R. 2009, No. 77, Applied:08 Jul 2009\] *](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2009-07-08/sr-2009-077#GS5@EN)The court, when constituted by one or more justices (one of whom is not a magistrate), may exercise only the following jurisdiction under the[Family Violence Act 2004](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2014-04-16/act-2004-067) :

\(a\) the granting or refusal of bail under [section 12](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2014-04-16/act-2004-067#GS12@EN) of that Act;

\(b\) the making, variation, extension or revocation of an interim FVO under [section 23](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2014-04-16/act-2004-067#GS23@EN) of that Act;

\(c\) the revocation (but not the making, variation or extension) of an FVO under [section 20](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/2014-04-16/act-2004-067#GS20@EN) of that Act.

### Chunk 2 — Standing in private applications (practitioner commentary)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: standing of police/parties in private family violence applications and legislative response] [CONCEPTS: standing, private applications, police party status, s31(2A), McLean v Kemp, Lacroix ambiguity, s14(8), PFVO revocation, IFVO, FVO, practitioner note]

This chunk covers practitioner observations about standing in private family violence applications and legislative changes affecting police party status.

Generally none. However:

There is authority that had deemed that police were not a party to family violence proceedings and that the Act was amended thereafter to permit it. And I did not have it to hand. It was the decision of the Supreme Court in *McLean v Kemp* \[2005\] TASSC 100. Parliament responded by introducing s31 (2A) to remedy the situation.

 

An ambiguity arises if similarly, to *Lacroix v Lacroix* \[2015\] TASSC 42, *Lacroix v Lacroix *\[2018\] TASFC 8, a fresh application, rather than extension/ variation, is filed.

 

It may be that given that under s14(8) the PFVO is revoked making of an IFVO or FVO, an application under s15 is be construed in these situations to also be made pursuant to Part 3. Which would mean that s31(2A) would engage the Police as a party.

 

Otherwise the situation would be that a PFVO could be weakened significantly by the making of a basic FVO without further input from the Commissioner for Police, despite a police officer being satisfied that a PFVO on particular terms was required to protect the safety wellbeing and interests of protected party and or any affected children.

---

### Chunk 3 — McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100 (case authority excerpt)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100] [CITATION: McLean v Kemp [2005] TASSC 100 - Leave to hear application] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Supreme Court decision re leave to change family violence order; substantial change test] [CONCEPTS: McLean v Kemp, leave to change FVO, substantial change, relevant circumstances, magistrate reasoning, fear evidence, Part 4, family violence]

This chunk covers the Supreme Court decision in McLean v Kemp concerning leave to hear an application to change an FVO.

**Decision was made in the context of granting leave to hear the application \*This is not the same as a determination that the order should in fact be changed! Just a question of leave. They are not the same. They are different thresholds!**

\[23\] The learned magistrate was required to be satisfied before granting leave that there had been a substantial change in the relevant circumstances since the order was made on 26 April.

\[24\] The words "substantial change" and "relevant circumstances" are not defined in the [Act](http://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fva2004158/). The ordinary meaning of those words would suggest that what was intended was that the Court be satisfied there had been a significant or obvious change in the circumstances involving the parties affected by the FVO since the date it was made.

28 There was clear material before the learned magistrate that neither the applicant nor Mr Kemp wanted the no contact provisions in the FVO to continue.

29 The learned magistrate took the view that he did not accept that the applicant could not have been scared of Mr Kemp if he did what she said. However, on the material before him, while he might reasonably have been satisfied the applicant had indeed been scared prior to the making of the FVO, there was no evidence apart from hers as to what had since occurred and she unequivocally said she now was not fearful. That is, apart from anything else, a clear change of circumstances.

---

### Chunk 4 — Family Court Orders (practitioner note re definition and s68R)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: Part 7 references; s68R] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 - Family Court order definition & s68R inconsistency powers] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: definition of Family Court order and limits of Family Law Act powers in FVO context] [CONCEPTS: Family Court order, Part 7, Family Law Act, parenting orders, inconsistency, s68R, FVO suspension, jurisdiction, check orders]

This chunk covers the definition of Family Court orders in the Family Violence Act and the limits on inconsistency powers under the Family Law Act.

Family court order defined in FV act as one made under Part 7 (children) of the Family Law Act. There are other types of orders from other parts that are made, so be sure to check.

Family law act powers of inconsistency (eg. 68 onwards) are also limited to considerations of Parenting orders under part 7. So can suspend Family Court parenting order for FVO if inconsistent. Per s. 68R.

<!-- block_030 master -->

# Victim Impact Statements — Overview
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: victim impact statements overview] [CONCEPTS: victim impact statement, sentencing, victim voice, voluntary statement, therapeutic benefit, victim support, sentencing considerations, admissibility]

Victim impact statements (VIS) are voluntary written statements by victims that describe injury, loss or damage and the effects of an offence and are intended to inform the court’s sentencing consideration. A VIS can be furnished after a person is found guilty or pleads guilty to an indictable offence and, in limited summary cases, where death or serious injury results or where the offence is a family violence offence. The VIS process offers often the only formal opportunity for victims to describe personal physical, emotional, social and financial effects of the crime; participation is optional and victims may seek help from Witness Assistance Scheme (WAS) officers or Victim Support Services to prepare the statement. Prosecutors should ensure victims understand that a VIS is not a substitute for evidentiary proof of medical or psychiatric diagnoses; where clinical conditions are asserted, independent reports from qualified health professionals should be obtained. VIS material commonly informs sentencing considerations such as harm, culpability and personal circumstances but the contents must be limited to consequences directly resulting from the offence of conviction.

# Victim Impact Statements — Permitted Content (What a VIS Should Include)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: permitted content of victim impact statements] [CONCEPTS: physical injury, psychological harm, loss of prospects, employment impact, financial loss, supporting reports, direct consequence, victim experience]

A victim impact statement should be limited to particulars of injury, loss or damage suffered as a direct consequence of the offence and a description of the effects of the offence on the victim. Typical permitted content includes: (1) details of physical injuries and their impact; (2) emotional and psychological damage; (3) changes in behaviour, thoughts, attitudes, coping skills, social life or relationships (comparisons of life before and after the offence); (4) impact on employment, education and future prospects; (5) financial impacts; and (6) relevant supporting medical or psychological reports. All material should relate directly to the offence for which the offender has been convicted. Including supporting clinical documentation strengthens assertions of medical or psychological harm; however, the VIS itself does not convert the material into expert evidence for contested medical issues.

# Victim Impact Statements — Prohibited Content (What a VIS Must Not Include)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: prohibited content of victim impact statements] [CONCEPTS: abusive comments, irrelevant impact, unconvicted conduct, sentencing suggestion, hearsay limits, scope limitation, relevance]

A victim impact statement must not include material that is irrelevant to the convicted offence or prejudicial beyond the victim’s personal experience of consequences directly caused by that offence. Specifically a VIS must not contain: abusive or derogatory comments about the accused; any impact that did not result from the crime concerned; particulars of conduct of which the accused has not been convicted; or any suggestion as to what the sentence should be. Irrelevant or prejudicial material may be excluded by the court and including impermissible content risks redaction or exclusion under the court’s power to omit parts considered irrelevant to sentencing.

# Victim Impact Statements — Presentation Options and Confidentiality
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: presenting a VIS in court] [CONCEPTS: written VIS, read aloud, nominated reader, confidentiality, copy to accused, judge's access, courtroom privacy]

A victim may present a victim impact statement to the court in one of three ways: (1) provide a written document for the judge to read privately; (2) read the VIS aloud to the court personally; or (3) nominate another person to read the VIS on their behalf. A written VIS is not automatically confidential: the prosecutor must provide a copy to the accused or the accused’s counsel, so the accused will ordinarily see the content. Judges frequently use extracts from VIS material in sentencing remarks. Victims should be informed that choosing the judge-only reading preserves courtroom privacy but does not prevent the accused seeing the document through disclosure to defence counsel.

# Victim Impact Statements — Practical Guidance When Reading and Challenge Procedures
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: reading VIS and dealing with challenges] [CONCEPTS: address to judge, not accused, defence challenge, witness box, proving material, legal consultation, victim consultation]

When a victim reads a VIS to the court, it should be addressed to the judge or magistrate and not directed to the accused. If defence counsel challenges any material in the VIS, the prosecutor must discuss the challenge with the victim and consider whether the challenged material needs to be proven. In rare circumstances the victim may be required to give evidence in the witness box to prove the contested factual assertions in the VIS; this occurs only if the defendant disputes factual claims that are material to sentencing and the court requires formal proof. Victims should be given advance notice of this possibility and legal or support assistance to prepare for any contested hearing.

# Prosecutorial Caution — VIS Is Not Expert Evidence of Psychiatric Illness
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: VIS not evidence of psychiatric illness] [CONCEPTS: psychiatric evidence, expert report, admissibility, medical report, probative value, sentencing material]

A victim impact statement is not, by itself, admissible evidence to establish that a victim has a psychiatric illness or disorder as a result of the crime; prosecutors must obtain a report from an appropriate expert where clinical diagnoses or psychiatric harm are asserted and central to sentencing or mitigation decisions. If a VIS contains assertions of psychiatric or medical conditions, the prosecutor should commission or obtain independent medical or psychological reports to verify the claims and enable the court to properly assess the probative weight of such matters during sentencing.

# Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 81A — Definitions (s81A(1))

<!-- block_030 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Victim Impact statements (practitioner commentary and overview)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Victim Impact Statements - Practitioner Commentary - Belbin ref] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Overview of victim impact statements, voluntariness, and prosecutor considerations] [CONCEPTS: victim impact statement, VIS, Sentencing Act s81A, prosecutor guidance, victim support]

Tactical coverage: This chunk covers practitioner commentary on victim impact statements, voluntariness, and prosecutor considerations.

See **Belbin v Bennett 2011 TASSC** for a duscission on the impact on the considerations for sentencing these can have.

Victim impact statements After a person has been found guilty of, or has pleaded guilty to, an indictable offence the law provides that the victim of the offence can make a victim impact statement (s81A of the Sentencing Act 1997). In respect of a summary offence, a VIS can be provided where death or serious injury has occurred as a result of the offence, or if it is a family violence offence. This is often the only opportunity for the victim to have their say. A VIS is entirely voluntary and if the victim feels it would be more traumatic to go through the process of preparing one then there is no obligation for them to do so. On the other hand, some people find the process of preparing and presenting a VIS therapeutic and want to have a role in the process. The decision whether or not to prepare a VIS can be discussed with a WAS officer who can also assist with the preparation of the document. Victim Support Services can also provide assistance with the preparation of a VIS.

Prosecutors should be aware that a VIS is not evidence to show that a victim is suffering from a psychiatric illness or disorder as a result of the crime. Where necessary, a report should be obtained from a relevant expert.

There are some limitations as to what can be included in a VIS. The statement must be limited to details elating only to the crime of which the offender is convicted. Following are some pointers to assist in the preparation of a VIS. It should include:

• details of any physical injuries caused by the crime and the impact of

these injuries

• details of emotional and psychological damage caused by the crime

• details of any changes in behaviour, thoughts, attitudes, coping skills,

social life or relationships with others (i.e. how life was before the crime

and how it has changed)

• details of the impact of the crime on employment, education or loss of

future prospects

• details of the financial impact of the crime

• relevant medical or psychological reports that support the VIS

It should not include:

• any abusive or derogatory comments about the accused

• any impact that is not the result of the crime concerned

• any conduct of which the accused has not been convicted

• any suggestion as to what the sentence should be

---

### Chunk 2 — Presenting a VIS to the court (options for presentation; reading guidance)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Victim Impact Statements - Presenting to Court - Options and Guidance] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Options for presenting a VIS to the court; reading protocol and consequences] [CONCEPTS: presenting VIS, handing up, victim reading, nominated reader, confidentiality, accused's access, sentencing use]

Tactical workflow for presenting a VIS to the court.

Presenting a VIS to the court

compuThere are three options as to how a VIS can be presented to the court. The first option is for the written document to be handed up to the judge to read to him or herself. Some people prefer this because no-one else in the courtroom will hear it. However, it must be borne in mind that a VIS is not an automatically confidential document. A copy must be given to the accused\'s lawyer so the accused will also see it. Also, judges regularly use extracts from a VIS in their sentencing comments.

The second option is for the victim to read their VIS to the court.

The third option is for the victim to nominate another person to read the VIS on their behalf

When reading out a VIS it should be addressed to the judge or magistrate, not directly to the accused. If defence counsel challenge any material in the VIS it will be necessary to discuss this with the victim. It is possible (though very unusual) that the victim may have to go into the witness box in order the prove the material.

---

### Chunk 3 — ENTENCING ACT 1997 - SECT 81A (legislative text excerpt)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: 81A] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s81A - Court may receive victim impact statement] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: s81A Court may receive victim impact statement (definitions and procedural provisions)] [CONCEPTS: victim impact statement, s81A, immediate family, indictable offence, rules of court]

Tactical coverage: Full text of s 81A governing victim impact statements and definitions.

**81A. Court may receive victim impact statement**

\(1\) In this section --

immediate family, in respect of a deceased victim, includes --

\(a\) the spouse or partner, within the meaning of the [[Relationships Act 2003]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ra2003173/), of the deceased victim; and

\(b\) a parent, guardian or step-parent of the deceased victim; and

\(c\) a child or stepchild of the deceased victim; and

\(d\) a brother, sister, stepbrother or stepsister of the deceased victim;

indictable offence means --

\(a\) an offence that is punishable on indictment even though in some instances it may be dealt with summarily; or

\(b\) any other offence that is prescribed for the purposes of this section;

victim, in respect of an offence, means --

\(a\) a person who has suffered injury, loss or damage as a direct consequence of the offence; and

\(b\) a member of the immediate family of a deceased victim of the offence.

\(2\) If a court finds a person guilty of an indictable offence, or a summary offence that has resulted in the death or serious injury of a person, or [a family violence offence]{.mark}, a victim of that offence may furnish to the court a written statement that --

\(a\) gives particulars of any injury, loss or damage suffered by the victim as a direct consequence of the offence; and

\(b\) describes the effects on the victim of the commission of the offence.

(2A) If a court finds a person guilty of an indictable offence, or a summary offence that has resulted in the death or serious injury of a person, or [a family violence offence,]{.mark} the court may, if it considers it appropriate to do so, allow a person, other than the victim of that offence, to furnish to the court a written statement, in lieu of a statement under subsection (2), that --

\(a\) gives particulars of the injury, loss or damage suffered by the victim as a direct consequence of the offence; and

\(b\) describes the effects on the victim of the commission of the offence.

\(3\) A statement referred to in subsection (2) or (2A) must comply with, and be furnished in accordance with, applicable Rules of Court or rules made under subsection (6).

\(4\) If the court finds a person guilty of an offence, the court must allow the victim, or the person who has furnished a statement under subsection (2A) (the \"other person\" ), or another person nominated by the victim or the other person, to read his or her statement to the court if the victim or the other person has so requested at the time of furnishing the statement to the court.

\(5\) .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  

\(6\) For the purposes of this section --

\(a\) Rules of Court may be made under section 12 of the Criminal Code Act 1924; and

\(b\) rules may be made under [[section 144]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ja1959119/s144.html) of the [[Justices Act 1959]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ja1959119/).

\(7\) This section does not derogate from [[section 81]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s81.html) and subsections (2) and (4) of that section apply to a statement furnished under this section.

### Chunk 4 — JUSTICES RULES 2003 - PART 9A (regs 54A–54I)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas)] [CITATION: Justices Rules 2003 (Tas) Part 9A - Victim Impact Statements (regs 54A-54I)] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Justices Rules Part 9A provisions governing victim impact statements in petty sessions court] [CONCEPTS: Justices Rules, VIS, rule 54A, rule 54I, court procedure, copies to offender, reading VIS]

Tactical coverage: Regulatory provisions governing victim impact statements in courts of petty sessions (regs 54A–54I).

**PART 9A - Victim Impact Statements 54A. Interpretation**

In this Part --

Court means a court of petty sessions;

victim means --

\(a\) a victim within the meaning of [[section 81A]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s81a.html) of the [[Sentencing Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/) or section 36A of the [[Youth Justice Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/yja1997185/); or

\(b\) a person allowed to furnish to the Court a written statement under [[section 81A(2A)]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s81a.html) of the [[Sentencing Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/) or section 36A(3) of the [[Youth Justice Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/yja1997185/);

victim impact statement means a statement furnished under [[section 81A]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s81a.html) of the [[Sentencing Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/), or section 36A of the [[Youth Justice Act 1997]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/yja1997185/), by a victim of an offence.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54B**

**54B. Duty of victim to provide copies of victim impact statement**

\(1\) A victim who wishes to furnish to the Court a victim impact statement is to provide the original of the statement to the prosecutor, and the prosecutor is to provide a copy of the statement to -- 

\(a\) the offender; or

\(b\) if the offender is represented, his or her counsel.

\(2\) On providing the statement under subrule (1), the victim is to advise the prosecutor whether he or she wishes to read the statement to the Court.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54C**

**54C. Provision of victim impact statement to Court**

The prosecutor is to --

\(a\) provide to the Court any victim impact statement relating to an offence which has been provided by a victim of the offence; and

\(b\) inform the Court whether the victim has requested to read the statement to the Court.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54D**

**54D. Reading of victim impact statement**

A victim impact statement is to be read to, or by, the Court before the Court passes sentence on the offender.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54E**

**54E. Postponement of reading of victim impact statement**

If a victim has requested to read a victim impact statement to the Court but is not present in Court, the Court may postpone the reading of the statement and is to cause notice of the time fixed for the reading of the statement to be given to the victim.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54F**

**54F. Power of victim to amend victim impact statement**

Subject to [[rule 54G]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_reg/jr2003168/s54g.html), a victim who has furnished a victim impact statement may amend the statement at any time before it is read to, or by, the Court.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54G**

**54G. Power of Court to exclude irrelevant material**

If the Court so directs, a victim is not to read out to the Court any part of the victim impact statement that the Court considers to be irrelevant to the proceedings.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54H**

**54H. Power of victim to withdraw victim impact statement**

A victim who has provided a victim impact statement may withdraw the statement at any time before it is read to, or by, the Court and, if he or she does so, the statement is not to be read to, or by, the Court.

**JUSTICES RULES 2003 - REG 54I**

**54I. Requirements for victim impact statement**

A victim may furnish to the Court only one victim impact statement and the statement --

\(a\) is to be legible; and

\(b\) is to be in the English language; and

\(c\) may be either printed or handwritten; and

\(d\) except with the leave of the Court, may not be longer than 20 pages, inclusive of medical reports and other annexures; and

\(e\) may, but need not, be in a form approved by the Chief Magistrate.

### Chunk 3: "Breach of the peace defined" — R v Howell
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Definition of "breach of the peace" as authority for police/common law power] [CONCEPTS: breach of the peace, R v Howell, Watkins LJ, common law power, public order, unlawful assembly, riot] [CASE: R v Howell [1981] 3 All ER 383] [CITATION: R v Howell - Breach of the Peace Definition - Case Authority]

Covers: Definition of "breach of the peace" and its use as authority.

**"Breach of the peace defined"**

As noted earlier, breach of the peace is not an offence, but instead it is the basis for the execution of the common law power which prevents public disorder. Furthermore, breach of the peace is one element of other public order offences such as unlawful assembly or riot.

In attempting to establish what breach of the peace entails, we can turn to the leading authority of the concept which can be found in *R v Howell*, where Watkins LJ in the English Court of Appeal, defined breach of the peace in the following way:

***"There is breach of the peace whenever harm is actually done or is likely to be done to a person or in his presence to his property or a person is in fear of being so harmed through an assault, an affray, a riot, unlawfully assembly or other disturbance."***

*R v Howell [1981] 3 All ER 383*

Cited in *Cintana v Burgoyne A Crim R 296 at para 13-19*

Broadly speaking, breach of the peace as defined in *Howell* has been generally accepted in Australian common law.

---

### Chunk 4: Section 106E - Procedure in relation to hearing and determining applications under this Part (Justices Act)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)] [SECTION: 106E] [CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 106E - Procedure in relation to hearing and determining applications under this Part] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Procedure for hearings under Part XA (restraint orders) — open court, evidence, representation, admissibility of declarations] [CONCEPTS: Part XA, open court, complainant treatment, statutory declaration admissibility, medical practitioner evidence, absence of respondent, transfer to Magistrates Court]

Covers: Procedure in relation to hearing and determining applications under this Part (text of s 106E).

106E. Procedure in relation to hearing and determining applications under this Part

(1) Except as otherwise provided by this Act or ordered by justices --

> (a) an application under this Part is to be heard and determined --
>
> (i) in open court; and
>
> (ii) in the same manner, as nearly as practicable, as a complaint for a simple offence; and
>
> (b) a person who makes an application under this Part is to be treated, as nearly as practicable, as a complainant.

(1A) At the hearing of an application under this Part, the following persons may conduct the applicant's or respondent's case and examine and cross-examine witnesses:

> (a) the applicant or respondent;
>
> (b) a lawyer who represents the applicant or respondent;
>
> (c) a police officer who represents the applicant or respondent.

(1B) If a police officer could, if available as a witness, give direct oral evidence of a fact in his or her professional capacity in the hearing of an application under this Part, the statutory declaration of the police officer in relation to that fact and made in support of the application is, subject to subsection (1C) , admissible as evidence of the fact in the hearing, notwithstanding that the police officer may be available as a witness.

(1C) A statutory declaration referred to in subsection (1B) is not admissible as evidence in the hearing of an application under this Part if the justices, having regard to all the circumstances, are of the opinion that the representation in the statutory declaration ought not to be admitted without being tested by cross-examination.

(1D) The representation of a medical practitioner in a document tending to establish a fact is admissible as evidence of the fact in the hearing of an application under this Part, notwithstanding that the medical practitioner may be available as a witness, if--

> (a) the medical practitioner, in his or her professional capacity, could give direct oral evidence of the fact in the hearing; and
>
> (b) the justices, having regard to all the circumstances, are of the opinion that justice does not require that the representation be tested by cross-examination.

[(2) If the justices hearing an application under this Part are satisfied that --]{.mark}

> [(a) a sealed copy of the application has been served on the respondent to the application; or]{.mark}
>
> [(b) reasonable attempts have been made to serve a sealed copy of the application on the respondent --]{.mark}
>
> [they may proceed in the absence of the respondent and may --]{.mark}
>
> [(c) make the order sought in the application or such other order as they consider necessary; or]{.mark}
>
> [(d) issue a warrant for the apprehension of the respondent.]{.mark}

(3) If the parties to an application under this Part consent, justices may make an order under this Part in accordance with the terms consented to by the parties.

(4) At any time in proceedings under this Part, the justices may determine that it is appropriate that those proceedings be heard and determined by the Magistrates Court (Youth Justice Division) or the Magistrates Court (Children's Division) and may transfer the proceedings to that court.

(5) If proceedings are transferred to the Magistrates Court (Youth Justice Division) or the Magistrates Court (Children's Division) under subsection (4) , that court has jurisdiction to hear and determine those proceedings

### Chunk 6: JUSTICES (RESTRAINT ORDERS) RULES 2013 - REG 8 Hearing of applications
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices (Restraint Orders) Rules 2013 (Tas)] [SECTION: Reg 8] [CITATION: Justices (Restraint Orders) Rules 2013 reg 8 - Hearing of applications] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Permitting evidence by affidavit and attendance for cross-examination at Part XA hearings] [CONCEPTS: affidavit evidence, attendance for cross-examination, refusal to allow affidavit, adjournment, justices rules]

Covers: Rule 8 of the Justices (Restraint Orders) Rules 2013 concerning evidence by affidavit and attendance for cross-examination.

JUSTICES (RESTRAINT ORDERS) RULES 2013 - REG 8

**Hearing of applications**

(1)  At the hearing of an application under Part XA of the Act, except the hearing of an application under section 106GA(1) of the Act, evidence may be given by affidavit.

(2)  A person who made an affidavit for the purposes of subrule (1) is not required to attend the hearing of the application unless a party to the proceedings, or the justice hearing the proceedings, so requires.

(3)  By notice in writing given to the person who made an affidavit for the purposes of subrule (1) , a party to the proceedings may require that the person attend the hearing for cross-examination and, if the person fails to attend, the court may --

(a) refuse to allow the affidavit to be used; or

(b) allow the affidavit to be used on any conditions the court thinks fit; or

(c) adjourn the proceedings until the person attends for cross-examination.

Oaths Act 2001

3.   Interpretation

In this Act --

***affidavit*** includes --

(a) any affirmation, statutory declaration or other declaration; and

(b) any acknowledgment, examination or attestation; and

(c) for the purpose of [section 7](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-2001-077#GS7@EN) , any deposition or statement made by an informant or a complainant;

### Chunk 8: Section 106I - Contravention of restraint order, &c.
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)] [SECTION: 106I] [CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 106I - Contravention of restraint order, &c.] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Offence and police arrest powers for contravention of restraint orders] [CONCEPTS: contravention, restraint order, offence, arrest without warrant, section 34 non-application, penalty units]

Covers: The offence and police powers when a restraint order is contravened (text of s 106I).

106I.   Contravention of restraint order, &c.

(1)  Where a person in respect of whom a restraint order, interim restraint order or telephone interim restraint order, as made, varied or extended has effect contravenes or fails to comply with the order, that person is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding 10 penalty units or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding 6 months.

(2)  Where a police officer has reasonable cause to suspect that a person has committed an offence under [subsection (1)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS106I@Gs1@EN) , he may, without warrant, arrest and detain that person and may, for the purpose of arresting and detaining such a person, enter, by force if necessary, any premises on which he has reasonable cause to believe that person is present and search those premises for that person.

(3)  [Section 34](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS34@EN) does not apply to a person arrested and detained under [subsection (2)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS106I@Gs2@EN) .

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)] [SECTION: 106J] [CITATION: Justices Act 1959 (Tas) s 106J - Power of justices to make orders under this Part at hearing of complaint for offence] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Justices may make restraint orders at hearing of summary complaints] [CONCEPTS: justices' powers, summary offence hearing, restraint order, s106B matters, additional orders]

Covers: Power of justices to make orders under this Part at hearing of complaint for offence (text of s 106J).

106J.   Power of justices to make orders under this Part at hearing of complaint for offence

Where, upon the hearing of a complaint for an offence punishable summarily, justices are satisfied on the balance of probabilities as to the matters set out in [section 106B](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS106B@EN) [(1)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1959-077#GS106B@Gs1@EN) , they may make an order under this Part in addition to any other order which they may make.

### Chunk 2 — A v Roughan [2019] TASSC 17 paras [11]-[13] — interpretation of s 11A(1)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000] [SECTION: s 11A(1)] [CITATION: Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000 s 11A(1) - A v Roughan [2019] TASSC 17 (paras 11-13) - Case Excerpt] [CASE: A v Roughan [2019] TASSC 17] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Meaning and elements of "evade police" under s 11A(1)] [CONCEPTS: evade police, s 11A(1), interpretation, driver, avoidance, interception]

Tactical coverage: the meaning and elements of "evade police" under s 11A(1), paras [11]-[13] of A v Roughan.

A v Roughan [2019] TASSC 17, particularly at [11]-[16], Brett J dealt with the meaning of the provision creating this offence, and he set out the applicable principles which are binding on my determination of this case:

."Evade police

[11] The relevant provision of s 11A(1) is as follows:

'(1) The driver of a vehicle must not take action to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer who is exercising his or her powers or performing his or her functions under any Act.'

[12] The submissions of each counsel were based on what was contended to be the proper interpretation of this provision. The starting point of statutory interpretation must be to give legal effect according to the plain and ordinary meaning of the text, read in the context of the surrounding provisions and the legislative scheme: Project Blue Sky Inc v Australian Broadcasting Authority [1998] HCA 28; (1998) 194 CLR 355. Although the provision is inelegantly drafted, there does not seem to be any great difficulty in attributing meaning to the simple words contained in s 11A(1). Accordingly, in order to establish the offence of evade police, the prosecution must prove that:

(a) the driver of a vehicle;

(b) has taken action to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer;

(c) who is exercising his or her powers or performing his or her functions under any Act.

[13] The term 'driver' is defined by s 3 of the Act to mean 'a person having control over the steering, movement or propulsion of a vehicle and includes a person riding a vehicle'. This definition suggests a clear nexus between the driving of the vehicle and the taking of action to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer. This conclusion is consistent with the long title of the Act which is as follows:

'An Act to confer additional powers on police officers to stop and search vehicles and to obtain information concerning the identity of drivers and owners of, and passengers in or on, vehicles used in connection with the commission of certain offences, and to provide for related matters.'

(End of chunk 2)

---

### Chunk 3 — A v Roughan [2019] TASSC 17 paras [14]-[16] — purpose, nexus, and requirement of actual police exercising powers
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000] [SECTION: s 11A(1)] [CITATION: Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000 s 11A(1) - A v Roughan [2019] TASSC 17 (paras 14-16) - Case Excerpt Part 2] [CASE: A v Roughan [2019] TASSC 17] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Purpose test for evasion; nexus between driver and action; requirement of police exercising powers] [CONCEPTS: evasive action, purpose, nexus, police exercising powers, subjective purpose]

Tactical coverage: purpose test for evasion; nexus between driver and action; requirement that police be exercising powers or functions under an Act (paras [14]-[16]).

[14] I am not satisfied, however, that the nexus requires the evasion be constituted solely by an act of driving, nor that the evasion and the driving must be contemporaneous and co-extensive. The requirement is that the driver of the vehicle takes action to avoid apprehension or interception. Taking action can involve a single act or a course of conduct. The use of that term without qualification is consistent with a legislative intention to prohibit any form of action which is intended to achieve evasion of a police officer who is acting within the law. The focus is not on the nature or effectiveness of the action, but rather its purpose. It is immaterial that the action is, or is not likely to be successful, in achieving the evasion of the police officer. In the second reading speech which accompanied the amending Act which inserted s 11A into the Act, the then Minister for Police and Emergency Management gave examples of the type of situation in which the section might operate:

'This provision will apply to situations where a driver becomes aware of a police presence and then takes action to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer. An example of such an offence includes drivers who turn their vehicle around, or pull over and run from their vehicle, when they are approaching a random breath test site, or drives in such a manner as to attempt to evade, elude and or prevent police from speaking to the driver or occupants.'

[15] Accordingly, provided that the Court is satisfied that the action taken by the driver of a vehicle is for the purpose of avoiding apprehension or interception by a police officer, it is not necessary that the evasion occur by the driving of the vehicle, nor that the vehicle is being driven throughout the act of evasion. It is necessary only that the purpose of the action taken be to avoid apprehension or interception by a police officer. The required nexus between the driving of the vehicle and the taking of action to avoid apprehension or interception will be satisfied if the Court can conclude that the action was taken by a person in his capacity as a driver of the vehicle, but this does not necessarily mean that he or she must actually be driving the vehicle at all times, or even at any time, throughout the evasion. Generally, as a practical consequence of this nexus, some contemporaneity can be expected between the actual driving of the vehicle and the action in question. However, because the taking of action can be constituted by a course of conduct, it is not necessary that the entirety of the relevant action be constituted by the driving of the vehicle. Hence, as in the example given by the Minister in the second reading speech, it would be sufficient if the driver has stopped the vehicle and decamped from it in order to avoid apprehension or interception. It is also possible to contemplate circumstances in which the evasive action commences after the driving has come to an end. An example might be a driver who leaves the scene immediately after an accident, for the purpose of evading apprehension by police who are approaching or already present at the scene, but who prior to the driving coming to an end upon the occurrence of the accident was not evading police.

"[16] The requirement that the evasion be of a police officer performing his or her functions under any Act, imports a requirement that the taking of action must be for the purpose of avoiding apprehension or interception by an actual police officer[. **The prosecution must prove that there is a police officer, that he or she is exercising power or performing functions under any Act**]{.mark}, and that it is the apprehension or interception by that police officer that the defendant has taken action to evade. It would not be sufficient, for example, that the person believes that police might be in the area, when that is not actually the case. On the other hand, it is not necessary for that police officer to have formed an intention to, nor be actually attempting to apprehend or intercept the driver. **[The power or function does not necessarily need to relate to the apprehension or interception of the driver]{.mark}. **The requirement that the driver take action to avoid apprehension or interception by the police officer refers to the subjective purpose of the driver, and does not rely upon or circumscribe the intentions of the police officer. **[It must be independently shown that the police officer is exercising powers or functions under an Act and, provided that is shown]{.mark},** it is irrelevant whether the driver has correctly determined that the police officer intends to apprehend or intercept him or her. The offence is established when the driver of the vehicle takes action for the purpose of avoiding apprehension or interception, whether or not that prospect is, in fact, about to occur."

(End of chunk 3)

### Chunk 5 — Aggravated Evade Police: timing of circumstances (chief Magistrate comments)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: When aggravated circumstances (s 11A(2A)) apply — timing during driving] [CONCEPTS: aggravated evade, s11A(2A), timing, while driving, course of conduct]

Tactical coverage: timing requirement for aggravated evade and practical consequences.

Most circumstances that lead to a 2A charge must exist when driver is taking action to avoid WHILST DRIVING.

So, lets say he is driving, Cop lights go on. He pulls over and gets out of the car THEN takes action to evade by running away, it's not an Agg evade for most of the 2A cricumsatnces, as "the ACT" needs to occur WHILST driving.
