### Chunk 6 — Sentencing submissions for aggravated evade: Murray v Tasmania Police [2022] TASSC 1 paras [55]-[56]
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Sentencing approach and legislative guideposts for aggravated evade] [CONCEPTS: sentencing, aggravated evade, s11A(2A), Murray v Tasmania Police, legislative guideposts] [CITATION: Murray v Tasmania Police [2022] TASSC 1 - Sentencing paras 55-56] [CASE: Murray v Tasmania Police [2022] TASSC 1] [ACT: Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000]

Tactical coverage: sentencing submissions and legislative context for aggravated evade (paras [55]-[56]).

Para [55] The magistrate correctly identified the purpose of the law as one to protect the public against the risk involved in a police pursuit and to facilitate the enforcement of the law. When subs (2A) was inserted into s 11A of the Act, the penalties for a breach of s 11A(1) -- evading police -- were increased. In the second reading speech the Minister noted that evading police had emerged as a significant safety problem for Tasmania Police and the whole Tasmanian community, and referred to the need for there "to be a more significant deterrent, as drivers should not see any advantage in evading police, and repeat offenders should be discouraged from undertaking a cost-benefit analysis of not stopping".

Para [56] The penalties prescribed by [s 11A(2A)](https://www6.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ppia2000358/s11a.html) of the *Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act* are indicative of the Parliamentary view of this type offending. For a first offence the penalty is a fine of not less than 20 penalty units and not more than 100 units, or a term of imprisonment not exceeding three years or both. For a second or subsequent offence the penalty is a fine within the same range or a term of imprisonment for not more than five years, or both. Such legislative changes and the maximum penalties set for a particular offence provide "legislative guideposts" to a court, and are matters to which a court should have regard: *Muldrock v The Queen* [\23011\] HCA 39, [244 CLR 120] at [27]; *Banks v Tasmania* [2019] TASCCA 1, [31 Tas R 342] at [33].

(End of chunk 6)

---

### Chunk 7 — Illicit drugs or drunk: requirement for blood test and Southern Regional Prosecution Services notice
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000; Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970] [SECTION: s 11A(2A) condition referencing s 6A RSAD Act] [CITATION: Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000 - Illicit drug proof requirement notice] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine / annotation] [TOPIC: Proof of illicit drugs for aggravated evade — blood vs oral fluid testing and operational advice] [CONCEPTS: blood test requirement, oral fluid, RSAD Act s6A, aggravated evade, Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act]

Tactical coverage: proof of illicit drugs for aggravated evade offences and operational advice from Southern Regional Prosecution Services.

Proof of drugs needs to come from blood test! Oral not enough until amendments are made (as of 2019). See Southern Pross notice below:

The creation of the offence of evade police with aggravated circumstances is found in the *Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000*. The offence came into effect on 13 September 2017. One of the conditions that meets aggravating circumstances, is that the driver had an illicit drug present in his or her breath or blood in contravention of section 6A of the *Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970*.

Since that time, amendments to the *Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970* have come into that allow oral fluid to be taken for drug testing, instead of a blood sample. These amendments were written and came into effect on 3 December 2018, over a year after the amendments creating the offence of evade police with aggravating circumstances came into being. This means the evade offence still requires the driver to have an illicit drug in their breath or blood. It does not cater to an illicit drug located in oral fluid.

Until the *Police Powers (Vehicle Interception) Act 2000* can be amended, members will be required to conduct a blood test if they are looking to charge a driver with Evade (Aggravated Circumstances) if the sole condition they are relying on the driver having an illicit drug in their system. Oral fluid testing can still be conducted as usual for all offences under the *Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970*.

*Andrew Keane, Inspector, Southern Regional Prosecution Services*

(End of chunk 7)

---

### Chunk 10 — "Drive": Barnes v Dale [2020] TASMC 17 — capacity to drive and link to Cooley v Lowe
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Whether accused had capacity/likelihood to drive; application of Cooley v Lowe principles in Barnes v Dale] [CONCEPTS: driving definition, capacity to drive, Cooley v Lowe, Barnes v Dale, likelihood to drive] [CITATION: Barnes v Dale [2020] TASMC 17 - Drive and Cooley v Lowe analysis] [CASE: Barnes v Dale [2020] TASMC 17] [CASE: Cooley v Lowe [1984] Tas R 107]

Tactical coverage: requirement that accused had capacity or at least likelihood to drive; relation to Cooley v Lowe.

**Barnes v Dale [2020] TASMC 17 (7 October 2020)**

Still requires that they had capacity or at least likelihood that they would drive

**Driving:**

***Cooley v Lowe*** [1984] Tas R 107

For it to be proven that person was 'driving' a motor vehicle the following matters are to be considered,

- The degree to which the 'driver' was operating the controls of the motor vehicle (steering, brakes etc.)

- The distance travelled.

- The road conditions, (undulations, corners etc.)

- The speed of the vehicle

- If the vehicle was being towed (as in this case), the length of the tow rope, communication (if any) between the vehicles, the manner in which the towed vehicle was steered and stopped

*... "driving"*, requires proof that the person charged *"\...had the capacity to do so, and that there was at least a reasonable possibility, if *not* likelihood, that he would attempt to drive the vehicle and so endanger the public\..." *in accordance with the Full Court decision in *Cooley v Lowe *[1984] Tas R 107.[2]

[27] In my view, when parliament amended the extended definition of *"driving"* by introducing s 2(4) and s 2(5), it did not intend to overturn the effect of the Full Court's decision in *Cooley v Lowe*. Indeed the amended definition is entirely consistent with the approach taken in that case.* *This view is reinforced by the nature of the Full Court's reasoning in that case where the Court approved what was said by Morris CJ in *Smith v* *Westell.*[14] In the relevant passages, the Court emphasised that the words extending the meaning of *"driving"* still require proof of a link between;

a.  The acts or omissions contemplated by the definition provisions; and

b.  The possibility or likelihood that the person will move the vehicle.

[28] My analysis is that despite the broad and inclusive amended definition in the Act, it remains the case that I am bound to decide this case by asking the questions posed in *Cooley v Lowe*; whether the defendant had the capacity to and that there was at least a reasonable possibility, if not likelihood, that he would attempt to drive the vehicle and so endanger the public. Whether the relationship between the defendant and his motor vehicle can be said to be *"driving"* is a question of fact to be determined in the circumstances of the particular case. I now turn to answer that question.

(End of chunk 10)

---

### Chunk 11 — DUI: Kerr v McLennan [2011] TASMC 21 and Police v Brown [2008] TASMC 7 — principles on "under the influence" and "proper control"
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Principles for intoxication and proper control; evidentiary indicia] [CONCEPTS: under the influence, proper control, objective standard, indicia, Pulleine v Button] [CITATION: Police v Brown [2008] TASMC 7 - The law on intoxication and proper control] [CASE: Police v Brown [2008] TASMC 7] [CASE: Kerr v McLennan [2011] TASMC 21]

Tactical coverage: the principles applicable to determining if a driver is under the influence and incapable of proper control (Police v Brown "The law").

See **Kerr v McLennan [2011] TASMC 21 (20 April 2011)**

Whether driver was under the influence to such a degree as to be incapable of exercising proper control of vehicle

**And Police v Brown [2008] TASMC 7**

#### Recent Magi decision

***Police v Brown* [2008] TASMC 7**

**The law**

[8]. The relevant principles applicable in this case are these:

Intoxication -- under the influence

- The question whether a person is under the influence of intoxicating liquor is a pure question of fact. What must be proved is that the defendant was influenced by intoxicating liquor - that is - that his reactions were affected; *Richardson v Shipp* [1970] Tas SR 105, at 112; *R v Burnside* [1962] VR 96

- It is recognised that there are indicia, being certain observable abnormalities of behaviour and certain physical signs, which evidence when a person who has ingested alcohol has become "influenced" by alcohol. If there is accepted evidence of sufficient manifestation of these indicia, one is entitled to conclude (in the absence of evidence to the contrary), that the indicia are the effect of the alcohol and that the subject person, no matter what his tolerance to alcohol, is "under the influence"; *O'Connor v Shaw* [1958] Qd R 384 at 386.

- The extent of the defendant's intoxication, i.e. whether he is so much under the influence as to be incapable of having proper control of the vehicle, is a question of fact upon which the court must be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt; *Pulleine v Button* [1948] SASR 1 at 7.

*Proper control*

- The standard of "*proper control"* is an objective standard, a standard common to drivers as a whole and not to the individual driver. Whether a person is under the influence of intoxicating liquor to the requisite extent must be judged by an objective test. The standard is that control which a driver of ordinary reasonable prudence and ability ought exercise while driving his/her motor vehicle; *Lowe v Suckling* [1983] Tas R 116 (NC) N9 (Unrep. 23/1983 at 9), approving *Lysaght v Police* [1965] NZLR 405 (CA) (see 409, 414).

- Actual direct observation of the defendant's lack of ability to control the vehicle is not essential -- the inference may be drawn from his behaviour: *Pulleine v Button (supra)* at 6-7.

- The court may reach the requisite state of satisfaction upon inferences drawn from evidence other than actual driving; or, notwithstanding evidence of acts of apparently normal driving by the defendant; *Pulleine v Button (supra)* at 7; *Lowe v Suckling (supra).*

(End of chunk 11)

---

### Chunk 14 — Passive ingestion of THC: Craig Gardner email (3 August 2018) — likelihood and timeframe
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Passive exposure and detectability of THC — Craig Gardner email] [CONCEPTS: passive THC, detectability, passive exposure timeframe, Craig Gardner, FFST] [CITATION: Craig Gardner email 3 August 2018 - Passive THC detectability]

Craig Gardner from FFST in an email dated 3 Auguste 2018:

Review of the literature suggests that although passive exposure can cause positive results for THC in blood, this is only likely within a few hours of exposure.

THC is rabidly metabolised, an in occasional smokers is typically only detectable for 4-8 hours, dose dependant.

### Chunk 15 — Prescribed/prescription THC: PSB RTPM records and medicinal cannabis (Schedule 8) — administrative note
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Real-time prescription monitoring records for Schedule 8 medicinal cannabis and operational advice] [CONCEPTS: Schedule 8, medicinal cannabis, RTPM, Poisons Act 1971, prescription records] [CITATION: PSB RTPM - Schedule 8 medicinal cannabis records - administrative note] [ACT: Poisons Act 1971; Poisons Regulations 2018]

Tactical coverage: availability of prescription records for Schedule 8 medicinal cannabis and confirmation re Mr Woodfield.

The Department of Health's Pharmaceutical Services Branch (PSB) is responsible for administering the *Poisons Act 1971* (Act) and *Poisons Regulations 2018* (Regulations). This includes the maintenance of Tasmania's real-time prescription monitoring (RTPM) systems.

The records of the supply to patients of all Schedule 8 (narcotic) substances and prescribed monitored medicines are sent to PSB by Tasmanian pharmacies. These records show who received, who prescribed, where and when these substances were dispensed. The records of all authorities to prescribe narcotic substances, issued to prescribers under the Act, are also kept on the database.

Schedule 8 medicinal cannabis is a narcotic substance and I can advise there is no record on our RTPM systems of Mr Woodfield being dispensed Schedule 8 medicinal cannabis in Tasmania from community pharmacies.

(End of chunk 15)

---

### Chunk 16 — Requirements: Smith v Marshall [2024] TASMC 12 — admissibility of internet research
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Evidentiary requirements re expert/research sources — Smith v Marshall] [CONCEPTS: admissibility, expert source, internet chat groups, Smith v Marshall, sourcing evidence] [CITATION: Smith v Marshall [2024] TASMC 12 - research admissibility] [CASE: Smith v Marshall [2024] TASMC 12]

Tactical coverage: requirements for use of internet-sourced material as evidentiary support.

See **Smith v Marshall [2024] TASMC 12**

In this decision, court said research on internet chat groups was not reasonable as it was not a legal practitioner or other appropriate authority

Needs to be tassie script from a tassie doc.

### Chunk 17 — Medications and positive results: email chain (Hogan -> Gardner and reply) and additional oral advice notes
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Whether listed prescribed medications could produce positive oral fluid methamphetamine results — email correspondence and expert reply] [CONCEPTS: oral fluid testing, methamphetamine, pseudoephedrine, Drugwipe 5S, laboratory analysis, Craig Gardner] [CITATION: Email chain Hogan to Gardner (9 Feb 2023) and Gardner reply (14 Feb 2023) - Medications and meth results] 

Tactical coverage: whether specified medications can produce positive methylamphetamine oral fluid results; email evidence and further notes.

Copy on file

**From:** Hogan, Thomas <thomas.hogan@police.tas.gov.au>  
**Sent:** Thursday, 9 February 2023 10:42 AM  
**To:** Gardner, Craig <Craig.Gardner@fsst.tas.gov.au>  
**Subject:** Prescribed medication and Meth

Good morning, Craig,

Can you please tell me whether a driver taking the below medications, alone or in combination, in any prescribed quantity, could return a positive result for the presence of Methamphetamine in an analysis of oral fluid?   

Paracetamol + Codeine

Paracetamol + Pseudoephedrine hydrochloride

Diazepam

Citalopram

Oxycodone

**From:** Gardner, Craig <Craig.Gardner@fsst.tas.gov.au>  
**Sent:** Tuesday, February 14, 2023 9:41 AM  
**To:** Hogan, Thomas <thomas.hogan@police.tas.gov.au>  
**Subject:** RE: Prescribed medication and Meth

Hi Tom,

None of the listed drugs would lead to a positive methylamphetamine result in a sample of oral fluid analysed by FSST. Pseudoephedrine may produce a roadside positive result on the Drugwipe 5S, but laboratory instrumentation used in the analysis is able to distinguish methylamphetamine from pseudoephedrine.

The presence of methylamphetamine indicates an administration of methylamphetamine.

Regards

**ALSO -- oral advice from Craig -- Lexapro (escitalopram) and Serequel (Quetiapine) will not give positive results either.**

**ALSO -- another email once sent to Ang Phipps Zoloft (sertraline) is way different. and Duromine (phentermine) will give positive lick test but not analysis.**

Also on advice from Juliette Cunningham [CLOMIPRAMINE]{.mark} wont. May lead to

(End of chunk 17)

# Forensic science opinions on passive methylamphetamine inhalation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: passive ingestion of methamphetamine / passive inhalation] [CONCEPTS: passive meth inhalation, methylamphetamine vapour, vapour vs smoke, crystal meth melting point, passive exposure evidence, FSST expert opinion, scientific impossibility of passive inhalation, condensation on smoking devices, vaping physics]

The scientific opinion recorded by Forensic Science Service Tasmania (FSST) staff is that passive inhalation of methylamphetamine vapour is not a viable route of exposure: methylamphetamine must be heated to form a vapour and does not exist as a persistent airborne gas at ambient room temperature, so passive inhalation (like cigarette smoke) is not plausible. Miriam Connor (FFST) (29 May 2018) stated that methylamphetamine is heated to vapour which is actively inhaled and does not exist in air like cigarette smoke; Juliette Cunningham (31 May 2017) noted that methylamphetamine hydrochloride ("crystal meth") has a melting point around 170–175°C and will vaporise only above that temperature, tends to condense on cooler surfaces of smoking devices, and therefore is unlikely to disperse into a room at detectable levels. This forensic-scientific assertion functions as an evidentiary principle: where defence asserts passive inhalation as an explanation for a positive drug test, the court may treat FSST's contemporaneous technical opinion as relevant expert evidence to rebut passive exposure hypotheses. The opinion is documentary/expert, not legislative; admissibility and weight remain matters for the court and must be supported by FSST witness evidence when relied upon in litigation.

# Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) — s 7A: obligation to submit to breath test
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 7A] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: offence / statutory obligation] [TOPIC: obligation to submit to breath test] [CONCEPTS: s7A, breath test obligation, driver liable to test, approved operator, reasonable belief of alcohol present, statutory duty to provide breath sample, Road Safety Act]

Section 7A of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) imposes the statutory obligation that a driver is liable to submit to a breath test when requested by an authorised officer. Specifically, s 7A(1) requires a driver to submit to a breath test administered by an authorised officer; s 7A(4) extends liability such that, whether or not an initial roadside test gives a positive reading, if an officer reasonably believes that alcohol may be present the driver becomes liable to submit to a breath analysis (more formal testing). This statutory duty is a primary source of criminal liability and of the power to require further testing; its breach may lead to offences and evidentiary consequences. The statutory obligation interacts with other provisions that set out police powers to require attendance and the consequences of refusal.

# Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) — s 10: police powers to require attendance and detain for breath analysis
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 10] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: procedural rule / statutory power] [TOPIC: police power to require attendance for breath analysis] [CONCEPTS: s10(1), power to require, proceed to place, detention for analysis, custody to effect analysis, 3 hour limit, relevant time, s10(2), s10(3), s10(4), s10(4A)]

Section 10(1) of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) authorises a police officer to require a person to proceed to a specified place or into a specified vehicle and there submit to a breath analysis. Section 10(2) permits the officer to take the person into custody for the necessary time to effect the obligation in s 10(1). Section 10(3) limits the use of s 10(2) to situations where the officer believes the analysis can be completed within three hours after the "relevant time" (as defined in the Act). Section 10(4) provides power to direct the person to submit to a breath analysis, and s 10(4A) requires the approved operator, where the person fails or refuses the direction in s 10(4), to inform the person that they may elect to take blood instead if it can be done within three hours of the relevant time. These subsections together define the lawful scope for requiring attendance, temporary detention, and the alternatives available to suspects.

# Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) — s 14(2): offence for failure or refusal to comply with breath analysis directions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 14(2)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: offence] [TOPIC: offence for refusing breath analysis] [CONCEPTS: s14(2), refusal offence, fail to comply, breath analysis directions, penal consequence, enforcement]

Section 14(2) of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) creates the offence of failing or refusing to provide a breath test in accordance with directions of an "approved operator". Where a suspect refuses or fails to follow lawful directions required under the Act and by the operator (who must comply with relevant regulations), that refusal may constitute a statutory offence. Prosecution under s 14(2) requires proof that the direction was lawfully given by an approved operator and that the subject failed or refused to comply.

# Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) — Regulations: device testing and approved operator procedural requirements (Regulation 6 and Regulation 7)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule / procedural rule] [TOPIC: device testing and operator procedural requirements] [CONCEPTS: Regulation 6, Regulation 7, device tested within 12 months, approved operator requirements, mouthpiece, unused mouthpiece, 15‑minute rule, printout, operator statement, instrument maintenance]

Regulation 6 (as cited in the source) requires that the breath analysis device must have been tested within 12 months before use. Regulation 7 (as summarised in the source) sets mandatory steps for approved operators when conducting breath analysis: (a) the person must give a breath sample into a mouthpiece supplied by the operator; (b) an unused mouthpiece must be provided; (c) the operator must be satisfied the person has not consumed intoxicating liquor during the 15 minutes preceding analysis; (d) after completion, the operator must record the resultant reading; inquire whether the person is one of the defined categories (e.g. persons covered by s 6(3)); complete and sign the required written statement under the relevant enactment; and (e) provide the person with a copy of the instrument printout. Compliance with these regulations is an evidentiary and procedural precondition to the lawful administration and admissibility of breath analysis results.

# Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) — ss 25, 26, 29 and admission by certificate (service requirements)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 25; 26; 29] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: evidentiary certificates and service requirements] [CONCEPTS: s25(2), s26, s29, evidentiary certificate, device functioning certificate, service 14 days, certs tendered without author, proof of service]

Sections 25(2) and 26 provide for statutory evidentiary certificates (for example, that the breath sample produced a particular reading and that the device was working). Section 29 imposes service requirements for certificates: certificates must be served 14 days prior to their tender for certain presumptions and evidentiary effects, and section 29(1A) makes a "certificate of service" proof of service. Section 29(2) permits certificates to be tendered without calling the author; however the defendant retains the right to call the analyst if they choose. Proper service under the Act is a precondition for relying on certificate evidence without oral proof of the analyst.

# Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) — s 13B: service of the Report of Analysis
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 13B] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: procedural rule / evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: service of Report of Analysis] [CONCEPTS: s13B, report of analysis, service on person, 21 day service obligation, approved analyst duties, proof of service]

Section 13B of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) obliges the analyst (or the laboratory/agency) to cause the "Report of Analysis" to be served on the person (the subject of analysis) within 21 days. In evidence practice, FSST analysts are asked whether they understand this obligation and to explain their usual practice for creating, peer-reviewing, filing and posting the report. Evidence of service (or lack of it) is relevant to the admissibility of certificates and to compliance with statutory timeframes; documentary proof of service — such as internal mail records or a certificate of service — may be invoked under section 29 to establish compliance.

# Forensic Science Service Tasmania — template examination-in-chief (EIC) questions for analyst testimony and memory refreshment
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule / procedural rule] [TOPIC: EIC template and refreshing memory under Evidence Act] [CONCEPTS: EIC template, FSST analyst questions, duties and qualifications, peer review, notes and instrument printouts, memory refreshment, section 32(3) Evidence Act, reading from notes, documentary refreshment]

This template provides a structured examination-in-chief for an FSST analyst called to give evidence about a blood or drug analysis. It begins with standard identity, occupation and duty questions; asks the analyst to identify qualifications and appointment as an "approved analyst" under the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas); queries the analyst's recollection of the specific sample and whether contemporaneous notes exist (instrument printouts, case file); asks whether notes were reviewed and whether reading them refreshed memory; and informs the court of a proposed application to permit the witness to refresh their memory or to read from the notes pursuant to section 32(3) of the Evidence Act. The template also elicits standard practice concerning peer review, filing, mailing of reports and the chain of custody. The template is an evidentiary tool and must be adapted to the facts of each matter; references to legislative provisions in the template are treated as matters for judicial ruling on admissibility and refreshment.

# Refusal to submit to blood test — Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC (needle phobia principle)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC] [TOPIC: needle phobia and refusal to submit to blood test] [CONCEPTS: needle phobia, medical incapacity, burden of proof, blood sample refusal, magistrate's findings, Lyons v Dyson, sedation and interventions, detriment to health]

Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC addresses claims of needle phobia as a basis to refuse a blood sample for Road Safety Act purposes. The magistrate held that establishing a mere subjective claim of needle phobia is insufficient: the defendant must satisfy the court not only that the phobia exists, but that taking the blood sample would be detrimental to his or her physical or mental health and that reasonable alternative interventions (for example sedation or calming techniques) could not be reasonably used to enable lawful sampling. Lyons v Dyson therefore establishes that needle phobia, without objective evidence of detriment and absence of reasonable alternative measures, will not ordinarily excuse a statutory obligation to provide blood.

# Driving under the influence of a drug — evidentiary standard and indicia (cases cited)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: proof of driving under the influence of drugs / evidentiary indicia] [CONCEPTS: beyond reasonable doubt, incapacity to exercise proper control, observable indicia, physical signs, inferences from behaviour, Richardson v Shipp, Pulleine v Button, O'Connor v Shaw, Bedelph v Weedon]

The legal question whether a defendant was driving "under the influence" of a drug to such an extent as to be incapable of having proper control of the vehicle is a question of fact for the prosecution to prove beyond reasonable doubt. Courts may draw inferences from observable abnormalities of behaviour and physical signs that evidence impairment. Authorities cited in the source include Richardson v Shipp, Pulleine v Button, O'Connor v Shaw (Philp J on indicia of influence from intoxication), and Bedelph v Weedon. The assessment is fact-specific and depends on contemporaneous observations, conduct, and physical indicators consistent with incapacity.

# Requirement to effectively communicate the "requirement" to submit — interpretive principle and McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: interpretive principle / case authority] [CASE: McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13] [TOPIC: communication of statutory requirement to submit to analysis] [CONCEPTS: requirement to communicate, unlawful arrest, s10(1) Road Safety Act, effective communication, admissibility under s138 Evidence Act, Arnesto v Hickman reasoning, McLean v Wilkie]

The effective communication of the statutory "requirement" to submit to a breath analysis is a precondition to the lawful exercise of police powers under Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 10(1). McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13 holds that if an officer fails to communicate the requirement (and the reason for it) the arrest/direction can be unlawful; however, evidence obtained may nevertheless be admitted in certain circumstances under section 138 of the Evidence Act (discretion to exclude). In McLean v Wilkie the court adopted reasoning in Arnesto v Hickman as to the meaning of "require" in related statutory contexts and applied it to the RSAD Act; the case therefore confirms that procedural communication obligations are substantive to the legality of enforcement action.

# Admissibility despite defective communication — Wilkie v McLean [2021] TASMC 5 (s 138 Evidence Act consideration)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Wilkie v McLean [2021] TASMC 5] [TOPIC: admissibility of evidence where communication defect occurred] [CONCEPTS: Wilkie v McLean, s138 Evidence Act, discretion to exclude, unlawfully obtained evidence, defective communication, breath analysis admissibility]

Wilkie v McLean [2021] TASMC 5 considered whether breath analysis results should be excluded where police failed to properly communicate the statutory requirement to submit. The decision indicates that even when there has been a defect in communication (which may render the arrest/direction unlawful), the court must exercise the s 138 Evidence Act discretion to determine whether the evidence should be excluded; admissibility is not automatic simply because of procedural defect. The case is authority that s 138 considerations (impropriety, prejudice, deterrence) remain salient where statutory communication obligations are not satisfied.

# OFT/OFA statutory framework — definitions and appointments (ss 2(3), 3(1), 3(5); Regulation 5)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 2(3); 3(1); 3(5)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory definition / administrative rule] [TOPIC: approved analyst and approved operator definitions and appointments] [CONCEPTS: s2(3), s3(1), s3(5), approved analyst, approved operator, ministerial appointment, commissioner authorisation, Regulation 5, minimum qualifications]

The OFT/OFA (Oral Fluid Test / Oral Fluid Analysis) scheme under the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) includes definitions and appointment mechanisms: s 2(3) defines liability for persons required to submit under provisions such as s 7B; s 3(1) permits appointment of an approved analyst by the Minister; s 3(5) defines "approved operator" as a person authorised by the Commissioner; Regulation 5 (as cited in source) prescribes minimum qualifications and experience for an approved analyst (for example, a relevant degree and two years' experience). These provisions establish the administrative and professional framework for who may perform analytical and operational functions under the scheme.

# OFT/OFA — s 7B "at or near" liability and case law on physical proximity (Police v Bentley; Bennett v Richards)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 7B(1); 7B(5)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory interpretation / case authority] [TOPIC: "at or near" driving location and liability to OFA] [CONCEPTS: s7B(1), "at or near", Police v Bentley, Bennett v Richards, proximity, accident context, public safety purpose]

Section 7B(1) places persons who, at or near the place of driving (or in specified circumstances), are liable to oral fluid testing or other analysis. Courts have interpreted the spatial element pragmatically: Police v Bentley [2008] TASMC 9 upheld a 100 metre distance in the context of an accident under s 2(3A), and Bennett v Richards [2011] TASMC 9 accepted a 1.2 km distance in another factual context. Both decisions emphasise legislative purpose (public safety) and reasonableness in the exercise of powers when assessing whether a person was "at or near" the relevant place.

# OFT/OFA — alternative liability pathways (s 8A(1)) and obligation to comply with directions (s 2B(1))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 8A(1); 2B(1)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory rule] [TOPIC: alternate grounds for OFT/OFA liability and continuing compliance] [CONCEPTS: s8A(1), reasonable suspicion, OFT liability, OFA liability, s2B(1), continuing compliance, directions until analysis complete]

Section 8A(1) allows liability to OFT/OFA where an authorised officer finds a person in circumstances giving rise to a reasonable suspicion of drug influence. Section 7B(5) also creates liability following the initial "at or near" finding. Section 2B(1) imposes an ongoing duty on the person to comply with directions in s 10(1) until the analysis is complete. Together these provisions outline primary and alternative grounds on which testing obligations arise and require continued compliance while testing is in progress.

# Sample taking and handling requirements — s 13C, Regulation 11 and Regulation 12 (containers, dissolution, labelling)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 13C] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: procedural rule / evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: oral fluid/blood sample procedure, containers and labelling] [CONCEPTS: s13C, Regulation 11, Regulation 12, sample dissolution, two suitable containers, labelling initialled, control sample, operator initials, Form 5, operator notice]

Section 13C(1)(a) requires that a sample be taken "in accordance with prescribed requirements". Regulation 11 (as cited) specifies that the taken sample must be dissolved and placed into two suitable containers; Regulation 12 prescribes labelling requirements, including that containers be initialled by the operator and the subject, include date and time, and one container be marked "control". Regulation 11(1A) obliges the approved operator to provide notice to the defendant after an oral fluid sample is provided (Form 5 under Regulation 17 and Schedule 1). These procedural safeguards are central both to chain-of-custody integrity and to the admissibility of analytical certificates.

# FSST reporting timelines — s 13C(2) and s 13C(3) (provision of report to police and to defendant)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 13C(2); 13C(3)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: FSST report timelines and service to police and defendant] [CONCEPTS: s13C(2), s13C(3), report within 21 days, TasPol, defendant copy, timing of report finished, police obligations]

Section 13C(2) requires that Forensic Science Service Tasmania (FFST/FSST) provide Tasmania Police with a report within 21 days after the "report [is] finished" (noting that "finished" may differ from the date of analysis). Section 13C(3) obliges the police to provide the defendant with a copy of the FSST report; the provision in the Act does not prescribe a strict time limit for police to provide the copy under s 13C(3), but these steps are relevant to compliance with section 29 service requirements for certificates and to the defendant’s right to inspect and challenge the analysis.

# Presumptions of drug presence within 6 hours — s 23A
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 23A] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule / statutory presumption] [TOPIC: 6‑hour presumption for drug detection] [CONCEPTS: s23A, 6 hours, presumption of drug in system, relevant time, evidential consequences, statutory presumption]

Section 23A creates a statutory presumption that a positive result obtained at any time within six hours of the relevant time of driving is taken to show that the driver had the drug in their system at that relevant time. This presumption assists prosecution proof of drug presence at the time of driving but interacts with expert evidence on pharmacokinetics and with defence contentions about timing, passive exposure, or cross-reactivity. The presumption is rebuttable by evidence to the contrary.

# Certificates of continuity and analysis — ss 26, 27A, 28, 28A and section 29 service mechanics
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 26; 27A; 28; 28A; 29] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: certificate of operator, delivery and analysis; service and admissibility] [CONCEPTS: s26, s27A(1), s27A(2), s28, s28A, service of certificates, certificate of service, admission without author, chain of custody certificate]

Sections 26–28A provide for various statutory certificates that record: the approved operator and functioning/instrument checks (s 26), the taking and dealing with a sample including dates/times and operator status (s 27A(1)), the delivery of the sample to the analyst and labelling/marking (s 27A(2)), and the certificate of analysis that corresponds to continuity/receipts (ss 28 and 28A). Section 29 imposes service requirements (14 days in certain contexts) for certificates used in evidence; proper service allows certificates to be tendered without calling the author while preserving the defendant’s entitlement to call the analyst if desired. The statutory certificate regime underpins admissibility of analytical results.

# Cost recovery for analysis — s 30A
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 30A] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: administrative rule] [TOPIC: charging for analysis] [CONCEPTS: s30A, costs of analysis, fee, recovery, laboratory fees, FSST charges]

Section 30A of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) authorises charging for analysis. This provision permits cost recovery by the agency or laboratory conducting analyses under the Act; procedures for invoicing, recovery and related administrative arrangements are governed by the Act and agency policy.

# Intercept / trespass and power to enter on pursuit — s 7AA and implied licence (Halliday v Nevill)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [SECTION: 7AA] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: procedural rule / case authority] [TOPIC: power to enter residence in uninterrupted continuous pursuit; implied licence] [CONCEPTS: s7AA, continuous pursuit, entry to private residence, implied licence, Halliday v Nevill, threshold for trespass exceptions]

Section 7AA of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) (as cited in the source) confers a power to enter a private residence where there is uninterrupted continuous pursuit. Courts have also recognised the concept of implied licence to enter a driveway or approach a house in appropriate circumstances; Halliday v Nevill [1984] HCA 80 is authority for the implied licence principle. The statutory pursuit exception and the implied licence doctrine together frame when police entry onto private property will not amount to trespass or render subsequent evidence inadmissible for that reason.

# V&T Act s 46A distinction — asking for licence v power to stop
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: s46A V&T Act — asking for driver's licence but not a power to stop] [CONCEPTS: s46A, ask for licence, no power to stop, distinction, investigatory powers, V&T Act]

The source notes that under s 46A of the V&T Act there is a power to ask for the driver's licence of the driver but that this provision does not itself constitute a power to stop. This distinction is relevant in assessing the legality of initial police interactions and whether subsequent detention or requirement to submit to tests was founded on a lawful stop or unlawful interference with liberty.

# Drug pattern defence and THC detection window
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine / evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: THC detection windows and timing defences] [CONCEPTS: THC detection window, 6 hours rule of thumb, drug pattern defence, mistake of fact re timing, expert evidence, De Wit v Ali]

The "drug pattern defence" argument relies on pharmacokinetic detection windows: the source notes that a THC positive result usually indicates use within about six hours and that beyond that timeframe THC is usually undetectable regardless of subjective characteristics. Defence strategies may assert mistake of fact as to timing of use; successful reliance typically requires expert evidence showing that the positive result is inconsistent with the alleged timing. De Wit v Ali [2021] TASMC 10 is a case example where the defendant argued four days had passed but expert evidence indicated detection cut‑offs made that improbable.

# Case authority — De Wit v Ali [2021] TASMC 10
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: De Wit v Ali [2021] TASMC 10] [TOPIC: timing of drug detection / mistake of fact] [CONCEPTS: De Wit v Ali, detection window, expert evidence, mistake of fact, timing of use, cannabis detection, TAS Magistrates Court]

De Wit v Ali [2021] TASMC 10 is authority on the interplay between expert evidence on detection windows and a defence of mistake of fact about timing of drug use. In that case the defendant argued that four days had passed since use, but expert evidence indicated that anything beyond six hours was likely outside the cut‑off for detection; the expert evidence thus undermined the defendant’s timing assertion. The case shows that courts give weight to appropriately qualified expert evidence on pharmacokinetics when evaluating claims about the timing of ingestion.

# Case authority — McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13] [TOPIC: communication of statutory requirement; unlawful arrest; admissibility] [CONCEPTS: McLean v Wilkie, communication requirement, s10(1) Road Safety Act, unlawful arrest, s138 Evidence Act, Arnesto v Hickman, effective communication]

McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13 held that police must effectively communicate the statutory requirement to proceed for analysis (and the reason for it) as a precondition to lawfully exercising enforcement powers under Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 10(1). The court emphasised that failure to communicate may render an arrest unlawful, though evidence may still be admitted under section 138 of the Evidence Act in some circumstances; the judgment adopted reasoning from Arnesto v Hickman about the meaning of "require" in related statutory settings.

# Case authority — Wilkie v McLean [2021] TASMC 5
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Wilkie v McLean [2021] TASMC 5] [TOPIC: admissibility of analysis results where communication defective] [CONCEPTS: Wilkie v McLean, admissibility, s138 Evidence Act, defective communication, breath test, magistrates' assessment]

Wilkie v McLean [2021] TASMC 5 considered whether breath analysis results should be excluded where the statutory communication requirements were not properly observed. The case confirms that an evidentiary discretion under s 138 of the Evidence Act must be exercised, and that defective communication does not automatically result in exclusion; courts will weigh the circumstances and potential prejudice.

# Case authority — Arnesto v Hickman (citation not provided in source) [REVIEW]
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Arnesto v Hickman] [TOPIC: interpretation of "require" in statutory context] [CONCEPTS: "require" meaning, Police Offences Act s55A, statutory interpretation, communication obligation, Arnesto v Hickman, adopted reasoning]

Arnesto v Hickman (citation not provided in the source) is relied upon for the interpretive proposition about the meaning of the word "require" in a statutory enforcement context (Police Offences Act s55A as cited in the source). The source reports that a judge adopted Wood J's reasoning in Arnesto v Hickman concerning the meaning of "require" and applied it by analogy to Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) s 10(1). Because the full citation was not supplied in the source text, this chunk is flagged [REVIEW] for completion with an authoritative citation before use as binding precedent.

# Case authority — Police v Bentley [2008] TASMC 9
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Police v Bentley [2008] TASMC 9] [TOPIC: proximity ("at or near") and reasonable distance in context of accident] [CONCEPTS: Police v Bentley, 100 metres, at or near, s2(3A), accident context, statutory purpose, reasonableness]

Police v Bentley [2008] TASMC 9 is authority upholding a finding that a person was sufficiently "at or near" the relevant location for statutory purposes where a 100‑metre separation was acceptable in the context of an accident. The decision emphasises pragmatic and purpose‑driven interpretation of proximity requirements in the Act when public safety is engaged.

# Case authority — Bennett v Richards [2011] TASMC 9
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Bennett v Richards [2011] TASMC 9] [TOPIC: proximity ("at or near") and reasonableness; 1.2 km accepted in context] [CONCEPTS: Bennett v Richards, 1.2 km, at or near, reasonableness, statutory purpose, context of exercise of power]

Bennett v Richards [2011] TASMC 9 is authority accepting a 1.2 kilometre separation as within the statutory "at or near" concept in that factual context. The decision, like Police v Bentley, treats distance as a contextual, purposive matter tied to the public safety objectives of the legislation.

# Case authority — Halliday v Nevill [1984] HCA 80
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Halliday v Nevill [1984] HCA 80] [TOPIC: implied licence to enter driveway / approach property] [CONCEPTS: Halliday v Nevill, implied licence, entry to driveway, consent to approach, trespass exception, access to house]

Halliday v Nevill [1984] HCA 80 is High Court authority on the implied licence to approach a house or enter a driveway for ostensible lawful purposes. The implied licence doctrine can justify initial police approaches that would otherwise be trespass and is relevant when considering the legality of subsequent police intervention pursuant to Road Safety Act powers.

# Case authority — Richardson v Shipp [1970] TASStRp 6; [1970] Tas SR 105
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Richardson v Shipp [1970] TASStRp 6; [1970] Tas SR 105] [TOPIC: indicia of influence and drawing inferences from physical signs] [CONCEPTS: Richardson v Shipp, indicia of intoxication, physical signs, behaviour, inference of influence, beyond reasonable doubt]

Richardson v Shipp (reported [1970] TASStRp 6; [1970] Tas SR 105) is an authority that courts may rely upon to draw inferences from observable abnormalities of behaviour and physical signs as evidence that a person who ingested a substance had become "influenced" by it. The case underlines the evidential basis for inferring impairment from conduct and signs.

# Case authority — Pulleine v Button [1948] SAStRp 5; [1948] SASR 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Pulleine v Button [1948] SAStRp 5; [1948] SASR 1] [TOPIC: evidence of influence from behaviour and physical signs] [CONCEPTS: Pulleine v Button, behavioural indicia, physical signs, inference, impairment evidence]

Pulleine v Button [1948] is a decision establishing that courts may infer influence from observable behaviours and physical signs; it is cited as a foundational authority for using indicia to prove impairment in driving‑under‑the‑influence prosecutions.

# Case authority — O'Connor v Shaw [1958] Qd R 384
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: O'Connor v Shaw [1958] Qd R 384] [TOPIC: observable abnormalities as evidence of alcohol influence; indicia] [CONCEPTS: O'Connor v Shaw, Philp J, observable abnormalities, indicia of influence, alcohol signs, inference of intoxication]

O'Connor v Shaw [1958] Qd R 384 (per Philp J) is authority that certain observable abnormalities of behaviour and physical signs enable an inference that a person who has ingested alcohol has become influenced by it. Although the decision addresses alcohol, the approach to indicia is applied analogously to drug cases when assessing capacity to control a vehicle.

# Case authority — Bedelph v Weedon [1963] TASStRp 9; [1963] Tas SR 69
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Bedelph v Weedon [1963] TASStRp 9; [1963] Tas SR 69] [TOPIC: indicators of intoxication and inferences] [CONCEPTS: Bedelph v Weedon, indicia, behaviour, physical signs, intoxication inference]

Bedelph v Weedon [1963] is cited for the proposition that courts may infer intoxication from certain behaviours and physical signs. The case supports the general doctrinal approach to proving driving under the influence through objective indicators.

# Case authority — Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC] [TOPIC: needle phobia and refusal to submit to blood test] [CONCEPTS: Lyons v Dyson, needle phobia, medical evidence, refusal to provide blood, magistrate findings, interventions]

Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC (paras 23–33) found that mere assertion of needle phobia is insufficient to avoid the statutory requirement to provide a blood sample; the defendant must demonstrate both the existence of a phobia and that sampling would be detrimental to health and that reasonable interventions could not be used to permit sampling.

### Chunk 1 — Passive ingestion of Meth — Miriam Connor email (29 May 2018)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Passive inhalation of methylamphetamine — FSST view] [CONCEPTS: methamphetamine, passive inhalation, vapour, FFST, crystal meth, smoking devices, condensation, vapour characteristics] [CITATION: Passive ingestion Meth - Miriam Connor 29 May 2018]

Covers: Passive ingestion of Meth — email from Miriam Connor, Forensic Science Service Tasmania (29 May 2018).

Methylamphetamine is heated to become a vapour which is then actively breathed into the lungs. It does not exist in the air, like smoke from cigarettes or cannabis -- therefore it cannot be inhaled passively. There is no scientific literature or research into passive methylamphetamine exposure, as it is just not possible to passively inhale methylamphetamine vapour, from our understanding. It will be difficult to write a proof for this, given there is no scientific evidence, but the physical characteristics of methylamphetamine mean that it does not exist in a gas form, it has to be heated to form a vapour.

---

### Chunk 2 — Passive ingestion of Meth — Cunningham, Juliette (FSST) email (31 May 2017)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Passive inhalation of methylamphetamine — FSST technical note] [CONCEPTS: methylamphetamine hydrochloride, melting point, vapour, condensation, smoking devices, THC vs meth smoking] [CITATION: Passive ingestion Meth - Cunningham Juliette 31 May 2017]

Covers: Further FSST commentary on passive inhalation of methamphetamine — Cunningham, Juliette (31 May 2017).

ng our advice on passive inhalation of methamphetamine?

 

Further to this, the only other information that I believe could be of help to you is that methylamphetamine hydrochloride (commonly referred to as crystal meth) the form typically "smoked" has a melting point of between 170-175 degrees. Obviously it will vaporise above this temperature but this gives an indication that methamphetamine is not just floating around in the air of a room at ambient temperature when people are using it.  It will, in fact, easily condense on the cooler parts of the glass smoking devices used before it has any chance of "escaping" into a room -- at any significant/detectable level. Meth and THC differ considerably in how they are "smoked". Cannabis is typically literally burnt as per a cigarette so that the smoke is inhaled/exhaled. Meth is heated until it changes from a solid to a liquid to a gas and this is what is inhaled . Smoke from a cannabis joint can be long lasting (given the right conditions e.g. lack of ventilation), meth vapour is not the same.

---

Covers: Drug pattern defence — timing of THC detection and relevance to mistake of fact.

THC positive usually indactes use within 6 hours. After that usually undectable regardless of subjective charactersitcs.

Mistake of fact for timing of drug use

See **De Wit v Ali [2021] TASMC 10**

Def argued 4 days had passed -- expert evidence said anything beyond 6 hours likely to meet cut off for analaysis

### Chunk 4 — Questions to be put to FFST Analyst in EIC — If you put the report in the 'mail out' tray yourself
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970] [SECTION: s13B; s32 Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) referenced] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s32 / RSAD Act s13B - FSST Analyst mail out tray script] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: script] [TOPIC: Scripted examination of FSST analyst where analyst placed Report of Analysis in mail out tray personally] [CONCEPTS: FSST analyst, Report of Analysis, s13B service, refresh memory, s32(3), peer review, mail out tray, instrument print-outs, approved analyst]

Covers: Scripted Q&A for examination-in-chief of an FSST analyst where the analyst put the Report of Analysis in the 'mail out' tray themselves.

[Evidence of the FSST Analyst -- if you put the report in the 'mail out' tray yourself]

Q -- Can you please tell me you name and occupation please?

A -- My name is ........... and I am a Forensic Scientist employed by Forensic Science Service Tasmania.

Q -- Can you please tell me what your duties at FSST are?

A -- My duties include but are not limited to receiving, preparing and analysing Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 blood samples for alcohol and/or illicit drugs as well as the preparation and review of reports for Police and the Coroner.

Q -- Please tell me what qualifications you have to carry out those duties.

A -- Detail education and experience etc. and the fact that you are an 'approved analyst' pursuant to the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970, such appointment being declared by the Governor in the Government Gazette on .../.../... (date).

**Just the month and year will be fine if you don't recall the exact date.**

Q -- You are aware that you have been summoned to give evidence in relation to ..... .... (this defendant) and the analysis of his/her blood sample?

A -- Yes

Q -- Do you have an independent recollection of what you did in relation to the defendant's blood sample?

A -- No I do not, as I am involved in the analysis of (....) samples of blood each year.

Q -- Do you make notes or keep records?

A -- Yes, we have instrument print-outs for each sample and keep a case file including all relevant paperwork including submitted forms and reports etc. which are subsequent used to create the 'Report of Analysis'.

Q -- Have you reviewed your notes in the last 24 hours?

A -- Yes, several times.

Q -- Has reading your notes refreshed your memory so that you can recall what you did in relation to this defendant's blood sample?

A -- No it has not.

**I will seek leave for you to be able to either use that document to refresh your memory, or read from it pursuant to section 32(3) of the Evidence Act.**

**At this point the court will allow you to read directly from your notes or the report of analysis to give your evidence about the analysis of the blood sample.**

Q - Are you aware of your obligation pursuant to the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act in relation to service of the \'Report of Analysis\'?

 

A -- Yes, my obligations are that I must cause it to be served on that person within 21 days, pursuant to section 13B of the Act.

 

Q - Do you recall what you did with the report relating to the report in this matter?

 

A -- I do not have an independent recollection of what I did with the report as is was some time ago and I complete many such reports as part of my duties.

Q - What is your usual practice when you complete a report?

A -- I am one of (\...\....) scientists in FSST responsible for analysing about (\...\....) blood samples every year.  As such I do not have a direct recollection of what I did with this report.  What I can say is, it is standard practice that a colleague, in this instance that was (.... ....) checks the report (peer review). The purpose for this is checking for errors and peer review.

I know that the peer review/checking was done because the records reflect that (.... ....) did this. I then made a copy for internal filing and placed it into the 'out' tray in the administration section so it will be posted. I am certain that I would have done this in this instance as I never deviate from this procedure after completing a Report of Analysis.

---

### Chunk 5 — Questions to be put to FFST Analyst in EIC — If you gave the report to another analyst who put it in the mail out tray
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970] [SECTION: s13B; s32 Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) referenced] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s32 / RSAD Act s13B - FSST Analyst handed to colleague script] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: script] [TOPIC: Scripted examination of FSST analyst where report was handed to colleague for mailing] [CONCEPTS: peer review, mail out tray, standard practice, approved analyst, service obligations, instrument print-outs]

Covers: Scripted Q&A for examination-in-chief of an FSST analyst where the analyst handed the Report of Analysis to another analyst who put it in the mail out tray.

[Evidence of the FSST Analyst -- if you gave the report to someone else (another analyst) who put it in the mail out tray]

Q -- Can you please tell me you name and occupation please?

A -- My name is ........... and I am a Forensic Scientist employed by Forensic Science Service Tasmania.

Q -- Can you please tell me what your duties at FSST are?

A -- My duties include but are not limited to receiving, preparing and analysing Road Safety blood samples for alcohol and/or illicit drugs as well as the preparation and review of reports for Police and the Coroner.

Q -- Please tell me what qualifications you have to carry out those dutes.

A -- Detail education and experience etc. and the fact that you are an 'approved analyst' pursuant to the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970, such appointment being declared by the Governor in the Government Gazette on .../.../... (date).

**Just the month and year will be fine if you don't recall the exact date.**

Q -- You are aware that you have been summoned to give evidence in relation to ..... .... (this defendant) and the analysis ofg his/her blood sample?

A -- Yes

Q -- Do you have an independent recollection of what you did in relation to the defendant's blood sample?

A -- No I do not, as I am involved in the analysis of (....) samples of blood each year.

Q -- Do you make notes or keep records?

A -- Yes, we have instrument print-outs for each sample and keep a case file including all relevant paperwork including submitted forms and reports etc. which are subsequent used to create the 'Report of Analysis'.

Q -- Have you reviewed your notes in the last 24 hours?

A -- Yes, several times.

Q -- Has reading your notes refreshed your memory so that you can recall what you did in relation to this defendant's blood sample?

A -- No it has not.

**I will seek leave for you to be able to either use that document to refresh your memory, or read from it pursuant to section 32(3) of the Evidence Act.**

**At this point the court will allow you to read directly from your notes or the report of analysis to give your evidence about the analysis of the blood sample.**

Q - Are you aware of your obligation pursuant to the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act in relation to service of the \'Report of Analysis\'?

 

A -- Yes, my obligations are that I must cause it to be served on that person within 21 days, pursuant to section 13B of the Act.

 

Q - Do you recall what you did with the 'Report of Analysis' that relates to the defendant in this matter?

 

A -- I do not have an independent recollection of what I did with the report as is was some time ago and I complete many such reports as part of my duties.

Q -- How many analysists are there at FSST analysing blood samples for alcohol and drugs, and how may are completed each year?

A -- I am one of (\...\....) scientists in FSST responsible for analysing about (\...\....) samples every year.  As such I do not have a direct recollection of what I did with this report.  

Q - What can you tell me if there is a standard practice for FSST analysists when you complete a report?

A - It is standard practice that I have a colleague check the report (peer review) for accuracy and errors. If they are satisfied with its accuracy then it is their responsibility to make a copy for internal filing and places the 'Report of Analysis' into the 'mail out' tray in the administration section so it will be posted. It is my expectation that this standard practice is carried out by whoever checks my reports and it is my belief that all staff are aware of their obligations. In this instance the records show that (.... ....) was the colleague that was responsible for these duties.

### Chunk 6 — Questions to be put to FFST Analyst in EIC — Evidence of the FSST state servant (service of blood papers)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: script] [TOPIC: Scripted examination of FSST state servant regarding mailing/service of Report of Analysis] [CONCEPTS: mail out, service, case number, handwriting recognition, contemporaneous records, s32 refresh memory, service obligations] [CITATION: FSST state servant mail service script]

Covers: Scripted Q&A for examination-in-chief of an FSST state servant responsible for mailing/service of Reports of Analysis.

[Evidence of the FSST state servant (service of blood papers)]

Q - Were you employed by Forensic Science Services Tasmania on the xx/xx/xx?

 

Q - What was your role there?

 

Q - How long had you been employed at FSST on that date?

 

Q - During that time how many Reports of Analysis did you mail out?  (an estimate is fine)

 

Q - Do you have a recollection of mailing each Report?

 

Q - At the time you mailed each report did you keep contemporaneous records or notes?

 

Q - Do you have a document there with you that has been given to you by court staff?

 

Q - Can you tell me the case number on that document please?

 

Q - What is that document?

 

Q - Can you look at page 4 of the document and tell me if recognise the handwriting and signature on that page?

 

Q - Does seeing the document refresh your memory enough so that you recall mailing that particular report?

 

**If you still do not recall mailing that report (and there is no issue if you do not) I will seek leave for you to be able to either use that document to refresh your memory, or read from it pursuant to section 32 of the Evidence Act.**

### Chunk 7 — Required to inform driver of requirement — McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Requirement to communicate obligation to submit to analysis; unlawful arrest issue] [CONCEPTS: communication requirement, unlawful arrest, s10(1), s138 Evidence Act, Arnesto v Hickman, McLean v Wilkie, admissibility] [CITATION: McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13 (24 February 2022)] [CASE: McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13 (24 February 2022)]

Covers: The need to communicate requirement to submit to breath analysis and consequences for arrest/admissibility (McLean v Wilkie).

**McLean v Wilkie [2022] TASSC 13 (24 February 2022)**

Need to communicate requirement. Failure to do so makes ait an unlawful arrest. But evidence may still get in under s138,

[11] "The defendant objected to the admissibility of the result of the breath analysis on the basis that Constable Prettyman (sic), had unlawfully arrested him and failed to communicate to the defendant that he was required \'*\...to proceed to such place or into such vehicle\... as the officer may indicate and there submit himself \... to that analysis\...\'* as contemplated by the *Road *Safety* Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970*, s 10(1) (the Act). The officer was also obliged to communicate the reason for the obligation. I adopted the reasoning of Wood J in *Arnesto v Hickman* as to the meaning of the word *\'require\' *in the [*Police Offences Act 1935*], [s55A] as logically applicable to the same expression* *in the Act, s 10(1).

### Chunk 8 — Refuse to submit to breath analysis (statutory/regulatory summary)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970] [SECTION: ss7A(1), 7A(4), 10(1)-(4A), 14(2), 25(2), 26, 29] [CITATION: RSAD Act 1970 - Refuse Breath Analysis statutory summary and Reg 7 requirements] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Statutory and regulatory framework for refusal to submit to breath analysis] [CONCEPTS: breath test, approved operator, s10 directions, custody, 3 hours rule, evidentiary certificates, device testing, Regulation 7, printout, mouthpiece, refusal offence]

Covers: Statutory and regulatory provisions relevant to refusal to submit to breath analysis.

Refuse Breath Analysis

Section 7A(1): Driver liable to submit to a breath test to a Police Officer

Section 7A(4): Whether a positive response or not to the breath test, if the Police officer reasonably believes alcohol may be present, they become liable to submit to a breath analysis.

Section 10(1): Police can require someone to go to a police station for a breath analysis

Section 10(2) Can take a person into "custody" for the necessary time period to effect sub-s 1

Section 10(3) Power under sub-s 2 restricted to where Police believe can be done within 3 hours after "relevant time" (defined in Act)

Section 10(4) Power to "direct' person to submit to breathe analysis

Section 10(4A) Where person fails or refuses direction in 10(4), the "approved operator" is to inform them that they may elect to take blood instead if it can be done within 3 hours of "relevant time"

Section 14(2) Fail or refuse to provide breath test in accordance with directions of "approved operator' is an offence

Section 25(2): Evidentiary certificate as prima facie evidence contained within.

Section 26 Evidentiary certificate that device was working.

Section 29 Caveat -- certs must be served 14 days prior (was given by cops to him at the time

Regulation 6 Device must have been tested within 12 months before using

Regulation 7 Requirements that the approved operator must comply with when undertaking a breathe analysis

> (a) that the person submitting to the breath analysis gives a sample of breath into a mouthpiece supplied by the operator; and
>
> (b) that an unused mouthpiece is provided for use in the breath analysis; and
>
> (c) that the operator does not carry out a breath analysis unless he or she is satisfied that the person has not consumed intoxicating liquor during the 15 minutes preceding the analysis; and
>
> (d) after the completion of the breath analysis --
>
> (i) that the operator records the resultant reading of the breath analysis; and
>
> (ii) that the operator inquires of the person whether the person is --
>
> (A) for a breath analysis carried out under and for the Principal Act, a person mentioned in [section 6(3)] of the Principal Act; or
>
> (B) for a breath analysis carried out under and for the Related Act, an operator of a commercial vessel; and
>
> (iii) that the operator completes and signs the written statement required by the relevant enactment; and
>
> (e) that the person submitting to the breath analysis is provided with a copy of the printout supplied by that instrument.

---

### Chunk 9 — Refuse to submit to blood test — Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Needle phobia and refusal to submit to blood — Lyons v Dyson] [CONCEPTS: needle phobia, blood sample refusal, Lyons v Dyson, magistrate findings, sedative, detriment to health] [CITATION: Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC - needle phobia] [CASE: Lyons v Dyson [2018] TASMC]

Covers: Summary of Lyons v Dyson on needle phobia as a defence to refusal to submit to blood.

Needle phobia no excuse.

See para 23-33 ***Lyons v Dyson*** [2018] TASMC

In summary, the magistrate found that satisfying the court that the defendant had a needle phobia is insufficient, he needed to satisfy the court that he had the needle phobia, and that the taking of a sample of blood would have been detrimental to his physical or mental health and that appropriate interventions (such as being given a sedative to calm him) could not be used to enable the sample to be taken.

---

### Chunk 10 — Driving Under the Influence — doctrinal note and authorities
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Proof standard and indicia for driving under the influence of drugs] [CONCEPTS: driving under influence, proof beyond reasonable doubt, observable abnormalities, Richardson v Shipp, Pulleine v Button, O'Connor v Shaw, Bedelph v Weedon] [CITATION: Driving Under the Influence - doctrinal note and cases] [CASE: Richardson v Shipp [1970] TASStRp 6; Pulleine v Button [1948] SAStRp 5; O'Connor v Shaw [1958] Qd R 384; Bedelph v Weedon [1963] TASStRp 9]

Covers: How to prove driving under the influence of drugs and relevant case authorities.

The question of whether the defendant was driving under the influence of a drug to such an extent as to be incapable of having proper control of the motor vehicle is a question of fact which must be proved beyond reasonable doubt: (see *Richardson v Shipp* [1970] TASStRp 6; [1970] Tas SR 105; *Pulleine v Button* [1948] SAStRp 5; [1948] SASR 1). If the drug in question was alcohol, then it is well established that inferences can be drawn from indicia, "*certain observable abnormalities of behaviour and certain physical signs which evidence when a person who has ingested alcohol has become 'influenced' by the alcohol*". (*O'Connor v Shaw* [1958] Qd R 384, per Philp J at 386. See also *Bedelph v Weedon* [1963] TASStRp 9; [1963] Tas SR 69).

---

### Chunk 11 — Requirement to communicate "requirement" to submit (s10(1) and s138 EA remark)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970] [SECTION: s10(1)] [CITATION: RSAD Act s10(1) - communication requirement and s138 Evidence Act admission remark (Wilkie v McLean)] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Communication of requirement as precondition to enforcement; possibility of s138 admission] [CONCEPTS: s10(1), communication, enforcement precondition, s138 Evidence Act, Wilkie v McLean] [CASE: Wilkie v McLean [2021] TASMC 5 (4 October 2021)]

Covers: The need to effectively communicate the requirement to submit and the possibility of adducing evidence despite failure to communicate under s138 Evidence Act.

"... the effective communication of the requirement is a precondition to the exercise of police power to enforce the obligation on the defendant to submit to breath analysis." *Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970*, *s10(1).

However, can argue under s 138 EA (under some circimustances) that results should be allowed in even when not communicated. **Wilkie v McLean [2021] TASMC 5 (4 October 2021)**
