### Chunk 12 — OFT / OFA law — statutory/regulatory summary and cases
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970] [SECTION: ss2(3), 2(3A), 2B(1), 3(1), 3(5), 7B(1), 7B(5), 8A(1), 10(1), 11, 11(1A), 13C(1)(a), 13C(2)-(3), 13C, 23A, 25, 26, 27A, 28, 28A, 29, 30A] [CITATION: RSAD Act 1970 - OFT/OFA law, Regs 5,11,12,17 and Sch1] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Liability to OFT/OFA, sample-taking, labelling, reporting, presumptions, certificates, and costs] [CONCEPTS: OFT, OFA, implied licence, s7B, 2(3A) accident, Police v Bentley, Bennett v Richards, approved analyst, regulations 11-12, 13C reporting, 23A presumption, certificates service, s29, costs s30A]

Covers: Law on Occupational Field Tests (OFT) / Oral Fluid Analysis (OFA) — powers, presumptions, sample procedure, labelling, reporting, certificates, and costs.

**LAW:**

**Intercept/ trespass**

Section 7AA RSAD Act: Power to enter private residence if uninterrupted continuous pursuit.

**OR**

Implied licence to enter driveway *Halliday v Nevill*, [1984] HCA 80

Asked for licence - Power under s **46A** of the **V&T** **Act** to ask for drivers licence of driver (but not a power to stop)

**Test/ Analysis**

Section 2(3): Liability -- defined as person required to submit under inter alia 7B

Section 3(1): Approved analyst can be appointed by Minster

Regulation 5: Approved analyst minimum qualifications and experience (degree and 2 year's experience)

Section 3(5): Approved operator defined as one authorised by commissioner.

7B (1): "At or near..."

***Police v Bentley [2008] TASMC 9*** 100m ok in context of 2(3A) "accident"

> ***Bennett v Richards [2011] TASMC 9*** 1.2km ok in context of 2(3A) "accident"
>
> Both argued on same policy grounds as giving effect to the stated purpose of the legislation (public safety)

7B (5): Then liable to OFA

**OR**

8A (1): found in circumstances -- giving rise to reasonable suspicion leads to liability to OFT and/or OFA

10 (1): After liability arising under 7B (5) this section gives power to require them to proceed to such place etc to submit to that analysis

2B (1): Def must comply with 10 (1) directions until analysis complete.

13C: Requirements of what is to be done when sample is taken

13C(1)(a): Taken in accordance with "prescribed requirements'

Regulation 11: Details specific procedure for taking of sample and that it must be dissolved and placed into 2 suitable containers.

Regulation 12: Labelling requirements -- initialled by operator and subject as well as date and time and one mut have "control written on them"

11(1A): Approved operator must provide notice to def after OF sample provided

(relevant form is form 5 under Regulation 17 and sch 1 of the Regulations)

13C(2): FFST must provide TAsPol with a report within 21 days after 'report finished" note maybe different to when sample analysed

13C(3): Cops must provide def with copy of report (this section does not put time limit on it)

**Presumptions:**

23A: Positive result at anytime within in 6 hours of relevant time of driving, is taken to have had drugs in system.

**Certificates:**

All subject to section 29 requiring service of certificates 14 days beforehand. Note: "Cert of service" is proof under s 29(1A) -- Note "service" same definition as *Acts Interpretation Act* per s 29(4)

Section 29(2) Certificates can tendered without the author being called.

- Note that def can call analyst if they want, and even if called by defence can be cx by the def.

Section 26: Certificate of approved OPERATOR and functions as to instruments

Section 27A(1): A cert (taking and dealing) stating person stated took sample at day and time of offender named and also that they were an approved operator and that sample was dealt with in certain way

Section 27A(2): A cert of delivery, by person named, to analyst with details as to marks and labels

Section 28/ 28A: A certificate of analysis that show corresponding continuity information as to "delivery receipt."

Note: Evidence of service under s. 13B/ 13C report satisfies service requirement for admission of 28 cert.

**Costs:**

S 30A: Can charge for analysis

---

<!-- block_034 master -->

# Evidence and Hearing Procedure for OFA/Breath Cases
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: hearing sequence for toxicology evidence] [CONCEPTS: evidence sequencing, certificates, Form 2, Form 5, sample storage, chain of custody, oral testimony, tendering documents, storage register, continuity]

The order of proof in prosecutions involving breath or oral fluid analysis should start with witness evidence of the sampling and handling, then documentary continuity and analytical certificates. First, call witnesses who can attest to the initial intercept and the taking of breath/oral-fluid samples and the driving/operational facts (e.g. evidence of intercept/driving). Second, call witnesses to prove belief or suspicion (positive roadside breath test) and identification of the approved operator who took the sample (oral evidence). Third, call the operator to establish that an oral fluid analysis (OFA) or breath analysis was conducted and to tender the relevant statutory forms (for example, Form 5 or Form 2 as applicable). Fourth, tender and prove the sample storage evidence (oral evidence plus the storage register document). Finally, tender certificates of delivery and certificate(s) of analysis and prove service of those certificates to the defendant where required. Each stage establishes a link in continuity: who took the sample, how it was labelled and delivered, how it was stored, and what the analyst found. Practical note: in-court oral testimony should be used to introduce documentary certificates before reliance is placed on statutory presumptions.

# Certificate of Service (sub-section 29(1A))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 29(1A)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: certificate of service requirement] [CONCEPTS: certificate of service, proof of service, statutory certificate, Acts Interpretation Act, postage as evidence, disclosure insufficient]

A certificate of service under sub-section 29(1A) must be proved to establish that the defendant was served with certificates before those certificates can be relied upon at trial. Documentary disclosure alone is insufficient; the prosecution must demonstrate service in accordance with the statutory requirements. The source material notes that, for proving service, evidence of postage can be sufficient under the Acts Interpretation Act (postal service regarded as proof of service), but mere disclosure of certificates without proof of service will not permit reliance on presumptive statutory certificates. Practically, the prosecutor should tender the certificate of service and, if necessary, call the server or tender postal records to establish compliance with service requirements.

# Section 27A(1) — Certificate that an Approved Operator Took and Dealt with a Sample
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 27A(1)] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory definition] [TOPIC: certificate of taking and dealing with sample] [CONCEPTS: approved operator, certificate of taking, sample identification, chain of custody, statutory certificate, evidentiary weight]

Section 27A(1) provides for a certificate stating that a named person (an approved operator) took the sample from the person named, at a stated date and time, and that the person was an approved operator who dealt with the sample in a stated way. The certificate identifies the sample, records the operator’s status as an approved operator, and describes the handling steps taken by the operator. Because the certificate is a statutory form, once properly proved and served, it can be relied upon to establish the act of taking and the immediate dealing with the sample unless rebutted according to statutory safeguards and evidentiary rules.

# Section 27A(2) — Certificate of Delivery to Analyst
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 27A(2)] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: certificate of delivery / continuity] [CONCEPTS: delivery certificate, marks and labels, analyst receipt, chain of custody, continuity evidence, statutory certificate]

Section 27A(2) authorises a certificate recording delivery of a taken sample to an analyst, including details as to identifying marks and labels. This certificate is designed to establish continuity between the sample as taken and the sample received by the laboratory analyst; it records who delivered the sample, the time and date of delivery, and the identifying characteristics used to match the sample to the person and sampling event. Proper proof of this delivery certificate assists the prosecution in establishing an unbroken chain of custody.

# Sections 28 and 28A — Certificate of Analysis and Connection to Section 19C
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 28] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: certificate of analysis and continuity] [CONCEPTS: certificate of analysis, laboratory report, continuity, s19C requirement, presumption of accuracy, delivery receipt]

A certificate under sections 28 and 28A records the analyst’s results and shows corresponding continuity information linked to the delivery receipt. This certificate operates with the statutory presumptions (for example, as to the accuracy of analysis) and covers requirements such as those in section 19C where relevant. The certificate should include identification of the sample as received, the tests performed, and the numeric or qualitative results; when properly proved and served it may attract presumptions of correctness subject to any defence evidence to the contrary.

# Liability "No Matter Intended Purpose" — Tasmania v Billinghurst (No 1) [2018] TASSC 3
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Billinghurst (No 1) [2018] TASSC 3] [TOPIC: liability irrespective of intended purpose] [CONCEPTS: impropriety, use of police powers, RBT sites, admissibility, public law issues, statutory power misuse]

Tasmania v Billinghurst (No 1) [2018] TASSC 3 confirms that certain investigatory or sampling arrangements (including sham or staged random breath test sites) do not necessarily amount to impropriety sufficient to exclude or vitiate evidence simply because of the site’s character. The Court observed that even a sham random breath test site would not necessarily involve impropriety, much less a symbiotic use of multiple police powers, where the conduct did not otherwise amount to an unlawful exercise of power or breach of statutory limits. The authority supports the proposition that technical or contrived testing locations do not automatically defeat the admissibility of breath or oral-fluid results absent other illegality.

# Defences to OFA — Burden on Defendant under Evidence Act and Chidiac v R
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: onus for defences to oral fluid analysis] [CONCEPTS: burden of proof, balance of probabilities, Evidence Act s142, Chidiac v R, evidential onus, displacing statutory presumptions]

The burden for establishing defences to oral fluid analysis (OFA) rests on the defendant on the balance of probabilities, following the framework of the Evidence Act and authorities such as Chidiac v R (1991) 171 CLR 432. A defendant seeking to displace statutory presumptions or to establish a defence (for example, that a sampling or analysis error occurred) must satisfy the court on the balance of probabilities. The practical effect is that defence adductions must be cogent and supported by evidence (often expert) to rebut presumptions of accuracy.

# Bass v Holbrow [2011] TASMC 4 — Magistrates and Onus in OFA Cases
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Bass v Holbrow [2011] TASMC 4] [TOPIC: onus and magistrate approach to OFA defences] [CONCEPTS: magistrate findings, onus on defendant, OFA defence, balance of probabilities, admissibility]

Bass v Holbrow [2011] TASMC 4 illustrates the magistrates’ approach to OFA defences and affirms that the onus is on the defendant to establish the defence on the balance of probabilities. The case is used as an example where the court required the defendant to adduce evidence sufficient to unsettle statutory presumptions and the prosecution’s certificate-based proof.

# Breath Analysis Powers — s8(1), s8(8) and s11
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 8(1); 8(8); 11] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory rule] [TOPIC: powers to require breath analysis and ancillary forms] [CONCEPTS: s8(1), suspicion threshold, roadside breath test, s8(8) negative result, Form 2, s11, requirement to provide form]

Section 8(1) of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas) confers power to require a person to undergo breath analysis where an officer has the requisite suspicion (a threshold arguably lower than belief in some authorities). An officer may use a roadside breath test to confirm presence of alcohol; however, a negative roadside reading can negate the statutory power to require further analysis under s8(8) (i.e. if the roadside breath test is negative, the liability to further testing may dissipate). Section 11 requires that after breath analysis the officer must give the defendant and read a Form 2 (or the statutorily prescribed form) informing the person of the result and statutory consequences. These procedural steps are important for establishing valid testing and triggering related evidentiary presumptions.

# Statutory Presumptions — s23(2), s23(4) and s23(8)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 23(2); 23(4); 23(8)] [ACT: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [CITATION: Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory rule] [TOPIC: presumptions about blood/breath concentration and relevant act of driving] [CONCEPTS: presumption of accuracy, deemed concentration, six-hour rule, relevant act of driving, evidential burden, temporal link]

Section 23(2) creates a presumption that a breath-analysis result is accurate at the time of testing unless proved otherwise on the balance of probabilities. Section 23(4) deems that the alcohol concentration shown by a breath or blood test shall be taken to have been the concentration at any time within six hours after the "relevant act of driving" (thus easing the prosecution’s proof of concentration at the time of driving). Section 23(8) defines "relevant act of driving" as the act of driving alleged to be an ingredient of the offence in the proceedings. Together these provisions position the statutory presumption and the deemed timing rule to facilitate proof where samples are taken within six hours of the alleged driving.

# Ausburn v Tasmania Police [2009] TASSC 21 — Interpretation of s23(4)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Ausburn v Tasmania Police [2009] TASSC 21] [TOPIC: operation and purpose of s23(4)] [CONCEPTS: deemed concentration, evidential burden, balance of probabilities, time of driving, statutory purpose]

Ausburn v Tasmania Police [2009] TASSC 21 explains that the primary purpose of s23(4) is to facilitate proof of drink-driving offences where the sample result alone may be insufficient to show an offence at the time of driving. The Court held that s23(4) casts a burden on the defendant to prove, on the balance of probabilities, that their alcohol concentration at the time of driving was not excessive. The decision treats the words of s23(4) as unambiguous: a tested concentration is "deemed" to have been the concentration at the relevant time unless the defendant demonstrates otherwise.

# Drink-Driving Sentencing Principles — McDonald v Nilsson [2009] TASSC 83
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: McDonald v Nilsson [2009] TASSC 83] [TOPIC: sentencing seriousness and prior convictions] [CONCEPTS: priors, imprisonment, high-range offending, awareness of intoxication, sentencing aggravation, prior convictions]

McDonald v Nilsson [2009] TASSC 83 is cited for the proposition that multiple prior convictions (for example, seven priors) can attract an immediate custodial sentence for drink-driving offences. The authority also notes that for high-range offences it is rarely acceptable to assert that the driver lacked appreciation of the risk of committing an offence, and prior offending and high readings heavily inform sentence severity.

# Sentencing Considerations — Manner and Reasons for Driving
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: factors relevant to mitigation/aggravation in drink-driving cases] [CONCEPTS: passengers, accident involvement, distance/time driven, location, motive, alternative arrangements, pre-offence conduct, police interactions, consumption history]

The manner and reasons for driving while over the prescribed limit are relevant sentencing considerations. Relevant factors include whether there were passengers, whether the driving caused an accident, the distance and duration of driving, where the defendant was driving, why they chose to drive while over the limit, what alternative arrangements existed and whether they were acted upon, and what occurred before the offence (including the manner of apprehension by police). For alcohol or drug offences, the court will expect detailed instructions from the client regarding consumption or use prior to driving and will consider contemporaneous statements to police when forming findings about culpability.

# Special Circumstances for Disqualification and Sentencing — Principles (Barratt v Thorpe and authorities)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: special circumstances in disqualification cases] [CONCEPTS: special circumstances, extraordinary or atypical, collective assessment, offender circumstances, offence circumstances, discretionary relief, statutory minimums]

A "special circumstances" argument to avoid the statutory minimum for disqualification or fine must show circumstances that are extraordinary, unusual or atypical and distinguishable from the general run of cases Parliament contemplated. Authorities such as Strickland v Whitehead, Bottomley v McDonald and Barratt v Thorpe emphasize that circumstances should be assessed collectively rather than in isolation; matters common to many offenders (e.g., injury from an alcohol-related accident) are unlikely to qualify as special. Even where special circumstances exist, the statute confers a discretion on the court — the presence of special circumstances does not compel a lesser penalty but permits the court to impose one.

# Section 6(3A) Administrative-Error Defence — Richardson v Pullen [1998] TASSC 167
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: statutory defence] [TOPIC: s6(3A) administrative error / onus on defendant] [CONCEPTS: s6(3A), administrative non-compliance, onus, license entitlement, proof requirements, Richardson v Pullen]

The defence under s6(3A) (administrative-error defence) requires the defendant to satisfy the court that, but for an unintentional failure to comply with an administrative requirement, the person would have been licensed to drive. Richardson v Pullen [1998] TASSC 167 confirms that the onus is on the defendant and that the court must consider relevant evidence to determine whether the defendant has established the ingredients of the defence; a mere assertion without examination of evidence is insufficient.

# Home Breathalysers — F v Ling [1985] Tas R 112
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: reliability of home breathalysers] [CONCEPTS: home breathalyser, reliability, admissibility, evidentiary weight, device accuracy]

Home breathalysers should not be relied upon as reasonable evidence of alcohol concentration for legal purposes. F v Ling [1985] Tas R 112 indicates that evidence from consumer home devices lacks the reliability and proven calibration necessary to displace statutory presumptions or serve as convincing exculpatory evidence in court.

# THC / Cannabis Driving — Poisons Act and Mandatory Minimums (practical note)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: cannabis driving and Poisons Act interaction] [CONCEPTS: THC, cannabis, Poisons Act, prescribing limitations, mandatory minimum penalty, legislative contemplation, special circumstances]

Notes from a practice file indicate that the Poisons Act’s limitation that only Tasmanian doctors can prescribe certain narcotic substances was likely within Parliament’s contemplation when drafting mandatory minimum penalties; therefore, such prescription limitations are unlikely to constitute "special circumstances" to reduce statutory penalties. This is a practical interpretive consideration where the illicit substance regulatory framework and sentencing minimums intersect.

# Costs of Analysis — Tasmania Police v Kelly Jane Suitters [2022] TASMC 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania Police v Kelly Jane Suitters [2022] TASMC 1] [TOPIC: recoverability of laboratory analysis costs] [CONCEPTS: costs of analysis, prosecution costs, laboratory fees, recover from defendant, discretionary award]

Tasmania Police v Kelly Jane Suitters [2022] TASMC 1 confirms that costs of laboratory analysis can be claimed against a defendant. The authority demonstrates that where analysis has been undertaken in connection with prosecuting a driving or drug matter, the court may award the prosecution the reasonable costs incurred for analysis.

# Drink-Pattern Defence — Requirements and Limits (Payne v Crawford; statistical limits)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: drink-pattern defence requirements] [CONCEPTS: drink-pattern, replicating consumption pattern, accredited expert, displacement of s23 presumption, statistical evidence, individual proof, peak blood alcohol, elimination rates]

A successful drink-pattern defence requires the defendant to replicate consumption times and quantities consistent with an expert analysis and to adduce accredited expert evidence to displace the statutory presumption (s23). Payne v Crawford (A/54/1992) establishes that defendants must provide detailed and credible evidence of consumption, timing and metabolism; most drink-pattern defences fail because defendants understate drinks or lack corroboration. Statistical generalisations about populations do not suffice to prove an individual's state on the balance of probabilities (State Government Insurance Commission v Laube); personal-specific elimination rates and corroborated evidence are required.

# Drink-Pattern Calculations and Factual Bases
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: drink pattern calculation inputs] [CONCEPTS: standard drink grams, 13oz stubby, whisky nip, alcohol grams, peak level times, elimination rates, BAC formula, total body water, personal variability]

Key factual bases for drink-pattern calculations include: definitions of drink sizes and grams of alcohol (example: 13 oz beer 4.5% ≈ 15.03 grams; 30 ml nip whiskey 37% ≈ 8.8 grams); peak blood alcohol commonly occurs between 30 and 120 minutes after consumption; personal elimination rates vary between approximately 0.01 and 0.02 grams per hour; and use of appropriate conversion formulae (e.g., G = BAC x 12.5 x TBW, where TBW = total body water informed by age, sex, height and weight). Courts expect personal, not population-average, elimination rates when assessing an individual defendant’s drink-pattern defence.

# Payne v Crawford (A/54/1992) and Statistical Limits — Authority
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Payne v Crawford (A/54/1992) (unreported)] [TOPIC: limits of statistical proof in drink-pattern defences] [CONCEPTS: statistical evidence, individual proof, balance of probabilities, expert evidence, drink-pattern, replication requirement]

Payne v Crawford (A/54/1992) (unreported) and related reasoning adopt the principle that statistical facts about a population cannot, by themselves, constitute legal proof on the balance of probabilities that a specific individual shares those characteristics. Consequently, a defendant cannot rely solely on population statistics (for example, average impairment at a given BAC) to prove their own lack of impairment; individualized evidence and expert analysis are required.

# WHITE v FAI (Underwood J) — Application of Payne Reasoning
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: WHITE v FAI (BC9600703 / 115 of 1994, B3/1996) (Underwood J)] [TOPIC: applying population statistics vs individual evidence] [CONCEPTS: expert evidence, population vs individual, impairment thresholds, judicial acceptance of expert evidence]

In WHITE v FAI (Underwood J), the court followed the reasoning in Payne v Crawford concerning the limits of statistical evidence but also accepted expert medical evidence that a particular BAC range is likely to impair an average person's driving faculties. The decision shows courts may accept expert testimony to bridge the gap between population statistics and individual effect where there is cogent medical evidence about impairment at particular BAC levels.

# Traffic Act s32(2B) — GBH from Driving as Summary Matter; Public Place Definition (Schubert v Lee)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 32(2B)] [ACT: Traffic Act] [TYPE: statutory rule] [TOPIC: GBH from driving summary classification and public place/public road definition] [CONCEPTS: s32(2B), grievous bodily harm, summary jurisdiction, public place, public road, open to or used by the public, Schubert v Lee]

Section 32(2B) of the Traffic Act treats GBH arising from driving as a summary matter. The concept of "public place" or "public road" should be understood by reference to Schubert v Lee [1946] HCA 28: words such as "open to or used by the public" describe a factual condition of real use by the public, distinct from use by licence of particular persons or occasional use. The prima facie class of "public" includes streets and places actually open to public use and in need of public protection.

# Schubert v Lee [1946] HCA 28 — Meaning of "Open to or Used by the Public"
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Schubert v Lee [1946] HCA 28] [TOPIC: interpretation of "open to or used by the public"] [CONCEPTS: public place, actual public use, licence to use, ordinary meaning, statutory interpretation, streets]

Schubert v Lee [1946] HCA 28 explains that the phrase "open to or used by the public" denotes a factual condition of real public use, as contrasted with use by a limited licence or occasional use. The case assists in determining whether a location qualifies as a public place or road for criminal offences requiring a public-context element.

# Vehicle and Traffic Act — s67 Evidentiary Certificates and Registrar Delegation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 67] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: evidentiary certificates and delegation] [CONCEPTS: s67 certificate, delegate signature, registrar power, s7 delegation, evidentiary admissibility, administrative delegation]

Section 67 of the Vehicle and Traffic Act permits certain evidentiary certificates to be signed by a delegate of the Registrar; the Registrar’s delegation power is drawn from section 7 of the Act. A certificate signed by a valid delegate remains effective for evidentiary purposes, but the prosecution should be prepared to prove the delegations and the delegate’s authority where challenged.

# Practical Questions to Challenge Service of Suspended Licence Papers
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: evidentiary lines to contest service of suspension notices] [CONCEPTS: mailbox security, delivery evidence, identification issues, timing of discovery, payment plan, compliance, witness statements]

When challenging service of suspended licence papers, relevant lines of inquiry include: whether the defendant was driving at the time alleged; identification issues (did the defendant receive or recognise the letter?); mailbox security and who has access to the mailbox; whether mail delivery to others at the address is reliable; the defendant’s location when the mail was delivered; circumstances of when and how the defendant discovered the suspension; statements made upon discovery; any delay in action after discovery (which may suggest prior knowledge); the nature and compliance with any payment plan (non-compliance undermines claims of ignorance). These questions help the court form findings on whether a defendant had the requisite notice.

# Requirement to Obtain a Tasmanian Licence — Vehicle and Traffic Act s8(1) & s8(2); Reg 49 (Driver Licensing & Registration Regulations 2021)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act] [TYPE: statutory rule] [TOPIC: Tasmanian licence requirement and exemption lapse] [CONCEPTS: s8(1), s8(2), interstate licence exemption, six-month validity, Regulation 49, continuous residence, exemption lapse, driver licensing]

Section 8(1) of the Vehicle and Traffic Act requires persons driving in Tasmania to hold a Tasmanian licence. Section 8(2) provides an exemption for those holding a licence issued elsewhere; however, Vehicle and Traffic (Driver Licensing and Vehicle Registration) Regulations 2021 reg 49 provides that a person ceases to be exempt under s8(2) after residing in Tasmania for a continuous period (the source notes 3 months). Practically, interstate licences are generally valid for six months for people temporarily in Tasmania, but longer residence triggers the statutory requirement to obtain a Tasmanian licence.

# Requirement to Register Vehicle in Tasmania — s28 and Regulation 129
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 28] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act] [TYPE: statutory rule] [TOPIC: registration exemption and lapse] [CONCEPTS: registration exemption, interstate registration, s28 exception, Regulation 129, three-month rule, ordinary residence, residential address]

Section 28 of the Vehicle and Traffic Act sets out exceptions from registration when the vehicle is registered under another jurisdiction’s law. Regulation 129 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Driver Licensing and Vehicle Registration) Regulations 2021 provides that the registration exemption lapses once the person has been in Tasmania for three months, unless they maintain an interstate residential address or ordinarily reside interstate. Thus, extended or ordinary residence in Tasmania ordinarily requires local registration.

# Driving Unlicensed — Interstate Licence Validity and Strict Liability
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: offence] [TOPIC: driving without a valid licence and strict liability nature] [CONCEPTS: driving unlicensed, interstate licence, six months, strict liability, Richardson v Pullen, public safety, statutory offence]

Driving without a valid Tasmanian licence (after the permitted transitional period) is a strict liability offence. Interstate licences are typically valid for six months for temporary visitors, but beyond that the exemption lapses and the driver must obtain a Tasmanian licence. Richardson v Pullen [1998] TASSC 167 is authority where the strict liability character of driving without a licence is highlighted; sentencing must reflect the public safety purpose of licensing rather than merely revenue protection.

# Sentencing for Driving Unlicensed — Lusted v Saunders [2012] TASSC 6
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lusted v Saunders [2012] TASSC 6] [TOPIC: sentencing rationale for unlicensed driving] [CONCEPTS: public safety, deterrence, licence purpose, sentencing principles, non-revenue purpose]

Lusted v Saunders [2012] TASSC 6 emphasises that licensing requirements are designed for public safety; sentencing for unlicensed driving should reflect that regulatory purpose. The offence is not trivial: absence of a licence raises concerns about the driver’s competence and public risk and is therefore subject to appropriate penalty considerations.

# Unregistered Vehicles — No Plate and s69
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: 69] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act] [TYPE: statutory rule] [TOPIC: unregistered vehicle and no plate presumption] [CONCEPTS: unregistered vehicle, s69, number plate absence, deemed unregistered, evidentiary consequence]

Under section 69 of the Vehicle and Traffic Act, a vehicle without a number plate can be deemed to be unregistered. The absence of required registration plates supports an inference of non-registration, which is material to summary or indictable charges concerning use of unregistered vehicles.

# Tasmania v Billinghurst (No 1) [2018] TASSC 3 — Authority (separate entry)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Billinghurst (No 1) [2018] TASSC 3] [TOPIC: impropriety and testing sites] [CONCEPTS: RBT, sham testing sites, impropriety, admissibility, police powers]

Tasmania v Billinghurst (No 1) [2018] TASSC 3 is an authority addressing allegations that contrived or "sham" random breath testing sites necessarily taint evidence. The court found that such contrivances do not automatically render testing improper or evidence inadmissible absent a specific unlawful exercise of power or procedural illegality.

# Chidiac v R (1991) 171 CLR 432 — Authority on Onus and Evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Chidiac v R (1991) 171 CLR 432] [TOPIC: burden of proof principles] [CONCEPTS: burden of proof, onus, criminal evidence, balance of probabilities, legal doctrine]

Chidiac v R (1991) 171 CLR 432 is a High Court authority on allocation of evidential and persuasive burdens; it is commonly cited for principles concerning when a defendant bears an evidential onus or a persuasive onus to prove matters on the balance of probabilities in criminal proceedings.

# Bass v Holbrow [2011] TASMC 4 — Authority (separate entry)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Bass v Holbrow [2011] TASMC 4] [TOPIC: magistrate approach to OFA defences] [CONCEPTS: OFA, magistrate findings, onus, credibility, defence evidence]

Bass v Holbrow [2011] TASMC 4 is a magistrates’ court authority illustrating how a tribunal approaches OFA defences and enforces the principle that the defendant bears the onus of proof for such defences.

# Peterson v Mitchell [1972] Tas SR 202 — Authority re s23(4) burden
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Peterson v Mitchell [1972] Tas SR 202] [TOPIC: s23(4) effect and defendant’s burden] [CONCEPTS: deemed concentration, evidential burden, balance of probabilities, statutory interpretation]

Peterson v Mitchell [1972] Tas SR 202 held that s23(4)-type provisions do not merely create an evidential burden but impose upon a defendant a substantive burden to prove, on the balance of probabilities, that their concentration at the time of driving was not excessive. The case is frequently relied on in interpreting s23(4) equivalents.

# Strickland v Whitehead [1995] TASSC 125 — Principles for "Special Circumstances"
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Strickland v Whitehead [1995] TASSC 125] [TOPIC: principles for special circumstances in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: special circumstances, collective assessment, non-exhaustive list, offender/offence circumstances, judicial discretion]

Strickland v Whitehead [1995] TASSC 125 sets out principles for assessing "special circumstances" under sentencing provisions: (1) circumstances cannot be exhaustively enumerated; (2) consider the circumstances collectively; (3) circumstances may concern both offender and offence; and (4) common circumstances do not ordinarily qualify as special.

# Gela v Cochrane [1992] TASSC 17 — Precedent cited on special circumstances
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Gela v Cochrane [1992] TASSC 17] [TOPIC: special circumstances precedents] [CONCEPTS: special circumstances, sentencing, precedential reasoning]

Gela v Cochrane [1992] TASSC 17 is one of the authorities discussing what may constitute "special circumstances" for the purposes of relief from statutory minima in disqualification or fines; it contributes to the judicial matrix for assessing atypicality.

# Johnston v Davies [1992] TASSC 51 — Special Circumstances and Judicial Discretion
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Johnston v Davies [1992] TASSC 51] [TOPIC: discretionary relief and special circumstances] [CONCEPTS: statutory minimums, discretionary relief, judicial consideration, special circumstances]

Johnston v Davies [1992] TASSC 51 confirms that the statutory provision allowing relief where "special circumstances" exist confers discretion on the court; the existence of special circumstances does not mandate a lesser penalty but permits the court to exercise discretion in light of all circumstances.

# Dellar v Crawford (1992) 62 A Crim R 171 — Authority on Special Circumstances
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Dellar v Crawford (1992) 62 A Crim R 171] [TOPIC: special circumstances precedent] [CONCEPTS: sentencing, special circumstances, appellate guidance]

Dellar v Crawford (1992) 62 A Crim R 171 is appellate authority referenced in the jurisprudence on what may constitute "special circumstances" to displace standard penalties in driving-related offences.

# Maynard v White (1994) 77 A Crim R 62 — Authority on Sentencing Context
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Maynard v White (1994) 77 A Crim R 62] [TOPIC: sentencing, special circumstances] [CONCEPTS: sentencing context, special circumstances, appellate review]

Maynard v White (1994) 77 A Crim R 62 is cited for its discussion of sentencing principles and the circumstances that may or may not amount to special circumstances in driving-related sentencing.

# Davies v Kennedy [1992] TASSC 58 — Special Circumstances Authority
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Davies v Kennedy [1992] TASSC 58] [TOPIC: sentencing and special circumstances] [CONCEPTS: special circumstances, sentencing discretion, precedential guidance]

Davies v Kennedy [1992] TASSC 58 is another Tasmanian authority forming part of the cluster of cases guiding the assessment of special circumstances in disqualification and related sentencing decisions.

# Bottomley v McDonald [2002] TASSC 27 — Special Circumstances and Judicial Discretion
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Bottomley v McDonald [2002] TASSC 27] [TOPIC: special circumstances interpretation] [CONCEPTS: special circumstances, extraordinary or atypical, sentencing discretion, relief from minima]

Bottomley v McDonald [2002] TASSC 27 emphasises that "special circumstances" must be extraordinary, unusual or atypical, clearly distinguishable from the general run of cases; the statutory provision does not require reduction where special circumstances exist but allows the court discretion to do so.

# Turner v Visser [1998] TASSC 93 — Supporting Authority on Special Circumstances
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Turner v Visser [1998] TASSC 93] [TOPIC: special circumstances principles] [CONCEPTS: special circumstances, sentencing discretion, case comparison]

Turner v Visser [1998] TASSC 93 is an authority cited alongside other cases when courts evaluate whether particular facts amount to special circumstances permitting relief from statutory minimum penalties.

# Richardson v Pullen [1998] TASSC 167 — Administrative Error Defence Authority
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Richardson, Roger v Pullen, Darren Arthur [1998] TASSC 167] [TOPIC: s6(3A) defence and onus] [CONCEPTS: administrative error, onus on defendant, licensing entitlement, evidence evaluation]

Richardson v Pullen [1998] TASSC 167 held that for a s6(3A) administrative-error defence to succeed, the magistrate must consider relevant evidence and determine whether the defendant has satisfied the court that, but for an unintentional administrative failure, they would have been licensed. The case highlights the need for explicit findings and consideration of onus.

# F v Ling [1985] Tas R 112 — Home Breathalysers Not Reasonable
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: F v Ling [1985] Tas R 112] [TOPIC: unreliability of home breathalysers] [CONCEPTS: home devices, reliability, admissibility, device accuracy]

F v Ling [1985] Tas R 112 establishes that consumer home breathalysers are generally not sufficiently reliable to be treated as reasonable evidence of alcohol concentration in criminal proceedings.

# Tasmania Police v Kelly Jane Suitters [2022] TASMC 1 — Costs of Analysis
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania Police v Kelly Jane Suitters [2022] TASMC 1] [TOPIC: recovery of laboratory costs] [CONCEPTS: prosecution costs, laboratory fees, recoverability, court discretion]

Tasmania Police v Kelly Jane Suitters [2022] TASMC 1 confirms that courts may award prosecution costs incurred for laboratory analysis against a defendant where analysis was necessary for prosecution.

# Payne v Crawford (A/54/1992) — Drink-Pattern Defence Authority
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Payne v Crawford (A/54/1992) (unreported)] [TOPIC: drink-pattern defence requirements] [CONCEPTS: replication of pattern, expert evidence, displacement of statutory presumption, credibility, corroboration]

Payne v Crawford is the key (unreported) authority establishing that a drink-pattern defence must replicate consumption times and quantities and be supported by accredited expert evidence; courts demand corroboration and individualised analyses to displace statutory presumptions.

# State Government Insurance Commission v Laube (1984) 37 SASR 31 — Statistical Evidence Limits
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: State Government Insurance Commission v Laube (1984) 37 SASR 31] [TOPIC: limits of population statistics as proof for individuals] [CONCEPTS: statistical evidence, population vs individual, proof on balance of probabilities, expert evidence]

State Government Insurance Commission v Laube (1984) 37 SASR 31 establishes the principle that statistical facts true of the majority cannot, alone, prove on the balance of probabilities that they are true of a given individual; individualised proof is required.

# WHITE v FAI (Underwood J) — Application of Drink-Pattern Principles
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: WHITE v FAI (BC9600703 / 115 of 1994, B3/1996) (Underwood J)] [TOPIC: expert evidence and impairment in drink cases] [CONCEPTS: expert testimony, BAC impairment thresholds, individual evidence, Payne v Crawford]

WHITE v FAI applies the Payne reasoning while accepting that cogent expert medical opinion can bridge from population-based impairment studies to probable impairment in a particular case when supported by facts.

# Lusted v Saunders [2012] TASSC 6 — Sentencing Purpose of Licensing
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lusted v Saunders [2012] TASSC 6] [TOPIC: licensing purpose and sentencing] [CONCEPTS: public safety, sentencing purpose, deterrence, licensing requirements]

Lusted v Saunders [2012] TASSC 6 underscores that licensing requirements are intended for public safety; sentencing for unlicensed driving should reflect this protective purpose and not be treated merely as revenue raising.

# Barratt v Thorpe [2020] TASMC 15 — Special Circumstances Applied
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Barratt v Thorpe [2020] TASMC 15] [TOPIC: special circumstances and disqualification] [CONCEPTS: special circumstances, judicial discretion, case collection, sentencing guidance]

Barratt v Thorpe [2020] TASMC 15 collects and applies authorities on "special circumstances," restating principles (via citation to Strickland, Bottomley and others) and demonstrating how courts should assess alleged special circumstances collectively and with caution.

# Schubert v Lee [1946] HCA 28 — Public Place Definition (separate entry)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Schubert v Lee [1946] HCA 28] [TOPIC: public place meaning] [CONCEPTS: open to public, used by the public, factual condition, statutory interpretation]

Schubert v Lee [1946] HCA 28 clarifies that "open to or used by the public" describes a factual condition of real public use (streets, thoroughfares) and assists in defining the public-place element of many traffic offences.

Evidence OFA conducted (oral)

Tactical workflow covering certificates required and order of proof in a hearing.

Start with sub-section 29(1A) "certificate of service." Must prove def served with certificates before they can be relied upon (disclosure not sufficient, but postage is -- per *Acts Interpretation Act*)

Section 27A(1): "A cert (taking and dealing)" stating person stated (approved Bas operator) took sample at day and time of offender named and also that they were an approved operator and that sample was dealt with in certain way

Section 27A(2): "A cert of delivery," by person named, to analyst with details as to marks and labels

Section 28/ 28A: "A certificate of analysis" that show corresponding continuity information as to "delivery receipt." Note this also covers Section 19C requirement)

(End of chunk)

Tactical note on breath analysis law provisions.

Liability under s 8(1). If suspicion (arguably lower threshold than belief -- authority somewhere) someone is liable to breath analysis. Officer can choose to check with road side breath test to confirm alcohol -- but if negative reading then liability dissipates (s. 8(8)).

Section 11 -- after breath analysis have to hand defendant and read a form 2

Presumptions

23(2) -- presumbed result of breath analysis ios accurate at time of testing unless proved otherwise on balance of probabilities.

23(4) breath level in def was -- at anytime within 6 hours after "relevant time" (i.e. when police found him under s 2A(2)(a) as specific time of driving

(8)  For the purposes of this section, ***relevant act of driving***, in relation to a person against whom proceedings to which this Division applies are brought, means the act of driving alleged to be an ingredient of the crime or offence with which the person is charged in those proceedings.

### Chunk 6: Presumptions (Ausburn)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Ausburn v Tasmania Police [2009] TASSC 21 (6 April 2009)] [CITATION: Presumptions - Ausburn v Tasmania Police] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: s23(4) statutory presumption and proof burden] [CONCEPTS: s23(4), deemed concentration, evidential burden, Peterson v Mitchell, presumption, drink driving]

Tactical note and quotation regarding statutory presumptions under s23(4).

The learned magistrate accepted that the applicant had consumed whisky and beer after he finished driving, but was not satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the concentration of alcohol in his breath at the time of his driving was .05 or less.

14 No doubt the primary purpose of s23(4) is to facilitate proof of drink driving offences. Proof of a motorist\'s alcohol concentration as at the time of the taking of a breath or blood sample is often insufficient to prove the commission of an offence. Without s23(4) or a provision like it, difficulties could often arise in proving that a driver\'s alcohol concentration exceeded the limit at the time of his or her driving. It would often be necessary to adduce evidence as to what the motorist drank and when, as well as expert evidence based on the result of a breath or blood analysis. By casting a burden on the defendant to prove innocence, rather than just proving a lower alcohol concentration at the time of driving, s23(4) relieved magistrates of the burden of making detailed findings as to alcohol concentrations in cases where defendants were unable to establish their innocence.

15 There have been cases about s23(4) in this Court before but, as far as I know, none of them has raised any question as to whether an offender might be deemed to have a particular alcohol concentration for one purpose but not another. In *Peterson v Mitchell* [1972 Tas SR 202], the Full Court held that s23(4) does not just create an evidential burden, but imposes a burden upon a defendant to prove on the balance of probabilities that his or her alcohol concentration was not excessive.

16 The words of s23(4) are absolutely unambiguous. The alcohol concentration, as determined by a breath or blood test, "shall be deemed to have been the concentration of alcohol \... at the time of the relevant act of driving"

(End of chunk)

---

### Chunk 7: Drink driving sentencing submissions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Drink driving sentencing submissions - McDonald v Nilsson and factors] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Sentencing considerations and factors for drink driving matters] [CONCEPTS: sentencing, priors, McDonald v Nilsson, manner and reasons for driving, passengers, accident, distance, location, intent]

Tactical checklist for sentencing submissions in drink driving matters.

7 priors means prison! ***McDonald v Nilsson [2009] TASSC 83***

For lower range offences, it is possible that a Court will accept that a person was not aware that they were over the prescribed limit. At the high range, it could rarely, if ever, be suggested that the person lacked the appreciation that he or she would run the risk of reaching a level of intoxication at which it is a criminal offence to drive a motor vehicle, at some point of time before the decision was made to get behind the wheel of motor vehicle

The Manner and Reasons for Driving •

Whether there were any passengers.

• Whether the matter resulted in a motor vehicle accident. • The distance and period over which your client was driving.

• Where your client was driving.

• Why your client drove while over the prescribed range.

• Whether your client made other arrangements in relation to driving the vehicle, and what happened to those plans.

• What occurred before the offence took place. How your client came to be apprehended?

For offences involving drugs or alcohol, you must have specific instructions in relation to consumption and use by your client before driving. Police will usually ask a person who has breath-tested positively about the quantity of alcohol consumed and the period of time over which it was consumed. You should make sure that you are aware of what (if anything) your client said to police at the time of being apprehended. This will be noted in the police facts that are given to your client

(End of chunk)

### Chunk 8: Special circumstances (Barratt v Thorpe and authorities)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Barratt v Thorpe [2020] TASMC 15] [CITATION: Special circumstances - Barratt v Thorpe and authorities] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Principles for "special circumstances" in disqualification sentencing] [CONCEPTS: special circumstances, s17(5), Strickland v Whitehead, Bottomley v McDonald, Davies v Kennedy, extraordinary, atypical]

Tactical annotation on what amounts to "special circumstances" for sentencing.

Injuries sustained as a result of an accident occurring with alcohol in system are not "special" as they are unfortunately common among drink drivers.

Barratt v Thorpe [2020] TASMC 15 (14 May 2020)

The Applicable Law

23. In *Strickland v Whitehead* [1995 TASSC 125] Zeeman J discussed the principles to be applied when considering whether particular circumstances amounted to special circumstances within the meaning of [section 17(5)] of the Act. At [6] his Honour said:

"The principles to be applied in considering whether particular circumstances may amount to special circumstances within the meaning of the Act, [s16(5)] have been considered in a number of cases (Gela v Cochrane; Johnston v Davies; Dellar v Crawford; Maynard v White; Davies v Kennedy). From those cases I derive the following principles.

1 It is not possible to enumerate exhaustively circumstances which may constitute special circumstances.

2 The circumstances put forward as constituting special circumstances ought to be viewed collectively for the purpose of determining whether special circumstances exist and it is not appropriate to separately consider each matter put forward and determine whether it amounts to a special circumstance and, if not, to thereafter disregard it in the reasoning process.

3 Circumstances amounting to special circumstances may concern matters connected with the commission of the offence and matters concerning the offender.

4 Generally speaking, circumstances which may be regarded as being common to a large number of breaches of the Act or of persons who commit them do not constitute special circumstances".

24. In *Bottomley v McDonald* [2002 TASSC 27] Blow J, as he then was, said the following with respect to the phrase "special circumstances" at [6]:

"The expression \"special circumstances\" in [s17(5)] covers circumstances both of the offence and the offender. For there to be special circumstances, there must be something extraordinary, unusual or atypical. ... Special circumstances must be clearly distinguishable from the general run of cases that Parliament had in mind when it provided for the penalty of disqualification..."

(End of chunk)

### Chunk 9: Section 6(3A) Defence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Richardson, Roger v Pullen, Darren Arthur [1998] TASSC 167 (23 December 1998)] [CITATION: s6(3A) Defence - Richardson v Pullen] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: s6(3A) administrative error defence and onus] [CONCEPTS: s6(3A), administrative error, onus, license, Richardson v Pullen]

Tactical note on s6(3A) defence.

Onus on defendant to satisfy the "admin" error.

Richardson, Roger v Pullen, Darren Arthur [1998] TASSC 167 (23 December 1998)

*For the purposes of a defence pursuant to s6(3A), it was necessary for the learned magistrate to consider any relevant evidence and decide whether the respondent had satisfied the court that but for an unintentional failure to comply with an administrative requirement, he would have been licensed to drive. The learned magistrate did not do so. In announcing his reasons for decision, he did not expressly refer to the fact that the onus of proving this defence was on the respondent, or deal with the evidence in order to establish whether the respondent had satisfied him of the ingredients of the defence. I am unable to discern why the learned magistrate found that this defence was established.*

(End of chunk)

### Chunk 13: Drink Pattern defence (Payne v Crawford; formulas)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Payne v Crawford (A/54 of 1992) ; WHITE v FAI - BC9600703 115 of 1994, B3/1996] [CITATION: Drink Pattern defence - Payne v Crawford and White v FAI] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Drink pattern defence, evidentiary requirements, formulas and authorities] [CONCEPTS: drink pattern, Payne v Crawford, White v FAI, BAC calculations, elimination rate, expert evidence]

Tactical annotation on the drink pattern defence and evidentiary requirements.

DRINK PATTERN DEFENCE.

It is generally accepted in the wake of ***Payne v Crawford***, that in order for a defendant to establish a 'drink pattern' defence sufficient to displace the statutory presumption (S.23), he or she must replicate the pattern of drink consumption in accordance with the established time scale (pattern) and submit to analysis, conducted by an accredited expert.

Most 'drink pattern' defences fail due to the defendant\'s in ability to satisfy the court as to the numbe.r ôf drinks consumed vis-â-vis ihe time scale. lt is usual in such cases for the number of drinks to be understated and the court tends to look for corroboration before accepting the word of a defendant whoses interests are best served by a lie

Facts underpinning calculations:

13oz Stubby of beer 4.5% alcohol content = 15.03grms 1 30mI nip Whiskey 37% alcohol content = 8.8 grrns

Peak alcohol blood level occurs between 30 & 120 minutes after consumption

Personal elimina\\ion rates vary between 0.01 and 0.02 grams per hour

Formula G = B.A.C. x 12.5 x T.B.W.

Where

G

=

Grams of alcohol consumed

B.A.C.

T.B.W. =

= Blood Alcohol Level

Nota! Body Weight (includes age, sex, height and weig ht)

IN PROVING A PARTICULAR 'DRINK PATTERN' READING, PERSONAL RATHER THAN

POLPULATION AVERAGE EMMINATION :.RATES MUST BE USED.

***Payne v Crawford Unreported Judgement A/54 of 1992***

There is authority for the proposition that the statistical fact that a\' particular I proposition is true of the majority of persons of itself is incapable of constituting legal proof on the I balance of probabilities that that proposition is true of any given individual. ft is to be found in the decision of the Full Court of the Supreme Court of South Australia in State Government Insurance Commission v Laube (1984) 37 SASR 31\' where King, CJ said at p33:

\" Despite the Eggleston view ([1980] Crim LR at p681). I am clearly of the

opinion that the statistical fact that a particular proposition is true of the majority of persons cannot of itself amount to legal proof on the balance of DFObabilities that the proposition is true of any 9iven individual. The fact that most people with a blood alcohol level of 0.15 per cent are incapable cf exercising effective control of a motor vehicle does not establish against any individual with that blood alcohol level that that individual is so incapable. II

(Millhouse J concurred; Prior J does not appear to have directly dealt with this point.)

I therefore conclude that even if the learned.magistrate had acceDted the evidence given by the applicant as to his consumption of intoxicating liquor at relevant times and the opinions expressed by Mr. Jones, there would not have been a sufficient basis upon which the learned magistrate could have been satisfied on the balance of probabilities that the concentration of alcohol in the blood of the applicant at the time that concentration was determined by breath analysis, was not greater than the prescribed concentration.

**WHITE v FAI - BC9600703 115 of 1994, B3/1996**

SUPREME COURT OF TASMANIA

UNDERWOOD J

For the reasons expressed by Zeeman J in Payne v Crawford A54/1992, I propose to follow the South Australian Full Court decision.

However, there is a major factual distinction to be drawn between the South Australian case and the present matter. In the former, the only evidence of impairment to driv\'e a motor vehicle was the fact that at the material time the concentration of alcohol in the blood was 0.15 per cent.

In my view there is such evidence in this case.

I accept Dr Collins\' evidence which, in essence, was that an average person\'s modalities would be impaired by a blood alcohol concentration in excess of .05 per cent. That opinion is consistent with the rationale behind the provisions of the Road Safety (Alcohol and Drugs) Act 1970. I also accept Dr Collins\' evidence that the faculties of a very significant proportion of the population wou)d be impaired by a blood alcohol concentration of .07 per cent. Finally, I also accept Dr Collins\' opinion that the faculties of only an extremely small proportion of the population would be unimpaired by a blood alcohol concentration of .1 per cent.

(End of chunk)

### Chunk 14: Traffic Act - GBH from driving; public place test
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Traffic Act - GBH summary and public place test (Schubert v Lee)] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Traffic Act: GBH summary and "open to or used by the public" test] [CONCEPTS: GBH driving, summary matter, public place, public road, Schubert v Lee, 'open to or used by the public']

Tactical note on Traffic Act and the public-use test.

32(2B) GBH from driving is a summary matter.

Public place/ public road/ open to the public for driving offences:

High Court case in *Schubert v Lee* [1946 HCA 28; (1946) 71 CLR 589] at 592 where the Court said:

*".. The words 'open to or used by the public' are apt to describe a factual condition consisting of any real use of the place by the public as the public - as distinct from use by licence of a particular person or only casual or occasional use. It may be necessary to distinguish places open to members of the public as such from places left open by the owner but obviously intended only for the use of a particular description of person, for examples visitors to his shop or other premises. Prima facie the words of the section mean streets, &c, which actually are open to or used by the public, so that there is some need for protection of the public in the use of such streets, &c."*

(End of chunk)

### Chunk 17: Requirement to get Tas licence after 6 months
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act (Tas) (referenced); Vehicle and Traffic (Driver Licensing and Vehicle Registration) Regulations 2021 (Tas) (referenced)] [SECTION: s8(1), s8(2); Regulation 49] [CITATION: Requirement to get Tas licence - s8(1) s8(2) Reg 49] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Requirement to obtain Tasmanian licence after residing period and exemption lapse] [CONCEPTS: Tas licence requirement, s8(1), s8(2), Regulation 49, exemption lapse, residency 3 months, interstate licence]

Tactical note on requirement to obtain Tasmanian licence.

Section 8(1) of the Vehicle and Traffic Act states there is a requirement to hold a tas licence

Section 8(2) of the Vehicle and Traffic Act says that they are exempt from sub (1) if they have a licence from elsewhere.

Recourse then to Regulation 49 of the *Vehicle and Traffic (Driver Licensing and Vehicle Registration) Regulations 2021*. That says a person CEASES to be exempt under section 8(2) when the person has resided in Tasmania for a continuous period of 3 months

(End of chunk)

### Chunk 18: Requirement to get Tas Registration after 3 months
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act (Tas) (referenced); Vehicle and Traffic (Driver Licensing and Vehicle Registration) Regulations 2021 (Tas) (referenced)] [SECTION: s28; Regulation 129] [CITATION: Requirement to get Tas vehicle registration - s28 Reg 129] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Registration exemption lapse after 3 months and residency/address exceptions] [CONCEPTS: vehicle registration, s28, Regulation 129, registration exemption, residency, residential address]

Tactical note on vehicle registration requirements.

Section 28 of the Vehicle and Traffic Act states there is an exception from registration when registered under another law.

Regulation 129 of *Vehicle and Traffic (Driver Licensing and Vehicle Registration) Regulations 2021* says that the exemption lapses once in Tas for 3 months, unless they have a residential address or ordinarily reside interstate (meaning if they travel here on an extended basis they don't need to register unless the move here)

(End of chunk)

### Chunk 20: Unregistered (no plate)

# Speeding

<!-- block_035 procedure -->

### Chunk 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012] [SECTION: regs 6, 8, 9, 10] [CITATION: Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012 - EIO of officer checklist] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: EIO checklist for officer in speeding matters and device compliance] [CONCEPTS: speeding checklist, radar/laser device, device testing, device sealing, officer training, serial number, Traffic Act s51, tender certificate]

Tactical workflow for evidentiary issues the officer should cover in examination in chief.

EIO of officer:

 1.  Identification of the driver

 2.  The speed zone -- was it a built up area or were there speed signs?

 3.  Do the sped signs comply with the legislation is size, and how displayed

 **See section 51 of the Traffic Act -- averment in a complaint is evidence.**

 4.  Was the device used approved?  (see regulation 6 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)

 5.  Was the device tested within the last 12 months? (see regulation 8 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)

 6.  Has it been repaired, altered or adjusted? (see regulation 8 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)

 7.  Was the device sealed so its speed computing circuitry can not be interfered with? (see regulation 8 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)

 8.  Do we have proof of the device having been tested in the last 12 months?  (see regulation 9 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)  Tender the regulation 10 certificate.

 9.  Was the police officer suitably trained and or experience in the use of the device? See Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSC 40

 10.  Make sure the police officer gives evidence of the serial number of the device that matches the serial number/s on the certificate.

### Chunk 3
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Traffic Act 1925 (Tas)] [SECTION: 51] [CITATION: Traffic Act 1925 s 51 - Facilitation of proof of certain matters] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Section 51 facilitation of proof / averment in complaint as evidence] [CONCEPTS: Traffic Act s51, averment in complaint, proof of age, proof of traffic signs, prima facie evidence, complaint averment, statutory facilitation, prosecution evidence]

This chunk covers the text of Traffic Act 1925 s 51 on facilitation of proof of certain matters.

Speeding -- Proof of certain matters

Traffic Act 1925

51.   Facilitation of proof of certain matters

(1)  In any proceeding under this Act --

(l) an averment in a complaint that on a specified day a specified person was --

(i) of, over or under a specified age; or

(ii) had or had not attained a specified age --

is evidence of the matter so averred; and

(m) an averment in a complaint that on a specified day a specified traffic sign or other device for the control of traffic applied to a specified length of road or area, or to another specified place, is evidence of the matter so averred; and

(n) the production of a certificate purporting to be signed by the Commissioner or Deputy-Commissioner of Police, or by an Assistant Commissioner of Police, and certifying that on or by a specified day a specified person had failed to report to any police officer a specified collision between 2 or more vehicles, or another specified traffic accident or incident, is evidence that that person failed to make such a report on or by that day.

### Chunk 4
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Allen v Broome [2003] TASSC 38] [CITATION: Allen v Broome [2003] TASSC 38 - Speedometer accuracy presumption] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Common law presumption of accuracy of scientific instruments / speedometer] [CONCEPTS: speedometer accuracy, presumption, scientific instruments, prima facie evidence, Allen v Broome, burden of proof]

This chunk covers the common law presumption about accuracy of scientific instruments and speedometers.

Speedometer Accuracy

Allen v Broome [2003] TASSC 38

Evidence - Burden of proof, presumptions, and weight and sufficiency of evidence - Presumptions - As to scientific instruments - Accuracy of speedometer.

"There is a common law presumption as to the accuracy of readings of scientific or technical instruments of a character which, by general experience, are known to be trustworthy. For a considerable time in a number of jurisdictions it has been accepted that a speedometer is such an instrument and that evidence of a speedometer reading is prima facie evidence of that fact without the need for evidence that the accuracy of the speedometer has been tested."

### Chunk 5
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Road Rules 2009 (Tas)] [SECTION: Regulations 23, 24 and 24 / Road Rules 2019 Rules 21-24 referenced] [CITATION: Prosecution points 2017 - Radar and Laser - initial list] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Prosecution points of proof for radar and laser devices (initial checklist)] [CONCEPTS: identification of driver, speed zone, sign compliance, device approval, device testing, device sealing, serial number, officer training, tender certificate]

This chunk covers the prosecution's points of proof for radar and laser devices (initial version).

Speeding -- Prosecution points of proof for Radar and Laser devices

1. Identification of the driver

2. The speed zone -- was it a built up area or were there speed signs?

3. Do the sped signs comply with the legislation in size (see Regulations 23, 24 and 24 of the Road Rules Regulations 2009) How were they displayed, were they clearly visible? Describe the signs.

4. Was the device used the approved type supplied by the DPFEM? (see regulation 6 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)

5. Was the device tested within the last 12 months? (see regulation 8 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)

6. Has it been repaired, altered or adjusted? (see regulation 8 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)

7. Was the device sealed so its speed computing circuitry can not be interfered with? (see regulation 8 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012)

8. Do we have proof of the device having been tested in the last 12 months? (see regulation 9 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2012) Tender the regulation 10 certificate.

9. Was the police officer gives evidence of the serial number of the device that matches the serial number/s on the certificate.

10. Was the police officer suitably trained and/or experienced in the use of the device? See Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSC 40

### Chunk 6
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSC 40] [CITATION: Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSC 40 - Proof of radar/laser devices and Wigmore propositions] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Wigmore propositions for scientific instruments and court acceptance of radar devices] [CONCEPTS: Wigmore propositions, radar devices, expert evidence, judicial notice, Leonard v Newell, Visser v Miller, device condition, operator qualification]

This chunk covers proof of use of radar and laser devices and the three Wigmore propositions as applied in Tasmanian authority.

Proving the use of Radar and laser devices

Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSC 40

17. Proof of a speed limit offence by use of a radar device is evidence of a fact in issue given by a scientific or measuring instrument. A series of Tasmanian cases has decided that evidence of the result of the operation of a speed analysing device is to be considered in accordance with the three principles advanced in Professor Wigmore's work, The Science of Judicial Proof, 3rd ed, at 220 and following: Leonard v Newell [1983] TASRp 11; [1983] Tas R 78 per Green CJ, referring to Leonard v Weatherburn A65/1981; Philpott v Boon [1968] TASStRp 12; [1968] Tas SR 97; Visser v Miller [1986] TASRp 12; [1986] Tas R 103. The three propositions are:

"A The type of apparatus purporting to be constructed on scientific principles must be accepted as dependable for the proposed purpose by the profession concerned in that branch of science or its related art. This can be evidenced by qualified expert testimony; or, if notorious, it will be judicially noticed by the judge without evidence.

B The particular apparatus used by the witness must be one constructed according to an accepted type and must be in good condition for accurate work. This may be evidenced by a qualified expert.

C The witness using the apparatus as the source of his testimony must be one qualified for its use by training and experience (§220)."

18. There are some mechanical or scientific instruments which are in such common use and which by general experience are known to be trustworthy such as to make their results acceptable in courts as substantially accurate without endorsement by expert evidence. However, a radar speed analyser has not, to date, been accepted as such a device: Leonard v Weatherburn at 9, Leonard v Newell at 82 and Visser v Miller at 109. In Leonard v Newell Green CJ added that it would "not be open to a court to take judicial notice of the nature of the device, how it works, or how it should be used". However, proof of the first two of the Wigmore propositions is facilitated by legislation.

### Chunk 7
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSSC 40] [CITATION: Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSSC 40 - Obligation to show reading to driver] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Whether police must show speed display to driver / admissibility of officer evidence of display] [CONCEPTS: duty to show reading, display evidence, magistrate credibility, witness observation, printout, photograph, admissibility]

This chunk covers whether police must show the measured speed display to the driver and admissibility of oral evidence of the display.

Obligation to show the driver the reading on the radar or laser

Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSSC 40, at para 12 and 13

12. There was evidence from Constable Adams in one case, and Sergeant Flude in the other, that the radar speed detection device was operated, and that the device displayed the resulting speed measurement. The applicant contends that the evidence of the police about what they did and saw should not have been accepted by the magistrate, and that the magistrate could only have been satisfied of the speed of the applicant's vehicle if there had been something like a "print out" or photograph of the speed shown on the radar detection device. In the case of the 29 December 2014 offence, the applicant placed emphasis on the evidence that he asked Constable Adams to show him the instrument display on the device, but the display no longer appeared because of something Constable McKenna did, and no evidence was adduced from Constable McKenna.

13. There is no merit in any of these grounds. Each of the learned magistrate's decisions must be considered separately. However, in each case, the applicant has not established that the decision to accept the evidence of the prosecution witness about the result displayed by the radar speed analyser is not one to which the magistrate, as a reasonable man, should have come. [The law imposed no obligation on either police officer to show the applicant the speed display on the device.]{.underline} Both gave evidence of what the display showed and thus the speed the device measured. The learned magistrate was able to hear and observe the prosecution witnesses, and in one hearing, the evidence of the applicant. He enjoyed an advantage in the assessment of the credibility and reliability of each witness I do not share. My review of all of the evidence leads me to conclude that it was reasonably open to the magistrate to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt of the truth of the evidence given by each witness about the result of the speed measurement. He was entitled to accept the evidence of the police witnesses.

, have you much experience using a radar speed analyzer

### Chunk 10
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2022; Road Rules 2019 (Tas)] [SECTION: regs 6, 8(1)(a)-(c), 9, 11; Road Rules 2019 Rules 21-24] [CITATION: Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2022 - Refined 2022 prosecution points] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Refined/provable prosecution points for radar/laser devices (2022 update)] [CONCEPTS: radar/laser, device testing 12 months, regulation 9 proof, accredited testing, NATA, serial number, training certificate, Road Rules 2019 signs]

This chunk covers the refined updated 2022 prosecution points of proof for radar and laser devices.

Refined updated 2022 questions:

Speeding -- Prosecution points of proof for Radar and Laser devices

1. Identification of the driver

2. The speed zone -- was it a built up area or were there speed signs?

3. Do the speed signs comply with the legislation in size (see Rules 21, 22, 23, and 24 of the Road Rules 2019)? How were they displayed, were they clearly visible? Describe the signs.

4. Was the device used a radar speed analyser, or a laser speed analyser? (see regulation 6 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2022)

5. Was the device tested within the last 12 months? - see regulation 8(1)(a) of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2022

6. Has it been repaired, altered or adjusted? - see regulation 8(1)(b) of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2022

7. Was the device sealed so its speed computing circuitry can not be interfered with? - see regulation 8(1)(c) of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2022

8. Do we have proof of the device having been tested in the last 12 months? - see regulation 9 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2022

9. Was the testing completed by an employee of
 - a school/uni etc of a registered higher education provider etc., or
 - an organisation accredited by the NATA, Australia
 - an organisation approved by the commissioner
 - see regulation 9 of the Vehicle and Traffic (Offence Detection Device) Regulations 2022

10. Tender the regulation 11 certificate.

11. When the police officer gives evidence of the serial number of the device it must match the serial number/s on the device certificate. The witness can not give the device ID number, it must be the serial number.

12. Was the police officer suitably trained and/or experienced in the use of the device? See Wheaton v Wilson [2017] TASSC 40

13. Tender the witness' training certificate/letter.
