### Chunk 14
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)] [SECTION: 13 and related sections] [CITATION: McDonald v Sherrin [1998] TASSC 126 - Disorderly conduct and public nuisance authorities] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: Disorderly conduct section 13 approach; objective test; authorities] [CONCEPTS: disorderly conduct, public decorum, objective test, Watson v Trenerry, Kruger v Humphreys, Samuels v Hall, McDonald v Sherrin, Samuels, Barrington v Austin, Melser v The Police, Reeve v Cornish]

This chunk covers the approach to evidence and legal tests for disorderly conduct/public nuisance under the Police Offences Act, with quoted authorities.

Disorderly / public nuisance etc

Section 13 etc POA

McDonald, Scott Raymond v Sherrin, Robyn Lynette [1998] TASSC 126 (14 October 1998)

When dealing with a charge involving disorderly conduct, the correct approach to the evidence is as stated in Watson v Trenerry, an unreported decision of the Supreme Court of the Northern Territory dated 3 October 1997 where Martin CJ said:

"It is for the tribunal of fact to say whether matters established before the tribunal or any of them in fact constitute disorderly behaviour (per Zelling J in Samuels v Hall [1969] SASR 296 at p309, and Mitchell J pointed out in Ellis v Fingleton [(1972) 3 SASR 437] at 440 that 'Whether conduct is disorderly ... is to be decided by an objective test.' It is a matter of judgment for the court drawing upon 'its own experience and knowledge of life' per Bray CJ in Romeyko v Samuels [(1972) 2 SASR 529] at p564."

Kim Hibble v Jody William Phegan, Kim Hibble v Dean Mark Jenkins David John Brooks v Leigh Andrew Phegan, Kim Hibble v Kim Maree Ogden, David John Brooks v Andrew David Hulse Files [1997] TASSC 52 (20 May 1997)

60... "In order to sustain a charge under the Act, s13(1)(c) it is sufficient to show that the conduct complained of, according to the time, place and circumstances, offends against public decorum and is calculated to disturb, alarm or annoy (Kruger v Humphreys [1968] SASR 75)"

McDonald, Scott Raymond v Sherrin, Robyn Lynette [1998] TASSC 126 (14 October 1998)

The word "disorderly" is not defined by the Act.

In Hebble v Phegan 50/1997 at 13, Zeeman J said of a charge of engaging in disorderly conduct in a public place in breach of the Act, s13(1)(c) that in order to sustain the charge "it is sufficient to show that the conduct complained of, according to the time, place and circumstances, offends against public decorum and is calculated to disturb, alarm or annoy (Kruger v Humphries [1968] SASR 75)."

In Barrington v Austin & Ors [1939] SASR 130, Napier J at 132 said that disorderly behaviour refers to "any substantial breach of decorum which tends to disturb the peace or to interfere with the comfort of other people who may be in, or in the vicinity of, the street or public place". In Melser & Ors v The Police [1967] NZCA 5; [1967] NZLR 437, the judges of the Court of Appeal all expressed views to the effect that conduct which is the subject of a complaint of disorderly conduct must do more than seriously offend against recognised values of orderly conduct and must be likely to cause a disturbance or to annoy others. North P at 443, Turner J at 444 and McCarthy J at 446.

In Reeve v Cornish 3/1973, Crawford J relied on Barrington v Austin & Ors (supra) and Melser & Ors v The Police (supra) when considering what amounted to disorderly conduct for the purposes of the Act, s10(1)(a). The passage quoted above from the decision of Napier J in Barrington v Austin & Ors (supra) has been approved by the Full Court in South Australia and favourably cited in nearly all of the authorities I have read on disorderly conduct.

It is clear from the authorities I have read that the limits of disorderly behaviour cannot be sharply defined. Subject to that qualification, I proceed on the basis that to obtain a conviction, the applicant had to satisfy the learned magistrate that the conduct complained of, viewed in the light of the surrounding circumstances, was a substantial breach of public decorum which was likely to disturb, alarm or annoy others.

### Chunk 15
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Lusted v Van der Meer [2012] TASMC 8] [CITATION: Lusted v Van der Meer [2012] TASMC 8 - Nuisance / public nuisance requirements] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Public nuisance requirement — materially affecting a class of Her Majesty's subjects] [CONCEPTS: public nuisance, materially affect public, Richter v Risby, obstruction of highway, reasonable comfort, nuisance elements, Lusted v Van der Meer]

This chunk covers the legal test for public nuisance as explained in Lusted v Van der Meer.

Nuisance: 

Lusted v Van der Meer [2012] TASMC 8 (23 February 2012)

[25] To commit a nuisance contrary to this provision the conduct must amount to a public nuisance. To do so it must materially affect "Her Majesty' s subjects generally or at least a class of Her Majesty's subjects"; see Richter v Risby and Others [1987] TASSC 4; (1987) Tas R 36 per Cox J at 30-34. A public nuisance is an act or omission which materially affects the reasonable comfort and convenience of the life of a class of the public. Obstruction of a public highway may amount to such a nuisance, depending upon the degree and length of time and reasonableness of the obstruction. Wallace v Powell and Ors [2000] NSWSC 406. It is not necessary to have evidence of annoyance, inconvenience or discomfort to particular persons before there can be a conviction; Smith v Cornish [1971] Tas SR 383 (NC 17).

<!-- block_036 master -->

# Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)

## Authority - Kemp v Shultz — abandoned vehicle issues [REVIEW]
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Kemp v Shultz] [TOPIC: abandoned vehicle; motor vehicle stealing] [CONCEPTS: abandonment, abandoned vehicle, motor vehicle stealing, consent, subjective knowledge, Police Offences Act 1935 s 37B, factual inquiry, ownerless property, constructive possession]

Kemp v Shultz concerns the legal treatment of vehicles believed to be abandoned in the context of motor vehicle stealing under Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37B. The source cites Kemp v Shultz in relation to whether a vehicle thought to be abandoned negates the element that the accused knew consent had not been given. The precise citation details and formal holding are not provided in the source material and therefore the case summary below is provisional and flagged for verification. [REVIEW]

Where a vehicle is genuinely abandoned, courts commonly treat that factual state as relevant to whether the accused subjectively knew the owner had not consented to use. Abandonment may support a defence (or negate an element of the offence) if the accused reasonably believed no owner existed to give or withhold consent. Determination of abandonment is fact‑sensitive: the court will consider indicia such as duration of disuse, visible signs of neglect, registration and ownership markers, location, whether the vehicle was locked or unsecured, and any communications or conduct of a putative owner. Even when a vehicle appears abandoned, the prosecution may still succeed if it proves the accused actually knew consent was absent. Practitioners should treat Kemp v Shultz as a local authority to be checked for exact ratio and application; until full citation and text are obtained the case should be used cautiously and validated against reported law on abandonment and s 37B.

### Dangerous Article — Police Offences Act 1935 s15C and s3 (inclusive definitions)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935] [SECTION: s15C; s3] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 s15C & s3 - Dangerous Article Definition] [CATEGORY: practitioner working document] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Definition of "dangerous article" under POA] [CONCEPTS: dangerous article, weapon, knife, adapted article, Police Offences Act]
Tactical coverage: Police Offences Act 1935 - Section 15C -- Dangerous Article and Section 3 interpretation.
**Police Offences Act 1935 - Section 15C -- Dangerous Article**

**Police Offences Act -- Section 3 -- Interpretation**

*dangerous article* includes --

(a) any weapon other than a firearm; and

(b) any article adapted or modified so as to be capable of being used as such a weapon; and

(c) any other article that is intended to be used as such a weapon; and

(d) a knife

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Possession - Momcilocvic v The Queen 2011 HCA 34 - Note] [CATEGORY: practitioner working document] [TYPE: annotation / case authority] [TOPIC: Meaning of "possession" in POA context] [CONCEPTS: possession, physical custody, control, Momcilocvic, HCA]

### Requisite belief — police officer belief standard (Tasmanian v Hodges 2022 TASSC)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Police belief standard - Tasmanian v Hodges 2022 TASSC - Note] [CATEGORY: practitioner working document] [TYPE: annotation / case authority] [TOPIC: Officer belief must be objectively reasonable] [CONCEPTS: belief, objectively reasonable, Tasmanian v Hodges, suspicion vs knowledge, officer grounds]
Covers: Requisite belief required by police officers for POA dangerous article offences.
#### Requisite belief:

Police officer must believe that the defendant is in possession or carries and dangerous article HELD, in the circumstances, to be based on objectively reasonable grounds.

See ***Tasmanian v Hodges 2022 TASSC***. Belief is something more than mere suspicion but less than actual knowledge based on proof. Ther must be grounds for their belief but certainty and proof are not required.

### 'Dangerous' and 'Dual Purpose' — Craig v Lowrey [2012] TASMC 41 (paras 12-15)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Craig v Lowrey [2012] TASMC 41] [CITATION: Craig v Lowrey 2012 TASMC 41 - Dual Purpose and "dangerous"] [CATEGORY: practitioner working document] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Dual purpose items and whether they are weapons/dangerous articles] [CONCEPTS: dual purpose, machete, weapon, Craig v Lowrey, offensive weapons]
Covers: Case discussion on "dangerous" and items with dual purposes (machete example).
***Craig v Lowrey* \[2012\] TASMC 41**

12\. The Oxford Dictionary defines "weapon" as "a thing designed for use for inflicting bodily harm or physical damage".

13\. Turning firstly to the three inclusive definitions set out in section 3, it is probably the case that I would find that the machete is a weapon, despite its dual purpose. Its purpose as a weapon worldwide is well known. Terrible injuries and death have flowed from machete attacks. The blade is known to be long, strong and sharp.

14\. However authorities have held that where there is a **dual purpose**, prosecution is required to prove the intended use as a weapon. See *Chadbourne v Ansell *[\[1975\] WAR 104](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1975%5d%20WAR%20104), *Threfall v Panzera* [\[1958\] VicRp 87](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/vic/VicRp/1958/87.html); [\[1958\] VR 547](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1958%5d%20VR%20547), *Coleman v Zanker* [\[1991\] SASC 3121](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/sa/SASC/1991/3121.html); [(1992) 58 SASR 7.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281992%29%2058%20SASR%207)However these cases deal with different statutory provisions relating to "offensive weapons". This appears to be a higher test than required for part (b) of the definition in this state's Act, where an item is modified so that it is only *capable* of being a weapon. This interpretation would also seem to defeat the work of the "lawful excuse" provision. I do not need to decide this point. The definition is an inclusive one, and therefore the overriding requirement is that the court must be satisfied that it is "a dangerous article" independently of whether or not it falls into the category of a "weapon". This would at face value cover a wider array of items.

15\. The Macquarie dictionary defines "dangerous" as "full of danger or risk; causing danger; perilous; hazardous; unsafe". The Oxford dictionary defines dangerous as "able or likely to cause harm or injury".

---

### Dangerous Article (Lawful Excuse) — Craig v Lowrey [2012] TASMC 41; Taikato v R [1996] HCA 28; Holmes v Hatton (18 SASR 412)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Craig v Lowrey [2012] TASMC 41; Taikato v R [1996] HCA 28; Holmes v Hatton 18 SASR 412] [CITATION: Dangerous Article - Lawful Excuse - Craig v Lowrey & Holmes v Hatton] [CATEGORY: practitioner working document] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: Lawful excuse principles for carrying dangerous articles] [CONCEPTS: lawful excuse, Taikato, Holmes v Hatton, dual purpose, machete, lawful purpose]
Covers: How "lawful excuse" is assessed for dangerous article offences (Craig v Lowrey and supporting authorities).
***Craig v Lowrey* \[2012\] TASMC 41**

20\. The overall effect of the defendant's explanations was that he kept the machete for recreational or farming purposes unrelated to attack or self-defence.

21\. In *Taikato v R* [\[1996\] HCA 28](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/cth/HCA/1996/28.html) the majority of the High Court in relation to a similar charge discussed the meaning of "lawful purpose". They held that as a general rule interpreting "lawful purpose" in a legislative provision to mean a purpose that is not forbidden, rather than positively authorised by law, is usually preferred. This is because statutes are interpreted in accordance with the presumption that parliament does not take away existing rights, unless it does so expressly or by necessary implication. In this matter it was not argued by prosecution that using the machete for the purposes of a tool, as the defendant describes, was in itself unlawful. It is not prohibited by law and I find that such use would be a lawful activity.

22\. Two decisions of the South Australian Supreme Court assist with the principles to be applied in proving "lawful excuse" in the context of this type of charge. I will set out relevant passages from those decisions.

23\. In *Holmes v Hatton *[18 SASR 412](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=18%20SASR%20412) Wells J stated at p418:

It seems to me that the expression "without lawful excuse" obliges a Court to examine all the circumstances that characterize the purpose and reasons for which the weapon is carried, and to determine, as a matter of fact and degree whether, reasonably appraised, those purposes and reasons constitute lawful excuse or not.

His Honour further stated as p419:

> *Where an offensive weapon is carried knowingly, lawful excuse or its absence is ordinarily to be judged by a consideration if the purpose or purposes, or the reason or reasons, for which the weapon is being carried. (If it should be -- and I leave this point for consideration when it directly arises -- that a man may "carry" a weapon within the meaning of the section even where he is unaware that he is carrying it -- for example, where he is helping to carry another's luggage which, unknown to him contains a gun -- absence of purpose or reason and an inadvertent carrying may amount to lawful excuse).*
>
> *The purpose or reasons for which an alleged offender was carrying a weapon are to be examined and assessed having regard to the united force of all the circumstances put together. If the sole purpose of reason for which the weapon is carried is plainly unlawful or is other-wise contrary to the section, the carrier cannot claim lawful excuse.*
>
> *Where the sole purpose or reason is lawful, there will be lawful excuse. Where there is a duality of purpose or reason, no hard and fast rule can provide a solution. The court, in determining the issue, must, in my opinion, decide a question of fact and degree, and must, in particular, ask whether that which, ex facie and taken in isolation, may be regarded as an unlawful purpose or reason, was, in the circumstances, so transient, so remotely related to the defendant's conduct, so speculative, so unlikely to become translated into action, so subsidiary -- in short, so unimportant or insignificant, as to leave the alleged offender with lawful excuse.*
>
> *The foregoing discussion, it will be observed, has omitted any reference to onus of proof. The application of the provision as to onus of proof should cause little difficulty in practice, but occasional cases, in which dual purpose is under examination, may arise where it will be necessary to keep steadily in mind what the defendant is obliged by [s. 15](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ea200180/s15.html) to prove.*
>
> *Plainly, given that all other elements are proved, if only a single purpose or reason is in issue, and if the defendant proves on the balance of probabilities primary facts that, in law, amount to lawful excuse, he will be acquitted. If proof falls short of that, he must be convicted. If the evidence as a whole raises a question of dual purpose or reason, the matter will, to my mind, stand thus. Where the evidence as a whole includes evidence suggesting an unlawful purpose or reason for carrying as well as of a lawful purpose or reason, in order to acquit the defendant the court must find itself persuaded that, having regard to the cogency with which the relevant facts and circumstance are proved by the evidence as a whole, the unlawful purpose or reason was so unimportant and insignificant that there probably was lawful excuse.*

---

No unprocessed items were listed in the prior COVERAGE REPORT. There are therefore no additional chunks to format.

# Property and Possession Offences

<!-- block_037 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Abandonment (practitioner commentary)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: abandonment and theft; mistake of fact] [CONCEPTS: abandonment, theft, mistake of fact, R v White, Ellerman's Wilson Line, dishonest, property]

Tactical note: covers legal observations on when abandoned property cannot be stolen and consequences of belief property abandoned.

Property that has been abandoned can not be stolen (see *post ss* 18 -- 24). If property has not been abandoned, but the defendant believes that it has, under the old law he could not be convicted of theft, whether his belief was reasonable or unreasonable : see *R v White* (1912) 7 Cr App. R 266; *Ellerman's Wilson Line Ltd. V Webster* \[1952\] 1 Ll.L.R. 179, D.C. It is submitted that this is still the law. Whether or not a mistake of fact has to be reasonable (see ss 17-4 *et seq.*), there would be no evidence that the defendant was acting dishonestly.

---

### Chunk 2 — Must take reasonable steps to find owner (Maingay v Charles)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Maingay v Charles [2009] TASSC 98] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: finder's belief that owner can be discovered by reasonable means] [CONCEPTS: finder, abandoned property, reasonable means, s226, Maingay v Charles, discover owner, belief]

Tactical workflow: Must take reasonable steps to find owner — extracts from Maingay v Charles and commentary.

"The respondent's stated belief was in effect that he did not believe he could discover the owner by any reasonable means. While there is no stated requirement in s226 that the belief itself be a reasonable one, it would make a nonsense of the legislation were an alleged offender to simply be able to state that he did not hold the requisite belief, notwithstanding that that stated belief might be entirely inconsistent with the surrounding circumstances, and for that statement to be determinative. Of course, the belief referred to in s226 is one that an owner can be discovered by \"reasonable means\". (see below)"

Maingay also sites Alexander Gordon MacDonald \[1983\] 8 A Crim R 248

(The defendant found foreign currency and kept it, and made no attempt to locate an owner and claimed he believed it was abandoned)

---

### Chunk 3 — Alexander Gordon MacDonald (quoted passage)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Alexander Gordon MacDonald [1983] 8 A Crim R 248] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: inference of finder's belief from conduct; larceny by finding] [CONCEPTS: larceny by finding, inference, finder's belief, locate owner, Russell on Crime, evidence]

Tactical note: Quoted authorities on how to infer finder's state of mind where the finder volunteers no direct evidence.

In Alexander Gordon MacDonald \[1983\] 8 A Crim R 248, the Court of Criminal Appeal of New South Wales considered how the state of mind of a finder of property is to be established when the finder has volunteered no information pointing to their state of mind at the relevant time. The Court (Lee, Maxwell and Yeldham JJ) observed (at p251):

\"The guilty mind or animus furandi requisite in larceny by finding is thus the belief of the finder that the owner can be found, and unless that belief is established beyond reasonable doubt, a conviction for larceny is not open. The Crown of course carries the onus or proving that belief beyond reasonable doubt, and there is no onus on the accused in any circumstances to prove that he did not have such a belief or that he believed the goods had been abandoned, or that the owner could not be found (May v O'Sullivan \[1955\] HCA 38; (1955) 92 C.L.R. 654). How then is the requisite belief in the finder to be established when the finder has volunteered no information pointing to the state of his mind at the relevant time, as is the case here? In Russell on Crime

(12 th edition) the learned author, at p1014, states:

\"In cases of taking on finding some of the strongest circumstances to rebut the implication that such taking was felonious \...are those which show that \[the taker\] endeavoured to discover the true owner and kept the goods till it might reasonably be supposed that the true owner could not be found. The finders belief, in our view, is to be inferred from the facts and circumstances surrounding the finding and the taking of the goods, and in this respect regard may be had not only to what the finder does in relation to the goods but also what he does not do that might reasonably be regarded as consistent with the actions of an honest man finding goods. Did the finder examine it closely to see if it gave any clue to its owner by name tag or otherwise? What avenues were reasonably open to the finder to locate the owner? A person finding goods may be taken to know that the person who has lost those goods may well retrace his steps with a view to recovering them, and so the leaving of the finders name and address with someone at the place of discovery may be one means of locating the

owner. It is common knowledge in our community, and the finder would know, that the police will receive lost property handed in and take care of it and accordingly the finder would think that the loser might make an enquiry of the police in the area where he believes he may have lost his property. It is common knowledge that newspapers publish ,,lost and found columns. These and perhaps other considerations would ordinarily be present to the mind of an honest person finding property in a suburb of Sydney. Each case nmust be looked at according to its own facts, and the place where the property is found and the nature of the property will, in most instances, readily indicate the avenues that are reasonably open to

find the owner.\"

---

### Chunk 4 — Criminal Code Act 1924 s 226 (Interpretation)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924] [SECTION: 226] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 s 226 - Interpretation] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: definition of stealing and 'takes' including finding] [CONCEPTS: stealing, takes, finds, interpretation, s226, intent to permanently deprive]

Concept anchor: reproduces s226 text on stealing and interpretation, including finding.

**Criminal Code Act 1924**

**PART VI - Crimes Relating to Property Chapter XXIV - Stealing Interpretation**

**226. Interpretation**

**(1)** A person who, without the consent of the owner thereof, dishonestly --

**(a)** takes; or

**(b)** being lawfully in the possession thereof, either as a servant of the owner or as a bailee or part owner thereof, converts to his own use or to the use of any person other than the owner --

anything capable of being stolen, with intent permanently to deprive the owner thereof, steals such thing.

**(2)** In this chapter --

**(a)** the term ***takes*** includes obtaining possession --

**(i)** by any trick;

**(ii)** by intimidation;

**(iii)** through a mistake on the part of the owner, if the taker knows of such mistake; or

[**(iv)** by finding, if at the time of the finding the taker believes that the owner can be discovered by reasonable means;]{.mark}

**(b)** the term ***owner*** includes any part owner or person having possession or control of, or a special property in, anything capable of being stolen.

**(3)** A servant who takes anything out of the possession of his master with intent to apply it to his master's use, and who so applies it, or who converts anything belonging to his master to his master's use, does not steal the thing, although such taking or conversion is wrongful.

---

### Chunk 5 — Two mental elements: Dishonestly - s226(1) (annotation)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: dishonesty test under s226(1); Feely/Ghosh transition] [CONCEPTS: dishonesty, Feely test, Ghosh, Peters, Macleod, Jovanovic, jury directions]

Concept anchor: outlines approach to instructing jury on dishonesty under s226(1).

- The test is both objective and subjective and it is now clear that courts will apply the *Feely* test without the *Ghosh* gloss. This was accepted by the High Court in *Peters* (1998) 192 CLR 493 and *Macleod* (2003) ALJR 1047. In determining dishonesty it is necessary to: 

  

- Identify the knowledge, belief or intent which is said to render the relevant act dishonest; and 

  

- Instruct the jury to decide whether the accused had that knowledge, belief or intent and, if so, to determine whether, on that account, the act was dishonest; and  

  

- Direct the jury that, in determining whether the conduct of D was dishonest, the standard is that of ordinary and decent people? 

  

  

- There is no additional requirement that D realised he or she was dishonest: *Jovanovic.*

---

### Chunk 6 — Intent to permanently deprive (annotation)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: intent to permanently deprive; cases] [CONCEPTS: permanently deprive, dominion, Lowe v Hooke, Holloway, intent]

Concept anchor: notes what constitutes intent to permanently deprive.

- An intent to assume dominion over the goods in such a manner as to deny (take dominion over) *Lowe v Hooke.* and not merely entrench upon the rights of the owner *Holloway* (using but not permanently depriving)

### Chunk 7 — No limitation period on charges for stealing (Moore v Howe)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Moore v Howe [2017] TASMC 10] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: limitation period for stealing charges] [CONCEPTS: limitation period, stealing, indictable offence, s234, Moore v Howe, Justices Act s72C]

### Chunk 8 — Notice of demand (practitioner notes)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: driver demand notice; basis to believe; owner/registered operator; evidence] [CONCEPTS: notice of demand, driver demand, owner, registered operator, basis of belief, Subremanium rule, original evidence, POA s37A]

Concept anchor: practical notes on serving a notice of demand in vehicle-related offences.

Need to have basis to believe occupant of car committed an offence

Can be served on owner or registered operator

"Owner" defined in section 37A of POA. It includes anyone police officer reasonably believes was the driver.

Don't need to prove actual ownership

Can run it on cop alone if they can give evidence of basis of belief (reps made by actual owner not hearsay as per Subremanium rule -- original evidence)

### 37GA POLICE OFFENCES ACT 1935 - SECT 37GA

A police officer may serve a notice of demand on the owner or registered operator

### Chunk 10 — Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 s 3 (registered operator definition)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999] [SECTION: 3] [CITATION: Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999 s 3 - registered operator definition] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: definition of registered operator] [CONCEPTS: registered operator, vehicle registration, register of motor vehicles, interpretation, VTA s3]

Concept anchor: defines "registered operator" for vehicle notices.

***Vehicle and Traffic Act 1999***

***3.   Interpretation***

***registered operator*** of a motor vehicle or a trailer means --

\(a\) the person recorded in the register of motor vehicles and trailers as the person responsible for it; or

\(b\) if the registration of a motor vehicle or a trailer has expired, or been cancelled, the person last recorded as the registered operator unless that person has notified the Registrar that he or she is no longer responsible for the motor vehicle or trailer;

### Chunk 11 — Baker v Smith [2025] TASSC 43 (difference between s37G and s37GA)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Baker v Smith [2025] TASSC 43] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: requirements for driver demand notice; allegation vs satisfaction; ownership proof; right to silence] [CONCEPTS: driver demand, s37G, s37GA, allegation vs belief, registered owner, right to silence, Baker v Smith]

Concept anchor: full case summary on difference between s37G and s37GA and validity of driver demand.

***Baker v Smith* \[2025\] TASSC 43**

**Facts**

- Baker's vehicle crashed (Wynyard, 18 Mar 2023). Driver left scene.

- Police served Baker (registered owner) with a **driver demand notice** (s 37GA *Police Offences Act 1935*), requiring details of driver/occupant.

- Baker did not comply.

- Magistrate convicted him (Aug 2024). Baker sought review.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Issues**

1.  Does s 37G require only an **allegation** of an offence, or officer's **satisfaction** of it?

2.  Was there sufficient proof Baker was the **registered owner**?

3.  Was the notice **invalid/ambiguous** (re: offence details; "driver or occupant" wording)?

4.  Did the officer lack **reasonable belief** an offence occurred?

5.  Did issuing the notice unlawfully abrogate Baker's **right to silence/self-incrimination** (given officer was "99.99% sure" Baker was driver)?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Decision**

- **Appeal dismissed** -- conviction upheld.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Ratio**

- **s 37G precondition:** only requires an **allegation**, not proof or officer's satisfaction.

- **Separate offences:** s 37G (demand) and s 37GA (notice) each create distinct offences with different penalties.

- **Ownership proof:** admissions and conduct can suffice; certificate of registration not essential.

- **Notice validity:**

  - "Indicate" = general statement of offence, not exact section.

  - Context showed allegation was against **driver**, not occupant → no ambiguity.

- **Reasonable belief:** Magistrate applied wrong test (belief vs allegation), but no miscarriage of justice.

- **Right to silence:** s 37G clearly abrogates it; no implied "necessity" limit.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Significance**

- Confirms **low threshold** for valid driver demand: mere allegation is enough.

- Clarifies ownership can be proved by **conduct/admissions**.

- Confirms strict but clear statutory abrogation of silence/self-incrimination rights.

- On review, findings will stand if **reasonably open**.

**Case:** *Baker v Smith* \[2025\] TASSC 43

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Issue**                                       **Applicant's Argument**                                                                                                 **Court's Reasoning / Outcome**
  ----------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **1. Preconditions (s 37G vs s 37GA)**          Proof required that officer was *satisfied* an offence occurred (s 37GA(1)), not just an allegation.                     Wrong -- s 37G only requires an *allegation* of an offence. Satisfaction standard in s 37GA not imported. Conviction can rest on allegation alone.

  **2. Ownership**                                No proper evidence Baker was registered owner. Body worn footage not enough; should have had registration certificate.   Ownership can be proved by admissions and context. Baker said "yes" when told he was the owner, nodded during reading of notice. Certificate not essential. Finding was reasonably open.

  **3. Validity / Ambiguity of Notice**           Notice invalid: (a) didn't specify exact offence provision (only Act); (b) ambiguous re: "driver or occupant".           "Indicate" (s 37GA(2)(a)) only requires general statement, not exact section. Offence described (driving under influence). Notice + officer's words made clear it related to *driver*. No ambiguity.

  **4. Reasonable Belief**                        Officer admitted he didn't know who driver was, or if under influence → no reasonable basis to issue notice.             Court: only an *allegation* is required. Even if higher "belief" test applied, magistrate's conclusion (based on CCTV, witness info, leaving scene) was open. No miscarriage.

  **5. Rights to Silence / Self-Incrimination**   Notice invalid because officer said he was "99.99% sure" Baker was driver → unnecessary, undermined rights.              Statute expressly abrogates rights in these circumstances. No "necessity" limit implied. Officer's statement = poor wording, but does not invalidate notice.

  **Overall**                                     Conviction unsafe; notice invalid; ownership not proved.                                                                 Conviction stands. Notice valid. Magistrate's findings reasonably open.
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

---

### Chunk 12 — Police Offences Amendment Bill 2014 (clause notes)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Police Offences Amendment Bill 2014 clause notes link] [CONCEPTS: Police Offences Amendment Bill, clause notes, second reading, explanatory material, 16 of 2014]

Concept anchor: links and note of clause notes/explanatory material for the 2014 amendment.

<!-- block_038 master -->

# Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 39 — "Reasonably believed" test
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 39] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory definition / evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: reasonable belief for possession or control of stolen property] [CONCEPTS: reasonable belief, subjective assessment, police belief, possession of stolen property, exclusion of alternatives, state of mind, evidentiary threshold, arrest precondition]

The phrase "reasonably believed" in Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 39 requires that a police officer hold a belief about the relevant facts that is sufficient to justify acting under the provision. This belief is assessed subjectively (it must be the officer's belief) but is constrained by the statutory requirement of reasonableness. The officer must have facts and circumstances that would reasonably induce the belief in a person in the officer's position; the objective circumstances supply the foundation for the subjective assent. The evidentiary threshold for belief is lower than proof on the balance of probabilities—an inclination of mind to assent to a proposition may suffice—but the officer must be able to point to cogent circumstances that exclude other propositions consistent with innocence. This conception prevents arbitrary exercises of police powers and requires some inquiry or objective basis before arrest or seizure where the statute mandates "reasonable belief".

# Authority - Lane v Jones [2012] TASMC 11: "acceptance to the exclusion of other propositions"
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lane v Jones [2012] TASMC 11] [TOPIC: degree of belief required under s 39 POA] [CONCEPTS: acceptance to exclusion of alternatives, belief vs suspicion, police belief, s 39, objective basis, exclusion of propositions, evidentiary standard]

Lane v Jones [2012] TASMC 11 establishes that, for the purposes of Police Offences Act s 39, an officer's belief must "amount to acceptance — to the exclusion of other propositions" that property was stolen or unlawfully obtained. Deputy Chief Magistrate Daly analysed the statutory phrase and concluded that mere suspicion is insufficient; the officer must have reached a state of assent which excludes reasonable alternative explanations. This case is authority for treating the requisite belief as more than a tentative suspicion and for requiring the officer's belief to have explanatory force against competing propositions consistent with innocence.

# Authority - Police v Lea [2008] TASMC 1: interpretation of "reasonably believes" and reliance on George v Rockett
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Police v Lea [2008] TASMC 1] [TOPIC: "reasonable grounds" and degree of suspicion in s 39 POA] [CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds, belief, suspicion, George v Rockett, Parker v Wood, officer inquiry, statutory protection against caprice]

Police v Lea [2008] TASMC 1 discusses Police Offences Act s 39 and relies on prior authorities to explain the statutory meaning of "reasonably believes". The decision cites Parker v Wood and adopts the George v Rockett formulation that when a statute prescribes "reasonable grounds" it requires objective facts sufficient to induce the state of mind in a reasonable person. Police v Lea emphasises that objective circumstances need to point clearly to the subject matter of belief, even though the evidence required for belief is less than proof. The judgment also considers factors that may reasonably incite suspicion — nature of the property, circumstances of discovery, and the defendant's behaviour — and treats the statutory requirement as a safeguard against capricious charging.

# Authority - Parker v Wood [1993] TASSC 26: "reasonable grounds" for state of mind
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Parker v Wood [1993] TASSC 26] [TOPIC: statutory "reasonable grounds" test] [CONCEPTS: reasonable belief, objective facts, inducement of state of mind, statutory interpretation, protection against arbitrary enforcement]

Parker v Wood [1993] TASSC 26 is cited for the proposition that where a statute requires "reasonable grounds" for a state of mind (suspicion or belief), there must be objective facts sufficient to induce that state of mind in a reasonable person. The case underwrites the objective limb which supports a subjective belief of an officer: the officer's belief must be grounded in facts which, objectively considered, could reasonably produce that belief. The decision is used as part of the legal infrastructure for assessing police conduct under provisions requiring "reasonable belief".

# Authority - George v Rockett [1990] HCA 26: meaning of "reasonable grounds"
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: George v Rockett [1990] HCA 26] [TOPIC: objective circumstances required for reasonable grounds] [CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds, objective test, belief vs proof, assent of belief, statutory "reasonable grounds", evidentiary threshold]

George v Rockett [1990] HCA 26 establishes the High Court's authoritative test for "reasonable grounds" where a statute prescribes such a requirement for a state of mind. The Court explained that objective circumstances sufficient to show a reason to believe must point clearly to the subject matter of belief, though they need not establish the proposition on balance of probabilities. Belief is described as an inclination of the mind to assent to a proposition; the grounds for that assent can be slender compared to proof, but they must be facts capable of inducing belief in a reasonable person. George v Rockett underpins Tasmanian decisions applying "reasonable grounds" language.

# Authority - Winwood v Burk [2002] TASSC 54 and O'Sullivan v Tregaskis [1948] SASR 12: factors inciting suspicion
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Winwood v Burk [2002] TASSC 54; O'Sullivan v Tregaskis [1948] SASR 12] [TOPIC: indicia and degree of suspicion] [CONCEPTS: suspicion, abnormality/incongruity, nature of property, circumstances of discovery, defendant's behaviour, indicia for police]

Winwood v Burk [2002] TASSC 54 (citing O'Sullivan v Tregaskis [1948] SASR 12) explains that suspicion may be incited by an impression of abnormality or incongruity — for example, the nature of the property, circumstances in which it is found, and the behaviour of the person in possession. O'Sullivan listed these non-exhaustive factors as relevant when deciding whether objective circumstances exist to attract reasonable suspicion. These authorities help define practical indicia that may form part of the objective facts underpinning a reasonable belief under Police Offences Act s 39.

# Authority - Burnett v Brown (1929) 24 Tas LR 23: meaning of "suspicion" historically
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Burnett v Brown (1929) 24 Tas LR 23] [TOPIC: historical meaning of suspicion] [CONCEPTS: suspicion, doubt, mistrust, degree of inclination, cogency of evidence, statutory antecedent]

Burnett v Brown (1929) 24 Tas LR 23 is a historical Tasmanian authority observing that the words "suspicion" and "suspect" connote doubt and mistrust; the inclination to believe may vary in cogency according to the evidence. The case demonstrates that suspicion inherently involves uncertainty, and that the strength of the inclination to believe will depend on the quality of supporting circumstances. This helps contextualise modern readings of "suspicion" and "reasonable suspicion" in Tasmanian statutory regimes.

# Authority - Cotton v Ramm [1976] 16 SASR 107: enquiry required before relying on another officer's information
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Cotton v Ramm [1976] 16 SASR 107] [TOPIC: reliance on second-hand information by arresting officer] [CONCEPTS: reliance on radio-room, enquiry, reasonable grounds, corroboration, officer's personal belief, duty to inquire]

Cotton v Ramm [1976] 16 SASR 107 holds that where an officer receives information from another officer about conduct (for example, driving manner), the arresting officer should, before forming the requisite belief, make reasonable enquiries into relevant circumstances if the initial information is not itself sufficient to justify the statutory action. The case supports the proposition that uncritical acceptance of bare allegations from a radio-room or another officer may be inadequate to establish 'reasonable belief' unless the information itself supplies the necessary factual detail.

# Authority - Hibble v Phegan: personal exercise of arrest power and requirement to form belief on one's own facts
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Hibble v Phegan (Tasmanian decision cited)] [TOPIC: personal exercise of police power and independent belief] [CONCEPTS: personal exercise of power, arresting officer's knowledge, direction from senior officer, lawful authority, personal inquiry, arrest legality]

Hibble v Phegan (Tasmanian decision as cited) affirms that a police officer's power of arrest must be exercised personally and on the facts known to that officer; a mere direction from a senior officer or reliance on another's conclusion does not in itself provide lawful authority for an arrest. The principle emphasises the requirement that the arresting officer form the requisite belief based on the officer's own knowledge and enquiry rather than uncritically adopting another's assertion.

# Authority - Rolls v Richardson [2014] TASMC 12: refusal to consent not sufficient; need for reasonable investigation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Rolls v Richardson [2014] TASMC 12] [TOPIC: insufficiency of refusal to consent and radio-room information] [CONCEPTS: refusal to consent, walking away, rights consistent with innocence, uncritical acceptance, radio-room, reasonable investigation, exclusions of propositions]

Rolls v Richardson [2014] TASMC 12 holds that a defendant's refusal to consent to a search or to engage with police is conduct consistent with innocence and cannot alone found the reasonable grounds necessary for arrest. The case criticised an officer's uncritical acceptance of a bare allegation from a radio room as insufficient to induce belief because such a statement may be based on unfounded suspicion. The judgment recognises that while prompt action may sometimes be justified, an arresting officer must nonetheless have sufficient information to exclude alternative propositions consistent with innocence; otherwise objective reasonable grounds are lacking.

# Trespass — implied licence to approach a residence and police entry limits
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: trespass, implied licence, revocation, police entry] [CONCEPTS: trespass, implied licence, knock and talk, revocable licence, lawful entry, consent, occupier's authority, police limits, private premises]

A trespass occurs when a person enters private premises without the consent of the owner or occupier; this rule applies to police officers. The law implies, in ordinary residential contexts, a licence to approach a dwelling, walk to the door, and knock or speak to the person inside (the "knock and talk" licence). That implied licence is revocable at will by the occupier. Police entry into private premises requires either the occupier's consent, some other lawful authority (warrant, statutory power), or circumstances that negate the need for consent. Without such lawful basis, police who enter become trespassers and commit an unlawful interference with property and liberty.

# Authority - Roy v O'Neill [2020] HCA 45: implied licence and its revocability
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Roy v O'Neill [2020] HCA 45] [TOPIC: implied licence to approach a dwelling] [CONCEPTS: implied licence, knock and talk, revocable licence, trespass, occupier's consent, limits on entry]

Roy v O'Neill [2020] HCA 45 confirms that the law implies a licence to approach residential premises for certain ordinary interactions (such as approaching a door to knock and speak), and that this implied licence can be revoked by the occupier at any time. The High Court's discussion frames the limits of lawful approach and the point at which further presence becomes trespass without express authority or other legal justification.

# Authority - Tasmania v Hodges [2022] TASSC 45: police entry to commercial/residential apartments and consent
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Hodges [2022] TASSC 45] [TOPIC: police entry to apartments, implied licence, consent and premises nature] [CONCEPTS: apartment entry, licence to occupy, occupier consent, police authority, implied licence limitations, management consent, commercial premises]

Tasmania v Hodges [2022] TASSC 45 examined police authority to enter an apartment in circumstances involving tenancy terms and competing rights between owners and occupants. The decision emphasises that even where a licence between occupier and property manager may have ended, the police authority to be on and enter the premises depends on the consent of the lawful occupier or an express lawful basis; no general implied licence exists to permit police entry into an apartment without such consent. The ruling illustrates complexity where premises have mixed commercial/residential features and where entry authority depends on property law relations as well as police powers.

# Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 267 — Injuries to property (intent to defraud; wilful; claim of right)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 267] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 267] [TYPE: statutory provision / offence element] [TOPIC: injuries to property; intent to defraud; wilfulness and claim of right] [CONCEPTS: intent to defraud, injury to property, owner causing injury, partial interest, wilfully, claim of right, property offences, mens rea]

Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 267 sets out principles for injuries to property: (1) an injury to property caused with intent to defraud is unlawful even if caused by the owner; (2) partial interest in property does not justify causing injury; and (3) an act causing injury to property does not constitute a crime under the chapter unless done wilfully and without claim of right. The section distinguishes unlawful acts done to defraud, clarifies that partial ownership does not license injurious conduct, and requires wilfulness and absence of claim of right as elements for criminal liability under the Code for property injuries.

# Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 273 — Unlawful injuries in general
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 273] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 273] [TYPE: statutory provision / offence definition] [TOPIC: unlawful injuries to property; Code offence] [CONCEPTS: unlawfully destroy, injure property, criminal offence, charge, mens rea, Code property offence]

Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 273 creates the Code offence: any person who unlawfully destroys or injures any property is guilty of a crime. The statutory charge is "Unlawfully injuring property." This section operates alongside other Code provisions (such as s 267) that define forms of unlawfulness, mens rea requirements such as wilfulness, and defences like claim of right.

# Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37 — Summary offences for damaging property
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 37] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37] [TYPE: statutory provision / offence definition] [TOPIC: summary offence of unlawfully destroying or injuring property] [CONCEPTS: unlawfully destroy, injure property, summary conviction, penalty units, animal cruelty, removal of public markers, council works interference]

Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37 provides summary offences for unlawfully destroying or injuring property and unlawfully and maliciously killing, maiming, or wounding animals the property of another. Subsection (2A) prescribes penalties (up to 10 penalty units or imprisonment up to 12 months). The section also contains specific offences for interference with public markers and council works with separate lower penalties. The Police Offences Act offence is a like or parallel offence to Code offences; procedural and penalty distinctions (summary procedure, limitation periods) apply.

# "Unlawful" and "wilfully" in property offences — Hodgson [1985] Tas R 75
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Hodgson [1985] Tas R 75] [TOPIC: meaning of "wilfully" in property crimes] [CONCEPTS: wilfully, intent, subjective recklessness, common law meaning, mens rea, arson analogy, property offences]

Hodgson [1985] Tas R 75 discussed the meaning of "wilfully" in the context of property offences. Cox J observed that "wilfully" can denote either a specific intent (a purpose to cause the consequence, e.g., to set fire) or subjective recklessness (an awareness of a substantial risk and proceeding anyway). The case illustrates that courts may treat wilfulness as embracing either intentional conduct or conduct where the actor foresees and disregards a known risk, depending on statutory context and common law interpretation.

# Jointly owned property and liability for injuring property — Police Offences Act s 37; Criminal Code s 267; s 54-55 (married persons)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas); Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: Police Offences Act 1935 s 37; Criminal Code Act 1924 ss 267, 54, 55] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas) s 37; Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 267, 54, 55] [TYPE: legal doctrine / statutory interaction] [TOPIC: liability for damage to jointly owned property] [CONCEPTS: joint ownership, unlawfully injure, partial interest, marital immunity, claim of right, destruction of jointly-owned property, family violence contexts]

A person who injures or destroys property unlawfully may be criminally liable even where the property is jointly owned. Police Offences Act s 37 and Criminal Code s 267 operate such that partial interest in property does not justify injury (Criminal Code s 267(2)). Thus joint ownership is generally not a defence to summary or Code charges for damaging property. There are, however, specific Code provisions concerning married persons: Criminal Code s 54 provides that a married person is generally not convicted for offences alleged to have been committed during cohabitation with respect to the property of the other spouse, with exceptions (e.g., leaving/deserting or intent to defraud third parties); s 55 states married persons otherwise incur the same criminal responsibility as if unmarried. Authorities such as Howell v Dakin have applied these provisions to reject a defence based solely on joint ownership. Comparative material (e.g., NTB v DDB, New Jersey) highlights different statutory wording elsewhere but is not binding in Tasmania.

# Authority - Howell v Dakin (1964) TSR 142: joint owner not a defence under s 37 POA
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Howell v Dakin TSR 142 (LCA 139/1964) (as cited)] [TOPIC: joint ownership and liability under POA s 37] [CONCEPTS: joint owner, statutory interpretation, unlawful destruction, s 37, no defence, animals exception]

Howell v Dakin (as cited in the source) held that being a joint owner does not provide a defence to an offence under Police Offences Act s 37, which prohibits unlawful destruction or injury of "any property." The decision reasons that the statutory text plainly covers jointly owned property and that an argument of inconvenience cannot override the express language of the statute.

# Comparative authority - NTB v DDB (A-4542-13T2) (New Jersey Appellate Division, 9 September 2015) — co-tenant liability (REVIEW)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: NTB v DDB (A-4542-13T2) 9/9/15 (New Jersey Superior Court, Appellate Division)] [TOPIC: co-tenant separate property interests (comparative)] [CONCEPTS: joint tenancy, tenancy by entirety, co-tenant liability, property of another, comparative law, criminal mischief] [REVIEW]

NTB v DDB (New Jersey Superior Court Appellate Division, 9 September 2015) is comparative material concerning whether a co-tenant's destructive act can constitute criminal mischief as damage to the property "of another." The Appellate Division held that co-tenants hold separate and distinct interests and that one co-tenant's damaging act could affect the property interest of the other spouse. This US decision is persuasive comparative analysis but does not control Tasmanian law; it is included for consideration in family-violence contexts where joint ownership issues arise. Marked [REVIEW] because it is foreign jurisdiction material and may require contextual caution.

# Assault — statutory forms and elements (general rule)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: statutory definition / offence elements] [TOPIC: assault forms and elements] [CONCEPTS: assault, applying force, attempt, threatening gesture, present ability, deprivation of liberty, mens rea, reasonable grounds for belief]

Assault may take one of several forms: (a) intentionally applying force to another person directly or indirectly; (b) attempting to apply force; (c) threatening by any gesture to apply force where the actor either intends to threaten or realises the threat may be perceived, and at the time of the gesture either (i) had the present ability to apply force, or (ii) caused the other person to believe, on reasonable grounds, that the actor had the present ability to apply force; and (d) an act that deprives another of his or her liberty. Each form requires the relevant mental element (intent to apply force or intent/realisation in the case of threats) and the requisite physical act or conduct that causes the other to reasonably apprehend force or results in deprivation of liberty.

# Defences negating unlawfulness — self-defence, punishment of child, reasonable force in arrest
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) ss 26, 46, 50] [TYPE: defence] [TOPIC: defences that negate unlawfulness] [CONCEPTS: self-defence, lawful arrest, reasonable force, punishment of child, justification, necessity, claim of right, statutory defence]

Statutory defences can negate the unlawfulness element of offences such as assault or injury to property. Key Code defences include: self-defence under Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 46 (force used in defence of self or others, subject to proportionality and reasonableness), punishment of one's child for correction under s 50 (narrow statutory allowance, with limits), and the use of reasonable force in executing a lawful arrest under s 26 (justification for force used in arrests lawfully made). Where a defendant successfully establishes a lawful justification under these sections, criminal liability for conduct that would otherwise be unlawful may be avoided.

<!-- block_038 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Reasonably Believed (Lane v Jones)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935] [SECTION: s 39] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 s39 - Lane v Jones - Reasonably Believed] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Meaning of "reasonably believed" and adoption of Lane v Jones test] [CONCEPTS: reasonably believed, subjective belief, s39, Lane v Jones, police arrest, exclusion of other propositions]

Covers: Reasonably Believed test as analysed in Lane v Jones and application to Sergeant Orr.

Reasonably Believed

evidence needs to amount to acceptance by the officer - to the exclusion of other propositions - that the property was stolen or unlawfully obtained at para \[23\] **Lane v Jones \[2012\]**

\-

This question is to be assessed subjectively. I note that the requisite state of mind is something more than mere suspicion.

* Lane v Jones [\[2012\] TASMC 11](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2012/11.html),* Deputy Chief Magistrate Daly analysed the meaning of this term as it appears in ![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} [s 39](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s39.html) ![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}, [Police Offences Act](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/). He concluded that a belief must amount \"to acceptance -- to the exclusion of other propositions --\" that the relevant state of affairs exists. With respect, I agree with His Honour\'s analysis and adopt that test in assessing whether Sergeant Orr held the requisite belief.

### Chunk 2 — Police v Lea [2008] TASMC 1 (Reasonable belief test)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935] [SECTION: s 39] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 s39 - Police v Lea [2008] TASMC 1 - Reasonable belief test] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Authorities on "reasonably believes" and objective facts required] [CONCEPTS: Police v Lea, reasonable grounds, George v Rockett, Parker v Wood, suspicion, s39]

Covers: Police v Lea analysis of "reasonably believes" including reliance on Parker v Wood and George v Rockett.

***Police v Lea \[2008\] TASMC 1 (29 January 2008)***

*8. The Act, s.39(1) provides that a person must not have possession or control of any property that is reasonably believed by a police officer to have been stolen or unlawfully obtained. The Supreme Court has not dealt with any case charged under the amended provision, which came into force on 17 December 2001.*

*9. In Parker v Wood [\[1993\] TASSC 26](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1993/26.html), Slicer J dealt with the test applicable to the words \"reasonably believes\" in the Road Safety Alcohol & Drugs Act 1970, s.8. Slicer J stated in relation to that particular requirement, that a the reason that a constable must hold a reasonable belief is to prevent random and capricious exercises of police powers. The Second Reading Speech is of no assistance whatsoever. However, Slicer J's comments are apposite here in relation to police capriciously or unreasonably charging persons under this provision. Slicer J held that the test in relation to the phrase \"reasonably supposes\" is that in George v Rockett [\[1990\] HCA 26](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/cth/HCA/1990/26.html); [(1990) 170 CLR 104](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281990%29%20170%20CLR%20104?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39) at 112:*

*\"When a statute prescribes that there must be \'reasonable grounds\' for a state of mind - including suspicion and belief - it requires the existence of facts which are sufficient to induce that state of mind in a reasonable person.\"*

*and at p116:*

*\"The objective circumstances sufficient to show a reason to believe something need to point more clearly to the subject matter of the belief, but that is not to say that the objective circumstances must establish on the balance of probabilities that the subject matter in fact occurred or exists: the assent of belief is given on more slender evidence than proof. Belief is an inclination of the mind towards assenting to, rather than rejecting, a proposition and the grounds which can reasonably induce that inclination of the mind may, depending on the circumstances, leave something to surmise or conjecture.\"*

*10. In relation to the degree of suspicion that might be required, in Winwood v Burk [\[2002\] TASSC 54](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2002/54.html), Underwood J, as he then was, said:*

*In O\'Sullivan v Tregaskis [\[1948\] SASR 12](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1948%5d%20SASR%2012?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39), Napier CJ saidat 16:*

*\"It is manifestly impossible to enumerate or define all the circumstances that may reasonably attract suspicion to the property, but, for this purpose, regard may be had to the nature of the property, to the circumstances in which it has been found, and to the behaviour of the defendant, with respect to it. (Ex parte Patmoy [(1944) 44 SR (NSW) 351](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281944%29%2044%20SR%20%28NSW%29%20351?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39) at p 357; LePoidevin v Hudson [\[1935\] SASR 223](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1935%5d%20SASR%20223?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39), at p 230).*

*Speaking generally, it seems to me that suspicion is commonly, and not unreasonably, incited by the impression of abnormality or incongruity in the thing being in the possession of the person who has it, or in his dealings with it.\"*

*11. In Burnett v Brown [(1929) 24 Tas LR 23](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASLawRp/1929/2.html), Clark J, at 34, said with respect to the Police Act 1905, s16, an earlier Tasmanian enactment similar to s.39:*

*\"The words \'suspicion\' and \'suspect\' convey the idea of doubt and mistrust as much as belief, a doubting and mistrusting inclination to believe. The inclination may exist in various degrees according to the cogency of the evidence, but the idea of doubt and mistrust is always present.\"*

### Chunk 3 — Rolls v Richardson [2014] TASMC 12 (refusal to consent not enough)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935] [SECTION: s 39] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 s39 - Rolls v Richardson [2014] TASMC 12 - Refusal to consent not sufficient] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Limits on reliance on conduct/refusal to consent; requirement to investigate radio room info] [CONCEPTS: Rolls v Richardson, refusal to consent, reasonable grounds, radio room, Sergeant Orr, Cotton v Ramm, Hibble v Phegan]

Covers: Rolls v Richardson holding that refusal to consent to search is not sufficient grounds and officer must independently hold reasonable belief based on investigation.

CONTRA

***Rolls v Richardson \[2014\] TASMC 12 (23 May 2014)***

Basically -- refusing to consent to a search is not sufficient grounds, and the belief must be independatly held based on reasonbale investigation. A call from a radio room probs not enough

*\[21\] In my view, the conduct of the defendant after being confronted by police does not amount to reasonable grounds for the requisite belief. He was entitled to refuse to allow police to search him, and entitled to walk away from them. The exercise of such rights is as consistent with innocence as with guilt, and could never form the basis of the belief necessary to carry out an arrest. A contrary conclusion would be entirely inconsistent with the rights which the statutory precondition to the exercise of the power of arrest is intended to protect. Further, the defendant\'s presence in the Mall close to the scene of the alleged crime could not have meant anything without understanding the factual basis of what had occurred. Once again, as pointed out by defence counsel, this information was as consistent with innocence as with guilt.*

*\[22\] The real issue in this case is whether it was reasonable for Sergeant Orr to uncritically accept the bare statement of the radio room operator that a person matching the defendant\'s description had committed a theft, as a basis for the requisite belief.*

*In Cotton v Ramm [\[1976\] 16 SASR 107](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1976%5d%2016%20SASR%20107?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s39), Jacobs J dealing with a question of whether or not a police officer believed on reasonable grounds that a driver\'s ability to drive the motor vehicle was impaired, considered the question of whether the officer was entitled to rely upon information received from another officer as to the defendant\'s manner of driving. His Honour said this:*

*\"The least that the police officer concerned should do, before he forms a requisite belief, is to ascertain by enquiry as much as he can of the circumstances that might be relevant to his belief, if the information first supplied is not of itself sufficient, in terms of the statute, to justify the request for a breath test.\"*

*In Hibble v Phegan at \[52\], Zeeman J said:*

*\"A police officer\'s power of arrest has to be exercised by him or her personally, and on the facts known to him or her. The mere direction of a senior officer provides no lawful authority for the making of an arrest by the police officer to whom the direction is given. An arrest is a serious interference with personal liberty. That liberty may not be infringed except by lawful authority. The direction of a senior officer does not provide that lawful authority.\"*

*In this case, Sergeant Orr was not acting in response to a direction from a senior officer. However, she did accept uncritically what in effect amounted to a conclusion or mere allegation on the part of either the radio room operator or some other police officer, that the defendant had committed the theft. She did not inquire as to the circumstances, or the first-hand information upon which the report was based. It is instructive in this case to note that had she done so, she may have been told, that there was no evidence that a theft had taken place and that the conclusion that there had been a theft was based entirely on the unfounded suspicion of Mr Badcock.*

*In my view, the simple statement by the radio room operator that the theft had been committed was not sufficient to induce a state of belief in a reasonable person in the position of the police officer. There was no detail or explanation as to the information upon which this allegation was based. A reasonable person in Sergeant Orr\'s circumstances had no basis to exclude propositions consistent with innocence, for example that the report was based on the unfounded suspicion of another. Had Sergeant Orr been told that the defendant had been seen taking the items then she may well have had reasonable grounds to form the necessary belief. On the other hand, had she been told that the allegation arose merely from Mr Badcock\'s suspicion based on the appearance of the defendant, and his refusal to permit a search, then she would have realised that there was not a sufficient basis for an arrest. Without any explanation at all, the latter proposition could not be excluded.*

*Mr Fairley submitted that Sergeant Orr ought to have investigated the circumstances herself, by going to the shop and speaking to witnesses before carrying out the arrest. I do not believe that she was necessarily required to go to those lengths. There were good reasons for her to act quickly. Belief on reasonable grounds does not equate to proof. Further, the information available would not need to satisfy her of the guilt of the suspect beyond reasonable doubt. The assent of belief is given on more slender evidence than proof. Belief is an inclination of the mind towards assenting to, rather than rejecting, a proposition and the grounds which can reasonably induce that inclination of the mind may, depending on the circumstances, leave something to surmise or conjecture (See George v Rockett, supra). Had the radio room operator explained the circumstances upon which the allegation was based in a way which permitted a reasonable conclusion that the theft had been committed, then there may have been a sufficient basis for the arrest. However, in this case, Sergeant Orr had no information at all upon which to assess the cogency of the bald allegation, and accordingly did not have objectively reasonable grounds for her belief.*

---

### Chunk 4 — Trespass (general principle; Roy v O'Neill)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Trespass - Roy v O'Neill - implied licence and police entry] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Police entry to private premises; implied licences and revocation] [CONCEPTS: trespass, police entry, implied licence, Roy v O'Neill, revoke licence, knock and speak]

Covers: Principle that entering private premises without consent is trespass; implied licences and ability to revoke.

1.  It is well established that any person who enters on private premises, even for a lawful purpose, without the consent of the owner or occupier, is a trespasser. This applies to a police officer: see *Roy v O\'Neill* [\[2020\] HCA 45](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2020/45.html), [385 ALR 187](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=385%20ALR%20187) per Keifel CJ at \[11\]. In some circumstances, a consent or licence to enter a property is implied by law. An example is in respect of residential premises, where the law implies a licence to walk to a door, knock on it and, if the door is answered, speak to the person inside. The licence can be revoked at any time at will: *Roy v O\'Neill* per Bell and Gageler JJ at \[34-35\].

---

### Chunk 5 — Tasmania v Hodges [2022] TASSC 45 (apartment checkout / police entry)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Tasmania v Hodges [2022] TASSC 45 - police authority to enter apartment after checkout] [CASE: Tasmania v Hodges [2022] TASSC 45] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Authority of police to enter apartment; consent/licence and management's role] [CONCEPTS: Tasmania v Hodges, implied licence, police entry, apartment tenancy, consent, owner/occupier]

Covers: Hodges analysis of police authority to enter apartment and effect of tenancy checkout.

**Tasmania v Hodges \[2022\] TASSC 45 (26 July 2022)**

13. In this case, the issue is complicated by a number of factors, including the nature of the premises and the rights, inter se, of the owners of the apartment and the accused in respect of the accused\'s occupation of the apartment. The prosecutor relies upon an assertion that the accused\'s licence to occupy the apartment had ended at 10am, in accordance with the terms and conditions of his tenancy. It is submitted that, accordingly, he had no right to still be in the room and, therefore, no right to refuse entry by police. I do not accept this argument. Firstly, as already noted, it is not clear that these terms and conditions applied to the agreement between the apartment business and the accused. Further, and in any event, the argument misses the point. Even if it was the case that the accused\'s licence to be on the premises had come to an end at 10am, and that under his contract with the apartment business, he could no longer lawfully exclude its representative from the room, this in itself does not address the authority of the police to enter the room. In the absence of any other lawful basis for entry, the authority of the police to be in the apartment building, and to enter the apartment in question, depended entirely on the express consent of the owner of the apartments, represented in this case by the management, and any lawfully authorised employee. There is no basis for any form of implied licence that would permit the police to enter the apartment, in the absence of such consent.

### Chunk 7 — Criminal Code Act 1924 — s 267 Injuries to property
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924] [SECTION: s 267] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 s267 - Injuries to property (statutory text)] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory text for s 267 Injuries to property] [CONCEPTS: s267, injuries to property, intent to defraud, wilfully, claim of right, unlawful]

Covers: Full statutory text of Criminal Code s 267 as provided.

**267.**   **Injuries to property**

(1)  Every injury to property which is caused with intent to defraud is unlawful, although caused by the owner of such property.

(2)  No person is justified in causing any injury to property by the fact that he has a partial interest therein.

(3)  An act causing injury to property shall not constitute a crime under this chapter unless it is done wilfully and without claim of right.

**Criminal Code Act 1924**

Version current from 12 December 2017 to date (accessed 20 August 2018 at 11:51)

Any person who unlawfully destroys or injures any property is guilty of a crime.

### Chunk 9 — Police Offences Act 1935 — s 37 Offences relating to property (text)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935] [SECTION: s 37] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 s37 - Offences relating to property (statutory text)] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory text for s 37 Offences relating to property] [CONCEPTS: Police Offences Act s37, unlawfully destroy, injure property, penalty units, summary conviction, boundary marks, council works]

Covers: Full statutory text of Police Offences Act 1935 s 37 as provided.

**POLICE OFFENCES ACT 1935 - SECT 37 Offences relating to property**

**PART IV - Injuries to Property Offences relating to property**

(1)  A person shall not unlawfully destroy or injure any property.

(2)  A person shall not unlawfully and maliciously kill, maim, or wound any animal the property of any other person.

(2A)  A person who contravenes subsection (1) or (2) is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding 10 penalty units or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 12 months.

(3)  A person shall not unlawfully remove, displace, deface, obliterate, or conceal any --

\(a\) boundary mark;

\(b\) beacon;

\(c\) survey mark;

\(d\) mark used in setting out any work;

\(e\) milestone or kilometre post;

\(f\) sign post; or

\(g\) notice --

set up or posted by or on behalf of a public authority.

(3A)  A person who contravenes a provision of subsection (3) is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding 2 penalty units.

(4)  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  

(4A)  A person must not unlawfully damage or interfere with any work of a council or any material used or provided for that work.

(5)  A person who contravenes subsection (4A) is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding one penalty unit.

(6)  A complaint made for the purposes of the [Justices Act 1959](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/ja1959119/) in relation to an offence against subsection (2A) in relation to subsection (1) is to be made within 12 months after the date of the offence.

### Chunk 10 — "Unlawful and wilfully" (Hodgson note)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Unlawful and wilfully - Hodgson [1985] Tas R 75] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Meaning of "wilfully" in injuries to property context] [CONCEPTS: wilfully, Hodgson, intent, subjective recklessness, common law meaning]

Covers: Note on the meaning of "wilfully" in property offences, citing Hodgson.

### Unlawful and wilfully

**Willful:**

*Hodgson* \[1985\] Tas R 75.

 

Cox J said that wilfully is a word of doubtful import. At common law the word means an "intent" (i.e. to to set fire to the building - specific intent) or subjective recklessness (foreseeing the likelihood of setting fire to the building).
