### Chunk 11 — Jointly Owned Property (Police Offences Act s37 and Code interplay; cases and commentary)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 / Criminal Code Act 1924] [SECTION: Police Offences Act s37; Criminal Code s267, s273, s54, s55] [CITATION: Jointly Owned Property - POA s37 & Code s267/s54 - case notes and US authority] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Effect of joint ownership on unlawful injuring/destroying property; statutory and case authority] [CONCEPTS: jointly owned property, s37 POA, s267 Criminal Code, s54 Criminal Code, Howell v Dakin, NTB v DDB, family violence, not defence]

Covers: Notes that POA destroy/injure is like Code offence and that joint ownership is not a defence; statutory extracts and case excerpts including Howell v Dakin and NTB v DDB.

**JOINTLY OWNED PROPERTY**

**Jointly Owned Property**

**Police Offences Act 1935**

**37.**   **Offences relating to property**

(1)  A person shall not unlawfully destroy or injure any property.

(2)  A person shall not unlawfully and maliciously kill, maim, or wound any animal the property of any other person.

(2A)  A person who contravenes [subsection (1)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/whole/html/inforce/current/act-1935-044#GS37@Gs1@EN) or [(2)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/whole/html/inforce/current/act-1935-044#GS37@Gs2@EN) is guilty of an offence and is liable on summary conviction to a penalty not exceeding 10 penalty units or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 12 months.

**Criminal Code Act 1924**

**267.**   **Injuries to property**

(1)  Every injury to property which is caused with intent to defraud is unlawful, although caused by the owner of such property.

(2)  No person is justified in causing any injury to property by the fact that he has a partial interest therein.

(3)  An act causing injury to property shall not constitute a crime under this chapter unless it is done wilfully and without claim of right.

**273.**   **Unlawful injuries in general**

Any person who unlawfully destroys or injures any property is guilty of a crime.

Charge:  Unlawfully injuring property.

**NOTE** -- POA Destroy/Injure is a like offence, so the 'code' principals apply. Therefore s 267(2) apples to POA Destroy/Injure Property charges. It is not defence to be a joint owner of property.

**Jointly Owned Property**

Note CODE defence for married parties:

**[Criminal Code Act 1924](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1924-069) 54.**   **Liability of husband and wife for offences committed by either with respect to the other\'s property**

(1)  Except as provided in [subsection (2)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/whole/html/inforce/current/act-1924-069#JS1@GS54@Gs2@EN) , no married person shall be convicted of any offence alleged to have been committed by him or her during cohabitation with respect to the property of the other spouse.

(2)  Every such person as aforesaid shall be criminally responsible for any offence committed with respect to any such property when leaving or deserting or about to leave or desert the other spouse; and for any offence committed with intent to defraud any person other than such spouse.

(3)  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  

**55.**   **Liability of married persons generally**

Except as hereinbefore expressly provided, a married person incurs the same criminal responsibility in respect of his or her acts and omissions as if such person were unmarried.

Hey,

*Howell v Dakin* TSR 142 (LCA 139/1964)

"The fact that the husband was a joint owner, likewise, affords no defence. Subsection (1) of s. 37 prohibits the unlawful destruction or injury of \"any property\". That this includes property jointly owned by a defendant and another is made plain by the form of subsection (2) of s. 37 which refers specifically to offences in respect of animals \"the property of any other person\". The argument of inconvenience cannot prevail against the plain words of the statute."

**Jointly Owned Property**

NTB v DDB (A-4542-13T2) 9/9/15 (New Jersey Superior Court Appellate Division)

The original trial judge stated;

Under N.J.S.A. 2C:17-3(a)(1), a person is guilty of criminal mischief if he \"purposely or knowingly damages tangible property of ***another***\...\" The trial Judge reasoned that since \"the statute does not prohibit a person from causing damage to their ***own property***\", D.D.B. failed to show that N.T.B. committed an act against the tangible property of ***another***, thereby preluding a finding of criminal mischief.

The Appellate Court concluded that while \"each tenant by the entirety is a tenant in common with the other **during the joint lives of the spouses**\", \"each co-tenant has a **separate and distinct** freehold title and each holds his or her title and interest **independently of the others**.\" In light of this, the Appellate Division determined that N.T.B. and D.D.B. each held a separate and distinct interest in their home and therefore, N.T.B.\'s act of breaking down D.D.B\'s bedroom door did in fact destroy the \"property of another\" (due to D.D.B.\'s undivided interest in the home) and he therefore committed the predicate act of criminal mischief.

The Appellate Division opined that to conclude otherwise would \"permit a spouse to purposely and maliciously totally destroy his or her jointly owned marital home, without sanction, leaving no recourse for the innocent spouse to secure an FRO on the basis of the home\'s ruin.\"

**Jointly Owned Property -- general thoughts in FV matters**

In Tasmania unlike the statue referred to in the US case we do not need to prove that the property was \"tangible property of another\", merely that the act of injuring or destroying property was \"unlawful\".

I would argue that the injury or destruction of property is unlawful if it occurs without permission, and if you don\'t own something outright you do not have permission to do unlawful acts to it.  For example, a person would have implied permission to drive her husband's car to the shop to get some corn chips and avocado dip, but not to smash out the windows when you find out he\'s been screwing the lady over the back fence, a la Tiger Woods\' wife.

### Chunk 12 — Assault (forms)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Assault - forms and elements (practitioner note)] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Forms and definition of assault] [CONCEPTS: assault, apply force, attempt, threaten, present ability, deprive liberty]

Covers: List of forms an assault may take.

Thus, an assault may take one of the following forms:

\(a\) an act of intentionally applying force to the person of another directly or indirectly;

\(b\) attempting to apply force to the person of another;

\(c\) threatening (either with intent to threaten or with a realisation that a threat may be perceived) by any gesture to apply force to the person of another; and at the time of the gesture

i had the present ability to apply force to the other person; or

ii caused the other person to believe, on reasonable grounds, that he had the present ability to apply force; and

\(d\) an act of depriving another of his or her liberty.

### Chunk 13 — Defences (self-defence, correction, lawful arrest)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924] [SECTION: s 26, s 46, s 50] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 - Defences (s26, s46, s50) - practitioner note] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Defences precluding unlawfulness (self-defence, correction, lawful arrest)] [CONCEPTS: defences, self-defence, correction of child, lawful arrest, Code s26, s46, s50]

Covers: Enumerated defences that preclude a finding of unlawfulness in assault or property damage.

There are a number of defences which would preclude a finding of unlawfulness, including self-defence (Code s 46), punishment of one's child for the purposes of correction (Code s 50) and the use of reasonable force in executing a lawful arrest (Code s 26).

# Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) - Consent and when consent is ineffective
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 182(4)] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4)] [TYPE: statutory definition] [TOPIC: consent to assault — statutory limits] [CONCEPTS: consent ineffective, s182(4), assault defence, public interest, breach of the peace, bodily harm, otherwise unlawful, Tasmanian consent rule]
The rule in Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) provides that, even where a putative victim has given consent to the application of force, that consent is ineffective and the assault is nevertheless unlawful if each of four statutory conditions is satisfied: (1) the act is otherwise unlawful; (2) the injury is of such a nature, or is done under such conditions, as to be injurious to the public; (3) the act is injurious to the person assaulted; and (4) the act involves a breach of the peace. The statute therefore creates a composite statutory test unique to Tasmania which allows the Crown to prove unlawfulness despite consent. The prosecution bears the onus of proof in relation to all four matters; it is not sufficient to prove only one or some of the requirements. This statutory provision operates alongside other provisions that limit the effect of consent (for example, where consent is made immaterial by other sections such as prohibitions on consenting to death or consent of minors), but s 182(4) establishes a specific four-part gateway by which consensual applications of force may still constitute criminal assault.

# Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) — Onus of Proof (Woolmington principle)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 182(4)] [CITATION: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas) s 182(4); Woolmington v DPP [1935] AC 462] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: prosecution onus for negating consent] [CONCEPTS: prosecution burden, beyond reasonable doubt, Woolmington, proof of all elements, consent negated, s182(4) elements]
The rule is that the Crown bears the onus of proof to establish each of the four conditions in s 182(4) beyond reasonable doubt; it is insufficient to prove only one or some of the listed elements. This principle is grounded in Woolmington v DPP [1935] AC 462 and was applied in Tasmanian analysis: the prosecution must satisfy the jury beyond reasonable doubt that (a) the act was otherwise unlawful, (b) injurious to the public, (c) injurious to the person assaulted, and (d) involved a breach of the peace. Only if the Crown discharges that burden will the existence of the victim’s consent be rendered ineffective as a defence.

### Chunk 3 — Defence as to consent — Invalid Consent: s 182(4) four conditions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 182(4)] [CITATION: Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) - Invalid Consent overview] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: s 182(4) four conditions where consent is ineffective] [CONCEPTS: s182(4), consent ineffective, otherwise unlawful, injurious to the public, injurious to person, breach of the peace, Woolmington]

Tactical workflow covering s 182(4) and the four conditions that make consent ineffective.

Section 182(4) provides that an assault may be unlawful notwithstanding the V's consent **where four conditions are satisfied:**

**Otherwise unlawful**

The assault is otherwise unlawful: this suggests that it must be also be another offence such as disturbing the peace by fighting contrary to the POA s13(1).

**Injurious to the public**

\- Fist fights and prize fights: (AG Ref No 6/1980; Coney; Jobidon; Holmes)

Sporting contests: Pallante v Stadiums; Jewell (players are deemed to consent to force reasonably expected to happen in the course of the game and not every infringement of the

rules is an assault -- so the question is was application of force within the reasonable contemplation of the players); Emmet v Arnold (did application of force exceed implied

consent);

\-

\- Sadomasochistic practices: Brown, McIntosh, but see Wilson (Branding acceptable which was permitted)

Dangerous exhibitions and rough but well intentioned horseplay: Jones; Aitken and ors, deciding that consent in the context of rough and undisciplined horseplay is a defence

where there is no intention to cause injury.

\-

The assault is injurious to the public; this apparently means not in the public interest which is clearly a policy decision and courts distinguish between:

Consent Summary Crim Sem 2 Final Page 24

**Injurious to the person assaulted**

There must be some harm to the person. Any harm or injury calculated to interfere with health or comfort. It need not be permanent, but must be more than transient or trifling McCallum

per Burbury CJ

**Involve a breach of the peace**

The assault is a breach of the peace: this is a word which had a technical meaning at common law; fist fights in public are a traditional breach of the peace but the term is wider and

includes situations where there is actual or threatened violence likely to cause harm: Howell; Holmes; Dowell v Higgins; Nicholson v Avon

2.2.3 Whilst the putative victim's consent to the application of force generally affords a defence there are two situations in which an assault will still be unlawful notwithstanding that consent. The first is where it is expressly provided that the victim's consent is immaterial to criminal responsibility. For example, under s 51 of the Code no consent may be given to the infliction of death upon oneself nor to the infliction of an injury likely to cause death. And, per s 127, the consent of a victim under the age of 17 years is no defence to a charge of indecent assault.

2.2.4 The second situation is where the defence of consent is not precluded expressly but the legislation provides an avenue for proving unlawfulness despite the existence of consent. It is this situation with which the current enquiry is concerned. In certain circumstances, the victim's consent is ineffective as an excuse or justification for an assault. The rules which operate to deny the exculpatory effect of consent are set out in s 182(4) of the Code.

Assuming that the victim's consent to the application of force has been given freely within the meaning of s 2A of the Code, s 182(4) specifically provides that the assault is nevertheless still unlawful if each of these four conditions is satisfied: • the act is 'otherwise unlawful'; • the injury is of such a nature, or is done under such conditions as to be injurious to the public; • the act is injurious to the person assaulted; and • the act involves a breach of the peace. The Crown bears the onus of proof in relation to all four matters. It is not sufficient for the Crown to prove one or more of the elements listed above, they must prove all four (Woolmington v DPP [1935] AC 462)

---

### Chunk 4 — Otherwise unlawful (s 182(4)) — annotation and Coney linkage
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 182(4)] [CITATION: Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) - Otherwise unlawful discussion] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Meaning of "otherwise unlawful" in s 182(4); Coney linkage and examples] [CONCEPTS: otherwise unlawful, Coney, affray, armed in public, duelling, consent defence, Brown case, social goods]

Tactical commentary covering the phrase "otherwise unlawful" in s 182(4).

2.6.2 There is a degree of uncertainty about when an act will be 'otherwise unlawful' in the context of this section. The condition is unique to Tasmania but it seems likely that it can be linked to Cave J's judgement in Coney's Case. His Honour distinguished between blows struck in anger and those struck in the context of sporting contests where there is no hostile intent and held that the former would amount to both an assault and a breach of the peace and would be, on that ground, unlawful in any case.35 The implication is that, before a consensual assault can be unlawful it must constitute an assault and some other offence as well. This is the sense in which it seems the condition of 'otherwise unlawful' is generally understood in the context of s 182(4). For example, the act could be a breach of s 178 of the Code --- being armed in public; taking part in an affray, s 80; or duelling contrary to s 81. The circumstances in Brown may not constitute 'otherwise unlawful' conduct in this sense, so that, in Tasmania, consent would remain an operative defence to sadomasochistic consensual sexual conduct of the kind engaged in that case. Consent would not be rendered immaterial by s 182(4). 2.6.3 Where the putative victim consents to an application of force, one of the fundamental justifications for the criminalisation of assault no longer applies --- there is no violation of the victim's physical integrity. Arguably, the requirement that the act constitutes another offence supplies the wrongfulness that is otherwise absent.

---

### Chunk 5 — Injurious to the public (s 182(4)) — annotation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 182(4)] [CITATION: Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) - Injurious to the public discussion] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Interpretation of "injurious to the public" and examples] [CONCEPTS: injurious to the public, Coney's Case, public interest, sports exceptions, sadomasochism, social good]

Tactical commentary covering what "injurious to the public" may mean under s 182(4).

2.6.4 The second hurdle for the Crown is to establish that the assault is injurious to the public. This phrase is not defined in the Code so it may be considered a 'word of doubtful import'. According to established principles for interpreting words in legal Codes, where the meaning of a word (or phrase) is unclear it is permissible to look to the common law for a definition.36 There have been a number of situations where the common law courts have considered the meaning of the phrase 'injurious to the public'. In Coney's Case it was held to mean 'not in the public interest', and this is generally the understanding evident in succeeding cases --- both Attorney-General's Reference (No 6 of 1980) and Brown refer to the infliction of bodily harm 'for no good reason'. The more difficult question that remains is what sorts of activities are or are not in the public interest? As mentioned above, the common law and the Code treat certain activities involving the application of force to another as non-criminal on the basis that they provide some social good --- games and sports, lawful chastisement or correction of children, 37 reasonable surgical interference,38 dangerous exhibitions and 'well-intentioned horseplay'. Activities involving the infliction of bodily harm which fall outside these well-established exceptions, including consensual sadomasochistic practices and at least some forms of intrusive body ornamentation, continue to be judged as injurious to the public.

---

### Chunk 6 — Injurious to the person assaulted (s 182(4)) — annotation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 182(4)] [CITATION: Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) - Injurious to the person assaulted discussion] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Meaning of "injurious to the person assaulted" and threshold of harm] [CONCEPTS: injurious to person, bodily harm, Donovan definition, harm threshold, health or comfort]

Tactical commentary on the "injurious to the person assaulted" limb of s 182(4).

2.6.5 The origin of the requirement (again unique to Tasmania) that the assault is injurious to the person assaulted is clearly Stephen J's judgment in Coney's Case: When one person is indicted for inflicting personal injury upon another, the consent of the person who sustains the injury is no defence to the person who inflicts the injury, if the injury is of such a nature, or is inflicted under such circumstances, that its infliction is injurious to the public as well as to the person injured.39 2.6.6 Apart from definitional questions about the degree of injury required and some potential arguments about whether only physical injury is contemplated, the requirement that the assault is injurious to the person assaulted is comparatively straight forward. 2.6.7 The phrase 'injurious ... to the person assaulted' is not defined. As 'injurious' is a word of doubtful import the common law can again be used in aid, however there are no cases from any jurisdiction that have interpreted the word in the context of assault. The Oxford English Dictionary defines the verb injure 'as to hurt, harm, damage' and the noun injury as 'hurt or loss caused or sustained by a person or thing, harm, detriment, damage'. However, despite the uncertainty as to its exact meaning the word, injurious or injury to the person must mean that some actual harm must be inflicted on the victim. The dictionary definition suggests that the threshold for harm is quite low and it may be satisfied by establishing mere 'bodily harm'. In Donovan, bodily harm was defined in accordance with its natural meaning as 'any hurt or injury calculated to interfere with health or comfort'.40

---

### Chunk 7 — Breach of the peace (s 182(4)) — annotation and practical note
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 182(4)] [CITATION: Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 182(4) - Breach of the peace discussion] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Breach of the peace limb in s182(4); violence element and standards] [CONCEPTS: breach of the peace, Howell definition, likelihood of violence, Nilsson v McDonald, low threshold defence]

Tactical commentary on the "breach of the peace" limb and practical comment.

2.6.8 The final condition that must be established if the Crown is to negate the effect of the victim's consent is that the assault constitutes a breach of the peace. Although this phrase is not defined in the Code, the UK Court of Appeal case of Howell41 provides an authoritative definition. According to Howell a breach of the peace occurs, whenever harm is actually done or is likely to be done to a person or in his presence to his property or a person is in fear of being so harmed through an assault, an affray, a riot, unlawful assembly or other disturbance.42 2.6.9 The essence of a breach of the peace is violence. That the phrase as used in the Code also carries this connotation was confirmed in Nilsson v McDonald where Blow J stated: 'I think it must follow that those words \[ie, 'breach of the peace'\] ... were intended to refer to the common law concept of a breach of the peace. ... There cannot be a breach of the peace ... unless there is violence or a likelihood of violence.'43

Basically never a good defence as "breach of peace" is pretty low threshold.

---

### Chunk 8 — R v Holmes [1993] TASSC 5 — quoted jury directions and consent discussion
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: R v Holmes — consent, bodily harm, breach of the peace and jury direction] [CONCEPTS: R v Holmes, jury direction, bodily harm, consent irrelevant if bodily harm intended or likely, s182(4), POA s13, arrest powers] [CITATION: R v Holmes [1993] TASSC 5 - Jury directions and consent]

Tactical content covering R v Holmes [1993] TASSC 5 (11 February 1993).

4\. At all events, those two decisions support the view that at common law in a case such as the present, where the defence does not deny that the accused inflicted violence upon the complainant and does not seek to rely upon self-defence, - and I understand that self-defence is not relied upon, although for obvious reasons I will be including it in the memorandum to the jury and I will be giving them a self-defence direction in due course - then in such circumstances the essential question for the jury is whether they are satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the blows struck by the accused were likely or were intended by him to cause bodily harm to the complainant. If the jury is so satisfied any consent, whether express or implied, becomes irrelevant in my opinion, and the jury should convict the accused, provided, of course, it is satisfied to the requisite degree of all the other essential elements of the crime of assault.

5\. "Bodily harm" in this context has its usual meaning and includes any hurt or injury which interferes with the health or comfort of the complainant. It need not be permanent but it must be more than merely transient or trifling.

...

7\. If the prosecution fails to persuade the jury that bodily harm to the complainant was likely in the circumstances or that the accused did not intend to cause bodily harm by his action, the jury may then consider the question of consent and unless satisfied that the complainant did not consent to the assault alleged, the jury should acquit the accused.

...

11\. It is of course necessary under s182(4) that to be unlawful the actions of the accused must also involve a breach of the peace if consent is present. Constable Slade said he arrested the accused originally for "using words calculated or likely to cause a breach of the peace". This of course is not determinative of or even evidence of the fact that a breach of the peace was occurring at the time the police arrived. However whilst the police powers of arrest are limited by the Code, s27(6) and the [Police Offences Act 1935](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/), ![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}** [ss13](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s13.html) **![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} and [55](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s55.html), so that an officer may only arrest a person committing a breach of the peace or behaving in a manner likely to cause a breach of the peace in certain defined circumstances, the limitation of police powers in no way circumscribes the nature of conduct which may amount to a breach of the peace.

12\. It seems to me to be completely clear that in this case, on any view of the evidence, a breach of the peace must have occurred as a consequence of the evening\'s events if indeed bodily harm was caused to Mrs Cetkovic or was likely to be caused as a result of the accused\'s physical violence towards her. As to what is involved in a breach of the peace, see ***Howell v R (1981) 73 Crim App R 31***. I refer to the accused\'s evidence in this respect at 259-261 where he has clearly assented to the proposition that during the course of the night\'s activities, both he and Mrs Cetkovic fought together.

### Chunk 9 — Howell v R (1981) 73 Crim App R 31 — breach of the peace definition
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Howell definition of breach of the peace] [CONCEPTS: Howell, breach of the peace, harm likely, affray, riot, unlawful assembly] [CITATION: Howell v R (1981) 73 Crim App R 31 - Breach of the peace definition]

Tactical quote covering Howell v R.

*"There is breach of the peace whenever harm is actually done or is likely to be done to a person or in his presence to his property or a person is in fear of being so harmed through an assault, an affray, a riot, unlawfully assembly or other disturbance."*

(Quoted material accepted as generally applicable in Australian common law.)

---

### Chunk 10 — Common intercourse of life s182(3) — Turner v Horan and Boughey
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 182(3)] [CITATION: Criminal Code 1924 (Tas) s 182(3) - Common intercourse of life and hostility] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: s182(3) excluding commonplace non-hostile acts; hostility may convert conduct to battery] [CONCEPTS: s182(3), common intercourse of life, Boughey v The Queen, Turner v Horan, hostility, battery]

Tactical note covering Turner v Horan [2021] TASSC 16 (16 April 2021) and Boughey v The Queen.

16. The applicant contends that the magistrate could only have been satisfied that he hugged Ms Beasley without hostile intent. However, it was not necessary for the prosecution to prove that the applicant applied force with hostility or hostile intent. The operation of s 182(3) was considered in *Boughey v The Queen *(above). At 24, Mason, Wilson and Deane JJ stated:

\"The effect of this provision is to exclude from an 'assault', for the purposes of the Code, commonplace, intentional but non-hostile acts such as patting another on the shoulder to attract attention or pushing between others to alight from a crowded bus. Such acts are, if committed inoffensively, regarded by the common law as ordinary incidents of social intercourse which do not, without more, constitute battery.\"

17. In a passage also referred to by the applicant, their Honours continued at 25:

\"Where the existence of hostility or hostile intent may be of decisive importance is in a case which would otherwise be of the kind which s 182(3) excludes from 'assault' for the purposes of the Code in that hostility or hostile intent may convert what would otherwise be unobjectionable as an ordinary incident of social intercourse into battery at common law or an assault for the purposes of the Code. Apart from such cases, however, the absence of such hostility or hostile intent towards the person to whom force is applied neither precludes the intentional application of force to the person of another from constituting battery at common law or assault under the Code nor, of itself, constitutes a justification or excuse for it.\"

---

### Chunk 12 — Indecent assault — Police v FRS [2020] TASMC 9 (elements and context)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1924 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 182, 127] [CITATION: Police v FRS [2020] TASMC 9 - Indecent assault elements and definition] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: What constitutes "indecent" and statutory elements for indecent assault] [CONCEPTS: indecent assault, sexual connotation, community standards, elements, ss182 and 127, voluntariness, lack of consent]

Tactical coverage of Police v FRS [2020] TASMC 9 (25 May 2020) on indecent assault.

\[71\] The term 'indecent' is not defined in the *Code*. To be regarded as indecent, an act must have a sexual connotation[^\[29\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/9.html#fn29), either because of the area of the body to which the assault is directed, or because the assailant uses the area of his body which would give rise to a sexual connotation in carrying the assault[^\[30\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/9.html#fn30). The behaviour must be unbecoming or offensive to common propriety[^\[31\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/9.html#fn31); an affront to modesty[^\[32\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/9.html#fn32); contrary to community standards of decency according to right minded persons[^\[33\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/9.html#fn33); or offend the ordinary modesty of the average person[^\[34\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/9.html#fn34). Context is an important consideration[^\[35\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2020/9.html#fn35).

Elements

\[46\] The *Code*, ss 182 and 127 require the prosecution to prove, on the hearing of each complaint:

- the defendant;  
- by an application of force  
- which was voluntary and intentional;  
- without the consent of the complainant;  
- unlawfully;  
- assaulted the complainant;  
- in indecent circumstances, as judged by the standards of ordinary and decent people.

---

### Chunk 13 — Brazendale v O [2008] TASMC 3 — indecency principles
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Brazendale on indecency and sexual connotation] [CONCEPTS: indecency, sexual connotation, area of body, intent, R v Harkin, Brazendale] [CITATION: Brazendale v O [2008] TASMC 3 - Indecency principles]

Tactical note.

\- indecency has been held to be unseemly, unbecoming or outrageous conduct. It is an act offending against propriety. It is conduct with a sexual connotation;

\- when applying force to the complainant, the defendant must have intended to do it in an indecent manner, judged according to the standards of ordinary reasonable members of the community;

 The area of the body concerned (the area of the chest/breasts particularly) gave rise to a sexual connotation: *R v Harkin* [(1989) 38 A Crim R 296 (NSW](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281989%29%2038%20A%20Crim%20R%20296?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s35) CCA) per Lee J (with whom the other members agreed) at 310.

---

### Chunk 14 — Collins v Coleman [2013] TASMC 19 — indecent intent and OED guidance
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 35(3)] [CITATION: Collins v Coleman [2013] TASMC 19 - Indecent intent and meaning of "indecent"] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Collins v Coleman on meaning of "indecent" and "indecent intent"] [CONCEPTS: Collins v Coleman, indecent intent, OED, sexual connotation, standards of propriety, s35(3)]

Tactical content quoting Collins v Coleman [2013] TASMC 19.

**Police v FRS referenced**.

51. In the context of ![](media/image3.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"}** [s.35(3)](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s35.html) **![](media/image4.png){width="0.16666666666666666in" height="0.16666666666666666in"} of the [*Police Offences Act*](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/), the word *'indecent' *has not been the subject of any authoritative statement by the Supreme Court of Tasmania. The offence of assault with indecent intent seems to be peculiar to the Tasmanian jurisdiction. There is, therefore, no guidance to be found in any decision from a superior court of another state. *'Indecent' *is an ordinary English word without a definite legal meaning. Because the legislation does not supply a definition, the word should be given its literal or grammatical meaning in its context[^\[1\]^](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/sinodisp/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2013/19.html?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s35#fn1).

52. The online edition of the *Oxford English Dictionary *defines *indecent *as follows:

*1. Unbecoming; highly unsuitable or inappropriate (†*to*); contrary to the fitness of things; in extremely bad taste; unseemly.*

*\...*

*3. Offending against the recognized standards of propriety and delicacy; highly indelicate, immodest; suggesting or tending to obscenity.*

53. In the context of a criminal prosecution for assault, the word *"indecent"* has long been used in law to describe acts involving lewdness or sexual connotation: *Crowe v Graham* [\[1968\] HCA 6](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/cth/HCA/1968/6.html); [(1967-1968) 121 CLR 375](http://classic.austli.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281967%2d1968%29%20121%20CLR%20375?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s35) per Windeyer J at p.390; *R v Harkin* [(1989) 38 A Crim R 296](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281989%29%2038%20A%20Crim%20R%20296?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s35) at 301.

54. [The prosecution case against the defendant was advanced solely on the basis that the defendant's intent was one of sexual indecency. In light of the principles above, the phrase *"indecent intent"* in the present context, requires proof beyond reasonable doubt that the physical acts which constitute the assault were done with the specific intent to arouse or gratify the defendant's sexual desires]{.mark}

55. It must be borne in mind that it is the defendant's *intention* accompanying the offending acts which must be proven to be indecent. It is *not* necessary for the prosecution to prove that the acts giving rise to the assault should, of themselves, be indecent.

---

### Chunk 15 — Aggravated assault (firearm) — Tasmania v Scott [2025] TASSC 53 elements
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Tasmania v Scott [2025] TASSC 53 - Aggravated assault (firearm) elements] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Elements of aggravated assault by pointing firearm] [CONCEPTS: aggravated assault, firearm, threatening gesture, present ability, belief of ability, intention to threaten, realisation, unlawful acts] [CASE: Tasmania v Scott [2025] TASSC 53]

Tactical elements for aggravated assault with a firearm from Tasmania v Scott [2025] TASSC 53.

Elements (**Tasmania v Scott [2025] TASSC 53 **)

6.  A person commits the crime of aggravated assault if it is proved beyond reasonable doubt that:

(a) the accused;

(b) by a voluntary act or acts;

(c) made a threatening gesture (either with intent to threaten or with a realisation that a threat may be perceived) to apply force to another person by pointing a firearm or toy firearm;

(d) at the time of the gesture, the accused used, threatened to use or was carrying a firearm;

(e) the accused:

i had the present ability to apply force to the other person; or

ii. caused the other person to believe, on reasonable grounds, that he had the present ability to apply force; and

(f) the acts were unlawful; and

(g) at the time of the acts, the accused either:

i.  intended to threaten the other person, that is, give some sort of indication that he may use the firearm to apply force; or

<!-- -->

ii. realised that the likely consequence of his actions was that the other person would apprehend that force was being threatened and proceeded regardless of the consequence.

---

### Chunk 1 — Threat by gesture: definition and Vallance reference
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 s182 - Threat by gesture overview] [CATEGORY: practitioner note] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: assault by threatening gesture overview] [CONCEPTS: assault by gesture, threatening gesture, present ability, Vallance, unlawful element]

Tactical note covering the definition of assault by a threatening gesture and the Vallance principle.

Def threatened by gesture to apply force to the person of the, that def had, or caused vic to believe on reasonable grounds that he had, the present ability to apply such force

Either: (R v Vallance 1960 at para 101)

[Threatening by any gesture to apply such force to the person of another if the assaulter has present ability to effect his purpose; or]{.mark} By threatening by any gesture to apply such force to the person of another if the assaulter causes the assaultee to believe on reasonable grounds that he has present ability to effect his purpose.

Unlawful.

Is an inherent element which must be proven on the prosecution case

### Chunk 2 — Emmet v Arnold [2006] TASSC 5: magistrate satisfaction on elements
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Emmet v Arnold [2006] TASSC 5] [CITATION: Emmet v Arnold 2006 - Magistrate must be satisfied of every element] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: requirement to prove unlawful element and all elements beyond reasonable doubt] [CONCEPTS: Emmet v Arnold, magistrate, burden of proof, unlawful element, s35(1), Police Offences Act]

Tactical note covering a case authority on proof of elements.

*Emmet v Arnold* [2006] TASSC 5

*[12] A magistrate may not find a charge of assault under [s35(1)](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s35.html) proven without being satisfied beyond reasonable doubt of every element of that offence. It is therefore necessary not just to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that there has been an intentional application of force, but also to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the application of force was unlawful.*

---

### Chunk 3 — Bonde v Maney [2018] TASSC 23: elements of assault by application of force
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Bonde v Maney [2018] TASSC 23] [CITATION: Bonde v Maney 2018 - Elements of assault by application of force] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: elements required for assault by application of force] [CONCEPTS: Bonde v Maney, elements of assault, applied force, voluntary act, Vallance state of mind]

Tactical note covering what must be proved for assault by application of force.

7.  The mental elements of assault were considered by me in *Tasmania v Oates *[\[2017\] TASSC 39.](http://www9.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2017/39.html) Although I was considering assault as defined by s 182 of the *Criminal Code*, my conclusions are apposite to a charge of common assault under [s 35(1)](http://www9.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s35.html) of the [*Police Offences Act *1935](http://www9.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/).

8.  Accordingly, in the case of an assault which is alleged to have been committed by the application of force to the person of another, the elements which must be proved by the prosecution are:

(a) that the accused has applied force to the person of another, directly or indirectly;

(b) by the commission of an act which is voluntary and intentional;

(c) that the act is committed with the intention of applying force to the person of another or with subjective foresight that this will occur and proceeding with the act nonetheless (the *Vallance* state of mind).

---

### Chunk 4 — Argument: no need to specify type of effect (practitioner note)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Practitioner note - pleading alternatives for threatening gesture] [CATEGORY: practitioner note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: pleading approach — specify types in the alternative] [CONCEPTS: pleading, alternatives, threatening gesture, particulars, Justices Act s30, drafting]

Tactical note covering pleading strategy.

Argument that There is no need to specify the type of effect "i.e. threatning gesture.) it might have on the victim. Can simply state them all in the alternative.

### Chunk 5 — Justices Act 1959 - s 30 (Statement of offences)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Justices Act 1959] [SECTION: 30] [CITATION: Justices Act 1959 s30 - Statement of offences] [CATEGORY: legislative reference] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: sufficiency of complaints and alternative pleading under s30] [CONCEPTS: Justices Act s30, complaint sufficiency, alternative pleading, particulars, simple offence]

Tactical note covering statutory pleading rules.

30. Statement of offences, &c.

(1) Any complaint, summons, warrant, or other document that is laid, issued, or made for the purpose of, or in connection with, proceedings before justices shall be sufficient if it --

(a) describes the matter of complaint with which the defendant is charged or of which he is convicted in ordinary language, avoiding as far as possible the use of technical terms and without necessarily stating all the essential elements of the matter of complaint; and

(b) contains such particulars as will give reasonable information of the nature of the matter complained of.

(2) Where an enactment constituting a simple offence or breach of duty states --

(a) the offence or breach of duty to be doing or omitting to do --

(i) any one of any different acts in the alternative; or

(ii) any act in any one of different capacities or with any one of different intentions; or

(b) any part of the offence or breach of duty in the alternative --

the acts, omissions, capacities, intentions, or other matters stated in the alternative in the enactment may be stated in the alternative in a complaint for that simple offence or breach of duty.

---

### Chunk 6 — Police Offences Act 1935 s182: Definition of assault
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935] [SECTION: 182] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 s182 - Definition of assault (threat by gesture)] [CATEGORY: legislative reference] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: statutory definition of assault — attempt or threatening by gesture] [CONCEPTS: Police Offences Act s182, definition of assault, threatening by gesture, present ability, apply force]

Tactical note covering the statutory text relevant to assault by gesture.

182.  Definition of assault

Attempting or ***threatening by any gesture to apply such force to the person of another*** if the person making the attempt or threat ***has***, or causes the other to believe on reasonable grounds that he has, ***present ability to effect his purpose***

---

### Chunk 7 — Thornton v The Queen (1984) - ingredients of gesture threat (para 9 and Green CJ)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Thornton v The Queen (1984) 14 Tas R 113] [CITATION: Thornton v The Queen 1984 - Ingredients of assault by gesture] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: essential ingredients of assault by threatening gesture] [CONCEPTS: Thornton v The Queen, threatening gesture, intention to induce apprehension, elements list, Green CJ]

Tactical note covering the Court of Criminal Appeal’s identification of ingredients.

[para 9] In addition to the requirement that the act must be unlawful, the ingredients of the two forms of the crime created by s 182(1) which were left to the jury are:

- 1 

  - (a) the act of threatening by any gesture,

Threaten

Green CJ.

[para 15] *Thornton v The Queen* (1984) 14Tas R 113

*"... In the other general sense of the word a gesture would only be a threat if it were done with an intention to actually apply force or to induce an apprehension in another that force is about to be applied. In my view, the word as used in s 182(1) should be given the latter connotation."*

- (b) to apply force to the person of another directly or indirectly,

- (c) if the person making the threat has present ability to effect his purpose; or

- 2 

  - (a) the act of threatening by any gesture, (s 13 intentional

  - (b) to apply force to the person of another directly or indirectly,

  - (c) if the person making the threat causes the other person to believe on reasonable grounds that he has present ability to effect his purpose.

Green CJ: In *Thornton v The Queen* Serial No 81/1984 ((1959-1995) [14 Tas R 113)](http://classic.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=14%20Tas%20R%20113?stem=0&synonyms=0&query=poa1935140%20s34b), a decision of the Court of Criminal Appeal, I held that an essential ingredient of the crime of assault by a threatening gesture is that the act must be done with an intention that the victim should thereby be induced to apprehend that the actor is about to apply force directly or indirectly to the person of the victim. I did not find it necessary to express an opinion as to whether, in the alternative, it would be sufficient to prove that the act was done with foresight, that the probable consequence of the act would be to induce in the victim an apprehension that force was about to be applied to him. Although the other members of the Court did not find it necessary to express a concluded view as to precisely what were the mental ingredients of this form of assault, there was no suggestion in any of the judgments, nor is there any suggestion in the Code itself, that an intention to actually apply force is an ingredient of the crime of assault when it takes the form of a threat by a gesture to apply force.

---

### Chunk 8 — Thornton v The Queen: Green CJ on mental element and Cosgrove J note
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Thornton v The Queen (1984) 14 Tas R 113] [CITATION: Thornton v The Queen 1984 - Mental element and Cosgrove J] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: intention to induce apprehension as necessary mental element] [CONCEPTS: Thornton, intention to induce apprehension, Cosgrove J, mental element, assault by gesture]

Tactical note covering further judicial observations on the mental element.

[21] *"In my view, an essential ingredient of the crime of assault when it takes either of the forms to which I have referred above, is that the act which constitutes the threatening gesture must be done with the intention that the victim should thereby be induced to apprehend that the actor is about to apply force directly or indirectly to the person of the victim. As the learned trial judge did not give the jury any directions about the mental element of the crime, [I find it unnecessary to express an opinion as to whether, in the alternative, it would be sufficient to prove that the act was done with foresight, that the probable consequence of the act would be to induce in the victim an apprehension that force was about to be applied to him."]{.mark}*

[Para 30] **Cosgrove J. "...** But in my opinion an action cannot amount to a threat (ie, a message of impending harm) unless it was intended as such, or was perceived as such by the person or persons to whom it was apparently directed."

---

### Chunk 9 — No temporal connection required: Lees v Visser [2000] TASSC 3
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Lees v Visser [2000] TASSC 3] [CITATION: Lees v Visser 2000 - No requirement of immediacy for threatened force] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: temporal element — immediacy not required for threat by gesture] [CONCEPTS: Lees v Visser, immediacy, temporal element, present ability, threatened force, s182]

Tactical note covering temporal requirement for threat.

There is NO temporal requirement of immediacy of the threat of application of the force (can threaten to stab tomorrow, so long as at the time the threat is made the def has a knife in his hands etc) , only that the victim believes that the defendant has the present ability to effect his purpose when the threat is made, namely applying force directly or indirectly to the other person. It need not be a threat of immediate force). ***Lees v Visser*** [2000] TASSC 3

*25 Section 182(1) contains no express requirement referable to a threat of immediate force. What is prescribed is \"threatening by any gesture to apply \... force to the person of another, (directly or indirectly) if the person making the \... threat causes the other to believe on reasonable grounds that he has present ability to effect his purpose\". A temporal element is introduced into the offence insofar as the section expressly requires that the complainant must believe that the accused has present ability to effect his purpose. That requirement is understandable. In the absence of such a belief, the apprehension caused to the complainant by the threat is likely to be significantly reduced. Whilst the requirement that the complainant believe that the accused has the present ability to effect his purpose may suggest the need for some proximity between the time of the threat and the time when the force is to be applied, this falls a long way short of requiring that the threatened force must be imminent.*

*26 The complainant must believe that the accused has a present ability to achieve his purpose, that is, directly or indirectly applying force to the complainant. No limit is expressly placed on the time when the force is to be applied. I can find no justification in the language used for extending the requirement that the complainant\'s belief be that the accused \"has, present ability to effect his purpose\" to encompass and impose a requirement that the purpose itself is to be effected immediately. Such an extension would be artificial and illogical. The gravamen of the offence is the accused\'s threat by gesture to apply force to the complainant and the complainant\'s belief referable to the same. I can see no reason for confining the offence to threats of immediate force. In some circumstances a threat of force at a time subsequent to the threat can be more intimidating and distressing than a threat of immediate force.*

*27 Had Parliament intended to confine the offence to threats of immediate force, it would have been easy to manifest that intention by inserting the word \"immediate\" into the section so that it read: \"threatening by any gesture to apply such force immediately to the person of another\". In the absence of language such as this, I am unpersuaded that the words used should be embellished by the addition of a requirement that the threatened force be immediate force.*

*28 As it is not necessary that the threatened force be immediate force, grounds 1 and 1A must fail. Insofar as it was necessary to do so, the learned magistrate made findings about the force the applicant intended the complainant to apprehend and the complainant's state of mind referable to that force. The learned magistrate inferred that the applicant intended to convey to the complainant that he would be subject to violence from the applicant by the use of a firearm if he did not pay the applicant. That finding is consistent with the evidence. In relation to the complainant\'s mental state, the learned magistrate said that he had no reason to doubt the complainant\'s evidence of his feelings and his perceptions of what the applicant did and said. It is clear from that finding, together with other findings, that the learned magistrate was satisfied to the requisite standard that the complainant believed that the applicant had the present ability to effect his purpose. There was ample evidence to support the findings and I agree with them.*

---

Tactical note covering whether recklessness suffices.

Can a defendant be held responsible for an intentional act, done reckless of the probable consequence being that it was induce an apprehension that force would be applies (subjective recklessness).

### Chunk 12 — Tasmania v Oates [2017] TASSC 39: para 20 and Cox J para 39 (subjective foresight)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Tasmania v Oates [2017] TASSC 39] [CITATION: Tasmania v Oates 2017 - Thornton, Vallance, and subjective foresight] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: whether specific intent required or subjective foresight suffices] [CONCEPTS: Tasmania v Oates, Thornton, Vallance, subjective foresight, Cox J, Beechey, R v Bennett]

Tactical note covering appellate discussion of mental element.

Taken from [para 20] of Tasmania v Oates [2017] TASSC 39 (1 June 2017)

"*Thornton v The Queen *![http://www.austlii.edu.au/images/displeft.png](media/image3.png){width="0.16527777777777777in" height="0.16527777777777777in"}** [[(1984) 14 Tas R 113.]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281984%29%2014%20Tas%20R%20113) **![http://www.austlii.edu.au/images/dispright.png](media/image4.png){width="0.16527777777777777in" height="0.16527777777777777in"} That case concerned the mental element relevant to an assault constituted by an attempt or threat by gesture to apply force to the person of another. It was held by two members of the Court of Criminal Appeal, Green CJ and Cox J (as he then was), that such a crime does require a mental element additional to the requirement for a voluntary and intentional act, in particular an intention to induce another to apprehend that force was to be applied. However, it is clear that the reasoning which led to this conclusion was based on the inherent effect of the formulation of the words used in the section, in particular, that there be an attempt or a threat. Green CJ declined to express an opinion as to whether \"in the alternative it would be sufficient to prove that the act was done with foresight, that the probable consequence of the act would be to induce in the victim an apprehension that force was about to be applied to him\". This would indicate that, although his Honour considered a mental element to be inherent in the relevant act of making a threat, it does not follow that that mental element requires proof of a specific intent, and subjective foresight may be sufficient.

Cox J expressed this sentiment with more clarity at [39]:

\"I agree that there is no occasion for this Court to have recourse to the common law when it is determining the elements of assault as defined by s 182(1). As presently advised, I see no reason to read into that definition the need for a specific intention to cause the victim to apprehend force where the assault takes the form of a threatening gesture any more than the High Court in *Vallance v The Queen* [[\[1961\] HCA 42]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/cth/HCA/1961/42.html); [[(1961) 108 CLR 56]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281961%29%20108%20CLR%2056?query=%2214%20Tas%20R%20113%22) found the need for such a specific intention to inflict a wound in relation to the crime created by s 172.\" 

---

### Chunk 13 — Wood v Beach [1985] TASSC 39: no need to intend to apply force
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Wood v Beach [1985] TASSC 39] [CITATION: Wood v Beach 1985 - intention to actually apply force not required for gesture threat] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: intention to induce apprehension suffices; no need to intend to apply force] [CONCEPTS: Wood v Beach, intention to apply force, threatening gesture, Green CJ, mental element]

Tactical note covering that an intention to actually apply force is not required.

No need to actually intend to apply force: only to create an apprehension or belief in the mind of the victim:

Wood v Beach [1985] TASSC 39 (20 August 1985)

GREEN CJ

[Para 7] If when he was making his ruling the learned magistrate was intending to refer to assault by a threatening gesture then, with respect, he was in error because an intention to actually apply force is not an ingredient of that form of assault. In *Thornton v The Queen* Serial No 81/1984 ((1959-1995) ![http://www.austlii.edu.au/images/displeft.png](media/image3.png){width="0.16527777777777777in" height="0.16527777777777777in"}** [[14 Tas R 113)]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=14%20Tas%20R%20113) **![http://www.austlii.edu.au/images/dispright.png](media/image4.png){width="0.16527777777777777in" height="0.16527777777777777in"}, a decision of the Court of Criminal Appeal, I held that an essential ingredient of the crime of assault by a threatening gesture is that the act must be done with an intention that the victim should thereby be induced to apprehend that the actor is about to apply force directly or indirectly to the person of the victim. I did not find it necessary to express an opinion as to whether, in the alternative, it would be sufficient to prove that the act was done with foresight that the probable consequence of the act would be to induce in the victim an apprehension that force was about to be applied to him. Although the other members of the Court did not find it necessary to express a concluded view as to precisely what were the mental ingredients of this form of assault, there was no suggestion in any of the judgments, nor is there any suggestion in the Code itself, that an intention to actually apply force is an ingredient of the crime of assault when it takes the form of a threat by a gesture to apply force.

---

### Chunk 14 — What is subjective recklessness? Vallance state of mind and Beechey v McDonald
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Beechey v McDonald [2010] TASSC 47; Vallance v R (1961) HCA 42] [CITATION: Vallance state of mind - subjective recklessness explained with Beechey v McDonald] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: definition and acceptance of subjective recklessness (Vallance state of mind)] [CONCEPTS: subjective recklessness, Vallance, Beechey v McDonald, actual intent, foresight, R v Bennett]

Tactical note covering the Vallance state of mind and acceptance in later authorities.

Vallance state of mind in respect of assault was accepted as correct by Evans J in *Beechey v McDonald* [[\[2010\] TASSC 47.]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2010/47.html) At [[\[9\]]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2010/47.html#para9), his Honour said:

The mental element that must be established in order to convict an offender of assault by the application of force is either that the offender intentionally applied force to the complainant, or that [the offender foresaw the likelihood that he or she might do so and proceeded regardless of that consequence]{.mark}; *Wilkinson v R *43/1985, Nettlefold J, at 4, and *Vallance v R *[[\[1961\] HCA 42]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/cth/HCA/1961/42.html); [[(1961) 108 CLR 56.]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281961%29%20108%20CLR%2056?query=%2214%20Tas%20R%20113%22) Adopting and adapting the words of Neasey J, agreed with by Underwood and Crawford JJ, as they then were, in *R v Bennett* [[\[1990\] TASRp 6]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASRp/1990/6.html); [[\[1990\] Tas R 72]{.underline}](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1990%5d%20Tas%20R%2072?query=%2214%20Tas%20R%20113%22), at 81, what the prosecution must prove is actual intent to apply force or subjective recklessness. Negligence, falling short of recklessness, is insufficient; *Vallance *(supra), Kitto J at 64.

---

### Chunk 15 — Thornton complaint example (STEPHEN GRANT THORNTON)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Thornton complaint - example of allegations in assault by dangerous conduct case] [CATEGORY: procedural document] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: example complaint text alleging firing rifle into dwelling with persons present] [CONCEPTS: complaint, Thornton, rifle firing, assault, dwelling, persons present]

Tactical note covering the complaint wording used in Thornton.

Tactical sequence / complaint text:

STEPHEN GRANT THORNTON at East Devonport in Tasmania on or about the 10th day of August 1983 unlawfully assaulted Garry David Storay, Denise Cathleen Steers, Paul Marshall Paterson, Christine King, Shaun Storay and Luke Steers by firing a rifle several times in quick succession into a dwelling house in which he knew there to be persons present and in which there were then present the said Garry David Storay, Denise Cathleen Steers, Paul Marshall Paterson, Christine King, Shaun Storay and Luke Steers.

---

### Chunk 16 — Assessment (practitioner commentary)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Practitioner assessment - indictability, mental element, prank issue] [CATEGORY: practitioner note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: assessment of factors relevant to indictability and mental element for assault by gesture / vehicle used as weapon] [CONCEPTS: assessment, indictability, mental element, dangerous driving, prank, Crown burden]

Tactical note covering practitioner's assessment of the issues.

The dominant consideration for assault and whether it should also be indictable is the seriousness of injuries. There were no physical injuries but serious potential for both the victim and other road users. The allegation is that the vehicle was effectively used as a weapon, and but for his actions she had the present ability to affect force. From the unreported decisions of Thornton and Wood v Beach the mental element is over and above the voluntary and intention act required in s13(1), being that the 'specific intention to induce such apprehension is an essential ingredient'. The Crown would have to get over any suggestion that it was a 'prank' and that perhaps her attention to was simply to annoy him or alert him to her presence as opposed have him consider he was about to be hit by her vehicle. (Eg in Thornton where the accused was firing the gun at the house simply to make some noise.) To conviction the jury would have to be satisfied she had the intention to cause 'requisite fear or apprehension' of force. This is of course not an element for the offence of dangerous driving.

Whether the mental element could include recklessness has been left open, but generally the approach in Thornton has been considered positively recently in Wood v White [2016] TASSC 76, Tasmania v Oates [2017] TASSC 39.

---

### Chunk 17 — Negligence vs sub-recklessness (Barnes v Henri [2017])
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Barnes v Henri [2017] TASMC 14] [CITATION: Barnes v Henri 2017 - negligence vs subjective recklessness] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: negligence not sufficient; subjective recklessness required] [CONCEPTS: negligence, subjective recklessness, Barnes v Henri, mens rea, assault]

Tactical note covering the mens rea threshold.

Negligence not enough! Needs to be subjective recklessness. Example of negligence not sub-reck: **Barnes v Henri [2017] TASMC 14**

---

### Chunk 18 — Assault police — Elements (Hickman v Richmond [2013] TASMC 36)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Hickman v Richmond [2013] TASMC 36] [CITATION: Hickman v Richmond 2013 - Elements of assaulting a police officer in execution of duty] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: elements for assaulting police in execution of duty] [CONCEPTS: assault police, elements, intentional kick, subjective recklessness, execution of duty, Hickman v Richmond]

Tactical note covering enumerated elements for assaulting police.

Elements:

Cited in **Hickman v Richmond [2013] TASMC 36 (3 July 2013)**

The elements of the offence of assaulting a police officer in the execution of duty are that:

a.  the defendant;

b.  intentionally kicked

c.  Constable Clark

d.  to the face - either

    i.  directly or

    ii. subjectively recklessly as to whether her leg or foot would strike Constable Clark's face; and

e.  at the material time, Constable Clark was acting in the execution of her duty.

---

### Chunk 19 — Sentencing submission re offences against police (Derrico v McKenna)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Derrico v McKenna (39/1980) - sentencing principle] [CITATION: Derrico v McKenna 1980 - sentencing considerations for offences against police] [CATEGORY: practitioner note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: sentencing approach — protection and vindication of police authority] [CONCEPTS: sentencing, police protection, Derrico v McKenna, vindicate authority, custodial sentences]

Tactical note covering sentencing submission language and authority.

Sentencing submission:

"police are entitled to expect to be supported and reinforced by court orders in sentence of matters"

In *Derrico v McKenna* 39/1980, Green CJ said of this offence:

"Offences of this kind must be regarded seriously and a court imposing a sentence in respect of them should be mindful of the fact that the work of police officers frequently exposes them to the risk of violence and should impose sentences which vindicate and reinforce the authority of police officers as the executive agents of the law. However, those considerations do not involve the consequence that the usual principles of sentencing do not apply to such offences, nor that in such cases a court which is considering sentence is relieved of its obligation to consider the particular circumstances of each individual offender and of each offence."

---

### Chunk 20 — Roughley v Rigall [1996] TASSC 82: custodial term appropriateness
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Roughley v Rigall [1996] TASSC 82] [CITATION: Roughley v Rigall 1996 - court supports custodial sentence for officers exposed to danger] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: appropriateness of custodial sentence to protect and encourage police] [CONCEPTS: Roughley v Rigall, custodial sentence, police protection, encouragement, sentencing principle]

Tactical note covering appellate approval of custodial sentences for offences against police.

The court upheld the appropriateness of the submission :

Police officers are called upon at times to expose themselves to threats to their own lives in the line of duty and where, as here, they have responded, the court should act in aid of them to encourage them in their duty and\
to offer protection to them when they do so. Anything less than an actual custodial term would be wholly unresponsive."

---

Dunford v Fletcher [1991] TASSC 154

Which also looked at the appropriateness of prison for offences against police offences.
