<!-- block_041 master -->

# Police Offences Act 1935 (Tas)

<!-- block_041 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Jones v Krushka [2021] TASMC 14 - "Execution or performance of duty"
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act 1935] [SECTION: s34B(1)(a)(i)] [CITATION: Police Offences Act 1935 s34B - Execution of duty - Jones v Krushka] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Meaning of "in the execution of his duty" for assault on police officer] [CONCEPTS: assault police, execution of duty, s34B, Jones v Krushka, officer acting in duty]

This chunk covers: meaning of "in the execution of his duty" as discussed in Jones v Krushka.

138 In order to establish counts 1 and 3 on the complaint it is necessary to establish that S/C Moir is a police officer and at the time he was assaulted he was acting *"in the execution of his duty"*: [*Police Offences Act *1935](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/) [s34B(a)(a)(i).](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s34b.html)

139 That is an essential element of the charge. If it is not established, the charge is not proved even if an assault is otherwise established: *Hibble v Phegan & Ors* [\[1997\] TASSC 52](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/1997/52.html) per Zeeman J at \[46\].

140 The scope of a police officer's duty was considered by Cosgrove J in *Innes v Weate *[\[1984\] TASRp 3](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASRp/1984/3.html); [\[1984\] Tas R 14.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1984%5d%20Tas%20R%2014) His Honour said at p20:

*"The word "duty" does not refer, to the constable's duty to obey superior officers. It refers to the duty of constables generally -- the duty to prevent and detect crime and to apprehend wrongdoers, to keep the peace, and to protect life and property (i.e. to protect persons from injury and property from damage). In Rice v Connolly [\[1966\] 2 QB 414](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1966%5d%202%20QB%20414), at 419, Lord Parker CJ said:*

*"It is also in my judgment clear that it is part of the obligations and duties of a police constable to take all steps which appear to him necessary for keeping the peace, for preventing crime or for protecting property from criminal injury. There is no exhaustive definition of the powers and obligations of the police, but they are at least those, and the would further include the duty to detect crime and to bring an offender to justice."*

141 His Honour said, at p21, that the duty cannot be stated other than in general terms and that the existence and nature of the duty depends upon a reasonable assessment by the constable of any given situation. That means there is a wide discretion to act swiftly and decisively but the exercise of that discretion is subject to scrutiny by the courts.

142 There is also a well-recognised distinction between acting in the course of duty and acting in the execution of duty: *Coates v R *[\[2001\] TASSC 141](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2001/141.html)* *per Slicer J at \[16\] (with whom Cox CJ agreed at \[1\]). A police officer acts in the course of his or her duty by attending or being at events. That is part of a general duty to keep the peace and ensure compliance with the law. His Honour said, at \[17\]:

*"But his capacity to engage in a particular action is defined by statute or common law powers. It is the nature or form of that particular action or response which constitutes 'execution of duty' rather than 'course of duty. The distinction can be described as being as that between "a shield" and "a sword". In executing a particular duty, he is said to be protected (by way of particular sanction) from obstruction."*

143 In *Tasmania v Gladwin *[\[2016\] TASSC 64](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2016/64.html), Brett J highlighted the distinction between being 'on duty'within the meaning of [s16A](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/s16a.html) of the [*Sentencing Act *1997](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/sa1997121/) and the concept of the *"due execution of his duty"*. His Honour said, at \[22\], that the latter

*"\...is a narrow concept and one which is referable to the nature of the function that the police officer is performing, rather than a temporal consideration."*

144 In considering whether or not the police officer is executing a duty it is irrelevant whether they are on duty or able to be on duty or acting in the course of their duty. What is necessary is to identify the particular duty the police officer is performing and the acts that are being undertaken to execute that duty. The assault must occur while those acts in execution of the identified duty are being performed."

Definition should be considered broadly per Full Court of the FC in ***R v K (1993) ALR 59*** and cited by ***Fairly in Police v Keates \[2016\] TASMC***

[CASE: Jones v Krushka [2021] TASMC 14]

---

### Chunk 2 — Innes v Weate [1984] Tas R 14 - Cosgrove J definition of duty
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Innes v Weate - Cosgrove J definition of constable's duty] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Definition of "duty" of constable] [CONCEPTS: Innes v Weate, constable duty, prevent crime, protect life, protect property] [CASE: Innes v Weate [1984] TASRp 3; [1984] Tas R 14]

This chunk covers: Cosgrove J's definition of a constable's duty from Innes v Weate.

*"The word "duty" does not refer, to the constable's duty to obey superior officers. It refers to the duty of constables generally -- the duty to prevent and detect crime and to apprehend wrongdoers, to keep the peace, and to protect life and property (i.e. to protect persons from injury and property from damage). In Rice v Connolly [\[1966\] 2 QB 414](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1966%5d%202%20QB%20414), at 419, Lord Parker CJ said:*

*"It is also in my judgment clear that it is part of the obligations and duties of a police constable to take all steps which appear to him necessary for keeping the peace, for preventing crime or for protecting property from criminal injury. There is no exhaustive definition of the powers and obligations of the police, but they are at least those, and the would further include the duty to detect crime and to bring an offender to justice."*

---

### Chunk 3 — Coates v R [2001] TASSC 141 - Shield vs Sword distinction
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Coates v R - course vs execution of duty - shield and sword] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Distinction between course of duty and execution of duty] [CONCEPTS: Coates v R, execution of duty, course of duty, shield, sword] [CASE: Coates v R [2001] TASSC 141]

This chunk covers: the distinction between acting in the course of duty and acting in the execution of duty as stated in Coates v R.

*"But his capacity to engage in a particular action is defined by statute or common law powers. It is the nature or form of that particular action or response which constitutes 'execution of duty' rather than 'course of duty. The distinction can be described as being as that between "a shield" and "a sword". In executing a particular duty, he is said to be protected (by way of particular sanction) from obstruction."*

---

### Chunk 4 — Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64 - Narrow concept of execution
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Tasmania v Gladwin - narrow concept of due execution of duty] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: "due execution of his duty" narrow and function-based] [CONCEPTS: Gladwin, due execution, on duty, function-based, sentencing s16A] [CASE: Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64]

This chunk covers: Brett J's observation that "due execution of his duty" is a narrow concept referable to the function performed by the officer.

*"\...is a narrow concept and one which is referable to the nature of the function that the police officer is performing, rather than a temporal consideration."*

---

### Chunk 5 — Wilkie v Bennett [2021] TASMC 1 - Execution of Duty summary
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Wilkie v Bennett - Execution of Duty summary and Re K reference] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Scope of police officer's duty and when officer acts in execution of duty] [CONCEPTS: Wilkie v Bennett, Innes v Weate, McHugh J, Re K, execution of duty, lawful task] [CASE: Wilkie v Bennett [2021] TASMC 1]

This chunk covers: summary of the scope of a police officer's duty and when an officer acts in the execution of duty (Wilkie v Bennett).

70. The scope of a police officer's duty involves a very wide discretion to (at least) take all steps reasonably necessary to prevent and detect crime, to apprehend wrongdoers, to keep the peace, and to protect life and property[^\[8\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2021/1.html#fn8). In *Innes v Weate**[^\[9\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2021/1.html#fn9) *Cosgrove J held that the duty:

    - \- involves a wide discretion to act swiftly and decisively in the interests of liberty, to intervene when it is properly called for and reasonably carried out;

    - \- arises from the law and is circumscribed by it;

    - \- is to prevent and detect crime, to apprehend wrongdoers, to keep the peace, and to protect life and property, (that is, to protect persons from injury and property from damage);

    - \- includes all steps which appear to him necessary for keeping the peace, for preventing crime or for protecting property from criminal injury; and

    - \- depends upon a reasonable assessment by the constable of the given situation.

71. In *Coleman v Power*[^\[10\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2021/1.html#fn10)* *at \[117\]-\[127\], McHugh J analysed the cases and considerations relating to police officers *"acting in the execution of* \[their\]* duty"*. He observed that provisions such as the [*Police Offences Act 1935*](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/), [s34B(1)(a)(ii)](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s34b.html) are predicated on the lawfulness of the task being performed by the constable which is said to be connected with her function as a police officer (at \[117\]). McHugh J approved and applied the decision of the Full Court of the Federal Court in *Re K**[^\[11\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2021/1.html#fn11) *in which the court reviewed the authorities on the scope of an officer's duty:

"The effect of all those cases is that a police officer acts in the execution of his duty from the moment he embarks upon a lawful task connected with his functions as a police officer, and continues to act in the execution of that duty for as long as he is engaged in pursuing the task and until it is completed, provided that he does not in the course of the task do anything outside the ambit of his duty so as to cease to be acting therein[^\[12\]^](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2021/1.html#fn12)."

---

### Chunk 6 — Hickman v Richmond [2013] TASMC 36 - Not in execution (intoxication / custody steps)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Hickman v Richmond - Not in execution] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Finding officer not acting in execution of duty due to failures re custody and safety] [CONCEPTS: Hickman v Richmond, execution of duty, intoxication, custody, reasonable steps] [CASE: Hickman v Richmond [2013] TASMC 36]

This chunk covers: circumstances where officer found not to be acting in execution of duty (Hickman v Richmond).

4A Intoxication.

Police did not take reasonable steps, make reasonable inquires as to find a safe place before taking her into custody.

---

### Chunk 7 — Jones v Bacic [2013] TASMC 14 - Knowledge of officer not required
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Reynhoudt principle applied in Jones v Bacic - knowledge not required] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Not necessary to prove defendant knew victim was a police officer] [CONCEPTS: Jones v Bacic, Reynhoudt, knowledge not required, s34B, mistake defence] [CASE: Jones v Bacic [2013] TASMC 14; The Queen v Reynhoudt [1962] HCA 23; Reg v Galvin]

This chunk covers: the principle that it is not necessary for the prosecution to prove the defendant knew the assaulted person was a police officer (Jones v Bacic and Reynhoudt).

5.  *On the High Court authority referred to below, I find that it was not necessary for the prosecution to prove that Mr Bacic knew that Senior Constables Jones and Cranefield were police officers.*

6.  *In the case of The Queen v Reynhoudt [\[1962\] HCA 23](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1962/23.html); [(1962) 107 CLR 381](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281962%29%20107%20CLR%20381), the High Court (Taylor**[^\[1\]^](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2013/14.html#fn1), Menzies**[^\[2\]^](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2013/14.html#fn2) and Owen**[^\[3\]^](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2013/14.html#fn3) JJ; Dixon CJ and Kitto J dissenting) dealt with the [Crimes Act 1958](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/vic/consol_act/ca195882/) (Vict.) [s.40](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/vic/consol_act/ca195882/s40.html), which provided relevantly that*

*Whosoever \... assaults, resists or wilfully obstructs a member of the police force in the due execution of his duty \... shall be guilty of a misdemeanour. *[*^\[4\]^*](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASMC/2013/14.html#fn4)

7.  *That provision is not, for present purposes, materially any different to the [Police Offences Act 1935](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/), [s.34B(1)(b)](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s34b.html) to warrant any departure from the principle stated by the High Court in Reynhoudt. That principle is applicable to this charge.*

8.  *The High Court in Reynhoudt approved the full court of the Victorian Supreme Court in Reg v Galvin (No.1) [\[1961\] VicRp 113](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/vic/VicRp/1961/113.html); [\[1961\] VR 733](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1961%5d%20VR%20733) and overruled Reg. v Galvin (No.2) [\[1961\] VicRp 114](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/vic/VicRp/1961/114.html); [\[1961\] VR 740.](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1961%5d%20VR%20740) In the earlier Galvin case, the full court of the Victorian Supreme Court held that in order to substantiate a charge of assault on a police officer, it was sufficient for the Crown to prove an assault upon a person who was proved to have been at the time of the assault a member of the police force then acting in the due execution of his duty as such. That decision however conceded the right of a person charged with such an offence to exculpate himself by establishing "on the balance of probabilities that he acted reasonably in the bone fide belief, based on reasonable grounds, that the police officer was in fact unlawfully assaulting or otherwise interfering with him\... And that, had the facts been as the accused supposed to be, his own actions would have been lawful and innocent": Reynhoudt per Taylor J at 391.*

*For cases where the principle in Reynhoudt is applied, see R v De Simone [\[2008\] VSCA 216](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/vic/VSCA/2008/216.html) at [\[56\]](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/vic/VSCA/2008/216.html#para56); In DPP v Bone [\[2005\] NSWSC 1239](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/nsw/NSWSC/2005/1239.html) at [\[40\]](https://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/nsw/NSWSC/2005/1239.html#para40).*

---

### Chunk 8 — Asportation: Kidnapping vs False Imprisonment (Davis v R; R v Wellard)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Davis v R - Asportation element - R v Wellard example] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Asportation requirement for kidnapping vs false imprisonment] [CONCEPTS: asportation, kidnapping, false imprisonment, Davis v R, R v Wellard] [CASE: Davis v R [2006] NSWCCA 392; R v Wellard [1978] 3 All ER 161]

This chunk covers: the distinction between kidnapping and false imprisonment hinging on asportation and how much movement is required.

32 It has been stated that what distinguishes kidnapping from false imprisonment is the additional element of asportation , that is, the carrying away of the victim from a place: see eg *Smith v State,* Wisconsin Supreme Court, Orton J, 1885, reported in (1885) 6 *Criminal Law Magazine* 708. Without this element, false imprisonment and kidnapping would be entirely coextensive; see "Kidnapping and the Element of Asportation " (1962) 35 *Southern California Law Review* 212 at 216.

33 However, the** **asportation  did not have to be very great. In *R v Wellard* \[1978\] 3 All ER 161, the appellant had convinced the female victim that he was a police officer and she walked with him to his car and got in it on this basis. At 163 Lawton LJ stated:

As Cairns LJ said in *R v Reid* (\[1972\] 2 All ER 1350 at 1353, \[1973\] QB 299 at 303)\...the essence of the matter is the carrying from a place\...All that has to be proved is the false imprisonment, the deprivation of liberty coupled with a carrying away from the place where the victim wants to be. It may be that in some circumstances the movement would not be sufficient in the estimation of the jury to amount to a carrying away. Every case has to be considered on its own facts. In this case the victim was carried away by the appellant for no less than 100 yards and put into a motor car. In our judgment, there was ample evidence that the victim was carried away from the place where she wanted to be\...

### Chunk 12 — FIREARMS Disposal and Firearms Act 1996 - s88 and s149 (quoted)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Firearms Act 1996] [SECTION: s88; s149] [CITATION: Firearms Act 1996 s88 & s149 - seizure and disposal provisions] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Seizure of firearms for non-compliant storage and disposal of surrendered/seized firearms] [CONCEPTS: s88 seizure, s149 disposal, forfeiture, magistrate orders, seized firearms, prosecutor limits]

This chunk covers: statutory provisions for seizure (s88) and disposal (s149) of firearms and a prosecutorial note re disposal requests.

FIREARMS Disposal:

As a prosecutor (non-cop), can only ask for it on conviction for a s 2(B) or Part 5 offence -- otherwise leave it to Firearm services.

**FIREARMS ACT 1996 - SECT 88**

**88. Seizure of firearms if storage requirements not met**

A police officer may seize any firearm, firearm part, ammunition or ordnance that the police officer has reasonable grounds to believe is not being kept in accordance with this Part.

**FIREARMS ACT 1996 - SECT 149**

**149. Disposal of surrendered or seized firearms**

\(1\) A police officer or a person who claims to be the owner of any firearm or ammunition surrendered or seized under this Act may apply to a magistrate for an order under this section.

\(2\) A magistrate may order that the firearm or ammunition--

\(a\) is forfeited to the Crown; or

\(b\) is to be returned to the person claiming to be the owner of the firearm or ammunition; or

\(c\) is to be disposed of in any manner the magistrate determines.

(2A) A magistrate may make an order under subsection (2)(a) if satisfied that --

\(a\) the owner of the firearm or ammunition --

\(i\) is not authorised by or under this Act to be in possession of the firearm or ammunition; and

\(ii\) has failed to provide an alternative method of disposal for the firearm or ammunition within a reasonable time; or

\(b\) the whereabouts of the owner of the firearm or ammunition has not been, and is not likely to be, ascertained by reasonable inquiry; or

\(c\) the owner of the firearm or ammunition has failed to comply with the requirements of this Act in relation to that firearm or ammunition.

(2B) If a person is convicted of an offence against any one or more of the following provisions, the firearm used in relation to the offence is to be forfeited to the Crown:

(a) [section 111](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fa1996102/s111.html);

(b) [section 112](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fa1996102/s112.html);

(c) [section 113(2)](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fa1996102/s113.html);

(d) [section 114](http://www.austli.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fa1996102/s114.html);

(e) [section 14B(2A)(a)](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/s14b.html) of the [Police Offences Act 1935](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/).

\(3\) If a person is convicted of an offence under Part 5 in respect of which any firearm or ammunition has been seized under [section 88](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fa1996102/s88.html), the court, in addition to imposing any other penalty, may order that the seized firearm or ammunition is forfeited to the Crown.

(3A) Any firearm or ammunition forfeited to the Crown under subsection (3) is to be disposed of as the Commissioner determines.

\(4\) .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  

\(5\) The Auditor-General is to arrange for an independent audit of all firearms or ammunition disposed of under this Act to be carried out once every year.

\(6\) A report of any audit carried out under subsection (5) is to be tabled in both Houses of Parliament.

\(7\) In this section --

ammunition includes a projectile falling within paragraph (b) of the definition of ordnance in [section 3](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fa1996102/s3.html);

firearm includes a weapon falling within paragraph (a) of the definition of ordnance in [section 3.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/fa1996102/s3.html)

### Chunk 16 — Evidence Types: Circumstantial evidence doctrine (Doney v R)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Doney v R - doctrine on circumstantial evidence] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Circumstantial evidence standard and exclusion of reasonable hypotheses] [CONCEPTS: circumstantial evidence, Doney v R, reasonable hypotheses, burden, Howlett v Budd, Betancur-Galvis] [CASE: Doney v R [1990] HCA 51; Howlett v Budd; R v Betancur-Galvis]

This chunk covers: doctrinal statement on circumstantial evidence and the requirement to exclude all other reasonable hypotheses.

\[8\] Circumstantial evidence is evidence which proves or tends to prove a fact or set of facts from which the fact to be proved may be inferred. Circumstantial evidence can prove a fact beyond reasonable doubt only if all other reasonable hypotheses are excluded. **Doney v R \[1990\] HCA 51; (1990) 171 CLR 207**

Circumstantial evidence pointing towards the guilt of the defendant. In order for such evidence to prove a fact beyond reasonable doubt, that conclusion is only open if all other reasonable hypotheses consistent with innocence are excluded: see *Doney v The Queen* [\[1990\] HCA 51](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1990/51.html); [\[1990\] 171 CLR 207](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1990%5d%20171%20CLR%20207) at 211. Guilt must be the only hypothesis reasonably open.

Any such explanation or hypothesis must be more than fanciful or far-fetched. The hypothesis, to be reasonable, must possess some degree of acceptability or credibility on the evidence before me. (See *Howlett v Budd* [\[2003\] TASSC 49)](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2003/49.html)

Even if the court is unconvinced by the evidence of the defendant - being his broad, simple, blanket denial of any knowledge of involvement -- the court must still be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt of the prosecution case before It can convict. As I have said, the defendant bears no onus to advance a reasonable hypothesis consistent with innocence. (See *R v Betancur-Galvis *[\[2003\] NSWCCA 333](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/nsw/NSWCCA/2003/333.html); [\[2003\] 142 A Crim R 527).](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b2003%5d%20142%20A%20Crim%20R%20527)

---

### Chunk 17 — Circumstantial DNA Evidence: Roughan v Haines [2019] TASMC 7
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Roughan v Haines [2019] TASMC 7 - circumstantial DNA evidence] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Approach to DNA evidence in circumstantial cases and assessing deposition and timing] [CONCEPTS: DNA evidence, circumstantial, Roughan v Haines, Fitzgerald v The Queen, reasonable hypothesis, assessment] [CASE: Roughan v Haines [2019] TASMC 7]

This chunk covers: assessment of circumstantial DNA evidence and its weight in a criminal case (Roughan v Haines).

\[15\] In that regard, even if the defendant's evidence itself does not itself create a reasonable doubt, I must necessarily assess the DNA evidence with care. Where DNA material is deposited, and how it is deposited must be considered: see *Fitzgerald v The Queen* [\[2014\] HCA 23](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2014/23.html) as an example, albeit one with a very different factual framework. Here, however, the nature of the sample and its position, and when it was found is such that, absent some other evidence, in my view no reasonable hypothesis consistent with innocence is apparent to me that would cause me to have any reasonable doubt as to the defendant's guilt.

In this it must be steadfastly kept in mind that the Def Haines bears no onus.

\[17\] In considering that circumstantial evidence against the defendant in this case, I must consider all of it combined, and I must consider "the weight which is to be given to the united force of all the circumstances put together":. See *Belhaven v Stenton Peerage* [\[1875\] 1 APP Cas 278](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1875%5d%201%20AC%20278) at 279 per Lord Cairns as recently approved of by Blow CJ in *Allen v Brown* [\[2016\] TASSC 22](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/tas/TASSC/2016/22.html) at 12; see also *Chamberlain v The Queen* (No 2) [\[1984\] HCA 7](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1984/7.html); [\[1984\] 153 CLR 521](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1984%5d%20153%20CLR%20521) at 535 and 599. As was found to be the correct position by Blow, CJ in *Allen v Brown*, this is a "strands in a cable" and not a case where it is necessary to identify intermediate facts that constitute "indispensable links in a chain of reasoning towards an inference of guilt": see *Shepherd v The Queen* [\[1990\] HCA 56](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1990/56.html); [\[1990\] 170 CLR 573.](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1990%5d%20170%20CLR%20573)

---

### Chunk 18 — Circumstantial evidence "long thread" - R v Edwards [1993] HCA 63
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: R v Edwards [1993] HCA 63 - lies and consciousness of guilt] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Post-offence lies as evidence of consciousness of guilt and standard of proof] [CONCEPTS: Edwards, lies, consciousness of guilt, standard of proof, circumstantial evidence] [CASE: R v Edwards [1993] HCA 63]

This chunk covers: High Court guidance on how lies/post-offence conduct may be used in the circumstantial case.

The jury do not have to conclude that the accused is guilty beyond reasonable doubt in order to accept that a lie told by him exhibits a consciousness of guilt. They may accept that evidence without applying any particular standard of proof and conclude that, when they consider it together with the other evidence, the accused is or is not guilty beyond reasonable doubt.

**It is considered together**

### Chunk 19 — Post-offence conduct as circumstantial evidence - Pickett v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 12
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Pickett v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 12 - flight evidence] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Significance of flight and immediate reaction as circumstantial evidence] [CONCEPTS: flight evidence, Pickett, circumstantial strength, immediate reaction, police pursuit] [CASE: Pickett v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 12]

This chunk covers: how flight evidence and immediate reactions during police pursuit can be cogent circumstantial evidence (Pickett v Tasmania).

*\[44\] These comments and principles should be applied by this Court when considering the significance of the flight evidence. While there were other potential reasons for flight by the appellant, and the evidence of itself could not have supported a finding of guilt, the immediacy and violence of his reaction upon seeing the police cars, and the desperation demonstrated by him during the police chase, were cogent pieces of circumstantial evidence to be taken into account with the other evidence presented by the prosecution. The strength of the circumstantial case will depend upon the combined force of that evidence.*

---

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: R v Baden-Clay [2016] HCA 35 - alternative hypothesis threshold] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Requirement that inference rests on more than conjecture; reasonable hypothesis test] [CONCEPTS: Baden-Clay, reasonable hypothesis, alternative explanation, HCA, inference standard] [CASE: R v Baden-Clay [2016] HCA 35]

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Exhibit D4 - text messages found on arrested defendant's phone] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Example of circumstantial exhibit - text messages on mobile] [CONCEPTS: exhibit, text messages, circumstantial, mobile phone, possession]

Circumstances:

1.  Exhibit D4 was a record of some text messages that were found on a mobile telephone that was in the def, McLachlan\'s possession when he was arrested, which was tendered as an exhibit by Jackson, a co-accused.

---

<!-- block_042 master -->

# Lies as consciousness of guilt (general doctrine)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: lies as evidence of consciousness of guilt] [CONCEPTS: lie as evidence, consciousness of guilt, Edwards direction, deliberate untruth, relevance of lies, innocent explanation, corroboration of lies, circumstantial evidence, jury direction]

Lies relied upon by a prosecution may be admissible as evidence of a defendant’s consciousness of guilt only if specific conditions are satisfied. The prosecutor must identify the precise statements said to be lies and the factual basis for treating each as a deliberate untruth. The established prerequisites are: (1) the statement must be a deliberate untruth (not merely an inconsistent account, confusion or mistake); (2) the lie must concern some circumstance or event connected with the offence; (3) the lie must have been told because the defendant realised that the truthful account would implicate him in the offence (and not only in a lesser or unrelated wrongdoing); and (4) the jury must be directed about possible innocent explanations for the lie (e.g. shame, panic, confusion, protecting another, intending to avoid embarrassment or other non-criminal motives). If any prerequisite is not satisfied, the lie should not be used as affirmative evidence of guilt. When the prosecution’s case treats lies as substantive evidence of guilt, the trial judge may need to give an Edwards-style direction to prevent the jury from misusing the evidence.

---

# Judicial requirement to identify precisely the lies relied upon
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: specificity of lies relied on by prosecution] [CONCEPTS: identify lies, pinpoint statements, basis for inference, jury direction, prosecution particulars, independent proof, corroboration]

Before allowing the jury to use a purported lie as evidence of guilt, the judge must require the prosecution to identify with precision which statements are relied upon as lies and the particular basis on which each statement is said to implicate the defendant in the charged offence rather than a lesser or different offence. The judge should ensure the prosecution explains what independent evidence (if any) demonstrates the statement is untrue and why the untruth bears on the core criminal allegation. This specificity assists the judge in assessing admissibility and allows the defence to respond, including by offering innocent explanations or proof that the statement was mistaken rather than deliberate.

---

# Requirement that the statement be a deliberate untruth
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: deliberate untruth as element for consciousness of guilt] [CONCEPTS: deliberate lie, mistaken account, confusion, intentionality, mens rea, credibility, evidence threshold, jury instruction]

A statement can be used as evidence of consciousness of guilt only if it is established that the defendant told a deliberate untruth; a mere discrepancy, inconsistency or imperfect recollection is insufficient. The trial judge must be satisfied that, on the evidence, the untruth was deliberate — that the defendant intended to deceive — rather than the product of confusion, faulty memory, stress, or an innocent misstatement. If the evidence does not permit a finding of deliberate falsehood, the statement must not be treated as probative of consciousness of guilt.

---

# Requirement that the lie relate to a circumstance connected with the offence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: relevance of lies to offence] [CONCEPTS: nexus to offence, relevance, probative value, contextual connection, timing of lie, causation of inference, circumstantial link]

A statement relied upon as a lie must concern some circumstance or event connected with the alleged offence in order to be probative of consciousness of guilt. The judge must consider the temporal, causal and content connection between the lie and the offending: if the subject matter of the lie is not sufficiently linked to an element or a critical circumstance of the offence, then its probative value as evidence of guilt is weak and it should not be used for that purpose.

---

# Requirement that the lie be told because truth would implicate defendant in the charged offence (not a lesser offence)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: motive for lying — realisation of implication] [CONCEPTS: motive to lie, conscious realisation, implicate in offence, excluding lesser offence, mens rea inference, jury caution, alternative motives]

To treat a lie as evidence of consciousness of guilt the court must be satisfied the defendant lied for the reason that the truthful account would have implicated him in the commission of the charged offence. The lie must be connected to a realisation that the truth could convict him of that offence; if the defendant’s motive was to conceal involvement in a lesser offence or to avoid some other non-criminal consequence, the lie does not legitimately support an inference of guilt of the charged offence. The judge should so instruct the jury and, if appropriate, explain how lies may be consistent with motives unrelated to guilt of the charged crime.

---

# Innocent explanations for lying — necessity of directing the jury
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: innocent explanations for lies] [CONCEPTS: shame, panic, confusion, embarrassment, protecting another, attempted exculpation, jury instruction, alternative motives]

If there is evidence that an innocent explanation could account for an apparent lie (for example, to hide shameful but non-criminal conduct, out of panic, to bolster an otherwise weak claim, to protect another person, or to avoid embarrassment), the judge must draw the jury’s attention to those possible explanations. Where such explanations are reasonably open on the evidence, the jury must be warned not to infer guilt simply because the defendant gave a false or misleading account. The prosecution bears the onus of establishing that the lie was motivated by consciousness of guilt rather than some innocent cause.

---

# Use of a lie to corroborate a witness — requirement of independent proof
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: lie as corroboration] [CONCEPTS: corroboration, accomplice corroboration, independent evidence, demonstration of falsehood, evidentiary independence, standards of proof, jury instruction]

When a prosecution relies on an alleged lie to corroborate the testimony of a particular witness (for example, an accomplice or informant), the law requires that the assertion be clearly shown to be false by evidence independent of the witness whose testimony the lie is intended to support. The judge should identify precisely which independent evidence demonstrates the falsity of the statement and warn the jury that, absent such independent proof, the alleged lie cannot be used to support corroboration of that witness’s evidence.

---

# When the lie is the only or a critical fact in the prosecution’s circumstantial case — higher threshold
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: lie as sole or critical evidence] [CONCEPTS: sole evidence, critical link, circumstantial case, beyond reasonable doubt, heightened satisfaction, Edwards-type safeguard, jury caution]

If the alleged lie is the only evidence against the accused or forms an indispensable link in the chain of circumstantial evidence, additional caution is required: the jury must be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt not only that the defendant lied, but also that he lied because he realised that the truth would implicate him in the offence charged. In such cases the evidentiary weight placed on the lie must be carefully considered and the trial judge should give a clear direction that conviction cannot rest on the lie unless that double satisfaction is reached.

---

# Edwards direction — circumstances for giving and judicial cautions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Edwards direction and judicial caution] [CONCEPTS: Edwards direction, judicial caution, jury misuse, risk of confusion, when to direct, enquiry of prosecutor, Edwards prerequisites]

An Edwards direction (originating from Edwards v The Queen) is a trial judge’s instruction emphasising that a lie may be explicable by innocent motives and cautioning the jury about treating lying as evidence of guilt. As a general rule, an Edwards direction should be given only where the prosecution contends the lie is evidence of guilt because the accused knew the truth would implicate him in the offence. Courts have stressed circumspection: the direction is required only where there is a real danger the jury will wrongly conclude the lie itself proves guilt. If there is risk of misunderstanding, the trial judge should enquire of the prosecutor how the lie is being relied upon before deciding whether to give the direction.

---

### Authority - Edwards v The Queen (1993) HCA 63; (1993) 178 CLR 193
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Edwards v The Queen [1993] HCA 63; (1993) 178 CLR 193] [TOPIC: Edwards direction — lies as evidence of consciousness of guilt] [CONCEPTS: Edwards direction, lie as evidence, HCA authority, deliberate untruth, jury caution, consciousness of guilt]

Edwards v The Queen establishes the high‑level principle that a lie told by an accused may be used as evidence of consciousness of guilt only where there is independent evidence showing the statement is false and where the lie was told because the accused knew the truth would implicate him in the offence. The case underpins the Edwards direction: judges must warn juries about innocent explanations for lies and only allow lies to be used as probative of guilt where the necessary inferences are properly supported by the evidence.

---

### Authority - Zoneff (2000) 200 CLR 234
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Zoneff (2000) 200 CLR 234] [TOPIC: use of Edwards direction; risk of jury misunderstanding] [CONCEPTS: Edwards direction, jury confusion, prosecutor’s case, judicial inquiry, limits on direction, HCA commentary]

Zoneff clarifies that an Edwards direction may be required where there is a risk the jury will misunderstand how a lie is being used by the prosecution, even where the prosecution does not expressly rely on the lie as proof of guilt. The decision indicates the judge should ask the prosecutor how the lies are being advanced and cautions that the direction is necessary only if there is a real danger of the jury drawing an improper inference from the lie.

---

### Authority - Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2] [TOPIC: application of Edwards-style analysis to post‑offence lies] [CONCEPTS: Shaw, Edwards-style lies, CCTV timing, insurance lie, independent evidence of falsehood, consciousness of guilt, Tasmanian appeal court]

Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 applied Edwards principles to a fire‑related prosecution in which the Crown relied on multiple post‑fire statements by the appellant as lies. The Court accepted that independent evidence (CCTV, arrival times, insurance correspondence) could establish falsity of certain statements and that repeated assertions inconsistent with independently verifiable facts could support an inference the appellant lied because the truth would have placed him at the scene when the fire started. The case illustrates how temporal and documentary evidence may independently demonstrate falsity and support an Edwards‑style inference.

---

### Authority - Whittaker v Tasmania [2006] TASSC 26
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Whittaker v Tasmania [2006] TASSC 26] [TOPIC: application/discussion of lies as evidence] [CONCEPTS: Whittaker, Tasmanian authority, lies evidence, conscious of guilt, appellate discussion]

Whittaker v Tasmania includes appellate discussion relevant to the use of lies as evidence of consciousness of guilt and is an example of Tasmanian authority considering how and when post‑offence statements may be treated by a jury.

---

# Use of prosecution’s lies allegation to support corroboration — procedural safeguards
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: independent proof when lie used to corroborate witness] [CONCEPTS: corroboration, independent proof, accomplice evidence, critical evidence, identification of independent sources, jury caution]

When the prosecution relies on an alleged lie to corroborate the evidence of a particular witness, the judge must identify the independent evidence (other than the witness whose testimony the lie is said to corroborate) that shows the defendant has lied. The jury must be directed that the statement is not proof of corroboration unless the falsity of the statement is clearly established by such independent evidence.

---

# Practical direction where lesser offence is open or charged
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: lies and potential lesser offences] [CONCEPTS: lesser offence, alternative motive, limiting direction, jury instruction, nexus to charged offence]

If a lesser offence is open or charged, the judge should direct the jury that a lie cannot be used as evidence of consciousness of guilt of the more serious charged offence if the lie may have been told to conceal involvement in the lesser offence. The jury must be warned that an explanation pointing to a lesser wrongdoing undermines the inference that the lie demonstrates guilt of the greater offence.

---

# Inference from failure to give evidence — Weissensteiner principle (general)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: failure to give evidence — drawing inferences] [CONCEPTS: failure to testify, Weissensteiner, facts in accused’s knowledge, circumstantial inference, reasonable expectation to deny, strengthening inference, jury use]

The Weissensteiner principle permits a jury to draw stronger inferences of guilt from proved facts when an accused elects not to give evidence regarding facts which can be readily perceived to be within his knowledge. The permissible use of the accused’s silence is confined to strengthening an inference of guilt arising from the proved circumstances; it cannot by itself create a primary inference of guilt where none reasonably exists. The judge should instruct the jury that silence may only be used in relation to facts reasonably perceived as within the accused’s knowledge.

---

### Authority - Weissensteiner (1993) 178 CLR 217
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Weissensteiner (1993) 178 CLR 217] [TOPIC: drawing inferences from accused’s silence] [CONCEPTS: Weissensteiner principle, failure to give evidence, facts in knowledge, circumstantial evidence, limitations on inference]

Weissensteiner holds that where crucial facts are such that an explanation could only come from the accused, the jury may more readily draw inferences adverse to the accused when he does not give evidence. The majority upheld that the trial judge did not err in directing that greater weight could be given to the prosecution’s inferences when the accused elected not to give evidence about facts plainly within his knowledge.

---

# Limitations — cannot infer state of mind from silence (B v Visser)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: limitation on Weissensteiner — no inferences of state of mind] [CONCEPTS: state of mind, Weissensteiner inapplicable, Visser, limitation, circumstantial inference, evidence boundaries]

The Weissensteiner principle does not permit drawing inferences about an accused person’s state of mind from their failure to give evidence. In B v Visser [2000] TASSC 141 the court held that Weissensteiner applies to inferences of guilt drawn from circumstantial facts, not to speculative inferences about mental states; magistrates and judges must not treat silence as proof of subjective mental elements.

---

### Authority - B v Visser [2000] TASSC 141
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: B v Visser [2000] TASSC 141] [TOPIC: limitation on application of Weissensteiner] [CONCEPTS: Weissensteiner limitation, state of mind, Tasmanian authority, appellate guidance, evidentiary limits]

B v Visser is a Tasmanian authority holding that the Weissensteiner principle does not extend to drawing inferences about an accused’s state of mind; it is confined to inferences of guilt from circumstantial evidence.

---

### Authority - Pickett v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 12
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Pickett v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 12] [TOPIC: application of Weissensteiner to joint charges and failure to give evidence] [CONCEPTS: Pickett, Tasmanian CCA, failure to give evidence, alternative hypotheses, facts solely within accused’s knowledge, circumstantial improbability]

Pickett v Tasmania considered the application of Weissensteiner where the accused was jointly charged and an alternative innocent hypothesis would have required facts solely within the appellant’s knowledge. The Court held the improbability of the innocent hypothesis, and the absence of evidence supporting it, favoured treating the lack of explanation as undermining the plausibility of that hypothesis.

---

# No case to answer — classic test (May v O’Sullivan)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: no case to answer test] [CONCEPTS: no case submission, legal question, May v O'Sullivan, could lawfully be convicted, judge’s function, review standard]

The classic legal test for whether there is “no case to answer” is whether, at the close of the prosecution case, there is evidence such that the defendant could lawfully be convicted; the question is not whether the defendant ought to be convicted but whether a conviction could properly be entered on the evidence. This is a question of law for the judge to decide.

---

# No case to answer in circumstantial cases — DPP (No 2 of 1993) and principles
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: no case in circumstantial evidence] [CONCEPTS: circumstantial evidence, DPP No 2, reasonable mind, exclude competing hypotheses, capacity in law, inferences favourable to prosecution]

In cases based solely or primarily on circumstantial evidence, the no‑case test requires that, even if all prosecution evidence were accepted and all inferences most favourable to the prosecution which are reasonably open were drawn, a reasonable mind must be capable of convicting beyond reasonable doubt. If the evidence is incapable in law of supporting a conviction because it cannot exclude reasonable hypotheses consistent with innocence, then there is no case to answer. The judge’s function is not to choose between reasonably open inferences but to ask whether a reasonable mind could convict on the material.

---

### Authority - Case Stated by DPP (No 2 of 1993) [1993] SASC 4152; (1993) 70 A Crim R 323
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Case Stated by DPP (No 2 of 1993) [1993] SASC 4152; (1993) 70 A Crim R 323] [TOPIC: test for no case to answer in circumstantial cases] [CONCEPTS: circumstantial test, reasonable mind, exclude hypotheses, King CJ, legal capacity of evidence]

Case Stated by DPP (No 2 of 1993) (per King CJ) sets out that where circumstantial evidence is relied upon, the test is whether that evidence, if accepted, is capable of producing in a reasonable mind a conclusion of guilt beyond reasonable doubt and is capable of excluding competing hypotheses as unreasonable. If not, there is no case to answer.

---

### Authority - May v O’Sullivan [1955] HCA 38; (1955) 92 CLR 654
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: May v O’Sullivan [1955] HCA 38; (1955) 92 CLR 654] [TOPIC: classic no-case test] [CONCEPTS: no case to answer, could lawfully be convicted, legal question, judge’s role, closing of prosecution case]

May v O’Sullivan articulates the fundamental proposition that the “no case to answer” submission requires the judge to consider whether, on the evidence as it stands at the close of the prosecution case, the accused could lawfully be convicted — a question of law.

---

### Authority - Doney v The Queen (1990) 171 CLR 207
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Doney v The Queen (1990) 171 CLR 207] [TOPIC: circumstantial evidence guidance] [CONCEPTS: circumstantial evidence, inference drawing, appellate guidance, evidentiary sufficiency, reasonable hypothesis]

Doney v The Queen provides High Court guidance on the approach to circumstantial evidence and the drawing of inferences, relevant to evaluating whether circumstantial material is capable in law of supporting a conviction.

---

# The rule in Browne v Dunn and procedural remedy
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Browne v Dunn rule and s 46 remedy] [CONCEPTS: Browne v Dunn, cross‑examination fairness, s 46 Evidence Act, recall witness, procedural fairness, independent evidence, written instructions]

The rule in Browne v Dunn is a procedural fairness rule requiring that if a party intends to rely on evidence that contradicts a witness’s testimony, that witness should be given an opportunity in cross‑examination to address the contradictory matter. If the rule is breached, remedies include allowing recall of the witness to give evidence about the new matter, which can be done under a statutory provision such as s 46 of the Evidence Act (when leave to recall is necessary). Compliance with the rule protects the accused’s right to test adverse evidence and avoid unfair surprise.

---

### Authority - Browne v Dunn (1894) 6 R 67
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Browne v Dunn (1894) 6 R 67] [TOPIC: rule in Browne v Dunn — cross‑examination fairness] [CONCEPTS: Browne v Dunn, cross‑examination, fairness, contradiction, opportunity to respond, procedural common law rule]

Browne v Dunn establishes that a witness should be put the case to meet on cross‑examination; a party cannot later lead evidence on a matter which was not put to the witness without explanation or opportunity to answer, otherwise unfair surprise can occur.

---

### Authority - R v Birks (1990) 19 NSWLR 677; R v Liristis [2004] NSWCCA 287
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Birks (1990) 19 NSWLR 677; R v Liristis [2004] NSWCCA 287] [TOPIC: applications and consequences of Browne v Dunn] [CONCEPTS: Birks, Liristis, Browne v Dunn consequences, recall witness, cross‑examination practice, NSW authorities]

R v Birks and R v Liristis provide detailed discussion and application of the Browne v Dunn principle and illustrate potential remedies and consequences when the rule is breached.

---

# Procedural practice note — using written client instructions to comply with Browne v Dunn
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: practical compliance with Browne v Dunn] [CONCEPTS: written instructions, client statement, cross‑examination preparation, disclosure, avoid surprise, defence preparation, evidence planning]

One practical method to reduce risk of breaching Browne v Dunn is to obtain and rely on written instructions from the client early in the case; a client’s written account helps counsel identify matters that might contradict witnesses’ evidence and ensures those matters are raised in cross‑examination or otherwise brought to the court’s attention.

---

Lies as consciousness of guilt:

First, you must be satisfied that the defendant has told a deliberate untruth. There is a difference between the mere rejection of a person's account of events and a finding that the person has lied. In many cases, where there appears to be a departure from the truth, It may not be possible to say that a dellhen3te Ile has been ft-4d. In such an eventuality the judge should precisely identify the evidence (independent of the witness whose evidence is said to be supported by the lie) which shows that the defendant has lied.]*

*[If the lie relied upon by the prosecution is the only evidence against the defendant, or is an indispensable link in a chain of evidence necessary to prove guilt then the following direction must be given.?]*

Finally, in this case the alleged lie is the only evidence against the defendant \[or is a critical fact in the prosecution\'s circumstantial case against him\]. ### Chunk 2 — Edwards direction authorities and cautionary notes
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Edwards and cautionary authorities - practitioner note] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Authorities and caution on giving an Edwards direction re lies] [CONCEPTS: Edwards v The Queen, Zoneff, Edwards direction, jury caution, post-offence conduct, flight and concealment]

Tactical workflow for deciding whether to give an Edwards-style direction and necessary inquiries of prosecution.

*As a general rule an Edwards ((1993) 178 CLR 193) direction should only be given if the prosecution contends that a lie is evidence of guilt, in the sense that it was told because "the accused knew that the truth would implicate him in (the commission) of the offence": Edwards, 211, 363, as explained is Zoneff (2000) 200 CLR 234 fin See also R v Hennig \[2010\] QCA 244 and R v Sheppard \[2010\] QCA 342. Courts of Appeal have warned of the need for circumspection and care in the use of this direction: Brennan \[1999\] 2 Qd 529, 531; Walton and Hannan \[2001\] QCA 309 \[61\]; R v Dykstra \[2011\] QCA 175 \[13\]. See Chang (2003) 7 VR 236 as to the circumstances whether as Edwards direction should be given concerning post offence conduct, particularly flight and concealment, where that conduct is relied upon by the prosecution as evidence of guilt or is likely to be used by the jury as such. Sec Direction No. 48. An Edwards direction may be required if that is a risk of misunderstanding on the part of the jury as to the use of lies notwithstanding the prosecution has not relied on the lie as showing a COIISCKAISDCSS of guilt: Zoneff at 244 \[16\]. If there is a risk of confusion as to the way the ps on puts its case, the trial Judge should inquire of the prosecutor as to the way the case is being put: Zoneff at 244 \[17\], R v Frank \[2010\] QCA 150 at \[41\]. However, an Edwards direction will only be required in these circumstances where there is a reel danger that the jury will wrongly conclude that the lie is evidence of guilt: Dhanhoa v R (2003) 217 CLR 1.*

### Chunk 4 — Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 (Excerpts on Edwards-style lies)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 - Evidence of Edwards-style lies] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Shaw v Tasmania — Crown's reliance on lies as showing consciousness of guilt (paras 136–138)] [CONCEPTS: Shaw v Tasmania, Edwards-style lies, CCTV timing, insurance policy lie, consciousness of guilt, Tasmanian Court of Criminal Appeal] [CASE: Shaw v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 2 (7 March 2022)]

Tactical example: detailed excerpt showing how lies were used by Crown to argue consciousness of guilt.

136 At the trial the Crown contended that the appellant had told a number of lies after the fire. It relied on *Edwards v The Queen *[\[1993\] HCA 63](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1993/63.html); [(1993) 178 CLR 193](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281993%29%20178%20CLR%20193), contending that there was independent evidence that assertions made by the appellant were lies, and that his lies were told because he knew that the truth would implicate him in the crimes charged.

137 The CCTV evidence, together with the evidence of the correct time at which the first firefighters arrived, established that the appellant and his companions left the house at 5.58pm. However there was evidence that the appellant told many witnesses that he had left earlier than that. One firefighter, Mr Suhr, gave evidence that he spoke to both Mr Wells and the appellant, and that one of them said they had left at 5.30pm. In the statutory declaration that he made on the night of the fire, the appellant said that he left between 5.30 and 5.45pm. Mr Paramanathan gave evidence that the appellant said he left at approximately 5.30. The appellant told Mr Brent Mills that he had been away from the property between 5.30pm and 6.45pm. In his police interview on 14 July 2016 the appellant said twice that he left at about 5.30 to 5.45pm. The appellant told Ms Webb that he left the house between 5.30 and 5.45pm. The only stated reason for the appellant and Mr Wells leaving the property was that they had to pick up the woman at the airport, whose flight was due to arrive at or after 6.30pm. The Crown contended that the appellant, who had left the property at 5.58pm, repeatedly asserted that he had left significantly earlier because truthful information would put him at the scene of the fire at the time it started.

138 The Crown contended that the appellant lied about not knowing whether his insurance policy was current. As we have said, he phoned Coles Insurance on the night of the fire at 7.18pm and enquired whether his insurance was up to date. A police officer was present for at least part of that conversation. However there was evidence of the phone call on 17 June, ten days before the fire, when the appellant made sure his premium payments were up to date. On 17 June Coles Insurance wrote to him advising that his next payment would be due on 15 July. The letter was tendered as an exhibit. It was reasonable to infer that the appellant would have received it before the fire. The Crown contended that he must have known at the time of the fire that his policy was current, and feigned ignorance in order to create an impression of innocence.

---

### Chunk 5 — Inference of guilt in failure to give evidence (Annotation and procedure)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Failure to give evidence - inference and limits - practitioner note] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: When an accused's failure to give evidence may strengthen circumstantial inferences] [CONCEPTS: failure to give evidence, inference of guilt, Weissensteiner, facts in accused's knowledge, circumstantial evidence, jury direction]

Tactical note on using an accused's silence to strengthen circumstantial inferences.

Inference of guilt in failure to give evidence

Looks like it can be made where there is only a circumstantial case.

There are some cases in which evidence (or an explanation) contradicting an apparently damning inference to be drawn from proven facts could come only from the accused. In the absence of such evidence or explanation, the jury may more readily draw the conclusion which the prosecution seeks.

*The use to which the appellant's failure to give evidence may be put is correctly restricted to the strengthening of an inference of guilt from the facts proved. And the jury is told not to use the appellant's failure to give evidence unless relevant facts 'can be easily perceived to be in his knowledge'. This additional requirement, which follows a decision of the Court of Criminal Appeal of Queensland in Reg v Whinfield37, ensures that the drawing of an inference of guilt will not be assisted by an accused's failure to give evidence unless it is reasonable to expect some denial, explanation or answer by the accused to the prima facie case made against him.*

Court in Weissensteiner. There the prosecution case was that the accused's guilt was to be inferred from circumstances, particularly the unexplained disappearance of those whom it was alleged he had murdered, and his possession of the boat and equipment which they owned and from which they had disappeared while on a voyage with the accused. The majority of the Court held that the trial judge in that case had made no error by directing the jury that they could more safely draw the inferences which the prosecution alleged should be drawn *"when the accused elects not to give evidence of relevant facts which can be easily perceived to be in his knowledge"*

NOTE the above does not apply if the facts said to be in the defendants knowledge assist him in only one or some of a number of charges against him on the same indictment. Cant say he has an obligation to get up there in the box and subject himself to hell of CX against all matters, when he can only defend himself against one or some. **Weissensteiner (1993) 178 CLR 217**

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Pickett v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 12 - accused's failure to give evidence] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Pickett v Tasmania — court discussion on failure to provide evidence for contrary hypothesis] [CONCEPTS: Pickett v Tasmania, failure to give evidence, circumstantial inference, contrary hypothesis, Tasmanian CCA] [CASE: Pickett v Tasmania [2022] TASCCA 12 (20 September 2022)]

**Pickett v Tasmania \[2022\] TASCCA 12 (20 September 2022)**

Def was jointly charged. Appealed conviction on basis that no jury could exclude hypnotises consistent with evidence. Def did not give evidence. Court discussed implication of def having failed to provide evidence to support contrary hypothesis

*\[49\] "In my view, these principles are relevant to the circumstances of this case, a least in respect of the hypothesis advanced by the appellant's counsel. The improbability and highly coincidental nature of any innocent involvement of the accused with the other men in the period intervening between their departure from the property after the crimes and their arrival at Campania meant that if there was such an explanation, the facts relevant to it must be solely within the knowledge of the appellant. The lack of evidence to support such an explanation tells against regarding the explanation as rational or reasonable."*

### Chunk 8 — Limited to inferences of fact — B v Visser [2000] TASSC 141
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: B v Visser [2000] TASSC 141 - limits on Weissensteiner] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: B v Visser — Weissensteiner not applicable to inferences about state of mind] [CONCEPTS: B v Visser, Weissensteiner limits, state of mind, inferences of fact, TASSC 2000] [CASE: B v Visser [2000] TASSC 141]

Tactical note on limits of Weissensteiner.

Limited to inferences of fact 

*B v Visser* \[2000\] TASSC 141

Cannot be uses to infer "state of mind"

*\[8\] In the absence of authority to the contrary, I think it is appropriate that I follow the Queensland Court of Criminal Appeal in Hocking. I hold that the Weissensteiner principle has no application in relation to the drawing of inferences as to an accused person's state of mind, as distinct from inferences of guilt drawn from circumstantial evidence, and that the learned magistrate erred in law in regarding Weissensteiner as applicable to this case.*

### Chunk 9 — No Case to Answer - the Test (May v O'Sullivan and opening statement)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: No case to answer - May v O'Sullivan test - practitioner doctrine] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Test for 'no case to answer' at close of prosecution case] [CONCEPTS: no case to answer, May v O'Sullivan, legal test, close of prosecution, lawful conviction]

Tactical checklist for assessing a 'no case to answer' submission at close of the Crown case.

No Case to Answer - the Test

The classic test as to whether or not a defendant has a case to answer is that expressed *in May v O'Sullivan* [\[1955\] HCA 38](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/1955/38.html); [(1955) 92 CLR 654](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%281955%29%2092%20CLR%20654) at 658:

*"When at the close of the case for the prosecution, a submission is made that there is 'no case to answer', the question to be decided is not whether on the evidence as it stands the defendant ought to be convicted, but whether on the evidence as it stands he COULD lawfully be convicted. This is really a question of law."*

---

### Chunk 10 — No case on a circumstantial case & submissions dependent solely on circumstantial evidence (King CJ / DPP (No 2))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: DPP (No 2 of 1993) - circumstantial evidence test - practitioner doctrine] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Tests for 'no case' where case depends on circumstantial evidence; application and explanation] [CONCEPTS: circumstantial evidence, DPP (No 2), King CJ, capable in law, reasonable mind, exclude hypotheses, Peacock, Doney] [CASE: Case Stated by DPP (No 2 of 1993) (1993) 70 A Crim R 323]

Tactical workflow and doctrinal guidance on 'no case' in circumstantial cases; steps for judge when assessing.

No case on a circumstantial case:

3.  In a case involving circumstantial evidence, the test is not, 'whether' there is evidence which, if accepted would provide evidence of each element of the charge' as held in *Doney v The* Oneen ((1990) 171 CLR 207) oi 'whether upon whole of the evidence it would be open to the jury to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the accused was guilty' as stated by Ipp J in fi v Moi'ris.^3\ (^(1997) 98 A Crim R 408 at 416-17)

4.  The principle to be applied in a circumstantial case was stated by King CJ in

*Case Stated by DPP (No 2 of 1993) ('DPP (No 2)')* at 327:

If the case depends upon circumstantial evidence, and that evidence, if accepted, is *capable* of producing in a reasonable mind a conclusion of guilt beyond reasonable doubt and thus is *capable* of causing a reasonable mind to exclude any competing hypothesis as unreasonable, there is a case to answer.

5.  His Honour, further stated that 'there is no case to answer, only if the evidence is not capable in law of supporting a conviction." In relation to circumstantial cases, His Honour said at 327:

In a cii cumstantial case that implies that even if all the evidence for the prosecution were accepted and all inferences most favourable to the prosecution which are reasonably open were drawn, a reasonable mind could not reach a conclusion of guilt beyond reasonable doubt, or to put it anothcr way, could not exclude all hypotheses consistent with innocence, as not reasonably open on the evidence.

When considering a no case submission, the function of the judge, as stated by King CJ in *('DPP tNo 2)'),* 'is not to choose between the inferences which are i easonably open to the jui\'y' and 'neither it is to decide whethei' any possible hypothesis consistent with innocence are reasonably *open on the* evidence'.^5^ A judge\'s only concern is 'whether a reasonable mind could reach a conclusion of guilt beyond reasonable doubt and therefore exclude any competing hypothesis as not reasonably open on the ev idence.'^6^

Ipp J in fi i Hoi-i is said, 'where the case is based on cii\'cumstantial evidence, the Magistrate is required to talie into account all inferences most favourable to the prosecution which could be reasonably drawn from the primary facts.'^7^

Explaining the meaning of the tern 'evidence not capable in law', and citing

*Gehert, Haley and Black,* Ining CJ said at 327:

If the evidence cannot exclude any i easonable hypothesis consistent with innocence, *Peacock (191 I) 13 CLR 69,* then the evidence is not capable in law of providing the chai\'ge beyond reasonable doubt and there is no case to answer.

17. Thei\'efore, there is a no case submission, only if the evidence lacks capacity to lead a reasonable mind to reject all such hypothesis as unreasonable (emphasis added).' It is the applicant\'s submission that on the evidence adduced by prosecution on the hearing of the charge, there is evidence, and further, that evidence has the capacity to exclude any reasonable hypothesis consistent with innocence. The defendant admitted to taking the inverter and giving his wife the pcrmissioii to sell the inverter. The HR Manager provided business records of all pm chases made by the defendant during the time of his employment. No business i ecords existed for the purchase of the inverter.

18.

# Unavailable witnesses and hearsay (Evidence Act)

## Authority - Ambrosoli (Mason P at [28]) [REVIEW]
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Objective circumstances focus in s 65 assessments] [CONCEPTS: circumstances of making, representation reliability, objective assessment, hearsay admissibility, fabrication risk, Mason P, Ambrosoli]

The rule attributed to Ambrosoli (as cited by Mason P at [28]) is that, for the purposes of Evidence Act s 65, the court's focus should be on the reliability of the prior representation itself rather than directly on the asserted fact to which the representation relates. This principle means that admissibility under s 65(2)(b) or (c) requires an objective inquiry into the circumstances in which the representation was made — for example, timing relative to the events ("shortly after"), specificity and contemporaneity of detail, absence of motive to fabricate, and surrounding conduct or statements of the maker — rather than an assessment that the substantive facts asserted are proven by independent corroboration. The source material indicates Ambrosoli was relied upon to support the proposition that the enquiry is concerned with the circumstances that warrant treating the representation as reliable notwithstanding its hearsay character. Because the original source text did not supply a full case citation for Ambrosoli, this chunk is marked [REVIEW]; the incomplete citation prevents providing standard case metadata (full citation and year). To apply the Ambrosoli principle in practice, practitioners should (a) identify precisely the representation to be tendered, (b) present evidence of the objective circumstances surrounding its making, and (c) resist conflating probative corroboration of the asserted fact with the separate statutory inquiry into the representation's intrinsic reliability under s 65.

<!-- block_043 procedure -->

### Chunk 3 — s 65(2)(b): Tasmania v Dolega paraphrase
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 65(2)(b)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65(2)(b) - Tasmania v Dolega Paraphrase] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: circumstances and fabrication test under s 65(2)(b)] [CONCEPTS: s65(2)(b), Tasmania v Dolega, fabrication test, hearsay exception, circumstances of making]

Paraphrase of the Dolega observation on s 65(2)(b): the need to consider the circumstances.
Paraphrased - there is the need to consider the circumstances in which the representation was made, and the effect of those circumstances on either the credibility or reliability of the representation, as the case may be.

Similar observations can be made in respect of the requirement in the second limb of s 65(2)(b). To allow the admission of the evidence in this case as an exception to the hearsay rule, the court must be satisfied that the circumstances in which the previous representation was made "make it unlikely that the representation is a fabrication".

---

### Chunk 4 — State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple [2025] TASSC 6 — hearsay after complainant died; timing; unfair prejudice
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 65(2); s 137] [CITATION: State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple [2025] TASSC 6 - Hearsay & Unfair Prejudice] [CASE: State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple [2025] TASSC 6] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: hearsay after death of complainant; timing; unfair prejudice; procedural considerations] [CONCEPTS: Allie & Maple 2025, hearsay after death, "shortly after", unfair prejudice, s65(2), Sio v The Queen, timing of representations]

This chunk covers the Allie & Maple decision on hearsay after the complainant died, timing, and unfair prejudice.
**State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple [2025] TASSC 6 **

This case considered hearsay evidence after the complainant had died before trial. The issues were the timing of the representations (i.e. shortly after) and "unfair prejudice" as defence had little opportunity to challenge the assertions of the complainant in interview. Which meant there was an unfair impetus for the defendant to have to give evidence in the trial to refute the claims -- a tactical disadvantage, and as such, unfairly prejudicial.

Specifically, at para 38, it is spelled out what issue defence took: *"It is contended for the accused that the representations were not made \"shortly after\" the events in question. Further, it is submitted on their behalf that it has not been shown that the representations were made \"in circumstances that make it unlikely that the representation is a fabrication\". In relation to s 65(2)( c), it is contended the higher threshold test is not met, namely that the representations were not made in \"circumstances that make it highly probable that the representation is reliable.\"*

The case referred to the HCA decision of: *Sio v the Queen *[2016] HCA 32, at para 34

This case spells out the procedure: *\"It then requires the identification of the particular representation to be adduced in evidence as proof of that fact. The circumstances in which that representation was made may then be considered in order to determine whether the conditions of admissibility are met. This process must be observed in relation to each relevant fact sought to be proved by tendering evidence under s 65.\"*

---

"This case spells out the procedure: *\"It then requires the identification of the particular representation to be adduced in evidence as proof of that fact. The circumstances in which that representation was made may then be considered in order to determine whether the conditions of admissibility are met. This process must be observed in relation to each relevant fact sought to be proved by tendering evidence under s 65.\"*"
