### Chunk 6 — s 65(2)(b) detailed analysis: "circumstances" (paras 51, 52, 55, 57) and day vs two days later
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 65(2)(b)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65(2)(b) - Detailed Analysis Paras 51-57] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: meaning of "circumstances" and reliability focus under s 65(2)(b)] [CONCEPTS: s65(2)(b) "circumstances", reliability, fabrication test, previous statements, same-day statements, later affidavit]

This chunk covers the detailed analysis of what "circumstances" means under s 65(2)(b) and examples on timing.
#### 65(2)(b)

Case also discussed meaning of "*Shortly after*" and *"make it unlikely that it is a fabrication."*

[51] The common approach is to focus upon the circumstances of the making of the representation to determine whether it is unlikely that the representation was a fabrication.

"circumstances":

[52] Section 65(2)(b) and (c) require courts to have regard to the \"circumstances\" in which the relevant representation was made. The focus is the reliability of the representation, not (directly) the reliability of the asserted fact: Mason P in *Ambrosoli *at [28]. The enquiry is not concerned with independent evidence that tends only to prove the asserted fact. The enquiry is concerned with the objective circumstances in which it was made, not the apparent truthfulness of the person making it: *Sio *at [70].

[55] "...The true concern of the provision is with the identification of circumstances which of themselves warrant the conclusion that the representation is reliable notwithstanding its hearsay character."

[57] It can be seen that the \"circumstances\" to be considered in the application of s 65(2)(b) can include previous, or subsequent, statements or conduct of the person who made the representation, to the extent to which they reflect on the reliability of the circumstances of the making of the particular hearsay representations.

Basically, in this case, representations made on the day were allowed under 65(2)(b), but the representations made 2 days later about the same events, in a stat dec, were not. This was despite the fact the statement was attested to as true. Though the court did accept it wayed in favour of reliability generally.

---

### Chunk 7 — s 65(2)(c): higher threshold "highly probable"
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 65(2)(c)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 65(2)(c) - Higher Threshold] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: s65(2)(c) "highly probable" reliability threshold] [CONCEPTS: s65(2)(c), "highly probable", reliability at large, hearsay exclusion, higher threshold, admissibility]

This chunk covers the higher threshold and application of s 65(2)(c).
#### 65(2)(c)

The court than considered the representation under 65(2)(c). It said that this test involves a higher threshold of \"highly\" probable and is concerned with reliability at large. As such it also excluded the later statement.

---

### Chunk 8 — Section 137: Exclusion of prejudicial evidence — Papakosmas and R v BD
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 137] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 - Papakosmas & R v BD] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine / case authority] [TOPIC: s137 exclusion of prejudicial evidence; Papakosmas precedent; definition of unfair prejudice] [CONCEPTS: s137, Papakosmas, unfair prejudice, R v BD, probative vs prejudicial, criminal proceedings]

This chunk covers s 137 principle and key authorities on unfair prejudice.
"137 Exclusion of prejudicial evidence in criminal proceedings"

***Papakosmas v The Queen *(1999) 196 CLR 297 at [91].**

In a criminal proceeding, the court must refuse to admit evidence adduced by the prosecutor if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the defendant."

Evidence is not unfairly prejudicial because it makes it more likely that the accused will be convicted. As Hunt CJ at CL said in *R v BD *[(1997) 94 A Crim R 131], 139:

\"The prejudice to which each of the sections [ss 135, 136 and 137] refers is *not* that the evidence merely tends to establish the Crown case; it means prejudice which is unfair because there is a real risk that the evidence will be misused by the jury in some unfair way.\"

---

### Chunk 9 — Defence submissions on unfair prejudice in Allie & Maple (detailed bullets)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 137; s 65] [CITATION: State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple [2025] TASSC 6 - Defence Submissions on Unfair Prejudice] [CASE: State of Tasmania v Allie and Maple [2025] TASSC 6] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist / annotation] [TOPIC: defence submissions alleging unfair prejudice from hearsay admission] [CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice submissions, cross-examination absent, forensic pressure, adverse inference, drug use issue, self-defence, joinder of issues, jury directions]

This chunk covers the defence's specific submissions about unfair prejudice in Allie & Maple — retain exact bullets.
The submissions by defence as to what amounted to unfair prejudice in the matter:

- representations will not only be unassailed by cross-examination (because he is an unavailable witness) but that this will occur in the context of a trial where the accused in their interviews were not provided with an opportunity to refute Mr Percy's representations of what happened.

- will create forensic pressure for him to give evidence to meet the State's cases and then the issues on his trial will be joined. It is submitted that the unfairness will be particularly marked if he does not give evidence. It is submitted that the jury are likely to draw an adverse inference from a failure to give evidence in this context.

- given there will be no cross-examination of the complainant, no forensic challenge, and no joinder of issues arising from Mr Allie's record of interview, there will be a greater likelihood of the jury, in some way, impermissibly informing itself on Mr Allie's trial using Mr Maple's police interview.

- the inability to cross-examine the complainant is that there is no opportunity to cross-examine the complainant about his drug use, which might have had implications for the reliability of his representations.

- the inability to cross-examine the complainant, the nature of the accounts given by the accused in their interviews which were lacking in detail and which, as a result, did not contradict the detail of the complainant's account. 

- It is said that self-defence is raised by the two accused in their interviews with police. Critical issues on the trial will be why the men were there and what they did. Unfortunately, however, the detail of what Mr Percy said in his statutory declaration was not put to the accused during their police interviews. It was pointed out in submissions for the accused Mr Allie that in relation to his interview, the police do not put to the accused most of the allegations that were made by Mr Percy. The only allegation put with any precision or detail relates to the taking of the vehicle and setting fire to it.

ALL were dismissed as being amenable to proper and detailed directions to the jury.

# Record of Interview (ROI) and Admissions — Overarching Evidentiary Principles
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 90] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: exclusion for oppressive questioning / fairness] [CONCEPTS: fairness, oppressive questioning, exclusion of evidence, admissions, confessions, ROI, s90 Evidence Act, undue persistence, cross-examination, coercion]

The evidentiary rule under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90 requires courts to exclude admissions or confessions when their admission would be unfair because of the manner in which they were obtained, including oppressive questioning. Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90 addresses unfairness and confessional evidence: a confession or admission obtained in circumstances that make its admission unfair must be excluded. Courts consider factors such as undue persistence, cross-examining style, expressions of disbelief, or tactics likely to overcome mental resistance of a person in custody. Authorities, including common law decisions and commentary in Cross on Evidence, inform the application of s 90 by identifying oppressive interview conduct (e.g., undue pressure, ridicule, or attempts to extort admissions) that may render otherwise probative material inadmissible to ensure a fair trial.

---

No unprocessed doctrinal units were listed in the Coverage Report from the prior output; therefore there are no additional formatted chunks to produce for this part.

<!-- block_044 procedure -->

### Chunk 1: Record of interview (ROI) - oppressive questioning (Tasmania v Purkiss & Otto)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 90] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 90 - Tasmania v Purkiss & Otto - Oppressive Questioning] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: exclusion of admissions/confessions for oppressive questioning] [CONCEPTS: ROI, oppressive questioning, exclusion, s90, admissions, confessions, cautions, police questioning, Purkiss & Otto]

This chunk covers: exclusion of admissions/confessions for oppressive questioning in recorded interviews.

1. "Tasmania v Purkiss & Otto [2019] TASSC 45 (27 September 2019)"

2. "Can be excluded on basis of overzeloue intervie tactics. Careful.."

3. "24. There have been many reported common law cases in which evidence of admissions and confessions has been excluded because of oppressive questioning, particularly questioning involving undue persistence, cross-examination or scepticism. Many of the cases are referred to in Cross on Evidence, 11th Australian edition, at [33730]. These cases are useful in determining questions of fairness for the purposes of [s 90] of the Evidence Act."

4. "25. In R v Amad [1962 VicRp 75]; [1962 VR 545], Smith J said, at 548:

\"A person in custody is, by that fact, ordinarily under great stress, and for that reason the law for his protection holds it to be improper to subject him, even after caution, to any form of cross-examination the tendency of which is in fact to extort admissions or to overcome his mental resistance to making admissions.\""

5. "26. In R v Pritchard [1991 VicRp 8]; [1991 1 VR 84], the Crown had relied at trial on a videotaped interview in which the interviewing officers had made it clear that they did not believe the interviewee. The Victorian Court of Criminal Appeal dismissed an appeal against conviction, but made critical comments about the form of questioning. Crockett, McGarvie and Beech JJ said, at 93:

\"The harm to him [the applicant] was that by the form of his questions the police officer was able to convey to the viewer of the tape the undisguised ridicule and derision he entertained about the answers of the applicant given in an endeavour to extricate himself from what obviously were real difficulties. The police would not at the trial have been permitted to express their incredulity or total disbelief in the applicant's answers. Why should they be allowed to do so by the form of questions chosen to be put to the applicant which can be, as they were, so vividly reproduced before the jury at trial?\""

6. "27. Several of Inspector Burk's questions involve expressions of disbelief in Ms Otto's answers and expressions of belief as to her involvement in the killing of her husband. He said things that he would not be allowed to say as a witness at the trial. It would have been unfair to Ms Otto for the jury to have seen and heard the inspector saying the things that I have quoted."

### Chunk 2: Defendant relying on Video Interview - obligation to tender whole interview (Spinks v L)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 59, s 81(2)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 81(2) - Spinks v L - Tender whole interview] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: hearsay exception and obligation to tender whole recorded interview] [CONCEPTS: video interview, hearsay, s59, s81(2), tender whole interview, Spinks v L, R v Soma, prosecution duty]

This chunk covers: hearsay status of interviews and the prosecution's obligation to tender the whole interview when exception applies.

"I recon I could argue no need to play if no "admission" exception to hearsay... However, if interview contains ANY admission of anysort, there is an arguable obligation to play the ROI in full:"

1. "SPINKS v L [2020] TASMC 5 (4 May 2020)"

2. "\"It is hearsay because it records a previous representation admitted to prove the truth the existence of some facts which the defendant intended to assert [(s 59(1))], but it is admissible under the Evidence Act 2001 [s 81(2)] as an exception to the hearsay rule. The obligation to put the case fairly requires the prosecution to tender the whole of the interview -- 'the good with the bad' (R v Soma [2003] HCA 13; (2003) 212 CLR 299 at [30]-[31])\""

### Chunk 3: Submission against ROI as the only account from Defendant (Police v Quinn)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [CITATION: Police v Quinn [2019] TASMC 4 - Defendant silence and weight of exculpatory statements] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: weight of untested exculpatory statements where defendant did not testify] [CONCEPTS: silence, weight of statements, untested, cross-examination, Police v Quinn, exculpatory statements, DCM decision]

This chunk covers: judicial approach to defendant's silence and weight given to exculpatory statements not given under oath.

1. "Submission against ROI as the only account from Def"

2. "***Police v Quinn [2019] TASMC 4***"

3. "**DECISION OF:** Daly DCM"

4. "[Para 14] \"For the reasons that follow, I have preferred the evidence of the police officers to that of the defendant, where their evidence conflicts. ***The defendant did not testify on oath in court. It was, of course, his right to remain silent in court. No adverse inference may be drawn from that. I assess the weight to be given to his exculpatory statements, where they are in conflict with the evidence the police officers, in light of the fact that his version of events was not given on oath and were untested by cross-examination. I give little weight to that material.\"***"

### Chunk 4: ROI must be played in full - mixed statements (Baker v Barratt; R v McCarrol)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 60] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 60 - Baker v Barratt & R v McCarrol - Mixed statements rule] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: mixed statements containing admissions and exculpatory material must be tendered in full] [CONCEPTS: mixed statement, admissions, exculpatory material, tender full interview, Baker v Barratt, R v McCarrol, Mule v R, prosecution obligation]

This chunk covers: rule that mixed statements containing admissions and exculpatory material must be admitted in full.

1. "Hearsay:"

2. "### ROI Record of interview must be played in full"

3. "Cannot substitute part or any of the recorded interview just with the transcript as the transcript is not evidence. Failure to do is error. Baker v Barratt [2019] TASSC 28 (4 July 2019) at [28]"

4. "**Malcolm CJ and Murray J in R v McCarrol [2004] WASCA 131;** BC200404491 at [19]:"

5. "The law as to the reception in evidence of a 'mixed' statement, one containing admissions or declarations against interest by an accused person and one containing exculpatory statements, is clear: Mule v R [2004] WASCA 7; BC200400034 ... Where the prosecution is permitted to tender such a statement, relying upon its confessional qualities, then it is obliged to accept in evidence the exculpatory material which may in turn be relied upon by the accused. All of the statement becomes evidence available to the jury for their consideration, but the admissions and the exculpatory material need not, and indeed probably should not in the generality of cases, be accorded equal weight."

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [CITATION: Jotheeswaran v Barnes [2019] TASSC 42 - admissions bring exculpatory assertions] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: admissions in interviews allow defendant to rely on exculpatory assertions] [CONCEPTS: admissions, exculpatory assertions, prior statements, evidence for defendant, Higgins, Jack v Smail, Jotheeswaran]

2. "**Jotheeswaran v Barnes [2019] TASSC 42 (17 October 2019)**"

3. ### Chunk 6: Formal caution and notice of suspect (Tasmania v Thompson)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Tasmania v Thompson [2022] TASSC 27 - fairness and caution] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: cautioning and informing suspect - minimum standards of fairness] [CONCEPTS: caution, suspect notice, fairness, Tasmania v Thompson, interview fairness, minimum standard, administration of caution]

This chunk covers: whether informing interviewee they are a suspect is required and role of caution in fairness.

1. "### Formal Caution necessary but notice as a "suspect" not"

2. "**FW: Tasmania v Thompson [2022] TASSC 27 (28 April 2022)**"

3. "*[11] \"Taken in context, it is clear that His Honour was not seeking to lay down a minimum standard of fairness which requires an interviewee to be told that he or she is a suspect or possible suspect before an interview. The Court was dealing with circumstances in which there had been no caution at all, and his Honour's comments were in the context that although a caution may not have been necessary given the stage that the investigation had then reached, informing the accused that he was suspect would have at least alerted him to the seriousness of this position and put him on guard. In my view, it is apparent that his Honour's comments presupposed that, irrespective of what else had been said, the administration of a caution would have certainly satisfied the requirements of fairness in the circumstances of that particular case. His Honour also made the point that \"the requirements of fairness are not to be turned into fixed categories\".\"*"

### Chunk 7: Recent complaint evidence under s 66(2) (Marysej v McCullough) and s 66(3) distinction
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 66(2), s 66(3), s 60] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66(2)/(3) - Marysej v McCullough - Recent complaint evidence] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: recent complaint evidence admissibility and distinction from statements made for court purposes] [CONCEPTS: s66, recent complaint, admissibility, Marysej v McCullough, Papakosmas, prior inconsistent statement, s60, Neill-Fraser, PIS, complainant statement]

This chunk covers: when recent complaint evidence under s 66(2) is admissible and the exception in s 66(3) where statements made for court purposes are excluded.

1. "### Recent complaint evidence (66(2)):"

2. "Permissible under s 66 where vic says to someone it happened so long as pre-conditions are met. However, not if they are saying it for the purposes of what will be said in court (i.e. a police formal statement) per s 66(3). So contrast this with a spontaneous representation at the scene."

3. "That means that a statement cant go in as evidence. But a neigbour they complain too, or the police they report to at the scene should be fine."

4. "See para 19 of ***Marysej v McCullough*** [2018] TASSC for a god example of the distinction."

5. "[19] The evidence of Ms Eberhart of the complainant's account of the applicant trying to pull her pants down was evidence of the truth of the complainant's assertions: Evidence Act 2001, s 66(2); Papakosmas v The Queen [1999] HCA 37, 196 CLR 297. On the same basis, the evidence of the assertions in the conversation with Senior Constable Carr was also evidence of the truth of the facts asserted unless the complainant said what she did for the purpose of indicating the evidence that she would be able to give in a court proceeding: Evidence Act, s 66(3). I think the only reasonable inference was that at the time of that conversation Senior Constable Carr was trying to find out what had happened, and that the complainant was enlightening her, rather than outlining the evidence she could give. It follows that the evidence of the descriptions of events given by the complainant to both Ms Eberhart and Senior Constable Carr was evidence of the truth of the facts asserted."

6. "3rd party wintess s evidence of vic's distress and the account she gave of the defendant's actions is relevant as it tends to corroborate vic's assertions as to def's conduct. Wintess's evidence that vic complained to her about def's conduct is admissible as evidence of the truth of the facts that vic asserted, Evidence Act 2001, s 66(2); Papakosmas v The Queen [1999] HCA 37, 196 CLR 297."

7. "NOTE -- section 66(3) does not restrict statements going in for PIS!!! Adduced not just as hearsay but also for credit"

8. "Prosecution can admit that document on the hearing, not just as evidence of a prior inconsistent statement admittedly made by the complainant, but also as evidence of the truth of the matters asserted in it (See Evidence Act, s60). There is abundant evidence that supports the prosecution submission that, once the prior representation is admissible as a prior inconsistent statement it is also admissible by operation of s 60 as proof of the truth of its content. (See Neill-Fraser v Tasmania (No2) (2019) TASSC 11 at par 20 per Brett J)"

### Chunk 8: Coincidence evidence approach (Tasmania v Harris; R v Gale; R v Duckworth)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 98, s 100(2), s 101(2)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98/s 100/s 101 - Tasmania v Harris - R v Gale; R v Duckworth summary] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: approach to admitting coincidence evidence under s 98] [CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, s98, s100, s101, similarity in events, reasonable notice, probative value, Tasmania v Harris, Gale, Duckworth]

This chunk covers: the stepwise approach to admissibility of coincidence evidence under s 98 as summarised in Tasmania v Harris quoting R v Gale; R v Duckworth.

1. "### COINCIDENCE EVIDENCE"

2. "**Tasmania v Harris [2016] TASSC 47**"

3. "**The approach to coincidence evidence**"

4. "29. In R v Gale; R v Duckworth [2012] NSWCCA 174, 217 A Crim R 487, Simpson J, with whom McClellan CJ at CL and Fullerton J agreed, summarised the approach to be adopted in considering the admission of evidence as coincidence evidence at [30]-[31] in the following way:"

5. "\"[30] The factual underpinnings of the [s 98] decision to admit or reject coincidence evidence are:

- that there is evidence capable of establishing the occurrence of two or more events; and

- that there is evidence capable of establishing similarities in the two or more events; or

- that there is evidence capable of establishing similarities in the circumstances in which two or more events occurred;

- that there is evidence capable of establishing both similarities in the two or more events and similarities in the circumstances in which the two events occurred.

[31] In a case in which it is found that there is such evidence, then, in my opinion, the correct process in the determination of the admission of evidence under [s 98] involves a series of steps, as follows:

- the first step is to identify the 'particular act of a person' or the 'particular state of mind of a person' that the party tendering the evidence seeks to prove;

- the second step is to identify the 'two or more events' from the occurrence of which the party tendering the evidence seeks to prove that the person in question did the 'particular act' or had the 'particular state of mind';

- the third step is to identify the 'similarities in the events' and/or the 'similarities in the circumstances in which the events occurred' by reason of which the party tendering the evidence asserts the improbability of coincidental occurrence of the events;

- the fourth step is to determine whether 'reasonable notice' has been given of the intention to adduce the evidence (or, if reasonable notice has not been given, whether a direction under [s 100(2)] ought to be given, dispensing with the requirement);

- the fifth step is to make an evaluation whether the evidence will, either by itself or in conjunction with other evidence already given or anticipated, 'have significant probative value';

- in a criminal proceeding, if it is determined that the evidence would have 'significant probative value', the sixth step is the determination whether the probative value of the evidence 'substantially outweighs' any prejudicial effect it may have on the defendant [(s 101(2)).]

- the sixth step necessarily involves some analysis both of the probative value of the evidence in question and any prejudicial effect it might have: R v RN [2005] NSWCCA 413, and a balancing of the two.\""

### Chunk 9: Concoction of multiple complainants (Biddle v Smith)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Biddle v Smith [2012] TASMC 24 - Coincidence: improbability of concoction] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: use of coincidence evidence to counter claim of concoction or mistake] [CONCEPTS: concoction, multiple complainants, coincidence evidence, similarities, improbability, Biddle v Smith]

This chunk covers: use of coincidence evidence to show improbability that multiple complainants concocted or were mistaken.

1. "### Concoction of multiple complainants."

2. "Another good use of coinceidence evidence is \"that having regard to the similarities in the events and the circumstances in which they are alleged to have occurred, it is improbable that, by coincidence, either child has either concocted or is mistaken about what occurred.\""

3. "See para 19 onwards of **Biddle v Smith [2012] TASMC 24**"

4. "And from [22]: *On the other hand, I am satisfied that the evidence in respect of one charge does have significant probative value in the proof of the other, on the basis that it is highly improbable, in my view, that both children would have coincidently either made up or been mistaken about being assaulted by the defendant. My reasons for arriving at this conclusion are as follows:*

*There are a number of similarities between the versions of the children, which make it unlikely that their stories have falsely or mistakenly arisen by coincidence. Those similarities include the following:*"

- "*The age of the children.*"

- "*The fact that the defendant has met the children through the church his wife attends.*"

- "*That each assault was of a sexual nature.*"

- "*Each assault was relatively short lived and involved a brief but deliberate application of force by the defendant to the child in question.*"

- "*That in each case the assault is alleged to have occurred in the context of a game being played by the defendant with the child.*"

- "*That the defendant has initiated the game in question on each occasion.*"

- "*A further circumstance of importance which contributes to the improbability of coincidence is that the allegations have been made by each child within two days of the other.*"

### Chunk 10: Tendency evidence admissibility steps and Police v FRS summary
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 97(1)(a), s 97(1)(b), s 101(2), s 137, s 3(1)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 & s101 - Police v FRS [2020] TASMC 9 - Tendency Evidence Procedure] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: steps and reasoning for admitting tendency evidence and case-specific application in Police v FRS] [CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, s97, probative value, s101, prejudice, Police v FRS, characterisation, uncharged acts, cross-admissible, evaluative judgment]

This chunk covers: how to characterise and admit tendency evidence, with the Police v FRS case facts and judicial reasoning.

1. "### Tendency EVIDENCE"

2. "**Police v FRS [2020] TASMC 9**"

3. "4.  By amended notice dated 19 2020 the State gave notice of an intention to adduce tendency evidence: Evidence Act 2001, s 97(1)(a). The notice (at para. 2) indicated the State's intention to adduce evidence that the defendant had a tendency to:

    a.  have a sexual interest in and/or engage in sexual contact with his nieces aged approximately 16 years;

        b.  impose himself sexually upon his nieces without seeking or obtaining their consent;

        v.  sexually touch his nieces under their clothing;

        d.  sexually touch his nieces when they stayed overnight;

        e.  sexually touch his nieces when they were in bed; and/or

        f.  sexually touch his nieces in circumstances when they were alone."

4. "5.  The evidence was tendered on both complaints as cross-admissible tendency evidence. The defendant's counsel told the Court that there was no dispute that the evidence was tendency evidence and that there was no objection to its admission. During the course of the trial, AB gave evidence that the defendant massaged her shoulders and moved his hands \"a bit too low\". It was common ground that this uncharged act was included in the amended tendency notice. The defendant's counsel conceded that the evidence of the uncharged act was admissible as tendency evidence. Even so, prosecuting counsel submitted that the Court should decide whether it is correct to characterise the evidence in question as tendency evidence[1]."

5. "6.  Tendency evidence is a species of circumstantial evidence[2]. It provides the foundation for an inference that, because a person has a relevant tendency, it is more likely that they acted in a particular way, or had a particular state of mind. It is indirect evidence which allows 'stepping stone' reasoning as follows[3]:"

> "That
>
> - on an occasion other than the charged occasion, the defendant acted in a particular way;
>
> - it can therefore be inferred that the person had a tendency to act in that way;
>
> - because of that tendency, it can be inferred that on the charged occasion, the person acted in conformity with that tendency.
>
> Alternatively, that
>
> - on an occasion other than the charged occasion, the defendant had a particular state of mind;
>
> - it can therefore be inferred that the person had a tendency to have that state of mind;
>
> - because of that tendency, it can be inferred that on the charged occasion, the person's state of mind conformed with that tendency."

6. "7.  The nature of tendency evidence was summarised by the High Court as follows[4]:"

"Applied to evidence of past conduct, tendency reasoning is no more sophisticated than: he did it before; he has a propensity to do this sort of thing; the likelihood is that he did it again on the occasion in issue.

Tendency reasoning, as courts have long recognised, is not deductive logic. It is a form of inferential or inductive reasoning..."

7. "8.  In Pattison v Tasmania[5] Wood J observed that the consideration of tendency evidence and the threshold requirements for its admissibility involves four steps. Firstly, the evidence must be properly characterised as tendency evidence. That requirement is satisfied in this case. The evidence is relevant to the issue whether the defendant committed each offence in circumstances where he had a disposition or inclination to be sexually interested in his nieces when aged about 16 and an inclination to act on that sexual interest by sexually touching them[6]. The particular features of the defendant's alleged sexual misconduct as stated in the tendency notice are sufficient to link the two complaints together and make it more likely that he committed the charged offences[7]."

8. "9.  Secondly, the evidence must have significant probative value: [s 97(1)(b).] I am satisfied that the evidence satisfies this requirement for the following reasons. The interpretation section of the Evidence Act 2001, s 3(1) defines the 'probative value' of evidence as the extent to which the evidence is capable of rationally affecting the assessment of the probability of the existence of the facts in issue. In criminal cases, the facts in issue are the facts which establish the elements of the charge (here -- the indecent assaults). The tendency evidence in this case is important in the assessment of the probability of whether the defendant indecently assaulted his two nieces."

9. "10. Thirdly, the probative value of the tendency evidence must outweigh its prejudicial effect: [s 101(2).] The expressions 'prejudicial effect' in [s 101], 'unfairly prejudicial' in [s 135] and 'unfair prejudice' in [s 137], each convey essentially the same idea of harm to the interests of the defendant by reason of a risk that the fact-finder might 'use the evidence improperly in some unfair way' against the defendant[11]. There is no danger of harm or misuse on either of the trials of these complaints (and it was not submitted that there was)."

10. "11. Fourthly and finally, in relation to the separate duty of the Court to refuse to admit the evidence if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the defendant, having conducted the evaluative judgment required by [s 137] I conclude that there is no relevant danger."

### Chunk 11: Probative value of tendency evidence (Brown v Tasmania; McPhillamy)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [CITATION: Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4 - Probative value of tendency evidence; McPhillamy v The Queen [2018] HCA 52] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: assessing probative value of tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: probative value, tendency evidence, McPhillamy, Brown v Tasmania, assessment, capable of proving tendency]

This chunk covers: requirement that tendency evidence be capable of proving the asserted tendency and assessing probative value.

1. "### Probative value of tendency evidence"

2. "**Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4**"

3. "At para [54]"

4. "In McPhillamy v The Queen [2018] HCA 52, 361 ALR 13, at [26], the plurality said:"

"\"... assessment of the probative value of tendency evidence requires the court to determine the extent to which the evidence is capable of proving the tendency.\""

5. "The evidence must be capable of proving the tendency, but this can be considered with other evidence such as the presence of the def's DNA on the complainant's nightie."

### Chunk 12: Tendency evidence from subsequent behaviour (Brown v Tasmania para 20)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [CITATION: Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4 para 20 - tendency evidence post-dating offences] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: admissibility of tendency evidence that post-dates charged conduct] [CONCEPTS: subsequent behaviour, post-dating events, tendency probative value, Brown v Tasmania, RH v The Queen]

This chunk covers: admissibility of tendency evidence that post-dates the offences and its effect on probative value.

1. "### Evidence of tendency can come from future/ subsequent behaviour."

2. "**Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4**"

3. "*[20] The first thing to notice about the proposed evidence is that it is of events which post-date the crimes. The fact that the tendency evidence relates to events after the alleged conduct in issue does not necessarily mean that the probative value of the evidence is reduced: RH v The Queen [2014] NSWCCA 71, 241 A Crim R 1 at [88]- [130].*"

<!-- block_045 master -->

### Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) — Statutory framework for tendency and coincidence evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 95, 97, 98, 101] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: statutory framework for tendency and coincidence evidence] [CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, coincidence evidence, Evidence Act 2001, s97, s98, s101, exclusion rule, admissibility notice]
The Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) governs the admission of tendency and coincidence evidence through ss 95, 97, 98 and 101 and establishes these rules as exclusionary: such evidence is not admissible for tendency or coincidence reasoning unless the statutory preconditions are met. Section 95 restricts the purposes for which evidence of character, propensity or similar matters may be used; s 97 permits tendency evidence only if the court thinks the evidence will have "significant probative value" (s 97(1)(b)); s 98 imposes an analogous criterion for coincidence evidence; and s 101 requires that the probative value of tendency or coincidence evidence substantially outweigh any prejudicial effect (s 101(2)). Under these provisions, the court must identify whether evidence is being led for a tendency or coincidence purpose and, if so, apply the statutory tests rather than admitting the evidence as a matter of general relevance.

---

### Distinction: Tendency evidence concerns a person; coincidence evidence concerns events
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: distinction between tendency and coincidence] [CONCEPTS: tendency vs coincidence, person-based evidence, event-based evidence, identification, similarity, linking features]
Tendency evidence concerns the propensity or past behaviour or state of mind of a person (typically the accused) and is led to infer that because the person acted in a particular way on past occasions they likely acted similarly in the occasion in issue. Coincidence evidence, by contrast, relates to the similarity between events and is used to infer that the same person committed both events because of their strong resemblance. The practical effect is that tendency reasoning links to a person-based pattern (a disposition or repeated conduct), whereas coincidence reasoning links separate events to identify a single perpetrator. This conceptual difference shapes the statutory tests: s 97 addresses tendency, s 98 addresses coincidence.

---

### Definition of tendency evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: statutory definition / legal doctrine] [TOPIC: what constitutes tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: tendency evidence definition, state of mind, prior conduct, propensity, Elomar v R, Patterson v Tasmania, proof of accused's conduct]
Tendency evidence is evidence tendered to establish that a person has or had acted in a particular way or had a particular state of mind, offered to support an inference that because the person had that tendency it is more likely they behaved in a particular way or had that state of mind at a time relevant to the dispute. Evidence of actual conduct or an actual state of mind is not necessarily tendency evidence where it is simply proof of an act on a specific occasion; tendency evidence is used to show a pattern or disposition: see Elomar v R [2014] NSWCCA 303 and Patterson v Tasmania [2017] TASCCA. Evidence of motive, or evidence that an accused actually did an act, is not per se tendency evidence unless it is adduced to establish a broader disposition.

---

### Definition of coincidence evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: what constitutes coincidence evidence] [CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence definition, event similarity, Pfennig, Sutton v R, identification by similarity, rare crimes, uniqueness]
Coincidence evidence concerns the similarity between distinct events, relied upon to make it improbable that different perpetrators committed the events and therefore to identify a single offender. Under s 98 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas), coincidence reasoning requires that the similar events be sufficiently alike — in degree, number, timing and particularity — to have significant probative value in proving identification or another fact in issue. Where a crime is rare or the similarities are highly distinctive, less similarity may be required to reach the threshold for admission: see Sutton v R (1984) 152 CLR 528 and Pfennig v R (1995) 182 CLR 461.

---

### Admissibility of tendency evidence — s 97(1)(b) “significant probative value”
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: admissibility test for tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: s97, significant probative value, admissibility, evaluative judgment, L v Tasmania, McPhillamy v R, Hughes v R]
Tendency evidence is admissible under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 only if the court is satisfied that the evidence, by itself or together with other evidence, will have "significant probative value" for a tendency purpose (s 97(1)(b)). The “significant probative value” test is an evaluative assessment: the court must take the evidence at its highest and determine whether it could be influential in resolving a disputed fact. Authorities emphasise that this is not a mere discretion but a legal judgment subject to appellate review (L v Tasmania (2006) 15 Tas R 381; McPhillamy v R [2018] HCA 52). The court must consider the nature of the evidence, its capacity to prove the alleged tendency, and the tendency’s capacity to support elements of the offence (Hughes v R [2017] HCA 20).

---

### Balancing test under s 101 — probative value must substantially outweigh prejudicial effect
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: balancing probative value and prejudice] [CONCEPTS: s101, substantial outweighing, prejudicial effect, unfair prejudice, Papakosmas, Festa, jury misuse]
Section 101 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) requires that tendency or coincidence evidence not be admitted unless its probative value substantially outweighs any prejudicial effect (s 101(2)). “Prejudicial effect” is not simply that the evidence proves the Crown case; it is prejudice in the sense the jury may give the evidence more weight than it deserves, use it improperly, or be inflamed (Papakosmas v R 196 CLR 297; Festa v R 208 CLR 593). The court must evaluate whether the evidence risks unfair reasoning (for example, substituting propensity proof for proof of the charged act) and whether clear directions or other safeguards will mitigate that risk.

---

### Probative value assessment — assume evidence accepted and consider context
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: assessing probative value of tendency/coincidence evidence] [CONCEPTS: probative value, assume evidence accepted, context, Tasmania v L, R v Shamouil, R v Sood, R v Bauer]
When assessing probative value the court must assume the evidence will be accepted and any favourable inference will be drawn; credibility or reliability is irrelevant to the threshold assessment. Probative value is measured by whether the evidence could rationally affect the probability of a fact in issue and must be considered in the context of the entire body of evidence rather than in isolation (Tasmania v L [2013] TASSC 47; R v Shamouil (2006) 66 NSWLR 112; R v Sood [2007] NSWCCA 214). Thus, tendency evidence that has limited standalone force may nonetheless be “significant” when combined with other circumstantial strands.

---

### Similarity requirement — linking features usually required; striking similarity not essential
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: role of similarity in tendency and coincidence evidence] [CONCEPTS: similarity requirement, linking features, R v Bauer, Hughes v R, signature, identity, striking similarity]
Tendency evidence does not have to be strikingly or closely similar to the alleged offence to have significant probative value; however, ordinarily there should be some feature or features linking the tendency evidence to the alleged offending. Where direct evidence of involvement exists, less similarity may be required for tendency evidence to be probative; where identity is in issue and there is no other linking evidence, more similarity or a striking signature may be necessary. Authorities emphasise identification of linking factors between past acts and the event in question as the critical inquiry (R v Bauer; Hughes v R [2017] HCA 20).

---

### Tendency evidence in single-complainant sexual-offence cases
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: prior sexual offending and tendency in single-complainant cases] [CONCEPTS: sexual offences, single complainant, propensity to act, McPhillamy v R, Bauer, grooming, acting on sexual interest]
In single-complainant sexual-offence contexts, proof that the accused committed sexual offences on one complainant can often suffice to establish a tendency of significant probative value without needing other special features because the propensity to act on sexual attraction is likely to recur. However, in multiple-complainant cases proof of a common feature beyond merely sexual nature is generally required to show a consistent mode of offending (R v Bauer; McPhillamy v R [2018] HCA 52). Mere sexual interest in a class of persons is generally insufficient; it is the tendency to act on that interest (grooming, similar modus operandi) that supplies probative force.

---

### Coincidence evidence — factors for probative value (degree, number, timing, rarity)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: factors in assessing coincidence evidence] [CONCEPTS: degree of similarity, number of similarities, timing, rarity, Pfennig, Sutton v R]
Coincidence evidence under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 requires similarity adequate to make it improbable that separate perpetrators committed the events. Factors to consider include the degree of similarity, the number of similarities, timing, and each similarity’s capacity (alone or in combination) to affect a fact in issue. The rarer or more distinctive the crime, the fewer similarities may be necessary. The overarching question is whether the evidence is cogent enough to prove identification (Sutton v R (1984) 152 CLR 528; Pfennig v R (1995) 182 CLR 461; Tognolini v R [2011] VSCA 394).

---

### Cross-admissibility: evidence may serve both tendency and coincidence purposes
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: cross-admissibility of evidence] [CONCEPTS: cross-admissible evidence, s97, s98, similarity, Donoghue v Tasmania, Standage v Tasmania, FB v R]
Evidence can be admissible for both tendency and coincidence purposes if it meets the significant-probative-value criteria in ss 97(1)(b) and 98(1)(b). Although the statutory tests differ, the degree of similarity is an important factor for both. Cases have accepted cross-admissibility where the evidence, assessed in context, is sufficiently cogent to support tendency and to make coincidence an improbable explanation (Standage v Tasmania [2012] TASSC 88; Donoghue v Tasmania [2016] TASCCA 17).

---

### Prejudicial effect — what amounts to unfair prejudice under s 101
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: unfair prejudice and s101 balancing] [CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, jury misuse, emotional responses, substitution of proof, Roach v R, GAG, Watkins, Papakosmas]
Prejudicial effect under s 101 involves a risk that the jury will give the evidence more weight than it deserves, use it for an improper purpose, or be inflamed — not mere prejudice arising from proof of the Crown case. Examples of unfair prejudice include the jury substituting general prior guilt for proof of the specific intent charged or allowing emotion to overwhelm rational assessment (Papakosmas v R 196 CLR 297; Roach v R 242 CLR 610; R v GAG (2007) 178 A Crim R 408; R v Watkins [2005] 153 A Crim R 434). The judge must assess whether clear directions or other safeguards will sufficiently neutralise the risk.

---

### Meaning of “substantial” in the balancing exercise
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: interpreting "substantially outweigh" in s101] [CONCEPTS: substantial, weighty evidence, VOT v WA, R v Fletcher, Tasmania v Martin, large significance]
The qualifier “substantially” in s 101(2) requires that the probative value be weighty or significant — not minimal. Authorities describe “substantial” as large, weighty or of absolute significance in the balancing exercise (VOT v WA 184 A Crim R 284; R v Fletcher (2005) 156 A Crim R 308). The court must be satisfied that the probative utility of the evidence is not merely present but sufficiently powerful to justify admission despite risks of prejudice.

---

### Concoction and contamination — common-law concern and statutory rule
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: role of concoction/contamination in probative assessment] [CONCEPTS: concoction, contamination, collusion, s101(5), Hoch v R, Bauer v R]
At common law, a real risk of concoction or contamination can erode the probative force of similar-fact evidence (Hoch v R (1988) 165 CLR 292). Under the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas), however, s 101(5) provides that the possibility that tendency or coincidence evidence may result from collusion, concoction or suggestion is not to be taken into account when determining probative value or prejudicial effect. Courts nonetheless examine and may exclude evidence for other reasons if independent signs suggest contamination undermines reliability (Bauer v R).

---

### Non-tendency purposes for similar evidence — notice rules and limiting directions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule / evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: non-tendency uses and notice requirements] [CONCEPTS: non-tendency evidence, opportunity, identification, motive, res gestae, ss97(1)(a), ss98(1)(a), limiting directions]
Evidence that could be tendency or coincidence evidence may nonetheless be admitted for non-tendency purposes — for example, to prove opportunity, identification, motive, res gestae, or aspects of relationship — in which case the statutory tendency/coincidence provisions do not apply provided the purpose is legitimate. If evidence is not being used for a tendency purpose, the notice requirements in ss 97(1)(a) and 98(1)(a) do not apply. The court must however scrutinise the proffered purpose and, where appropriate, give limiting directions to the jury that the evidence cannot be used for tendency or coincidence reasoning (Patterson v Tasmania [2017] TASCCA).

---

### Appellate review — admissibility is an evaluative judgment, not a discretionary exercise
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: appellate review of tendency admissibility] [CONCEPTS: evaluative judgment, appeal, L v Tasmania, Donoghue v Tasmania, McPhillamy v R, KMJ v Tasmania]
The admissibility determination for tendency or coincidence evidence is an evaluative legal judgment rather than an ordinary discretion. Consequently, on appeal the Court of Criminal Appeal must determine whether, on the evidence given at trial, the tendency evidence was correctly admitted or whether its admission caused a miscarriage of justice (L v Tasmania (2006) 15 Tas R 381; KMJ v Tasmania (2011) 218 A Crim R 87; McPhillamy v R [2018] HCA 52). The appellate court must re‑assess the probative value and prejudicial risk in light of the trial material.

---

### Tendency as a circumstantial “strand in the cable” — identity and cumulative reasoning
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: tendency as a circumstantial strand for identity] [CONCEPTS: circumstantial evidence, strands in the cable, identity, DNA, flight, false alibi, Brown v Tasmania]
Tendency evidence often functions as a strand in a circumstantial case rather than the sole basis for conviction. When combined with other circumstantial evidence — for example DNA evidence, evidence of flight, or a false alibi — tendency evidence may contribute significant probative force to establish identity. The Court in Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4 accepted that a broadly-defined tendency, though insufficient alone, became important when combined with other evidence, thereby reinforcing the “strands in the cable” approach to circumstantial reasoning.

---

### Jury directions — assumed capacity to follow complex directions when mitigating prejudice
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: role of jury directions in prejudice assessment] [CONCEPTS: jury directions, mitigation of prejudice, Gilbert v R, R v PWD, presumption of compliance]
In assessing the risk of unfair prejudice from tendency or coincidence evidence a judge should account for the mitigating effect of appropriate jury directions. Courts proceed on the basis that juries are capable of following complex legal directions and will disregard inadmissible or improperly-used evidence when instructed (Gilbert v R (2000) 201 CLR 414; R v PWD (2010) 205 A Crim R 75). The availability and likely efficacy of directions inform whether prejudicial effect can be neutralised sufficiently to admit the evidence.

---

### Identity issues: when more similarity is required
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: heightened similarity requirement for identity issues] [CONCEPTS: identity, striking similarity, signature, rare crimes, Pfennig, Hughes v R]
When identity is a live issue and there is little direct evidence linking the accused to the crime, courts often require greater similarity or a “signature” quality in the comparable events to reach significant probative value. Where the crime is rare or distinctive, however, less similarity may be needed because the improbability of coincidence is greater (Pfennig v R (1995) 182 CLR 461; Hughes v R [2017] HCA 20). The key is whether the combined evidence makes it improbable that anyone other than the accused committed the acts.

---

### Prejudice examples and risks of jury misuse
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: examples of prejudicial use of tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: jury misuse, substitution of guilt, emotional prejudice, Watkins, GAG, Roach, Neill-Fraser]
Courts have identified concrete risks of unfair prejudice from similar-fact evidence: the jury may substitute recognition of the accused’s past wrongdoing for proof of the charged offence, treat repeated uncharged incidents as cumulative proof of guilt, undervalue the possibility of other persons sharing the tendency, or be swayed by emotion instead of logic (R v Watkins [2005] 153 A Crim R 434; R v GAG (2007) 178 A Crim R 408; Roach v R 242 CLR 610). These risks must be balanced against probative value when applying s 101.

---

### Evidence of identical witness accounts — independent confirmation increases probative force
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: independent witness similarity and probative force] [CONCEPTS: independent witnesses, similarity of accounts, WRC, BBH v R, OPP v Boardman, contamination risk]
Independent, non-collusive witnesses giving substantially similar accounts of an accused’s conduct can significantly increase the probative value of tendency or coincidence evidence, because independent confirmation reduces the likelihood of concoction or collusion. The High Court in BBH v R [2012] HCA 9 recognised that independent, coincident evidence strengthens probative force. At common law such similarity also supported probative utility (OPP v Boardman [1975] AC 421), subject to assessment of contamination risks.

---

### Non-tendency evidence examples: opportunity, identification, motive, res gestae, relationship evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: legitimate non-tendency purposes for similar evidence] [CONCEPTS: opportunity evidence, identification evidence, motive, res gestae, relationship evidence, Patterson v Tasmania]
Evidence that might otherwise resemble tendency or coincidence material can legitimately be adduced for other purposes such as opportunity to commit the offence, identification of the accused by an eyewitness, motive, res gestae of an event, or matters about the relationship between parties. When led for these non-tendency purposes the statutory tendency/coincidence exclusion does not apply, but the court must ensure the evidence is genuinely adduced for that purpose and provide appropriate limiting directions to the jury (Patterson v Tasmania [2017] TASCCA).

---

### Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4 — tendency evidence as circumstantial strand; similarity not required to be identical
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4] [TOPIC: tendency evidence need not be identical; circumstantial strands] [CONCEPTS: Brown v Tasmania, tendency evidence, circumstantial evidence, strands in the cable, identity, probative value]
Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4 confirms that a proposed tendency need not be closely or strikingly similar to the alleged offence to have significant probative value. The Court explained that the relevant question is whether there is some feature or features linking the tendency evidence to the alleged offending; if so, and when combined with other circumstantial evidence (DNA, flight, false alibi), the tendency evidence can be an important “strand” contributing to a strong circumstantial case on identity. The decision endorses assessing tendency evidence as part of the totality of proof rather than requiring identical conduct.

---

### Hughes v The Queen [2017] HCA 20 — similarity and probative value
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Hughes v The Queen [2017] HCA 20] [TOPIC: similarity not essential; probative assessment] [CONCEPTS: Hughes v The Queen, striking similarity, probative value, tendency, coincidence, context]
Hughes v The Queen [2017] HCA 20 held that tendency or similar fact evidence does not have to be closely or strikingly similar to the charged act to have significant probative value, though striking similarity will usually make such evidence highly probative. The High Court emphasised that the assessment depends on whether the evidence, in the context of the whole case, could influence proof of an issue in dispute and that linking features between past conduct and the charged conduct are material to that assessment.

---

### R v Bauer — linking features and single-complainant sexual offences
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Bauer (authorities cited in source)] [TOPIC: linking features; sexual offences tendency] [CONCEPTS: R v Bauer, linking features, sexual offences, grooming, single complainant, significant probative value]
R v Bauer (as relied on in the source authorities) establishes that tendency evidence requires linking features between the prior act and the alleged offence, and that in single-complainant sexual-offence cases proof of a prior sexual act against the same complainant can show a tendency to act on sexual attraction without additional special features. Bauer further illustrates how tendency evidence may combine with other circumstantial material to support identity and explores contamination/concoction concerns.

---

### Elomar v R [2014] NSWCCA 303 — defining tendency evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Elomar v R [2014] NSWCCA 303] [TOPIC: definition and scope of tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: Elomar v R, tendency definition, state of mind, prior conduct, NSWCCA]
Elomar v R [2014] NSWCCA 303 confirms that tendency evidence is intended to show a person’s past conduct or state of mind to make it more probable they acted similarly at the relevant time. The decision assists in distinguishing evidence of an actual act (proof of a fact) from evidence of a general tendency or propensity and clarifies the purpose for which such evidence can be led.

---

### Gardiner v R (2006) 162 A Crim R 233 — context for tendency evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Gardiner v R (2006) 162 A Crim R 233] [TOPIC: tendency evidence context] [CONCEPTS: Gardiner v R, tendency, probative value, sexual offences]
Gardiner v R (2006) 162 A Crim R 233 is cited in discussions of tendency evidence to illustrate application in sexual-offence contexts and to assist in framing the kind of inferences tendency evidence is intended to support.

---

### Patterson v Tasmania [2017] TASCCA — non-tendency uses and actual conduct vs tendency
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Patterson v Tasmania [2017] TASCCA] [TOPIC: non-tendency evidence and distinguishing actual conduct] [CONCEPTS: Patterson v Tasmania, non-tendency purpose, actual conduct, notice requirements, limiting directions]
Patterson v Tasmania [2017] TASCCA explains that evidence of actual conduct or motive is not necessarily tendency evidence and highlights the court’s duty to examine whether evidence proffered for a non-tendency purpose is legitimately used for that purpose. If such evidence could be misused for tendency reasoning, the trial judge must direct the jury accordingly and consider the necessity of notice or exclusion.

---

### Phillips v R 225 CLR 303 — need to prove the accused committed the act, not someone else
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Phillips v R 225 CLR 303] [TOPIC: proof of identity and accused's actual act] [CONCEPTS: Phillips v R, identity, proof of accused's act, mistaken identity, burden of proof]
Phillips v R 225 CLR 303 underscores that evidence must prove that the accused — not someone else — committed the act in issue. Similarity or propensity evidence cannot substitute for proof that the accused specifically committed the alleged act; tendency or coincidence material must assist the fact-finder in bridging that gap.

---

### Sutton v R (1984) 152 CLR 528 — coincidence reasoning and uniqueness
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Sutton v R (1984) 152 CLR 528] [TOPIC: coincidence evidence and striking similarity] [CONCEPTS: Sutton v R, coincidence evidence, uniqueness, striking similarity, identification]
Sutton v R (1984) 152 CLR 528 is a foundational authority on coincidence reasoning: it explains that where the Crown relies on similarities between crimes to identify a single perpetrator, the similarities must be sufficiently unique or striking to exclude coincidence, particularly where there is no other connecting evidence.

---

### R v Bell [2002] NSWCCA 2 — coincidence where accused intricately involved
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Bell [2002] NSWCCA 2] [TOPIC: coincidence reasoning in complex involvement cases] [CONCEPTS: R v Bell, coincidence, intricate involvement, pattern, identification]
R v Bell [2002] NSWCCA 2 demonstrates that coincidence reasoning may succeed without striking similarity where the evidence establishes the accused’s intricate involvement in multiple events, permitting an inference that the accused was responsible for each event when viewed in their entirety.

---

### Tasmania v Standage [2012] TASSC 88 — combined reasoning and identity
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Standage [2012] TASSC 88] [TOPIC: combined tendency and coincidence reasoning] [CONCEPTS: Standage, combined evidence, identity, similarity, circumstantial proof]
Tasmania v Standage [2012] TASSC 88 discusses how coincidence and tendency reasoning may be combined, stressing that the probability of similarities being explicable by coincidence is crucial when admitting coincidence evidence and that similarity remains a significant factor when assessing tendency evidence’s strength.

---

### Tognolini v R [2011] VSCA 394 — cogency test for identification via similar-fact evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tognolini v R [2011] VSCA 394] [TOPIC: cogency threshold for identification by similar facts] [CONCEPTS: Tognolini, cogent enough, identification, similar fact evidence, admissibility]
Tognolini v R [2011] VSCA 394 holds that the question for admissibility of similar-fact evidence is whether it is cogent enough, in the context of the whole case, to be admitted as proof of identification of the offender. The decision highlights that cogency depends on all available evidence, not on similarity alone.

---

### CW v R [2010] VSCA 288 — pattern and improbability against coincidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: CW v R [2010] VSCA 288] [TOPIC: pattern evidence creating improbability of coincidence] [CONCEPTS: CW v R, pattern of behaviour, coincidence improbable, business dealings, arson examples]
CW v R [2010] VSCA 288 illustrates that when a pattern of behaviour clearly connects the accused to multiple events (for instance, repeated threats followed by fires), it may be contrary to ordinary experience to explain the series of events by coincidence, thereby supporting admissibility of similar-fact evidence.

---

### OPP v Boardman [1975] AC 421 — independent witness corroboration and similarity
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: OPP v Boardman [1975] AC 421] [TOPIC: independent witness similarity and probative force] [CONCEPTS: Boardman, independent witnesses, corroboration, concoction, probative force]
OPP v Boardman [1975] AC 421 provides that similarity in independent witnesses’ accounts increases the improbability of concoction and therefore has special probative force, subject to considerations about contamination and independence.

---

### Hock v R (1988) 165 CLR 292 — contamination/concoction issues at common law
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Hock v R (1988) 165 CLR 292] [TOPIC: contamination and concoction] [CONCEPTS: Hock v R, concoction, contamination, reliability, prior inconsistent accounts]
Hock v R (1988) 165 CLR 292 addresses the common-law reduction in probative force of similar-fact evidence where there is a real chance that the evidence has been concocted or contaminated, emphasizing scrutiny of independent accounts.

---

### WRC (2002) 130 A Crim R 89 — witness similarity and common-law basis
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: WRC (2002) 130 A Crim R 89] [TOPIC: similar witness accounts and probative value] [CONCEPTS: WRC, witness similarity, independent evidence, common law, probative strength]
WRC (2002) 130 A Crim R 89 is cited for the proposition that similarities in independent witness accounts can render the evidence highly probative, but courts recognize these were historically common-law principles and must be reconciled with statutory provisions.

---

### BBH v R [2012] HCA 9 — independent witness corroboration strengthens probative force
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: BBH v R [2012] HCA 9] [TOPIC: independent witness corroboration] [CONCEPTS: BBH v R, independent evidence, corroboration, probative value, similarity]
BBH v R [2012] HCA 9 held that when an independent witness’s evidence coincides with a complainant’s account in a way that excludes collusion, the coincidence substantially increases the probative force of the material and is a valid basis for admissibility.

---

### Donoghue v Tasmania [2016] TASCCA 17 — evaluative admissibility and cross-admissibility approval
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Donoghue v Tasmania [2016] TASCCA 17] [TOPIC: admissibility evaluation and cross-admissibility] [CONCEPTS: Donoghue v Tasmania, evaluative test, s97, cross-admissibility, appellate guidance]
Donoghue v Tasmania [2016] TASCCA 17 reiterates that admissibility under s 97 is an evaluative judgment and approves cross-admissibility reasoning where the evidence may serve both tendency and coincidence purposes, subject to statutory thresholds.

---

### L v Tasmania (2006) 15 Tas R 381 — evaluative process for admissibility
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: L v Tasmania (2006) 15 Tas R 381] [TOPIC: evaluative nature of admissibility decisions] [CONCEPTS: L v Tasmania, evaluative judgment, s97, appellate review, significant probative value]
L v Tasmania (2006) 15 Tas R 381 explains that the admissibility of tendency evidence is an evaluative task requiring the judge to weigh probative value against prejudice and that the appellate court must reassess that evaluative judgment where required.

---

### KMJ v Tasmania (2011) 218 A Crim R 87 — appellate assessment of tendency admissibility
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: KMJ v Tasmania (2011) 218 A Crim R 87] [TOPIC: appellate role in admissibility] [CONCEPTS: KMJ v Tasmania, appellate review, miscarriage of justice, s97, significant probative value]
KMJ v Tasmania (2011) 218 A Crim R 87 confirms the appellate court’s responsibility to determine whether tendency evidence was correctly admitted and whether its admission resulted in a miscarriage of justice.

---

### McPhillamy v R [2018] HCA 52 — tendency standards and appellate principles
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: McPhillamy v R [2018] HCA 52] [TOPIC: high court guidance on tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: McPhillamy v R, significant probative value, appellate assessment, sexual offending, tendency]
McPhillamy v R [2018] HCA 52 provides High Court guidance on the assessment of similar-fact evidence, emphasising careful analysis of whether the tendency alleged is sufficiently particularized and materially probative to justify admission.

---

### Tasmania v L [2013] TASSC 47 — take probative value at its highest
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v L [2013] TASSC 47] [TOPIC: assume evidence accepted in probative assessment] [CONCEPTS: Tasmania v L, assume accepted, probative value at highest, s97]
Tasmania v L [2013] TASSC 47 instructs that, for threshold purposes, the court must assume the tendency evidence will be accepted and draw any favourable inference when judging probative value.

---

### Tasmania v W (No.2) [2012] TASSC 48 — context in assessing probative value
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v W (No.2) [2012] TASSC 48] [TOPIC: context of assessment and non-necessity of striking similarity] [CONCEPTS: Tasmania v W (No.2), context, probative value, not necessary to be strikingly similar]
Tasmania v W (No.2) [2012] TASSC 48 reiterates that tendency evidence need not be strikingly similar to be significant and that evaluation must be contextualised within the full evidentiary matrix.

---

### Tasmania v Finnegan (No. 2) [2012] TASSC 1 — application examples for probative assessment
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Finnegan (No. 2) [2012] TASSC 1] [TOPIC: probative assessment examples] [CONCEPTS: Finnegan, probative examples, contextual assessment, s97]
Tasmania v Finnegan (No. 2) [2012] TASSC 1 offers applied examples of how courts assess probative value of tendency evidence in context, illustrating common evaluative considerations.

---

### DSR v R (2012) 215 A Crim R 349 — probative value authorities
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: DSR v R (2012) 215 A Crim R 349] [TOPIC: probative value principles] [CONCEPTS: DSR v R, probative value, similar fact evidence, threshold]
DSR v R (2012) 215 A Crim R 349 contributes to authorities explaining that tendency evidence must be influential in the context of fact-finding to reach the threshold of significant probative value.

---

### R v Shamouil (2006) 66 NSWLR 112 — assessing probative force
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Shamouil (2006) 66 NSWLR 112] [TOPIC: assessing probative force of tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: Shamouil, NSWLR, probative force, evaluation, admission]
R v Shamouil (2006) 66 NSWLR 112 is used to illustrate how courts analyse the potential influence of similar-fact evidence on disputed issues and the requirement that it be significant in achieving that influence.

---

### R v Sood [2007] NSWCCA 214 — context for probative evaluation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Sood [2007] NSWCCA 214] [TOPIC: probative evaluation in NSWCCA decisions] [CONCEPTS: R v Sood, probative evaluation, NSWCCA, significant probative value]
R v Sood [2007] NSWCCA 214 sets out considerations adopted in appellate review of the probative value of tendency evidence, particularly in sexual offence contexts.

---

### R v Lockyer (1996) 89 A Crim R 457 — significance requirement for probative value
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Lockyer (1996) 89 A Crim R 457] [TOPIC: significant probative value threshold] [CONCEPTS: R v Lockyer, significant probative value, Hunt CJ, admission threshold]
R v Lockyer (1996) 89 A Crim R 457 emphasises that tendency or similar-fact evidence must be more than merely relevant to be “significant” — it must be important or of consequence in addressing an issue in dispute.

---

### R v Fletcher (2005) 156 A Crim R 308 — probative and prejudice balancing
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Fletcher (2005) 156 A Crim R 308] [TOPIC: balancing probative value and prejudice] [CONCEPTS: R v Fletcher, prejudice balancing, substantial probative value, admissibility]
R v Fletcher (2005) 156 A Crim R 308 provides guidance on weighing probative value against prejudicial effect, describing how significant probative value can justify admission despite potential prejudice when safeguards exist.

---

### R v PWD (2010) 205 A Crim R 75 — tendency probative context
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v PWD (2010) 205 A Crim R 75] [TOPIC: tendency probative value in context] [CONCEPTS: R v PWD, probative context, combination of evidence, jury directions]
R v PWD (2010) 205 A Crim R 75 is cited for the proposition that tendency evidence’s probative value must be evaluated in context and that trial directions may mitigate prejudice sufficiently to permit admission.

---

### IMM v R [2016] HCA 14 — tendency must be influential in fact-finding
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: IMM v R [2016] HCA 14] [TOPIC: influence requirement for probative value] [CONCEPTS: IMM v R, influential probative value, HCA, tendency evidence]
IMM v R [2016] HCA 14 establishes that tendency evidence must be potentially influential in the context of fact-finding — that is, it should be able to affect the probability of a fact in issue — to have significant probative value.

---

### Pfennig v R (1995) 182 CLR 461 — rarity increases weight of similarity
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Pfennig v R (1995) 182 CLR 461] [TOPIC: rarity of crime and similarity weight] [CONCEPTS: Pfennig, rarity, uniqueness, similarity weight, coincidence]
Pfennig v R (1995) 182 CLR 461 explains that the rarer or more unusual the offending, the greater the weight that can be attached to any similarity between events, and correspondingly fewer similarities may be needed to reach the threshold of significant probative value.

---

### Perry v R (1982) 150 CLR 580 — independent witness similarity and improbability of concoction
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Perry v R (1982) 150 CLR 580] [TOPIC: independent witness similarity] [CONCEPTS: Perry v R, independent witnesses, similarity, concoction, probative force]
Perry v R (1982) 150 CLR 580 supports the view that independent witnesses giving similar accounts increase the improbability of concoction and strengthen the probative force of the evidence.

---

### BP v R; R v BP [2010] NSWCCA 303 — similarity need not be striking
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: BP v R; R v BP [2010] NSWCCA 303] [TOPIC: degree of similarity required] [CONCEPTS: BP v R, NSWCCA, similarity, not necessarily striking, probative value]
BP v R; R v BP [2010] NSWCCA 303 confirms that similar fact evidence need not be strikingly similar to be probative; degree of similarity is one factor among others in the evaluative assessment.

---

### R v Ford (2009) 273 ALR 286 — similarity/probative assessment
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Ford (2009) 273 ALR 286] [TOPIC: similarity and probative assessment] [CONCEPTS: R v Ford, probative assessment, similarity factors]
R v Ford (2009) 273 ALR 286 reinforces that courts must assess whether similar-fact material materially affects an issue in the case and that lack of close resemblance does not automatically render it non-probative.

---

### Tasmania v Y [2007] TASSC 112 — credibility vs tendency; admissibility basis
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Y [2007] TASSC 112] [TOPIC: credibility-related evidence and tendency basis] [CONCEPTS: Tasmania v Y, credibility, tendency, inadmissible for credibility alone]
Tasmania v Y [2007] TASSC 112 notes that evidence only relevant to credibility is generally inadmissible under the common law approach and such evidence may be admitted as tendency evidence where the accused is shown to have a consistent mode of acting; identical complainant accounts contributed to probative value in that case.

---

### VOT v WA 184 A Crim R 284 — meaning of “substantial” weight
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: VOT v WA 184 A Crim R 284] [TOPIC: interpreting "substantial" in balancing] [CONCEPTS: VOT v WA, substantial, weighty evidence, s101 interpretation]
VOT v WA 184 A Crim R 284 supplies judicial commentary that “substantial” in the balancing test connotes weighty, significant evidence that carries real force in tending to prove or disprove facts in issue.

---

### Papakosmas v R 196 CLR 297 — prejudice not merely proof of Crown case
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Papakosmas v R 196 CLR 297] [TOPIC: scope of prejudicial effect] [CONCEPTS: Papakosmas, prejudicial effect, unfair prejudice, jury misuse]
Papakosmas v R 196 CLR 297 explains that evidence is not prejudicial simply because it supports the Crown case; unfair prejudice involves a risk of jury misuse or inflammatory reaction that distorts rational assessment.

---

### Festa v R 208 CLR 593 — jury and prejudice considerations
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Festa v R 208 CLR 593] [TOPIC: jury capacity and prejudice] [CONCEPTS: Festa v R, jury directions, prejudice mitigation, presumption of compliance]
Festa v R 208 CLR 593 addresses the role of jury directions in mitigating prejudice and the court’s general presumption that juries will follow proper judicial directions.

---

### R v Watkins [2005] 153 A Crim R 434 — danger of prior guilt diverting jury
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Watkins [2005] 153 A Crim R 434] [TOPIC: risk of jury distraction by prior guilt] [CONCEPTS: R v Watkins, prior guilt, jury distraction, intent question]
R v Watkins [2005] 153 A Crim R 434 emphasises the danger that recognition of a defendant’s prior misconduct could divert a jury from properly considering the evidence as it bears on intent or other charged elements.

---

### R v GAG (2007) 178 A Crim R 408 — emotional prejudice risk
