[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v GAG (2007) 178 A Crim R 408] [TOPIC: emotional prejudice from similar-fact evidence] [CONCEPTS: R v GAG, emotional prejudice, unfair influence, sexual offences]
R v GAG (2007) 178 A Crim R 408 warns that similar-fact evidence can evoke emotional responses in jurors leading to unfair prejudice where emotion rather than reason dictates verdicts.

---

### Roach v R 242 CLR 610 — jury treating similar facts as establishing guilt
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Roach v R 242 CLR 610] [TOPIC: risk of jury inferring guilt from similar facts] [CONCEPTS: Roach v R, jury inference, misuse of similar facts, prejudice]
Roach v R 242 CLR 610 identifies the risk that juries will treat similar facts as establishing guilt where logic or experience does not warrant that conclusion, which is the kind of unfair prejudice s 101 guards against.

---

### Tasmania v Martin (No. 2) — examples of prejudice balancing
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Martin (No. 2) (citation in source)] [TOPIC: prejudice balancing examples] [CONCEPTS: Tasmania v Martin (No.2), balancing, prejudice, s101, evidentiary weight]
Tasmania v Martin (No. 2) provides judicial examples and commentary about how to measure prejudice and the combined effect of probative force and potential unfairness.

---

### Neill‑Fraser v Tasmania [2012] TASCCA 2 — prejudice examples
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Neill‑Fraser v Tasmania [2012] TASCCA 2] [TOPIC: prejudice and evidentiary risk] [CONCEPTS: Neill-Fraser, TASCCA, prejudice, unfair influence]
Neill‑Fraser v Tasmania [2012] TASCCA 2 addresses the kinds of prejudicial impact that similar-fact evidence can have, illustrative for s 101 assessments.

---

### R v Hawker [2004] NSWCCA 427 — uncharged conduct and tendency evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Hawker [2004] NSWCCA 427] [TOPIC: admissibility of evidence of uncharged conduct] [CONCEPTS: R v Hawker, uncharged conduct, prior bad acts, admissibility]
R v Hawker [2004] NSWCCA 427 confirms that the fact that tendency evidence addresses conduct for which the person has not been charged is not, in itself, a ground for exclusion; the statutory tests remain the focus.

---

<!-- block_045 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4 — Para [55] extract and practitioner note
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4] [CITATION: Brown v Tasmania (2019) TASCCA 4 - Para 55 extract] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: tendency need not be closely similar to alleged offence] [CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, similarity, probative value, Hughes v R, R v Bauer]

This chunk covers: Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4 para [55] and the point that tendency need not be closely similar to alleged offence.

[55] *... There is no requirement for the tendency evidence to be closely similar to the alleged offence for it to have significant probative value, although if it is strikingly similar, it will have significant probative value: see Hughes v The Queen [\[2017\] HCA 20](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2017/20.html), [344 ALR 187](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=344%20ALR%20187) at [\[39\]](http://www8.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/viewdoc/au/cases/cth/HCA/2017/20.html#para39). What is required, as occurred in this case, is that there is some feature or features between the tendency evidence and the alleged offending that links them: see R v Bauer (above) at \[85\]. The linking factors were referred to by the trial judge as set out at \[52\].*

---

### Chunk 2 — Brown v Tasmania — Practitioner commentary from para [3] onwards
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4] [CITATION: Brown v Tasmania (2019) TASCCA 4 - Practitioner commentary para 3 onwards] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: practitioner response to defence similarity argument] [CONCEPTS: defence argument, circumstantial evidence, tendency as circumstantial, identity]

This chunk covers: practitioner commentary from para [3] onwards of Brown v Tasmania and the counter to the defence's similarity argument.

Essentially when faced with the defence argument: *There was not sufficient similarity between the conduct said to establish the tendency, and the conduct of the person who perpetrated the crime alleged in the indictment, to support a reasoning process that it was highly probable that the same person must have committed both crimes.*

The counter is that the tendency is: *Yes, however, when combined with other evidence, the asserted tendency evidence constituted an important component of a strong circumstantial case.*

The gist is that the proposed 'tendency' is just another form of circumstantial evidence that is to be considered with other evidence.

In this case the stated alleged tendency was:

> *"a tendency to impose himself in a sexual manner upon females whilst they were in a vulnerable position, namely whilst asleep in bed"*

---

### Chunk 3 — Brown v Tasmania — Facts and trial court ruling
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Brown v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 4] [CITATION: Brown v Tasmania (2019) TASCCA 4 - Facts and trial ruling] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: facts, age differences, nature of tendency evidence, trial judge reasoning] [CONCEPTS: spooning, vulnerable position, probative value, DNA, flight, false alibi]

This chunk covers: the facts in Brown and the trial court's ruling on the probative value of tendency evidence.

Facts:

- Victims were vastly different ages

- "Tendency evidence was not an actual rape just spooning while vic was asleep. And this was after the offence date

- They were a year apart

The trial court ruled, correctly:

*The crucial aspect with respect to the circumstantial reasoning open to the jury on the question of identity was that the appellant had a tendency to impose himself in a sexual manner upon females. The fact that the female was in a vulnerable position at the time contributed to the probative value of the tendency, but was not dependent on the female being in bed or asleep. The feature of imposition meant that the appellant\'s tendency was to physically engage in a sexual way with a female without seeking or obtaining her consent. Clearly, taken alone, this tendency was too broad to identify him as the offender in this case. However, when considered with the other circumstantial evidence relating to the question of identity, the evidence supported the improbability that another person with similar characteristics, who was in the area at the same time, and had the same tendency, that is, a tendency to impose himself sexually upon females without seeking or obtaining their consent, was responsible for the crime. It also strengthened the significance of the location of DNA which had a high probability match with the accused, and supported the conclusions available from the evidence of flight and false alibi. The tendency evidence was a piece of circumstantial evidence, which the trial judge correctly concluded, having regard to the balance of the circumstantial case, had significant probative value. It was an important \"strand\" in a \"strands in the cable\" circumstantial case. The evidence was correctly admitted.*

---

### Chunk 4 — Distinction: TENDENCY CONCERNS THE PERSON; COINCIDENCE CONCERNS EVENTS
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 95] [CITATION: Distinction - Tendency vs Coincidence - s95] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: fundamental distinction between tendency and coincidence evidence] [CONCEPTS: tendency, coincidence, s95, admissibility, person vs event]

This chunk covers: the core distinction between tendency and coincidence evidence and reference to s 95 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas).

- **TENDENCY CONCERNS THE PERSON**

- **COINCIDENCE CONCERNS EVENTS**

Evidence is generally inadmissible to prove a tendency purpose or to prove a fact coincidentally although it may be admissible for another purpose (s 95 of the *Evidence Act 2001* (the Act)) unless it is admissible under the provisions of the Act.

Tendency and coincidence evidence is governed by ss 97, 98 and 101 of the Act.

It is a rule of exclusion. That is, evidence of tendency or coincidence is not admissible unless the above provisions are satisfied.

Now when dealing with the evidence, the first point to understand is the difference between the two types of evidence.

---

### Chunk 5 — Definition of tendency evidence (what it is, cited cases)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 97, 101] [CITATION: Definition of tendency evidence - Elomar/Gardiner/Patterson references] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: what tendency evidence is and when it is used] [CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, state of mind, inference, Elomar v R, Patterson v Tasmania]

This chunk covers: the definition and purpose of tendency evidence and key authorities.

In the case of tendency evidence we are dealing with a person. In criminal law that is normally the accused, although not necessarily. Tendency evidence is evidence tendered to establish that a person has or had acted in a particular way, or has or had a particular state of mind. It is evidence that is tendered in order to provide the foundation for an inference that because a person has or had that tendency, it is more likely that he or she behaved in a particular way or had a particular state of mind at a time or in circumstances relevant to the issues in dispute: *Elomar v R* \[2014\] NSWCCA 303 at \[253\]; *Gardiner v R* (2006) 162 A Crim R 233; *Patterson v Tasmania* \[2017\] TASCCA per Wood J at \[68\]

Evidence of actual conduct and an actual state of mind is not tendency evidence: *Patterson v Tasmania* supra per Wood, J at \[25\]. Similarly, evidence that an accused actually had a motive to do something is not tendency evidence: *Patterson v Tasmania* supra at \[22\].

It is important to remember that the evidence must prove that the accused committed the particular act or had a particular state of mind and not somebody else, such as the complainant: *Phillips v R* 225 CLR 303.

---

### Chunk 6 — Coincidence evidence: definition and when it applies
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 98] [CITATION: Coincidence evidence - definition and application s98] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: what coincidence evidence is and its use in identification reasoning] [CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, events, similarity, identification, Sutton v R]

This chunk covers: the nature of coincidence evidence and how similarity between events can be used for identification.

Coincidence evidence does not relate to persons, it relates to events, such events being similar. For the purpose of the criminal law, in effect, the coincidence rule applies where the Crown contends that because two crimes are so substantially similar one person must have committed both crimes. In that way, the Crown may not have sufficient evidence in respect of each individual crime but a jury can use the evidence in respect of each crime to conclude the accused committed both crimes. Where there is no other evidence the similarities between the two crimes would have to be unique or striking: *Sutton v R* (1984) 152 CLR 528.

Similar conduct on different occasions can reveal a pattern from which it may be inferred, via coincidence reasoning, that an accused did a particular act or had a particular state of mind even though the crimes may not be strikingly similar but the evidence shows the accused is intricately involved in both crimes: *R v Bell* \[2002\] NSWCCA 2; *Tasmania v Standage* \[2012\] TASSC 88.

Or there may be a combination of the above two options, which makes it outside human experience that the crimes were carried out in such a like manner and the accused behaved in such a like manner other than the accused was committing both crimes.

---

### Chunk 7 — Case law excerpts: Tognolini v R, Judd AJA, CW v R, Perry v R, and discussion
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Tognolini/CW/Judd/Perry - Case extracts and commentary] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: leading authorities on similarity, probative value, pattern behaviour and independent witnesses] [CONCEPTS: striking similarity, signature, pattern of behaviour, independent witnesses, concoction]

This chunk covers: quoted passages from Tognolini v R, Judd AJA's observations, CW v R and Perry v R and their implications.

In *Tognolini v R* \[2011\] VSCA 394 Nettle JA said at \[6\] -

> > Ultimately, the question is not whether the means of commission of the crime is in each case the same or strikingly similar but whether the evidence sought to be admitted \"is cogent enough to be admitted as proof of identification of this offender\". Logically, that depends upon all the evidence in the case and whether, when the putative similar fact evidence is considered in the context of all the evidence, its probative value sufficiently outweighs any prejudicial effect as to render its admission just.
>
> > Understandably, cases concerning the admissibility of similar fact evidence tend to divide according to whether or not there is direct testimony of the accused\'s commission of the offence charged. Where there is no direct evidence of the accused\'s commission of the offence charged, the need for striking similarity or signature is logically more likely to apply. But where there is some direct evidence of involvement apart from the similar fact evidence, the similar fact evidence need not be as \"uniquely denominative\" of the accused, because it is not the sole means of identifying the accused, and something less than striking similarity or signature is more likely to suffice.
>
> > So, too, if there is evidence which in some way connects an accused with a series of offences, as well as with the offence which is charged, then, even though it may fall short of striking similarity or signature, the combined effect of the evidence can render it so improbable that any person other than the accused committed each of the offences as to make evidence of all admissible.
>
> > \.....the applicant made threats of consequences which, not long later, were followed by conflagration.

And Judd AJA said at \[54\] -

> While the evidence disclosed some similarity in the way the fires were started, that was not the primary connecting factor upon which the Crown case against the applicant depended. There was an evident pattern of behaviour on the part of the applicant that was before his Honour at the time of his ruling, and the existence of the pattern was established on the evidence at trial. The applicant sought to advance his own business and eliminate competition by approaching the operators of competing businesses, threatening them with fire, or alluding to the risk of fire, and demanding their commercial cooperation.

In *CW v R* \[201O\] VSCA 288 at \[19\]:

> Not only was each victim a person with whom the applicant had had business dealings, but he was in a current dispute with each of them. In the circumstances, the judge was entitled to conclude\...that\...it would be contrary to ordinary experience for this series of fires, affecting these particular victims, to have occurred by coincidence.

See also *Perry v R* (1982) 150 CLR 580 at 587.

More controversially, similarities in the accounts of two or more witnesses regarding the conduct of an accused make it improbable that the witnesses are telling lies in the absence of concoction or contamination: See *OPP v Boardman* \[1975\] AC 421; *Hock v R* (1988) 165 CLR 292; *WRC* (2002) 130 A Crim R 89; "Uniform Evidence Law" Odgers 13^th^ ed pp 255-6.

---

### Chunk 8 — Admissibility of tendency evidence under ss 97 and 101; evaluative judgment
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 97, 101] [CITATION: Admissibility - s97(1)(b) and s101(2) - evaluative judgment] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: statutory admissibility tests and appellate review] [CONCEPTS: s97(1)(b), s101(2), significant probative value, substantial outweigh, evaluative process]

This chunk covers: statutory admissibility requirements for tendency evidence and the evaluative nature of the admissibility decision.

Tendency evidence is governed by ss 97 and 101 of the Act. Such evidence is admissible only if the court thinks that the evidence, either by itself or having regard to other evidence, will have "significant probative value" (s 97(1)(b)).

The evidence is not admissible unless its probative value substantially outweighs any prejudicial effect it may have on the accused (s 101(2)).

Determination·of the admissibility of such evidence involves an evaluative judgment: *L v Tasmania* (2006) 15 Tas R 381 per Underwood CJ at \[52\]; *Donoghue v Tasmania* \[2016\] TASCCA 17 per Estcourt J at \[22\].

The evaluative process involves a consideration of the nature of the evidence, the probative value of the evidence and an evaluation of whether the probative value substantially outweighs any prejudicial effect.

Because it is an evaluative process and not a discretion, on appeal before the Court of Criminal Appeal it is that court's task to determine on the evidence at trial whether the tendency evidence was correct or whether it caused a miscarriage of justice: *L v Tasmania* supra; *KMJ v Tasmania* (2011) 218 A Crim R 87 per Evans J at \[4\]; *McPhillamy v R* \[2018\] HCA 52 at \[11\]. The principles in *House v R* do not apply.

---

### Chunk 9 — Probative value: assessment principles and authorities
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 97, 101] [CITATION: Probative value - assessment principles and cases] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: how to assess probative value; significance in context of other evidence] [CONCEPTS: probative value, take evidence at its highest, IMM v R, Hughes v R, R v Bauer, context]

This chunk covers: principles for assessing probative value of tendency/coincidence evidence and key authorities.

In assessing the probative value of the evidence, it must be assumed that the evidence will be accepted and that any inference open to a jury and favourable to the Crown will be drawn. The probative value of the evidence must be taken at its highest. The credibility or reliability of the evidence, or the witness giving the evidence, is irrelevant. The consideration is whether the evidence could rationally effect the probability of a fact in issue: *Tasmania v L* \[2013\] TASSC 47 at \[42\]; *Tasmania v W (No.2)* \[2012\] TASSC 48 at \[11\]; *Tasmania v Finnegan (No. 2)* \[2012\] TASSC 1 at \[22\]; *KMJ v Tasmania* supra at \[30\] & \[34\]; *DSR v R* (2012) 215 A Crim R 349; *R v Shamouil* (2006) 66 NSWLR 112 at \[61\]; *R v Sood* \[2007\] NSWCCA 214 at \[26\]-\[40\]; *R v Bauer* \[2018 HCA 40 at \[95\].

Tendency and coincidence evidence has significant probative value if it has more than mere relevance and if it is important or of consequence. It does not have to have a substantial degree of relevance: *Tasmania v W (No.2)* supra; *R v Lockyer* (1996) 89 A Crim R 457 per Hunt CJ at CL at 459; *L v Tasmania* supra at \[31\], \[391\]-\[393\]; *R v Fletcher* (2005) 156 A Crim R 308 at \[67\], \[110\]-\[111\]; *R v PWD* (2010) 205 A Crim R 75 at \[66\].

To have probative value that is significant, tendency evidence must potentially be influential in the context of fact finding or potentially have importance in establishing one or more of the disputed facts: *IMM v R* \[2016\] HCA 14 at \[46\]; *Hughes v R* \[2017\] HCA 20 at \[40\].

The assessment of whether the tendency evidence has significant probative value involves consideration of two interrelated but separate matters. Firstly, taking the evidence at its highest, does it support, and if so to what extent, the alleged tendency? Secondly, to what extent does the tendency support the elements of the offence: *Hughes v R* supra at \[26\] and \[34\].

In assessing the probative value of the evidence, it must be assessed in the context of the other evidence and the nature of the facts in issue: *IMM v R* (2016) 257 CLR 300 per French CJ, Kiefel, Bell and Keane JJ at \[46\]; *R v Bauer* supra at \[58\]-\[60\]; *Hughes v R* supra at \[40\]. Thus, the tendency evidence does not have to have significant probative value looking at it in isolation but rather looking at it in the context of the entire evidence.

It is not necessary for tendency evidence to be strikingly similar, or even closely similar, to the alleged offence for it to have significant probative value but if the evidence is strikingly similar it is likely to have significant probative value: *Hughes v R* supra at \[39\]; *BP v R; R v BP* \[2010\] NSWCCA 303 at \[108\]; *R v Ford* (2009) 273 ALR 286 at \[38\]; *R v PWD* supra at \[79\]; *Tasmania v W (No. 2)* supra at \[13\]; *R v Fletcher* supra at \[57\]-\[60\]; *R v Bauer* supra at \[58\].

What is required is that ordinarily there should be some feature between the tendency evidence and the alleged offending that links them: *R v Bauer* supra at \[56\]; *McPhillamy v R* supra at \[31\].

In a single complainant sexual offence case, generally the fact that the accused has committed sexual offences on one complainant is sufficient connection to prove a tendency of significant probative value without proving some other special feature because where one person is sexually attracted to another and has sought to fulfil that attraction by committing a sexual act with him or her, it is more likely that person will continue to seek to fulfil the attraction by committing further sexual acts when the occasion presents: *R v Bauer* supra at \[60\].

However, in multiple complainant sexual offence cases there must be some common feature of or about the offending, apart from the fact it is alleged a sexual offence occurred that demonstrates the accused acted in a particular way, proof of which increases the likelihood that the account under consideration is true: *Bauer v R* supra at s8; *McPhillamy v R* \[2018\] HCA 52.

Generally speaking, a mere sexual interest in a particular class of persons, although relevant, would not meet the requirement of significant probative value for the admission of tendency evidence. Generally, it is the tendency to act on the sexual interest that gives evidence in sexual cases its probative value: *McPhillamy v R* supra at \[28\].

An inclination on the part of an adult to engage in sexual conduct with underage persons and to act upon it is a matter outside ordinary human experience. When led in with other factors such as grooming or engaging in such behaviour where there is a risk of detection the conduct will have significant probative force: *Hughes v R* supra at \[57\].

Often when identity is in issue more similarity will be required to reach the level of significant probative value. However, where a crime is rare or unusual the more weight that can be given to a similarity the more difficult it is to dismiss it as a coincidence, thus giving it greater probative force: *Pfennig v R* (1995) 182 CLR 461 at 472; *Tasmania v Standage* \[2012\] TASSC 88 per Evans J at \[110\].

Further, in assessing the force of any similarities they should not be looked at in isolation but as a whole in the context of the entire case: *Sutton v R* (1984) 152 CLR 528 at 536 and 550; *Tasmania v Standage* supra at \[110\]; *Hughes v R* supra at \[40\].

---

### Chunk 10 — Coincidence evidence: requirements and factors (practical checklist)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 98] [CITATION: Coincidence evidence - factors checklist s98] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: factors to consider when assessing coincidence evidence] [CONCEPTS: degree of similarity, number of similarities, timing, capacity to affect fact in issue, rarity of crime]

This chunk covers: practical factors to assess probative value of coincidence evidence under s 98.

Unlike tendency evidence, in order for coincidence evidence to be admitted pursuant to s 98 of the Act it does have to be similar. How similar as to reach significant probative value will depend on the nature of the case and the other evidence.

Factors to be taken into account when determining probative value in respect to coincidence reasoning include:

- The degree of similarity

- The number of similarities

- The timing of the similarities

- The capacity of each similarity, either individually or in conjunction with one another, or with the other evidence, to affect a fact in issue.

  See *Sutton v R* (1984) 152 CLR 528 per Gibbs CJ at 536 and Brennan J at 550.

  The more rare the crime, the less similarities that are required for the evidence to be of significant probative value.

  Further, in many cases, it may not be just similarities that will give the evidence significant probative value. The test is whether the evidence is cogent enough to be admitted as proof of identification of the accused. If there are similarities between the crimes and other evidence that intrinsically link the accused to the crimes, this may be of significant probative value even if the crimes are not strikingly similar: *Tognolini v R* supra at \[6\]-\[8\]; *CW v R* supra at \[10\]-\[22\]; *Perry v R* (1980) 150 CLR 580 per Gibb CJ at 587.

  Further, in respect to tendency and coincidence evidence, the court should not focus on the dissimilarities, rather it should focus on the similarities and whether they supports the tendency and coincidence reasoning: *Hughes v R* supra at \[56\].

  Although it is necessary to keep a distinction between tendency and coincidence evidence because the test is different, the distinction becomes less important where it is being argued that the same conduct should be admitted both for a tendency purpose and for coincidence reasoning. In *Standage v Tasmania* supra Evans J at \[5\] said:

> For the evidence to be cross-admissible as either or both coincidence evidence or tendency evidence, it must have significant probative value, s97(1)(b), and s98(1)(b). As to coincidence evidence, consistent with s98(1)(b), the probability of the similarities between the killings being explicable on the basis of coincidence is a crucial aspect of the assessment of the probative value of the evidence and its capacity to prove that the accused was the killer on each occasion. Whilst no similar statutory reference to similarities underpins an assessment of the probative value of the evidence as tendency evidence, it is well settled that a significant factor in the assessment of the strength of tendency evidence is the degree of similarity between the conduct on the occasions relied upon. See *FB v R* \[2011\] NSWCCA 217 at pars\[28\] -- \[30\]; *BP v R* \[2010\] NSWCCA 303 at \[108\], and *R v Fletcher* (2005) 156 A Crim R 308 at pars\[57\]-\[60\]. So whilst it is necessary to keep the distinction between coincidence evidence and tendency evidence in mind, the distinction is less stark in this case than it can be, as in arguing for the admission of the evidence as both coincidence evidence and tendency evidence ...

This passage was approved by the Court of Criminal Appeal in *Donohue v Tasmania* \[2016\] TASCCA 17.

---

### Chunk 11 — Prejudicial effect: s 101, "substantial" and "prejudice" explained with cases
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 101] [CITATION: Prejudicial effect - s101 substantial and prejudice] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: assessing unfair prejudice and substantial weight] [CONCEPTS: s101, substantial, unfair prejudice, jury misuse, Gilber v R, Papakosmas]

This chunk covers: the requirement under s 101 that probative value substantially outweigh prejudicial effect, what "substantial" and "prejudicial" mean and examples of prejudicial effects.

Pursuant to s 101 of the Act the probative value must substantially outweigh the prejudicial effect of the evidence.

**Substantial**

In *VOT v WA* 184 A Crim R 284 at 288, Steytler J said \"... *the evidence must be evidence that carries substantial weight in tending to prove or disprove facts in issue*\".

In *R v Fletcher* 156 A Crim R 308 \... substantial means large, weighty or big and indicates an absolute significance.

In *Tasmania v Martin (No. 2)* supra, Porter J said for the purposes of s 194M it is a combination of the above.

**Prejudice**

Evidence is not prejudicial merely because it establishes the Crown case. It has to be prejudicial in the sense that a jury will give it more weight than it deserves, or in other words that a jury will use it in some unfair way or it will inflame their prejudices (s 101): *Papakosmas v R* 196 CLR 297 per McHugh J at 325; *Festa v R* 208 CLR 593 per Gleeson CJ at 603 and McHugh J at 609-10.

- "\[the\] danger that the jury\'s recognition of the appellant\'s prior guilt was likely to divert them from a proper consideration of the evidence as bearing on the question of his intent in the charges before them": *R v Watkins* \[2005\] 153 A Crim R 434 at \[50\].

- "The primary danger is notwithstanding directions given by the trial judge the jury might reason no more rationally than that, if the respondent molested \[two other persons\] he did the same to the complainant and that emotion not rationality would govern": *R v GAG* (2007) 178 A Crim R 408.

- "\...the possibility that the jury will treat the similar facts as establishing an inference of guilt where neither logic nor experience would necessitate the conclusion that it clearly points to the guilt of the accused": *Roach v R* 242 CLR 610 at 617.

  See also *Tasmania v Martin (No. 2)* supra at \[63\]; *Neill-Fraser v Tasmania* \[2012\] TASCCA 2 at \[184\]; *Tasmania v W (No. 2)* supra at \[11\]; *BP v R; R v BP* supra at \[109\].

  Other prejudicial effects include:

- The jury may fail to appreciate a person to have a tendency to have a particular state of mind or to act in a particular way, may not have had that state of mind or may not have acted in that particular way on the occasion in issue.

- The jury may underestimate the number of persons who share that particular tendency.

- It may require the accused to answer a raft of uncharged conduct stretching back over many years.

  See *Hughes v R* supra at 17.

  In *Bauer* supra at \[62\] "*the trial judge was correct to hold that RC\'s evidence was not productive of unfair prejudice*". The relevant portion of the trial judge\'s ruling is contained at \[21\], specifically --

> ...Any prejudice is due to the inculpatory nature of the tendency evidence and is not unfair prejudice. The proposed tendency evidence goes to proof of the fact in issue in each charge and will not distract the jury from its task, and clear directions \... will ensure that there is no substitution of the tendency evidence for the charged acts and no impermissible reasoning towards guilt.

The directions by a trial judge to the jury must be taken into account when assessing the risk of unfair prejudice. Juries are often required to follow complex legal directions and disregard inadmissible evidence. It must be assumed that a jury will follow directions from the trial judge: *Gilbert v R* (2000) 201 CLR 414 at \[31\]-\[32\]; *R v PWD* supra at \[89\]-\[90\].

The fact that the tendency evidence addressed by the prosecution alleges criminal conduct of which the person has not been charged is irrelevant: *R v Hawker* \[2004\] NSWCCA 427.

---

### Chunk 12 — Concoction and contamination: common law vs statutory approach
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Concoction and contamination - common law and s101(5)] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: how collusion/concoction affects probative force under common law and Evidence Act] [CONCEPTS: concoction, contamination, collusion, s101(5), Hoch v R]

This chunk covers: how concoction or contamination affects tendency/coincidence evidence under common law and the Evidence Act.

At common law tendency and coincidence evidence will lose its probative force if there is a real chance that the evidence was concocted or contaminated: *Hoch v R* (1988) 165 CLR 292.

Under the Act the probability that the tendency or coincidence evidence may be the result of collusion, concoction or suggestion is not to be taken into account when determining the probative value of the evidence or its prejudicial effect (s 101(5)): *Bauer v R* supra at \[69\].

---

### Chunk 13 — Non-tendency evidence: when Part 6 exclusion provisions do not apply; examples
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 97(1)(a), 98(1)(a), ss 135, 137, s95] [CITATION: Non-tendency evidence - when Part 6 doesn't apply and examples] [CATEGORY: Evidentiary guidance] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: evidence admissible for non-tendency purposes and judicial duty to examine] [CONCEPTS: non-tendency, opportunity, identification, motive, res gestae, relationship evidence]

This chunk covers: when evidence is not being led for a tendency purpose, the Part 6 exclusion provisions do not apply, and examples of non-tendency evidence.

Now it is important to remember the exclusion provisions of Part 6 only apply if you are leading the evidence to prove tendency or coincidence reasoning. If you are leading the evidence for some other purpose, you do not have to comply with ss 97(1)(b), 98(1)(b) or 101 of the Act. Although you would still have to comply with ss 135 and 137 of the Act. In such circumstances, the court has a duty to examine the evidence of the parties. This is necessary because the court needs to determine whether there is a legitimate tendency purpose and, equally, if it is characterised as non­tendency evidence, the court will need to undertake an examination to determine whether there is a legitimate non-tendency purpose: *Patterson v Tasmania* supra at \[64\]. Of course, evidence may be relevant for a non-tendency purpose but it could also be tendency evidence where it is not intended to be led for a tendency purpose or it is inadmissible on that basis. The judge would have to direct a jury that it cannot use the evidence for tendency or coincidence reasoning (s 95 of the Act).

Where evidence is not being used for a tendency purpose there is no need to give notice of intention to adduce such evidence (ss 97(1)(a) and 98(1)(a)). Such evidence can include:

- evidence of opportunity

- identification

- motive

- *res gestae*

  And probably the most contentious of all, relationship evidence.

---

<!-- block_046 master -->

# Relationship evidence — Tasmanian criminal law

No items were listed under [UNPROCESSED] in the prior coverage report; therefore there are no additional doctrinal units to format from the uploaded part and no further chunks to produce.

<!-- block_046 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Standard objection and Response (Relationship evidence)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Relationship evidence - Standard objection and response] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: script] [TOPIC: Standard objection and counsel response regarding relationship evidence] [CONCEPTS: relationship evidence, objection, prejudicial questioning, relevance, tendency]

Tactical coverage: Standard objection and response regarding relationship evidence.

Standard objection:

Objection -- prejudicial questioning/ relevance/ tendency

Response:

- Response was admissible as context/ relationship evidence.

Relevant as it is part of the Factual matrix- puts specific complaint in context as offending behaviour that is escalating -- not submitted to go so far as tendency.

---

### Chunk 2 — Gipp v R (1998) discussion
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Gipp v R (1998) 194 CLR 106] [CITATION: Gipp v R (1998) - relationship evidence commentary] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: High Court authority on relationship evidence and Pfennig test] [CONCEPTS: Gipp v R, Pfennig, relationship evidence, propensity, High Court, tendency]

Tactical coverage: High Court positions on relationship evidence in Gipp v R.

In *Gipp v R* (1998) 194 CLR 106, Gaudron J said that the test in *Pfennig* applied to relationship evidence, Callinan J agreed. McHugh and Hayne JJ said it was not propensity evidence whilst Kirby J said it may be tendency evidence but in any event it was admissible.

---

### Chunk 3 — Practitioner note: relationship evidence can take many different forms
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Relationship evidence - forms and reasons] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Practical note on forms and reasons for leading relationship evidence] [CONCEPTS: relationship evidence, forms, tactical reasons, context, non-tendency, practitioner note]

Tactical coverage: Practitioner reminder on the variety of forms and reasons for leading relationship evidence.

I think with relationship evidence the important point to remember is that it can take many different forms and there are many different reasons why you might want to lead it.

If you are leading it merely to prove the accused had sex with the complainant in the past and therefore he had sex on this occasion, that is clearly tendency evidence.

However, it may also occur in some of the following forms:

---

### Chunk 4 — Example 1: Wilson v R — relationship evidence to prove motive
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Wilson v R (1970) 123 CLR 334] [CITATION: Wilson v R (1970) - relationship evidence to prove motive] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Relationship evidence used to prove motive (murder)] [CONCEPTS: Wilson v R, motive, relationship evidence, murder, non-tendency]

Tactical coverage: Use of relationship evidence in murder cases to establish motive rather than tendency.

1.  In the context of a marriage, evidence of a bad relationship between the accused and his deceased wife may be led to prove he had a motive to murder her: *Wilson v R* (1970) 123 CLR 334. Clearly, the relationship evidence is not being led to prove tendency.

---

### Chunk 5 — Example 2: Relationship evidence to show why complainant acquiesced (subpoints a–d)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Relationship evidence - acquiescence purposes and sub-arguments] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Ways relationship evidence can explain acquiescence, knowledge, conduct and credibility] [CONCEPTS: acquiescence, non-consent, knowledge, complainant conduct, context, credibility]

Tactical workflow: Reasons to lead relationship evidence to show why complainant acquiesced and related non-tendency purposes.

2.  It may be led to show why a complainant acquiesced to sexual intercourse. If the accused had regularly committed acts of violence upon the complainant in the past then such evidence could be led to prove:

    (a) That on this occasion the complainant was not consenting to sex;

    (b) The accused had knowledge that the complainant was not consenting to sex. Again, the evidence is being led for a non-tendency purpose;

    (c) To explain why the complainant behaved in the manner she did or why she did not complain, etc. Again, a non-tendency reasoning.

    (d) To show the complainant's version of events is more capable of belief when seen in the context of what the complainant says was her sexual relationship with the accused.

---

### Chunk 6 — Authorities for relationship evidence not excluded by Part 6
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Authorities - Conway; Lock; Roach; WFS] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Authorities that relationship evidence may not fall within Part 6 exclusion provisions] [CONCEPTS: Conway v R, R v Lock, Roach v R, WFS v R, Part 6, propensity exclusion]

Tactical coverage: Authorities supporting that certain relationship evidence does not fall within exclusion provisions of Part 6.

Authority for this type of evidence does not fall within the exclusion provisions of Part 6 of the Act: *Conway v R* 98 FCR 204 (Full Court of the Federal Court) at 233; *R v Lock* (1997) 91 A Crim R 336; *Roach v R* 242 CLR 610 at pp 624-5; *WFS v R* (2011) 33 VR 406.

---

### Chunk 7 — P v Tasmania [2009] TASSC 81 (11 September 2009) — relationship evidence notes
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: P v Tasmania [2009] TASSC 81 - relationship evidence summary] [CASE: P v Tasmania [2009] TASSC 81 (11 September 2009)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: P v Tasmania on relationship evidence, relevance and context] [CONCEPTS: P v Tasmania, relevance, s 55(1), context, complainant behaviour, relationship evidence]

Tactical coverage: Key points from P v Tasmania about relationship evidence and relevance.

Relationship evidence:

Not being relied upon as tendency evidence, but relationship evidence which could help the jury to evaluate evidence of what happened at the time of the alleged offence.

Relevant evidence is defined under s 55(1)

That which if accepted could rationally affect directly or indirectly the assessment of the probability of the existence of a fact in issue in the proceeding.

Relationship assists the court as it places actions and conduct in context and to assist the court assess the evidence of a persons action

Relationship evidence can be used to explain why a non-consenting person acts in a way in which they were consenting.

Relationship evidence may explain a statement or an event that would otherwise appear curious or unlikely. It may cut down or bolster the plausibility of something that a witness has said.

In the absence of relationship evidence the victims account of what otherwise would have been presented as a single and apparently isolated act might have been regarded as difficult to believe.

A victim, for instance may express no surprise at the actions of the defendant; may make no protest to a criminal act directed towards them, behaving, rather as if it was a common occurrence (which I'm leading it is).

If a victim was not permitted to say as such, a victim's evidence may seem hard to believe.

---

### Chunk 8 — Motive: when motive is or is not tendency evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Motive - when it is tendency evidence] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Distinction between motive and tendency evidence with cited cases] [CONCEPTS: motive, tendency, HML v R, Riseley v R, Patterson v Tasmania, Leonard v R, Bauer v R]

Tactical coverage: When motive is characterised as tendency evidence and when it is not.

Motive may or may not be tendency evidence. If, for example, the motive for killing somebody is theft or to avoid them telling police about a crime the person has committed, this is not tendency evidence.

However, if the motive is a sexual interest or indecent passion towards a certain class of people then that is likely to be characterised as tendency evidence: *HML v R* supra; *Riseley v R* [1970] Tas R 1; *Tasmania v Martin (No. 2)* supra at [66]-[68]; *Patterson v Tasmania* supra at [65].

Prior conduct directed towards an individual may be characterised as motive if it shows a person is attracted to or has a sexual interest in a person: *Leonard v R* (2006) 67 NSWLR 545 per Hodgson JA at [49]-[52]. The distinction between motive and tendency is a fine one. However, given the recent High Court decision in *Bauer v R,* that makes such evidence admissible as tendency evidence, in respect of a single complainant case, irrespective of the fact that the acts are of a different nature is, in practice, likely to make the distinction an academic one.

---

### Chunk 9 — Implements suggesting drug business (Giretti; Falzon)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Implements as circumstantial evidence - Giretti & Falzon] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Implements as circumstantial evidence not propensity reasoning] [CONCEPTS: implements, drug business, circumstantial evidence, knowledge, intention, Giretti, Falzon]

Tactical coverage: When implements are admissible as circumstantial evidence without propensity reasoning.

Evidence of implements that suggest a person carries out a drug business, such as a stash, scales or fire arms, is circumstantial evidence that a person may have knowledge of a drug or an intention to sell a drug and such reasoning is not tendency or propensity reasoning. This is the case even where the Crown is not conducting its case on the basis of a *Giretti* count (*Giretti v R* (1986) 24 A Crim R 112) or that the implements can be linked to the actual case alleged in the indictment: *R v Falzon* [2018] HCA 29 at [40]-[44].

---

### Chunk 10 — Res gestae / evidence forming part of a relevant transaction
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Res gestae - relevant transaction principle and authorities] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Admissibility of acts forming integral part of chain of events (res gestae)] [CONCEPTS: res gestae, relevant transaction, chain of events, non-tendency purpose, O'Leary, Harriman, Lyons, Samadi]

Tactical coverage: Admissibility of acts integral to a chain of events to explain what happened, as non-tendency evidence.

Evidence of acts, even discredited ones, may be admissible if they form an integral part of a chain of events and without which would lead to the evidence being unintelligible or not easily understood. Such evidence is not led for a tendency purpose but rather to explain what happened. Such evidence was admissible at common law and not excluded as propensity evidence: *O'Leary v R* (1946) 73 CLR 566; *Harriman v R* (1989) 167 CLR 590 per McHugh J at 628; *Lyons v R* (1992) 1 Tas R 193 per Wright J at 197-8.

The principle has not been excluded by the Uniform Evidence Act. Such evidence is relevant because it could rationally affect (directly or indirectly) the assessment of the probability of a fact in issue and thus it satisfies the test of relevance. It is not excluded by ss 97 and 98 of the Act because it is being used for a non-tendency purpose: *R v Adam* (1999) 106 A Crim R 510 at [25]-[31]; *Samadi v R* (2008) 192 A Crim R 251 per Beazley JA at [49]-[61].

Often such evidence will be closely connected in time with the alleged offence. However, this is not necessarily always the case. In *R v FDP* (2008) 74 NSWLR 645, the NSW Court of Criminal Appeal said at [36]-[37] --

> In our opinion the evidence of what occurred on 16 July and the making of the ADVO was clearly relevant to what occurred thereafter, including the incident on 2 August. It was an example of background or context evidence that explained why the parties did what they did and said what they did both before and on the 2 August. It set the scene for the incident that gave rise to the charges. For example, the evidence explained why the appellant could not have direct contact with the complainant or the children and the arrangements that had to be made for his access to the children through the complainant's parents.
>
> It is the type of evidence that was recognised at common law in a series of cases including *O'Leary v The King* (1946) 73 CLR 566; *Wilson v The Queen* (1970) 123 CLR 334 and *Harriman v The Queen* (1989) 167 CLR 590. Such evidence has been admitted as evidence either of the relationship between the accused and some other person or of a connected series of events, such that what occurred at one particular point in time in that relationship or in that series of events was relevant to a determination of what occurred at another point in time when the offence was committed. This type of evidence continues to be admissible under the *Evidence Act: R v Quach* (2002) 137 A Crim R 345

On occasions there will be an overlap between transactional evidence, relationship and motive.

---

### Chunk 11 — R v FDP (2008) NSWCCA quoted excerpt (as authority)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: R v FDP (2008) 74 NSWLR 645] [CITATION: R v FDP (2008) - contextual/background evidence quote] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Quoted reasoning on background/context evidence and res gestae] [CONCEPTS: R v FDP, background evidence, ADVO, context, res gestae, NSWCCA]

Tactical coverage: Direct quote from R v FDP on why prior incidents and ADVO were relevant background/context evidence.

> In our opinion the evidence of what occurred on 16 July and the making of the ADVO was clearly relevant to what occurred thereafter, including the incident on 2 August. It was an example of background or context evidence that explained why the parties did what they did and said what they did both before and on the 2 August. It set the scene for the incident that gave rise to the charges. For example, the evidence explained why the appellant could not have direct contact with the complainant or the children and the arrangements that had to be made for his access to the children through the complainant's parents.
>
> It is the type of evidence that was recognised at common law in a series of cases including *O'Leary v The King* (1946) 73 CLR 566; *Wilson v The Queen* (1970) 123 CLR 334 and *Harriman v The Queen* (1989) 167 CLR 590. Such evidence has been admitted as evidence either of the relationship between the accused and some other person or of a connected series of events, such that what occurred at one particular point in time in that relationship or in that series of events was relevant to a determination of what occurred at another point in time when the offence was committed. This type of evidence continues to be admissible under the *Evidence Act: R v Quach* (2002) 137 A Crim R 345

### Chunk 12 — Section 137 of the Act: statutory rule and application
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 137] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 - exclusion for probative value outweighed by unfair prejudice] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: s 137 exclusion of prejudicial evidence in criminal proceedings] [CONCEPTS: s137, Evidence Act, unfair prejudice, probative value, exclude evidence, DPP v Lynch]

Tactical coverage: Statutory rule requiring exclusion of prosecution evidence where probative value is outweighed by unfair prejudice.

Section 137 provides:

> In a criminal proceeding, the court must refuse to admit evidence adduced by the prosecutor if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the defendant.

Probative value again must be assessed at its highest and similarly to s 101 of the Act, it is only unfair prejudice that is relevant. It should be noted that s 137 does not provide a judge with a discretion. It is an evaluation that requires a judge to exclude the evidence if the probative value is not outweighed by the dangers of unfair prejudice to the defendant: *DPP v Lynch* [2006] TASSC 89.

---

### Chunk 13 — Unfair prejudice vs prejudice (Crawford CJ / Neill-Fraser reference)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Unfair prejudice vs prejudice - Neill-Fraser commentary] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Distinction between general prejudice and unfair prejudice] [CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, jury misuse, emotional basis, Papakosmas, Neill-Fraser, real risk]

Tactical coverage: Difference between evidence that is merely prejudicial and evidence that is unfairly prejudicial.

Unfair prejudice vs prejudice

Crawford CJ: *Neill-Fraser v Tasmania* [2012] TASCCA 2 [184]

There is a difference. All evidence that may tend to convict is prejudicial. What is meant by unfair prejudice is that the jury may use the evidence to make a decision on an improper, perhaps emotional basis. If there is a real risk that the evidence may be misused by the jury in some way, then it may be unfairly prejudicial.

*Papakosmas v The Queen* (1999) 196 CLR 297 at [91].

---

### Chunk 14 — s137 statutory text block (explicit)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 137] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137 - statutory extract] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory text of s137] [CONCEPTS: statutory extract, s137, Evidence Act, exclusion, unfair prejudice]

Tactical coverage: Exact statutory wording of s 137.

"137 Exclusion of prejudicial evidence in criminal proceedings"

In a criminal proceeding, the court must refuse to admit evidence adduced by the prosecutor if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice to the defendant.

---

### Chunk 15 — Evidence not unfairly prejudicial merely because it tends to convict (R v BD citation)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Evidence not unfairly prejudicial because it makes conviction more likely - R v BD] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Clarification of what "prejudice" means under ss 135-137] [CONCEPTS: unfair prejudice, R v BD, Hunt CJ, ss135-137, misuse by jury]

Tactical coverage: Clarifying that prejudice means unfair misuse, not mere tendency to convict.

1.  Evidence is not unfairly prejudicial because it makes it more likely that the accused will be convicted. As Hunt CJ at CL said in *R v BD* [(1997) 94 A Crim R 131], 139:

"The prejudice to which each of the sections [ss 135, 136 and 137] refers is *not* that the evidence merely tends to establish the Crown case; it means prejudice which is unfair because there is a real risk that the evidence will be misused by the jury in some unfair way."

---

### Chunk 16 — Onus of proof: general principles and tendency evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Onus of proof - Shepherd; Edwards; HML; BBH; Bauer] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: When jury must be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt about pieces of evidence, especially tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: onus, beyond reasonable doubt, tendency evidence, Shepherd v R, HML, BBH, Bauer v R]

Tactical coverage: Standards for jury satisfaction on evidence and special rules for tendency evidence.

Onus of proof

Normally a jury does not have to be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt about a piece of evidence unless it is an indispensable link in proving guilt: *Shepherd v R* (1990) 170 CLR 573; *Edwards v R* (1993) 178 CLR 193.

However, at common law, a jury must at least be satisfied beyond reasonable doubt in respect of tendency or propensity evidence: *HML v R* supra.

But not every piece of evidence that goes to prove the tendency has to be proved beyond reasonable doubt: *BBH v R* supra at [167]-[168].

The reason for this at common law was the high test of admissibility of tendency and coincidence evidence. Such a direction should not be given under the Uniform Evidence Act, unless it is apprehended that in the particular circumstance of the case there is a significant possibility of the jury reading the uncharged acts as an indispensable link in their chain of reasoning to guilt: *Bauer v R* supra at [86]. Otherwise, as with any other piece of circumstantial evidence, the jury must be satisfied of it.

---

### Chunk 17 — Notice: requirement and content for tendency/coincidence notices
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Tendency notice requirements - R v AB; Martin v R; Gardiner; Zhang; McPhillamy; Patterson; Hughes] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: What a tendency or coincidence notice should contain and consequences of inadequate notice] [CONCEPTS: tendency notice, reg6(2), Martin v R, specificity, Gardiner, Zhang, notice consequences]

Tactical checklist: Items to include and purposes of a tendency/coincidence notice.

Notice

- It is sufficient notice to refer to the Crown papers where it is readily identified to say such evidence will be used for a tendency purpose: *R v AB* [2001] NSWCCA 496.

- In *Martin v R* [2002] NSWCCA 337 at [91] -

  > The purpose of a reg6(2) notice is first, to ensure that attention is given to specific conduct and the circumstances of the conduct, and secondly, to enable the person whose conduct is in question to meet the tendency evidence. The purpose is linked with the decision upon probative value to be made by the Court, since only with knowledge of specific conduct and the circumstances of the conduct can a proper assessment be made of the probative value of the evidence in relation to the conduct alleged in the trial.

- A tendency notice should explicitly identify the fact or facts in issue upon which the tendering party asserts the evidence bears. It should also explicitly identify the tendency sought to be proved: *Gardiner v R* (2006) 162 A Crim R 233.

- In respect of coincidence evidence, a properly drafted coincidence notice requires the identification of two or more related events, the person whose conduct or state of mind is the subject of the proposed evidence, whether the evidence is tendered to prove the person did a particular act and, if so, what the act was, or if to prove a state of mind, what the state of mind was: *R v Zhang* supra at 534-535.

- It is important to remember that admissibility of the evidence will be judged against what the tendency notice states the tendency is. It is not for the court to think of other tendencies which may make the evidence admissible: *McPhillamy v R* supra per Elderman J at [32].

- The failure to provide an adequate notice means the tendency evidence will be inadmissible or could lead to a miscarriage of justice: *Patterson v Tasmania* supra per Wood J at [120]-[131].

- A tendency notice that expresses a tendency of a high level of generality may not establish anything more than relevance where a tendency expressed at a level of particularity will more likely be a requirement: *Hughes v R* supra at [64].

---

### Chunk 18 — Tendency and coincidence evidence adduced by the defence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Defence adducing tendency/coincidence evidence - Re Van Beelen; s135] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Rules and limits when the defence leads tendency or coincidence evidence] [CONCEPTS: defence evidence, tendency, coincidence, Re Van Beelen, s135, probative value, joint trials]

Tactical coverage: When the defence leads tendency or coincidence evidence and applicable restrictions.

Tendency and coincidence evidence adduced by the defence

Clearly an accused person can raise a hypothesis that somebody else committed the crime and can lead evidence to that effect: *Re Van Beelen* (1974) 9 SASR 163.

In doing so an accused may wish to raise tendency evidence regarding another person or coincidence reasoning between two events to show somebody else may have committed the crime. This typically occurs in, but is not limited to, cases where there are co-accused.

Such evidence will be inadmissible unless it complied with ss 97 or 98 of the Act. However, the further restriction in s 101 does not apply because the evidence is not adduced by the prosecution. However, the discretionary rejection (particularly applicable in joint trials) still applies (s 135 of the Act).

Thus, for the evidence to be admissible it must have significant probative value. However, in the context of the defence case, the question is whether it shows a reasonable possibility that somebody else may have committed the offence or, in a circumstantial case, whether it raises a hypothesis consistent with the accused's innocence as distinct from that of the prosecution, that the evidence supports that the accused did, in fact, commit the offence.

At common law where an accused seeks to rely on tendency evidence in respect to identity, there has to be shown a significant connection between a fact in issue and the tendency evidence: *Duffy v R* [1979] FCA 83; *Cheney v R* [1991] FCA 37; *Beamish v R* [2005] WASCA 62.

---

### Chunk 19 — Cheney v R quote and explanation on "similar fact" evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Cheney v R [1991] FCA 37] [CITATION: Cheney v R (1991) - similar fact evidence for identity extract] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Quote on requirements for similar fact evidence to prove identity] [CONCEPTS: Cheney v R, similar fact, identity, Duffy, connection, probative]

Tactical coverage: Quoted Full Court guidance on admissibility of similar fact evidence for identity issues.

In *Cheney v R* supra, Von Doussa J said at [19] --

> In Duffy v R [1979] FCA 83; (1979) 39 FLR 315 at 347-50 a Full Court of this Court considered the admissibility of evidence which the accused at trial sought to lead as "similar fact" evidence. At p 348 the Court said:
>
> "Evidence of similar facts is admissible to prove a fact in issue when the connexion between the alleged similar fact and the fact in issue is so close or immediate that in the common experience of mankind the fact in issue is likely to have occurred if the similar fact occurred. An allegation of a mere similarity between the way in which a person performs a particular task on one occasion and the way in which he is alleged to have performed it on another does not, at least in the generality of cases, show the kind and degree of connexion between the occasions which is essential to the admission of evidence of the former occasion as a similar fact to prove the way in which the task was performed on the latter occasion."
>
> In my opinion, where the issue is one of the identity, "similar fact" evidence to be admissible at the calling of the accused must be evidence which demonstrates a sufficient degree of similarity between the circumstances surrounding the crime charged and the circumstances of the other event to give rise to the real possibility, based on common experience of human affairs, that the person who committed the crime charged was the person responsible for the other event. In addition, of course, it would be necessary for the accused to lead evidence to the effect that the person responsible for the other event was not the accused.

---

### Chunk 20 — Unsworth v R mention (logical probative requirement)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Unsworth v R - logical probative requirement] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Requirement that evidence be logically probative of the issue] [CONCEPTS: Unsworth v R, logical probative, probative value, Tasmanian Supreme Court, identity evidence]

Tactical coverage: Statement on requirement for logical probative connection for defence tendency evidence.

It has to be logically probative of the issue: *Unsworth v R* TASSC 63 per Underwood J at [17].

---

### Chunk 21 — Reasonable hypothesis standard and Baden-Clay quote
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: R v Baden-Clay (2016) 258 CLR 308; [2016] HCA 35] [CITATION: Baden-Clay (2016) - reasonable inference and circumstantial reasoning] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Standard for reasonable inference and evaluation of circumstantial case] [CONCEPTS: Baden-Clay, reasonable hypothesis, circumstantial evidence, inference, guilty, mere conjecture]

Tactical coverage: High Court guidance on inferences and reasonable hypotheses in circumstantial cases.

Where an accused seeks to raise a reasonable hypothesis consistent with innocence, it must possess some degree of acceptability or credibility and not be fanciful, incredible, non-tenable, remote or tenuous. It must not be based on a view that is irrational, absurd or ridiculous. To raise a reasonable hypothesis, the evidence must support that hypothesis, not merely leave it open: *East v Repatriation Commission* (1987) 16 FCR 517; *Burke v R* (1997) 96 A Crim R 334 per Malcolm CJ at 336 and Walsh J at 353. There must be some evidence to support the hypothesis: *Mickelburg v R* supra at 303.

In *R v Baden Clay* (2016) 258 CLR 308; [2016] HCA 35, the High Court said at [47]:

> For an inference to be reasonable, it "must rest upon something more than *mere conjecture*. The bare possibility of innocence should not prevent a jury from finding the prisoner guilty, if the inference of guilt is the only inference open to reasonable men upon a consideration of all the facts in evidence" (emphasis added). Further, "in considering a circumstantial case, *all of the circumstances* established by the evidence are to be considered and weighed in deciding whether there is an inference consistent with innocence reasonably open on the evidence" (emphasis added). The evidence is not to be looked at in a piecemeal fashion, at trial or on appeal.

---

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Police v FRS [2020] TASMC 9 (25 May 2020)] [CITATION: Police v FRS [2020] TASMC 9 - example tendency notice] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Example tendency notice listing alleged sexual tendencies and conduct] [CONCEPTS: tendency notice, sexual interest, minors, nieces, overnight, sexual touching, Police v FRS, TASMC]

a.  have a sexual interest in and/or engage in sexual contact with his nieces aged approximately 16 years;

    a.  impose himself sexually upon his nieces without seeking or obtaining their consent;

    <!-- -->

    i.  sexually touch his nieces under their clothing;

    <!-- -->

    a.  sexually touch his nieces when they stayed overnight;

    <!-- -->

    a.  sexually touch his nieces when they were in bed; and/or

    a.  sexually touch his nieces in circumstances when they were alone.

### Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 - Tendency evidence: definition and purpose
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 97] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: tendency evidence, particular state of mind, propensity, relevance for inference, conforming behaviour, probative purpose, Evidence Act s97, admissibility test]

Tendency evidence under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97 is evidence adduced to prove that a person had a tendency to act in a particular way or to have a particular state of mind, and is used to support an inference that the person acted in that way (or had that state of mind) on a particular occasion relevant to the proceeding. The critical feature of tendency evidence is its inferential purpose: evidence of past conduct or propensity is tendered not merely for its own sake but to make it more probable that the person acted similarly on the occasion in issue. Tendency evidence therefore requires careful analysis of relevance and probative utility and attracts the statutory controls in ss 95–101. A party seeking to avoid the application of s 97 cannot simply assert an alternative purpose; they must demonstrate that the evidence is relevant to a fact in issue by reasoning that does not involve drawing an inference from tendency to conforming behaviour.

### Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 - Coincidence (related events) evidence: definition and threshold
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 98] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: coincidence / related events] [CONCEPTS: coincidence evidence, related events, significant probative value, similarity, improbability of coincidence, Evidence Act s98, admissibility threshold, rational effect on probability]

Coincidence or "related events" evidence under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 98 concerns two or more events alleged to be connected and admissible to show that, because of similarities, it is improbable they occurred coincidentally; such evidence may be adduced to prove that the accused did a specified act or had a specified state of mind that is a fact in issue. Section 98 requires that, having regard to similarities in the events and circumstances, the evidence have significant probative value: similarity is the central factor from which probative force is derived. If events are not sufficiently similar to make coincidence improbable, s 98 will not admit them for coincidence purposes. If evidence does not meet s 98, it may nevertheless be excluded under Evidence Act s 137 because its probative value is outweighed by unfair prejudice.

### CGL v DPP (Vic) [2010] — Four-step admissibility test for coincidence evidence (authority)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: CGL v DPP (Vic) [2010]] [TOPIC: coincidence admissibility test] [CONCEPTS: coincidence test, four-step test, similarity assessment, significant probative value, balancing prejudice, juror assessment, related events]

CGL v DPP (Vic) [2010] sets out a practical four-step framework for assessing admissibility of coincidence/related-events evidence: (1) Are the similarities in the specified events and their circumstances such that it is improbable the events occurred coincidentally? (2) If yes, would evidence of those events tend to prove that the accused did the specified act or had the specified state of mind that is a fact in issue? (3) If yes, does the evidence have significant probative value, either alone or having regard to other evidence the prosecution has adduced or will adduce? (4) If yes, does the probative value substantially outweigh any prejudicial effect on the accused? This framework operationalises the statutory s 98 requirement that similarity produce significant probative value and the s 101/s 137 balancing against prejudice.

### R v PWD — similarity and tendency/coincidence (authority)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v PWD (as cited in source)] [TOPIC: similarity requirement and tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: R v PWD, tendency evidence, similarity not necessary to be striking, particular state of mind, violence, control, detaining, probative use]

R v PWD, as cited in the source, confirms that for admissibility under s 97 it is not necessary that similar conduct be strikingly similar or closely similar; the authorities allow a range of similarities to support a tendency inference. The case emphasises that tendency evidence is adduced to render more probable an inference that the person acted in a particular way on the occasion in issue and that tendency need not show commission of the same crime on other occasions — it may show a general tendency (for example, to use violence or to control a partner) that is probative of whether the accused detained or acted in a particular way on the night in question.

### Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 95 — Use of evidence admitted for another purpose does not automatically permit tendency/coincedence use
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 95] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 95] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: limits on re‑use of evidence] [CONCEPTS: s95, hearsay exceptions, character evidence, re-purposing evidence, tendency use, coincidence use, admissibility precondition]

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 95 establishes that evidence admitted for one purpose (for example, admissible under a hearsay or character exception) cannot automatically be re‑used for tendency or coincidence purposes: if a party seeks to rely on that evidence as tendency or related-events evidence, the statutory requirements of s 97 or s 98 must still be satisfied. In short, admission under one head of admissibility does not waive the separate threshold and balancing exercises required for tendency or coincidence evidence; the prosecution cannot simply re-label admitted material as related-events evidence without satisfying the s 97/98 criteria.

### Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101 — Further restrictions on tendency and coincidence evidence adduced by the prosecution
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 101] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 101] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: prosecution restrictions for tendency/coincedence] [CONCEPTS: s101, prosecution evidence limits, substantial outweighing, prejudicial effect, opening the door, notice requirement, fairness, significant probative value]

Section 101 of the Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) imposes additional limits when the prosecution seeks to adduce tendency or coincidence evidence against an accused: under s 101(2) the probative value of such evidence must substantially outweigh any prejudicial effect on the accused; ss 101(3)–(4) create exceptions where the accused "opens the door" by adducing relevant matter; and the section works with the notice and procedural requirements in ss 97–100. These provisions reflect heightened protection for accused persons when propensity-type material is led by the Crown and require careful judicial balancing of probative value against risk of unfair prejudice.

### Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 100 - Judicial directions as an exception/control
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 100] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 100] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: judge's direction - tendency/coincedence] [CONCEPTS: s100, limiting direction, jury directions, evidence use limitation, judicial control, trial fairness, trial judge]

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 100 empowers the trial judge to give directions concerning the use of tendency or coincidence evidence, including limiting instructions and explanations of the proper inferential use of the material. A judicial direction may mitigate the prejudicial effect of propensity material and is a recognised exception/control mechanism rather than a standalone basis to admit otherwise inadmissible evidence.

### Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97(2)(b) — Tendency evidence admissible to rebut opposing tendency evidence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 97(2)(b)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97(2)(b)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: rebuttal tendency evidence] [CONCEPTS: rebuttal, s97(2)(b), responding propensity evidence, probative relevance, evidence of tendency, defensive use]

Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 97(2)(b) provides that tendency evidence may be adduced to rebut tendency evidence put forward by another party; in that limited context evidence of a tendency may be admissible not to prove the accused's propensity generally but to meet an opposing party's case about tendency. This is a targeted exception to the general restrictiveness of s 97 and requires that the rebuttal purpose be genuine and proportionate.

### DSJ v The Queen — s 98 capacity test (authority)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: DSJ v The Queen (as cited in source)] [TOPIC: capacity to affect assessment of fact in issue] [CONCEPTS: s98 capacity, significant degree, rational effect on probability, hypothetical jury, admissibility, related events]

DSJ v The Queen (as cited) holds that s 98 asks whether the evidence is capable, to a significant degree, of rationally affecting the assessment (ultimately by a jury) of the probability of the existence of a fact in issue. The case emphasises a realistic judicial assessment of the evidence' capacity: if a hypothetical jury would likely regard the evidence as unimportant to the facts in issue, a trial judge may readily conclude the evidence lacks the required capacity for admissibility under s 98.

### W v The Queen — interplay with Evidence Act s 137 (authority)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: W v The Queen (as cited in source)] [TOPIC: s137 and related-events evidence] [CONCEPTS: s137, unfair prejudice, exclude evidence, related events, balancing test, criminal proceedings]

W v The Queen (as cited) illustrates that if evidence does not qualify as a "related event" under s 98, its admissibility must still be tested under Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 137; the court must refuse admission of evidence adduced by the prosecutor if its probative value is outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice. Thus failure to meet s 98 does not automatically permit admission — the s 137 exclusion remains available to exclude prejudicial material.

### Lockyer — relevance threshold for tendency/coincedence evidence (case note)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lockyer (as cited in source)] [TOPIC: degree of relevance for admissibility] [CONCEPTS: Lockyer, relevance threshold, more than mere relevance, degree of consequence, admissibility standard, propensity material]

Lockyer, as cited in the source notes, articulates that admissibility of tendency/related-events evidence requires "something more than mere relevance, but less than a substantial degree of relevance" — the evidence must also be important or of consequence. This formulation situates the threshold between trivial relevance and full proof of the disputed issue and supports the statutory requirement that probative value be significant.

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Jacara v Perpetual Trustees; Townsend v Townsend (as cited in source)] [TOPIC: cited case authorities] [CONCEPTS: Jacara, Townsend, citation noted, authority cited, case law reference, [REVIEW]]
