### Chunk 14 — Section 32 — Refreshing memory: chain of questions and statutory text
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 32] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 32 - Refreshing memory checklist and statutory text] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Use of s 32 to refresh witness memory — preconditions, chain of questions, and statutory text] [CONCEPTS: s32, refreshing memory, leave, document, contemporaneous, fresh in memory, read aloud, produce document, considerations]
This chunk covers refreshing memory under s 32, the chain of questions to satisfy preconditions, practical notes, and the statutory text.

This chunk covers Refreshing memory:

- Must be a contemporaneous document

- Document must have been made or verified by the witness

- Be prepared to show the document to the witness

Chain of questions to satisfy essential preconditions of refreshing memory:

Ask witness if after the event they made a note of what was said:

- How long after did you make the note?

- Where did you make the note?

- When you made the note, were the words still first in your memory?

- How did you make the notes?

- Did the notes accurately record your memory at the time?

- It has been x months since the incident?

- Are you able to recall what was said at the time?

- Are you able to produce those notes?

- DO you seek leave of the court to refer to those notes to refresh your memory as to what was said?

Section 32(1) of the evidence act provides that a witness may use a document to revive their memory, if the court gives leave. Section 32(2) provides the considerations the court should consider.

Rely on this section if a witness says that they cannot remember, or if I know that they have forgotten something that was in their statement.

- Can you remember anything else

- Is there anything that would help you remember (why yes! I did a statement).

- Do you have that statement with you (why yes, I do)

- When was that statement made? (when I was interviewed by police shortly after the event)

First question establishes that memory is exhausted. The second question that there is a document that would revive the witnesses' memory. The third question establishes that it is the original and the 4^th^ that it was made why the matter was still fresh in the memory of the maker.

32(2) allows a witness, given leave, to actually read aloud from the statement.

---

### Chunk 15 — Section 32 — Procedure, factors, and statutory provisions (full text excerpt)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 32(1)-(4)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 32 - Full statutory text and procedural notes] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory provisions of s 32 and procedural factors for attempting to revive memory] [CONCEPTS: statutory text, s32(1), s32(2), s32(3), s32(4), revive memory, leave, produce document]
This chunk covers the statutory text of s 32 and accompanying procedure/factors to consider.

This chunk covers Section 32 -- read statement

Consider using if the witness genuinely cant remember what happened -- given the course of time that has passed since it happened. Need to ensure 192 has been considered and the pre-conditions in 32 (recall otherwise or not; make sure the statement was made at the time

Need to ensure the court is statisfied the statement was made at the time and was agreed by the witness as being true (may need to get police officer who took it at the time to give evidence on voir dier

Had witness highlighted portions, let them read it to themselves, then take it back and then ask them the question.

### Procedure

Get what the witness does remember through EIC

No problems memory generally

Satisfied exhausted memory (s 2(a))

Fresh in memory OR (s 2(b)(i))

Found by the witness to be accurate (5 2(b)(ii))

Leave under s 192

- Evidence important and relates to a fundamental particular

**Factors:**

*32. **Attempts to revive memory in court***

*(1)A witness, in the course of giving evidence, must not use a document to try to revive his or her memory about a fact or opinion unless the court gives leave.*

*(2) Without limiting the matters that the court may take into account in deciding whether to give leave, it is to take into account ---*

*(a) whether the witness will be able to recall the fact or opinion adequately without using the document; and*

*(b) whether so much of the document as the witness proposes to use is, or is a copy of, a document that ---*

*(i) was written or made by the witness when the events recorded in it were fresh in his or her memory; or*

*(ii) was, at such a time, found by the witness to be accurate.*

*(3) If a witness, while giving evidence. has used a document to try to revive his or her memory about a fact or opinion, the witness, with the leave of the court, may read aloud as part of his or her evidence so much of the document as relates to that fact or opinion.*

*(4) The court, on the request of a party, is to give any directions it thinks fit to ensure that so much of the document as relates to the proceeding is produced to that party.*

---

### Chunk 16 — Section 66(3) — Contemplation of court proceedings; Saunders [2004] TASSC 95
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 66(3)] [CITATION: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) s 66(3) - Contemplation of court proceedings and Saunders commentary] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: s 66(3) issues about statements made in contemplation of proceedings; Saunders authority; maker's purpose] [CONCEPTS: s66(3), contemplation of proceedings, Saunders, maker's purpose, representation admissibility] [CASE: Saunders [2004] TASSC 95]
This chunk covers s 66(3) issues and the Saunders authority on whether a representation was made for the purpose of indicating evidence in a proceeding.

This chunk covers Contemplation of court proceedings -

- Representation by a person who is available, by s 60, becomes admissible as to the truth of the assertion.

- Defence object on the basis of s 66 (3) and the representation was made for the purpose of indicating the evidence she would give in an Australian proceeding

This was dealt with by *Saunders* \[2004\] TASSC 95 par 60, where Crawford said that reference to s (3) is clearly a reference to the maker, not who heard the representation (also in Esposito --- Wood J).

Case is not so clear where person at the scene makes a representation to a police officer

who just arrived and is trying to find out what happened --- ie it may be to make a complaint

or to assist officer who has just arrested someone.

Therefore, it is significant what the purpose of the maker is. Reasonable to infer she was not prepared to make a statement as scared, she did not perceive that representation she gave was an account she might be able to give in a court proceeding. No basis for concluding at the time the purpose was for indicating what evidence she would give at Court proceedings.

Clear SC authority it is a reference to the purpose of the maker not the person who heard the representation.

---

<!-- block_050 master -->

### Privilege against self-incrimination — general description (s128 certificates)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: privilege against self-incrimination] [CONCEPTS: self-incrimination, certificate, use immunity, derivative use immunity, compelled testimony, witness protection, testimonial disclosure, evidentiary privilege, s128 procedure]
Privilege against self-incrimination under s128 is an evidentiary rule that protects a witness from having evidence given by them in court used against them in future proceedings by providing a certificate when certain conditions are met. The section applies when a witness objects to giving particular evidence or evidence on a particular matter because the evidence may tend to prove the witness has committed an offence under Australian or foreign law or is liable to a civil penalty. The statutory scheme balances the witness's protection with the needs of the proceeding by allowing the court to inform the witness of their rights, possibly compel evidence in defined circumstances, and issue a certificate conferring use and derivative-use immunity for the evidence and related material, subject to statutory exceptions.

---

### Procedural purpose and scope of s128
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: purpose and scope of s128] [CONCEPTS: protect witness, enable proceeding, testimonial disclosure, hearing-only application, pre-trial limits, Part 3.10, s131A, s128A]
The purpose of s128 is to protect a witness who objects to giving evidence because of a real risk of self-incrimination while enabling the proceeding to receive the benefit of the witness's evidence where appropriate. The protection is focused on testimonial disclosure at hearings; other contexts such as pre-trial processes are governed by the common law or by other statutory provisions. The statutory scheme sets out a process to assess objections, inform witnesses of consequences, determine whether to compel evidence, and issue certificates when appropriate.

---

### Court's duty to ensure awareness of s128 (s132)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s132] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: judicial duty to inform] [CONCEPTS: court duty, awareness, s128 effect, procedural fairness, advising witnesses, judicial obligation]
If the court considers a witness or party may have grounds for applying or objecting under s128, the court must ensure that the witness or party is aware of the effect of s128. This judicial duty supports informed decision-making by the witness and ensures that any claim of privilege is properly made and considered in accordance with the statutory procedure.

---

### Trial management — warnings and absence of jury for s128 applications
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: handling s128 applications in trials] [CONCEPTS: warning co-offender, alternative suspect, absence of jury, in-camera determination, jury directions, trial fairness]
When a court warns a possible co-offender or alternative suspect of the availability of the s128 privilege, it is prudent to do so in the absence of the jury. Similarly, applications for a s128 certificate should generally be determined without the jury present to avoid prejudice and to allow the trial judge to assess the legal questions and discretionary issues raised by the claim.

---

### Court obligations when reasonable grounds are found (s128(3))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(3)] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: informing witness and certificate effect] [CONCEPTS: need not give evidence, certificate will be given, voluntary evidence, compelled evidence, consequences explained]
If the court determines there are reasonable grounds for an objection under s128(1), s128(3) requires the court to inform the witness that they need not give the evidence unless required by the court, that the court will issue a certificate whether the witness gives evidence voluntarily or after being required to do so, and to explain the effect of the certificate. This ensures the witness understands the protection and the potential consequences of giving or refusing to give evidence.

---

### Power to require witness to give evidence and certificate issuance (s128(4)–(5))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(4), s128(5)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: compulsion and certificate requirement] [CONCEPTS: compel evidence, judicial discretion, interests of justice, foreign law exception, certificate issuance, use immunity, derivative use immunity]
Section 128(4) permits the court to require a witness to give evidence despite an objection if two statutory conditions are met: (a) the evidence does not tend to prove an offence under a foreign law or liability to a foreign civil penalty, and (b) the interests of justice require the witness to give the evidence. If the court compels the witness under s128(4), s128(5) requires issuance of a certificate that provides the statutory protection set out in s128(7). The test is discretionary and requires judicial weighing of competing considerations.

---

### Foreign-law protection limb (s128(4)(a))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(4)(a)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: foreign-law exception to compulsion] [CONCEPTS: foreign law, extraterritorial risk, non-compellability, cross-border prosecution, respect for foreign jurisdictions]
Under s128(4)(a) the court cannot compel a witness to give evidence if that evidence tends to prove an offence or liability to a civil penalty under a foreign law, because an Australian court cannot guarantee that a certificate issued domestically will be respected in another jurisdiction. This limb protects legitimate claims to privilege where compulsion would expose the witness to unmitigable foreign jurisdictional risk.

---

### "Interests of justice" limb and factors (s128(4)(b))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(4)(b)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: interests of justice test] [CONCEPTS: interests of justice, discretionary factors, importance of evidence, fairness, reliability, prior proceedings, tactical unfairness, limiting publication]
The requirement that "the interests of justice require" that the witness give evidence under s128(4)(b) is a broad discretionary test. Relevant factors include the importance of the evidence, the likelihood that evidence would be unreliable even with a certificate, whether the proceeding is criminal and who seeks to adduce the evidence, the nature of the offence or liability, likelihood of prosecution from the evidence, any unfairness to parties from compelling disclosure, whether the substance of the evidence is already public, and the availability of measures to limit publication or misuse.

---

### Topic-by-topic compulsion approach
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(4)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: discrete topic determination] [CONCEPTS: topic-by-topic, severable topics, piecemeal compulsion, judicial calibration, tailored certificates]
A court may assess whether the interests of justice require compulsion on a topic-by-topic basis rather than treating the witness's objection as global; this permits finely calibrated decisions that admit non-incriminating aspects of testimony while protecting incriminating parts unless the statutory tests for compulsion are satisfied.

---

### Effect and territorial/institutional reach of certificates (s128(7), s128(9), ss128(12)-(13) Evidence Act 1995 (Cth))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(7), s128(9)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: certificate effect and limits] [CONCEPTS: use immunity, derivative use immunity, tribunals, Victorian courts, federal limits, retrial exception, cross-jurisdictional limits, admissibility]
A certificate under s128 confers both use and derivative-use immunity in proceedings before courts or bodies authorised to hear evidence under Victorian law (and, broadly, tribunals authorised to receive evidence). The immunity does not extend to proceedings for falsity (perjury) nor to retrials or trials of offences arising from the same facts (s128(9)). Under Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) ss128(12)-(13), a Victorian certificate can prohibit use in Commonwealth and ACT courts for offences under Commonwealth or ACT law, but does not prevent use in other state courts or overseas jurisdictions.

---

### Form and regulation of certificate
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: certificate form] [CONCEPTS: Evidence Regulations 2009 (Vic), Form 1, certificate content, administrative formality, evidentiary record]
Regulation 9 of the Evidence Regulations 2009 (Vic) prescribes that a certificate for the purposes of s128 and s128A may be in the form set out in Form 1 of the Schedule to those Regulations. This provides a standard form and aids clarity as to the protection conferred.

---

### Challenge to certificate validity and protective effect (s128(8))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(8)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: certificate challenge] [CONCEPTS: validity challenge, protective effect, estoppel-like protection, error in issuance, ongoing reliance, continuity of immunity]
Section 128(8) provides that a certificate has effect notwithstanding any subsequent challenge to its validity, ensuring that a witness relying on the certificate can do so in future proceedings even if the certificate was granted in error. The provision secures the policy objective of giving witnesses certainty that evidence given under a certificate will not be used against them later.

---

### "Witness" defined and scope of protection
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: who is a witness] [CONCEPTS: party as witness, person called by party, accused as witness, exclusions, spouse, deponent, compellability]
For s128 purposes, a "witness" includes a party, a person called by a party to give evidence and an accused. However, the protection excludes an accused who gives evidence about an act or state of mind that is a fact in issue (s128(10)) and an accused in voir dire proceedings (s189(6)). The section protects only the witness's own interests, not interests of others (for example, it does not protect a spouse's interests, although separate compellability rules may apply to spouses).

---

### "Particular evidence, or evidence on a particular matter" — scope after 2007 amendments
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: subject-matter scope] [CONCEPTS: subject matter, breadth, documentary evidence, oral evidence, affidavit evidence, cumulative questions]
The 2007 amendment expanded the protection from "particular evidence" to also include "evidence on a particular matter," allowing the s128 process to operate in respect of a subject matter rather than requiring invocation for each individual question. The phrase has been interpreted broadly and deliberately avoids distinguishing between oral and documentary evidence.

---

### Application to affidavits and pre-trial processes; limits (s131A and s128A)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: affidavits and pre-trial limits] [CONCEPTS: affidavits, compelled affidavit, volunteering statements, pre-trial exclusion, s131A, s128A, common law privilege]
A witness compelled to make an affidavit may raise an s128 objection without oral testimony in some authorities, but s128 generally does not apply at pre-trial stages because s131A applies most of Part 3.10 to pre-trial processes but excepts s123 and s128. Courts should avoid contriving procedures to force a person to become a deponent for the sake of bringing s128 into play; specific statutory provisions (for example s128A regarding disclosure affidavits under freezing or search orders) may alter that result in limited contexts.

---

### "Tends to prove" and past-offence limitation (s128(1)(a))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(1)(a)] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: tend to prove requirement] [CONCEPTS: tends to prove, link in the chain, past offence, not future offence, perjury exclusion, reasonable grounds to object]
Section 128 applies only to evidence that may tend to prove past commission of an offence by the witness; it does not protect against exposure for an offence that the witness would commit by giving evidence (for example perjury). "Tends to prove" requires only a reasonable possibility that the evidence could contribute to proving an offence and can include evidence that is a 'link in the chain' rather than conclusive proof.

---

### "Liable to a civil penalty" — meaning and limits (s128(1)(b))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(1)(b)] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: civil penalty meaning] [CONCEPTS: civil penalty, disciplinary penalty, damages vs penalty, non-criminal sanctions, statutory proceedings, ecclesiastical censure exclusion]
"Liable to a civil penalty" refers to liability under proceedings that impose a penalty under an Australian or foreign law but excludes criminal proceedings; the common law distinction between punitive penalties and compensatory damages is relevant. The privilege historically has applied to disciplinary penalties imposed by statute but does not extend to matters that merely disgrace a witness or to ecclesiastical censure.

---

### "Reasonable grounds for the objection" — meaning, burden and standard (s128(2), s142)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(2)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: reasonable grounds test] [CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds, judicial determination, burden on claimant, balance of probabilities, apprehension insufficient, evidentiary proof, prospect of prosecution, limiting factors]
"Reasonable grounds for the objection" requires judicial assessment; a witness's bare apprehension is insufficient. The person asserting protection bears the burden of proof on the balance of probabilities (s142) to show reasonable grounds exist. The court must consider whether there is a real prospect of criminal prosecution based on the evidence, taking into account factors such as limitation periods and the plausibility that answers would be directly incriminating or lead to further investigation.

---

### No adverse inference and civil consequences (versus Jones v Dunkel)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: no adverse inference] [CONCEPTS: no adverse inference, civil proceedings, Jones v Dunkel, failure to call evidence, adequacy of explanation, weight of evidence]
As at common law, no adverse inference should be drawn simply because a witness claims the s128 privilege. However, in civil proceedings claiming the privilege may not provide an adequate explanation for failing to call evidence; courts may apply the rule in Jones v Dunkel to draw inferences against a party who claims s128 to avoid producing evidence, treating the claim as potentially insufficient explanation for non-production.

---

### Retrials and treatment of evidence given under certificate (s128(9))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(9)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: retrials and admissibility] [CONCEPTS: retrial exception, admissions at first trial, admission in later trial, preservation of Cornwell result, retrial not always 'proceeding', statutory exception]
Section 128(9) was inserted to address the issue that admissions made under a certificate at one trial could be tendered in a retrial or a trial for an offence arising from the same facts; the statutory amendment preserves the traditional ability in retrials for the prosecution to use such admissions and responds to High Court concerns that prior drafting left uncertainty as to whether a retrial constituted a "proceeding" to which certificate protection extended.

---

### Exception for accused giving evidence on facts in issue (s128(10))
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [SECTION: s128(10)] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: accused exception] [CONCEPTS: fact in issue, state of mind, direct and inferential evidence, accused testimony, voir dire exception, s189(6)]
Section 128(10) provides that in criminal proceedings the section does not apply to evidence an accused gives about an act that is a fact in issue or a state of mind which is a fact in issue. This exclusion is not limited to direct testimony but extends to testimony of facts from which the act or state of mind could be inferred. Additionally, s189(6) excludes this limitation in voir dire proceedings, allowing an accused to rely on privilege when giving evidence on preliminary admissibility questions.

---

### Relationship between s128 and the common law privilege
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: common law vs statutory privilege] [CONCEPTS: ousting common law, limited statutory scope, non-judicial proceedings, discovery, interrogatories, testimonial context]
Within its scope — that is, objections to giving evidence in court — s128 displaces the common law privilege against self-incrimination. The common law privilege continues to apply outside s128's statutory sphere, such as to non-judicial proceedings, discovery, and pre-trial interlocutory processes, unless otherwise displaced by statute.

---

### Compellability and witness box — cannot refuse to enter the box
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: duty to attend the witness box] [CONCEPTS: compellable witness, refusal to enter, witness box obligation, in-court objection, practical compellability, court power]
A witness cannot evade testimony by refusing to enter the witness box on the basis of claiming s128 privilege; the statutory protection arises only when a witness is giving evidence in the proceeding. Courts have held that a witness cannot simply decline to take the oath or give evidence to rely pre-emptively on s128.

---

### Case authority — Song v Ying (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; [2010] NSWCA 237
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Song v Ying (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; [2010] NSWCA 237] [TOPIC: s128 not available for answers to own counsel; compellable witness may object] [CONCEPTS: examination-in-chief, re-examination, compellable witness, not compelled by own counsel, no 'objection' when volunteering, counsel-led questioning, certificate requirement]
Song v Ying held that while any compellable witness may object to giving evidence, s128 does not apply where a party gives evidence in examination-in-chief or re-examination in response to questions from their own lawyer, because such evidence is not given under compulsion and thus does not qualify as an "objection" under s128(1). The decision also confirmed that compellable witnesses may object at any stage, subject to the compulsion analysis.

---

### Case authority — KH v R [2014] NSWCCA 294
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: KH v R [2014] NSWCCA 294] [TOPIC: warning to co-offender in absence of jury] [CONCEPTS: warning co-offender, absence of jury, trial management, judicial prudence, s128 notice]
KH v R supports the prudential practice that when warning a possible co-offender or alternative suspect about the availability of the privilege, the warning is best given in the absence of the jury to avoid prejudice.

---

### Case authority — Spence v The Queen [2016] VSCA 113
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Spence v The Queen [2016] VSCA 113] [TOPIC: s128 applications determined absence of jury] [CONCEPTS: certificate hearing in camera, jury exclusion, procedural fairness, evidentiary ruling]
Spence v The Queen confirms that applications for certificates under s128 should be determined in the absence of the jury to protect trial fairness and avoid prejudicial disclosure of contested legal issues.

---

### Case authority — DPP v Peters (2019) 59 VR 203
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: DPP v Peters (2019) 59 VR 203] [TOPIC: s128 limited to in-court objections and ousts common law] [CONCEPTS: statutory limitation, common law displacement, in-court objection, testimonial scope]
DPP v Peters holds that s128 is limited to witnesses objecting to giving evidence in court and, within that sphere, effectively displaces common law privilege principles so that the statutory scheme governs the assessment and resolution of claims.

---

### Case authority — Versace v Monte [2001] FCA 1572
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Versace v Monte [2001] FCA 1572] [TOPIC: no adverse inference from asserting privilege] [CONCEPTS: no adverse inference, ALRC citation, testimonial protection, admissibility weight]
Versace v Monte affirms the principle that no adverse inference should be drawn from a witness claiming the statutory privilege against self-incrimination, consistent with recommendations of law reform bodies and common law precedents.

---

### Case authority — Chong v CC Containers Pty Ltd (2015) 49 VR 402; [2015] VSCA 137
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Chong v CC Containers Pty Ltd (2015) 49 VR 402; [2015] VSCA 137] [TOPIC: cannot refuse to enter the witness box] [CONCEPTS: witness box obligation, compelled testimony, privilege only when testifying, procedural limits]
Chong v CC Containers establishes that a witness cannot avoid the obligation to give evidence by refusing to enter the witness box on the basis of claiming s128; the privilege arises in the context of evidence actually being given in the proceeding.

---

### Case authority — Cornwell v R (2007) 231 CLR 260; [2007] HCA 12
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Cornwell v R (2007) 231 CLR 260; [2007] HCA 12] [TOPIC: retrial and 'proceeding' interpretation; certificate certainty] [CONCEPTS: retrial, admission use, definition of proceeding, certificates effect, lost certificate scenario]
Cornwell v R held that a retrial was not necessarily a "proceeding" to which the then-drafting of certificate protection applied, leading to the statutory insertion of s128(9) to address retrial use of admissions. The High Court also discussed circumstances in which it would be absurd to deny certificate protection (for example loss of the certificate), highlighting the need for certainty in reliance on certificates.

---

### Case authority — Aitken v Murphy [2011] FamCA 785
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Aitken v Murphy [2011] FamCA 785] [TOPIC: Song v Ying applied in family context] [CONCEPTS: examination-in-chief, family proceedings, counsel-led evidence, voluntariness, certificate inapplicability]
Aitken v Murphy applied the reasoning from Song v Ying in family law context, confirming that counsel-led examination is not compulsion for s128 purposes and thus may not attract certificate protection when the witness voluntarily gives evidence at their counsel's prompting.

---

### Case authority — Re Lime Gourmet Pizza Bar (Charlestown) Pty Ltd [2014] NSWSC 1898
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Re Lime Gourmet Pizza Bar (Charlestown) Pty Ltd [2014] NSWSC 1898] [TOPIC: voluntariness v compulsion; counsel questioning] [CONCEPTS: corporate statements, voluntary evidence, compellability, privilege scope]
Re Lime Gourmet Pizza considered limits on s128 where evidence is given voluntarily in counsel-led examination, aligning with the view that absence of compulsion can defeat a claim that the witness "objects" under s128(1).

---

### Case authority — Que Noy v Qadir [2020] NTSC 73
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Que Noy v Qadir [2020] NTSC 73] [TOPIC: application of Song v Ying principle in NT context] [CONCEPTS: examination-in-chief, voluntariness, counsel questions, privilege inapplicability]
Que Noy v Qadir applied the Song v Ying principle in the Northern Territory, holding that evidence given in examination-in-chief by one's own counsel does not amount to compulsion and so s128 protection is not engaged where a party voluntarily gives such evidence.

---

### Case authority — Clayton Utz (a Firm) v Dale (2015) 47 VR 48
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Clayton Utz (a Firm) v Dale (2015) 47 VR 48] [TOPIC: limits on s128 for voluntary statements] [CONCEPTS: professional firms, voluntariness, evidentiary protection, counsel-led testimony]
Clayton Utz v Dale discussed the inapplicability of s128 where evidence is provided voluntarily in a context where the witness is not subject to compulsion, reinforcing distinctions between compelled testimony and voluntary disclosure in court.

---

### Case authority — Environmental Protection Authority v Caltex (1993) 178 CLR 477; [1993] HCA 74
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Environmental Protection Authority v Caltex (1993) 178 CLR 477; [1993] HCA 74] [TOPIC: corporate officers may claim privilege personally] [CONCEPTS: corporate officer, personal privilege, corporate representative, individual right, corporate speech]
Environmental Protection Authority v Caltex confirms that while a corporate entity cannot claim the privilege against self-incrimination, an individual officer speaking in their personal capacity (not as corporate representative) may claim the privilege on their own behalf.

---

### Case authority — Ferrall v Blyton [2000] FamCA 1442
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Ferrall v Blyton [2000] FamCA 1442] [TOPIC: broad interpretation of 'particular matter' and affidavits] [CONCEPTS: subject-matter protection, affidavit objections, volunteering affidavit, certificate sought, family law context]
Ferrall v Blyton interpreted "particular evidence, or evidence on a particular matter" broadly and illustrates instances where a deponent sought certificate protection in relation to affidavits, highlighting procedural approaches to affidavit-based claims.

---

### Case authority — Ollis v Melissari [2005] NSWSC 1016
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Ollis v Melissari [2005] NSWSC 1016] [TOPIC: broad interpretation of subject matter] [CONCEPTS: subject matter, documentary evidence, oral testimony, affidavit context]
Ollis v Melissari supports a broad construction of "particular evidence or evidence on a particular matter," applying s128 protection to subject-matter based objections including documentary material.

---

### Case authority — AMP General Insurance Ltd v Prasad [1999] NSWSC 349
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: AMP General Insurance Ltd v Prasad [1999] NSWSC 349] [TOPIC: affidavit-maker may assert objection without oral evidence] [CONCEPTS: affidavit compulsion, objection in writing, documentary objection, judicial acceptance]
AMP General Insurance v Prasad is authority that a maker of a compelled affidavit may assert a self-incrimination objection and raise entitlement to certificate protection without giving oral evidence, in appropriate circumstances.

---

### Case authority — Ross v Internet Wines (2004) 60 NSWLR 436; [2004] NSWCA 195
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Ross v Internet Wines (2004) 60 NSWLR 436; [2004] NSWCA 195] [TOPIC: limits on forcing deponents to become witnesses; avoid subverting common law privilege] [CONCEPTS: compelled affidavit, pre-trial compulsion, deponent status, common law protection, procedural caution]
Ross v Internet Wines cautioned courts not to contrive procedures that artificially force persons to become deponents or witnesses simply to bring s128 into play, thus preserving the operation of common law privilege in pre-trial contexts.

---

### Case authority — Pathways Employment Services v West [2004] NSWSC 903
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Pathways Employment Services v West [2004] NSWSC 903] [TOPIC: pre-trial objectors and common law privilege] [CONCEPTS: pre-trial stage, objecting deponent, common law, affidavit protection, statutory limits]
Pathways Employment Services v West reinforces that s128 should not be used to circumvent the common law privilege in pre-trial settings and that courts must respect the different regimes governing testimonial and non-testimonial contexts.

---

### Case authority — Application concerning Section 80 ... [2004] NSWSC 614
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Application concerning Section 80 of the Supreme Court Act and Sections 119 and 128 of the Evidence Act [2004] NSWSC 614] [TOPIC: 'tends to prove' — link in chain standard] [CONCEPTS: tends to prove, link in the chain, reasonable grounds, evidentiary sufficiency]
This authority confirms that evidence "tends to prove" commission of an offence when it provides a reasonable link in the chain of evidence even if not conclusive, supporting the lower threshold for invoking s128 protection.

---

### Case authority — Roo-Roofing Pty Ltd v The Commonwealth (Ruling No 5) [2018] VSC 338
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Roo-Roofing Pty Ltd v The Commonwealth (Ruling No 5) [2018] VSC 338] [TOPIC: reasonable grounds requires prospect of prosecution; burden and evidence] [CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds, real prospect, evidence required, judicial decision, limitations relevance]
Roo-Roofing held that reasonable grounds for objection require the court to evaluate whether there is a real prospect of prosecution from the evidence, and that this determination may require evidentiary material rather than mere assertion by the witness.

---

### Case authority — Sorby v Commonwealth (1983) 152 CLR 281; [1983] HCA 10
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Sorby v Commonwealth (1983) 152 CLR 281; [1983] HCA 10] [TOPIC: common law background for reasonable grounds; historical principle] [CONCEPTS: common law precedent, witness compulsion, conviction impossibility, foundation of test]
Sorby v Commonwealth provides common law background on the reasonable grounds concept, including statements that a witness cannot refuse to answer where the alleged crime could not lead to conviction or punishment, contributing to interpretive depth for s128(2).

---

### Case authority — R v Bikic [2001] NSWCCA 537
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Bikic [2001] NSWCCA 537] [TOPIC: reasonable grounds assessed against jeopardy risk] [CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds, jeopardy, prospect of prosecution, judicial evaluation, historical authority]
R v Bikic articulated that "reasonable grounds for objection" must take into account whether the witness can be placed in jeopardy by giving the particular evidence, reflecting a protective core to the statutory test.

---

### Case authority — R v Boyes (1861) 121 ER 730
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Boyes (1861) 121 ER 730] [TOPIC: historical common law phrasing supporting s128 wording] [CONCEPTS: historical authority, common law phrasing, foundational precedent, refusal to answer limits]
R v Boyes is a foundational common law authority cited for historical support of the language and underlying rationale embodied in modern statutory formulations of the privilege.

---

### Case authority — Gedeon v R [2013] NSWCCA 257
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Gedeon v R [2013] NSWCCA 257] [TOPIC: requirement that admission of evidence under s128(4) serves interests of justice] [CONCEPTS: interests of justice, abrogation of common law, judicial discretion, compelled testimony]
Gedeon v R emphasised that in determining compulsion under s128(4) the court must be satisfied that the interests of justice require admission of the evidence, not merely that it is relevant, reflecting the significant incursion into common law rights.

---

### Case authority — R v Lodhi [2006] NSWSC 638
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Lodhi [2006] NSWSC 638] [TOPIC: foreign-law risk and reliability considerations] [CONCEPTS: foreign law enquiry, reliability of evidence, unfairness, publication limits, balancing factors]
R v Lodhi addressed s128(4) considerations, including whether evidence tended to prove an offence under a foreign law and the balancing of multiple factors such as importance and reliability of the evidence, likelihood of prosecution, and potential unfairness.

---

### Case authority — R v Collisson [2003] NSWCCA 212
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Collisson [2003] NSWCCA 212] [TOPIC: interplay of charges already laid and s128 considerations] [CONCEPTS: charges already brought, finalisation of charges, admission of evidence, retrial context]
R v Collisson is authority on how the existence and status of charges arising from the same events affect the assessment of whether compelling evidence is appropriate under s128.

---

### Case authority — Turco v HP Mercantile Pty Ltd (No 2) [2009] NSWCA 209
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Turco v HP Mercantile Pty Ltd (No 2) [2009] NSWCA 209] [TOPIC: importance and reliability as factors] [CONCEPTS: evidence importance, reliability concerns, appellate guidance, s128 balancing]
Turco v HP Mercantile discussed the relevance of the importance and probable reliability of the evidence as factors in the "interests of justice" balancing under s128(4).

---

### Case authority — R v Hore [2005] NSWCCA 3
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Hore [2005] NSWCCA 3] [TOPIC: jury directions when witness not required to give evidence] [CONCEPTS: jury approach, directions, refusal to give evidence, tribunal of fact, evidentiary handling]
R v Hore examined how a tribunal or jury should approach and be directed regarding a witness's refusal to give evidence, an important consideration when deciding whether to compel testimony under s128.

---

### Case authority — Rich v Attorney General (NSW) [2013] NSWCA 419
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Rich v Attorney General (NSW) [2013] NSWCA 419] [TOPIC: construction of broad statutory phrasing and judicial scope] [CONCEPTS: statutory interpretation, 'interests of justice' construed broadly, judicial view of justice]
Rich v Attorney General (NSW) provides interpretive guidance that broadly worded statutory phrases such as "interests of justice" are intended to afford judicial officers scope to give effect to their view of the justice of the case in question.

---

### Case authority — Cureton v Blackshaw Services Pty Ltd [2002] NSWCA 187
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Cureton v Blackshaw Services Pty Ltd [2002] NSWCA 187] [TOPIC: breadth of 'interests of justice' permitting questions on credit] [CONCEPTS: credit questioning, interests of justice, admissibility, civil context]
Cureton v Blackshaw observed that the phrase "interests of justice" should be construed broadly and may permit questions going to the credit of a witness to be asked even where privilege is asserted.

---

### Case authority — Bax Global (Australia) Pty Ltd v Evans (1999) 47 NSWLR 538; [1999] NSWSC 815
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Bax Global (Australia) Pty Ltd v Evans (1999) 47 NSWLR 538; [1999] NSWSC 815] [TOPIC: use in cross-examination and 'use against the person'] [CONCEPTS: cross-examination, admissibility in cross, 'use against the person', statutory interpretation, derivative-use immunity]
Bax Global considered whether evidence the subject of a certificate could be used in cross-examination and noted a plausible view that s128(7) might not prevent such use, though commentators treat such cross-examination as "use against the person" within the statute.

---

### Case authority — Police Service Board v Morris (1985) 156 CLR 397; [1985] HCA 9
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Police Service Board v Morris (1985) 156 CLR 397; [1985] HCA 9] [TOPIC: privilege in disciplinary proceedings] [CONCEPTS: disciplinary penalty, statutory disciplinary sanction, privilege application, non-criminal penalty]
Police Service Board v Morris exemplifies the application of the privilege in relation to disciplinary penalties imposed by statute, distinguishing such penalties from criminal sanctions.

---

### Case authority — Attorney-General (NSW) v Borland [2007] NSWCA 201
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Attorney-General (NSW) v Borland [2007] NSWCA 201] [TOPIC: publication limits and interests of justice balancing] [CONCEPTS: publication limitation, coronial context analogue, fairness, evidence importance]
Attorney-General (NSW) v Borland considered considerations analogous to s128 balancing in the coronial context, including the likely effects of requiring evidence and means to limit publication.

---

### Case authority — Ferrall v Blyton; A-G (Cth) [2000] FamCA 1442
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Ferrall v Blyton; A-G (Cth) [2000] FamCA 1442] [TOPIC: affidavit volunteering while claiming certificate] [CONCEPTS: affidavit voluntariness, certificate request, family law affidavit, procedural strategy]
Ferrall v Blyton illustrates situations where a witness may volunteer incriminating statements in an affidavit while simultaneously asserting an entitlement to certificate protection and seeking to file the affidavit only with certificate protection.

### Case authority — Sheikholeslami v Tolcher (2009) 75 NSWLR 418; [2009] NSWSC 920
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Sheikholeslami v Tolcher (2009) 75 NSWLR 418; [2009] NSWSC 920] [TOPIC: affidavit and certificate interplay] [CONCEPTS: affidavit objection, certificate sought, procedural handling]
Sheikholeslami v Tolcher is another authority dealing with claims of privilege in relation to affidavits and the procedural approach to securing certificate protection.

In the Marriage of Atkinson contains judicial disagreement on whether compulsion conditions under s128(4)(a) were met in a family law context, illustrating practical application complexities concerning foreign-law risk and compulsion.

### Case authority — R v Collisson [2003] NSWCCA 212
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Collisson [2003] NSWCCA 212] [TOPIC: charges already brought and s128 analysis] [CONCEPTS: finalised charges, criminal prosecution probability, impact on compulsion decision]
