<!-- block_050 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Initial note and Privilege in respect of self-incrimination (paras 1–8)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128 - Privilege in respect of self-incrimination - Procedural Overview] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure / annotation] [TOPIC: Overview of s128 procedure and initial practitioner note] [CONCEPTS: s128 certificate, self-incrimination, compel witness, jury absence, s128(3), s128(4), certificate effect, procedure for objection, witness rights] [CASE: Song v Ying (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; KH v R [2014] NSWCCA 294; Spence v The Queen [2016] VSCA 113]

Covers: Initial practitioner note and procedural overview for s128 certificates (paras 1–8).

Privilege against self-incrimination (s 128 certificates)

Not available to **[Defendant]{.underline}** in EIC or RX to questions from their own lawyer! Not "compelled" (*Song v Ying* (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; \[2010\] NSWCA

Privilege in respect of self-incrimination in other proceedings

1.  Section 128 applies if a witness objects to giving particular evidence or evidence on a particular matter because doing so may tend to prove the witness has committed an offence against, or arising under, an Australian law or a law of a foreign country, or is liable to a civil penalty (s128(1)). It details a procedure which both protects the witness claiming the privilege and enables the proceeding in which the witness is called to have the benefit of his or her evidence.

2.  If the court considers that a witness or party may have grounds for applying or objecting under this Part, it must satisfy itself that the witness or party is aware of the effect of s128 (s132).

3.  If the court takes the precaution of warning a possible co-offender or alternative suspect of the availability of the privilege, it is prudent to do so in the absence of the jury (*KH v R *\[2014\] NSWCCA 294 at \[35\]). Similarly, any application for a s 128 certificate should be determined in the absence of the jury (*Spence v The Queen *\[2016\] VSCA 113, \[55\]).

4.  If the court determines there are reasonable grounds for the objection, it must inform the witness:

    - that he or she need not give the evidence unless required by the court to do so under s128(4); and

    - that the court will give a certificate under s128 if:

      - the witness willingly gives the evidence without being required to do so; or

      - the witness gives the evidence after being required by the court to do so; and

    - of the effect of the certificate (s128(3)).

5.  The court may require the witness to give the evidence if it is satisfied that the interests of justice require the witness to give the evidence, provided that the evidence does not tend to prove that the witness has committed an offence or is liable to a civil penalty under a law of a foreign country (s128(4)).

6.  The following cannot be used against a person who gave the evidence in any proceeding in a Victorian court or before any person or body authorised by a law of Victoria, or by consent of parties, to hear, receive and examine evidence:

    - evidence given by the person in respect of which a certificate has been given; and

    - any information, document or thing obtained as a direct or indirect consequence of the person having given that evidence (s128(7)).

7.  This protection does not apply to a criminal proceeding in respect of the falsity of the evidence (s128(7)). Nor does it apply to proceedings which are a retrial of the accused for the same offence, or a trial of the accused for an offence arising out of the same facts that gave rise to that offence (s128(9)).

8.  The section does not apply in criminal proceedings where an accused gives evidence that he or she:

    - did an act, the doing of which is a fact in issue; or

    - had a state of mind, the existence of which is a fact in issue (s128(10)).

---

### Chunk 2 — Section 128 and common law; Application and no adverse inference (paras 9–14)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128 - Common law interaction and adverse inferences] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: s128's relationship with common law privilege and inferences] [CONCEPTS: common law privilege, testimonial disclosure, s131A, adverse inference, Jones v Dunkel, Chong v CC Containers] [CASE: DPP v Peters (2019) 59 VR 203; Versace v Monte [2001] FCA 1572; Chong v CC Containers Pty Ltd (2015) 49 VR 402; Jones v Dunkel]

Covers: How s128 interacts with the common law privilege and the rule on adverse inferences (paras 9–14).

9.  While the common law privilege against self-incrimination can apply to both judicial and non-judicial proceedings, along with associated procedures like discovery and interrogatories, s 128 is limited to a witness who objects to giving evidence in court (*DPP v Peters* (2019) 59 VR 203, \[74\], \[114\]).

10. Within its sphere of operation, s 128 ousts the common law. Thus, where the issue concerns the privilege against self-incrimination for a witness giving or about to give evidence in court, there is no room for common law principles to apply (*DPP v Peters* (2019) 59 VR 203, \[88\]-\[89\], \[114\]).

Application and no adverse inference

11. Absent specific provision in other legislation, s128 applies only in proceedings to which the Act applies. Also, it is implicit that it applies only in respect of 'testimonial disclosure' at a hearing (in all other contexts the common law applies). This is reinforced by s131A which applies most of Part 3.10 to pre-trial processes but specifically excepts s123 and s128.

12. One consequence of the structure of s128, and its application to hearings, is that the section only applies when the witness is giving evidence. A witness cannot refuse to enter the witness box on the basis of the privilege (*Chong v CC Containers Pty Ltd* (2015) 49 VR 402; \[2015\] VSCA 137 at \[236\]-\[237\]).

13. As at common law, no adverse inference should be drawn from the fact that privilege is claimed under s128 (*Versace v Monte* \[2001\] FCA 1572 at \[6\] per Tamberlin J (citing ALRC 26:1 at \[862\])).

14. However, it is likely that claiming the privilege under s128 does not provide an adequate explanation for a failure to call evidence in civil proceedings. This means that the rule in *Jones v Dunkel* may apply to a defendant in a civil proceeding who claims the s 128 privilege (see *Chong v CC Containers Pty Ltd* (2015) 49 VR 402; \[2015\] VSCA 137 at \[221\]-\[229\] and cases cited).

---

### Chunk 3 — s128(1): 'witness' (paras 15–22)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128(1), s128(10), s189(6), s18] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128(1) - Definition of 'witness' and related exclusions] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: meaning of 'witness', who can claim s128, exclusions and case commentary] [CONCEPTS: witness definition, accused, spouse compellability, voir dire, Song v Ying, Cornwell v R, Aitken v Murphy] [CASE: Cornwell v R (2007) 231 CLR 260; Song v Ying (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; Aitken v Murphy [2011] FamCA 785; Environmental Protection Authority v Caltex (1993) 178 CLR 477]

Covers: Who counts as a 'witness' for s128 purposes, exclusions, and practitioner/case commentary (paras 15–22).

15. A witness includes a party, a person called by a party to give evidence and an accused (see clause 7, Part 2, UEA Dictionary). However, it does not include:

    - an accused who gives evidence with respect to either an act done by him- or herself, which act is a fact in issue or a state of mind the existence of which is a fact in issue (s128(10)); or

    - an accused in voir dire proceedings (s189(6)).

16. The section protects only the witness. It does not protect the interests of other persons, including a spouse. The possible unreliability of such evidence should affect its weight rather than its admissibility (ALRC 26:1 at \[862\]).

17. In any event, s18 (Compellability of spouses and others in criminal proceedings generally) allows a spouse, de facto partner, parent or child of an accused to object to giving evidence as a prosecution witness.

18. [The High Court, in heavily qualified obiter dicta in *Cornwell v R* questioned whether a witness (including an accused) who wishes to reveal some criminal conduct in evidence in chief, because of the forensic advantage in doing so, should receive the benefit of s128. The dicta considered whether such a witness' objection may be regarded as objecting to giving evidence. The possible forensic advantages identified for the accused in *Cornwell* was avoiding the risk of not being asked questions about the matter in cross-examination and, if asked, having to do so in circumstances less favourable to his interests (*Cornwell v R* (2007) 231 CLR 260; \[2007\] HCA 12 at \[111\]-\[112\]).]{.mark}

19. [The Court expressed concern about straining the meaning of the words \'object\' in s128(1) and 'require' in s128(5), when dealing with a party who is a witness and who clearly wishes to adduce the evidence, but seeks the protection of a certificate. There is, however, no explicit requirement in s128(1) for the judge to consider the motives behind the objection. These are more likely to be relevant under s128(4) when considering whether to compel the witness to give the evidence.]{.mark}

20. [In *Song v Ying*, the NSW Court of Appeal held that any witness who is compellable to give evidence may object to giving evidence without the protection of a certificate at any stage. This includes examination-in-chief and re-examination (*Song v Ying* (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; \[2010\] NSWCA 237 at \[20\] per Hodgson JA (Giles and Basten JJA agreeing); see also *Aitken v Murphy* \[2011\] FamCA 785 at \[114\] per Young J).]{.mark}

21. [However, the NSW Court of Appeal held that s 128 does not apply where a party gives evidence-in-chief, or in re-examination, in response to questions from his or her own lawyer. This is because such evidence is not evidence which the party is compelled to give. A party who wants to give evidence, but only if he or she has the protection of a s128 certificate, does not 'object' to giving that evidence, within the meaning of s128(1) 'because there is no element of compulsion or potential compulsion which makes the expression 'objects' apposite' (*Song v Ying* (2010) 79 NSWLR 442; \[2010\] NSWCA 237 at \[28\] per Hodgson JA (Giles and Basten JJA agreeing). See also *Re Lime Gourmet Pizza Bar (Charlestown) Pty Ltd (formerly under administration)* \[2014\] NSWSC 1898; *Que Noy v Qadir* \[2020\] NTSC 73, \[74\]; *Clayton Utz (a Firm) v Dale* (2015) 47 VR 48, \[164\]-\[180\]).]{.mark}

22. While a body corporate may not claim the privilege, an individual officer of that body, speaking in his or her own right (as opposed to as a representative of the body corporate), may claim the privilege on his or her own behalf (see *Environmental Protection Authority v Caltex* (1993) 178 CLR 477; \[1993\] HCA 74).

---

### Chunk 4 — s128(1): 'particular evidence, or evidence on a particular matter' (paras 23–28)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128(1); s131A; s128A] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128(1) - Particular evidence or matter; affidavits and pre-trial limits] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: scope of 'particular evidence or matter', affidavits, pre-trial application limits] [CONCEPTS: particular evidence, evidence on a matter, affidavits, pre-trial, s131A, s128A, volunteer affidavit] [CASE: Ferall v Blyton [2000] FamCA 1442; Ollis v Melissari [2005] NSWSC 1016; AMP General Insurance Ltd v Prasad [1999] NSWSC 349; Ross v Internet Wines (2004) 60 NSWLR 436]

Covers: Scope of "particular evidence, or evidence on a particular matter" and issues about affidavits and pre-trial application (paras 23–28).

23. The 2007 amendments to this provision expanded the scope of evidence from 'particular evidence' also to include 'evidence on a particular matter'. This overcomes the need to invoke the s128 process with respect to each question to ensure it can apply to the 'subject matter' of the questions (see ALRC 102 at \[15.95\], \[15.106\]).

24. The phrase 'particular evidence, or evidence on a particular matter' has been interpreted broadly (*Ferall v Blyton* \[2000\] FamCA 1442; *Ollis v Melissari* \[2005\] NSWSC 1016).

25. The provision deliberately avoids distinguishing between giving oral and documentary evidence (ALRC 26:1 at \[862\]; ALRC 38 at \[214\]).

26. While courts originally required the maker of an affidavit to give oral evidence and raise an objection under s128, later decisions have held that a witness who is compelled to make an affidavit may raise the objection without giving oral evidence (see *AMP General Insurance Ltd v Prasad* \[1999\] NSWSC 349 per Hamilton J; *Ross v Internet Wines* (2004) 60 NSWLR 436; \[2004\] NSWCA 195 per Giles JA, (Spigelman CJ and McColl JA agreeing); compare *HPM Industries Pty Ltd v Graham (No 2)* \[1996\] NSWSC unreported, 27 August 1996 per Young CJ in Eq).

27. It is uncertain, however, whether s128 can apply when a witness is not compelled to make an affidavit. Traditionally, courts have allowed a witness or a party to volunteer incriminating statements in an affidavit while simultaneously asserting the privilege under s128 (see *Ferrall v Blyton; A-G* (Cth) (Intervener) \[2000\] FamCA 1442 at \[89\]-\[90\], where the witness objected to filing the affidavit without a certificate; *Sheikholeslami v Tolcher* (2009) 75 NSWLR 418; \[2009\] NSWSC 920).

28. Section 128 does not apply at a pre-trial stage (s131A). When a person objects to making an affidavit, the court must consider whether he or she is \'a witness\' and whether the potential affidavit is for the purpose of giving evidence. The court should not fashion an artificial procedure to compel a person to become a deponent or a witness who is giving evidence, and subvert the operation of the common law privilege against self-incrimination (*Ross v Internet Wines* (2004) 60 NSWLR 436; \[2004\] NSWCA 195; *Pathways Employment Services v West* \[2004\] NSWSC 903). However, see s128A on disclosure affidavits under freezing or search orders.

---

### Chunk 5 — 'tend to prove' and 'liable to a civil penalty' (paras 29–36)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128(1)(a), s128(1)(b)] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128(1)(a)-(b) - 'tend to prove' and civil penalty meaning] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: meaning of 'tend to prove' and scope re civil penalties] [CONCEPTS: tend to prove, past offences, link in the chain, foreign law, civil penalty, disciplinary penalties, ALRC guidance] [CASE: DPP v Peters (2019) 59 VR 203; Application concerning Section 80 of the Supreme Court Act and Sections 119 and 128 of the Evidence Act (2004) NSWSC 614; Police Service Board v Morris (1985) 156 CLR 397]

Covers: What "tends to prove" means and the s128(1)(b) civil penalty limb (paras 29–36).

29. Section 128 only applies to evidence that may tend to prove the witness has committed an offence. It is therefore limited to evidence of past offences, and cannot apply to an offence a witness will commit by giving evidence, such as where the witness will commit answer untruthfully and commit perjury (*DPP v Peters* (2019) 59 VR 203, \[78\], \[114\]-\[115\]).

30. The offence may arise under a written or unwritten law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory, or the written or unwritten law of a foreign country.

31. 'Tends to prove' means the evidence sought to be adduced need not prove in fact the commission of an offence or liability. It is sufficient if there are reasonable grounds for objecting on the ground that the evidence may tend to prove the commission of an offence (*Application concerning Section 80 of the Supreme Court Act and Sections 119 and 128 of the Evidence Act* \[2004\] NSWSC 614 at \[20\]-\[24\] per Brownie JA).

32. Evidence will be sufficient to 'tend to prove' the commission of an offence by the witness even if it provides only a 'link in the chain' (*Application concerning Section 80 of the Supreme Court Act and Sections 119 and 128 of the Evidence Act* \[2004\] NSWSC 614 at \[35\]-\[36\] per Brownie JA).

33. 'Civil penalty' means a penalty arising under proceedings under an Australian or foreign law excluding criminal proceedings (refer clause 3, Part 2, UEA Dictionary).

34. The common law distinguishes a penalty ('punishment') from damages ('compensation').

35. The common law privilege has been applied in relation to disciplinary penalties imposed by statute (*Police Service Board v Morris* (1985) 156 CLR 397; \[1985\] HCA 9).

36. The privilege is not available for matters that might tend to 'disgrace' a witness, to show him or her to be guilty of forfeiture or adultery or liable to ecclesiastical censure (ALRC 26:1 at \[862\]).

---

### Chunk 6 — 'reasonable grounds for the objection' (paras 37–43)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128(2); s142] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128(2) - Reasonable grounds for objection and burden] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: what are 'reasonable grounds' and burden/standard of proof] [CONCEPTS: reasonable grounds, burden of proof, balance of probabilities, apprehension insufficient, real prospect of prosecution, limitations periods] [CASE: Roo-Roofing Pty Ltd v The Commonwealth (Ruling No 5) [2018] VSC 338; Sorby v Commonwealth (1983) 152 CLR 281; R v Bikic [2001] NSWCCA 537]

Covers: How to assess 'reasonable grounds' for objecting under s128 and who bears the burden (paras 37–43).

37. The Act does not define or offer guidance regarding the term 'reasonable grounds'.

38. A witness' own apprehension that there is a risk of incrimination is not sufficient to establish reasonable grounds. The court must be able to see that there are reasonable grounds for the objection. This is a matter that must be decided judicially and can require evidence (*Roo-Roofing Pty Ltd v The Commonwealth (Ruling No 5)* \[2018\] VSC 338, \[26\]; *Sorby v Commonwealth* (1983) 152 CLR 281, 289; *Jackson v Gamble* \[1983\] 1 VR 552, 555).

39. The words of the provision are 'reasonable grounds for objection' and not reasonable grounds for the conclusion that the evidence may tend to prove the witness has committed an offence (*R v Bikic* \[2001\] NSWCCA 537 at \[13\] per Giles JA (Sully and Levine JJ agreeing)).

40. His Honour, referring to *R v Boyes* (1861) 121 ER 730 (at 738) (as cited in *Sorby v The Commonwealth* (1983) 152 CLR 281 at 289 per Gibbs CJ; \[1983\] HCA 10), observed that the terms of s128 recall those used at common law. And at common law, a witness could not refuse to answer a question which tended to show he or she had committed a crime for which he or she could not be convicted or punished (*R v Bikic* \[2001\] NSWCCA 537 at \[14\] per Giles JA (Sully and Levine JJ agreeing)).

41. His Honour went on to hold that 'reasonable grounds for objection must pay regard to whether or not the witness can be placed in jeopardy by giving the particular evidence' (*R v Bikic* \[2001\] NSWCCA 537 at \[15\] per Giles JA (Sully and Levine JJ agreeing)).

42. This requires the court to consider whether there is a real prospect of criminal prosecution based on the evidence. Factors such as limitations periods, or the improbability of the answers either directly incriminating, or leading to a trail of investigation which might ultimately lead to prosecution, are relevant to deciding whether there are reasonable grounds for the objection (*Roo-Roofing Pty Ltd v The Commonwealth (Ruling No 5)* \[2018\] VSC 338).

43. The burden of proving there are 'reasonable grounds' lies with person seeking protection of the provision. By s142, the standard is the 'balance of probabilities'.

---

### Chunk 7 — s128(3) and s128(4) general (paras 44–48)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128(3), s128(4)] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128(3)-(4) - Court's duty to inform and compulsion test] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure / annotation] [TOPIC: court obligations when reasonable grounds found; compulsion and judicial discretion] [CONCEPTS: court must inform, certificate issuance, interests of justice, compel to give evidence, s128(5) certificate obligation, relevance vs interests of justice] [CASE: Gedeon v R [2013] NSWCCA 257]

Covers: What the court must inform the witness if reasonable grounds found (s128(3)) and the court's power to compel evidence (s128(4)) (paras 44–48).

44. If the court finds there are reasonable grounds for a witness' objection under s128(1), s128(3) requires the court to:

    - inform the witness that he or she need not give evidence unless required by the court (s128(3)(a));

    - inform the witness that whether he or she gives evidence willingly or by the court's direction under s128(4), he or she will be issued with a certificate (s128(3)(b)); and

    - explain the effect of such a certificate (s128(3)(c)).

45. There is no express provision for the loss of this privilege. Odgers suggests that questions of waiver may be considered in this context of determining 'reasonable grounds' and it would be relevant to refer to common law principles (Odgers, *Uniform Evidence Law *(2022) at \[EA.128.450\]).

46. Section 128(4) allows the court to compel a witness to give evidence and so subjects the protection against self-incrimination to the exercise of a judicial discretion. If s128(4) is applied, s128(5) requires the court to issue a certificate of immunity. A certificate provides the protection set out in s128(7).

47. Section 128(4) requires the terms of both paragraphs (a) and (b) to be met.

48. In determining whether to admit the evidence, the court must be satisfied that the interests of justice require admission, not merely that the evidence is relevant. This is consistent with the fact that the Act essentially abrogates a common law right (*Gedeon v R* \[2013\] NSWCCA 257 at \[286\] per Bathurst CJ (Beazley P, Hoeben CJ at CL, Blanch and Price JJ agreeing)).

---

### Chunk 8 — s128(4)(a) & s128(4)(b) (paras 49–57, including Odgers factors)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128(4)(a), s128(4)(b)] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128(4) - Foreign law limitation and 'interests of justice' factors] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: limits on compelling where foreign law implicated; factors for 'interests of justice'] [CONCEPTS: foreign law, interests of justice, Odgers factors, importance of evidence, reliability, unfairness, publication limits, prior charges] [CASE: In the Marriage of Atkinson (1997) 136 FLR 347; R v Lodhi [2006] NSWSC 638; R v Collisson [2003] NSWCCA 212; Turco v HP Mercantile Pty Ltd (No 2) [2009] NSWCA 209; Rich v Attorney General (NSW) [2013] NSWCA 419; Cureton v Blackshaw Services Pty Ltd [2002] NSWCA 187; R v Hore [2005] NSWCCA 3; Attorney-General (NSW) v Borland [2007] NSWCA 201]

Covers: s128(4)(a) foreign-law limitation and s128(4)(b) meaning of "interests of justice" with illustrative factors (paras 49–57).

49. ALRC 102 explains that, as a person may be compelled to give evidence and an Australian court cannot guarantee that any certificate issued by it will be respected in a foreign jurisdiction, a legitimate claim to privilege in this context should not be overruled. Thus, a court's ability to compel answers in the interests of justice ought not be removed when it cannot guarantee protection against the risk of incrimination in relation to offences in foreign jurisdictions (at \[15.104\]).

50. This requirement is discussed in *In the Marriage of Atkinson* (1997) 136 FLR 347; (1997) 21 Fam LR 279 where Baker J (at 288) and Lindenmayer J (at 315-16) disagree on the facts of the case about whether its terms have been met.

51. In *R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638, Whealy J was not satisfied the evidence sought to be adduced by the Crown did not tend to prove an offence under a foreign law (*R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638 at \[29\], \[31\]-\[39\] per Whealy J).

52. The Act does not provide any guidance as to the meaning of 'the interests of justice'. It is for the party seeking to have the witness compelled to give evidence to satisfy the court that the interests of justice 'require' the evidence to be adduced.

53. In determining whether the interests of justice require such action in any given case, it is important to consider the scope and purpose of the provision, as well as what is its real objective. Often, the real purpose of the legislature in using such broad wording is to give the judicial officer scope to give effect to his or her 'view of the justice of the case', while remaining cognisant of the general purpose of the legislation (see *Rich v Attorney General (NSW)* \[2013\] NSWCA 419 at \[19\]--\[20\] per Leeming JA (Bathurst CJ and Beazley P agreeing), citing *Klein v Domus Pty Ltd* (1963) 109 CLR 467 at 473 per Dixon CJ; \[1963\] HCA 54).

54. In *Cureton v Blackshaw Services Pty Ltd* \[2002\] NSWCA 187, Sheller JA observed the term should be 'should be construed broadly and would permit questions to be put going to credit' (*Cureton v Blackshaw Services Pty Ltd* \[2002\] NSWCA 187 at \[37\] per Sheller JA).

55. In *Lodhi*, Whealy J notes this limb requires consideration of 'complex of matters which, by their nature, tend to pull in different directions' (*R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638 at \[40\] per Whealy J).

56. Odgers (Uniform Evidence Law (2022), \[EA.128.540\] suggests that the following factors may be considered when determining whether to admit evidence

    - the importance of the evidence in the proceeding (*R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638 at \[41\]--\[46\]; *Gedeon v R* \[2013\] NSWCCA 257 at \[289\]--\[290\]; *Turco v HP Mercantile Pty Ltd* (No 2) \[2009\] NSWCA 209 at \[24\]--\[25\] per Hodgson JA, at \[30\] per Campbell JA);

    - the likelihood that the evidence will be unreliable even if a certificate is given (*R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638 at \[56\]; *R v Collisson* \[2003\] NSWCCA 212; compare *Turco v HP Mercantile Pty Ltd* (No 2) \[2009\] NSWCA 209 at \[19\]--\[23\], \[27\]--\[28\], \[30\]; see also *The Honourable Tim Smith QC, 'The More Things Change the More They Stay the Same? The Evidence Acts 1995 -- An Overview' (1995) 18 UNSWLJ 1* at 30-31 and *In the Marriage of Atkinson* (1997) 136 FLR 347 at 355-357 per Baker J);

    - if the proceeding is a criminal proceeding -- whether the party seeking to adduce the evidence is a defendant or the prosecutor;

    - the nature of the offence, cause of action or defence alleged in the proceeding (*R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638 at \[47\]);

    - the nature of the offence or liability to penalty to which the evidence relates;

    - the likelihood of any proceeding being brought to prosecute the offence or impose the penalty (*R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638 at \[41\]-\[57\]);

    - any resulting unfairness to a party (e.g., unfairness arising from the party being forced to disclose potentially incriminatory conduct which would not be protected by a certificate for tactical reasons) (*R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638 at \[36\]; *Gedeon v R* \[2013\] NSWCCA 257 at \[292\]);

    - the likely effects of requiring the evidence to be given, and the means available to limit its publication (*R v Lodhi* \[2006\] NSWSC 638 at \[41\]-\[57\] per Whealy J; see also *Attorney-General (NSW) v Borland* \[2007\] NSWCA 201 which considered a similar provision in the *Coroners Act 1980* (NSW));

    - whether the substance of the evidence has already been published;

    - where a charge/s has/have already been brought arising out of the events to which the evidence relates, whether the charge/s have been finally dealt with (*R v Collisson* \[2003\] NSWCCA 212);

    -  if the witness will not be required to give evidence, the way in which the refusal to give evidence is to be approached by the tribunal of fact (thus, in a jury trial, the content of anticipated directions to the jury regarding the approach to any refusal to give evidence) (*R v Hore* \[2005\] NSWCCA 3 at \[218\]--\[219\]).

57. It may be appropriate to consider the question of whether 'the interests of justice require that the witness give the evidence' on a topic by topic basis (*Rich v Attorney General* (NSW) \[2013\] NSWCA 419 at \[45\]-\[49\] per Leeming JA (Bathurst CJ and Beazley P agreeing)).

---

### Chunk 9 — Giving a certificate; Effect of a certificate (paras 58–68)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Evidence Regulations 2009 (Vic) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128(5), s128(6), s128(7), s128(9), s128(12)-(13) (Cth)] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128(5)-(7),(9) - Certificate form and immunity effect] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: form of certificate and use/derivative use immunity, limits and inter-jurisdictional effect] [CONCEPTS: certificate form, Evidence Regulations Form 1, use immunity, derivative use immunity, retrial exception, federal recognition, tribunals, cross-examination use] [CASE: Bax Global (Australia) Pty Ltd v Evans (1999) 47 NSWLR 538; Cornwell v R (2007) 231 CLR 260]

Covers: Form of certificate, its legal effect (use and derivative use immunity), jurisdictional limits and retrial exception (paras 58–68).

58. Regulation 9 of the *Evidence Regulations 2009* (Vic) provides that a certificate for the purposes of s128 and s128A of the Act may be in the form set out in Form 1 of the Schedule to those Regulations.

59. Section 128(7) confers both a use and a derivative use immunity with respect to evidence which is the subject of a certificate in any proceeding:

    - in a Victorian court (see UEA Dictionary); and

    - before any person or body authorised by a Victorian law, or by consent of parties, to hear, receive and examine evidence.

60. The second limb, bodies authorised to hear, receive and examine evidence, was added to the Uniform Evidence Law following an ALRC recommendation in 2005 (see ALRC 102 at \[15.119\]-\[15.121\], and Recommendation 15-9). This was designed to ensure that the certificate was effective in proceedings where the rules of evidence do not apply, such as tribunals.

61. A certificate does not prevent the tender of evidence in criminal proceedings relating to the falsity of the evidence.

62. In addition, pursuant to s128(12)-(13) of the *Evidence Act 1995 *(Cth), the use of evidence covered by a Victorian certificate is prohibited in Federal and ACT courts, in any proceeding for an offence against a law of the Commonwealth or the ACT or for recovery of a civil penalty under a law of the Commonwealth or the ACT. A Victorian certificate will not prohibit the use of the evidence in other State courts or overseas jurisdictions.

63. 'Proceeding' is not defined but, given the inclusion of both courts and tribunals, can be taken to cover any step in an action or suit where there is an issue in dispute between parties and which includes evidence to be adduced, whether or not under the rules of evidence.

64. In *Cornwell v R* (2007) 231 CLR 260; \[2007\] HCA 12, the High Court held that a retrial was not a 'proceeding' to which s128 (as it then was) applied (and so the second trial judge was not bound by a decision made by the first trial judge).

65. The Court observed that, traditionally at a retrial, parties were able to tender evidence of admissions made at the first trial and there were no clear words in s128 (as it then was) to negate that possibility.

66. Section 128(9) was inserted expressly to provide that the prosecution in a criminal trial would, in a retrial (or a trial of the accused for an offence arising from the same facts), be able to tender evidence of admissions made by the accused under the protection of a certificate at the first trial. While this amendment preserves the result of *Cornwell*, the High Court's construction of 'proceeding' remains available.

67. With respect to use of the evidence in cross-examination as opposed to admitting it in the proceeding, in *Bax Global (Australia) Pty Ltd v Evans* (1999) 47 NSWLR 538; \[1999\] NSWSC 815 it was observed that 'there is a plausible view that s128(7) would not prevent the use of the \[evidence the subject of a certificate\] in cross-examination' (*Bax Global (Australia) Pty Ltd v Evans* (1999) 47 NSWLR 538; \[1999\] NSWSC 815 at \[53\] per Austin J).

68. However, as Odgers observes, such use is 'use against the person' within the terms of the provision (Odgers, *Uniform Evidence Law *(2022) at \[EA.128.600\]).

---

### Chunk 10 — Challenge to certificate and exception for accused (paras 69–74)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Evidence Act 2001 (Tas) / Uniform Evidence Law] [SECTION: s128(8), s128(10), s189(6), s189(1)] [CITATION: Evidence Act s128(8) & s128(10) - Certificate challenge and accused exception] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: effect of challenge to certificate and criminal proceedings exception for accused's evidence] [CONCEPTS: certificate invalidity challenge, Cornwell v R, s128(8) protection, falsity exception, accused evidence exception, voir dire exception] [CASE: Cornwell v R (2007) 231 CLR 260]

Covers: Effect of challenge to a s128 certificate and the exception where accused gives evidence on facts in issue (paras 69–74).

69. Section 128(8) responds to another issue raised in *Cornwell v R *(2007) 231 CLR 260; \[2007\] HCA 12 -- in this instance, when the validity of the certificate is called into question.

70. In *Cornwell*, the Court considered a range of circumstances whereby it would be 'absurd' if the witness were not to receive the protection of a certificate -- for instance, 'merely because the witness and the registry lost all copies of the certificate' (*Cornwell v R *(2007) 231 CLR 260; \[2007\] HCA 12 at \[91\] per Gleeson CJ, Gummow, Heydon and Crennan JJ).

71. A certificate has effect irrespective of the outcome of any challenge to its validity (s128(8)). Section 128(8) recognises that the grant of a certificate under s128 is not the same as any other evidential ruling. To ensure the policy behind s128 is effective, the witness must be certain of being able to rely on a certificate in future proceedings, including even if the certificate was given in error.

72. This subsection provides that, in criminal proceedings, s128 does not apply to the giving of evidence which goes to either an act or a state of mind which is a fact in issue.

73. This is not limited to 'direct evidence'; it extends to 'the giving of evidence by the defendant of facts from which the doing of the act or the having of the state of mind can be inferred' (*Cornwell v R* (2007) 231 CLR 260; \[2007\] HCA 12 at \[84\]).

74. Section 189(6) provides that s128(10) does not apply to a hearing to decide a preliminary question under s189 (The voir dire). Section 189(1) defines a preliminary as a question about whether evidence should be admitted. This means an accused may rely on the privilege against self-incrimination when giving evidence under s189.

<!-- block_051 master -->

# Public interest immunity and informer protection
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: public interest immunity — informer confidentiality] [CONCEPTS: public interest immunity, informer protection, disclosure refusal, confidentiality of sources, law enforcement secrecy, recruitment of informers, HCA authority, AB v CD, Crupi]

Public interest immunity protects the confidentiality of informers and can justify refusal to disclose informer identity or information that would reveal a source. The High Court reaffirmed the primacy of protecting informers in Chief Commissioner of Police v Crupi [2024] HCA 34, reiterating the reasoning in AB (a pseudonym) v CD (a pseudonym) [2018] HCA 58. The essential rationale is that public knowledge that a source’s expectation of confidentiality has been breached would undermine police ability to recruit informers in future. Where disclosure would substantially prejudice the public interest in effective law enforcement or the safety of informers, a court may uphold an immunity claim and withhold disclosure. The balancing exercise assesses the public interest in disclosure against the countervailing public interest in maintaining confidentiality of sources.

### Authority - Chief Commissioner of Police v Crupi [2024] HCA 34
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Chief Commissioner of Police v Crupi [2024] HCA 34] [TOPIC: informer confidentiality reaffirmed] [CONCEPTS: public interest immunity, informer identity, HCA, disclosure refusal, confidentiality expectations, law enforcement]

Chief Commissioner of Police v Crupi [2024] HCA 34 confirms that courts should give primacy to protecting the confidentiality of police informers where disclosure would undermine future recruitment of informers or threaten their safety. The decision reiterates principles stated in AB (a pseudonym) v CD (a pseudonym) [2018] HCA 58 and emphasises that the public interest in effective law enforcement may outweigh the public interest in disclosure in appropriate circumstances.

### Authority - AB (a pseudonym) v CD (a pseudonym) [2018] HCA 58
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: AB (a pseudonym) v CD (a pseudonym) [2018] HCA 58] [TOPIC: public interest immunity principles] [CONCEPTS: public interest immunity, informer protection, source confidentiality, High Court, disclosure balancing, law enforcement]

AB (a pseudonym) v CD (a pseudonym) [2018] HCA 58 articulated the modern test for public interest immunity where confidentiality of informers is claimed, setting out the balancing of competing public interests and confirming that the protection of informer identity is a legitimate ground for non-disclosure when disclosure would harm law enforcement objectives.

# Fraud — indictable offences (charging guidance)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: prosecutorial / charging guidance] [TOPIC: fraud — summary vs indictable charging thresholds] [CONCEPTS: fraud charging, indictable vs summary, DPP guidance, computer-related fraud, monetary threshold, $20,000 rule, course of conduct, co-accused consistency, penalty adequacy]

For computer-related fraud allegations, the DPP's charging guidance prefers a summary charge where the alleged fraud amount is less than $20,000, unless (1) the charge forms part of a course of conduct or series of crimes that are indictable; (2) circumstances relating to the alleged offender or the conduct make the lower court penalty inadequate; or (3) a co-accused was dealt with on indictment (consistency between co-offenders should be sought unless compelling reasons exist). Prosecutors should assess indictability against seriousness, multiplicity, and comparative sentencing exposures.

# Commonwealth offence — Criminal Code s480.4 (dishonestly obtaining or dealing in personal financial information)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code (Cth)] [SECTION: s480.4] [CITATION: Criminal Code s480.4 (Cth)] [TYPE: offence] [TOPIC: dishonestly obtaining/dealing in personal financial information] [CONCEPTS: personal financial information, passwords, identity fraud, bank accounts, Commonwealth offence, deceit to elicit information, access to accounts]

Criminal Code s480.4 (Cth) creates an offence of dishonestly obtaining or dealing in personal financial information, and is used where a person uses deceit to elicit personal financial information (for example, passwords) to access bank accounts, obtain loans, or commit other financial crimes. The provision targets deceitful elicitation of information that leads to misuse by involving banks, lending institutions, or corporations. Prosecutors commonly use this provision where the conduct relies on obtaining personal financial information to commit fraud.

### Authority - JOD v R [2009] NSWCCA 205
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: JOD v R [2009] NSWCCA 205] [TOPIC: identity theft and sentencing parity] [CONCEPTS: identity fraud, organised syndicate, objective criminality, parity principle, sentencing]

JOD v R [2009] NSWCCA 205 considered identity theft by an organised syndicate that used false documents to open bank accounts and defraud financial institutions. The Court applied parity principles, noting higher objective criminality for an offender with a leading role and repeated offending, and dismissed the appeal against sentence. The case illustrates assessing relative roles and objective seriousness in sentencing co-offenders.

# Definitions relevant to fraud investigations
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Crimes Act 1900 (NSW)] [TYPE: statutory definition / doctrinal definition] [TOPIC: deception — definitional contours] [CONCEPTS: deception, deliberate misleading, computer response, accessing computers, intent or recklessness, NSW Crimes Act, misrepresentation]

Deception is defined in the Crimes Act 1900 (NSW) as conduct (words or acts) deliberately or intentionally intended to mislead a person. Examples include deceiving someone about intentions or causing a computer to respond without proper authority (such as accessing another’s computer by entering their password without consent). To ground a fraud offence, the deception must be either intended or reckless — recklessness exists where the actor was aware the conduct might be deceptive and proceeded regardless.

# Dishonesty — judicial standard
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: dishonesty — ordinary person standard] [CONCEPTS: dishonesty test, ordinary person standard, subjective awareness, jury/magistrate decision, community standards, whether conduct dishonest]

Dishonesty is assessed by reference to the standards of ordinary people and whether the defendant knew the conduct would be regarded as dishonest by those standards. Whether an act is dishonest is a matter for the tribunal of fact (judge or jury), applying community standards to the defendant’s conduct and awareness.

# Obtaining property belonging to another — doctrinal definition
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: obtaining property — intent to permanently deprive] [CONCEPTS: obtaining property, control/possession, intention to permanently deprive, inducement of third person, property belonging to another]

"Obtains property belonging to another" means obtaining or enabling ownership, possession, retention or control of property owned by another, or inducing a third person to act so that property is obtained. To convict for fraudulently obtaining property, the prosecution must ordinarily prove an intention to permanently deprive the owner of the property.

# Obtaining a financial advantage — doctrinal definition
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: obtaining a financial advantage by deception] [CONCEPTS: financial advantage, deception to obtain advantage, cause financial disadvantage, inducement of third party, permanent or temporary advantage]

"Obtaining a financial advantage" by deception occurs where a person deceives another to dishonestly obtain a financial advantage for themselves or another, or induces a third person to act resulting in advantage or disadvantage. The advantage or disadvantage may be temporary or permanent; it encompasses causing a financial disadvantage to another person.

# Charging differences — Obtaining goods by false pretences (Criminal Code s250-4)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code] [SECTION: s250-4] [CITATION: Criminal Code s250-4] [TYPE: offence / element of offence] [TOPIC: obtaining goods by false pretences] [CONCEPTS: false representation, inducement, direct evidence, s250-4, goods obtained, mens rea]

Obtaining goods by false pretences under Criminal Code s250-4 requires proof that a false representation of fact made by words or otherwise induced the person who supplied the goods to deliver them. The Crown must ordinarily prove beyond reasonable doubt that the false representation acted on the mind of the supplier and was the operative inducement. A promise as to future conduct is insufficient unless false at the time it was made.

### Authority - Laverty (1970) 54 Cr App R 495 and Sullivan (1945) 30 Cr App R 132
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Laverty (1970) 54 Cr App R 495; Sullivan (1945) 30 Cr App R 132] [TOPIC: proof of inducement in false pretence offences] [CONCEPTS: inducement proof, direct evidence, only-reason principle, false pretence, evidentiary sufficiency]

Laverty (1970) 54 Cr App R 495 stresses that the proper way to prove that a false representation induced a supplier is by direct evidence from the person induced, although Sullivan (1945) 30 Cr App R 132 permits circumstantial proof where the false pretence is the only plausible operative inducement. Courts are cautious about extending Sullivan beyond narrow facts.

### Authority - Gordon Daniel McLeod v R [1996] TASSC 10
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Gordon Daniel McLeod v R [1996] TASSC 10] [TOPIC: insufficiency of evidence of inducement] [CONCEPTS: false pretence, inducement, evidentiary sufficiency, Tasmanian authority]

Gordon Daniel McLeod v R [1996] TASSC 10 held that the Crown failed to establish that a false representation induced the company to deliver goods; the evidence was inadequate. The decision illustrates that courts require proof linking the representation to the supplier’s decision to deliver.

# Charging differences — Dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage — Criminal Code s252A
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code] [SECTION: s252A] [CITATION: Criminal Code s252A] [TYPE: offence] [TOPIC: dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage] [CONCEPTS: s252A, false representation, financial advantage, deception, alternative conviction, intention]

Dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage under Criminal Code s252A covers obtaining a financial advantage through deception, and can capture conduct such as a false representation of intention to pay later (which may not be a false pretence but could amount to deception). Nettlefold J observed in Murphy v R that "services, money or property amount to a financial advantage."

### Authority - Murphy v R [1987] TASSC 15
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Murphy v R [1987] TASSC 15] [TOPIC: financial advantage — interpretation] [CONCEPTS: financial advantage, services as advantage, Tasmanian authority, Nettlefold J, interpretation of s252A]

Murphy v R [1987] TASSC 15 (Nettlefold J) held that services, money or property can amount to a financial advantage for the purposes of the relevant Code provision, supporting a broad understanding of what constitutes "financial advantage."

### Authority - Tasmania v Clark [2018] TASSC 64
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Clark [2018] TASSC 64] [TOPIC: elements of dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage] [CONCEPTS: dishonestly acquire, elements, Tasmanian authority, s252A interpretation]

Tasmania v Clark [2018] TASSC 64 addresses the elements and definitions relevant to the offence of dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage under the Code, clarifying required proof for such an offence in a Tasmanian context.

# Charging differences — Computer-related fraud (Police Offences Act s43A)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Police Offences Act] [SECTION: s43A] [CITATION: Police Offences Act s43A] [TYPE: offence] [TOPIC: computer-related fraud examples] [CONCEPTS: computer fraud, self-service checkout, ATM withdrawal, automated payment systems, online purchases, no staff interaction]

Police Offences Act s43A covers computer-related fraud and is typically used where transactions involve no interaction with a staff member (for example, purchases using a stolen credit card via self-service checkout, ATM fraud, automated phone payments, or online purchases without direct contact). The distinction between offences that involve interpersonal inducement and automated/computer-based transactions is relevant to charging and proof of inducement.

# Supporting evidentiary provision — CODE s255A (intention to deceive or defraud)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Criminal Code] [SECTION: s255A] [CITATION: Criminal Code s255A] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: evidence of intent to deceive or defraud] [CONCEPTS: s255A, intent to deceive, element of offence, not necessary to prove specific victim, evidentiary simplification]

Under Code s255A, on the trial of a person charged with a crime in the relevant Chapter where an intent to deceive or defraud is an element, it is not necessary to prove an intent to deceive or defraud any particular person. The provision allows the prosecution to prove intent without identifying a specific victim where the statutory element is general.

# Compensation orders — summary compensation (Lusted v Doornbusch [2011] TASMC 28)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Lusted v Doornbusch [2011] TASMC 28] [TOPIC: compensation orders — summary, scope and limitations] [CONCEPTS: compensation orders, summary procedure, s68(6), evidence suffices, civil damages on account, not for complex claims, Town or Magistrates’ Court practice]

Lusted v Doornbusch [2011] TASMC 28 explains that straightforward compensation orders can be granted on the spot in summary proceedings. The legislation's purpose is to provide a summary and inexpensive method to compensate victims for personal injury or property damage; it is not intended for complicated claims that require extensive investigation. Under s68(6) (as applied in the decision), a court may make a compensation order if the facts sufficiently appear from evidence presented at trial, available documents, or admissions. An award is in the nature of an account of civil damages and the court should decline to make orders where there is a real issue requiring civil proceedings.

### Authority - Attorney-General for Tasmania v B [2010] TASCCA 6
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Attorney-General for Tasmania v B [2010] TASCCA 6] [TOPIC: compensation awards as on-account of civil damages] [CONCEPTS: compensation order, civil damages, summary procedure, Tasmanian Court of Criminal Appeal]

Attorney-General for Tasmania v B [2010] TASCCA 6 confirms that amounts awarded by way of summary compensation orders are on account of damages recoverable in civil proceedings and emphasises the summary, limited nature of such awards.

# Sentencing — presentation of facts at sentencing (FFP)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: prosecutor facts for sentencing — evidence standards] [CONCEPTS: facts for prosecutor, sentencing evidence, Evidence Act s4(2), Sentencing Act s81, Parker v Tasmania, informal evidence, proof standard]

Facts relied upon by the prosecution to support sentencing must be proved by evidence; they may be presented formally or informally. Evidence can be submitted under Evidence Act s4(2) (formal evidence) or informally under Sentencing Act s81; in Tasmania it is common to submit facts informally at sentencing, as explained in Parker v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 16 at [28]. The sentencing court will assess the reliability and weight of the evidence when determining aggravating circumstances.

### Authority - Parker v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 16
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Parker v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 16] [TOPIC: admissibility and use of facts at sentencing] [CONCEPTS: sentencing evidence, informal admissions, s81 Sentencing Act, Tasmania practice]

Parker v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 16 clarifies Tasmanian practice that prosecutors commonly rely on informal presentation of facts under the Sentencing Act, subject to the sentencing court's assessment and evidentiary requirements.

# Correcting sentence and requirement for an actual sentence (Sentencing Act s94)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act] [SECTION: s94] [CITATION: Sentencing Act s94] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: requirement for an actual sentence] [CONCEPTS: s94, actual sentence, dismissal consequences, s7 dismissal, TNE (transfer of non-sentence), sentence correction]

Sentencing Act s94 requires an actual sentence to be imposed in appropriate circumstances. A dismissal following a plea or finding of guilty under s7 of the Sentencing Act constitutes a sentence; however, dismissal following a transfer to a non-sentence (TNE) or other mechanisms may not amount to an actual sentence. The court must ensure the correct legal form of the sentencing order is recorded.

# De Simoni principle — limits on sentencing for uncharged offences
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: De Simoni principle — sentencing limits] [CONCEPTS: De Simoni, sentencing limits, uncharged conduct, aggravating facts, not punishable if constitute different offence, Tasmania application, Walsh, Lovegrove]

The De Simoni principle (The Queen v De Simoni (1980) 147 CLR 383) provides that a person may only be punished for the offence of which they have been convicted and a sentencing judge cannot take into account circumstances of aggravation that would have warranted conviction for a more serious offence with different elements. The principle applies to Tasmania as reflected in Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64 and earlier authorities (Walsh v The Queen [1996] TASSC 55; Lovegrove v The Queen [1961]). While a sentencing judge may consider all conduct of the accused, they must not impose punishment for an offence they were not charged with or convicted of.

### Authority - R v De Simoni (1980) 147 CLR 383
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v De Simoni (1980) 147 CLR 383] [TOPIC: sentencing — limits on consideration of uncharged offences] [CONCEPTS: De Simoni principle, sentencing boundaries, uncharged conduct, High Court authority]

R v De Simoni (1980) 147 CLR 383 established that a sentencing court may not take into account aggravating circumstances which would have warranted conviction for a more serious offence of which the offender was not convicted; sentencing must be confined to the offence of conviction.

### Authority - Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64] [TOPIC: application of De Simoni in Tasmania] [CONCEPTS: De Simoni application, Tasmania, sentencing law, limitations on aggravating factors]

Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64 explains the application of the De Simoni principle in the Tasmanian context, affirming that sentencing judges must not treat facts as aggravating where they constitute ingredients of a different offence.

### Authority - Walsh v The Queen [1996] TASSC 55
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Walsh v The Queen [1996] TASSC 55] [TOPIC: De Simoni principle application] [CONCEPTS: sentencing limits, Tasmania, overlapping offences]

Walsh v The Queen [1996] TASSC 55 is a Tasmanian authority applying the De Simoni principle and illustrating how overlapping elements and separate offences should be treated at sentencing.

# Use of uncharged conduct in mitigation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: using uncharged conduct to negate mitigation] [CONCEPTS: mitigation, uncharged conduct, sentencing, Banks v Tasmania, negative mitigation, factual negation]

While sentencing cannot punish uncharged offences, uncharged conduct may be used to negate pleas in mitigation — for example, to deny a mitigating account of the offence. Banks v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 1 exemplifies that uncharged conduct can be used to show that the defendant’s asserted mitigation is false or limited.

### Authority - Banks v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Banks v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 1] [TOPIC: uncharged conduct used to negate pleas in mitigation] [CONCEPTS: mitigation, uncharged conduct, sentencing evidence, Tasmanian authority]

Banks v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 1 shows that sentencing courts can take into account uncharged conduct where it rebuts or limits pleas for mitigation, provided it is not used to impose punishment for a separate uncharged offence.

# Sentencing doctrines — proportionality and totality
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: proportionality and totality in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: proportionality, totality, overall criminality, just sentence, Veen (No 2), R v KM, crushing sentence, aggregate sentence]

Proportionality requires that a sentence be proportionate to the objective circumstances of the offence (Veen v The Queen (No 2) (1988) 164 CLR 465). Totality requires that when imposing sentences for multiple offences, the court ensure the overall sentence is just and appropriate, avoiding crushing punishments while ensuring aggregate punishment matches overall criminality (R v KM [2004] NSWCCA 65).

### Authority - Veen v The Queen (No 2) (1988) 164 CLR 465
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Veen v The Queen (No 2) (1988) 164 CLR 465] [TOPIC: proportionality principle in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: proportionality, sentencing principle, High Court, objective seriousness]

Veen v The Queen (No 2) (1988) 164 CLR 465 established the principle that sentences must be proportionate to the objective seriousness of the offending.

### Authority - R v KM [2004] NSWCCA 65
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v KM [2004] NSWCCA 65] [TOPIC: totality principle in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: totality, aggregate sentences, just overall sentence, NSWCCA]

R v KM [2004] NSWCCA 65 discusses the totality principle requiring sentencing courts to ensure the aggregate sentence for multiple offences is just and proportionate.

# Sentencing considerations — youth and rehabilitation (Garcie v Lusted [2014] TASSC 27)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Garcie v Lusted [2014] TASSC 27] [TOPIC: sentencing youth and rehabilitation emphasis] [CONCEPTS: youth sentencing, rehabilitation, public interest, custody avoidance, reform opportunities, Tasmania]

Garcie v Lusted [2014] TASSC 27 recognises that young offenders (which may include late teens) have significant consideration for rehabilitation; courts should give every reasonable opportunity for rehabilitation and avoid exposing youths to the corrupting influences of custody where rehabilitation prospects outweigh deterrence or denunciation.

# Delay as a mitigating factor (Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6] [TOPIC: delay in prosecution as mitigation] [CONCEPTS: delay mitigation, anxiety and stress, rehabilitation during delay, unnecessary delay, self-inflicted delay, Tasmanian authorities]

Delay in prosecution can be a substantial mitigating factor in sentencing when it is unnecessary (longer than ordinarily required given the case complexity) or has resulted in anxiety and stress to the accused or demonstrable rehabilitation during the delay. Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6 summarises the two bases: (1) delay causes punitive anxiety/stress; (2) delay may evidence rehabilitation. Delay that is self-inflicted (absconding, evasion) and delay attributable to the accused reduces or negates mitigation credit. The decision aggregates earlier authorities explaining the limits and conditions under which delay is mitigatory.

### Authorities cited in Palmer and relevant to delay
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Todd [1982] 2 NSWLR 517; Prehn v The Queen [2003] TASSC 55; R v Whyte [2004] VSCA 5; R v Pickard [2011] SASCFC 134; R v SP [2004] ACTCA 16; Davidson v Tasmania [2018] TASCCA 9; R v Fahda [1999] NSWCCA 267; R v Barker [2006] NSWCCA 20] [TOPIC: delay jurisprudence] [CONCEPTS: delay mitigation, unnecessary delay, self-inflicted delay, rehabilitation during delay, case-by-case analysis]

These authorities together supply principles: delay can mitigate if unnecessary or if rehabilitation occurred during the delay; delay caused by the accused’s actions reduces mitigation; exercise of legal rights (e.g., seeking trial or appealing) may not wholly negate delay mitigation but limits its weight.

### Public interest immunity for disclosure
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Public interest immunity - Chief Commissioner v Crupi 2024] [CATEGORY: practice note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: primacy of protection of informers and HCA authority] [CONCEPTS: public interest immunity, informers, confidentiality, Chief Commissioner v Crupi, AB v CD, HCA]

Tactical note: Primacy of protection of informers. See recent HCA case of Chief Commissioner of Police v Crupi [2024] HCA 34 (11 September 2024) which reiterates AB (a pseudonym) v CD (a pseudonym), [2018] HCA 58; (2018) 93 ALJR 59; 362 ALR 1.

"Public knowledge that a source's expectations of confidentiality had been breached would certainly undermine the ability of police to recruit informers in future"

### Indictable offences: Fraud — Charging advice from DPP
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Justices Act 1959 s72 - DPP Charging Advice - Fraud] [ACT: Justices Act 1959 (Tas)] [SECTION: s72] [CATEGORY: practice note] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: charging approach for computer-related fraud — summary vs indictment] [CONCEPTS: fraud charging, summary charge, indictment, computer-related fraud, $20,000 threshold, co-accused consistency]

Tactical workflow for charging decision in computer-related fraud.

Where the computer-related crime involves fraud a similar approach to stealing as provided by s72 of the Justices Act 1959 should be taken. That is, a summary charge should be preferred where the amount of the alleged computer-related fraud is less than $20,000 unless:

1. the charge involved forms part of a course of conduct or series of crimes which are indictable
2. circumstances relating to the alleged offender and/or to the conduct are such that the penalty provision in the lower court would not be adequate
3. a co-accused was dealt with on indictment (prosecutors should strive for consistency as between co-offenders unless there are compelling reasons not to

### Commonwealth offence: Criminal Code s480.4 — Dishonestly obtaining or dealing in personal financial information
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Criminal Code s480.4 - Personal financial information offence (Commonwealth)] [ACT: Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth)] [SECTION: s480.4] [CATEGORY: practice note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Commonwealth offence for dishonestly obtaining personal financial information] [CONCEPTS: s480.4, personal financial information, passwords, CDPP charging advice, identity theft, bank requirement]

This covers the practitioner's note on Commonwealth offence use.

Commonwealth offence:

See Cth Code Section 480.4 of the Criminal Code - Dishonestly obtaining or dealing in personal financial information, (in this case it was passwords), (CDPP Charging advice pdf saved in research file)

Seems to be used when they use deceit to elicit information either from vic or otherwise to then use that info to access accounts or get loans such as credit card numbers, verbal passwords and account numbers. It appease thi "personal financial information' is distinct from "personal info such as drivers licence etc).

MUST involve a bank or lending institution or corporation.

### JOD v R [2009] NSWCCA 205 — identity theft case authority
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: JOD v R [2009] NSWCCA 205 (25 Aug 2009)] [CITATION: JOD v R 2009 NSWCCA 205 - Identity theft sentencing] [CATEGORY: case note] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: identity theft — organised syndicate, sentencing and parity] [CONCEPTS: identity theft, organised crime, sentencing, parity, financial institutions]

Tactical note: Case summary for reference.

JOD V R - [2009] NSWCCA 205 (25 08 2009)
GILES J ; HALL J ; HULME J ;
Identity theft - applicant involved in organised crime syndicate which took over identities of legitimate individuals and entities in order to defraud financial institutions - applicant presenting false documents to open bank accounts in false names, withdraw money from genuine bank accounts or make fraudulent claims in relation to credit cards and bank loans - application of parity principles - offenders charged with different offences not strictly co-offenders having regard to relevant factual and other differences in respective offending - higher objective criminality than co-offender due to his role in syndicate and repeated offending over lengthy period - no disproportion between head sentences imposed on co-offenders - appeal against sentence dismissed
Subjects: IDENTITY FRAUD ;
Legis. Cons.: FINANCIAL TRANSACTION REPORTS ACT 1988 S24 1 ; CRIMINAL CODE S145.1 5 ; CRIMINAL CODE S480.4 ; CRIMES ACT 1900 NSW S178BA ; CRIMES ACT 1900 NSW S300 2 ;
Cases Cons.: LOWE V R - 1984 154 CLR 606 ; POSTIGLIONE V R - 1997 189 CLR 295 ; PHAM MINH TAN V R - 2009 193 ACrimR 220 ; GAO V R - [2008] NSWCCA 234 ;
Notes: Applicant: Jay Owen Dowling
Related Items: R V DOWLING - NSW DISTRICT COURT 20 11 2007
View the Judgment

---

### Key Definitions relating to Fraud (anecdotal — not from authority or acts)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Key Definitions - Fraud Definitions Anecdotal] [CATEGORY: practice note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: practitioner definitions of deception, dishonesty, obtaining property and financial advantage] [CONCEPTS: deception, dishonesty, obtaining property, financial advantage, recklessness]

This chunk covers practitioner-provided definitions used to understand fraudulent acts.

To understand what may constitute a fraudulent act, there are some key terms that need to be defined. 

DECEPTION

Deception is defined in the Crimes Act 1900 (NSW) as something that you say or do where the act of doing so was conceived and executed to deliberately and intentionally mislead a person (victim). 

The crimes act provides examples of deception as;

- Deceiving somebody about your intentions.

- Causing a computer to respond in a way that you don't have the proper authority to do so.  Such as accessing somebody else's computer by entering their password without their consent. 

To commit fraud, the deception must be either intended or reckless.

Recklessness is where you were aware that what you were doing was possibly deceptive, but you did it anyway. You were, essentially, indifferent to the possibility that your conduct was deceptive.

DISHONESTY

Whether the act was dishonest or not is based on the standards of ordinary people and whether the act was known to have been dishonest by the defendant, according to the standards of ordinary people.

Dishonesty is a matter to be decided by the courts. In deciding whether somebody has been dishonest the magistrate or jury, depending on the case, will apply the standards of ordinary people to determine if the conduct was dishonest.

OBTAINS PROPERTY BELONGING TO ANOTHER

This is where a person obtains or enables ownership of property belonging to another person or where a third person is induced to do something that results in obtaining, retaining, possessing or controlling the property.  To be found guilty of fraudulently obtaining property belonging to another, the person must have intended to permanently deprive the owner of the property.

OBTAINING A FINANCIAL ADVANTAGE

Obtaining a financial advantage is a fraudulent act where a person deceives another to dishonestly obtain a financial advantage for themselves or for someone else.  It can also include inducing a third person to do something that results in obtaining a financial advantage for oneself or another person. 

Obtaining a financial advantage includes where a person causes any financial disadvantage to another or where a third person is induced to do so.  This advantage or disadvantage could be permanent or temporary.

---

### Charging differences — OBTAINING GOODS BY FALSE PRETENCES (Criminal Code s250-4) and examples
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Criminal Code s250-4 - Obtaining goods by false pretences & prosecution guidance] [ACT: Criminal Code (Commonwealth/Tasmania context as applicable)] [SECTION: s250-4] [CATEGORY: practice note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: differences between obtaining goods by false pretences and other fraud offences; prosecution proof and examples] [CONCEPTS: false pretence, s250-4, direct evidence, inducement, Laverty, Sullivan, examples]

This chunk covers the distinction between offences and prosecution proof requirements for false pretences.

OBTAINING GOODS BY FALSE PRETENCES

Criminal Code Section 250-4

EXAMPLES:

Where a person uses a stolen credit card to purchase goods from a business and the transaction occurs between the offender and a staff member.

Where a person pretends to be an employee of a business to obtain goods from a supplier of that business.

DISHONESTLY ACQUIRING A FINANCIAL ADVANTAGE

Criminal Code Section 252A-1/7

EXAMPLES:

Where a person takes a price sticker off a lower priced item and swaps it with a higher priced item in order to purchase the item for a cheaper price.

Where a person obtains (from a staff member) pre-paid mobile phone credit using a stolen credit card.

Where a person advertises an item for sale on ebay/gumtree and receives funds from the buyer and fails to deliver the item purchased.

COMPUTER RELATED FRAUD

Police Offences Act Section 43A

EXAMPLES:

Where a person purchases items with a stolen credit card using a "self-service" checkout and has no interaction with a staff member.

Where a person uses a stolen ATM card and withdraws cash from an ATM.

Where a person uses a stolen credit card to pay a bill using an automated phone payment system.

Where a person uses a stolen credit card to purchase items online with no communication with an actual person.

Need direct evidence form the victim (person induced) that he false representations of the defendant induced them to provide the foods.

Promise as to future conduct is not enough. Must be false at time.

Summary: the Crown does have to prove in each case that the false pretence acted on the people who were delivering them, because if somebody was just giving out goods willy nilly to anybody who came past, that hypothetical situation it may not matter what you called yourself if they're prepared to just give them to you and not worried about paying. But of course I suggest in the real world that doesn't happen." On the basis of the evidence subsequently elicited from the main Crown witness on count 1 it might be suspected that in the real world it does happen.

4. Prosecuting counsel should heed the advice of the Court of Appeal in Laverty (1970) 54 Cr App R 495 at 497, 498:

"It is axiomatic that it is for the prosecution to prove that the false representation acted on the mind \... and in the ordinary way, and the Court emphasises this, the matter should be proved by direct evidence. However, it was said in SULLIVAN (1945) 30 Cr App R 132 that the inducement need not be proved by direct evidence, and I quote from the headnote 'If the facts are such that the alleged false pretence is the only reason that could be suggested as having been the operative inducement.' In the special facts of that case the Court held that the prosecution had given sufficient proof, although they made it very clear that the proper way and the ordinary way of proving the matter was by direct evidence. \... This Court is very anxious not to extend the principle in SULLIVAN (supra) more than is necessary. The proper way of proving these matters is through the mouth of the person to whom the false representation is conveyed \... "

### Gordon Daniel McLeod v R [1996] TASSC 10 — evidentiary insufficiency for false representation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Gordon Daniel McLeod v R [1996] TASSC 10 (19 March 1996)] [CITATION: McLeod v R 1996 TASSC 10 - false representation and inducement] [CATEGORY: case note] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: requirement to prove false representation induced delivery of goods; evidentiary comparison] [CONCEPTS: false representation, inducement, ss249-250, Clemesha, evidentiary insufficiency]

This chunk covers the judgment note on proof of inducement.

Gordon Daniel McLeod v R [1996] TASSC 10 (19 March 1996)

8. To secure a conviction it was incumbent on the Crown to establish beyond reasonable doubt that by means of a false representation of fact, made by words or otherwise, the appellant obtained the goods from the company or induced the company to deliver them to him (see ss249 and 250). The evidence was inadequate in that regard. The state of the evidence may be compared with that in Clemesha v R [(1978) WAR 193] where at 202 Jones J said that "the signing of the false name certainly could be regarded as a representation of a matter of fact which was false, but that was not an inducing factor; it was a matter of indifference to X whether the name of his supposed hirer was Y or Z". I agree with Wright J that this case is clearly distinguishable from Michell v R A21/1990

[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: CODE: 255A - Evidence relating to intention to deceive or defraud] [CATEGORY: statutory note] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: evidentiary rule on intent in offences where intent to deceive is an element] [CONCEPTS: intent to deceive, CODE 255A, evidentiary rule, fraud elements, no need to prove particular person]

This chunk covers the statutory evidentiary rule regarding intent to deceive in Chapter offences.

Evidence relating to intention to deceive or defraud

On the trial of a person charged with a crime under this Chapter of which an intent to deceive or defraud is an element, it is not necessary to prove an intent to deceive or defraud any particular person.

### Dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage contrary to the Code, s252A — Murphy v R and Tasmania v Clark
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Code s252A - Murphy v R 1987 TASSC 15 - dishonestly acquiring financial advantage] [ACT: Criminal Code (Tasmania or Commonwealth as applicable)] [SECTION: s252A] [CASE: Murphy v R [1987] TASSC 15; Tasmania v Clark [2018] TASSC 64] [CATEGORY: case note] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: elements of dishonestly acquiring financial advantage and judicial interpretation] [CONCEPTS: s252A, financial advantage, Murphy v R, Tas v Clark, Nettlefold J]

This chunk covers authority on what constitutes "financial advantage".

Dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage contrary to the Code, s252A.

Nettlefold J in Murphy v R [1987] TASSC 15; (1987) Tas R 178 at 184, "services, money or property amount to a financial advantage".

Note: as an alternative conviction - A false representation as to the offender's intention to pay at a later date, whilst not a false pretence, could constitute a deception for the purposes of a charge of dishonestly acquiring a financial advantage contrary to the Code, s252A.

### Compensation orders — Lusted v Doornbusch [2011] TASMC 28
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Lusted v Doornbusch [2011] TASMC 28 (4 November 2011)] [CITATION: Lusted v Doornbusch 2011 TASMC 28 - compensation orders summary] [CATEGORY: case note] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: summary of when compensation orders can be made on the spot and statutory approach under s68] [CONCEPTS: compensation orders, s68, summary, civil alternative, evidentiary sufficiency, Kneeshaw, Braham]

This chunk covers guidance on compensation orders in summary proceedings.

Where straight forward and simple they can be granted on the spot.

6. "The general purpose of the legislation is to provide a summary and inexpensive method of compensating a victim for personal injuries or property damage. In an appropriate case, the legislation provides a convenient means for a victim to avoid instituting separate civil proceedings to recover damages. The legislation is not intended to cater for claims involving complicated or extensive inquiry or investigation; R v Kneeshaw [1975] QB 57 at 60, R v Braham [1977] VicRp 11; [1977] VR 104 at 110 - 111 and R v Ferrari [1997] QCA 73. If it appears to the court that there is a real issue to be determined, it should decline to make an order and leave the question to be determined by a civil court in accordance with its normal procedures; Landolt [(1992) 63 A Crim R 220] and 223."

7. See also Attorney-General for Tasmania v B [2010] TASCCA 6 at [20] and following. An amount awarded is merely on account of the damages an injured party might recover in a civil court; refer Crawford CJ at [22].

8. By [s68(6)] I may exercise the power to make a compensation order "if the facts sufficiently appear from any evidence or material presented to the court, or from the available documents, together with admissions made by or on behalf of any person in connection with the proposed exercise of the powers". The evidence or material presented to the court is the evidence given on the trial or material presented during the sentencing hearing. [S68] makes no provision for the court to conduct a hearing in the nature of a trial to receive further evidence about a claim but it is not bound by the rules of evidence and in a case involving "loss, destruction or damage to property" may inform itself in any way it considers appropriate; subs(9).

### Sentencing — Power to read Facts for Prosecutor (FFP) for sentencing and evidence norms
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Sentencing evidence norms - FFP and s4(2) Evidence Act / s81 Sentencing Act] [ACT: Evidence Act (Tas) / Sentencing Act (Tas)] [CATEGORY: practice note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: use of formally or informally submitted facts to support charges for sentencing; Parker v Tasmania authority] [CONCEPTS: FFP, facts for sentencing, s4(2) Evidence Act, s81 Sentencing Act, Parker v Tasmania]

Tactical workflow: evidence for sentencing submissions.

Evidence of facts to support charge must be proved by evidence. That evidence can be submitted formally or informally (s 4(2) evidence act or informally under s. 81 of the Sentencing Act. That is the common way in Tas (para [28] Parker v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 16 (8 October 2019)

### Correcting sentence (sentencing order) — s94 Sentencing Act note
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Sentencing Act s94 - Correcting sentence and what qualifies as an actual sentence] [ACT: Sentencing Act (Tas)] [SECTION: s94] [CATEGORY: practice note] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: requirements for an actual sentence and status of dismissals] [CONCEPTS: s94, actual sentence, dismissal, TNE, sentencing order correction]

This covers when a sentence is 'actual' for purposes of s94.

Section 94 sentencing act requires an actual sentence. Dismissal following a plea or finding of guilty under s 7 of the sentencing act is a sentence. But Dismissal following a TNE or otherwise is not.
