### De Simoni principle — R v De Simoni and Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: R v De Simoni (1981) 147 CLR 383; Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64] [CITATION: De Simoni principle - Tasmania v Gladwin 2016 application] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: limits on taking into account uncharged conduct at sentencing — De Simoni principle] [CONCEPTS: De Simoni, sentencing limits, uncharged conduct, Walsh, Lovegrove, mitigation, aggravation]

This chunk covers the De Simoni principle as applied in Tasmania.

The principal in R v De Simoni

Tasmania v Gladwin [2016] TASSC 64

12. The principle in R v De Simoni (1980) 147 CLR 383, as it has been applied in Tasmania, is that a person may only be punished for the offence of which he or she has been convicted, without regard to circumstances which are the ingredients of a different offence, with which he or she has not been charged or convicted (see Walsh v The Queen [1996] TASSC 55; (1996) 6 Tas R 70). In De Simoni, it was acknowledged that this principle had already been recognised and applied in Tasmania in Lovegrove v The Queen [1961] TASStRp 13; [1961] Tas SR 106

In The Queen v De Simoni (1981) 147 CLR 383 at 389, Gibbs CJ said:

the general principle that the sentence imposed on an offender should take account of all the circumstances of the offence is subject to a more fundamental and important principle, that no one should be punished for an offence of which he has not been convicted ... The combined effect of the two principles, so far as it is relevant for present purposes, is that a judge, in imposing sentence, is entitled to consider all the conduct of the accused, including that which would aggravate the offence, but cannot take into account circumstances of aggravation which would have warranted a conviction for a more serious offence.

Applies not only to contextual facts that would lead to a more serious charge, but also to lesser charges where there are not overlapping elements. EG: rape may include threats of violence, but it would not be appropriate to charge with assault (by threat) independently, as assault is an element of the rape. So in this instance it would be ol. However, if the facts for the rape also included a breach of PFVO, that should be separately charged offence, and ought not be submitted as being an aggravating circumstance to the Rape. See Walsh (1996) 6 Tas R [93].

However, can use uncharged conduct or facts to negate pleas in mitigation! "As to the offending, I am able to take into account that the acts of driving were done in attempt to avoid apprehension, even though that conduct is, or may be, the subject of a different charge: Banks v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 1, 31 Tas R 342."

---

### Proportionality and Totality — sentencing doctrines
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Proportionality and Totality - Veen No 2; R v KM] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: sentencing principles requiring proportionality and totality] [CONCEPTS: proportionality, totality, Veen v The Queen (No 2), overall criminality, just and appropriate sentence, crushing sentence]

This covers the core sentencing principles.

The common law principles of proportionality and totality should be considered.

Proportionality requires that a sentence should be proportional to the objective circumstances of the offence: Veen v The Queen (No 2)(1988) 164 CLR 465.

Totality requires a court in imposing sentences for a number of offences, to ensure the overall sentence is 'just and appropriate' and adequately reflects the overall criminality of all the offences. This principle seeks to ensure both that the sentence imposed for the offences is not crushing and that the sentences imposed for the offences are not lower than they otherwise would have been: R v KM [2004] NSWCCA 65 at [55]

---

Garcie v Lusted [2014] TASSC 27

{Para 10]

Age of offender (youth can include 19 yo)

High public interest youths being rehabilitated

They should be given every reasonable opp to reform rather than exposed to corrupting nature of custody

Rehabilitation prospects outweigh consideration of dteretance punishment or denunciation.

### Delay as mitigating factor — Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6 (5 July 2024)] [CITATION: Palmer v Tasmania 2024 TASCCA 6 - delay mitigation principles] [CATEGORY: case note] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: delay in prosecution as mitigating factor and relevant principles] [CONCEPTS: delay mitigation, anxiety and stress, rehabilitation during delay, unnecessary delay, self-inflicted delay, Palmer v Tasmania, appellate guidance]

This chunk covers the appellate guidance on delay as mitigation with substantive excerpt.

Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6 (5 July 2024)

"[29] It is well established that delay in an appropriate case can be considered a substantial "mitigating factor": R v Todd [1982] 2 NSWLR 517; Prehn v The Queen [2003] TASSC 55. But is it not mitigatory as such: Director of Public Prosecutions v Allen [2017] TASCCA, 27 Tas R 260 at [19]. Delay as a mitigating factor fixes on two particular points. The first relates to the anxiety and stress of the offender who waits for a considerable period of time before the determination of the charges. The rationale behind this seems to be that it amounts to a form of punishment. In this case, the Court was asked to infer anxiety and stress from the circumstances. As earlier noted, the appellant made a suicide attempt shortly before his appeal against conviction was to be heard and spent some time in hospital."

"30. The second basis on which delay can be treated as a significant mitigating factor is where the offender has demonstrated rehabilitation during the period of delay, or at least what has occurred during the period of delay suggests favourable prospects of rehabilitation. See R v Whyte [2004] VSCA 5, 7 VR 397 at [25], R v Pickard [2011] SASCFC 134 at [95] and R v SP [2004] ACTCA 16 at [31]-[35]."

"31. A review of the authorities reveals two particular points that need to be considered in relation to the question of delay. The first is that the delay must be unnecessary: Pickard (above) at 95, Davidson v Tasmania [2018] TASCCA 9. That is to say, unnecessary in the sense that the resolution of the matter has taken more time than would usually be the situation given the nature of the matter. That involves considerations of the investigations necessary and of the complexity of the prosecution case: Davidson at [4] per Wood J."

"32. The second point is that for it to be mitigatory, the delay must not be "self-inflicted" in the sense of the delay being fairly attributed to the accused where they had absconded from bail, fled the jurisdiction or otherwise avoided being brought to justice. "A delay must necessarily become of less significance, even to the point of given less credit for rehabilitation established during that period": R v Whyte (above) at [25]."

"33. Care needs to be exercised in discounting periods where accused persons have exercised their rights: see R v Fahda [1999] NSWCCA 267 per Simpson J (with whom Studdert J agreed). Even in circumstances where it might be said an accused "can hardly complain of the delay up to committal and from committal to trial when they could have pleaded guilty at any time", delay is not necessarily treated as totally irrelevant: R v Barker [2006] NSWCCA 20 at [78]-[80]."

---

<!-- block_052 master -->

# Aggravating and Mitigating Factors — Palmer v Tasmania (sentencing analysis)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: aggravating and mitigating factors in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: aggravating factors, mitigating factors, family violence, strangulation, ligature, presence of child, psychological harm, prior convictions, remorse, late plea]

Aggravating and mitigating factors in sentencing identify circumstances that increase or decrease the appropriate severity of punishment. Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6 contains a detailed list of aggravating matters (family‑violence context; strangulation/choking; use of ligature; deliberate threat to kill; ongoing abuse and threatening behaviour since September 2019; presence of infant child during offending; significant physical and psychological injuries) and the absence of mitigating matters (no youth, no evidence of remorse, plea entered late after trial commencement, substantial prior convictions including family‑violence breaches, mental health history not shown to reduce culpability). The court’s assessment shows sentencing judges weigh contextual features (place of offending, method, fear imposed, injury, recurrence, presence of witnesses/children) and offender characteristics (prior record, remorse, timeliness of plea, mental health evidence) when calibrating punishment. Sentencing submissions that allege mitigating facts must be supported by evidence where the facts, if disputed, are likely to affect the sentence; the onus is often on the offender to establish such facts on the balance of probabilities. This chunk encapsulates the practical aggravating/mitigating considerations illustrated in Palmer and the procedural consequences when mitigation is unsupported.

# Authority - Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6] [TOPIC: sentencing aggravating/mitigating factors] [CONCEPTS: aggravation, mitigation, family violence sentencing, strangulation, ligature, presence of child, lack of remorse, late plea, prior convictions]

Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6 is authority for the identification and weighting of multiple aggravating factors in family‑violence sentences and for the principle that the absence of mitigating factors (no remorse, late plea, extensive prior offending, and unproven mental‑health mitigation) justifies greater severity. The decision exemplifies treating strangulation and the use of a ligature as serious aggravating features, the specific relevance of offending in the complainant’s home and in the presence of an infant (citing Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 13(a)), and considering ongoing abusive background as enhancing culpability. The case also records that a guilty plea entered only after trial commencement may be deprived of most utilitarian benefit and so attract limited mitigation.

# Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 13(a) — presence of child aggravation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 13(a)] [CITATION: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 13(a)] [TYPE: statutory definition] [TOPIC: family violence — presence of child as aggravating circumstance] [CONCEPTS: Family Violence Act, presence of child, aggravating circumstance, household offending, s 13(a), child exposure, harm to children]

The Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas) s 13(a) identifies circumstances in which behaviour constitutes family violence; offending occurring in the presence of or near an infant or child is relevant to the assessment of harm and may be treated as an aggravating feature at sentencing. In Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6 the court relied on the fact that certain offending occurred in the presence of the complainant’s infant — and that the appellant knew a child was present — to elevate the seriousness because of the additional risk of harm and the heightened fear experienced by the victim and child. Sentencers should record the child’s exposure and consider its impact on denunciation, general deterrence and protection of vulnerable persons.

# Martial arts training as an aggravating sentencing factor — Dahmes v The King [2025] VSCA 213
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [CASE: Dahmes v The King [2025] VSCA 213] [TOPIC: martial arts training and sentencing] [CONCEPTS: martial arts, sentencer reliance, aggravating factor, prior training, physical skills, dangerousness, culpability, sentencing relevance]

Martial arts or professional combat training can be an aggravating factor in sentencing where the offender’s training increases the danger of the conduct or demonstrates enhanced capacity to inflict harm. Dahmes v The King [2025] VSCA 213 (Court of Appeal of Victoria) held that martial arts training was a relevant factor and that a sentencing judge did not err in placing weight on it; the applicant’s previous training enhanced the gravity of his violent acts (headbutting, punching, kneeing and elbowing at a nightclub). The court found no procedural unfairness in taking such training into account. Sentencers should weigh whether the knowledge and skills derived from training made the offending more dangerous, and if so, treat training as aggravating.

# Authority - Dahmes v The King [2025] VSCA 213
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Dahmes v The King [2025] VSCA 213] [TOPIC: martial arts training as aggravation] [CONCEPTS: VSCA, martial arts, aggravation, sentencing, skill increases danger, procedural fairness, leave to appeal]

Dahmes v The King [2025] VSCA 213 confirms that previous martial‑arts training is a legitimate sentencing consideration where it makes violent conduct more dangerous and reflects on culpability; courts may legitimately treat specialized physical training as aggravating if it materially increases the risk or severity of harm.

# Authority — Elsayed v The Queen [2019] VSCA 113 (referred)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Elsayed v The Queen [2019] VSCA 113] [TOPIC: sentencing authorities on related issues] [CONCEPTS: sentencing precedent, comparative authority, VSCA, mitigation, aggravation, reference]

Elsayed v The Queen [2019] VSCA 113 is a referred authority in the martial‑arts/training context and can be consulted for comparative sentencing principles. The source cites Elsayed as a relevant prior decision in the debate about when training in combat techniques should be considered in sentencing.

# Sentencing Act — voluntary intoxication in mitigation (s 11C as referred)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act] [SECTION: s 11C] [TYPE: statutory definition] [TOPIC: voluntary intoxication and pleas in mitigation] [CONCEPTS: voluntary intoxication, mitigation, Sentencing Act s 11C, plea in mitigation, self-induced intoxication, sentencing discount]

Sentencing Act s 11C (as cited in the source) provides that voluntary (self‑induced) intoxication is irrelevant as a plea in mitigation. The practical rule is that an offender’s voluntary intoxication should not ordinarily reduce culpability in mitigation, although limited exceptions exist (for example, where intoxication made the offender unaware that intoxication would produce aberrant criminal behaviour — see Vergados and Morrison). Sentencers must apply any statutory prohibition in s 11C to prevent self‑induced intoxication from operating as a mitigating factor, while still permitting the court to consider intoxication where it bears on remorse, rehabilitation, or lack of premeditation if legally permissible.

# Authority — Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 (intoxication in sentencing)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58] [TOPIC: intoxication and sentencing mitigation] [CONCEPTS: intoxication, mitigation, remorse, lack of premeditation, Vergados, Morrison, R v Groom, sentencing discretion]

Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 discusses intoxication in mitigation, noting that intoxication will rarely be a valid mitigating factor but may reduce culpability if the offender did not foresee intoxication would cause criminally aberrant behaviour. Brown cites Vergados v The Queen [2011] VSCA 438 and Morrison v The Queen [2012] VSCA 222 for that limited principle, and observes that intoxication can be aggravating if it increases fright to the victim (R v Groom [1998] VSCA 146).

# Authority — Vergados v The Queen [2011] VSCA 438
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Vergados v The Queen [2011] VSCA 438] [TOPIC: intoxication and culpability] [CONCEPTS: intoxication, foresight of effect, reduction in culpability, mitigation exception, VSCA authority]

Vergados v The Queen [2011] VSCA 438 is authority for the limited proposition that intoxication may reduce culpability where the offender was not aware that intoxication would make them behave in a criminally aberrant manner; it is cited as a narrow exception to the general rule that voluntary intoxication is not mitigating.

# Authority — Morrison v The Queen [2012] VSCA 222
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Morrison v The Queen [2012] VSCA 222] [TOPIC: intoxication cases] [CONCEPTS: voluntary intoxication, mitigation, appellate guidance, reduced culpability, VSCA]

Morrison v The Queen [2012] VSCA 222 provides appellate commentary on when intoxication might play a mitigating role, reinforcing the limited circumstances in which lack of foresight of intoxicating effects can reduce moral culpability.

# Authority — R v Groom [1998] VSCA 146
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Groom [1998] VSCA 146] [TOPIC: intoxication as aggravating where victim terrified] [CONCEPTS: intoxication aggravating, victim fright, sentencing, VSCA, violent offence]

R v Groom [1998] VSCA 146 is authority for treating intoxication as an aggravating feature in violent offences where the offender’s intoxication made the offending more frightening for the victim.

# Sentencing submissions and evidentiary rules (s 81 Sentencing Act; s 4(2) Evidence Act)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act] [SECTION: s 81] [TYPE: evidentiary rule] [TOPIC: admissibility of material in sentencing submissions] [CONCEPTS: sentencing submissions, admissibility, s 81, Evidence Act s 4(2), informal evidence, prosecution submissions, defence submissions]

Sentencing submissions may be made by both the defence and the prosecution; the formal rules of evidence do not strictly apply to sentencing hearings. The source notes that s 81 of the Sentencing Act (as cited) and s 4(2) of the Evidence Act allow the court to receive material in sentencing that would not be admissible at trial. Sentencers retain discretion to accept or reject factual assertions made in mitigation or aggravation, but where the court intends to reject a fact that, if accepted, would materially affect sentence, it should alert the offender and give an opportunity to address the issue (see Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43).

# Authority — Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43 (court not bound to accept asserted facts)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43] [TOPIC: fact‑finding in sentencing; procedural fairness] [CONCEPTS: rejection of asserted facts, procedural fairness, notification, Nash v Haas, onus in mitigation, R v Olbrich]

Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43 confirms that a sentencing court is not bound to accept facts asserted by prosecution or defence. However, if the court intends to reject a mitigating fact that would materially affect the sentence, it should provide the offender an opportunity to address that factual dispute. The decision references Nash v Haas and R v Olbrich [1999] HCA 54 on the burden to prove mitigating facts on the balance of probabilities.

# Authority — Nash v Haas [1972] TASStRp 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Nash v Haas [1972] TASStRp 1] [TOPIC: sentencing procedural fairness] [CONCEPTS: procedural fairness, sentencing, notification, opportunity to respond, mitigation facts]

Nash v Haas [1972] establishes the procedural fairness principle that sentencing courts should give an offender a chance to address factual matters put in mitigation which the court proposes to reject.

# Authority — R v Olbrich [1999] HCA 54
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Olbrich [1999] HCA 54] [TOPIC: onus to prove mitigating fact] [CONCEPTS: onus, balance of probabilities, mitigation, HCA authority, sentencing fact‑finding]

R v Olbrich [1999] HCA 54 holds that where a fact is offered in mitigation and would materially affect sentence, the onus is on the offender to establish that fact on the balance of probabilities.

# Plea timing and utilitarian benefit (late plea of guilty)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: sentencing principle] [TOPIC: late plea of guilty and mitigation] [CONCEPTS: guilty plea, utilitarian benefit, early plea discount, late plea, plea entered after trial commencement, victim stress, reduced discount]

The rule is that an early guilty plea attracts a reduced sentence because it saves court resources and spares victims and witnesses the stress of trial. A plea entered only after trial has commenced, or after witnesses have been required to give evidence, is often deprived of much of its utilitarian benefit and so attracts little or no discount. Sentencers examine whether the plea genuinely avoided burden on the courts and other parties; where all benefits have already been expended, the mitigation is minimal.

# Repeat offending, offending on bail, and persistence as aggravating features
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: repeat offending and offending on bail] [CONCEPTS: repeat offending, persistence, aggravation, offending on bail, contempt for law, deterrence, R v Devine, Veen v R]

Continued offending — including committing offences while on bail or after being charged — is frequently treated as an aggravating factor because it demonstrates persistence, contempt for legal authority and increased risk to the community. Authorities such as R v Devine (No 2) and the High Court in Veen v R (No 2) [1988] HCA 14 support that repeated conduct while at liberty or on bail can justify a more severe penalty to achieve retribution, deterrence and protection.

# Authority — R v Devine (No 2) (cited)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Devine (No 2) (56/1998) Slicer J] [TOPIC: offending on bail as aggravation] [CONCEPTS: offending on bail, aggravation, persistence, contempt for law, sentencing literature]

R v Devine (No 2) is cited for the principle that committing offences while on bail is aggravating because it demonstrates persistence of criminal conduct and contempt for legal constraints, thereby justifying harsher punishment.

# Childhood deprivation and Bugmy principles in mitigation — Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [CASE: Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1] [TOPIC: childhood deprivation as mitigation] [CONCEPTS: childhood deprivation, Bugmy principles, rehabilitation weighting, moral culpability, Bergman, Engert, mitigation degree]

Childhood deprivation may reduce moral culpability and increase the weight given to rehabilitation at sentencing, but the extent of moderation is a matter of degree and requires evidence before the sentencing judge. Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1 observes that lifelong damage from childhood exposure to violence, abuse or neglect can elevate rehabilitation as a sentencing purpose (citing Bergman v The Queen [2021] VSCA 148 at para [100]), but stresses that if material showing childhood deprivation is not placed before the sentencing judge, Bugmy principles will not be enlivened on appeal. Sentencers must be provided with evidence establishing the nature and effect of deprivation to give it substantive weight.

# Authority — Bergman (pseudonym) v The Queen [2021] VSCA 148
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Bergman (a pseudonym) v The Queen [2021] VSCA 148] [TOPIC: childhood deprivation weight in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: Bugmy, childhood deprivation, rehabilitation, sentencing weight, VSCA]

Bergman v The Queen [2021] VSCA 148 is authority that substantial childhood deprivation may justify greater weight to rehabilitation when sentencing, but the extent depends on the evidence and interplay with deterrence and denunciation.

# Conviction recording discretion and orders short of conviction — John Parker v Adrian John Hall (BC201512153) (unreported)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: discretion not to record conviction; orders under s 7(f)] [CONCEPTS: no conviction recorded, s 7(f), purposes of non-conviction orders, public interest, rehabilitation, Attorney‑General v Smith, R v Brown, recording conviction discretion]

The court’s discretion whether to record a conviction requires consideration of the statutory purposes of orders short of conviction (for example, s 7(f) of the sentencing framework as cited in the source). Factors to weigh include rehabilitation, triviality of the offence, appropriateness of not recording a conviction, and broader public interest (including impact on employers, immigration and licensing). John Parker v Adrian John Hall (BC201512153) (unreported) analyses statutory provisions and authorities (Attorney‑General v Smith [2002] TASSC 10; R v Brown ex parte A‑G [1994] 2 Qd R 182) and underscores the balancing of the offender’s interests (economic or social consequences) against the need for an official record, and whether victims might feel vindicated by a recorded conviction.

# Authority — Attorney‑General v Smith [2002] TASSC 10
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Attorney‑General v Smith [2002] TASSC 10] [TOPIC: public interest and non‑recording of conviction] [CONCEPTS: public interest, recording conviction, consequences of non-recording, sentencing discretion, TASSC]

Attorney‑General v Smith [2002] TASSC 10 discusses the public interest in recording convictions and the consequences of failing to record a conviction (effect on employment, insurance, immigration, firearm licensing), informing the balancing exercise when deciding whether to record a conviction.

# Authorities on non‑recording consequences — R v Briese; R v Brown ex parte A‑G [1994] 2 Qd R 182
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Briese; R v Brown ex parte A‑G [1994] 2 Qd R 182] [TOPIC: consequences and discretion on recording convictions] [CONCEPTS: non-recording consequences, public interest, sentencing discretion, Qld authorities, precedent]

R v Briese and R v Brown ex parte A‑G [1994] 2 Qd R 182 provide Queensland appellate authority on the effect of not recording convictions and the appropriate exercise of discretion, including public interest considerations and the potential deterrent effect of recorded convictions.

# Principle of totality — Postiglione v R (1997) 189 CLR 295
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Postiglione v R (1997) 189 CLR 295] [TOPIC: totality principle in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: totality, aggregate sentences, combined effect, just and appropriate punishment, High Court authority]

Postiglione v R (1997) 189 CLR 295 establishes the totality principle: when multiple sentences are imposed, the court must ensure the aggregate sentence is just and appropriate, considering prior sentences imposed for related conduct and the overall criminality.

# Sentencer treatment of professional status and antecedents — John Parker discussion
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: offender professional status and antecedents] [CONCEPTS: antecedents, professional status, culpability, sentencing equality, knowledge of orders, aggravation]

Professional status (such as being a legal practitioner) is not automatically an aggravating factor to justify heavier sentencing; however, specific knowledge and understanding of legal obligations borne from professional status can be relevant to culpability. John Parker v Adrian John Hall discusses that professional knowledge may bear on whether the offender appreciated the imperative to comply with orders, but mere professional status alone should not attract harsher punishment than for others.

# Use of prevalence statistics in sentencing — cautionary principle
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: statistical evidence and sentencing] [CONCEPTS: crime prevalence, statistical data, caution, police figures, Dowie, Braslin, evaluation of gravity]

Statistical evidence about crime prevalence may assist sentencers but must not overshadow the court’s own evaluation of the gravity of the particular offence. Wright J (in Dowie) and the Tasmanian Court of Criminal Appeal in Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1 emphasize circumspection when using police statistics due to subjective judgment and potential to displace case‑specific assessment.

# Sentencing youth offenders — Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1] [TOPIC: youthful offender mitigation limits] [CONCEPTS: youthful offender, age, planning, adult behaviour, deterrence weight, mitigation limits]

Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1 records that while youth can be mitigating, offences demonstrating adult‑like premeditation and planning may justify treating the offender as having adult culpability; deterrence and denunciation may still be given considerable weight despite youth.

# Self‑induced voluntary intoxication — statutory and doctrinal rule
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act] [SECTION: s 11C] [TYPE: statutory definition] [TOPIC: self-induced intoxication not mitigating] [CONCEPTS: voluntary intoxication, s 11C, mitigation barred, sentencing, self-induced intoxication]

Self‑induced voluntary intoxication is not a valid mitigating factor under the Sentencing Act s 11C as cited in the source. Sentencers should exclude voluntary intoxication from mitigation but may still consider its indirect effects (e.g. whether it bears on remorse or likelihood of rehabilitation) only to the extent permitted by statute and authority.

# Offending on bail — aggravation and authority (Veen; commentary)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: offending on bail as aggravating] [CONCEPTS: offending on bail, aggravation, contempt for law, deterrence, R v Devine, Fox & Freiberg commentary, Veen v R]

Committing further offences while on bail is commonly aggravating because it demonstrates a continuing disregard for the law and reduces prospects of leniency. Academic commentary (Fox & Freiberg) and case law (R v Devine; Veen v R (No 2) [1988] HCA 14) support treating repeat conduct on bail as increasing gravity and warranting stronger deterrent and protective sentencing objectives.

# Authority — Veen v R (No 2) [1988] HCA 14
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Veen v R (No 2) [1988] HCA 14] [TOPIC: sentencing principles (general)] [CONCEPTS: sentencing principles, deterrence, general principles, HCA authority, aggravation, culpability]

Veen v R (No 2) [1988] HCA 14 is a leading High Court authority on sentencing principles, including consideration of retribution, deterrence and protection, and is cited for the proposition that repeated offending and contempt for law can justify harsher penalties.

# Authority — Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1] [TOPIC: childhood deprivation in mitigation] [CONCEPTS: childhood deprivation, sentencing mitigation, Bugmy principles, evidence requirement, rehabilitation weight]

Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1 reiterates that childhood deprivation can justify increased weight to rehabilitation, but such a claim requires evidence before the sentencing judge; absent such material, the appellate court will not treat Bugmy principles as engaged.

# Authority — Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1] [TOPIC: youth and sentencing] [CONCEPTS: youthful offender, sentencing, planning, deterrence, Tas CCA]

Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1 is authority for limiting the mitigatory weight of youth where offences demonstrate premeditation and adult‑like planning, thereby allowing deterrence and denunciation to retain prominence.

# Authority — Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 (reproduced as authority chunk)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58] [TOPIC: intoxication, sentencing] [CONCEPTS: intoxication mitigation, victim fright, remorse, sentencing discretion, Tas S C]

Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 is cited for assessing intoxication’s limited role in mitigation and the possibility of intoxication being aggravating if it increases the victim’s fear.

# Authority — Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43 (reproduced)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43] [TOPIC: rejecting asserted facts in mitigation] [CONCEPTS: sentencing evidence, onus, balance of probabilities, procedural fairness, Nash v Haas]

Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43 is authority on the sentencing court’s power to reject asserted facts in mitigation, subject to procedural fairness obligations where the fact is material to sentence.

# Authority — R v Olbrich [1999] HCA 54 (reproduced)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: R v Olbrich [1999] HCA 54] [TOPIC: onus for mitigation facts] [CONCEPTS: onus, balance of probabilities, mitigating evidence, HCA]

R v Olbrich [1999] HCA 54 confirms the offender bears the onus of proving mitigating factual propositions on the balance of probabilities where such facts would materially affect sentence.

# Authority — Postiglione v R (1997) 189 CLR 295 (reproduced)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [CASE: Postiglione v R (1997) 189 CLR 295] [TOPIC: totality principle] [CONCEPTS: totality, aggregate sentence, combined effect, High Court, sentencing]

Postiglione v R (1997) 189 CLR 295 establishes how sentencers must ensure aggregate sentences reflect total criminality and be just and appropriate, considering prior related sentencing.

# Authority — Additional cited authorities (list)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: assorted sentencing authorities cited] [CONCEPTS: R v Briese, R v Brown ex parte A‑G, Attorney‑General v Smith, Walden v Hensler, Badcock v White, Maingay v Seabourne, Collins v Caccavo, Woods v Wood, R v McInerney, Devine, Veen, Braslin]

The source cites a range of authorities for particular sentencing principles mentioned above: R v Briese (ex parte A‑G), R v Brown ex parte A‑G [1994] 2 Qd R 182, Attorney‑General v Smith [2002] TASSC 10, Walden v Hensler (1987) 163 CLR 561, Badcock v White [2004] TASSC 59, Maingay v Seabourne [2009] TASSC 67, Collins v Caccavo [2015] TASSC 53, Woods v Wood [1991] TASSC 24, R v McInerney (1986) 42 SASR 111, R v Devine (No 2), Veen v R (No 2) [1988] HCA 14 and Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1. Each authority supports discrete sentencing points above (non‑recording discretion, nature of offence, antecedents, totality, repeat offending, youth, statistical caution).

### Chunk 1 — Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6 - Aggravating factors and absence of mitigating factors
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Family Violence Act 2004 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 13(a)] [CITATION: Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6 - Aggravating and Mitigating Factors] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: aggravating factors and absence of mitigation in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: aggravating factors, mitigation absent, strangulation, ligature, family violence]

Tactical content: this chunk covers the respondent's written submissions on aggravating factors and the absence of mitigating factors as recorded in Palmer v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 6.

43. In the respondent's written submissions, aggravating factors and the absence of mitigating factors are detailed. Those submissions are as follows:

"Aggravating factors

It is asserted the following were aggravating factors:

(a) The charges were matters of family violence which took place in the complainant's home.

(b) Both counts involved proved allegations of strangulation or choking.

(i) In respect of count 1, the complainant felt pain and was unable to breathe nor speak. She was also rendered less able to defend herself from the subsequent head-butt that took place while she was lying down on the bed.

(ii) The conduct which was the subject of count 2 was especially serious involving, as it did, the use of a ligature. The complainant felt as though she could not breathe and as though was going to lose consciousness on multiple occasions.

(c) The conduct was deliberately calculated to cause the complainant fear and to cause her to believe she would die. During the acts the subject of count two, the Appellant told the complainant that her daughter would not see her again.

(d) The acts of violence the subject of the charges occurred against a background of ongoing abuse and threatening behaviour. This had begun in September 2019 with the accused verbally abusing the complainant and confronting her about his belief that the complainant was bringing other men to their home. The Appellant also sent a string of abusive text messages to the complainant shortly before the offending took place. After count 1 occurred the Appellant followed the complainant out of the bedroom where count 2 then took place. It could not be said that the Appellant's conduct was isolated.

(e) The complainant suffered significant physical injuries.

(f) The complainant also suffered significant psychological and emotional impacts as a result of the offending. It is significant that those effects were still prevalent almost four years after the commission of the crimes.

(g) The offending (particularly that which was the subject of count 1) occurred in the presence of or near to the complainant's infant child and the Appellant knew that to be the case: Family Violence Act 2004, s 13(a).

Absence of mitigating factors

It is asserted that the following mitigating factors were absent:

(a) The Appellant was not a youthful offender.

(b) There was no evidence of remorse, nor any submissions made on his behalf to that effect. The learned sentencing judge noted that in the course of the Appellant's video recorded interview the Appellant felt justified in committing the acts of violence that he admitted to. Further, those admissions did not reflect the full extent of his criminal conduct.

(c) Although he entered a plea of guilty to the charges, that was only after a trial had already begun and, because of the nature and extent of the matters later brought into dispute, the complainant was required to give evidence and be cross-examined, as were a number of other witnesses. In those circumstances the plea was deprived of its utilitarian benefit.

(d) The Appellant had substantial prior convictions, including for family violence. In January 2019 he was sentenced by way of conviction and fine for five charges of breaching a police family violence order in respect of a different partner. He also had prior convictions in Queensland and Western Australia for violent offending. It could not be said that the offending was an isolated aberration. Specific deterrence was clearly a relevant sentencing consideration.

(e) Although the Appellant had what was described by his counsel as a "history of mental health concerns" there was no suggestion made to the learned sentencing judge, nor was there any evidence put before the Court, that any of those concerns contributed to the Appellant's offending or to the level of moral culpability to be ascribed to him in respect of it."

Dahmes v The King [2025] VSCA 213 (05 September 2025) (Taylor and Kidd JJA)

### Chunk 3 — Impact of intoxication in mitigation and Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 11C] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 11C - Intoxication Irrelevant in Pleas in Mitigation; Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58] [CASE: Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 (22 November 2021)] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine / case authority] [TOPIC: voluntarty intoxication and mitigation] [CONCEPTS: intoxication, mitigation, Sentencing Act s11C, Brown v Jones, Vergados, Morrison]

Tactical content: this chunk covers the statutory rule that voluntary intoxication is irrelevant in pleas in mitigation and the Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 discussion.

SENTENCING ACT now says that vol intoxicating is irrelevant in Pleas in Mitigation per 11C

Brown v Jones [2021] TASSC 58 (22 November 2021)

[27] Counsel acknowledged that the applicant was intoxicated, but submitted it shows a lack of pre-meditation and that in light of his history, the offence was "out of character". It seems that by the last point, counsel was not suggesting intoxication as a mitigatory factor. It will rarely be so; it may reduce culpability if the offender was not aware that intoxication would cause him or her to behave in a criminally aberrant way: Vergados v The Queen [2011] VSCA 438 at [47], Morrison v The Queen [2012] VSCA 222 at [20]. As an explanation, it is relevant to remorse, to rehabilitation and thus to specific deterrence. I should add -- although there was no suggestion of it in this case -- that intoxication can also be an aggravating factor in cases of violence to the person where it makes the offence more frightening for the victim: R v Groom [1998] VSCA 146, [1999] 2 VR 159 at [24].

Sentencing submissions:

Can be made by Defence and Pross.

Rules of Evidence do NOT need to apply per s 81 of the Sentencing Act and s. 4(2) of the Evidence Act.

Repeat offending:

### Chunk 6 — Plea in mitigation: Childhood deprivation and Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1 - Childhood Deprivation in Mitigation] [CASE: Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1 (1 February 2024)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: childhood deprivation as mitigating consideration and Bugmy principles] [CONCEPTS: childhood deprivation, Bugmy, rehabilitation weight, personal deterrence, Banfield]

Tactical content: this chunk covers how childhood deprivation may affect sentencing and the limits where material was not placed before sentencing judge.

Plea in mitigation:

Sentencing submissions for mitigation:

Childhood deprivation:

Childhood deprivation is basically the claim that the background of the appellant was so dysfunctional or so deprived as to lead to a conclusion that her moral culpability was substantially reduced thereby impacting the role sentencing aims such as general deterrence and denunciation should play in the exercise of the sentencing discretion. 

Banfield v Tasmania [2024] TASCCA 1 (1 February 2024)

Lifelong damage that is the result of childhood exposure to violence, abuse, or neglect may elevate the weight to be given to rehabilitation as a sentencing consideration. See Bergman (a pseudonym) v The Queen [2021] VSCA 148 at [100]. If an individual's background of childhood deprivation allows the weight ordinarily given to personal and general deterrence to be moderated in favour of other purposes of punishment, such as rehabilitation, there remains a question of degree; that is, to what extent such moderation should occur. As noted by Gleeson CJ in Engert, the interplay of considerations relevant to sentencing may be complex and the facts may point in different directions.

While the Bugmy principles were correctly referred to in submissions on appeal, material showing childhood deprivation had not been placed before the learned sentencing judge. As a result, this was not a case where the principles referred to were enlivened.

---

### Chunk 7 — Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43 - Not required to accept as "fact" sentencing submissions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43 - Onus to Prove Mitigation Facts] [CASE: Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43 (24 June 2022)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: rejection of facts in mitigation and opportunity to address] [CONCEPTS: mitigation facts, onus, Nash v Haas, R v Olbrich, acceptance of facts]

Tactical content: this chunk covers the principle that sentencing courts are not bound to accept mitigation facts and the need to give the offender opportunity to address if the court intends to reject material facts.

Not required to accept as "fact" sentencing submissions

Burns v Robinson [2022] TASSC 43 (24 June 2022)

[12] It is well-established that a sentencing magistrate is not bound to accept facts asserted by either the prosecution or the defence. However, it is also well established that if the court intends to reject a fact put in mitigation, which if accepted would be material to the assessment of sentence, then the court should alert the offender to the intended use and provide an opportunity to address the factual issue either by submission or evidence. See Nash v Haas [1972] TASStRp 1; [1972] Tas SR 1; Parker v Tasmania [2019] TASCCA 16.

Because it was offered in mitigation, the onus was on the applicant to establish the fact on the balance of probabilities: R v Olbrich [1999] HCA 54, 199 CLR 270.

### Chunk 8 — JOHN PARKER v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 (Unreported) — Statutory framework for orders without conviction (s 58, s 9)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 58; s 9; s 7(f)] [CITATION: JOHN PARKER v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 - Statutory Framework for Non-Recording of Conviction] [CASE: JOHN PARKER (Senior Sergeant) v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 (Unreported)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: statutory purposes for orders short of conviction and principles to consider] [CONCEPTS: s58, s9, non-recording, 7(f), purposes of orders, public interest]

Tactical content: this chunk covers the statutory provisions and judicial exposition governing orders that fall short of recording a conviction, as set out in JOHN PARKER v ADRIAN JOHN HALL (unreported).

JOHN PARKER (Senior Sergeant) v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 Unreported Judgments Tas

[25] I turn now to relevant sentencing considerations which apply to the exercise of the court's discretion whether or not to record a conviction. There are statutory provisions which govern the exercise of the court's discretion. The court must have regard to the purposes of orders falling short of a conviction specified in s 58. This section provides:

1.  Purpose of orders to adjourn, discharge or dismiss

An order under section 7(f), (g) or (h) may be made for such one or more of the following purposes, as is relevant in the circumstances, as the court thinks fit:

to provide for the rehabilitation of an offender by allowing the offender's sentence to be served in the community unsupervised;

to take account of the trivial, technical or minor nature of the offence committed by an offender;

to allow for circumstances in which it may be inappropriate to record a conviction against an offender;

to allow for circumstances in which it may be inappropriate to inflict any punishment other than a nominal punishment on an offender;

to allow for the existence of other extenuating or exceptional circumstances that may justify the court showing mercy to an offender.

---

### Chunk 9 — JOHN PARKER v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 (Unreported) — Application and judicial commentary on factors (paras [26]-[40])
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 7(f); s 9] [CITATION: JOHN PARKER v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 - Application of s9 and non-recording factors] [CASE: JOHN PARKER (Senior Sergeant) v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 (Unreported)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: application of s9 factors and weighing public interest vs mercy] [CONCEPTS: non-recording conviction, s9(a), s9(b), nature of offence, antecedents, public interest, totality]

Tactical content: this chunk contains the court's reasoning on weighing public interest and offender mitigation when considering whether to record a conviction, including references to precedent and the totality principle.

[26] Of course, there are sentencing purposes achieved by recording a conviction which must also be considered. In Attorney-General v Smith [2002] TASSC 10, Crawford J considered the public interest and the need that may exist for an official record to be made of the commission of the offence. He referred to the consequences of failing to record a conviction:

1.  ... The offender would be entitled to assert, even on oath or statutory declaration, that he or she had no previous conviction, or to expressly deny having been convicted of the relevant offence, and could not later be charged with perjury. As was pointed out in R v Briese, ex parte A-G [1998] 1 Qd R 487 at 491, a failure to record a conviction is capable of considerable effect in the community. Persons who might have an interest in knowing the truth in such matters include potential employers, insurers and various government departments including the Immigration Department. Under the Firearms Act 1996, the offender would not be absolutely disqualified by s 29(3)(a) from obtaining a licence because he would not come within the category of a person who "within the period of 5 years before the application was made, has been convicted in Tasmania or elsewhere of any crime involving violence to another person".

1.  In R v Brown, ex parte A-G [1994] 2 Qd R 182 at 194, it was noted that the recording of a conviction is in itself an element of punishment of the offender and may encourage him or her not to engage in further criminal activity and conceivably it could act as some deterrence to others if it becomes known.

1.  When considering whether to record a conviction, a court must weigh up the public interest, and the need for an official record to be made of the commission of the offence, against the beneficial nature to the offender of a conviction not being recorded. Regard must be had to the purposes specified in s 58. If the offence is of a relatively serious nature, the Court may feel compelled to record a conviction. In addition to public interest questions, it is proper that the Court have regard to whether the victim of such an offence might reasonably not feel vindicated by the failure to record a conviction.

[27] Section 9 applies to the exercise of the court's discretion whether or not to record a conviction:

1.  Conviction or non-conviction

In exercising its discretion whether or not to record a conviction, a court must have regard to all the circumstances of the case including--

the nature and circumstances of the offence; and

the offender's antecedents and character; and

the impact that a conviction would have on the offender's economic or social wellbeing or employment prospects.

[28] Under s 9(a), the court is required to have regard to all the circumstances of the case, including those specified. The matters specified are not exhaustive of all relevant circumstances. Those matters specified must not be overlooked, and the relative weight of each depends on the circumstances of each particular case: R v Brown; Ex parte A-G (Qld) [1994] 2 Qd R 182, at 185 and 193.

[29] The "nature and circumstances of the offence" in s 9(a) require the court to have regard to the actual conduct constituting the offence charged and the actual circumstances in which it was committed: Walden v Hensler (1987) 163 CLR 561 at 577 and 595. It allows the court to have regard to those circumstances which mean the offence is inherently less serious than other examples of offending in contravention of the section. In Badcock v White [2004] TASSC 59 Crawford J stated at [17]:

1.  The fact that imprisonment is a sentencing option or that the offence could be committed in grossly serious circumstances in some cases, is not determinative. By way of illustration, I note that in the criminal courts of this State since the commencement of 2003, five offenders have been dealt with under s 7(f) without the recording of convictions for crimes for which imprisonment for 21 years was prescribed. Their respective crimes were four aggravated burglaries and seven stealings; aggravated sexual assault; forgery, uttering and stealing; burglary and stealing; and indecency. In Queensland, in R v Brown, ex parte A-G [1994] 2 Qd R 182 the Court of Appeal refused to intervene in a case of dangerous driving causing two deaths, where a conviction had not been recorded pursuant to the application of a statutory discretion requiring consideration of circumstances similar to those in s 9.

[30] The application of s 7(f) and other like orders is not reserved for offences at the more trivial end of the sentencing scale: R v Brown, per Macrossan CJ at 185. Even inherently serious offences may attract a sentencing order which does not involve recording a conviction: Collins v Caccavo [2015] TASSC 53, per Estcourt J at [21].

[31] The "nature of the offence" allows consideration of general matters, such as the purpose of the legislation, the social harm caused by family violence addressed by the Family Violence Act, and the need for general deterrence in order to protect others from family violence offences. The protective purpose of the Act is evident from its terms, such as the long title and the objects provision in s 3. In matters of sentencing, it is clear that the court's role is protective, and deterrence, both specific and general, are important considerations: Tennent J in Maingay v Seabourne [2009] TASSC 67 at [23].

[32]-[40] (see next chunk for application to facts and totality).

---

### Chunk 10 — JOHN PARKER v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 (Unreported) — Application to facts: weighing extenuating matters, antecedents, character, totality (paras [32]-[40])
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 9] [CITATION: JOHN PARKER v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 - Application to Facts and Totality] [CASE: JOHN PARKER (Senior Sergeant) v ADRIAN JOHN HALL - BC201512153 (Unreported)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: application of factors to decide recording of conviction; totality; professional status not aggravating] [CONCEPTS: extenuating circumstances, antecedents, totality, professional status, victim vindication]

Tactical content: this chunk completes the unreported judgment's application to the facts and the court's reasoning on mitigating and aggravating features, antecedents, professional status, and totality.

[32] In this case, there were circumstances which can be said to be extenuating and which weigh in favour of not recording a conviction, and some which weigh against. The respondent's conduct did not involve violence, threats of violence, aggression or antagonistic behaviour. The offences were confined to breaches of conditions precluding contacting and approaching the complainant. They were committed with the compliance and agreement of the complainant. Of course, that is no justification for the breaches. However, it is a point of distinction with other offenders where there is no complicity and the offender knows that the contact made with the complainant is contrary to the wishes of the complainant. Weighing against leniency and the order made, was that the respondent consciously placed himself in proximity to the complainant when he was aware of the order and its terms. Nonetheless, the respondent's offences cannot be characterised as serious examples.

[33] Another matter is the repeated nature of his offending. To repeat the offence, as he did, increases the overall culpability of his offending. The commission of six breaches is a matter which points towards the need for a sanction carrying some deterrent effect.

[34] The Chief Magistrate characterised the respondent as being, quite deliberately, in breach of the order. As a characterisation, it accords with his findings in general, but it may be worth noting that there is an exception with respect to the second offence. The second offence referred to above, involving the respondent's conduct in leaving a letter so that the complainant could read it if she chose to, was evidently committed in the belief that the conduct did not breach the police family violence order. Ultimately, the court rejected the argument, but the respondent's belief bears on an assessment of his culpability with respect to that offence. In addressing whether the respondent's conduct with respect to the third and fifth offences amounted to approaching the complainant, his Honour found that the respondent consciously placed himself in proximity to the complainant while he was aware of the order that was in place at the particular time.

[35] The second matter the sentencing court must take into account, pursuant to s 9(b) of the Sentencing Act, is "the offender's antecedents and character". In terms of antecedents, at the time of sentencing, the respondent was not someone who had committed and been dealt with by the court for a similar or relevant offence. He was a first offender. Nonetheless, the two offences committed in the same time period were matters that the court could properly take into account. In Woods v Wood [1991] TASSC 24, Green CJ considered the approach to be taken in sentencing for an offence that occurred prior to a subsequent sentencing order for subsequent conduct:

[36] In light of that guidance, the following is noted. In this case, the six offences under review were not the only breaches that the respondent had committed, and in the same time frame there were two others committed of a similar kind. The plea in mitigation before the Chief Magistrate drew attention to the effect of the sentencing order on 6 May 2013 and that, since then, there had been no further contact between the respondent and the complainant. Since he was sentenced on 6 May 2013 for the two other offences, there have been no further offences committed by the respondent, and the sentence imposed by the court pursuant to s 7(f), requiring his good behaviour, had been complied with and proved to be an effective deterrent.

[37] Relevant to the matter of the respondent's antecedents and character is whether, as contended by the applicant, the fact that the respondent was a legal practitioner added to the seriousness of the matter. The Chief Magistrate declined to take into account that in a "legal sense" the respondent, as a legal practitioner "should have known better". It is noteworthy that it is not asserted on review that the Chief Magistrate erred in this regard. In my view, the respondent was not to be sentenced more heavily than others merely by virtue of the fact that he was a legal practitioner. His professional status was not an aggravating factor. However, a factor bearing on his culpability is that he was equipped with certain knowledge. It is his knowledge which was significant and that he understood the imperative of complying with the orders. Undoubtedly, he knew that the complainant's acquiescence did not excuse his breaching the orders. He would have been well aware, as the Chief Magistrate observed, that he could apply to the court to vary or revoke the orders.

[38] Finally, under s 9, the sentencing court was required to take into account the impact that a conviction would have on the offender's economic or social well-being or employment prospects. A submission was not made to the Chief Magistrate as to the impact of a conviction in this regard. Equally, this is not a case where it was pointed out by the prosecution that there were public interest considerations weighing in favour of convictions being recorded. It may be noted that if his offending fell to be considered in the context of any disciplinary proceedings under the Legal Profession Act 2007, a possibility alluded to during submissions, a conviction under that Act is defined broadly to encompass a finding of guilt, "whether or not a conviction is recorded": s 11. Mr Richardson submitted that as a matter of reality prior convictions provided to the court for sentencing purposes always include orders made without conviction. It seems that there are no pressing public interest concerns in having an official record made of his offending.

[39] In terms of the circumstances of the case generally, there were other matters to be considered. A mitigating factor is that the matter had attracted significant publicity and that the respondent had, to a degree, already felt the consequences of his offending behaviour.

[40] Another matter is the principle of totality, which meant that the court was to have regard to the earlier sentence imposed on 6 May 2013, and consider the combined effect of that sentence with the sentence to be imposed. The court should ensure that the aggregate of the two sentences is a just and appropriate measure of the total criminality involved: Postiglione v R (1997) 189 CLR 295, per Dawson and Gaudron JJ at 304, per McHugh J at 307-308. It was submitted by the respondent, before the Chief Magistrate, that if he had been dealt with for all eight offences at the same time, he may have received no greater penalty than that which he received on 6 May 2013. This submission alludes to the totality principle. An enquiry as to the adequacy of the recent sentence should take into account the earlier sentence imposed for offending that occurred in the same time frame. It is noteworthy that he had been subject to an order for 12 months, and after that order had expired, he was subject to a further order in the same terms for a period of another 12 months. Further, as noted, the second order had been complied with and there had been no further offending.

---

### Chunk 11 — Plea in mitigation: Late plea of guilty (practitioner note)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Plea in mitigation - Late guilty plea note] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: late plea strategy and effect on plea discount] [CONCEPTS: late plea, plea discount, early plea benefit, amendment, defence strategy]

Tactical content: this chunk preserves the practitioner's note on late pleas, last-minute amendments, and competing arguments about plea discount.

Late plea of guilty 

Last min amendment to one of a series -- usually used by defence as a way of arguing, in light of the amendment this should be considered early plea. Counter -- there was more than one charge, no indication of disputed particulars, or acceptance of related charges. Benefit of erarly plea reflects the reduced burden on the courts, the state and the witnesses. All have been expended.

---

### Chunk 12 — Offending on bail: aggravation and authorities
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Offending on bail - aggravating factor and authorities] [CASE: Maingay v Seabourne [2009] TASSC 67; R v Devine; Veen v R (No 2) (1988) 164 CLR 465] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation / case authority] [TOPIC: offending while on bail as aggravating factor] [CONCEPTS: offending on bail, aggravation, contempt for law, persistence, deterrence, Maingay]

Tactical content: this chunk preserves the practitioner's compilation of authority and commentary that offending while on bail is often regarded as aggravating.

Offending on bail

"A continuation of criminal conduct after having been charged and bailed is frequently regarded by courts as a matter of aggravation. It shows utter contempt for the law and authority. In R v Devine (No 2) 56/1998, Slicer J at 2 referred to it as being aggravating because it shows persistency of conduct. Professors Fox and Freiberg in Sentencing -- State and Federal Law in Victoria, 2nd ed, observed at 289, 'the fact that the offender has repeated the offence previously charged while entrusted to remain in the community adds markedly to the gravity of the offence'. ... If an offender manifests a continuing attitude of disobedience of the law, 'retribution, deterrence and protection of society may all indicate that a more severe penalty is warranted'. Veen v R (No 2) [1988] HCA 14; (1988) 164 CLR 465 at 477." [cited by the TASSC in Maingay v Seabourne 2009]

16 As to the offences of dishonesty, again particularly in 2008, the respondent continued to offend on a regular basis, more than once while he was on bail for earlier matters. The overwhelming inference to be drawn from the respondent's behaviour was that he treated police family violence orders and bail orders with contempt. This is further supported by the respondent's attitude to appearing for sentence. The respondent pleaded guilty to a number of offences which involved his failure to appear before the court in respect of both the dishonesty and family violence matters. Notwithstanding that, when he was bailed to appear before the learned magistrate for sentencing in December 2008, he failed to appear. As a consequence, sentencing was delayed for approximately three months.

### Chunk 13 — Self-induced voluntary intoxication: statutory bar per s 11C
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 11C] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 11C - Self-induced Intoxication Not Mitigating] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: self-induced voluntary intoxication excluded from mitigation] [CONCEPTS: voluntary intoxication, s11C, mitigation excluded, sentencing, statutory bar]

Tactical content: this chunk records the practitioner's succinct note on voluntary intoxication and sentencing.

Self-induced vol intoxication 

Self-induced intoxication not to be mitigating factor in sentencing per s 11C Sentencing Act

---

### Chunk 14 — Social prevalence of crimes and caution on statistics (Dowie / Braslin & Cowen)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Social prevalence of crimes - Use of statistics and caution; Braslin & Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1] [CASE: Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: use of crime prevalence statistics in sentencing] [CONCEPTS: prevalence statistics, deterrence, caution, Dowie, Braslin, sentencing evaluation]

Tactical content: this chunk preserves guidance on the cautious use of police statistics in submissions about prevalence and deterrence.

Social prevalence of crimes

Deterrence is a relevant sentencing factor. The fact that a particular crime is prevalent at the time can be submitted, however court must be circumspect about it.

Deterrence is a factor which was given prominence in his Honour's reasons, and increased prevalence is a matter which his Honour may well have taken into account. But I think caution needs to be exercised in using police figures relating to the "commission" of crimes of a particular type, given that they may involve subjective judgment.

Wright J continued on to say in Dowie, (cited in Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1 (28 January 2010)) is applicable in this context; that is, a court may frequently be aided by the provision of statistical data but that "it cannot allow such material to overshadow or displace its own evaluation of the gravity of the offence before it in light of all the known facts and circumstances."

---

### Chunk 15 — Sentencing youth offenders: Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1 - Sentencing Youth Offenders] [CASE: Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1 (28 January 2010)] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority / annotation] [TOPIC: sentencing youth and limits of youthful offender mitigation] [CONCEPTS: youthful offender, youth mitigation, deterrence, Braslin, culpability, premeditation]

Tactical content: this chunk preserves the authority and practitioner note on the limited scope of youthful offender mitigation in the face of premeditated serious offending.

Sentencing youth offenders

Being under 18 is a get out of jail free card.

"[30] Whilst still qualifying as a youthful offender, Mr Braslin was approaching the end of the time for which such a claim can be made. Regard must be had to the nature and circumstances of these offences, particularly the premeditation and the detailed planning involved. The commission of such crimes, which include a crime of strong violence and considerable gravity, indicates the behaviour of an adult. It is true that Mr Braslin has no convictions for any matters of real significance, but it must be said that the frequency and extent of his offending in a short period of time, at the least shows a disregard for the law. For those reasons, factors of deterrence and retribution/denunciation were not overshadowed by issues of youth and rehabilitation, but were entitled to be given considerable weight. Braslin and Cowen v Tasmania [2010] TASCCA 1 (28 January 2010)"

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) — Overview of 2018 amendments
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: 2018 amendments — new sentencing options] [CONCEPTS: commencement date, home detention, community correction order, removal of probation, removal of community service, sentencing options, legislative amendment, replacement sentences]

The amendments to the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) introduced on 14 December 2018 created two new sentencing options — home detention orders (listed in s 7(c)) and community correction orders (listed in s 7(d)) — and removed probation orders and community service orders as available sentencing options. These changes mean a court must consider the new statutory framework when sentencing from that date, and that older sentencing options (probation and community service) are no longer available to be imposed as new orders. The amendments also inserted Parts 5A and 5B of the Act containing the detailed provisions for home detention and community correction orders respectively. The practical effect is that home detention and community correction are now standalone sentencing options with their own statutory pre-conditions, core and special conditions, variation and breach procedures.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AC — Home detention: scope and pre-conditions
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AC] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AC] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: scope and pre-conditions for home detention order] [CONCEPTS: pre-sentence report, offender consent, residence suitability, co-resident consent, family violence restriction, significant risk test, would otherwise be imprisoned, suspended sentence]

Section 42AC of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) provides that a court may make a home detention order only if it considers it appropriate in all the circumstances and the court would otherwise have sentenced the offender to a period of imprisonment (this includes wholly or partly suspended imprisonment) (s 42AC(1)–(2)(a)). The section further mandates statutory pre-conditions: the offender must undergo a pre-sentence report assessing suitability for home detention (s 42AC(2)(b)); the offender must consent to the order (s 42AC(2)(c)); the court must be satisfied the proposed residence is suitable (s 42AC(2)(d)); each adult resident (other than group premises) must consent (s 42AC(2)(e)). Section 42AC(3) prevents home detention where, in family violence, violent or sexual offence cases, the victim will reside at the relevant address during all or part of the order, or where the court is of the opinion there is a significant risk the offender may commit a violent or sexual offence during the order (s 42AC(3)(a)–(b)). These statutory pre-conditions are mandatory elements to the exercise of discretion to impose a home detention order.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AD — Core conditions of a home detention order (including electronic monitoring)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AD] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AD] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: core conditions for home detention] [CONCEPTS: core conditions, prohibition on imprisonable offences, residence times, entry and search, electronic monitoring, interaction with probation, compliance duties]

Section 42AD of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) sets out the core conditions that must attach to a home detention order. Key mandatory conditions include that the offender must not commit an offence punishable by imprisonment (s 42AD(1)(a)); the offender must reside at the specified premises and be present at times set out in the order unless a relevant reason exists (s 42AD(1)(b)–(c)); the offender must permit police, a probation officer or a prescribed officer to enter the premises (s 42AD(1)(d)); and must permit a police officer to search the premises, frisk search the offender and take samples of substances found on the premises or the offender (s 42AD(1)(e)). The section also contains other core obligations relating to contact with probation officers and electronic monitoring requirements. The Act establishes these core terms as the baseline enforceable obligations of a home detention order.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AD(2)–(4) — Electronic monitoring requirement, times and approved activities
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AD(2)–(4)] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AD(2)–(4)] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: electronic monitoring and permitted absences] [CONCEPTS: electronic monitoring mandatory, exceptions to monitoring, approved activities, specified times, medical emergency exception, probation approval]

Section 42AD(2) makes submission to electronic monitoring a core condition of every home detention order unless the court determines and specifies suitable reasons not to require it (for example, immobility, illness, or where monitoring is unnecessary because the offender is unlikely to breach) (s 42AD(2)). Section 42AD(3) allows the order to specify different times for different days during which the offender must be at the premises. Section 42AD(4) creates exceptions to presence requirements: an offender need not be at the premises to seek urgent medical treatment or where it would be dangerous to remain (s 42AD(4)(a)–(b)); and, with approval of a probation officer or prescribed officer, may leave premises to attend employment, education, training, rehabilitation programs or similar activities (s 42AD(4)(c)). These provisions structure how electronic monitoring interacts with permitted movements and necessary exceptions.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AF and s 42AG — Maximum duration, aggregation of multiple home detention orders, and commencement
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AF; s 42AG] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) ss 42AF–42AG] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: duration, multiple orders, commencement] [CONCEPTS: 18 months maximum, multiple orders, aggregated limit, commencement date, operational period]

Section 42AF(2) of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) prescribes the maximum operational period for a home detention order at 18 months. Section 42AF(3) limits the aggregate period where multiple home detention orders are made to an offender so that the total operating period cannot exceed 18 months. Section 42AC(4) permits a court to make more than one home detention order and to make home detention orders where the offender is already subject to another home detention order, community correction order or community service order, subject to the aggregation cap in s 42AF(3). Section 42AG allows a home detention order to commence either on the day it is made or on a later day specified in the order. These statutory provisions control duration and timing of home detention.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AE — Special conditions for home detention orders
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AE] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AE] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: special conditions — reducing reoffending] [CONCEPTS: special conditions, discretionary conditions, reduce reoffending, behaviour modification, tailoring conditions, court discretion]

Section 42AE of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) empowers the court to include special conditions in a home detention order that the court considers appropriate for the purpose of reducing the likelihood of reoffending during the order. The provision is deliberately broad, allowing the court to tailor special conditions to the offender and circumstances. The inclusion of a special condition is discretionary, and breach of a special condition permits the court to issue a warrant to arrest the offender. Special conditions are therefore an important tool for individualising home detention to risk, rehabilitation needs and public protection.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AH — Applications to vary or cancel a home detention order
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AH] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AH] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: variation and cancellation of home detention orders] [CONCEPTS: apply to vary, authorised person, grounds for variation, compliance assessment, alter monitoring, alter times, cancel order, revoke electronic monitoring]

Section 42AH of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) governs applications to vary or cancel a home detention order. An offender or an authorised person may apply to the court that made the order (s 42AH(1)). In determining applications the court must consider the extent of the offender’s compliance with the order (s 42AH(7)). The court may vary or cancel the order if satisfied that changes to the offender’s circumstances render compliance impossible, the offender is no longer willing or able to comply, or it is otherwise appropriate (s 42AH(6)). On variation the court may add, remove or alter special conditions, alter the operational period, change the relevant premises or times, or alter the times during which electronic monitoring applies or revoke the monitoring requirement (s 42AH(4)(a)). The court may also cancel the order and deal with the offender as if they had just been found guilty (s 42AH(4)(b)–(c)), or refuse the application (s 42AH(4)(d)).

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AI — Breach procedure for a home detention order (authorised person applications)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AI] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AI] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: breach applications and court powers] [CONCEPTS: authorised person, application for breach, vary, cancel, confirm, treat as s 42AJ if offence, procedural response]

Section 42AI of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) provides the statutory pathway when it appears to an authorised person that an offender has breached a condition of a home detention order. An authorised person may apply to the court for variation or cancellation. On hearing such an application, the court may confirm, vary (including adding, removing or altering special conditions), or cancel the home detention order, mirroring the powers available under s 42AH.
