Importantly, if the court finds the offender breached a condition by committing an offence, the court must deal with the application as if it were an application under s 42AJ (the section dealing with breach by committing an offence), even if the application was not brought under s 42AJ (s 42AI(6)). This procedural rule integrates the breach provisions.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AJ — Breach by committing an offence while under home detention
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AJ] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AJ] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: breach by new offence — mandatory cancellation for imprisonable offence] [CONCEPTS: oral application, found guilty, imprisonable offence, mandatory cancellation, exceptional circumstances, impose sentence afresh]

Section 42AJ of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) addresses the situation where an offender subject to a home detention order is found guilty of a further offence. An authorised person may make an oral application while the offender is before the court for variation or cancellation (s 42AJ(1)). If the new offence is not punishable by imprisonment, the court may vary, cancel or confirm the home detention order as under ss 42AH or 42AI (s 42AJ(4)). However, if the new offence is punishable by imprisonment, the court must cancel the home detention order (and any other order as appropriate) and then impose sentence as if the offender had just been found guilty of that offence (s 42AJ(5)). The court may depart from the mandatory cancellation only where satisfied there are exceptional circumstances, in which case it may confirm, vary or cancel the order (s 42AJ(6)). This section ensures immediate, structured treatment of new criminality.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AL(4) — Police power to arrest for suspected breach of home detention
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AL(4)] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AL(4)] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: police power of arrest for breach] [CONCEPTS: police arrest, reasonable grounds, breach, imminent breach, enforcement powers, community safety]

Section 42AL(4) of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) authorises a police officer to arrest an offender where the officer believes on reasonable grounds that the offender has breached, is breaching, or is about to breach a condition of a home detention order. This provision gives operational enforcement power to police to respond promptly to suspected breaches, supporting immediate interventions where necessary to protect victims or preserve the integrity of the order.

---

### Practical operation of electronic monitoring devices (supplier, pairing, fallbacks, battery and charging)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: device technology and practical operation] [CONCEPTS: Buddy supplier, ankle device, RF beacon, GPS fallback, telephone towers, Wi‑Fi fallback, battery pack, daily charging, no offender battery readout]

Electronic monitoring devices used in Tasmanian home detention are supplied by the company 'Buddy' and are fitted to the offender's ankle. Each device pairs with a Radio Frequency (RF) beacon installed at the approved premises; when in range it operates on RF (passive monitoring) and when out of RF range it switches to GPS (active monitoring). If GPS fails, the device can fall back to cellular tower triangulation or Wi‑Fi. The devices use a removable small battery pack to recharge the unit without requiring the offender to connect to a charger directly; the offender is required by order condition to charge the device daily. Offenders do not have direct visibility of battery level; the monitoring unit receives low-battery alerts (for example at about 30% and 10%) and may notify the offender to charge the device. These technical features inform compliance obligations and practical constraints of monitoring.

---

### Victim alert device integration and two‑way activation
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: victim alert device integration] [CONCEPTS: victim alert, pairing, GSP activation, two-way communications, family violence device, victim-activated alert, monitoring response]

Electronic monitoring can be integrated with a victim alert device that pairs with the offender’s monitoring unit. When a victim presses two buttons on the device an alert is sent to the monitoring unit; that activation can trigger GPS information and initiate two-way communications, similar to family violence alert devices used by police. Community Corrections intends to administer such victim alert systems once its monitoring and compliance unit is fully established. The integration provides a safety tool in family violence contexts and allows monitoring staff to respond promptly to proximity alerts between offender and victim devices.

---

### Assessment process for home detention suitability — two-part pre-sentence assessment (offender and home)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: pre-sentence and home assessment process] [CONCEPTS: 6 week assessment, pre-sentence report, home suitability, co-resident consent, police checks for co-residents, mental health suitability, lease verification]

The assessment process for a home detention order is conducted over approximately six weeks and has two components: an assessment of the offender (functioning as a pre-sentence suitability report) and an assessment of the proposed home. The offender assessment evaluates the offender's understanding of the order, capacity to comply, and suitability (noting concerns about suitability where mental health issues might impede compliance). The home assessment checks technological suitability (device pairing with RF beacon), physical facilities, tenancy/lease stability (assessors may liaise with housing services to confirm tenancy duration), and whether co-residents consent to the home being used — all adult co-residents must consent and submit to police checks. If co-residents with criminal records exist, the court retains discretion; lack of consent by co-residents results in the home being deemed unsuitable without notifying the offender of the reason. The written assessment informs the court’s exercise of discretion under s 42AC.

---

### Process after making a home detention order — fitting, scheduling and case management
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: implementation after order made] [CONCEPTS: immediate fitting, Community Corrections, initial schedule, probation meetings, case management, uninterrupted employment]

When a court makes a home detention order the offender must proceed immediately to Community Corrections to be fitted with the electronic monitoring device. Pre-sentence assessors obtain information for the offender’s first week's schedule in time for the subsequent court date so that approved activities (for example employment) continue without interruption. Once the device is fitted the offender engages with regular meetings with a probation officer and detailed case management. Community Corrections administers monitoring and compliance, and the monitoring unit will coordinate scheduling, permitted absences and responses to alerts. The implementation framework aims to minimise disruption to employment and routine while maintaining supervision.

---

### Monitoring and compliance architecture — statewide unit, remote resolution, and offender obligations
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: monitoring unit and compliance processes] [CONCEPTS: Hobart monitoring unit, statewide monitoring, remote resolution, phone contact, mobile phone requirement, case management intensity, decrease over time]

Community Corrections operates a central monitoring and compliance unit (located at Hobart Community Corrections) responsible for state‑wide monitoring. Most alerts are resolved remotely by the compliance unit, often by telephoning the offender; consequently orders typically require the offender to carry and answer a mobile phone at all times. Case management intensity starts high for all offenders and is reduced as compliance is demonstrated. The monitoring unit works in partnership with police for enforcement where necessary. Offenders are not informed of the precise level of active versus passive monitoring applied at any given time; this operational opacity is intended to preserve monitoring effectiveness and compliance incentives.

---

### Triaged alerts used by monitoring unit (categories and operational purpose)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: procedural rule] [TOPIC: alert triage categories] [CONCEPTS: exclusion zone, buffer zone, inclusion zone, staff duress, strap alert, low battery alerts, no communication, RF removal, interest zone, response prioritisation]

The monitoring system uses a triage of alert types for operational response: (1) exclusion zone alerts (areas set by the court, often small and tied to a person or place); (2) buffer zone alerts (wider zones set by Community Corrections to enable pre-emptive response before an exclusion breach); (3) inclusion zone alerts; (4) staff duress signals; (5) strap alerts indicating tampering or removal attempts; (6) low battery alerts (e.g. 30% and 20% thresholds—offender is not told of exact trigger levels); (7) no communication (device not pairing with RF beacon); (8) no location; (9) RF beacon removal; and (10) interest zone alerts to detect frequenting of particular addresses. These categories prioritise responses and determine whether remote contact or law enforcement intervention is warranted.

---

### Practical enforcement and breach consequences (discretion, termination, custodial sentence risk)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: breach consequences and discretion] [CONCEPTS: discretionary sanctions, minor breach, significant breach, termination, custodial sentence likely, not automatic, case management discretion]

Consequences for breaches of a home detention order are not rigidly defined and depend on the nature of the breach and offender circumstances. Probation officers and case management teams exercise significant discretion: minor breaches (for example forgetting to charge the battery or missing a curfew for a circumstance outside the offender’s control) generally result in remote resolution, admonishment or adjusted supervision rather than immediate severe sanction. Significant breaches can lead to termination of the order. Where the order is terminated, a custodial sentence is not automatically triggered by statute but, because an intention to impose custody is a precondition to making the order, it is likely that the court will impose a custodial sentence when termination follows serious non‑compliance. This practical enforcement approach balances proportionality, rehabilitation and public protection.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AN — Community correction orders: scope and replacement of probation/community service
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AN] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AN] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: scope of community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: community correction order, replace probation, replace community service, with or without conviction, suspended sentence alternative, broader use]

Section 42AN of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) allows a court to make a community correction order, with or without recording a conviction, replacing the previous probation and community service orders. The provision contemplates community correction orders being appropriate where the court would otherwise have imposed a wholly or partly suspended period of imprisonment (s 42AN(2)), reflecting a legislative intent to permit community correction orders to be used across a broader range of offences and circumstances than the older regimes. Community correction orders are therefore a flexible community‑based sentencing option designed to incorporate supervision, obligations and rehabilitative conditions.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) ss 27Y and 36B — Prohibition on new community service and probation orders
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 27Y; 36B] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) ss 27Y, 36B] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: abolition of new probation and community service orders] [CONCEPTS: prohibition, transitional arrangements, replaced sentencing options, statutory removal]

Sections 27Y and 36B of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) provide that courts may not impose any new community service orders or probation orders respectively. These provisions operationalise the removal of those sentencing options following the 2018 amendments, channeling courts to the newly created community correction orders (Part 5B) where appropriate, and preventing duplication of community‑based sentencing regimes.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) ss 42AQ and 42AR — Duration and commencement of community correction orders
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 42AQ; 42AR] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) ss 42AQ–42AR] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: operational period and commencement of community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: maximum 3 years, specify period, commencement date, operational period, time limits]

Sections 42AQ and 42AR of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) require that the court specify the period during which the offender must comply with a community correction order and that the period cannot exceed three years (s 42AQ). The court must also specify when the operational period of the order commences (s 42AR). These provisions fix the temporal scope of community correction supervision and establish the statutory maximum term.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AY(3) — Police arrest power for Community Correction Orders (leaving the State)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AY(3)] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AY(3)] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: police arrest power re: leaving State] [CONCEPTS: police arrest, reasonable grounds, leaving State, enforcement, breach by exit, interstate enforcement]

Section 42AY(3) of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) grants a police officer power to arrest an offender if the officer believes on reasonable grounds that the offender has breached, is breaching, or is about to breach a condition of a community correction order by leaving, or attempting to leave, the State of Tasmania. This is a specific statutory enforcement power tied to the travel restriction core condition found in community correction orders.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AO — Core conditions for community correction orders
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AO] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AO] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: core conditions for community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: prohibition on imprisonable offences, reporting requirement, probation directions, notify change of address, leave Tasmania restriction]

Section 42AO of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) sets out core conditions for community correction orders. Core requirements include that the offender must not commit an offence punishable by imprisonment; must report to a probation officer as specified and as required; must comply with reasonable and lawful directions of a probation officer or supervisor; must notify a probation officer of any change in address or employment; and must not leave or remain outside Tasmania without permission of a probation officer. These core conditions form the statutory baseline for supervision under community correction orders.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AP and s 42AS — Special conditions for community correction orders and maximum community service hours
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AP; s 42AS] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) ss 42AP, 42AS] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: special conditions and mandatory supervision/community service choice; maximum hours] [CONCEPTS: special conditions, supervisory condition, community service, mandatory choice, maximum 240 hours, flexible duration of conditions]

Section 42AP of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) permits the court to impose a wide range of special conditions on a community correction order, including any condition the court considers appropriate (s 42AP(n)). Critically, s 42AP(2) requires the court to include a special condition that either the offender submit to supervision by a probation officer (s 42AP(1)(c)) or perform community service under s 42AP(1)(d). Section 42AS sets the maximum number of community service hours that can be imposed as a special condition at 240 hours. Special conditions may run for all or part of the operational period, allowing graduated intensity of supervision or obligations.

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AU — Variation and cancellation of community correction orders
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AU] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AU] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: variation and cancellation of community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: authorised person, offender application, compliance assessment, grounds for variation, cancel on completion, cancel and deal as if guilty]

Section 42AU of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) allows an offender or an authorised person to apply to the making court for variation or cancellation of a community correction order (s 42AU(1)). The court must consider the extent of compliance (s 42AU(7)). The court may cancel an order where community service has been completed (s 42AU(4)(d)). Except where community service is completed, to vary or cancel the court must be satisfied that changes to the offender’s circumstances render compliance impossible, the offender is unwilling or unable to comply, or it is otherwise appropriate (s 42AU(6)). On hearing an application the court may add/remove/alter special conditions, alter the operational period, cancel the order and deal with the offender as if found guilty, or refuse the application (s 42AU(4)(a)–(e)).

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AV — Breach procedure for community correction orders (authorised person applications)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AV] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AV] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: breach applications and court powers for community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: authorised person, application for breach, confirm, vary, cancel, deal as if guilty, continuing jurisdiction]

Section 42AV of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) provides that an authorised person may apply to the court if it appears the offender has breached a condition of a community correction order (s 42AV(1)). If the court finds a breach, it must either confirm the order, vary the order by adjusting special conditions or operational period, or cancel the order and, if appropriate, any other order and deal with the offender as if found guilty of the offence (s 42AV(4)). The court’s jurisdiction to deal with the offender as if the offender had just been found guilty applies whether or not the order remains in force (s 42AV(4)(e)). If the breach involves commission of a new offence, the court must treat the matter as if an application under s 42AW had been made (s 42AV(6)).

---

### Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AW — Breach of community correction order by committing an offence
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AW] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AW] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: treatment where offender found guilty of new offence under community correction order] [CONCEPTS: found guilty, treat similarly to s 42AV, vary, cancel, confirm, sentencing consequences]

Section 42AW of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) governs applications where an offender subject to a community correction order is found guilty of a further offence; the court must proceed in a manner substantially similar to the processes under s 42AV. The court may therefore confirm, vary or cancel the community correction order, and if cancellation occurs may deal with the offender as if they had just been found guilty of the offence, including imposing any sentence appropriate for the newly convicted offence. This ensures consistent statutory treatment where further offending occurs during the operational period of a community correction order.

---

### Interpretive note on references to s 23A in amended sections
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: interpretive principle] [TOPIC: references to s 23A in the Act] [CONCEPTS: s 23A, legislative drafting, second reading speech, suspended sentences, omitted provision, drafting inconsistency]

The source material notes an interpretive issue: several provisions within the Parts of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) relevant to home detention and community correction orders refer to section 23A of the Act, but section 23A does not appear to have been included in the amendments. The second reading speech discussed s 23A in the context of limiting suspended sentences, but because the section was not enacted as part of the amendments the practical advice in the source is to treat references to s 23A in the Act as inoperative or to be ignored pending legislative correction or judicial interpretation. This is an interpretive observation about drafting inconsistencies rather than an operative legal rule.

<!-- block_053 procedure -->

### Chunk 1 — Basics — Briefing on Home Detention and Electronic Monitoring
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Home Detention Basics - Practice Note] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Overview of amendments and practical basics of home detention and electronic monitoring] [CONCEPTS: home detention, electronic monitoring, sentence alternatives, permitted activities, victim alert device, active monitoring, passive monitoring]

Covers the basics of Home Detention and Electronic Monitoring.

The amendments to the *Sentencing Act 1997* will come into force on 14 December 2018. From this date onwards the court will have to power to order an assessment for an order for electronic monitoring and home detention.

An order for electronic monitoring and home detention can be made where, if not for the order, the offender would otherwise have served a custodial sentence. The Magistrate is not required to state the duration of the custodial sentence s/he would have imposed (it is not a substitute sentence).

The maximum duration for an order is 18 months.

The offender will be subject to home detention for the duration of the order. They will be permitted to leave the home only within certain times for approved activities. Approved activities include, for example, employment, education, training, rehabilitation courses. The weekly schedule will be pre-planned to allow for travel time to and from approved activities.

The perimeters of the permitted area in the home are not necessarily defined by the boundaries of the property and can be narrowed by Community Corrections. This may be the case where the property is, for example, a large rural property. The perimeters of the permitted area may be set to only those necessary for the day to day use of the property.

The device can be paired with a 'victim alert device' which will alert the monitoring unit when the device enters a certain radius of the victim device. This is similar to the family violence devices used by police. The victim devices are activated by the victim pressing two buttons on the device, which then sends an alert and activates the GSP and 2 way communications. Community Corrections will be taking over the administration of these once their monitoring and compliance unit is fully established.

The system uses a combination of 'active' and 'passive' monitoring. This means that the devices are always recording the data (passive) but are only in 'active' mode at certain times. A device may be in active mode where an offender is travelling to and from an approved activity and the monitoring and compliance unit wish the monitor their travel. A device may be switched to active mode at any time.

---

### Chunk 2 — The device — technical and operational details
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Electronic Monitoring Device - Buddy System - Practice Note] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Technical description of the Buddy device, pairing, battery and alerts] [CONCEPTS: Buddy device, RF beacon, GPS fallback, telephone towers, Wi-Fi, battery pack, charging requirement, battery alerts]

Covers the electronic monitoring device and operational details.

The electronic monitoring system is supplied by 'Buddy', a UK company.

The device is fitted to the offender's ankle. Each device pairs with a Radio Frequency beacon which is installed in the house. When the device is in range of the beacon it pairs with the beacon. When the device goes out of range of the RF beacon it switches to GPS. If the GPS fails the device can pair to telephone towers or Wi-Fi.

The battery life of the devices will vary depending on whether it is in active (GPS -- away from the home) or passive (RF -- within the home) monitoring mode. The device can be charged by attaching a small battery pack to it. The battery pack transfers the charge to the device, without the need for the offender to attach the device to a charger. The battery pack can then be removed from the device and recharged.

It will be a condition of the order that the offender charge the device once a day. The offender will have no way of knowing the battery level of their device. There are two battery alerts for the devices that will be sent to the monitoring unit. When the battery reaches, e.g. %30 and when the battery reaches, e.g. 10%. The offender will not be aware of the alerts. The monitoring unit can notify the offender and request that the charge the device.

---

### Chunk 3 — Assessments — pre-sentence and home suitability
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Home Detention Assessments - 6 Week Process - Practice Note] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Assessment process for offenders and homes prior to home detention orders] [CONCEPTS: pre-sentence report, home assessment, co-resident consent, police check, suitability, mental health considerations, housing services]

Covers the 6-week assessment process for home detention orders.

Assessments take 6 weeks and are done in two parts -- assessment of the offender and assessment of the home.

The assessment of the offender is essentially a pre-sentence report to determine whether the offender is a suitable candidate for home detention. The assessment will make clear to the offender what the impact of the order is, and ensure that they understand and believe they can comply with the order. It has been suggested that home detention may not be suitable for those with mental health issues who may find compliance with the strict routines and schedules particularly difficult.

The assessment of the home involves assessing whether the home is suitable for detention. This involves ensuring that the device works in the home and ensuring the home has appropriate facilities. The assessor can obtain information from housing services to ensure that, for example, a lease of a house to be used for detention is not going to expire or be terminated before the end of the period of detention.

In other jurisdictions, there are restrictions on the types of offences for which such orders are available (for example violent offences, sex offences). Here, there are no such limitations. The limitations relate to whether the home is appropriate. For example, in violent offences (particularly family violence offences), if the victim lives at the home proposed for detention then it will not be suitable. An order may still be made if a suitable alternative address can be identified.

All co-residents of the home must consent to the home being used for the detention. All co-residents are required to submit to a police check. If co-residents have a criminal record this does not automatically preclude an order being made as the power remains discretionary. If the co-residents do not consent to the home being used for the detention the offender will not be informed that the co-residents withheld consent (they will be told that the home was deemed unsuitable).

After an assessment is made, this information is provided to the court and the Magistrate exercises their discretion in whether or not to make the order.

---

### Chunk 4 — The order — immediate post-sentence process and case management
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Home Detention Order - Fitting and First Week Schedule - Practice Note] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Steps following making of a home detention order including fitting, scheduling and probation] [CONCEPTS: immediate fitting, Community Corrections appointment, first-week schedule, probation officer, case management, uninterrupted employment, operational changes]

Covers what happens once a Magistrate makes a home detention order.

If the Magistrate makes an order for home detention and electronic monitoring the offender must proceed immediately to Community Corrections to be fitted with the device. At the pre-sentence assessment stage the assessor will obtain all the information required for the first week's schedule from the next court date. This ensures that the offender does not have any interruption in, for example, employment, while waiting for their first probation appointment. This process may change if there are a high number of offenders being assessed for orders but few orders made.

The offender will meet regularly with their probation officer, and engage with a detailed case management process.

---

### Chunk 5 — Monitoring and Compliance — unit responsibilities, modes and alerts
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Monitoring and Compliance - Community Corrections - Practice Note] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Monitoring responsibilities, compliance partnership with police, offender phone requirement, and alert triage] [CONCEPTS: Community Corrections, monitoring unit, Hobart, active monitoring, passive monitoring, mobile phone requirement, triaged alerts, buffer zone, exclusion zone]

Covers monitoring and compliance arrangements and the programmed alert types.

Community Corrections will be responsible for monitoring and compliance. The monitoring unit will be based at Hobart Community Corrections. This unit will be responsible for monitoring the whole of the State. Compliance will be in partnership with Community Corrections and Police. The majority of compliance issues can be resolved remotely. Most alerts relate to innocuous alerts such as low batteries or offenders running slightly off schedule. These can be resolved by the compliance unit calling the offender on their phone. It is a requirement of the order that the offender carry their mobile phone at all times and answer their phone when called.

The devices are programmed with the following triaged alerts:

1.  Exclusion zone -- this zone is set by the Court and is generally a small zone relating to a particular place or person

2.  Buffer zone -- this zone is set by Community Corrections and creates a buffer around the exclusion zone. It is wider than the exclusion zone to enable Community Corrections to respond before the issue becomes critical (i.e. once the offender has entered the exclusion zone it is likely already too late to respond adequately).

3.  Inclusion zone

4.  Staff duress -- used where staff have activated their 'duress' signal on their devices

5.  Strap alert -- activates where an offender is attempting to tamper with or remove the device

6.  Low battery 30% - offender will not be aware of the alert and will not be told at what level these are programmed at

7.  Low battery 20% - offender will not be aware of the alert and will not be told at what level these are programmed at

8.  No communication -- where the device is not pairing with the RF beacon

9.  No location

10. Radio Frequency removal -- this is triggered if the RF beacon in the home is removed.

11. Interest zone -- this zone is set by Community Corrections and can be to monitor areas of interest to identify if the offender is frequenting particular addresses or locations

All offenders will begin with a high level of case management and monitoring. Depending on their progress this will decrease. The offender will not be aware of the level of active or passive monitoring.

---

### Chunk 6 — Breaches — discretionary approach to responses
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Home Detention Breaches - Practice Note] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: How breaches of home detention orders may be dealt with; discretion and likely outcomes] [CONCEPTS: breach consequences, discretion, termination, custodial sentence, minor breaches, significant breaches, prosecutorial discretion]

Covers the practical approach to breaches of home detention orders.

There is not strict rule for consequences of breaches. It will depend on the nature of the breach and the circumstances of the offender. The probation officer and case management team will have a high degree of discretion. Minor breaches such as forgetting to charge the battery or missing a curfew due to circumstances outside the offenders control will not result in any significant consequences. Significant breaches may result in termination of the order. In the event that the order is terminated a custodial sentence is not automatically enlivened. However, given that the intention to impose a custodial sentence is a precondition to making an order, it is likely that a custodial sentence would be imposed.

---

### Chunk 7 — Changes to the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) — Overview
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: Part 5A / Part 5B (overview)] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) - Amendments Overview - 14 Dec 2018] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Overview of amendments introducing home detention and community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas), home detention orders, community correction orders, repeal probation, repeal community service, Part 5A, Part 5B]

Covers the overview of legislative changes to the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) effective 14 Dec 2018.

Recent changes to the *Sentencing Act* came into force on 14 December 2018. The changes introduced two new sentencing options: home detention orders (s 7 (c)) and community corrections orders (s 7 (d)). The changes also removed probation orders and community service orders as sentencing options.

Provisions relating to Home Detention Orders are contained in Part 5A of the *Sentencing Act*.

Provisions relating to Community Correction Orders are contained Part 5B of the *Sentencing Act*.

---

### Chunk 8 — Home Detention orders — statutory summary
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AC, s 42AF, s 42AD, s 42AG, s 42AL] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AC - Home Detention Orders - Statutory Summary] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory basis and key features of home detention orders] [CONCEPTS: s 42AC, s 42AF, s 42AD, s 42AG, s 42AL, 18 months, premises, electronic monitoring, arrest power]

Covers the statutory provisions and key features of home detention orders under the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas).

Section 42AC of the Act allows a court to make a home detention order. A court can only make an order if it considers that it would be appropriate, in all the circumstances, to make the order (s 42AC(2)(a)). A court can make a home detention order if it convicts an offender and considers that it would otherwise have sentenced the offender to a period of imprisonment (this can include a suspended period of imprisonment) (s 42AC(1)).

A home detention order is not imposed as a substitute sentence for a period of imprisonment, it is a sentencing option in its own right.

A home detention order can run for a period of 18 months (s 42AF(2)).

A home detention order must specify the premises that will be the premises at which the offender must reside during the order (s 42AC(5).

A court can make more than one home detention order, and can make a home detention order in relation to a an offender who is already subject to another home detention order, a community correction order or a community service order (s 42AC(4)). However, the total of operation period of multiple home detention orders cannot exceed 18 months (s 42AF(3)).

It is a core condition of a home detention order that the offender submit to electronic monitoring unless the court determines, and specifies, that there are suitable reasons for the offender not to be required to submit to electronic monitoring (s 42AD(2)). In the second reading speech, the minister listed potential reasons electronic monitoring may not be required as: where an offender is highly unlikely to breach a condition of their order, or because of immobility or illness they are unable to leave their premises.

An order may specify different times for different days of the week during which an offender is required to be at the relevant premises (s 42AD(3)). An offender is not required to be at the relevant premises if they are seeking urgent medical treatment or it would be dangerous to remain on the premises (s 42AD(4)(a) and (b)).

With approval of a probation officer or prescribed officer, an offender is able to leave the premises to engage in activities such as employment, education or rehabilitation programs (s 42AD(4)(c)).

A home detention order can commence on the day it is made or an a later day specified in the order (s 42AG).

A police officer can arrest an offender if the police officer believes on reasonable grounds that the offender has breached, is breaching, or is about to breach, a condition of the order (s 42AL(4)).

---

### Chunk 9 — Pre-conditions to making a home detention order (statutory checklist)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AC(2)-(3)] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AC(2)-(3) - Pre-conditions Checklist] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Statutory pre-conditions required before a court may make a home detention order] [CONCEPTS: pre-sentence report, consent, residence suitability, co-resident consent, family violence, significant risk, police check]

Covers the statutory pre-conditions that must be met before a court may make a home detention order.

[Pre-conditions]{.underline}

There are a number of pre-conditions to the making of a home detention order:

- The offender must undertake a pre-sentence report to consider whether they are suitable for home detention (s42AC(2)(b));

- The offender must consent to the order being made (s 42AC(2)(c));

- The court must be satisfied that the residence is suitable for a home detention order (s 42AC(2)(d);

- Each adult who resides at the premises (other than in a group premises) must consent to the order being made (s 42AC(2)(e);

- If the relevant offence is a family violence offence, a violent offence, or a sexual offence, a home detention order cannot be made if the victim of the offence is to, or is likely to, reside during all or part of the period, at the relevant address (s 42AC(3)(a);

- The Court cannot make an order if it is of the opinion that there is a significant risk that the offender may commit a violent offence, or sexual offence, during the intended period of the order (s 42AC(3)(b).

---

### Chunk 10 — Core conditions of home detention orders (statutory checklist)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AD(1)] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AD(1) - Core Conditions Checklist] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Core statutory conditions that must be included in a home detention order] [CONCEPTS: s 42AD(1), offence prohibition, residence requirement, searches, samples, probation interaction, electronic monitoring]

Covers the statutory core conditions that must be included in a home detention order.

[Core conditions]{.underline}

Home detention orders must be subject to a number of core conditions. These are listed in s 42AD(1) of the Act. The conditions are numerous, and without listing them in their entirety, include conditions that:

- The offender must not commit an offence that is punishable by imprisonment (s 42AD(1)(a);

- The offender must reside at the relevant premises, and must be at that premises during times specified in the order except for a relevant reason (s 42AD(1)(b) and (c));

- The offender must permit a police officer, probation officer or prescribed officer to enter the premises (s 42AD(1)(d);

- The offender must permit a police officer to conduct a search of the premises, conduct a frisk search of the offender, and take a sample of any substance found on the premises or on the person of the offender (s 42AD(1)(e);

This section contains other core conditions relating to an offenders required interactions with probation officers and conditions relating to electronic monitoring.

---

### Chunk 11 — Special conditions of home detention orders (s 42AE)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AE] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AE - Special Conditions] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Court's discretion to include special conditions in home detention orders] [CONCEPTS: s 42AE, special conditions, reduce re-offending, warrant to arrest, court discretion]

Covers the statutory provision for special conditions in home detention orders.

[Special conditions]{.underline}

Section 42AE of the Act contains special conditions that may be included in an order. Section 42AE(1)(d) allows the court to include any condition that it considers appropriate for the purpose of reducing the likelihood of the offender re-offending during the period of the order.

If an offender fails to comply with a special condition, the Court may issue a warrant to arrest the offender.

---

### Chunk 12 — Applications to vary or cancel a home detention order (s 42AH)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AH] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AH - Applications to Vary or Cancel] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory framework for varying or cancelling home detention orders] [CONCEPTS: s 42AH, vary, cancel, authorised person, compliance assessment, change of circumstances]

Covers the statutory process for applying to vary or cancel a home detention order.

[Applications to vary or cancel]{.underline}

Conditions relating to varying or cancelling a home detention order are contained in s 42AH of the Act.

An offender or an authorised person can apply to the court that made the order for the order to be varied or cancelled (s 42AH(1). The Court must consider the extent to which the offender has complied with the order (s 42AH(7)).

In order to vary or cancel an order, the Court must be satisfied that either:

- Changes to the offender's circumstances since the making of the order have rendered the offender unable to comply with one or more of the conditions of the order; or

- The offender is no longer willing or able to comply with the order; or

- It is otherwise appropriate to do so (s 42AH(6)).

On hearing an application to vary or revoke an order the Court can vary an order by:

- Adding, removing or altering a special condition of the order;

- Altering the operational period of the order, the relevant premises or the relevant times the offender must be present at the premises;

- Altering the times during which the offender must submit to electronic monitoring or revoking a condition requiring the offender to submit to electronic monitoring (s 42AH(4)(a)).

A court can cancel the home detention order, and any other order in respect of the offences, and deal with the offender in any manner in which the court could deal with the offender if they had just been found guilty of the offence (ss 42AH(4)(b) and (c)).

A court can refuse to vary or cancel a home detention order (S 42AH(4)(d).

---

### Chunk 13 — Breach of a condition of a home detention order (s 42AI)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AI] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AI - Breach of Condition (Home Detention) ] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory procedure when an authorised person alleges breach of home detention order conditions] [CONCEPTS: s 42AI, authorised person, vary cancel confirm, s 42AJ referral, offence findings]

Covers the statutory procedure for alleged breaches of home detention order conditions.

[Breach of a condition of an order]{.underline}

Provisions relating to the breach of the conditions of an order are contained in s 42AI of the Act.

An authorised person can apply to the Court for the Court to vary or cancel the order if it appears to them that the offender has breached a condition of the order.

On hearing an application, a court can vary, cancel or confirm a home detention order in much the same way as explained above in relation to applications under s 42AH of the Act.

If the Court finds that the offender has breached a condition of the order by committing an offence, the Court must deal with the application as if it were an application under s 42AJ (breach of condition of home detention order where offender found guilty of offence) even if the application was not brought under that section (s 42AI(6)).

---

### Chunk 14 — Breach by committing an offence while on home detention (s 42AJ)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AJ] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AJ - Breach by Committing an Offence (Home Detention)] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory consequences when offender commits an offence while subject to a home detention order] [CONCEPTS: s 42AJ, oral application, vary cancel confirm, punishable by imprisonment, exceptional circumstances]

Covers the statutory treatment of breaches that involve committing a new offence while subject to a home detention order.

[Breach of condition of an order by committing an offence.]{.underline}

Provisions relating to the breach of an order by committing further offences are contained in s 42AJ of the Act.

If the Court finds an offender guilty of an offence, an authorised person can make an oral application while the offender is before the court for the court to vary or cancel a home detention order (s 42AJ(1).

If the new offence is not punishable by imprisonment, the Court can deal vary, cancel or confirm a home detention order in much the same was as explained above in relation to applications under ss42AH or 42AI of the Act (s42AJ(4)).

If the new offence is punishable by imprisonment, the Court must cancel the home detention order and, if appropriate, any other order and impose a sentence as if it had just found the offender guilty of the offence (s 42AJ(5)).

However, if the Court is satisfied that there are exceptional circumstances, it must confirm, vary or cancel the order in the manner mentioned above (s 42AJ(6)).

---

### Chunk 15 — Community Correction Orders — statutory summary
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AN, s 42AQ, s 42AR, s 42AY, s 27Y, s 36B] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AN - Community Correction Orders - Statutory Summary] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Overview of community correction orders and legislative context replacing probation/community service] [CONCEPTS: community correction order, s 42AN, suspended sentence alternative, maximum 3 years, recording conviction, police arrest power, repeal probation, repeal community service]

Covers the statutory basis and context for community correction orders.

Section 42AN of the Act allows a court to make a community correction order. An order can be made with or without recording a conviction (s 7(d)). This sentencing option was introduced to replace probation orders and community service orders. In the second reading speech, the minister states that community correction orders are likely to be imposed in a broader range of circumstances than either community service orders or probation orders.

Section 27Y of the Act provides that the court may not impose any new community service orders. Section 36B of the Act provides that the court may not impose any new probation orders.

The Court has discretion as to when to impose a community correction order, but the Act stipulates that it may be appropriate to impose a community correction order where a court would otherwise have sentenced an offender to a wholly or partly suspended period of imprisonment (s 42AN(2)).

The Court must specify the period during which the offender must comply with an order, and an order cannot be specified to be more than 3 years (s 42AQ)). The Court must also specify when the operational period of the order commences (s 42AR).

A police officer can arrest an offender if the police officer believes on reasonable grounds that the offender has breached, is breaching, or is about to breach, a condition of the order by leaving, or attempting to leave, the State (s 42AY(3)).

There is no requirement in the Act that the offender undertake a pre-sentence report before the imposition of a community correction order.

In the relevant report (Phasing Out of Suspended Sentences (2016)), the Sentencing Advisory Council noted that Community Correction Orders have been used in Victoria for relatively serious offences including armed robbery, aggravated burglary and intentionally causing serious harm amongst numerous other offences. It recommends that community correction orders in Tasmania should also be able to be used for relatively serious offences, as is reflected in the statement in s 42AN(2) that they can be used where the Court would otherwise have imposed a suspended period of imprisonment.

---

### Chunk 16 — Core conditions of community correction orders (s 42AO)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AO] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AO - Core Conditions (Community Correction Orders)] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Statutory core conditions for community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: s 42AO, prohibition on offences, leave Tasmania permission, report to probation officer, notify change of address, comply with directions]

Covers the statutory core conditions imposed on community correction orders.

[Core Conditions]{.underline}

An order is subject to a number of core conditions, these are contained in s 42AO of the Act.

An offender must not:

- Commit an offence punishable by imprisonment;

- Leave or remain outside Tasmania without permission of a probation officer.

An offender must:

- Report to a probation officer as specified in the order and as required by the probation officer;

- Comply with reasonable and lawful directions of a probation officer or supervisor;

- Notify a probation officer of any change in address or employment.

---

### Chunk 17 — Special conditions of community correction orders (s 42AP and s 42AS)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AP, s 42AS] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AP & s 42AS - Special Conditions and Community Service Hours] [CATEGORY: checklist] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Statutory special conditions for community correction orders including mandatory supervision or community service and max hours] [CONCEPTS: s 42AP, mandatory supervision, community service, s 42AS, 240 hours, special conditions duration]

Covers the requirement for special conditions attached to community correction orders.

[Special Conditions]{.underline}

An order can be subject to a number of special conditions. These are listed in s 42AP of the Act. The Court can impose any condition that it thinks appropriate (s 42AP(n)).

The Court MUST include a special condition that either the offender submit to the supervision of a probation order in accordance with s 42AP(1)(c) of the Act or perform community service in accordance with s 42AP(1)(d) of the Act (s 42AP(2)).

The maximum hours of community service that can be imposed is 240 (S42AS).

A special condition can run for the whole, or any part of the operational period of the order (s 42AP(3)). For example, an offender may be subject to less stringent conditions as the order progresses.

---

### Chunk 18 — Application to vary or cancel a community correction order (s 42AU)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AU] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AU - Application to Vary or Cancel (Community Correction Orders)] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory process for varying or cancelling community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: s 42AU, vary, cancel, authorised person, compliance assessment, complete community service]

Covers statutory procedures for varying or cancelling a community correction order.

[Application to vary or cancel]{.underline}

Provisions relating to an application to vary or cancel an order are contained in s 42AU of the Act.

An offender or an authorised person can apply to the court that made the order for the order to be varied or cancelled (s 42AU(1)). The Court must consider the extent to which the offender has complied with the order (s 42AU(7)).

The Court may cancel an order where an offender has completed community service (s 42AU(4)(d)).

Except where community service has been completed, in order to vary or cancel an order, the Court must be satisfied that either:

- Changes to the offender's circumstances since the making of the order have rendered the offender unable to comply with one or more of the conditions of the order; or

- The offender is no longer willing or able to comply with the order; or

- It is otherwise appropriate to do so (s 42AU(6)).

On hearing an application to vary or cancel an order, the Court may:

- Vary the order by adding, removing, or altering a special condition or altering the operational period of the order;

- Cancel the order and, if appropriate, and other order in respect of the offence, and deal with the offender as if the court had just found the offender guilty of the offence;

- Refuse to vary or cancel the order (ss 42AU(4)(a), (b), (c) and (e).

---

### Chunk 19 — Breach of condition of a community correction order (s 42AV)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AV] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AV - Breach of Condition (Community Correction Orders)] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory response where authorised person alleges breach of community correction order] [CONCEPTS: s 42AV, authorised person, confirm vary cancel, deal as if guilty, referral to s 42AW]

Covers statutory procedures where a breach of a community correction order is alleged.

[Breach of condition of an order]{.underline}

The provisions relating to a breach of a condition of an order are contained in s 42AV of the Act.

An authorised person can apply to the Court under this section if it appears to them that the offender has breached a condition of the order (s 42AV(1)).

If the Court finds that the offender has breached a condition of the order, it must either:

- Confirm the order;

- Vary the order by adding, removing or altering a special condition or extending the operational period;

- Cancel the order, and if appropriate, any other order, and deal with the offender in respect of the offence as if it had just found the offender guilty of that offence (s 42AV(4)).

The court can deal with the offender as if they had just found them guilty of the offence whether or not the order is still in force (s 42AV(4)(e)).

If the Court finds that the offender has breached a condition of the order by committing an offence, the Court must deal with the application as if it were an application under s 42AW (breach of condition of community correction order where offender found guilty of offence) even if the application was not brought under that section (s 42AV(6)).

---

### Chunk 20 — Breach by committing an offence while on community correction order (s 42AW)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 42AW] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 42AW - Breach by Committing an Offence (Community Correction Orders)] [CATEGORY: legal doctrine] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Statutory approach to offenders who commit further offences while on community correction orders] [CONCEPTS: s 42AW, proceed similarly to s 42AV, applications, court powers]

Covers the statutory treatment of breaches involving new offences for community correction orders.

[Breach of condition of an order by committing an offence]{.underline}

Provisions relating to the breach of an order by committing further offences are contained in s 42AW of the Act.

If a court hears an application under this section, it must proceed in much the same way as mentioned above in relation to an application under s 42AV of the Act.

---

### Chunk 21 — Further note re references to s 23A in the Act (practitioner annotation)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) - s 23A References Note] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Practitioner note about references to s 23A in amended sections] [CONCEPTS: s 23A, suspended sentences, drafting anomalies, practitioner note, legislative drafting]

Covers a practitioner observation about references to s 23A in the amended Act.

As a side note, in the sections of the Act relevant to home detention orders and community corrections orders, there are multiple references to section 23A of the Act. This section was discussed in the second reading speech of the bill, intending to limit the circumstances in which suspended sentences could be imposed. The section does not seem to have been included in the amendments, so I would think that references to section 23A in the legislation can ignored.

---

<!-- block_054 master -->

# Drug Treatment Orders — Overview
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [TYPE: statutory definition] [TOPIC: Drug Treatment Orders — purpose and nature] [CONCEPTS: drug treatment order, court mandated diversion, rehabilitation, custodial sentence suspended, community-based program, drug-crime link, treatment instead of prison, program compliance]

A Drug Treatment Order (DTO) under the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) is a court-mandated diversion program that substitutes a period of treatment and supervision for immediate imprisonment where illicit drug use contributed to the commission of an imprisonable offence. The DTO is designed to address the substance-use causes of offending and to break the link between drug use and criminal behaviour by placing the offender in a structured, supervised treatment regime instead of serving prison time immediately. The program is intensive and includes multiple requirements and reviews; it carries an attached custodial sentence held in suspense such that failure to comply can lead to activation of imprisonment or other sanctions. Participation is voluntary but conditional on guilty plea or a finding of guilt and suitability criteria under the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas).

# Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 27A — Making a Drug Treatment Order
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 27A] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [TYPE: statutory provision] [TOPIC: statutory criterion for making DTO] [CONCEPTS: s 27A, eligibility, drug-contribution to offence, guilty plea, sentencing discretion, diversion order, sentencing alternative]

Section 27A of the Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) permits the court to make a Drug Treatment Order when the offender pleads guilty or is found guilty of an offence and the court is satisfied that illicit drug use contributed to the commission of the imprisonable offence or offences. The statutory criterion focuses on the causal contribution of drug use to offending behaviour and links eligibility to either a plea of guilty or a finding of guilt. The provision authorises the court to impose a treatment-and-supervision scheme in substitution for immediate imprisonment, subject to other statutory constraints and exclusions elsewhere in the Act.

<!-- block_054 procedure -->

### Drug Treatment Orders — SNAP SHOT
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Overview of Court Mandated Diversion Program / Drug Treatment Orders] [CONCEPTS: drug treatment order, court mandated diversion, DTO overview, diversion program, eligibility, rehabilitation]
What this chunk covers: Overview of Drug Treatment Orders (Court Mandated Diversion Program) and general description.
**Drug Treatment Orders**

SNAP SHOT:

DTO instead of into prison. This is called the **Court Mandated Diversion Program**, and involves drug treatment orders.

It's done to address the issues behind your drug use and offending --- and therefore hopefully help you break the link between your drug use and offending, and improve your life.

This is a strict program with many requirements that will take up a lot of your time. It is not an easy option.

---

### Eligibility for DTOs
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Eligibility criteria for Drug Treatment Orders] [CONCEPTS: eligibility, plead guilty, found guilty, age 18+, sexual offences exclusion, parole, Supreme Court order, suitability]
What this chunk covers: Eligibility and ineligibility criteria for Drug Treatment Orders.
**Eligibility**

You are eligible if you:

- plead guilty, or are found guilty

- have a history of drug use and offending linked to your drug use

- are 18 years or over

- are willing to participate in the program

You are not eligible if you:

- have a current or outstanding sexual or offence including actual serious bodily harm (can be psych)

- are subject to a Supreme Court Order or Parole Order

---

### Court diversion officers
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Role and functions of court diversion officers in DTOs] [CONCEPTS: court diversion officer, case manager, assessment, progress reports, suitability assessment, referrals]
What this chunk covers: The assessment role and ongoing case management duties of court diversion officers.
**Court diversion officers**

A court diversion officer will assess your suitability by asking about your previous and current drug use, criminal activity, and lifestyle.

The court diversion officer will also:

- be your case manager throughout the program and help you meet your goals and make sure you get the most suitable treatments

- give regular progress reports to the Magistrate

---

### Drug treatment orders: duration and sentencing consequences
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Duration of DTOs and sentencing consequences if not completed] [CONCEPTS: 24 months, custodial component, sentence on hold, imprisonment, community service, sentence reduction, cancellation]
What this chunk covers: The structure of DTOs (length and attached sentence) and outcomes for compliance or non-compliance.
**Drug treatment orders**

A drug treatment order:

- can last for 24 months

- has a sentence attached, which means if you don't successfully complete it, you could serve time in prison

Essentially, your sentence is 'on hold' while you take part in the program:

- if you **don't** successfully complete or comply with the program, you could be imprisoned or do community service

- if you **do **successfully complete the program, your sentence could be cancelled or reduced

---

### The program in detail — obligations, services, and phases
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: Detailed program requirements and three-phase structure of DTOs] [CONCEPTS: abstain, urinalysis, case management, counselling, court reviews, residential rehab, life skills, Phase 1, Phase 2, Phase 3]
What this chunk covers: Detailed obligations and services in the DTO and the three phases with specific frequency requirements.
These restrictions are designed to help you recover from drug use and break the drug-crime cycle.

**You must abstain from all illicit drug use.**

Usually, you must also:

- have regular, random urinalysis

- go to regular face-to-face meetings with your court diversion officer

- go to regular court reviews with a Magistrate

- go to individual counselling and group programs

You may be offered residential rehabilitation and medically-supervised withdrawal.

You'll also be offered services to help you improve your life skills, such as:

- lessons in literacy and numeracy

- programs addressing anger and family violence, gambling, parenting

- employment services

There are three phases to the program:

Phase 1: Stabilisation (6 to 12 months)

- Weekly case management

- Weekly counselling

- Random drug testing (at least once a week)

- Regular court appearances

Phase 2: Consolidation (4 to 7 months)

- Fortnightly case management

- Fortnightly counselling

- Random drug testing (at least once a fortnight)

- Reduced court appearances

Phase 3: Re-integration (3 to 5 months)

- Monthly case management

- Monthly counselling

- Random drug testing (at least once a month)

- Reduced court appearances

---

### Section 27A — Sentencing Act: when a DTO can be made
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 27A] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 27A - DTO eligibility trigger] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Section 27A DTO eligibility where illicit drug use contributed] [CONCEPTS: s27A, Sentencing Act 1997, DTO eligibility, drug-contributed offending, guilty plea]
What this chunk covers: The statutory trigger (s 27A) for when a Drug Treatment Order can be made.
**Section 27A Sentencing Act**

Can be made where defendant pleads guilty or found guilty to offences where illicit drug use contributed to the commission of the imprisonable offence or offences.

---

### Section 27B — Sentencing Act: conditions preventing DTO
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 27B] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 27B - DTO exclusion criteria] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: checklist] [TOPIC: Statutory exclusions preventing making of DTOs under s 27B] [CONCEPTS: s27B, sexual offences, actual physical harm, parole, Supreme Court sentence, previous DTO, outstanding proceedings]
What this chunk covers: Statutory exclusions under s 27B that prevent a court from making a DTO.
**Section 27B Sentencing Act**

- Sexual offences

- Offences amounting to actual physical harm (see *Tasmania v Joseph* \[2017\] TASSC 23)

- Outstanding proceedings in any court for the **above type offences**

- Subject to Parole order

- Subject to Supreme Court sentence

- Subject to a previous Drug Treatment Order

---

### Actual bodily harm — Tasmania v Joseph [2017] TASSC 23 (excerpt)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CASE: Tasmania v Joseph [2017] TASSC 23] [CITATION: Tasmania v Joseph [2017] TASSC 23 - actual bodily harm includes psychiatric injury] [CATEGORY: case authority] [TYPE: case authority] [TOPIC: Judicial interpretation that 'actual bodily harm' can include psychiatric harm] [CONCEPTS: actual bodily harm, psychiatric injury, Tasmania v Joseph, McCallum, aggravated robbery, psychological harm]
What this chunk covers: Judicial reasoning that "actual bodily harm" can include psychiatric injury and related authorities.
*15. The term "bodily harm" is not defined in Tasmanian legislation including the Code, the Act or the [Police Offences Act](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/tas/consol_act/poa1935140/). Despite this, it is a concept regularly referred to as an element of various crimes and offences. For example, the crime of aggravated robbery occurs when a person commits a robbery and "causes bodily harm to any person immediately before, at, or immediately after the time of committing that robbery". The definition of "bodily harm" which is consistently applied in Tasmania, is derived from McCallum [\[1969\] TASStRp 11](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASStRp/1969/11.html); [\[1969\] Tas SR 73](http://www.austlii.edu.au/cgi-bin/LawCite?cit=%5b1969%5d%20Tas%20SR%2073) at 88 per Burbury CJ as follows:*

*"Bodily harm includes any hurt or injury calculated to interfere with health or comfort. It need not been permanent and must be more than transient or trifling."*

*21. Further, irrespective of the policy behind the unavailability of a drug treatment order as a sentencing option for such offences, there does not seem to be any logical reason to distinguish, for the purposes of this provision, between cases where the injury inflicted is psychological rather than physical. Both involve significant personal harm to a victim of the crime, and such harm will invariably have been inflicted by serious violence, albeit that it may be threatened or indirect. This case is a good example, and sits comfortably with the notion of "an offence involving the infliction of actual bodily harm". The defendant held a knife up to the employee and made serious threats to directly harm her with it if she did not follow his instructions. The defendant's purpose and intention was to cause such fear that any resistance from her or other staff members would be overcome. In those circumstances, the causation of psychological harm and development of psychiatric injury was likely. The injury is as serious and debilitating as physical injury. There is no valid reason, as a matter of policy or interpretation to distinguish between psychiatric and physical injury for the purposes of this provision. As I said in the case of Bartel v State of Tasmania [\[2016\] TASCCA 18:](http://www.austlii.edu.au/au/cases/tas/TASCCA/2016/18.html)*

*"Armed robbery is a crime against people, notwithstanding that it also involves property. By definition, it involved threatened or actual violence. The threat and trauma to which victims are exposed, more often than not, result in devastating long-term consequences, irrespective of the use or extent of actual physical violence."*

---

### DTO procedural mechanics — family violence, assessments, core/program conditions, case conferences, immunity, sanctions, contraventions, subsumption
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: ss 27D, 27G, 27H, 27I, 27T, 27K, 27M, 27P] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) ss 27D/27G/27H/27I/27T/27K/27M/27P - DTO procedures and sanctions] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: procedure] [TOPIC: DTO procedural steps: assessment, core conditions, case conferences, immunity, sanctions, contraventions, subsuming sentences] [CONCEPTS: s27D assessment, core conditions, program conditions, case conference, closed court, s27T immunity, sanctions, s27M, subsume, s27P]
What this chunk covers: Procedures for DTOs including family violence conditions, assessment report, case conferences, evidential immunity, sanctions and subsuming new sentences.
Family violence offences:

Where the DTO stems from Family Violence matters, additional "core" conditions of the DTO must include the requirement that no other family violence occur.

Where the court is satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that the defendant has committed a crime punishable by more than 12 months in prison, an application may be made by prosecution to activate part or all of the custodial component of the order.

First step where Magi considering an order is to get an assessment report (per s 27D)

Basically, court sentences them to prison, but then says need not serve that part unless activated under the sentencing act/

- All the DTO have "core conditions" under s 27G

- There are additional "program conditions" which can be applied under s 27H

- Case conferences are held regularly for updates -- but in a closed court s27I

  - Anything said by the defendant here is with immunity. Cannot use admissions for a charge. (s. 27T)

- There are reward and punishments for compliance under ss 27K and 27M

- Contraventions that amount to offences punishable by 12 months imprisonment can lead to punishment under s 27M. The court needs only be satisfied on the balance of probabilities that a contravention has occurred, unless they want to send them to prison under s 27M(1)(d). That section has the usual requirement.

- If the defendant accrues 13 days punishment under sanction, then they have to "actually" serve it, as opposed to just earning the sanctions.

- Where the contravention is an offence with a punishment greater than 12 months, the court must act under s 27M. They can go further than just sanction days, they can cancel the DTO and sentence as if the DTO wasn't there for the original offending. The court can do this on its own motion, or on application.

- If a defendant is to be sentenced on "new" charges, whether they occurred before or during the life of the extant DTO, the court, under s 27P can subsume the new sentence into the extant DTO custodial period, where such sentence is less that hey length of the extant DTO custodial sentence.

---

### Cancellation of DTOs — Section 27Q (text)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 27Q] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 27Q - Cancellation of DTO treatment and supervision part] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Grounds and statutory text for cancellation of the treatment and supervision part of a DTO] [CONCEPTS: s27Q, cancellation, balance of probabilities, materially changed circumstances, unwillingness to comply, purpose of order, statutory grounds]
What this chunk covers: The statutory grounds and text for cancellation of the treatment and supervision part of a Drug Treatment Order under s 27Q.
**27Q.**   **Cancellation**

\(1\) A court may cancel the treatment and supervision part of a drug treatment order made by the court if it is satisfied on the balance of probabilities that --

> \(a\) before the order was made, the offender's circumstances were not accurately presented to the court or the authors of the relevant drug treatment order assessment report; or
>
> \(b\) the offender will be unable to comply with a condition of the order because his or her circumstances have materially changed since it was made; or

\(c\) the offender is no longer willing to comply with one or more conditions of the order; or

> \(d\) the continuation of the treatment and supervision part of the order is unlikely to achieve one or more of the purposes for which the order was made.

---

### Five Stages of Change In Recovery
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [CATEGORY: annotation] [TYPE: annotation] [TOPIC: Five Stages of Change model applied to addiction recovery] [CONCEPTS: precontemplation, contemplation, preparation, action, maintenance, relapse prevention, recovery stages]
What this chunk covers: Descriptions of the Five Stages of Change in addiction recovery relevant to engagement with treatment programs.
1. Precontemplation Stage of Addiction Recovery

People who are in the first stage of addiction recovery aren't yet ready for any addiction treatment program. This phase is characterized by defensiveness and endless justification of their behavior. There's a clear lack of insight into the negative impact of excessive drug or alcohol use and a strong focus on the positive effects they experience from using their drug of choice.

2. Contemplation Stage of Recovery

The next phase is characterized by contemplative readiness. This means the person is ready to bring about change in the future, but not immediately. Unlike the previous stage, they're aware of the pros of becoming drug-free.

3. Preparation Stage of Addiction Recovery

When it comes to the preparation stage, the individual is building a sense of urgency regarding their desire for sobriety. They've usually made steps toward taking action, such as intending to join a gym, seeing a counselor or attempting to quit addiction by themselves without attending a treatment center.

4. Action Stage of Recovery

During the action stage, the person has made significant changes in their lives and is committed to change. This stage of change is characterized by prolonged periods of abstinence and the inclination to turn to professionals for help before or after relapse.

5. Maintenance Stage of Addiction Recovery

During the maintenance stage, the individual is working hard to prevent addiction recovery relapse. They're also keeping up the lifestyle changes they made, like getting regular exercise, recreational activities, staying sober, paying attention to sleep hygiene and attending support groups. They don't feel the urge to relapse as frequently as people in the action stage, so their confidence grows and they truly believe in their ability to maintain sobriety long term.

---

### Suspended sentences — Sentencing Act 1997 s 27 (breach of order suspending sentence)
[DOMAIN: Tasmanian Criminal Law] [ACT: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas)] [SECTION: s 27] [CITATION: Sentencing Act 1997 (Tas) s 27 - Breach of order suspending sentence provisions (4B, 4C)] [CATEGORY: procedure] [TYPE: legal doctrine] [TOPIC: Activation and variation options under s 27 when suspended sentence breached] [CONCEPTS: suspended sentence, activate sentence, s27(4B), s27(4C), substituted sentence, vary conditions, unjust]
What this chunk covers: Statutory provisions in s 27 about activation of suspended sentences on breach and the court's alternative powers under subsection 4C.
**Sentencing Act 1997**

**27.**   **Breach of order suspending sentence**

(4B)  If, on the hearing of an application under this section, the court is satisfied that the offender has been found guilty of a new offence, the court must activate the sentence of imprisonment that is held in suspense and order the offender to serve it.

(4C)  If the court is of the opinion that an order under [subsection (4B)](https://www.legislation.tas.gov.au/view/html/inforce/current/act-1997-059#GS27@Gs4B@EN) would be unjust, the court may instead --

\(a\) activate part of the sentence that is held in suspense and order the offender to serve it; or

\(b\) order that a sentence (in this section called the \"**substituted sentence**\") take effect in place of the suspended sentence; or

\(c\) by order, vary the conditions on which the execution of the sentence was suspended, including extending the period of suspension in the order to a day no later than 12 months after the day the offender was found guilty of the new offence; or

\(d\) make no order in respect of the suspended sentence.
